topics.title: Mechanical Properties of Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Properties of Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Mechanical Properties of Materials

The **Mechanical Properties of Materials** are the fundamental characteristics that describe how a solid body responds to externally applied forces, loads, deformations, or environmental conditions. These properties govern the suitability of a material for a given structural or mechanical application and form the foundation of **Mechanics of Solids (Strength of Materials)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PCMRT303 – Module 1):**
> Mechanical properties describe the behavior of a material under the action of external forces. They are determined experimentally through standardized destructive and non-destructive tests (e.g., Universal Testing Machine, Charpy, Brinell hardness testers) and form the basis for safe and economical design of machine and structural components.

---

### 1.2 Primary Mechanical Properties at a Glance

| Property | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Stress** | Internal resistance force per unit area developed inside a material when subjected to external load. |
| **Strain** | Ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension, representing the geometric deformation. |
| **Elasticity** | Ability of a material to regain its original shape and size after removal of load. |
| **Plasticity** | Ability of a material to undergo permanent (irreversible) deformation without rupture. |
| **Ductility** | Capacity of a material to be drawn into wires or stretched plastically before fracture. |
| **Brittleness** | Property of a material to fracture without appreciable plastic deformation. |
| **Toughness** | Ability of a material to absorb energy and plastically deform before fracturing. |
| **Hardness** | Resistance of a material to localized plastic deformation (indentation, scratching, abrasion). |
| **Resilience** | Capacity of a material to absorb energy within the elastic range. |
| **Fatigue** | Failure of a material under fluctuating/cyclic stresses below the static strength. |
| **Creep** | Time-dependent permanent deformation under sustained load at elevated temperatures. |

---

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 1 — Stretching a Rubber Band vs. a Steel Wire:**
> Pull a rubber band and release it — it snaps back perfectly (high **elasticity**). Pull a soft copper wire slowly — it stretches permanently and doesn't return (high **ductility & plasticity**). Strike a piece of cast iron with a hammer — it shatters instantly (high **brittleness**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 2 — Stress and Strain Visualization:**
> Imagine a queue of people (atoms) standing in a corridor. When you push one end (apply force), each person squeezes the next — this internal "squeezing" is **stress**, while the small displacement each person experiences relative to their neighbour is **strain**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy 3 — Toughness vs. Hardness (Coconut vs. Glass):**
> A coconut shell is hard (resists scratching) but not tough — it cracks on impact. Mild steel is tough — it bends and absorbs energy before breaking. **Hardness ≠ Toughness.**

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard values are universally used in KTU examination numericals:

- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **1 MPa** $= 10^6 \text{ N/m}^2 = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2$
- **1 GPa** $= 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2 = 10^3 \text{ MPa}$
- **Young's Modulus of Steel:** $E \approx 200 \text{ GPa}$
- **Young's Modulus of Aluminium:** $E \approx 70 \text{ GPa}$
- **Poisson's Ratio of Steel:** $\nu \approx 0.30$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Units Reminder (KTU 2024):**
> - Force → **N** or **kN**
> - Stress → **N/m² (Pa)**, **MPa**, or **N/mm²**
> - Strain → **dimensionless** (no units)
> - Modulus of Elasticity → same units as stress → **GPa** or **MPa**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress — The Internal Resistance

When an external load is applied to a body, internal forces are mobilized at every imaginary cross-section to maintain equilibrium. The intensity of these internal forces at a point is defined as **Stress**.

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. Consider a bar of cross-sectional area $A$ subjected to an axial tensile force $P$.
2. Cut the bar with an imaginary plane perpendicular to the axis.
3. For equilibrium, each segment must develop an internal resistive force equal and opposite to $P$.
4. The stress is the limiting value of this internal force per unit area as the area approaches a point.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta A} = \frac{dP}{dA}$$

**Types of Stress:**

- **Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** Acts perpendicular to the surface.
$$\sigma_n = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{(Tensile: +ve, Compressive: -ve)}$$

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$):** Acts tangential (parallel) to the surface.
$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$
where $V$ is the shear (tangential) force.

- **Bearing Stress ($\sigma_b$):** Contact stress between a bolt shank and its hole.
$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}$$
where $d$ is bolt diameter and $t$ is plate thickness.

---

### 2.2 Strain — The Geometric Deformation

Strain is a dimensionless measure of deformation representing the relative change in shape or size.

**Normal Strain (Axial Strain):**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0}$$

**Shear Strain:**
$$\gamma = \frac{\Delta x}{y} = \tan \theta \approx \theta \text{ (for small angles in radians)}$$

**Volumetric Strain:**
$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V_0}$$

---

### 2.3 Hooke's Law and Elastic Moduli

Within the **proportional limit**, stress is directly proportional to strain.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E$ is **Young's Modulus** (Modulus of Elasticity), measured in **GPa**.

For shear loading:
$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$
where $G$ is the **Shear Modulus** (Modulus of Rigidity).

For hydrostatic pressure (volumetric change):
$$p = K \cdot \varepsilon_v$$
where $K$ is the **Bulk Modulus**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Concept:**
> Hooke's Law is the foundation of **Linear Elasticity**. All KTU Module 1 design equations (axial deformation, thermal stresses, indeterminate problems) assume the material remains within this linear range.

---

### 2.4 Poisson's Ratio

When a body is stretched in one direction, it contracts in the transverse directions. **Siméon Denis Poisson** quantified this lateral strain response:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x}$$

The negative sign ensures $\nu$ is positive (lateral strain is opposite in sign to axial strain).

---

### 2.5 Relationship Between Elastic Constants

For a homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material, the three moduli are related by:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} \qquad K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

---

### 2.6 Engineering Stress-Strain Diagram (KTU High-Yield Topic)

The complete stress-strain curve from a **Universal Testing Machine (UTM)** reveals the mechanical personality of a material.

**Key Points on the Curve:**

1. **Proportional Limit (A):** Stress-strain is linear; Hooke's Law valid.
2. **Elastic Limit (B):** Maximum stress up to which the body regains its original shape.
3. **Yield Point (C):** Stress at which material begins to deform plastically without increase in load.
   - **Upper Yield Point (UYP):** Initial peak
   - **Lower Yield Point (LYP):** Plateau region
4. **Ultimate Stress Point (D):** Maximum stress the material can withstand; necking begins.
5. **Fracture/Necking Point (E):** Material breaks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Mild Steel (KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked):**
> Mild steel exhibits a distinct **yield point phenomenon** with a flat plateau between UYP and LYP — this is the basis for design using **yield stress ($\sigma_y$)**, NOT ultimate stress.

---

### 2.7 Mechanical Properties from Stress-Strain Curve

| Property | Mathematical Expression | Physical Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Modulus of Resilience ($u_r$)** | $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | Energy stored per unit volume up to elastic limit |
| **Modulus of Toughness ($u_t$)** | $u_t = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Total energy per unit volume before fracture (area under full $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve) |
| **Proof Stress ($\sigma_{0.2\%}$)** | Stress at 0.2% plastic strain | Used for materials without clear yield point (e.g., aluminium alloys) |
| **Factor of Safety (FOS)** | $\text{FOS} = \dfrac{\sigma_{ultimate}}{\sigma_{working}} \text{ or } \dfrac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Safety margin against failure |
| **Percentage Elongation (%EL)** | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Measure of ductility |
| **Percentage Reduction in Area (%RA)** | $\%RA = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Measure of ductility |

---

### 2.8 Hardness Tests (KTU 2024 – Brief Overview)

| Test | Indenter | Load Range | Material Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Brinell** | Hardened steel ball (10 mm) | 500–3000 kgf | Cast iron, soft metals |
| **Vickers** | Diamond pyramid | 1–100 kgf | All metals, thin sections |
| **Rockwell** | Diamond cone / steel ball | 60–150 kgf | Production line testing |
| **Mohs** | Scratch mineral scale | — | Minerals, ceramics |

**Brinell Hardness Number (BHN):**
$$\text{BHN} = \frac{2P}{\pi D \left[ D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2} \right]}$$

where $P$ = applied load, $D$ = ball diameter, $d$ = indentation diameter.

---

### 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this topic critical in KTU & Engineering Practice?**
> - **Civil Engineering:** Designing RCC, steel girders, bridges — relies on yield strength, ductility, and toughness to prevent catastrophic collapse.
> - **Mechanical Engineering:** Designing shafts, pressure vessels, IC engine components — needs fatigue strength, creep resistance, hardness.
> - **Aerospace:** Wing spars, turbine blades — demands high strength-to-weight ratio, fatigue, and creep resistance.
> - **Marine Engineering:** Hull plates and propeller shafts — toughness against impact loading is critical.
> - **Manufacturing:** Forming, forging, rolling operations — depends on plasticity and ductility.

---

### 2.10 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula Quick Reference — Module 1 / Mechanical Properties**

| Sl. No. | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Normal stress (MPa) |
| 2 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{A}$ | Shear stress (MPa) |
| 3 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{P}{d \cdot t}$ | Bearing stress (MPa) |
| 4 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Normal strain (dimensionless) |
| 5 | $\gamma = \dfrac{\Delta x}{y}$ | Shear strain (radians) |
| 6 | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ | Hooke's Law |
| 7 | $\tau = G \gamma$ | Hooke's Law in shear |
| 8 | $p = K \varepsilon_v$ | Bulk deformation |
| 9 | $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lat}}{\varepsilon_{ax}}$ | Poisson's ratio |
| 10 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | $E$–$G$–$\nu$ relation |
| 11 | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | $E$–$K$–$\nu$ relation |
| 12 | $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | Modulus of resilience |
| 13 | $\text{FOS} = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{working}}$ | Factor of safety |
| 14 | $\%EL = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure |
| 15 | $\text{BHN} = \dfrac{2P}{\pi D\left[D - \sqrt{D^2 - d^2}\right]}$ | Brinell hardness |

> **Units Convention Used in KTU Examinations:**
> Force in **N** or **kN**, area in **mm²** or **m²**, stress in **MPa** or **N/mm²**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Derivation of Axial Deformation from Hooke's Law

We aim to derive the fundamental expression for the elongation of a uniform prismatic bar under an axial tensile load, which is the foundation of all KTU Module 1 numerical problems.

**Given:**
- A uniform bar of length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$.
- Subjected to an axial tensile force $P$ at its ends.
- Material obeys Hooke's Law within the elastic range.

**Step 1 — Compute Internal Stress:**
The internal axial stress developed at any cross-section (perpendicular to the load) is given by:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law:**
Hooke's Law states that within the proportional limit, stress is proportional to strain:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where $E$ is Young's Modulus (a material constant) and $\varepsilon$ is axial strain.

**Step 3 — Express Strain Geometrically:**
By definition of normal strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

where $\Delta L$ is the change in length (elongation) and $L$ is the original length.

**Step 4 — Substitute and Solve for $\Delta L$:**
Substituting $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ and $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L}$ into Hooke's Law:

$$\frac{P}{A} = E \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

Rearranging for $\Delta L$:

$$\Delta L = \frac{P \cdot L}{A \cdot E}$$

**Step 5 — Final Expression:**

$$\boxed{\Delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

This is the **fundamental KTU axial deformation formula**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The deformation $\Delta L$ is directly proportional to the load $P$ and length $L$, but inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$ and material stiffness $E$. Doubling the length doubles the elongation; doubling the area halves it.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Relationship Between $E$, $G$, and $\nu$

For an isotropic linear elastic material, the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Shear Modulus ($G$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Consider a Cube under Pure Shear:**
Apply complementary shear stresses $\tau$ on all four faces of a small elemental cube. This generates principal stresses $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ at $45°$ to the shear plane, with magnitudes:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau \qquad \sigma_2 = -\tau$$

**Step 2 — Apply Generalized Hooke's Law:**
The strains along the principal directions are:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\right]$$

For plane stress with $\sigma_3 = 0$, $\sigma_1 = \tau$, and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau)] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 3 — Compute Shear Strain:**
The shear strain $\gamma$ is the total angular distortion between the original and deformed faces:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = 2\varepsilon_1 = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1 + \nu)$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear:**
By definition, $\tau = G \cdot \gamma$, so:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{2\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $G$:**

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Expression for Modulus of Resilience

The **Modulus of Resilience** ($u_r$) is defined as the strain energy stored per unit volume when a material is loaded up to its elastic limit.

**Step 1 — Strain Energy Density:**
Strain energy per unit volume (strain energy density) is:

$$u = \int_0^{\varepsilon} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hooke's Law:**
Since $\sigma = E\varepsilon$, we have $d\sigma = E\, d\varepsilon$. Substituting:

$$u = \int_0^{\varepsilon_y} (E\varepsilon) \, d\varepsilon = E \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_y^2}{2} = \frac{E \varepsilon_y^2}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express in Terms of Yield Stress:**
Using $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$:

$$u_r = \frac{E}{2}\left(\frac{\sigma_y}{E}\right)^2 = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$

$$\boxed{u_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examination Note:**
> When asked for **total strain energy**, multiply $u_r$ by the volume: $U_r = u_r \cdot V = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} \cdot A \cdot L$. Units: **Joules (N·m)**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Stress-Strain Curve Comparison

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison of Ductile (Mild Steel) vs. Brittle (Cast Iron) Stress-Strain Behaviour**

| Parameter | Ductile Material (Mild Steel) | Brittle Material (Cast Iron) |
|---|---|---|
| Yield Point | Clearly defined | Not clearly defined |
| Plastic Region | Extensive | Negligible / None |
| Fracture Strain | High ($\varepsilon_f \approx 0.25$) | Low ($\varepsilon_f \approx 0.003$) |
| Failure Mode | Necking then fracture | Sudden fracture without warning |
| Energy Absorbed | High (high toughness) | Low |
| Strain Hardening | Present | Absent |
| Design Stress Basis | Yield stress ($\sigma_y$) | Ultimate stress ($\sigma_u$) divided by large FOS |
| Example Applications | Beams, shafts, pressure vessels | Concrete, ceramics, castings |

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Style)

**Problem:**
A steel rod of length 2 m and cross-sectional area 400 mm² supports an axial tensile load of 80 kN. Take $E = 200$ GPa. Calculate:
(a) The stress in the rod.
(b) The total elongation.
(c) The strain energy stored.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \text{ N}}{400 \text{ mm}^2} = 200 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) — Elongation:**
$$\Delta L = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{(80 \times 10^3)(2 \times 10^3)}{(400)(200 \times 10^3)} = \frac{160 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^6} = 2.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Part (c) — Strain Energy:**
$$U = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot V = \frac{(200)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^3} \cdot (400 \times 2000) = 80 \text{ N·m} = 80 \text{ J}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Classification of Mechanical Properties of Materials

The following Mermaid diagram presents a functional architecture of mechanical properties as classified in the KTU 2024 syllabus for PCMRT303.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["MECHANICAL PROPERTIES OF MATERIALS"]:::root
    B["ELASTIC PROPERTIES"]:::branch
    C["PLASTIC PROPERTIES"]:::branch
    D["STRENGTH PROPERTIES"]:::branch
    E["TOUGHNESS AND HARDNESS"]:::branch
    F["TIME DEPENDENT"]:::branch
    G["STRESS STRAIN RELATED"]:::branch
    A --> B
    A --> C
    A --> D
    A --> E
    A --> F
    A --> G
    B --> B1["Stress and Strain"]
    B --> B2["Hookes Law"]
    B --> B3["Modulus of Elasticity E"]
    B --> B4["Poissons Ratio nu"]
    B --> B5["Modulus of Rigidity G"]
    B --> B6["Bulk Modulus K"]
    C --> C1["Yield Stress"]
    C --> C2["Ultimate Stress"]
    C --> C3["Percentage Elongation"]
    C --> C4["Ductility and Brittleness"]
    D --> D1["Yield Strength"]
    D --> D2["Ultimate Tensile Strength"]
    D --> D3["Factor of Safety"]
    E --> E1["Resilience Modulus"]
    E --> E2["Toughness Modulus"]
    E --> E3["Hardness Brinell Vickers Rockwell"]
    F --> F1["Fatigue Failure"]
    F --> F2["Creep Deformation"]
    G --> G1["Engineering Stress Strain Curve"]
    G --> G2["True Stress Strain Curve"]
    G --> G3["Proportional Limit"]
    G --> G4["Elastic Limit"]
    classDef root fill:#1a3a6e,color:#ffffff,stroke:#0d2444,stroke-width:3px
    classDef branch fill:#d4e6f1,color:#1a3a6e,stroke:#1a3a6e,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Key Points on the Stress-Strain Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    O["ORIGIN O"]:::point --> P["PROPORTIONAL LIMIT A"]:::point
    P --> E["ELASTIC LIMIT B"]:::point
    E --> Y["YIELD POINT C UYP AND LYP"]:::point
    Y --> U["ULTIMATE STRESS POINT D"]:::point
    U --> F["FRACTURE POINT E"]:::point
    Y --> R["RESILIENCE AREA O to A to B"]:::highlight
    U --> T["TOUGHNESS AREA O to A to B to C to D"]:::highlight
    classDef point fill:#fff3cd,color:#7a5a00,stroke:#b8860b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef highlight fill:#cce5ff,color:#003366,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Resilience** = Area under $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve **up to elastic limit** (energy stored elastically).
> - **Toughness** = Area under the **entire curve** up to fracture (energy absorbed before failure).
> - **Stiffness** = Slope of linear portion ($E$) — material's resistance to elastic deformation.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Failure Mode Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LOAD["EXTERNAL LOAD APPLIED"]:::start
    EL["BELOW ELASTIC LIMIT"]:::safe
    YLD["BETWEEN YIELD AND ULTIMATE"]:::warn
    FAIL["ABOVE ULTIMATE STRESS"]:::danger
    E1["Material Returns to Original Shape"]:::safe
    E2["Permanent Deformation Begins Plastic Flow"]:::warn
    E3["Necking and Fracture Occur"]:::danger
    LOAD --> EL
    LOAD --> YLD
    LOAD --> FAIL
    EL --> E1
    YLD --> E2
    FAIL --> E3
    classDef start fill:#2c3e50,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,stroke-width:2px
    classDef safe fill:#d4edda,color:#155724,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    classDef warn fill:#fff3cd,color:#856404,stroke:#ffc107,stroke-width:2px
    classDef danger fill:#f8d7da,color:#721c24,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Elastic Constants Inter-Relationship

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E["E YOUNG MODULUS"]:::const
    G["G SHEAR MODULUS"]:::const
    K["K BULK MODULUS"]:::const
    NU["NU POISSONS RATIO"]:::const
    E -->|E equals 2G times 1 plus nu| G
    E -->|E equals 3K times 1 minus 2nu| K
    G -->|G equals E by 2 times 1 plus nu| E
    K -->|K equals E by 3 times 1 minus 2nu| E
    NU -->|nu defines lateral strain ratio| E
    classDef const fill:#e8d8f0,color:#4a148c,stroke:#6a1b9a,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Note on Diagram Usage:**
> In KTU answer sheets, students are expected to draw the **stress-strain diagram** with labelled points (Proportional limit, Elastic limit, Yield point, Ultimate stress, Fracture). The block diagrams above serve as conceptual architecture maps.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP Set A]`
> **Differentiate between ductile and brittle materials with suitable examples. Mention the design stress basis for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Ductile Material | Brittle Material |
|---|---|---|
| Plastic Deformation | Large before fracture | Negligible / None |
| Fracture Type | Cup-and-cone, necking | Sudden, flat fracture |
| Examples | Mild steel, copper, aluminium | Cast iron, concrete, ceramics |
| Design Stress Used | Yield stress ($\sigma_y$) | Ultimate stress ($\sigma_u$) |
| Factor of Safety | Low (1.5 – 2.5) | High (3 – 8) |
| Warning Before Failure | Yes (visible deformation) | No (catastrophic) |

Ductile materials fail gradually with visible deformation (necking), while brittle materials fail suddenly with little to no warning.

**[3 Marks — Complete answer requires table + at least 4 distinguishing points + design stress basis]**

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model QP]`
> **Define the following terms with units: (i) Stress (ii) Strain (iii) Young's Modulus.**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Stress:** Internal resistive force per unit area developed within a material when subjected to external load.
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa), MPa}$$

**(ii) Strain:** Ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension.
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} \quad \text{Units: dimensionless (mm/mm)}$$

**(iii) Young's Modulus:** The slope of the stress-strain curve within the proportional limit, representing the stiffness of the material.
$$E = \frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} \quad \text{Units: N/m}^2 \text{ (Pa), GPa}$$

---

### 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`**

> **Q. (a)** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus ($E$), Shear Modulus ($G$), and Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$) for a homogeneous isotropic material. Mention the typical range of $\nu$ for engineering materials. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A brass rod 1.5 m long, 25 mm diameter, is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN. If $E = 105$ GPa and $\nu = 0.33$, calculate: (i) Stress, (ii) Strain, (iii) Elongation, (iv) Change in diameter. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Consider a small element subjected to pure shear stress $\tau$ on all four faces. This produces principal stresses:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

By generalized Hooke's Law:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)] = \frac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)] = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau - \nu(\tau)] = -\frac{\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

Shear strain $\gamma$ (total angular distortion):

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

Since $\tau = G \gamma$:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{2\tau}{E}(1+\nu)$$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**Range of Poisson's Ratio:** For most engineering metals, $-1 < \nu < 0.5$. For stable isotropic materials, $\nu$ ranges from **0 to 0.5** (with 0.5 being the theoretical upper limit for incompressible materials like rubber).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> Students often forget to **derive** the relationship and simply state the formula. **Marks are awarded for the step-by-step logical derivation**, not just the final equation. Also, **mentioning the range of $\nu$** carries the remaining marks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Length $L = 1.5$ m $= 1500$ mm
- Diameter $d = 25$ mm → $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87$ mm²
- Load $P = 50$ kN $= 50{,}000$ N
- $E = 105$ GPa $= 105 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\nu = 0.33$

**(i) Stress:**
$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{490.87} = 101.86 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Strain:**
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{101.86}{105 \times 10^3} = 9.7 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(iii) Elongation:**
$$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L = (9.7 \times 10^{-4})(1500) = 1.455 \text{ mm}$$

**(iv) Change in Diameter:**
$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -(0.33)(9.7 \times 10^{-4}) = -3.20 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lateral} \cdot d = (-3.20 \times 10^{-4})(25) = -0.008 \text{ mm}$$

Diameter **decreases** by $8 \times 10^{-3}$ mm.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall — Unit Inconsistency:**
> KTU students frequently mix **m and mm** in calculations. If $E$ is in **MPa (N/mm²)**, then $A$ must be in **mm²**, $L$ in **mm**, and $P$ in **N**. Always verify units **before** substituting values.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`**

> **Q. (a)** Draw a fully labelled engineering stress-strain diagram for a ductile material (mild steel) and explain the significance of the following points: Proportional limit, Elastic limit, Yield point, Ultimate stress, Fracture point. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q. (b)** A mild steel specimen tested in a UTM has the following data: Diameter = 20 mm, Gauge length = 50 mm, Load at yield point = 80 kN, Maximum load = 120 kN, Final length after fracture = 65 mm, Final diameter at neck = 14 mm. Calculate: (i) Yield stress, (ii) Ultimate stress, (iii) Percentage elongation, (iv) Percentage reduction in area. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Stress-Strain Diagram & Significance:**

```
Stress (σ)
   ↑
   |        D - Ultimate Stress Point
   |       /|\
   |      / | \
   |     /  |  \  E - Fracture Point
   |    /   |   \
   |   / C -|    \
   |  / (YLD|     \
   | /  PNT)|
   |/______|_______\
   A  B     |        Strain (ε)
   |  |
   Prop. Elastic
   Limit Limit
```

**Significance of Key Points:**

1. **Proportional Limit (A):** The maximum stress at which stress is directly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law is valid). The slope here gives Young's Modulus $E$.

2. **Elastic Limit (B):** The maximum stress up to which the material regains its original shape upon unloading. Beyond this, permanent (plastic) deformation occurs.

3. **Yield Point (C):** The stress at which material begins to deform plastically without any increase in load. Mild steel shows an **Upper Yield Point (UYP)** and **Lower Yield Point (LYP)**. This is the **design stress** for ductile materials.

4. **Ultimate Stress Point (D):** The maximum stress the material can withstand. Beyond this, necking begins, and the apparent stress decreases (engineering stress).

5. **Fracture Point (E):** The stress and strain at which the material actually breaks. The area under the entire curve up to this point represents **toughness**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Mark Distribution Tip:**
> A neat diagram with proper labels (A, B, C, D, E) is mandatory — minimum **3 marks**. Remaining 4 marks come from the explanation of each point with its physical significance.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d_0 = 20$ mm → $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.16$ mm²
- Gauge length $L_0 = 50$ mm
- Load at yield $P_y = 80$ kN
- Maximum load $P_u = 120$ kN
- Final length $L_f = 65$ mm
- Final diameter $d_f = 14$ mm → $A_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(14)^2 = 153.94$ mm²

**(i) Yield Stress:**
$$\sigma_y = \frac{P_y}{A_0} = \frac{80{,}000}{314.16} = 254.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**(ii) Ultimate Tensile Stress:**
$$\sigma_u = \frac{P_u}{A_0} = \frac{120{,}000}{314.16} = 381.97 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Percentage Elongation:**
$$\%EL = \frac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{65 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 30\%$$

**(iv) Percentage Reduction in Area:**
$$\%RA = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{314.16 - 153.94}{314.16} \times 100 = 51\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:**
> Many students compute percentage elongation using the **fracture load**, not the **gauge length difference**. Always use $\%EL = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0} \times 100$, where $\Delta L = L_f - L_0$.
> Also, **never use $A_f$ to compute engineering yield or ultimate stress** — engineering stress uses the **original area $A_0$**. $A_f$ is only for $\%RA$.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 1 — Mechanical Properties of Materials: Quick Revision Checklist**

- **Stress** is force per unit area, **Strain** is deformation per unit length — both foundational to all mechanics problems.
- **Hooke's Law** ($\sigma = E\varepsilon$) is valid only up to the **Proportional Limit**.
- **Young's Modulus $E$** measures **stiffness**, not strength.
- **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$)** is the design basis for ductile materials; **Ultimate Stress ($\sigma_u$)** for brittle materials.
- **Poisson's Ratio $\nu$** has typical range **0 to 0.5** for engineering materials; for steel $\nu \approx 0.3$.
- Key elastic constant relation: $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$.
- **Resilience** = energy absorbed within elastic limit = $\dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$.
- **Toughness** = energy absorbed up to fracture = area under full $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve.
- **Ductility** measures (a) percentage elongation and (b) percentage reduction in area.
- **Hardness tests** (Brinell, Vickers, Rockwell) measure resistance to localized plastic deformation.
- **Fatigue** failure occurs under cyclic loading even below yield stress.
- **Creep** is time-dependent deformation under sustained load at elevated temperatures.
- **Factor of Safety (FOS)** is the ratio of strength to working stress — essential for design safety.
- **Units Mantra:** $\sigma$ in MPa = N/mm²; $E$ in GPa = kN/mm²; strain is dimensionless.
- **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Module 1 typically carries a 14-mark question testing either (i) stress-strain curve interpretation OR (ii) numerical on deformation / elastic constants.
- Always draw a **neat, labelled diagram** for stress-strain questions — minimum **3 marks** are reserved for it.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Stress

In the discipline of **Mechanics of Solids**, **stress** is formally defined as the **internal resistive force per unit area** developed within a deformable body when an external load is applied to it. Mathematically, it is expressed as the ratio of the internal force acting on an infinitesimal area to that area as the area approaches zero.

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Normal stress (N/m² or Pascal)
- $\Delta F$ = Internal force (N)
- $\Delta A$ = Area over which force acts (m²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** Stress is a *tensor quantity* (not a vector) characterized by 9 components in 3D space, though its magnitude in a specific direction is a vector. The SI unit is **N/m² = Pascal (Pa)**, with practical units being **MPa (10⁶ Pa)** or **GPa (10⁹ Pa)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a rubber band stretched between your two hands. The rubber band is not a rigid object — it *deforms* and pulls back against your hands. That "pulling back" force, distributed across the microscopic cross-section of the rubber, **is** stress. You cannot see stress directly; you see its *effects* (stretching, shortening, or twisting).

**Real-world analogy:** Think of stress as the *crowd pressure* in a packed metro train during rush hour. The force each person exerts is spread across their body's contact area with others. More people (more force) in the same small space (less area) = *higher pressure/stress*. If the "area" of contact gets smaller while force remains the same, the stress (intensity) shoots up.

### 1.3 Classification of Stress

Stress is broadly classified into two fundamental types:

| Type | Direction of Force | Symbol | Common Examples |
|------|-------------------|--------|-----------------|
| **Normal Stress** | Perpendicular to surface | $\sigma$ | Tension in cable, Column compression |
| **Shear Stress** | Parallel to surface | $\tau$ | Riveted joints, Bolts under torsion |
| **Bearing Stress** | Contact (compressive) | $\sigma_b$ | Bolt bearing on plate |

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):**
> - **Tensile stress (pulling apart)** → **Positive (+)**
> - **Compressive stress (pushing together)** → **Negative (−)**
> - **Shear stress** → Sign depends on rotation tendency (positive if it tends to rotate element clockwise)

### 1.4 Why "Per Unit Area" Matters

Force alone does not describe internal intensity. A thin steel wire and a thick steel rope can carry the *same axial force*, but the stress in the thin wire is *much higher* because its cross-sectional area is smaller. This is why **mechanical failure is governed by stress, not force**.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is why a small nail can support a heavy picture (large contact stress in wall, but small in nail shank) — but a paper clip breaks easily when bent (small area, high stress concentration).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Normal Stress (Uniaxial State)

When a uniform axial force $P$ is applied along the centroidal axis of a prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$, the **average normal stress** is:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

- If $P$ pulls the bar → **Tensile stress** ($\sigma > 0$)
- If $P$ pushes the bar → **Compressive stress** ($\sigma < 0$)

**Uniformity Assumption:** This formula is valid *only* when the stress is *uniformly distributed* over the cross-section. This occurs away from points of load application (Saint-Venant's principle states the disturbance localizes within a distance equal to the largest cross-sectional dimension).

### 2.2 Shear Stress

When a force acts **parallel** to a surface, it creates **shear stress**:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Shear (transverse) force (N)
- $A$ = Area parallel to the force (m²)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress (Pa)

**Common KTU application:** A bolted connection where a force tries to slice the bolt across its cross-section.

### 2.3 Bearing Stress

**Bearing stress** is the *contact compressive stress* between two bodies pressed together, e.g., a bolt head pressing against a plate.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b}$$

Where $A_b$ is the *projected contact area* (e.g., for a bolt of diameter $d$ on a plate: $A_b = d \cdot t$, where $t$ is plate thickness).

### 2.4 Stress on an Oblique Plane (Critical KTU Concept)

Consider a uniaxial member under axial force $P$. The cross-sectional area perpendicular to the axis is $A$. If we cut the member at an angle $\theta$ from the perpendicular cross-section, the new area is:

$$A_\theta = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}$$

The force $P$ can be resolved into:
- **Normal component:** $P_n = P \cos\theta$
- **Shear component:** $P_s = P \sin\theta$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P_n}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \cos\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A} \cos^2\theta$$

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P_s}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \sin\theta}{A / \cos\theta} = \frac{P}{A} \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{P}{2A} \sin 2\theta$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Values (Key KTU Result):**
> - $\sigma_\theta$ is **maximum** when $\theta = 0°$ → $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{P}{A}$ (normal cross-section)
> - $\tau_\theta$ is **maximum** when $\theta = 45°$ → $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{P}{2A}$
> - This is why **ductile materials fail along 45° planes** in tension tests (maximum shear plane).

### 2.5 The Concept of Stress at a Point

In reality, stress is rarely uniform. We must define stress at a *mathematical point* using a limiting process. Consider a small area $\Delta A$ with internal force $\Delta F$:

$$\sigma = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

This gives the **stress vector** at a point on a given plane. To fully describe the stress state at a point, we need stress on **three mutually perpendicular planes** — this is the foundation of the **stress tensor** (covered in Module 2).

### 2.6 Units & Conversion

| Quantity | SI Unit | Practical Unit | Conversion |
|----------|---------|----------------|------------|
| Stress | N/m² = Pa | MPa, GPa | 1 MPa = 10⁶ Pa = 1 N/mm² |
| Force | N | kN | 1 kN = 10³ N |
| Area | m² | mm² | 1 m² = 10⁶ mm² |
| Stress (English) | psi | ksi | 1 ksi = 1000 psi |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\sigma = P/A$ | Average normal stress | Pa |
| 2 | $\tau = V/A$ | Average shear stress | Pa |
| 3 | $\sigma_b = P/A_b$ | Bearing stress | Pa |
| 4 | $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$ | Normal stress on inclined plane | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_\theta = \sigma_x \sin\theta \cos\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin 2\theta$ | Shear stress on inclined plane | Pa |
| 6 | $\sigma_{max} = P/A$ at $\theta = 0°$ | Max normal stress | Pa |
| 7 | $\tau_{max} = P/(2A)$ at $\theta = 45°$ | Max shear stress | Pa |
| 8 | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L$ (related strain) | Strain (companion concept) | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL NOTATION RULE:** Always use **$\sigma$ for normal** and **$\tau$ for shear** stress. KTU examiners instantly mark wrong symbols as errors.

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Stress analysis in bridges, columns, and beams to prevent failure.
- **Mechanical Engineering:** Design of pressure vessels, shafts, gears, and bolts.
- **Aerospace:** Stress in aircraft wings (combined bending + shear + torsion).
- **Biomedical:** Stress in bone implants and prosthetics.
- **Material Selection:** Engineers choose materials with yield stress > applied working stress × factor of safety.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stress on an Inclined Plane (Uniaxial Loading)

**Problem Setup:** A prismatic bar of cross-sectional area $A$ is subjected to an axial tensile force $P$ along its longitudinal axis. We want to find the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at angle $\theta$ to the cross-section.

**Step 1: Define the Inclined Plane Geometry**

Consider a section cut through the bar at an angle $\theta$ measured from the **perpendicular cross-section**.

The original cross-sectional area is $A$. The inclined cut creates a new surface. If the perpendicular cut has width $b$ and the inclined cut has length $b'$, then:

$$b' = \frac{b}{\cos\theta}$$

Therefore, the inclined area is:

$$A_\theta = A \cdot b' / b = \frac{A}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 2: Resolve the Force $P$ into Components**

The axial force $P$ is resolved into two components relative to the inclined plane:

- Component **normal (perpendicular)** to the inclined surface:
$$P_n = P \cos\theta$$

- Component **shear (parallel)** to the inclined surface:
$$P_s = P \sin\theta$$

**Step 3: Apply Stress Definitions**

The normal stress on the inclined plane is the normal force divided by the inclined area:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P_n}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \cos\theta}{A / \cos\theta}$$

Cancel and simplify:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{P}{A} \cos^2\theta$$

Let $\sigma_x = P/A$ (the normal stress on the perpendicular cross-section). Therefore:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$$

**Step 4: Shear Stress Derivation**

The shear stress on the inclined plane is the tangential force divided by the inclined area:

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P_s}{A_\theta} = \frac{P \sin\theta}{A / \cos\theta}$$

Cancel and simplify:

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P}{A} \sin\theta \cos\theta$$

Apply the trigonometric identity $2\sin\theta\cos\theta = \sin 2\theta$:

$$\tau_\theta = \frac{P}{2A} \sin 2\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin 2\theta$$

**Step 5: Identify Maximum Values**

To find the maximum normal stress, differentiate $\sigma_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$:

$$\frac{d\sigma_\theta}{d\theta} = -2\sigma_x \cos\theta \sin\theta = 0$$

This gives $\theta = 0°$ or $\theta = 90°$. At $\theta = 0°$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_x = \frac{P}{A}$$

To find the maximum shear stress, differentiate $\tau_\theta$:

$$\frac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = \sigma_x \cos 2\theta = 0$$

This gives $2\theta = 90°$ → $\theta = 45°$. Substituting back:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin 90° = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} = \frac{P}{2A}$$

**Step 6: Complementary Relationship**

Add the squares of the stresses:

$$\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \sigma_x^2 \cos^4\theta + \sigma_x^2 \sin^2\theta \cos^2\theta$$

Factor out $\sigma_x^2 \cos^2\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \sigma_x^2 \cos^2\theta (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta)$$

Using $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$:

$$\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \sigma_x^2 \cos^2\theta = \sigma_\theta \cdot \sigma_x$$

This is the **stress circle relationship** (Mohr's circle foundation) — verified.

### 3.2 Derivation: Shear Stress in Bolted Connection (Double Shear)

**Problem Setup:** A lap joint connects two plates using a bolt. A tensile force $P$ is applied. The bolt has diameter $d$. Determine the shear stress in the bolt.

**Step 1: Identify Shear Planes**

In a **single shear** configuration, the bolt has *one* shear plane:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{A_{bolt}} = \frac{P}{\frac{\pi d^2}{4}} = \frac{4P}{\pi d^2}$$

In a **double shear** configuration, the bolt has *two* shear planes, so each plane carries $P/2$:

$$\tau = \frac{P/2}{A_{bolt}} = \frac{2P}{\pi d^2}$$

**Step 2: Bearing Stress on Plates**

The bolt bears on the plate over a projected area. For plate thickness $t$:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{d \cdot t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Tip:** Always identify *single* vs *double* shear in bolted/riveted joint problems. Misidentifying this is a common 2-mark deduction.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Stress in Different Loading Scenarios

| Loading Type | Primary Stress | Formula | Direction |
|--------------|----------------|---------|-----------|
| Axial Tension | Normal | $\sigma = P/A$ | Along axis |
| Axial Compression | Normal (compressive) | $\sigma = -P/A$ | Along axis |
| Pure Shear | Shear | $\tau = V/A$ | Parallel to surface |
| Bearing | Compressive (contact) | $\sigma_b = P/(d \cdot t)$ | Normal to contact |
| Hydrostatic Pressure | Normal (compressive) | $\sigma = -p$ | All directions |
| Inclined Cut | Normal + Shear | $\sigma_\theta, \tau_\theta$ formulas | Depends on $\theta$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: Stress Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied] --> B[Identify Cross Section]
    B --> C[Resolve Force Components]
    C --> D{Force Direction?}
    D -->|Perpendicular| E[Normal Stress sigma = P/A]
    D -->|Parallel| F[Shear Stress tau = V/A]
    D -->|Contact| G[Bearing Stress sigma_b = P/A_b]
    E --> H[Check Uniformity]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I[Compute Stress Intensity]
    I --> J[Compare with Allowable Stress]
    J --> K{Within Limit?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Design Safe]
    K -->|No| M[Redesign / Increase Area]
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Stress on Inclined Plane

```mermaid
graph LR
    P0[Axial Force P] --> P1[Resolve into Components]
    P1 --> Pn[Normal Force P cos theta]
    P1 --> Ps[Shear Force P sin theta]
    Pn --> A1[Inclined Area A / cos theta]
    Ps --> A1
    A1 --> S1[Normal Stress sigma_theta]
    A1 --> S2[Shear Stress tau_theta]
    S1 --> R1[Maximum at theta = 0 deg]
    S2 --> R2[Maximum at theta = 45 deg]
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Bolted Joint Shear Configurations

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph SS[Single Shear]
        SS1[Plate 1] --> SSB[Bolt]
        SSB --> SS2[Plate 2]
    end
    subgraph DS[Double Shear]
        DS1[Plate 1] --> DSB[Bolt]
        DSB --> DSM[Middle Plate]
        DSM --> DS2[Plate 3]
    end
```

### 4.4 Functional Architecture: Stress State at a Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Point in Solid Body] --> B[Define Three Mutually Perpendicular Planes]
    B --> C[Plane 1 X]
    B --> D[Plane 2 Y]
    B --> E[Plane 3 Z]
    C --> F[Stress Vector Components]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[3 Normal Stresses]
    F --> H[6 Shear Stresses]
    G --> I[Complete Stress State at Point]
    H --> I
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** These flow diagrams represent the analytical workflow a KTU student must follow when solving any stress problem. Memorize this as a problem-solving template.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define **normal stress** and **shear stress**. State the SI unit of stress and explain the sign convention used in Mechanics of Solids.

**Model Answer:**

**Normal Stress:** The internal resistive force per unit area acting *perpendicular* to a cross-section is called normal stress. It is denoted by $\sigma$ (sigma).

$$\sigma = \frac{P_n}{A}$$

**Shear Stress:** The internal resistive force per unit area acting *parallel* (tangential) to a cross-section is called shear stress. It is denoted by $\tau$ (tau).

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$

**SI Unit:** The SI unit of stress is **N/m²** or **Pascal (Pa)**. Practical units are **MPa** and **GPa**.

**Sign Convention:**
- **Tensile normal stress** (pulling apart) → **Positive**
- **Compressive normal stress** (pushing together) → **Negative**
- **Shear stress** is positive when it produces **clockwise rotation** of the element.

> [!TIP]
> **Valuation Key:** KTU expects you to mention **tensile = positive** and **compressive = negative** for full marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** A circular bar of diameter **20 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **50 kN**. Calculate the average normal stress developed in the bar.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 20$ mm $= 0.020$ m
- Axial tensile load $P = 50$ kN $= 50 \times 10^3$ N

**Step 1: Calculate Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.020)^2}{4}$$

$$A = \frac{\pi \times 0.0004}{4} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Calculate Normal Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma = 159.15 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 159.15 \text{ MPa}$$

**Result:** The average normal stress in the bar is **159.15 MPa (Tensile)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Conversion Trap:** KTU often gives diameter in mm and force in kN. Always convert to consistent units (N and m²) OR use MPa with N and mm² (since 1 N/mm² = 1 MPa).

---

### Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** A solid steel rod of **diameter 30 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **120 kN**.

**(a)** Determine the average normal stress and the average shear stress on a plane inclined at **30°** to the cross-section. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Find the orientation of the plane on which the **normal stress is maximum** and the plane on which the **shear stress is maximum**. Compute these maximum stress values. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Diameter $d = 30$ mm = 0.030 m
- Axial tensile load $P = 120$ kN = $120 \times 10^3$ N
- Inclination $\theta = 30°$

**Step 1: Compute Cross-Sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (30)^2}{4} = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Compute Normal Stress on Perpendicular Section**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{120 \times 10^3}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 169.77 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 169.77 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Normal stress on plane at 30°:**

$$\sigma_{30°} = \sigma_x \cos^2(30°) = 169.77 \times (0.866)^2$$

$$\sigma_{30°} = 169.77 \times 0.75 = 127.33 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}$$

**Shear stress on plane at 30°:**

$$\tau_{30°} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin(2 \times 30°) = \frac{169.77}{2} \sin(60°)$$

$$\tau_{30°} = 84.88 \times 0.8660 = 73.51 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (a) Final Answers:**
- Normal stress: **127.33 MPa (Tensile)**
- Shear stress: **73.51 MPa**

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Maximum Normal Stress:**

The normal stress $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$ is maximum when $\cos^2\theta$ is maximum, i.e., when $\theta = 0°$.

$$\sigma_{max} = \sigma_x = 169.77 \text{ MPa}$$

This occurs on the **perpendicular cross-section** ($\theta = 0°$).

**Maximum Shear Stress:**

The shear stress $\tau_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin 2\theta$ is maximum when $\sin 2\theta = 1$, i.e., when $2\theta = 90°$ → $\theta = 45°$.

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} = \frac{169.77}{2} = 84.88 \text{ MPa}$$

This occurs on the plane inclined at **45°** to the cross-section.

**Part (b) Final Answers:**
- $\sigma_{max} = 169.77$ MPa at $\theta = 0°$
- $\tau_{max} = 84.88$ MPa at $\theta = 45°$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse the angle of the *plane* with the angle of the *normal to the plane*. KTU convention: $\theta$ is measured from the **perpendicular cross-section** to the inclined plane. Drawing a clear free-body diagram before solving is worth 1 mark and saves you from sign errors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** A mild steel bar of cross-section **40 mm × 20 mm** is used as a tie member in a truss. It carries an axial pull of **80 kN**.

**(a)** Calculate the **normal stress**, **shear stress on a 30° inclined plane**, and verify the relation $\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \sigma_x \sigma_\theta$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bolt of **diameter 16 mm** is used in a **double-shear** lap joint. Find the **shear stress in the bolt** and the **bearing stress** if the plate thickness is **12 mm** under a load of **48 kN**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given for Part (a):**
- Cross-section: 40 mm × 20 mm
- Axial pull $P = 80$ kN
- Inclination $\theta = 30°$

**Step 1: Compute Area**

$$A = 40 \times 20 = 800 \text{ mm}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2: Normal Stress on Perpendicular Section**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{80 \times 10^3}{8 \times 10^{-4}} = 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ MPa}$$

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Normal stress on 30° plane:**

$$\sigma_{30°} = \sigma_x \cos^2(30°) = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ MPa (Tensile)}$$

**Shear stress on 30° plane:**

$$\tau_{30°} = \frac{\sigma_x}{2} \sin(60°) = \frac{100}{2} \times 0.866 = 43.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification of relation:**

$$\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = (75)^2 + (43.30)^2 = 5625 + 1874.89 = 7499.89 \approx 7500$$

$$\sigma_x \cdot \sigma_\theta = 100 \times 75 = 7500$$

**LHS ≈ RHS ✓** (Relation verified.)

**Part (a) Final Answers:**
- Normal stress: **75 MPa (Tensile)**
- Shear stress: **43.30 MPa**
- Relation: **Verified**

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Given:**
- Bolt diameter $d = 16$ mm
- Plate thickness $t = 12$ mm
- Load $P = 48$ kN = $48 \times 10^3$ N
- Configuration: **Double shear**

**Step 1: Shear Stress in Bolt (Double Shear)**

In double shear, the load is shared equally by two shear planes. Force per shear plane = $P/2$.

Area of bolt cross-section:

$$A_{bolt} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (16)^2}{4} = 201.06 \text{ mm}^2$$

Shear stress:

$$\tau = \frac{P/2}{A_{bolt}} = \frac{48 \times 10^3 / 2}{201.06}$$

$$\tau = \frac{24 \times 10^3}{201.06} = 119.37 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Bearing Stress on Plate**

Bearing area is the projected area of bolt on plate:

$$A_b = d \times t = 16 \times 12 = 192 \text{ mm}^2$$

Bearing stress:

$$\sigma_b = \frac{P}{A_b} = \frac{48 \times 10^3}{192} = 250 \text{ MPa}$$

**Part (b) Final Answers:**
- Shear stress in bolt: **119.37 MPa**
- Bearing stress on plate: **250 MPa**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Single vs Double Shear:** Many students forget to divide $P$ by 2 for double shear. This loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Bearing Area:** Use **projected area** ($d \times t$), NOT curved surface area. This is a common error.
> 3. **Unit Consistency:** KTU deducts marks if you mix N/mm² and MPa incorrectly. Remember: **1 N/mm² = 1 MPa**.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 - Stress:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Stress = Internal force per unit area (tensor quantity, not a vector).
- ✅ **SI Unit:** N/m² = Pa. **Practical:** MPa, GPa.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Tension = +, Compression = −.
- ✅ **Two Main Types:** Normal ($\sigma$) and Shear ($\tau$).
- ✅ **Bearing Stress:** Contact compressive stress, $A_b = d \times t$.
- ✅ **Inclined Plane Formulas:**
  - $\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta$
  - $\tau_\theta = (\sigma_x / 2) \sin 2\theta$
- ✅ **Maximum Values:**
  - $\sigma_{max} = P/A$ at $\theta = 0°$ (perpendicular cut)
  - $\tau_{max} = P/(2A)$ at $\theta = 45°$ (45° inclined cut)
- ✅ **Double Shear:** Load is divided by 2 across two shear planes.
- ✅ **Bearing Area:** Always use **projected area**, not curved/contact area.
- ✅ **Mohr's Circle Foundation:** $\sigma_\theta^2 + \tau_\theta^2 = \sigma_x \sigma_\theta$ — verified in Q2.
- ✅ **Engineering Significance:** Stress (not force) governs material failure — explains why thin wires break before thick ropes.
- ✅ **45° Failure Plane:** Ductile materials in tension fail along the plane of maximum shear stress (45°).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Strain** is a dimensionless measure of deformation representing the relative displacement between particles in a body. Mathematically, it is defined as the **ratio of change in dimension to the original dimension** of the body. Since it is a ratio of two like quantities (length/length), strain is a **dimensionless quantity** (no units), though it is frequently expressed in **mm/m, μm/m, microstrain (με), or strain (in/in)** in experimental contexts.

Strain is the kinematic counterpart to stress. While stress quantifies internal **force intensity** (a *cause*), strain quantifies the resulting **geometric distortion** (an *effect*). Under the assumption of **small deformations** (infinitesimal strain theory, valid for most engineering materials within the elastic limit), strain is treated as a small quantity, and products of strain terms are neglected.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the rubber band and the sponge:**
> Imagine a spring held between your hands. When you pull, the spring stretches — every small segment of the spring elongates relative to its original length. Now stretch two segments, one twice as long as the other. The longer segment *changes* more in absolute length, but the **fractional change** (the strain) can be the same. Strain essentially measures "how much each tiny bit of the material has been squeezed, stretched, or twisted **per unit of its original size**." This per-unit normalization is what makes strain a universal measure of deformation — independent of how big or small the original object is.

## 1.3 Classification of Strain (Primary Types)

Strain in three-dimensional solids is broadly classified as follows:

1. **Normal (Linear) Strain** — deformation along a single axis (tensile or compressive).
2. **Shear (Angular) Strain** — change in angle between two originally perpendicular line elements.
3. **Volumetric (Dilatational) Strain** — change in volume per unit original volume.
4. **Principal Strains** — normal strains along directions where shear strain vanishes.
5. **Natural / Logarithmic Strain** — true strain used in large deformation analysis.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Focus:** Module 1 of PCMRT303 typically introduces **1-D, 2-D (plane), and 3-D strain** with emphasis on small-strain analysis, principal strains (2-D), and Mohr's circle for strain.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 1-D Normal Strain (Uniaxial State)

Consider a straight bar of original length $L$. Under an axial load, its length changes to $L + \delta L$. The **engineering (Cauchy) normal strain** along the axis is:

- **Tensile strain** → bar elongates → $\delta L > 0$ → $\varepsilon > 0$
- **Compressive strain** → bar shortens → $\delta L < 0$ → $\varepsilon < 0$

The longitudinal strain in a uniaxially loaded bar relates to lateral strain through **Poisson's ratio** $\nu$:

$$\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \, \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$$

## 2.2 2-D Strain State (Plane Strain / Plane Stress Analogy)

For a 2-D element, the strain state is fully described by **three independent components**: $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, and $\gamma_{xy}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why three?** The symmetric strain tensor in 2-D has three independent entries out of four (the off-diagonal shear components are equal: $\gamma_{xy} = \gamma_{yx}$).

**Volumetric strain in 2-D** (change in area per unit area):

$$\varepsilon_v^{(2D)} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y$$

## 2.3 3-D Strain State

A generic 3-D strain tensor has **six independent components** (three normal + three shear):

$$\varepsilon_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x & \gamma_{xy}/2 & \gamma_{xz}/2 \\ \gamma_{yx}/2 & \varepsilon_y & \gamma_{yz}/2 \\ \gamma_{zx}/2 & \gamma_{zy}/2 & \varepsilon_z \end{bmatrix}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{xy}$ is the **total change in angle** (in radians) between two originally perpendicular line elements, and it equals **twice** the tensorial shear component $\varepsilon_{xy}$. The factor of 2 is a convention difference — keep this in mind when comparing with tensor notation.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | Normal (linear) strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Change in length per unit original length | dimensionless |
| 2 | Lateral strain (uniaxial) | $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \, \varepsilon_{\text{ax}}$ | Lateral contraction under axial loading | dimensionless |
| 3 | Shear strain | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$ | Change in right angle (radians) | rad (dimensionless) |
| 4 | Tensorial shear strain | $\varepsilon_{xy} = \gamma_{xy}/2$ | Half the engineering shear | dimensionless |
| 5 | Volumetric strain (3-D) | $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ | Sum of normal strains | dimensionless |
| 6 | Principal strain (2-D) | $\varepsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ | Normal strain on principal planes | dimensionless |
| 7 | Principal angle | $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}$ | Orientation of principal axes | rad / deg |
| 8 | Maximum shear strain | $\gamma_{\max} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ | (2-D) difference of principal strains | dimensionless |
| 9 | Natural (log) strain | $\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \displaystyle\int_{L_0}^{L} \dfrac{dL}{L} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{L}{L_0}\right)$ | True strain for large deformations | dimensionless |
| 10 | Mohr's strain radius | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$ | Radius of Mohr's circle | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In the **principal strain** formula, the shear term is $\left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2$, **not** $\gamma_{xy}^2$. Forgetting the factor of 2 will produce wrong answers. Memorize the **half-shears** correctly.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Strain measurement is the backbone of **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)**. **Strain gauges** (foil, semiconductor, fiber-Bragg gratings) bonded to bridges, aircraft wings, pressure vessels, and turbine blades convert microscopic deformations into electrical signals. Engineers use these signals to validate finite element models, detect fatigue cracks, and ensure that operational loads stay within safe strain limits (typically yield strain of the material).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Average Normal Strain in a Bar

**Problem setup:** A bar of original length $L_0$ is stretched axially. A point at distance $x$ from the left end displaces by $u(x)$.

**Step 1:** Original length between two points $A$ and $B$:

$$L_0 = x_B - x_A$$

**Step 2:** After deformation, the new positions are $x_A + u_A$ and $x_B + u_B$. The new length becomes:

$$L_f = (x_B + u_B) - (x_A + u_A) = L_0 + (u_B - u_A)$$

**Step 3:** The change in length:

$$\delta L = L_f - L_0 = u_B - u_A$$

**Step 4:** The **average normal strain** over the segment:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\delta L}{L_0} = \frac{u_B - u_A}{x_B - x_A} \\
&= \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta x}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Taking the limit as $\Delta x \rightarrow 0$, the strain at a point becomes the **derivative of displacement**:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{\Delta u}{\Delta x} = \frac{du}{dx}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Strain is the **spatial rate of change** of displacement. If displacement is uniform, the material is just translating as a rigid body — strain is **zero**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Shear Strain from Geometry

**Problem setup:** Two line elements originally along the $x$- and $y$-axes, meeting at right angles ($\pi/2$ rad). After deformation, the angle between them becomes $\theta'$.

**Step 1:** Engineering shear strain is defined as the **decrease in angle**:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$

**Step 2:** Using small-angle approximation (valid for small deformations), for a point $(dx, 0)$ that displaces vertically by $v$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan \psi &\approx \psi = \frac{v}{dx} = \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \\
\Rightarrow \gamma_{xy} &= \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** In tensorial notation:

$$\varepsilon_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\gamma_{xy} = \frac{1}{2}\!\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Symmetry insight:** The strain tensor is symmetric ($\varepsilon_{ij} = \varepsilon_{ji}$) because it is derived from a **scalar displacement field** $u_i$ via a second-order partial derivative that is itself symmetric: $\dfrac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \, \partial x_k} = \dfrac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_k \, \partial x_j}$.

## 3.3 Derivation: Principal Strains (2-D) from Eigenvalue Analysis

**Problem setup:** A 2-D strain state $(\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \gamma_{xy})$ at a point. We seek orientations where shear strain vanishes.

**Step 1:** Strain transformation equation for normal strain at angle $\theta$ from $x$-axis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x'} &= \varepsilon_x \cos^2\theta + \varepsilon_y \sin^2\theta + \gamma_{xy} \sin\theta \cos\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Using double-angle identities ($\cos^2\theta = \frac{1+\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $\sin^2\theta = \frac{1-\cos 2\theta}{2}$, $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x'} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} + \frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\cos 2\theta + \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\sin 2\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** To find **principal strain**, set $\dfrac{d\varepsilon_{x'}}{d\theta} = 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{d\varepsilon_{x'}}{d\theta} &= -(\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y)\sin 2\theta + \gamma_{xy} \cos 2\theta = 0 \\
\Rightarrow \tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting back into the transformation equation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{1,2} = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** Sum of principal strains equals sum of original normal strains (strain invariant): $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y$. This is a useful check in numerical problems.

## 3.4 Derivation: Volumetric Strain in 3-D

**Problem setup:** A rectangular parallelepiped of sides $dx, dy, dz$ deforms with normal strains $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$.

**Step 1:** Original volume:

$$V_0 = dx \cdot dy \cdot dz$$

**Step 2:** New side lengths (assuming small strains and neglecting product terms):

$$
\begin{aligned}
dx' &= dx(1 + \varepsilon_x) \\
dy' &= dy(1 + \varepsilon_y) \\
dz' &= dz(1 + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** New volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &= dx(1+\varepsilon_x) \cdot dy(1+\varepsilon_y) \cdot dz(1+\varepsilon_z) \\
&= V_0 (1+\varepsilon_x)(1+\varepsilon_y)(1+\varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Expanding and neglecting **second-order** strain products ($\varepsilon_i \varepsilon_j \approx 0$ for small strains):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_f &\approx V_0 \left[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z + \underbrace{\varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \cdots}_{\approx 0}\right] \\
&\approx V_0 (1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** Volumetric strain:

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{V_f - V_0}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z}
$$

## 3.5 Worked Example: Principal Strain in a Bracket

**Given:** Strain rosette readings on a steel bracket give:
- $\varepsilon_x = 400 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\varepsilon_y = -200 \times 10^{-6}$
- $\gamma_{xy} = 600 \times 10^{-6}$

**Find:** Principal strains and orientation.

**Step 1:** Center of Mohr's circle:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{(400 - 200) \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 100 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 2:** Radius:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(\frac{600 \times 10^{-6}}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{600 \times 10^{-6}}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(300)^2 + (300)^2} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 300\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} \approx 424.26 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Principal strains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= C + R = (100 + 424.26) \times 10^{-6} = 524.26 \times 10^{-6} \\
\varepsilon_2 &= C - R = (100 - 424.26) \times 10^{-6} = -324.26 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Principal angle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y} = \frac{600 \times 10^{-6}}{600 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \\
2\theta_p &= 45^\circ \Rightarrow \theta_p = 22.5^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Check:** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = 524.26 \times 10^{-6} + (-324.26 \times 10^{-6}) = 200 \times 10^{-6} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y$ ✓ Invariant preserved.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram: Strain Types on a 2-D Element

The following Mermaid block diagrams the **interrelationships between different strain components** in a continuum mechanics framework — a Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow suitable for a complex physical topic.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Strain Tensor in 3D Solid"] --> B["Normal Strain Components"]
    A --> C["Shear Strain Components"]
    A --> D["Volumetric Strain"]

    B --> B1["epsilon x along x axis"]
    B --> B2["epsilon y along y axis"]
    B --> B3["epsilon z along z axis"]

    C --> C1["gamma xy in xy plane"]
    C --> C2["gamma yz in yz plane"]
    C --> C3["gamma xz in xz plane"]

    D --> D1["eps_v equals eps_x + eps_y + eps_z"]
    D --> D2["Hydrostatic component of strain"]

    B1 --> E["Principal Strains via Eigen Decomposition"]
    B2 --> E
    C1 --> E
    E --> E1["eps_1 maximum normal strain"]
    E --> E2["eps_2 intermediate normal strain"]
    E --> E3["eps_3 minimum normal strain"]

    C1 --> F["Mohrs Circle for Strain"]
    B1 --> F
    B2 --> F
    F --> F1["Center C equals eps_x plus eps_y over 2"]
    F --> F2["Radius R function of half shear and half normal diff"]
    F --> F3["Principal point on circle"]

    B1 --> G["Compatibility Conditions"]
    B2 --> G
    B3 --> G
    C1 --> G
    C2 --> G
    C3 --> G
    G --> G1["Ensure single valued continuous displacement field"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram: Mohr's Circle for Strain (Functional Architecture)

The diagram below maps the **analytical workflow** for solving a 2-D strain problem using Mohr's circle.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1 Input eps_x eps_y gamma_xy"] --> S2["Step 2 Compute Center C and Radius R"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3 Plot point X on horizontal axis"]
    S2 --> S4["Step 3 Plot point Y on horizontal axis"]
    S3 --> S5["Step 4 Draw circle center C radius R"]
    S4 --> S5
    S5 --> S6["Step 5 Principal strains at horizontal axis crossings"]
    S5 --> S7["Step 6 Max shear strain at top and bottom of circle"]
    S5 --> S8["Step 7 Read principal angle 2 theta from circle"]
    S6 --> OUT["Output eps_1 eps_2 principal directions"]
    S7 --> OUT
    S8 --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Mohr's circle for strain is **structurally identical** to Mohr's circle for stress, except the **vertical axis uses shear strain $\gamma/2$ (not $\tau$)**. Always plot the point $X$ at $(\varepsilon_x, +\gamma_{xy}/2)$ and $Y$ at $(\varepsilon_y, -\gamma_{xy}/2)$ — note the **opposite sign convention** for the second point compared to stress Mohr's circle.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define normal strain and shear strain. State their units and typical magnitudes in engineering materials.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Normal strain** is the ratio of change in length to the original length of a line element:
> $$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L_0}$$
> It is a **dimensionless** quantity, commonly expressed in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$). For steel at yield, $\varepsilon_{\text{yield}} \approx 1000$ to $2000 \, \mu\varepsilon$.
>
> **Shear strain** $\gamma$ is the change (in radians) between two originally perpendicular line elements:
> $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\pi}{2} - \theta'$$
> Also dimensionless, with typical elastic values $\gamma \approx 500$ to $1500 \, \mu\text{rad}$.

*(3 marks: 1 mark definition, 1 mark units/dimensionlessness, 1 mark typical magnitudes)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Supplied]**
**State the relationship between volumetric strain and the three normal strains in a 3-D body. Why is it valid only for small strains?**

**Model Answer:**

The volumetric strain is the **sum of the three normal strains**:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

**Reason for small-strain limitation:** The derivation assumes the product terms $\varepsilon_i \varepsilon_j$ are negligible compared to individual strains. This holds only when $|\varepsilon_i| \ll 1$ (typically $|\varepsilon| < 0.01$, i.e., < 1% strain). For large deformations, true (logarithmic) strain must be used.

*(3 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark correct summation, 1 mark small-strain justification)*

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** A point in a 2-D strained body has strain components $\varepsilon_x = 300 \times 10^{-6}$, $\varepsilon_y = -100 \times 10^{-6}$, and $\gamma_{xy} = 400 \times 10^{-6}$. Determine:
(i) The principal strains at the point. **(4 marks)**
(ii) The orientation of the principal planes. **(3 marks)**

**(b)** For the same point, determine the **maximum in-plane shear strain** and the orientation on which it acts. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a) Part (i) and (ii):**

**Step 1 — Center and radius of Mohr's circle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \frac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} = \frac{(300 - 100) \times 10^{-6}}{2} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{\left(\frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{2}\right)^2} \\
&= \sqrt{(200)^2 + (200)^2} \times 10^{-6} \\
&= 200\sqrt{2} \times 10^{-6} \approx 282.84 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Principal strains:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_1 &= C + R = (100 + 282.84) \times 10^{-6} \approx 382.84 \times 10^{-6} \\
\varepsilon_2 &= C - R = (100 - 282.84) \times 10^{-6} \approx -182.84 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Principal angle:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan 2\theta_p &= \frac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y} = \frac{400 \times 10^{-6}}{400 \times 10^{-6}} = 1 \\
2\theta_p &= 45^\circ \Rightarrow \theta_{p1} = 22.5^\circ \text{ (for } \varepsilon_1\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

The second principal direction is at $\theta_{p2} = 22.5^\circ + 90^\circ = 112.5^\circ$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Step 1 — Maximum in-plane shear strain:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\gamma_{\max,\text{in-plane}} &= \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = 2R \\
&= 2 \times 282.84 \times 10^{-6} = 565.69 \times 10^{-6}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Orientation of maximum shear:**

Maximum shear planes are oriented at $45^\circ$ from the principal planes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_{s1} &= \theta_{p1} - 45^\circ = 22.5^\circ - 45^\circ = -22.5^\circ \\
\theta_{s2} &= \theta_{p1} + 45^\circ = 22.5^\circ + 45^\circ = 67.5^\circ
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average normal strain on maximum shear planes:**

$$\varepsilon_{\text{avg on shear planes}} = C = 100 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Step 4 — Final summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\varepsilon_1$ | $+382.84 \, \mu\varepsilon$ |
| $\varepsilon_2$ | $-182.84 \, \mu\varepsilon$ |
| $\theta_{p1}$ | $22.5^\circ$ |
| $\gamma_{\max}$ (in-plane) | $565.69 \, \mu\varepsilon$ |
| $\theta_{s1}$ | $-22.5^\circ$ |
| Strain on shear planes | $100 \, \mu\varepsilon$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified] — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **volumetric strain** in a 3-D element subjected to normal strains $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$. State the **assumptions** clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel cube of side $50$ mm is subjected to strains $\varepsilon_x = 0.0008$, $\varepsilon_y = 0.0004$, $\varepsilon_z = -0.0002$. Compute:
(i) The change in volume. **(3 marks)**
(ii) The volumetric strain. **(2 marks)**
(iii) The new dimensions of the cube. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (a):**

**Step 1 — Original volume of rectangular parallelepiped:**

$$V_0 = a \cdot b \cdot c$$

**Step 2 — New side lengths after deformation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a' &= a(1 + \varepsilon_x) \\
b' &= b(1 + \varepsilon_y) \\
c' &= c(1 + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — New volume:**

$$
V = a(1+\varepsilon_x) \cdot b(1+\varepsilon_y) \cdot c(1+\varepsilon_z)
$$

**Step 4 — Expand and neglect product terms (small-strain assumption):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V &\approx abc \left[1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z + \underbrace{\varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z + \varepsilon_z\varepsilon_x}_{\text{neglect}} + \underbrace{\varepsilon_x\varepsilon_y\varepsilon_z}_{\text{neglect}}\right] \\
&\approx V_0 (1 + \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric strain:**

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_v = \frac{V - V_0}{V_0} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z}
$$

**Assumptions stated:**
1. Strains are small ($|\varepsilon_i| \ll 1$).
2. Material is homogeneous and continuous.
3. Products of strain terms are negligible (second-order smallness).
4. Deformation is uniform within the element.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-B (b):**

**Step 1 — Volumetric strain:**

$$
\varepsilon_v = 0.0008 + 0.0004 + (-0.0002) = 0.0010
$$

**Step 2 — Original volume:**

$$
V_0 = (50)^3 = 125{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Change in volume:**

$$
\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V_0 = 0.0010 \times 125{,}000 = 125 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 4 — New dimensions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
a' &= 50(1 + 0.0008) = 50.04 \text{ mm} \\
b' &= 50(1 + 0.0004) = 50.02 \text{ mm} \\
c' &= 50(1 - 0.0002) = 49.99 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — New volume (verification):**

$$
V = 50.04 \times 50.02 \times 49.99 \approx 125{,}125 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
\Delta V = 125{,}125 - 125{,}000 = 125 \text{ mm}^3 \;\; \checkmark
$$

---

## **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in Strain problems:**
>
> 1. **Mohr's Circle Sign Convention:** For strain Mohr's circle, plot point $X$ at $(\varepsilon_x, +\gamma_{xy}/2)$ and point $Y$ at $(\varepsilon_y, -\gamma_{xy}/2)$. **This is opposite to stress Mohr's circle.** Mixing up these signs gives the wrong principal angle direction. (-2 marks typical)
>
> 2. **Factor of 2 in Principal Strain Formula:** The term under the square root is $\left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2$, **not** $\gamma_{xy}^2$. Many students write $\gamma_{xy}$ directly and inflate the principal strain values by 2×. (-1 to -2 marks)
>
> 3. **Units of Strain:** Strain is **dimensionless**. Writing "strain = 0.0004 mm/mm" instead of just "0.0004" or "$400 \, \mu\varepsilon$" is technically correct but loses elegance points. Express in microstrain for clarity.
>
> 4. **Angle Quadrant Confusion:** $\tan 2\theta_p = 1$ has solutions $2\theta_p = 45^\circ$ **and** $2\theta_p = 225^\circ$.
