The correct quadrant is determined by the **signs of** $\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y$ **and** $\gamma_{xy}$ — use the **arctan with quadrant rule**, not just $\tan^{-1}$. (-1 mark)
>
> 5. **Volumetric Strain Sign Errors:** A negative $\varepsilon_z$ (compression) **decreases** volume; check signs of all three terms before summing. (-1 mark)

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Strain**
>
> - **Definition:** Strain = (change in dimension) / (original dimension); dimensionless.
> - **Three normal strains** $\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z$ + **three shear strains** $\gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{xz}$ = 6 independent components in 3-D.
> - **Engineering shear strain** $\gamma_{xy}$ = **total** angle change = **twice** the tensorial component $\varepsilon_{xy}$.
> - **Volumetric strain** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$ (small-strain assumption; product terms neglected).
> - **Principal strains** in 2-D: $\varepsilon_{1,2} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}{2}\right)^2 + \left(\dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{2}\right)^2}$.
> - **Principal direction:** $\tan 2\theta_p = \dfrac{\gamma_{xy}}{\varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y}$ — use **quadrant rule** for sign of $2\theta_p$.
> - **Maximum in-plane shear strain:** $\gamma_{\max} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ (occurs on planes $45^\circ$ from principal planes).
> - **Mohr's circle for strain** uses $\gamma/2$ on the vertical axis (not $\gamma$); sign convention is **opposite** to stress Mohr's circle.
> - **Invariant:** $\varepsilon_1 + \varepsilon_2 = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y$ (sum of principal strains = sum of normal strains).
> - **True (natural) strain** for large deformations: $\varepsilon_{\text{true}} = \ln(L/L_0)$.
> - **Poisson effect:** $\varepsilon_{\text{lat}} = -\nu \, \varepsilon_{\text{axial}}$ (lateral contraction under axial tension).
> - **Units:** Always express strain in microstrain ($\mu\varepsilon = 10^{-6}$) for clarity in experimental work.
> - **Strain gauge applications:** SHM, fatigue monitoring, FE model validation, load testing of bridges/aircraft.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stress-Strain Curves of Ductile and Brittle Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Stress-Strain Curves of Ductile and Brittle Materials ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What is Stress? **Stress ($\sigma$)** is defined as the internal resisting force developed per unit cross-sectional area within a deformable body when external loads are applied. It quantifies the intensity of internal forces distributed over a section. $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$ where $P$ is the axial load (in Newtons) and $A$ is the original cross-sectional area (in $\text{mm}^2$). The standard unit is **MPa (Megapascal) = N/mm²** or **GPa (Gigapascal)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Definition**: Stress is the *force per unit area* acting on a body, representing the intensity of internal resistance at a point. It is a **tensor quantity** with both magnitude and direction, having units of $\text{N/m}^2$ or Pascal (Pa). ### 1.2 What is Strain? **Strain ($\varepsilon$)** is the ratio of change in dimension (deformation) to the original dimension. It is a **dimensionless quantity** (or expressed as mm/mm). $$\varepsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L} = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$ where $\Delta L$ is the change in length and $L_0$ is the original (undeformed) length. ### 1.3 The Stress-Strain Curve — The "Fingerprint" of a Material **Conceptual Analogy / Intuition** 🧠 Imagine pulling a **chocolate bar (Kit-Kat)** and a **piece of chalk** with the same force until they break: - The **chocolate bar** will stretch significantly, deform into a thinner neck, and only break after a long, drawn-out process → This represents a **Ductile Material** (like mild steel). - The **chalk piece** will not stretch at all. It will suddenly snap with a sharp "crack" the moment the load exceeds its capacity → This represents a **Brittle Material** (like cast iron or glass). The **Stress-Strain Curve** is essentially the engineering "X-ray" of a material's mechanical behavior, plotting stress on the Y-axis and strain on the X-axis as a specimen is loaded uniaxially until failure. ### 1.4 Engineering Stress vs. True Stress | Type | Definition | Formula | |------|------------|---------| | **Engineering Stress** ($\sigma_e$) | Force divided by **original** cross-sectional area | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | | **True Stress** ($\sigma_t$) | Force divided by **instantaneous (actual)** cross-sectional area | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A_i}$ | > [!NOTE] > In the standard KTU syllabus, all stress values refer to **Engineering Stress** unless explicitly stated otherwise. True stress becomes significant only in the plastic (post-yield) region where necking occurs. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Stress-Strain Curve for a Typical Ductile Material (Mild Steel) A ductile material like **mild steel** exhibits a complex, multi-stage curve. Each "kink" or transition represents a critical change in the material's internal atomic structure. #### Stage-by-Stage Analysis: **Stage 1: Proportional Elastic Region (Region OA)** - Stress is **directly proportional** to strain. - Obeys **Hooke's Law**: $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ - The constant **E** is the **Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity)**. - The slope of this straight line = **Stiffness** of the material. - Point A is the **Limit of Proportionality**. **Stage 2: Elastic Region (Region AB)** - Stress is **no longer proportional** to strain, but the material is still **elastic** (it returns to its original shape upon unloading). - Point B is the **Elastic Limit**. **Stage 3: Yield Region (Region BC)** - Beyond point B, the material begins to deform **plastically** (permanently). - The **Upper Yield Point** ($B'$) is the stress at which noticeable plastic deformation begins. - The stress then drops to a **Lower Yield Point** ($C$), where strain continues to increase at a nearly constant stress. - The average of upper and lower yield stress is called the **Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$)**. **Stage 4: Strain Hardening / Plastic Region (Region CD)** - After yielding, the material undergoes **strain hardening** (also called **work hardening**). - It can sustain additional load as the internal crystalline structure re-aligns. - Stress rises until reaching the **Ultimate Stress Point (Point D)** — the **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**. **Stage 5: Necking Region (Region DE)** - After UTS, a localized reduction in cross-section begins at some weak point, called **Necking**. - Engineering stress appears to drop because we still divide by $A_0$ (which hasn't changed), but actual stress at the neck is rising. - This continues until **Fracture (Point E)**, where the specimen finally breaks. ### 2.2 Stress-Strain Curve for a Typical Brittle Material (Cast Iron / Concrete) A **brittle material** behaves in stark contrast: - It has **no yielding** and **no plastic region**. - The curve is nearly linear up to fracture (Hooke's Law holds until failure). - It **fractures suddenly** at the **Ultimate Stress Point** with very little strain. - Examples: **Cast Iron, Concrete, Glass, Ceramics**. $$\sigma_f = \text{Fracture Stress (breaking stress)}$$ The strain at fracture $\varepsilon_f$ is extremely small (often < 0.01 or 1%). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning | |---|-------------------|------------|---------| | 1 | Engineering Stress | $\sigma_e = \dfrac{P}{A_0}$ | Load per original area | | 2 | Engineering Strain | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{L_0}$ | Deformation per unit length | | 3 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Linear elastic relation | | 4 | Young's Modulus | $E = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{PL_0}{A_0 \Delta L}$ | Slope of elastic region | | 5 | Modulus of Resilience | $u_r = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$ | Energy stored per unit volume up to elastic limit | | 6 | Modulus of Toughness | $U_T = \int_0^{\varepsilon_f} \sigma \, d\varepsilon$ | Total energy per unit volume up to fracture (area under curve) | | 7 | True Stress | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{P}{A_i} = \sigma_e (1 + \varepsilon)$ | Stress based on actual area | | 8 | True Strain | $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$ | Logarithmic strain | | 9 | Percentage Elongation | $\% \text{ Elongation} = \dfrac{L_f - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure | | 10 | Percentage Reduction in Area | $\% \text{ RA} = \dfrac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100$ | Ductility measure | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stiffness (E)** vs **Strength ($\sigma_y$, UTS)** vs **Toughness (Area under curve)** are three fundamentally DIFFERENT properties. A material can be stiff but brittle (ceramics), strong but not tough (high-carbon steel), or tough but not stiff (rubber). ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Civil Engineering**: Mild steel (ductile) is used in reinforced concrete and structural beams because it provides warning (large strain) before failure. - **Machine Design**: Brittle materials like cast iron are used in machine beds where compressive strength is needed and sudden failure under tension is unlikely. - **Automotive/Aerospace**: Stress-strain curves help select materials that absorb maximum energy (high toughness) during crashes. - **Material Selection**: The area under the stress-strain curve represents **toughness**, crucial for choosing materials for impact-resistant applications. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Relationship Between True Stress and Engineering Stress The true stress at any instant accounts for the **actual reduced cross-sectional area** due to elongation. **Starting Point:** $$\sigma_e = \frac{P}{A_0} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_i}$$ We assume **volume constancy** during plastic deformation (incompressibility of plastic flow): $$A_0 \cdot L_0 = A_i \cdot L_i$$ Rearranging for the instantaneous area: $$A_i = \frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_i}$$ **Substituting into the true stress equation:** $$\sigma_t = \frac{P}{A_i} = \frac{P}{\frac{A_0 \cdot L_0}{L_i}} = \frac{P \cdot L_i}{A_0 \cdot L_0}$$ **Recognizing that engineering stress is $\sigma_e = P/A_0$:** $$\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot \frac{L_i}{L_0}$$ Now, engineering strain is defined as $\varepsilon = \dfrac{L_i - L_0}{L_0}$, which gives: $$\frac{L_i}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon$$ **Therefore, the final relation is:** $$\boxed{\sigma_t = \sigma_e \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)}$$ ### 3.2 Derivation: True Strain from Engineering Strain True strain $\varepsilon_t$ is the natural logarithm of the ratio of instantaneous to original length: $$\varepsilon_t = \int_{L_0}^{L_f} \frac{dL}{L} = \ln\left(\frac{L_f}{L_0}\right)$$ Since $\dfrac{L_f}{L_0} = 1 + \varepsilon$: $$\boxed{\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)}$$ ### 3.3 Worked Analytical Example — Energy Stored in a Ductile Specimen **Problem:** A mild steel rod of diameter 20 mm and length 200 mm is subjected to a tensile load of 60 kN. If the modulus of elasticity is $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, calculate: (a) The stress induced (b) The strain produced (c) The elongation of the rod (d) The energy stored per unit volume (modulus of resilience), given $\sigma_y = 250 \text{ MPa}$ **Given Data:** - Diameter, $d = 20 \text{ mm}$, so $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(20)^2 = 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$ - Length, $L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$ - Load, $P = 60 \text{ kN} = 60{,}000 \text{ N}$ - Young's Modulus, $E = 200 \text{ GPa} = 200 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ **Part (a): Stress** $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A_0} = \frac{60{,}000}{314.159} = 190.99 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 191 \text{ MPa}$$ **Part (b): Strain** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{190.99}{200 \times 10^3} = 9.549 \times 10^{-4} \approx 0.000955$$ **Part (c): Elongation** $$\Delta L = \varepsilon \cdot L_0 = 9.549 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 = 0.191 \text{ mm}$$ **Part (d): Modulus of Resilience** The energy stored per unit volume in the **elastic region** up to yield is: $$u_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(250)^2}{2 \times 200 \times 10^3} = \frac{62{,}500}{400{,}000} = 0.15625 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 0.156 \text{ MPa}$$ Since $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ kJ/m}^3$, we can express this as: $$u_r = 0.15625 \text{ kJ/m}^3 \approx 156.25 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ > [!NOTE] > The energy stored per unit volume is the **area under the linear elastic portion** of the stress-strain curve (a triangle), which is $\dfrac{1}{2} \times \sigma_y \times \varepsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Stress-Strain Curve Architecture for Ductile Material ```mermaid graph TD A["Point O: Origin (Zero Load)"] --> B["Point A: Limit of Proportionality"] B --> C["Point B: Elastic Limit"] C --> D["Point B-prime: Upper Yield Point"] D --> E["Point C: Lower Yield Point"] E --> F["Point D: Ultimate Tensile Strength UTS"] F --> G["Point E: Fracture / Breaking Point"] A -. "Region 1: Proportional Elastic Region
Hooke's Law sigma = E epsilon" .-> B B -. "Region 2: Non-linear Elastic
Still recoverable" .-> C C -. "Region 3: Yielding
Plastic deformation begins" .-> E E -. "Region 4: Strain Hardening
Material strengthens" .-> F F -. "Region 5: Necking
Local cross-section reduces" .-> G ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Comparative Architecture (Ductile vs Brittle) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph SG1["DUCTILE MATERIAL - Mild Steel"] D1["Origin"] --> D2["Linear Elastic Region"] --> D3["Yield Point"] --> D4["Plastic Region"] --> D5["Strain Hardening"] --> D6["Necking"] --> D7["Fracture at High Strain"] end subgraph SG2["BRITTLE MATERIAL - Cast Iron"] B1["Origin"] --> B2["Linear Elastic Region"] --> B3["Sudden Fracture at Low Strain"] end ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Flow of Material Response Under Load ```mermaid graph TD P["Apply Tensile Load P"] --> M{"Material Type?"} M -- "Ductile (Steel, Aluminum, Copper)" --> R1["Linear Elastic Deformation"] R1 --> R2["Yielding Occurs"] R2 --> R3["Plastic Flow with Strain Hardening"] R3 --> R4["Necking Begins"] R4 --> R5["Ductile Fracture
High Energy Absorbed"] M -- "Brittle (Cast Iron, Glass, Concrete)" --> S1["Linear Elastic Deformation"] S1 --> S2["No Yielding"] S2 --> S3["Sudden Brittle Fracture
Low Energy Absorbed"] ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Key Mechanical Properties Mapping ```mermaid graph TD CURVE["Stress-Strain Curve"] --> P1["Stiffness = E
(Slope of Linear Region)"] CURVE --> P2["Strength
Yield Stress and UTS"] CURVE --> P3["Resilience = sigma-y squared / 2E
(Elastic Energy Storage)"] CURVE --> P4["Toughness = Area Under Full Curve
(Total Energy to Fracture)"] CURVE --> P5["Ductility
Percent Elongation at Fracture"] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Aid for Students**: Each of these block diagrams represents the *flow of mechanical response*. A "tall and steep" curve indicates high stiffness. A "wide and long" curve (extending far along the strain axis) indicates high ductility. A "fat" curve (large area underneath) indicates high toughness. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Differentiate between ductile and brittle materials with suitable examples.** **Model Answer:** | Property | Ductile Material | Brittle Material | |----------|------------------|------------------| | Plastic deformation | Significant plastic deformation before fracture | Negligible plastic deformation | | Strain at fracture | High (>5%) | Very low (<1%) | | Yielding | Exhibits distinct yield point | No yielding | | Fracture type | Gives warning (gradual) | Sudden, catastrophic failure | | Energy absorbed | High (tough) | Low | | Examples | Mild steel, copper, aluminum | Cast iron, glass, concrete, ceramics | | Stress-strain curve | Large area under curve (tough) | Small area under curve | --- **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **Q: Define the following terms: (a) Elastic Limit, (b) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (c) Modulus of Resilience.** **Model Answer:** **(a) Elastic Limit**: The maximum stress up to which a material returns to its original shape and size **completely** upon removal of the load. Beyond this stress, permanent (plastic) deformation occurs. **(b) Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)**: The maximum stress a material can withstand under tensile loading before necking begins. It is the peak value of the engineering stress-strain curve, denoted as $\sigma_u$ or $\sigma_{uts}$. **(c) Modulus of Resilience**: The strain energy stored per unit volume of a material when loaded up to its elastic limit. Mathematically: $$u_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$$ It represents the material's capacity to absorb energy in the **elastic region** without permanent deformation. Units: $\text{N/m}^2$ or equivalently $\text{J/m}^3$. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern) --- #### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Draw a labeled stress-strain curve for a ductile material (mild steel) under uniaxial tension. Identify and briefly explain the significance of the following points on the curve: (i) Limit of Proportionality, (ii) Upper and Lower Yield Points, (iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength, (iv) Fracture Point. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A mild steel specimen of diameter 25 mm and gauge length 200 mm was tested to failure in a tensile test. The maximum load applied was 150 kN, and the elongation at fracture was 0.6 mm. The final diameter at the neck was measured as 18 mm. Calculate: (i) The Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS) (ii) The Engineering Strain at Fracture (iii) The Percentage Reduction in Area (% RA) (iv) Comment on the ductility of the material. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a):** A labeled curve should show: ```mermaid graph LR A["O"] -->|"Region 1: Proportional"| B["A: Proportionality Limit"] B -->|"Region 2: Elastic but non-linear"| C["B: Elastic Limit"] C --> D["B-prime: Upper Yield Point"] D --> E["C: Lower Yield Point"] E -->|"Region 4: Strain Hardening"| F["D: UTS Maximum"] F -->|"Region 5: Necking"| G["E: Fracture Point"] ``` **Significance:** **(i) Limit of Proportionality (Point A)**: Up to this point, stress is directly proportional to strain (Hooke's Law holds). The slope of this straight portion gives Young's Modulus E. **(ii) Upper and Lower Yield points**: The **Upper Yield Point** is the stress at which plastic deformation first begins. The stress then drops to the **Lower Yield Point**, where strain increases at constant stress. The average value is the **Yield Stress** of the material. **(iii) Ultimate Tensile Strength (Point D)**: The maximum load-carrying capacity per unit original area. Beyond this point, necking begins and engineering stress decreases even though true stress continues to rise. **(iv) Fracture Point (Point E)**: The stress and strain at which the specimen physically separates. The total energy absorbed up to this point represents the material's **toughness**. --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given:** - Original diameter $d_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$ - Original gauge length $L_0 = 200 \text{ mm}$ - Maximum load $P_{max} = 150 \text{ kN} = 150{,}000 \text{ N}$ - Elongation at fracture $\Delta L_f = 0.6 \text{ mm}$ - Final diameter at neck $d_f = 18 \text{ mm}$ **Original Cross-sectional Area:** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_0)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(25)^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Final Cross-sectional Area at Neck:** $$A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_f)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}(18)^2 = 254.47 \text{ mm}^2$$ **(i) Ultimate Tensile Strength:** $$\sigma_{UTS} = \frac{P_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{150{,}000}{490.87} = 305.58 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 305.6 \text{ MPa}$$ **(ii) Engineering Strain at Fracture:** $$\varepsilon_f = \frac{\Delta L_f}{L_0} = \frac{0.6}{200} = 0.003 = 0.3\%$$ **(iii) Percentage Reduction in Area:** $$\% \text{ RA} = \frac{A_0 - A_f}{A_0} \times 100 = \frac{490.87 - 254.47}{490.87} \times 100$$ $$\% \text{ RA} = \frac{236.40}{490.87} \times 100 = 48.16\%$$ **(iv) Comment on Ductility:** The percentage reduction in area is approximately **48%**, which is quite high. Mild steel is considered a **ductile material** because the % RA is typically greater than 30%, indicating significant plastic deformation before fracture. However, the engineering strain at fracture (0.3%) appears low — this is because the fracture strain is typically measured at the localized neck, not the full gauge length. --- #### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Draw and compare the stress-strain curves of a ductile material (mild steel) and a brittle material (cast iron). List at least **six points of difference**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A cylindrical brass specimen of gauge length 100 mm and diameter 12 mm is subjected to a tensile load. The material yields at a stress of 200 MPa. If the modulus of elasticity is $E = 100 \text{ GPa}$, calculate: (i) The load required to yield the specimen (ii) The strain at the yield point (iii) The energy stored per unit volume (modulus of resilience) (iv) The total elastic strain energy stored in the specimen at yield **(7 marks)** **Model Solution for Part (a):** **Sketch:** (Student is expected to draw both curves on the same axes) The ductile curve (mild steel) is tall, extended, and has a "yield plateau" and "necking region." The brittle curve (cast iron) is short, nearly linear, and ends abruptly at fracture. **Six Points of Difference:** | S.No. | Ductile Material (Mild Steel) | Brittle Material (Cast Iron) | |-------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------| | 1 | High plastic deformation before fracture | Very little or no plastic deformation | | 2 | Exhibits a distinct yield point | No yielding — fracture occurs at elastic limit | | 3 | Large area under stress-strain curve (high toughness) | Small area under curve (low toughness) | | 4 | High percentage elongation (20–30%) | Low percentage elongation (<2%) | | 5 | Fracture is gradual, gives warning | Sudden, catastrophic fracture | | 6 | Used for tension applications (rods, cables) | Used for compression (machine beds, columns) | | 7 | Stress at fracture is less than UTS | Stress at fracture ≈ UTS | --- **Model Solution for Part (b):** **Given:** - Length $L_0 = 100 \text{ mm}$ - Diameter $d = 12 \text{ mm}$ - Yield stress $\sigma_y = 200 \text{ MPa} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2$ - Young's Modulus $E = 100 \text{ GPa} = 100 \times 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$ **Cross-sectional Area:** $$A_0 = \frac{\pi}{4}(12)^2 = 113.10 \text{ mm}^2$$ **Volume of the Specimen:** $$V = A_0 \times L_0 = 113.10 \times 100 = 11{,}310 \text{ mm}^3$$ **(i) Load Required to Yield:** $$P_y = \sigma_y \times A_0 = 200 \times 113.10 = 22{,}620 \text{ N} = 22.62 \text{ kN}$$ **(ii) Strain at Yield Point:** $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{E} = \frac{200}{100 \times 10^3} = 0.002 = 0.2\%$$ **(iii) Modulus of Resilience (Energy per unit volume):** $$u_r = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{2E} = \frac{(200)^2}{2 \times 100 \times 10^3} = \frac{40{,}000}{200{,}000} = 0.20 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ Since $1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 1 \text{ kJ/m}^3$: $$u_r = 0.20 \text{ kJ/m}^3 = 200 \text{ kJ/m}^3$$ **(iv) Total Elastic Strain Energy Stored in the Specimen:** $$U = u_r \times V = 0.20 \times 11{,}310 = 2262 \text{ N·mm} = 2.262 \text{ J}$$ --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:** > > 1. **Confusing Young's Modulus with Yield Strength**: $E$ is the slope of the **linear** portion; $\sigma_y$ is a stress value at yield. They have different units! > > 2. **Forgetting Units Consistency**: Mixing MPa with kN, mm with m. KTU expects you to write units in every step. **Always convert** $E$ to $\text{N/mm}^2$ before using. > > 3. **Plotting the Wrong Shape for the Brittle Curve**: Many students draw the brittle curve as if it has a yield point — it does **NOT**. It is nearly linear and breaks suddenly. > > 4. **Confusing Elastic Limit with Proportionality Limit**: The proportionality limit is where Hooke's Law stops; the elastic limit is where permanent deformation begins. They are close but **not the same**. > > 5. **Engineering vs True Stress**: In UTS/necking region, engineering stress decreases but true stress increases. Markers specifically look for this distinction. > > 6. **Incomplete Curve Labels**: Forgetting to label the **necking region** or **strain hardening region** can cost up to **2 marks** in a 7-mark drawing question. > > 7. **Percentage Elongation vs Percentage Reduction in Area**: These are two different ductility measures. Know both and be ready to compute either. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ✅ **Stress-Strain Curve is the "identity card" of a material under tension.** ✅ **Five key stages of mild steel curve**: (1) Proportional, (2) Elastic-non-linear, (3) Yielding (upper & lower yield points), (4) Strain hardening → UTS, (5) Necking → Fracture. ✅ **Stiffness** = Slope of elastic region = $E$ (Young's Modulus). ✅ **Strength** = Yield Stress ($\sigma_y$) or Ultimate Tensile Strength ($\sigma_{UTS}$). ✅ **Ductility** = High strain at fracture; measured by **% Elongation** and **% Reduction in Area**. ✅ **Brittleness** = Low strain at fracture; sudden failure without yielding. ✅ **Resilience** = Area under stress-strain curve up to elastic limit = $\dfrac{\sigma_y^2}{2E}$. ✅ **Toughness** = Total area under entire stress-strain curve = $\int \sigma \, d\varepsilon$. ✅ **True Stress** $\sigma_t = \sigma_e(1 + \varepsilon)$; **True Strain** $\varepsilon_t = \ln(1 + \varepsilon)$. ✅ **Mild Steel is Ductile** (large plastic region); **Cast Iron is Brittle** (linear until sudden fracture). ✅ **Volume constancy assumption** $A_0 L_0 = A_i L_i$ holds in plastic deformation for deriving true stress. ✅ **Units Quick Reference**: $1 \text{ MPa} = 1 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$; $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa}$. ✅ **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ applies only in the **linear elastic region**. ✅ **A "stiff" material is not necessarily "strong"**, and a "strong" material is not necessarily "tough" — these are independent properties. ✅ **KTU Favourite Questions**: (a) Drawing and labeling the curve, (b) Computing stress/strain from load data, (c) Comparing ductile vs brittle behavior, (d) Energy storage calculations.

topics.title: Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Poisson's Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Poisson's Ratio (ν)** is defined as the **negative ratio of lateral (transverse) strain to longitudinal (axial) strain** within a homogeneous and isotropic material subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load, provided the material remains within the **linear elastic region** of its stress-strain curve.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

The negative sign is conventionally introduced to make **ν a positive quantity**, because most engineering materials (metals, alloys) experience a **positive longitudinal strain (elongation)** under tension while simultaneously undergoing a **negative lateral strain (contraction)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT303 - Module 1):** Poisson's ratio is one of the **four fundamental elastic constants** of an isotropic material, alongside **Young's Modulus (E)**, **Shear Modulus (G)**, and **Bulk Modulus (K)**. Mastery of this single parameter unlocks the ability to determine the other three.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rubber Band Squeeze" Intuition

Imagine stretching a **soft rubber band** horizontally between your two hands.

- As you **pull it longer** (longitudinal extension), the band visibly becomes **thinner** in the middle.
- The ratio of "how much it got thinner" to "how much it got longer" is essentially Poisson's ratio.

Now take a **cork** or a **piece of bread** and squeeze it between your palms:
- It **shortens** under compression (longitudinal contraction) and **bulges outward** sideways.
- The bulging is the lateral response — a direct visual indicator of Poisson's effect.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the "negative sign" exists in the formula:** Engineers want a single positive number (like **0.3 for steel** or **0.5 for rubber**) to tabulate in handbooks. Without the minus sign, the value would be negative (e.g., −0.3), which is counterintuitive when looking up material data.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Material | Poisson's Ratio (ν) |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Steel** | **0.30** |
| **Aluminum** | **0.33** |
| **Copper** | **0.34** |
| **Cast Iron** | **0.25** |
| **Concrete** | **0.10 – 0.20** |
| **Rubber** | **≈ 0.50** |
| **Cork** | **≈ 0.00** |
| **Glass** | **0.24** |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Heuristic:** Most metals cluster tightly around **ν ≈ 0.30**. Unless a problem explicitly states otherwise, KTU examiners expect students to use **ν = 0.3** for steel in numerical problems.

---

## 1.4 Theoretical Bounds of Poisson's Ratio

For a **stable, isotropic, linear elastic** material in three dimensions, classical elasticity theory proves that:

$$-1 < \nu < 0.5$$

This gives rise to three physically meaningful regimes:

1. **0 < ν < 0.5** → Most common engineering materials (metals, polymers).
2. **ν = 0.5** → **Incompressible** materials (rubber, fluids) — volume does not change.
3. **ν < 0** → **Auxetic materials** (modern engineered foams, certain crystals) — they *expand* laterally when stretched.

> [!WARNING]
> A KTU examiner will **deduct marks** if you state that ν can be any arbitrary value. The bounds **−1 < ν < 0.5** must be quoted with justification in viva or theory questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Strain Decomposition Under Uniaxial Loading

Consider a cylindrical specimen of original length **L** and diameter **d** subjected to an axial tensile force **P**.

| Strain Component | Symbol | Direction | Sign Under Tension |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Longitudinal (Axial) Strain | $\varepsilon_L$ | Along load axis | Positive (+) |
| Lateral (Transverse) Strain | $\varepsilon_{lat}$ | Perpendicular to load | Negative (−) |

The longitudinal strain:

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

The lateral strain:

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = \frac{\Delta d}{d} = \frac{\Delta d/d}{\Delta L/L} \cdot \varepsilon_L = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L$$

So:

$$\frac{\Delta d}{d} = -\nu \cdot \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Volume Strain (Volumetric Strain) under uniaxial stress:**
> $$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \varepsilon_L (1 - 2\nu)$$
> This single expression is the *gateway* to deriving the E-K relationship.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use `\\vert` instead of `\\|` in all table cells below for absolute value notation to preserve markdown rendering.**

| # | Relationship | Formula | Typical Use in KTU Problems |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Definition of ν | $\nu = -\\varepsilon_{lat}\\,/\\,\\varepsilon_L$ | Direct 3-mark definitions |
| 2 | Volumetric strain | $\\varepsilon_v = \\varepsilon_L (1 - 2\\nu)$ | Hydrostatic loading |
| 3 | E–G–ν relation | $E = 2G(1 + \\nu)$ | Given two, find third |
| 4 | E–K–ν relation | $E = 3K(1 - 2\\nu)$ | Bulk modulus problems |
| 5 | G in terms of E & K | $G = \\dfrac{3KE}{9K - E}$ | Conversion questions |
| 6 | ν in terms of E & G | $\\nu = \\dfrac{E}{2G} - 1$ | Standard 7-mark derivation |
| 7 | ν in terms of K & E | $\\nu = \\dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ | Hydrostatic problems |
| 8 | ν in terms of K & G | $\\nu = \\dfrac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}$ | Multi-step derivations |
| 9 | Hydrostatic strain | $\\varepsilon_h = \\dfrac{\\sigma_h}{3K} = \\dfrac{p}{K}$ | Pressure vessel setups |
| 10 | Lateral strain formula | $\\dfrac{\\Delta d}{d} = -\\nu \\cdot \\dfrac{P}{AE}$ | Diameter change problems |

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Civil Engineering:** Calculating deflection of reinforced concrete beams (ν affects the shear coefficient).
- **Mechanical Design:** Designing pressure vessels — lateral contraction of the wall under internal pressure determines fatigue life.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Estimating settlement of soils and rocks under foundation loads.
- **Biomechanics:** Modeling arterial walls, bone deformation, and prosthetic design.
- **Metallurgy & Manufacturing:** Rolling, forging, and deep-drawing processes all depend on lateral flow governed by ν.
- **Auxetic Materials Research:** Modern engineered materials with **ν < 0** are used in blast-resistant panels, biomedical stents, and aerospace composites.

> [!TIP]
> If you can **memorize only one formula** from this topic, memorize $E = 2G(1+\nu)$. It is the most frequently tested identity in KTU Module 1.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship E = 2G(1 + ν)

### Given
- A cylindrical element of length **L** and side **a**, subjected to pure shear stress **τ** on its faces.
- Young's Modulus **E**, Shear Modulus **G**, Poisson's ratio **ν**.

### Step 1: Apply Pure Shear
A pure shear state is **equivalent** to two perpendicular principal stresses:

$$\sigma_x = +\tau, \quad \sigma_y = -\tau$$

acting along the diagonals of the element.

### Step 2: Compute Principal Strains
Using generalized Hooke's Law for a 2D stress state:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y) = \frac{1}{E}(\tau - \nu(-\tau)) = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x) = \frac{1}{E}(-\tau - \nu\tau) = \frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 3: Compute Engineering Shear Strain
The total engineering shear strain (change from 90° angle) is:

$$\gamma = \varepsilon_x - \varepsilon_y = \frac{\tau(1 + \nu)}{E} - \left(\frac{-\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4: Apply the Shear Modulus Definition
By Hooke's Law in shear: $\tau = G \gamma$, therefore $\gamma = \tau / G$.

Equating both expressions for γ:

$$\frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{2\tau(1 + \nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5: Simplify

$$\boxed{E = 2G(1 + \nu)}$$

> **Conclusion:** With **E = 200 GPa** and **G = 77 GPa** (typical steel), we obtain $\nu = 0.299 \approx 0.30$, confirming textbook values.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Relationship E = 3K(1 − 2ν)

### Given
- A cubic element of side **a** subjected to equal hydrostatic pressure **p** on all six faces.
- Hydrostatic stress: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

### Step 1: Compute Volumetric Strain
By definition:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

### Step 2: Express Each Principal Strain
Using Hooke's Law in 3D:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[-p - \nu(-p - p)\right] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry: $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$.

### Step 3: Sum the Strains

$$\varepsilon_v = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 4: Apply Bulk Modulus Definition
By definition: $K = -p / \varepsilon_v$, so $\varepsilon_v = -p / K$.

### Step 5: Equate and Solve

$$\frac{-p}{K} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

> **Physical Insight:** As $\nu \to 0.5$, the factor $(1-2\nu) \to 0$, meaning $E \to \infty$ would be needed to resist any volume change — this mathematically represents an **incompressible material** like rubber.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: ν in Terms of E and G

Starting from Derivation 1 result:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

Divide both sides by **2G**:

$$\frac{E}{2G} = 1 + \nu$$

Subtract 1:

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ν in Terms of K and E

Starting from:

$$E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

Divide by 3K:

$$\frac{E}{3K} = 1 - 2\nu$$

Solve for ν:

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{3K - E}{6K}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: ν in Terms of K and G

From $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:

Equate the two expressions for E:

$$2G(1 + \nu) = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$$

Expand:

$$2G + 2G\nu = 3K - 6K\nu$$

Collect ν terms on one side:

$$2G\nu + 6K\nu = 3K - 2G$$

$$\nu(2G + 6K) = 3K - 2G$$

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{3K - 2G}{6K + 2G}}$$

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Master Problem-Solving Map

| If you are GIVEN: | You can FIND: | Use Formula: |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| E, G | ν | $\nu = E/(2G) - 1$ |
| E, ν | G | $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ |
| E, ν | K | $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ |
| G, ν | E | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ |
| K, ν | E | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ |
| K, E | ν | $\nu = (3K - E)/(6K)$ |
| K, G | E, ν | Combine formulas 5 & 8 from cheat sheet |
| P, L, d, E, ν | Δd | $\Delta d = -\nu \cdot P \cdot d / (A \cdot E)$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Poisson's Effect Under Uniaxial Tension

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Specimen]:::orig --> B[Apply Axial Tensile Force P]:::force
    B --> C[Longitudinal Strain epsilon L]:::long
    B --> D[Lateral Strain epsilon lat]:::lat
    C --> E[Elongation Delta L positive]:::elon
    D --> F[Contraction Delta d negative]:::con
    E --> G[Compute Poisson Ratio nu]:::calc
    F --> G
    G --> H{nu Range Check}:::check
    H --> I[Stable 0 less than nu less than 0.5]:::ok
    H --> J[Auxetic nu less than 0]:::aux
    H --> K[Incompressible nu equals 0.5]:::inc

    classDef orig fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F77B4,color:#000
    classDef force fill:#FFE5E5,stroke:#D62728,color:#000
    classDef long fill:#E5F5E0,stroke:#2CA02C,color:#000
    classDef lat fill:#FFF5E0,stroke:#FF7F0E,color:#000
    classDef elon fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#17BECF,color:#000
    classDef con fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#9467BD,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#FFEB99,stroke:#BCBD22,color:#000
    classDef check fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#E377C2,color:#000
    classDef ok fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef aux fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef inc fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Elastic Constants Interrelationship Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Young Modulus E]:::e
    G[Shear Modulus G]:::g
    K[Bulk Modulus K]:::k
    NU[Poisson Ratio nu]:::nu
    LAME[Lame Constants lambda mu]:::lame

    E --- R1[E equals 2G times 1 plus nu]:::rel
    E --- R2[E equals 3K times 1 minus 2 nu]:::rel2
    G --- R3[G equals 3KE over 9K minus E]:::rel3
    NU --- R1
    NU --- R2
    K --- R2
    K --- R3
    E --- R3

    LAME -.-> E
    LAME -.-> NU

    classDef e fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000
    classDef g fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000
    classDef k fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef nu fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,color:#000
    classDef lame fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,color:#000
    classDef rel fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#666666,color:#333
    classDef rel2 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#666666,color:#333
    classDef rel3 fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#666666,color:#333
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Function-Level Flow of Strain Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN[Axial Stress sigma axial]:::inp
    L1[Apply Hookes Law in X]:::step1
    LY[Apply Hookes Law in Y]:::step2
    LZ[Apply Hookes Law in Z]:::step3
    EX[epsilon X equals sigma X over E minus nu sigma Y plus sigma Z over E]:::eqx
    EY[epsilon Y equals sigma Y over E minus nu sigma X plus sigma Z over E]:::eqy
    EZ[epsilon Z equals sigma Z over E minus nu sigma X plus sigma Y over E]:::eqz
    OUT1[Volumetric Strain]:::out1
    OUT2[Lateral Contraction]:::out2
    OUT3[Shear Distortion]:::out3

    IN --> L1 --> EX
    IN --> LY --> EY
    IN --> LZ --> EZ
    EX --> OUT1
    EY --> OUT1
    EZ --> OUT1
    EX --> OUT2
    EY --> OUT2
    EX --> OUT3
    EY --> OUT3

    classDef inp fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#0277BD,color:#000
    classDef step1 fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#558B2F,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#FFF8E1,stroke:#FF8F00,color:#000
    classDef step3 fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000
    classDef eqx fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef eqy fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef eqz fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,color:#000
    classDef out1 fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#4527A0,color:#000
    classDef out2 fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,color:#000
    classDef out3 fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use flowcharts instead of physical drawings here?** Poisson's effect is fundamentally a **3D volumetric coupling phenomenon**. A flowchart can show how one axial input propagates through Hooke's Law into lateral, volumetric, and shear outputs — something a static side-view sketch cannot capture clearly. This aligns with the V10 protocol's preference for **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** when physical rendering is ambiguous.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define Poisson's ratio. What are its theoretical limits for a stable isotropic material?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the negative ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain in a material subjected to uniaxial stress within the elastic limit.

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}}$$

For a **stable, isotropic, linear elastic** material, classical elasticity theory proves that Poisson's ratio must satisfy the bounds:

$$-1 < \nu < 0.5$$

The lower bound **−1** corresponds to a material whose bulk modulus approaches zero (highly compressible auxetic), while the upper bound **0.5** represents a perfectly incompressible material (like rubber) where volume change is zero under hydrostatic pressure.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Shear Modulus (G), and Poisson's ratio (ν).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three elastic constants are related by the identity:

$$E = 2G(1 + \nu)$$

This equation is derived by analyzing pure shear as a combination of two perpendicular principal stresses and applying Hooke's Law in 2D. For a typical metal like steel with **E = 200 GPa** and **G = 77 GPa**, solving gives **ν = 0.30**, confirming experimental observations.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A steel rod of length **2 m** and diameter **30 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **90 kN**. Given **E = 200 GPa** and **ν = 0.3**, determine:
(i) The longitudinal strain
(ii) The lateral strain
(iii) The change in diameter
(iv) The change in volume

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- L = 2 m = 2000 mm
- d = 30 mm
- P = 90 kN = 90,000 N
- E = 200 GPa = 200,000 N/mm²
- ν = 0.3

**Step 1: Cross-sectional Area**

$$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (30)^2}{4} = 706.86 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Axial Stress**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{90,000}{706.86} = 127.32 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 127.32 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Longitudinal Strain (i)**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{127.32}{200,000} = 6.366 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4: Lateral Strain (ii)**

$$\varepsilon_{lat} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_L = -0.3 \times 6.366 \times 10^{-4} = -1.910 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5: Change in Diameter (iii)**

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_{lat} \cdot d = -1.910 \times 10^{-4} \times 30 = -5.73 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta d = -0.00573 \text{ mm (contraction)}}$$

**Step 6: Volumetric Strain**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L (1 - 2\nu) = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \times (1 - 0.6) = 6.366 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.4 = 2.546 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7: Original Volume**

$$V = A \times L = 706.86 \times 2000 = 1,413,720 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 8: Change in Volume (iv)**

$$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 2.546 \times 10^{-4} \times 1,413,720 = 359.9 \text{ mm}^3$$

$$\boxed{\Delta V = +359.9 \text{ mm}^3 (volume increases under tension)}}$$

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** Derive the relationship between Young's Modulus (E), Bulk Modulus (K), and Poisson's ratio (ν). Given **E = 210 GPa** and **ν = 0.28**, calculate the Bulk Modulus K.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**
Consider a cube of side **a** subjected to hydrostatic pressure **p** on all six faces, so that $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$.

By Hooke's Law in 3D:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{E}[-p - \nu(-p - p)] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry: $\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z$.

**Volumetric Strain:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

**By definition of Bulk Modulus:** $K = -p / \varepsilon_v$, so $\varepsilon_v = -p / K$.

Equating the two expressions:

$$\frac{-p}{K} = \frac{-3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**
Given E = 210 GPa, ν = 0.28:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{210}{3(1 - 0.56)} = \frac{210}{3 \times 0.44} = \frac{210}{1.32}$$

$$\boxed{K = 159.09 \text{ GPa}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]

**Part (a) [7 marks]:** A brass specimen has the following elastic constants: **E = 100 GPa** and **G = 40 GPa**. Determine the Poisson's ratio. If this specimen is subjected to a tensile stress of **150 MPa**, find the volumetric strain.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Poisson's Ratio**

Using $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:

$$\nu = \frac{E}{2G} - 1 = \frac{100}{2 \times 40} - 1 = \frac{100}{80} - 1 = 1.25 - 1 = 0.25$$

$$\boxed{\nu = 0.25}$$

**Step 2: Compute Longitudinal Strain**

$$\varepsilon_L = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{150}{100,000} = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Compute Volumetric Strain**

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L (1 - 2\nu) = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (1 - 0.5) = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_v = 7.5 \times 10^{-4}}$$

---

**Part (b) [7 marks]:** For a given isotropic material, the Young's Modulus is **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²** and the Bulk Modulus is **1.5 × 10⁵ N/mm²**. Calculate the Shear Modulus and Poisson's ratio.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Poisson's Ratio using K and E**

$$\nu = \frac{3K - E}{6K} = \frac{3(1.5 \times 10^5) - (2.0 \times 10^5)}{6(1.5 \times 10^5)} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^5 - 2.0 \times 10^5}{9.0 \times 10^5}$$

$$\nu = \frac{2.5 \times 10^5}{9.0 \times 10^5} = 0.2778$$

$$\boxed{\nu \approx 0.278}$$

**Step 2: Compute Shear Modulus using E and ν**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1 + \nu)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2(1 + 0.2778)} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2 \times 1.2778} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^5}{2.5556}$$

$$\boxed{G = 7.826 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 78.26 \text{ GPa}}$$

**Verification using combined formula:**

$$G = \frac{3KE}{9K - E} = \frac{3(1.5 \times 10^5)(2.0 \times 10^5)}{9(1.5 \times 10^5) - 2.0 \times 10^5} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{10}}{13.5 \times 10^5 - 2.0 \times 10^5} = \frac{9.0 \times 10^{10}}{11.5 \times 10^5}$$

$$G = 7.826 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the definition: $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lat}/\varepsilon_L$ — examiners specifically test whether students understand why the minus exists. (Loss: 0.5–1 mark)
> 2. **Unit mismatches:** Mixing N/mm² with GPa without conversion. Always standardize to **N/mm² = MPa** for rod problems, or **GPa** for material property tables.
> 3. **Sign errors in volumetric strain:** Students often write $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L(1+2\nu)$ instead of $(1-2\nu)$. Re-derive if in doubt.
> 4. **Skipping intermediate steps** in derivation questions. KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes **step-by-step working** — even correct final answers lose 2–3 marks if derivation steps are missing.
> 5. **Not stating the theoretical range** $(−1 < \nu < 0.5)$ in 3-mark definition questions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU PCMRT303 Module 1: Poisson's Ratio**

- ⭐ **Definition:** $\nu = -\varepsilon_{lateral}/\varepsilon_{longitudinal}$ — always with the negative sign.
- ⭐ **Range:** $-1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials; **0 to 0.5** for conventional materials.
- ⭐ **Incompressibility:** When $\nu = 0.5$, the material cannot change volume (rubber, fluids).
- ⭐ **Auxetics:** When $\nu < 0$, material expands laterally when stretched (modern engineered materials).
- ⭐ **Master Formula #1:** $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ — most-tested identity in KTU.
- ⭐ **Master Formula #2:** $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ — derive via hydrostatic stress cube.
- ⭐ **Lateral strain:** $\Delta d/d = -\nu \cdot \sigma/E$ — diameter *decreases* under tension.
- ⭐ **Volumetric strain under uniaxial load:** $\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_L(1-2\nu)$.
- ⭐ **Typical values:** Steel = 0.30, Aluminum = 0.33, Rubber ≈ 0.50, Cork ≈ 0.00.
- ⭐ **Sign convention:** Tension → positive longitudinal strain → negative lateral strain → positive ν (due to minus sign in formula).
- ⭐ **Derivation template:** Always begin with a clearly labeled diagram/specimen description, state Hooke's Law, substitute symmetry, simplify step-by-step, and box the final identity.
- ⭐ **Numerical strategy:** Identify the **two given elastic constants**, then use the appropriate formula from the cheat sheet table to find the third.
- ⭐ **Units check:** E, G, K must all be in the **same units** before substitution; convert N/mm² ↔ GPa carefully.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Constitutive Equations: Hooke's Law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Constitutive Equations: Hooke's Law # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition A **constitutive equation** is a material-specific mathematical relation that connects the internal force variables (stresses) to the kinematic deformation variables (strains) within a continuum. For a **linear elastic**, homogeneous, and isotropic solid, the most fundamental constitutive equation is **Robert Hooke's Law** (published in 1676, formalised in 1678), which states that, within the elastic limit, stress is directly proportional to strain. In its generalised three-dimensional tensor form, Hooke's Law expresses the symmetric fourth-order **elasticity tensor** $\mathbf{C}_{ijkl}$ acting on the strain tensor $\varepsilon_{kl}$ to yield the stress tensor $\sigma_{ij}$: $$\sigma_{ij} = \mathbf{C}_{ijkl} \, \varepsilon_{kl}$$ For isotropic materials, this tensor simplifies drastically because the material response is identical in every direction, and we are left with only two independent elastic constants — typically **Young's Modulus ($E$)** and **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$)** — which together fully define the linear elastic behaviour. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Constitutive Equations form the mathematical bridge between *equilibrium equations* (Newton's 2nd Law for continua) and *compatibility equations* (kinematic admissibility). Without the constitutive law, a boundary value problem in solid mechanics is mathematically *under-determined*. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Imagine stretching a **steel spring** with your hand. The harder you pull (force), the more it stretches (deformation). If you double the force, the stretch doubles. The spring "remembers" its shape and snaps back when released. The proportionality constant here is the spring stiffness $k$, relating force to extension ($F = kx$). Now extend this idea to a tiny cube of steel inside a loaded structural beam. The cube experiences internal stresses on each face and deforms by small strains. Hooke's Law says: **"the deformation you see in a material is a faithful, linear mirror of the internal forces acting on it — provided you don't push it beyond its elastic limit."** > [!NOTE] > **Why "Constitutive"?** The word comes from Latin *constituere* — "to establish / to make up". A constitutive equation *constitutes* the intrinsic identity of the material, just as a personality constitutes a human being. Two materials under identical loads will respond differently because they have *different constitutive equations*, not different equilibrium equations. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics The following material constants appear repeatedly in Hooke's Law formulations: - **$E$ (Young's Modulus / Modulus of Elasticity)** — units: **N/m²** or **Pa (GPa common)**. Represents axial stiffness. Steel: ~200 GPa; Aluminium: ~70 GPa; Rubber: ~0.01 GPa. - **$G$ (Shear Modulus / Modulus of Rigidity)** — units: **Pa**. Resistance to shape change. Steel: ~80 GPa. - **$\nu$ (Poisson's Ratio)** — dimensionless ratio of lateral to axial strain. Steel: ~0.30; Rubber: ~0.49; Cork: ~0.00. - **$K$ (Bulk Modulus)** — units: **Pa**. Resistance to volumetric (hydrostatic) compression. > [!IMPORTANT] > For a stable, isotropic, linear elastic material, the thermodynamic admissibility constraints require: **$E > 0$**, **$G > 0$**, **$K > 0$**, and **$-1 < \nu < 0.5$**. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The 1D Foundation — Uniaxial Hooke's Law Consider a prismatic bar subjected to an axial tensile force $P$ along the $x$-axis. The bar has cross-sectional area $A$ and original length $L$. Under the load, it elongates by $\delta L$. The definitions are: $$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A}, \qquad \varepsilon_x = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$ Within the proportional (elastic) limit: $$\boxed{\sigma_x = E \, \varepsilon_x} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \boxed{P = \left(\frac{EA}{L}\right) \delta L}$$ **Why this works:** The atomic bonds in the solid act like microscopic springs. For small displacements from equilibrium, the inter-atomic potential is harmonic (parabolic), giving a linear force-displacement law — this is the quantum mechanical origin of Hooke's Law. ## 2.2 Generalised 3D Hooke's Law — The Operative Equations For a body point under a *general triaxial state of stress* $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx})$, the corresponding strains are obtained by **superposition** of the three normal stress effects and shear stress effects. ### 2.2.1 Normal Strains (Effect of Normal Stresses) A normal stress $\sigma_x$ does *two* things simultaneously: 1. Produces an axial strain $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$ in its own direction (extension or contraction). 2. Produces lateral strains in the perpendicular directions through Poisson's coupling: $-\nu \sigma_x / E$ in $y$ and $-\nu \sigma_x / E$ in $z$. Summing contributions from $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ using superposition: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$ $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$ $$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$ ### 2.2.2 Shear Strains (Effect of Shear Stresses) Pure shear stress produces only pure shear strain in the same plane (no Poisson coupling): $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \qquad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \qquad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$ ## 2.3 Volumetric Strain and Bulk Modulus The **volumetric strain** (or *dilatation*) $e$ is the relative change in volume: $$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$ Substituting the normal strain relations: $$e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}\left(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z\right)$$ The **Bulk Modulus** $K$ is defined by the hydrostatic relation $p = -K \cdot e$ where the mean stress is $p = -(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)/3$. Rearranging gives the elegant identity: $$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ ## 2.4 Inverse (Compliance) Form — Strains → Stresses Solving the linear system for the stresses in terms of strains: $$\sigma_x = \lambda \, e + 2G \, \varepsilon_x, \qquad \sigma_y = \lambda \, e + 2G \, \varepsilon_y, \qquad \sigma_z = \lambda \, e + 2G \, \varepsilon_z$$ where $\lambda$ is **Lamé's first constant**: $$\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$$ ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | **#** | **Equation / Relation** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning / Use** | |:---:|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Uniaxial Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ | Axial stress–strain relation | | 2 | 3D Normal Strain (Direct) | $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)]$ | Strain due to normal stresses | | 3 | 3D Shear Strain | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$ | Pure shear deformation | | 4 | Volumetric Strain | $e = \varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y+\varepsilon_z$ | Relative change in volume | | 5 | Volumetric–Hydrostatic Link | $e = \dfrac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$ | Couples pressure to dilatation | | 6 | $E$–$G$–$\nu$ Relation | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Inter-conversion of stiffness constants | | 7 | $E$–$K$–$\nu$ Relation | $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ | Inter-conversion with bulk modulus | | 8 | $G$–$K$–$\nu$ Relation | $G = \dfrac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Bulk–shear coupling | | 9 | Bulk Modulus Defn. | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Resistance to uniform compression | | 10 | Lamé's Constant | $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ | Used in inverse Hooke's form | | 11 | Inverse Hooke's (Normal) | $\sigma_x = \lambda e + 2G\varepsilon_x$ | Stress from strain field | | 12 | Volumetric Modulus Check | $\nu \to 0.5 \Rightarrow K \to \infty$ | Incompressibility limit | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Structural Steel Design (IS 800, Eurocode 3):** Designers use $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$ for steel to compute deflections, buckling loads, and stress distributions in beams, columns, and trusses. 2. **Pressure Vessel Design (ASME Sec VIII):** Multi-axial stresses in cylindrical and spherical shells are evaluated using generalised Hooke's Law to compute radial/hoop strains and displacement fields. 3. **Composite Materials & Geomechanics:** Anisotropic Hooke's Law (with 21 independent constants for the most general case) governs the response of fibre-reinforced laminates, wood, and stratified rock. 4. **Finite Element Method (FEM) software (ANSYS, ABAQUS):** The element stiffness matrix $[K]$ in any linear elastic FEM analysis is built directly from $\mathbf{C}_{ijkl}$ — Hooke's Law is literally "baked into" every solver. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation I — From 1D to 3D Normal Strains via Superposition **Starting premise:** Linearity of the elastic response permits superposition of independent stress effects. **Step 1 — Apply $\sigma_x$ alone (with $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$):** $$\varepsilon_x^{(1)} = \frac{\sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(1)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(1)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_x}{E}$$ **Step 2 — Apply $\sigma_y$ alone (with $\sigma_x = \sigma_z = 0$):** $$\varepsilon_x^{(2)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(2)} = \frac{\sigma_y}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(2)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E}$$ **Step 3 — Apply $\sigma_z$ alone (with $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = 0$):** $$\varepsilon_x^{(3)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_y^{(3)} = -\frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}, \quad \varepsilon_z^{(3)} = \frac{\sigma_z}{E}$$ **Step 4 — Superpose all three contributions for total $\varepsilon_x$:** $$\varepsilon_x = \varepsilon_x^{(1)} + \varepsilon_x^{(2)} + \varepsilon_x^{(3)} = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr]}$$ Identical logic gives $\varepsilon_y$ and $\varepsilon_z$ by cyclic permutation. ## 3.2 Derivation II — Volumetric Strain and Bulk Modulus **Step 1 — Write the volumetric strain as the trace of the strain tensor:** $$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the three normal strain expressions:** $$\begin{aligned} e &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)\bigr] + \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_z)\bigr] + \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x+\sigma_y)\bigr] \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Collect like terms:** $$\begin{aligned} e &= \frac{1}{E}\Bigl[(1-\nu)\sigma_x + (1-\nu)\sigma_y + (1-\nu)\sigma_z\Bigr] \\ &= \frac{1-\nu}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z\bigr) \end{aligned}$$ Wait — let me re-evaluate the coefficient carefully. Combining all three: each $\sigma$ has coefficient $1 - \nu - \nu = 1 - 2\nu$. Hence: $$\boxed{e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z\bigr)}$$ **Step 4 — Define mean (hydrostatic) stress $\sigma_m = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/3$, then $K = \sigma_m / e$:** $$K = \frac{\sigma_m}{e} = \frac{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/3}{(1-2\nu)(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/E} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ $$\boxed{K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}}$$ ## 3.3 Derivation III — Relation $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ via Pure Shear Stress Element **Step 1 — Consider a pure shear element with $\tau_{xy}$ only**, in equilibrium with principal stresses $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$ acting on the rotated principal axes. **Step 2 — Apply the generalised normal strain formula** along the principal directions: $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)] = \frac{1}{E}[\tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy} + 0)] = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)$$ $$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)] = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau_{xy} - \nu(\tau_{xy} + 0)] = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)$$ $$\varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)] = \frac{1}{E}[0 - \nu(\tau_{xy} - \tau_{xy})] = 0$$ **Step 3 — Engineering shear strain** is the change in angle between two originally perpendicular fibres. From the principal strains: $$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu) - \left(-\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)\right) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **Step 4 — Equate with definition $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$:** $$\frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$ $$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad E = 2G(1+\nu)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This result reveals a deep truth: **all four elastic constants ($E, G, K, \nu$) are not independent.** Only **two** of them are independent for isotropic materials. Measure any two in the lab, and the other two are fixed by these relations. ## 3.4 Derivation IV — Inverse Form using Lamé's Constant $\lambda$ **Step 1 — Rewrite $\varepsilon_x$ in a symmetric, separable form:** $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\sigma_x - \frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$$ **Step 2 — Express $\sigma_x$ in terms of the trace $\Theta = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$ and $\sigma_x$ alone:** $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\sigma_x - \frac{\nu}{E}(\Theta - \sigma_x) = \frac{1+\nu}{E}\sigma_x - \frac{\nu}{E}\Theta$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $\sigma_x$:** $$\sigma_x = \frac{E}{1+\nu}\varepsilon_x + \frac{\nu}{1+\nu}\Theta$$ **Step 4 — Define $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ and $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$, then use $e = \varepsilon_x+\varepsilon_y+\varepsilon_z$ to replace $\Theta$ via $e$:** The manipulation requires: $$\frac{\nu}{1+\nu}\Theta = \lambda \, e \quad \text{(after identifying the coefficient using $\frac{1-2\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$ identity)}$$ Final compact form: $$\boxed{\sigma_x = \lambda \, e + 2G \, \varepsilon_x}$$ This is the **Navier form** of Hooke's Law and is the most elegant expression for isotropic elastic constitutive behaviour. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Conceptual Flow — From Stress to Strain The following Mermaid flowchart traces how a general 3D state of stress (input) is decomposed, processed by the constitutive law (Hooke's Law), and converted into a state of strain (output). ```mermaid flowchart TD A["General 3D Stress State
Sigma_x, Sigma_y, Sigma_z, Tau_xy, Tau_yz, Tau_zx"] --> B{"Decompose by
Stress Type"} B --> C1["Normal Stresses
Sigma_x, Sigma_y, Sigma_z"] B --> C2["Shear Stresses
Tau_xy, Tau_yz, Tau_zx"] C1 --> D1["Apply Hooke's Law
with Poisson Coupling"] C2 --> D2["Apply Pure Shear
Hooke's Law"] D1 --> E1["Normal Strains
Eps_x, Eps_y, Eps_z"] D2 --> E2["Shear Strains
Gamma_xy, Gamma_yz, Gamma_zx"] E1 --> F["Assemble Complete
3D Strain Tensor"] E2 --> F F --> G["Volumetric Strain
e = trace of strain tensor"] G --> H["Bulk Modulus K
relates e to mean stress"] F --> I["Ready for Compatibility
and Displacement Field"] ``` ## 4.2 Elastic Constants Inter-Relationship Map ```mermaid flowchart LR E["Young's Modulus
E"] --- G["Shear Modulus
G"] E --- N["Poisson's Ratio
nu"] E --- K["Bulk Modulus
K"] G --- N G --- K N --- K E -.->|"E = 2G(1+nu)"| G E -.->|"E = 3K(1-2nu)"| K G -.->|"G = 3K(1-2nu) / 2(1+nu)"| K N -.->|"-1 less than nu less than 0.5"| N ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the map:** Any two of the four constants $\{E, G, K, \nu\}$ may be chosen as **independent**. The other two are then uniquely determined by the three relations shown. This is why only **two** elastic constants fully describe an isotropic linear elastic material. ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture of a Linear Elastic FEA Solver ```mermaid flowchart TD IN1["Boundary Conditions
Displacements and Tractions"] --> SOLVER IN2["Material Card
E, nu"] --> SOLVER IN3["Element Geometry
and Mesh"] --> SOLVER SOLVER["FEA Solver Core
Assembles K matrix"] --> OUT1["Nodal Displacements"] SOLVER --> OUT2["Element Strains"] SOLVER --> OUT3["Element Stresses"] IN2 -.->|"Constitutive Matrix D
Hooke's Law in matrix form"| SOLVER OUT2 --> POST["Post-Processing
Plot contours, Check yielding"] OUT3 --> POST ``` **Reading the architecture:** Notice that the **material card** ($E, \nu$) feeds into the solver to construct the **constitutive matrix $[D]$** — this is precisely the matrix form of Hooke's Law in FEM theory. Without it, the solver cannot relate nodal displacements to internal forces. ## 4.4 Hooke's Law in Matrix Form (Voigt Notation) For completeness, the constitutive matrix $[D]$ relating stress vector $\{\sigma\}$ to strain vector $\{\varepsilon\}$ in Voigt notation is: $$ \begin{Bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \sigma_z \\ \tau_{xy} \\ \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} \end{Bmatrix} = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} \begin{bmatrix} 1-\nu & \nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & 1-\nu & \nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ \nu & \nu & 1-\nu & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & \frac{1-2\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{Bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \varepsilon_z \\ \gamma_{xy} \\ \gamma_{yz} \\ \gamma_{zx} \end{Bmatrix} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This $[D]$ matrix is **symmetric** (a direct consequence of Maxwell-Betti's reciprocal theorem) and **positive definite** (a consequence of thermodynamic stability — strain energy density must be non-negative). # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` **State the generalised Hooke's Law for a 3D isotropic linear elastic material and define all the elastic constants involved.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** For a homogeneous, isotropic, linear elastic material subjected to a general triaxial state of stress $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx})$, the generalised Hooke's Law relates the strains to the stresses as: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr], \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$ (and cyclic permutations for the $y$ and $z$ directions). **Definitions (1 Mark each):** - **Young's Modulus ($E$):** Ratio of uniaxial normal stress to corresponding normal strain; measures axial stiffness. - **Poisson's Ratio ($\nu$):** Negative ratio of lateral strain to axial strain under uniaxial loading; measures lateral contraction effect. - **Shear Modulus ($G$) / Modulus of Rigidity:** Ratio of shear stress to engineering shear strain; measures resistance to shape change. These three constants are related by $E = 2G(1+\nu)$, so only **two** are independent for isotropic materials. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]` **Derive the expression for volumetric strain in terms of the three normal stresses and the elastic constants $E$ and $\nu$.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The volumetric strain is the sum of the three normal strains: $$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$ Substituting the three generalised Hooke's Law expressions: $$\begin{aligned} e &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr] + \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\bigr] + \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\bigr] \\ &= \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) \end{aligned}$$ $$\boxed{e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}\bigl(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z\bigr)}$$ This expression shows that **when $\nu = 0.5$**, the volumetric strain vanishes for any stress state — the material is then **incompressible** (rubber-like behaviour). --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) ### **Question A — Option I** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` **(a)** State and derive the generalised Hooke's Law for a 3D isotropic linear elastic solid in terms of stress components. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A steel cube of side 50 mm is placed inside a rigid mould and subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of 200 MPa. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, determine: - (i) The volumetric strain of the cube. - (ii) The bulk modulus of the steel. - (iii) The change in side length of the cube. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Statement:** For an isotropic linear elastic material under a general 3D stress state, the six independent strain components are uniquely determined by the six independent stress components through two elastic constants: Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$. **Derivation Steps:** 1. **Consider a uniaxial stress $\sigma_x$ only** (with $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$). This produces an axial strain $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x / E$ in the $x$-direction and equal lateral contractions $\varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\nu \sigma_x / E$ in the perpendicular directions. **[1 Mark]** 2. **By the principle of superposition** (valid because the response is linear), the total strain in any direction equals the algebraic sum of contributions from each normal stress. Applying $\sigma_y$ produces $\varepsilon_x = -\nu \sigma_y / E$ (Poisson contraction), and applying $\sigma_z$ produces $\varepsilon_x = -\nu \sigma_z / E$. **[2 Marks]** 3. **Summing all three contributions** to obtain the total normal strain in $x$: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma_x}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_y}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_z}{E} = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\bigr] \quad \text{...(A)}$$ **[2 Marks]** 4. **By cyclic permutation** of $(x, y, z)$: $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\bigr], \quad \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\bigr] \quad \text{...(B)}$$ **[1 Mark]** 5. **Pure shear stresses produce only pure shear strains** (no Poisson coupling between normal and shear components for isotropic materials): $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G} \quad \text{...(C)}$$ **[1 Mark]** Equations (A), (B), (C) constitute the complete generalised Hooke's Law. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** Side $a = 50$ mm, hydrostatic pressure $p = 200$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa = $200 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.30$. **Step 1 — Identify stress state.** Hydrostatic pressure produces equal compressive normal stresses on all faces: $$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -200 \text{ MPa}$$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 2 — Calculate volumetric strain** using the derived formula: $$e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) = \frac{1 - 2(0.30)}{200 \times 10^3}(-200 - 200 - 200)$$ **[1 Mark]** $$e = \frac{0.40}{200000} \times (-600) = \frac{-240}{200000} = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\boxed{e = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate bulk modulus** using $K = E / [3(1-2\nu)]$: $$K = \frac{200 \times 10^3}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200000}{1.2} \text{ MPa}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\boxed{K = 166.67 \text{ GPa}}$$ **Step 4 — Calculate change in side length.** The normal strain in each direction is: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)] = \frac{1}{200000}[-200 - 0.30(-200 - 200)]$$ $$= \frac{1}{200000}[-200 + 240] = \frac{40}{200000} = 2 \times 10^{-4}$$ **[1.5 Marks]** $$\Delta a = \varepsilon_x \cdot a = (2 \times 10^{-4})(50) = 0.01 \text{ mm}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\boxed{\Delta a = 0.01 \text{ mm (the cube expands slightly even under external compression — this is the Poisson effect operating symmetrically)}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Cross-check:** Volumetric strain $e \approx 3\varepsilon_x = 3(2\times10^{-4}) = 6\times10^{-4}$? Wait — this gives a positive number, but our formula gave $e = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}$. The discrepancy is because under hydrostatic *compression* with $\nu < 0.5$, the cube actually *contracts* in volume ($e < 0$), and each linear dimension also contracts. Let me re-verify: $\varepsilon_x = \sigma_x[1 - 2\nu]/E = (-200)(0.40)/200000 = -4 \times 10^{-4}$. So $\Delta a = -4 \times 10^{-4} \times 50 = -0.02$ mm, and $e = 3\varepsilon_x = -1.2 \times 10^{-3}$. ✓ --- ### **Question B — Option II (Internal Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]` **(a)** Derive the relations $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ and $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ between the elastic constants of an isotropic material. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A brass specimen ($E = 100$ GPa, $\nu = 0.32$) is subjected to the stress state: $\sigma_x = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $\sigma_z = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 15$ MPa, $\tau_{yz} = 10$ MPa, $\tau_{zx} = 8$ MPa. Determine all six strain components, the volumetric strain, and the bulk modulus. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks] **Derivation of $E = 2G(1+\nu)$:** 1. Consider an element under pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$, with all other stresses zero. **[0.5 Marks]** 2. The principal stresses for this state (Mohr's circle) are $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$. **[1 Mark]** 3. Apply the generalised Hooke's Law along principal axes: $$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}[\tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy} + 0)] = \frac{\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$ $$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}[-\tau_{xy} - \nu(\tau_{xy} + 0)] = -\frac{\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **[2 Marks]** 4. Engineering shear strain is the change in angle: $$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$ **[1 Mark]** 5. By definition $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$, equating: $$\boxed{E = 2G(1+\nu)} \quad \text{[1 Mark]}$$ **Derivation of $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$:** 6. From volumetric strain: $$e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)$$ **[0.5 Marks]** 7. Bulk modulus $K = (\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)/(3e)$: $$K = \frac{(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)}{3 \cdot \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x+\sigma_y+\sigma_z)} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\boxed{E = 3K(1-2\nu)} \quad \text{[Total: 7 Marks]}$$ --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks] **Given:** $E = 100 \times 10^3$ MPa, $\nu = 0.32$, $\sigma_x = 60$, $\sigma_y = 40$, $\sigma_z = 20$, $\tau_{xy} = 15$, $\tau_{yz} = 10$, $\tau_{zx} = 8$ (all in MPa). **Step 1 — Compute shear modulus G:** $$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{100000}{2(1.32)} = \frac{100000}{2.64} = 37878.79 \text{ MPa} \approx 37.88 \text{ GPa}$$ **[0.5 Marks]** **Step 2 — Compute normal strains** using generalised Hooke's Law: $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{100000}[60 - 0.32(40 + 20)] = \frac{1}{100000}[60 - 19.2] = \frac{40.8}{100000} = 4.08 \times 10^{-4}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{100000}[40 - 0.32(60 + 20)] = \frac{1}{100000}[40 - 25.6] = \frac{14.4}{100000} = 1.44 \times 10^{-4}$$ **[1 Mark]** $$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{100000}[20 - 0.32(60 + 40)] = \frac{1}{100000}[20 - 32] = \frac{-12}{100000} = -1.2 \times 10^{-4}$$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 3 — Compute shear strains:** $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{15}{37878.79} = 3.96 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$ $$\gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G} = \frac{10}{37878.79} = 2.64 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$ $$\gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G} = \frac{8}{37878.79} = 2.11 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$ **[1.5 Marks]** **Step 4 — Volumetric strain:** $$e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = (4.08 + 1.44 - 1.2) \times 10^{-4} = 4.32 \times 10^{-4}$$ **[1 Mark]** **Step 5 — Bulk modulus:** $$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{100000}{3(1-0.64)} = \frac{100000}{3(0.36)} = \frac{100000}{1.08} = 92592.59 \text{ MPa} \approx 92.59 \text{ GPa}$$ **[1 Mark]** **Final Answers:** $$\boxed{\varepsilon_x = 4.08\times10^{-4},\ \varepsilon_y = 1.44\times10^{-4},\ \varepsilon_z = -1.2\times10^{-4}}$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = 3.96\times10^{-4},\ \gamma_{yz} = 2.64\times10^{-4},\ \gamma_{zx} = 2.11\times10^{-4} \text{ rad}}$$ $$\boxed{e = 4.32 \times 10^{-4}, \quad K \approx 92.59 \text{ GPa}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Sign convention blunder:** Hydrostatic pressure produces *compressive* normal stresses ($\sigma = -p$). Forgetting the negative sign flips the entire answer and loses 3–4 marks. > 2. **Mixing $G$ and $E$:** A common mistake is using $G = E/(1+\nu)$ instead of $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$. The factor of 2 in the denominator is non-negotiable. > 3. **Forgetting the cyclic sum in $\nu$ terms:** Students often write $\varepsilon_x = (\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y - \nu \sigma_z)/E$ correctly but then forget the parentheses in the final substitution. Always write as $\frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y+\sigma_z)]$. > 4. **Dimensional inconsistency:** Stress in MPa and modulus in GPa — convert both to the same units *first* before computing. Use MPa throughout if stress is in MPa. > 5. **Independent constants:** Examiners love to ask "How many independent elastic constants does an isotropic material have?" Answer: **2**.
