Saying "4" or "3" is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Hooke's Law** is the **constitutive equation** for linear elastic materials: it links stress to strain via material constants. **[Recall]** - **Uniaxial form:** $\sigma = E\varepsilon$ — the simplest statement of Hooke's Law. **[Recall]** - **3D form (normal):** $\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$, and cyclic permutations. **[Apply]** - **3D form (shear):** $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$, $\gamma_{yz} = \tau_{yz}/G$, $\gamma_{zx} = \tau_{zx}/G$. **[Apply]** - **Volumetric strain:** $e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$. **[Derive]** - **Key relations among constants:** $E = 2G(1+\nu) = 3K(1-2\nu)$, and $G = \frac{3K(1-2\nu)}{2(1+\nu)}$. **[Derive & Apply]** - **Independent constants:** Only **2** elastic constants are independent for isotropic linear elastic materials. **[Recall]** - **Stability bounds:** $E > 0$, $G > 0$, $K > 0$, $-1 < \nu < 0.5$. **[Recall]** - **Incompressibility limit:** $\nu \to 0.5 \Rightarrow K \to \infty$ (no volume change under any pressure). **[Recall]** - **Lamé's constant:** $\lambda = \frac{E\nu}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}$; used in inverse form $\sigma_x = \lambda e + 2G\varepsilon_x$. **[Apply]** - **Poisson's effect:** A tensile stress $\sigma_x$ causes *lateral contraction* in $y$ and $z$ (negative strains). **[Recall]** - **Symmetry of $[D]$ matrix:** The constitutive matrix in Voigt notation is symmetric (from Betti's reciprocal theorem). **[Recall]** - **Engineering vs tensorial shear strain:** $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$. The factor of 2 appears because engineering shear strain is the *change in angle*, not the tensorial component. **[Apply]** - **Use of the relations in problems:** If $E$ and $\nu$ are given, compute $G$ and $K$ via the relations before proceeding to strain calculations. **[Apply]**

topics.title: Equations for Linear Elastic Isotropic Solids in Terms of Young's Modulus and Poisson's Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Equations for Linear Elastic Isotropic Solids in Terms of Young's Modulus and Poisson's Ratio ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Linear Elastic Isotropic Solid:** A material is termed *linear elastic* if its strain response is directly proportional to the applied stress (obeys Hooke's Law) and is fully recoverable upon unloading. It is termed *isotropic* if its mechanical properties are identical in all directions at a given point. For such a material, the complete stress–strain behaviour at any interior point is governed by **only two independent elastic constants**. The most common and board-preferred pair is: - **Young's Modulus (E)** — measures axial stiffness - **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** — measures lateral contraction per unit axial extension The stress–strain relationships for a 3D element subjected to normal stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ and shear stresses $\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$ are collectively known as the **Generalized Hooke's Law**. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates derivation of all stress–strain equations, volume strain expressions, and plane stress/plane strain simplifications explicitly in terms of **E and ν**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **rubber cube held in your hand** and squeezed uniformly from three perpendicular faces: - It compresses axially (this resistance is what *Young's Modulus E* quantifies). - At the same time, it bulges outward sideways — a lateral expansion. The ratio of this sideways bulge to the axial compression is *Poisson's Ratio ν*. If you replace the rubber with a hard **steel cube**, the same push produces: - A much smaller compression (high **E**). - A proportionally smaller bulge, but the *ratio* between bulge and compression is still fixed (this is the constant **ν**). Now imagine replacing your three-perpendicular push with a **shear/twisting** motion (sliding one face of the cube parallel to another). The cube distorts into a parallelepiped. The resistance to this twisting is a third property called the **Shear Modulus G**. Remarkably, for an isotropic material, **G is not independent** — it is mathematically fixed by E and ν through the equation $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$. ### 1.3 Why Only Two Constants? For a fully anisotropic material, the generalized Hooke's law has **21 independent elastic constants**. Isotropy reduces this drastically because every direction is equivalent. Counting the number of mathematically distinct equations in 3D leads to the conclusion that only **two constants** can independently describe the entire elastic response. | Property | Independent? | |---|---| | Young's Modulus E | **Yes (independent)** | | Poisson's Ratio ν | **Yes (independent)** | | Shear Modulus G | No — derived: $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ | | Bulk Modulus K | No — derived: $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Generalized Hooke's Law (3D) For a 3D element, six stress components ($\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z, \tau_{xy}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zx}$) produce six strain components ($\varepsilon_x, \varepsilon_y, \varepsilon_z, \gamma_{xy}, \gamma_{yz}, \gamma_{zx}$). **Normal strains** (extensional strains along principal Cartesian axes): $$ \begin{aligned} \varepsilon_x &= \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\big] \\ \varepsilon_y &= \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\big] \\ \varepsilon_z &= \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\big] \end{aligned} $$ **Shear strains** (engineering shear strain = 2 × tensorial shear strain): $$ \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{xy} $$ $$ \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{yz} $$ $$ \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G} = \frac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{zx} $$ ### 2.2 Volumetric (Dilatation) Strain The fractional change in volume per unit original volume: $$ e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z $$ Substituting the three normal strain expressions: $$ e = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}\,(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) $$ Defining mean (hydrostatic) stress as $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$: $$ e = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}\,\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_m}{K} $$ This defines the **Bulk Modulus**: $\;K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$. ### 2.3 Plane Stress (Thin Plates, $\sigma_z = 0$) For thin plates loaded in-plane only: $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y) $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x) $$ $$ \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) $$ ### 2.4 Plane Strain (Long Bodies, $\varepsilon_z = 0$) For long bodies (dams, tunnels, retaining walls) where the third dimension is constrained: $$ \sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x\big] $$ ### 2.5 Principal Stress Form When the stress tensor is diagonal (only $\sigma_1, \sigma_2, \sigma_3$): $$ \varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_3 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_3 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2)\big] $$ ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]$ | Normal strain in x | dimensionless | | 2 | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)]$ | Normal strain in y | dimensionless | | 3 | $\varepsilon_z = \dfrac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]$ | Normal strain in z | dimensionless | | 4 | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{xy}$ | Shear strain in xy-plane | rad | | 5 | $\gamma_{yz} = \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{yz}$ | Shear strain in yz-plane | rad | | 6 | $\gamma_{zx} = \dfrac{2(1+\nu)}{E}\tau_{zx}$ | Shear strain in zx-plane | rad | | 7 | $e = \dfrac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ | Volumetric strain | dimensionless | | 8 | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear modulus in terms of E, ν | GPa | | 9 | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ | Bulk modulus in terms of E, ν | GPa | | 10 | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$ | Poisson's ratio via K, G | dimensionless | | 11 | $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K + G}$ | Young's modulus via K, G | GPa | | 12 | $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ (plane strain) | Out-of-plane stress | MPa | | 13 | $\varepsilon_z = -\dfrac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ (plane stress) | Out-of-plane strain | dimensionless | ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility These equations form the foundation of: - **Pressure vessel design** (boilers, LPG cylinders, rocket fuel tanks). - **Structural analysis of dams and tunnels** (plane strain idealization). - **Sheet metal forming and rolling** (plane stress idealization). - **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** input data for isotropic material models. - **Geomechanics** — soil and rock behaviour under hydrostatic and deviatoric loading. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ We use a pure shear state superimposed on a carefully chosen uniaxial tension so that the principal stresses of the combined system are $\sigma_1 = \tau$ and $\sigma_2 = -\tau$ (simple shear with shear stress $\tau$). **Step 1 — Identify the principal stresses of pure shear.** For a pure shear element with shear stress $\tau$ acting on faces whose normals are along x and y: $$ \sigma_1 = +\tau, \qquad \sigma_2 = -\tau, \qquad \sigma_3 = 0 $$ **Step 2 — Compute principal strains using generalized Hooke's law.** $$ \varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)\big] = \frac{1}{E}\big[\tau - \nu(-\tau + 0)\big] = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} $$ $$ \varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_2 - \nu(\sigma_1 + \sigma_3)\big] = \frac{1}{E}\big[-\tau - \nu(\tau + 0)\big] = -\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the engineering shear strain.** The principal directions of a pure shear state are oriented at 45° to the original x-face. The engineering shear strain $\gamma$ on the original x-y faces relates to the principal strains as: $$ \gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 $$ Substituting: $$ \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E} - \left(-\frac{\tau(1+\nu)}{E}\right) = \frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the definition $G = \tau/\gamma$.** $$ G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma_{xy}} = \frac{\tau}{\frac{2\tau(1+\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} $$ This is the master relation between E, G, and ν. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation of $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ **Step 1 — Apply equal triaxial pressure (hydrostatic compression).** $$ \sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p \quad \text{(compressive hydrostatic pressure)} $$ **Step 2 — Compute normal strain in any direction (using x as representative).** $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\big] = \frac{1}{E}\big[-p - \nu(-p - p)\big] = \frac{-p(1 - 2\nu)}{E} $$ **Step 3 — Sum the three normal strains to get volumetric strain.** $$ e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = 3 \cdot \frac{-p(1-2\nu)}{E} = \frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E} $$ **Step 4 — Apply the definition $K = -p/e$ (pressure required per unit volumetric compression).** $$ K = \frac{-p}{e} = \frac{-p}{\frac{-3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} $$ This is the master relation between E, K, and ν. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Volumetric Strain $e = \dfrac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$ **Step 1 — Write the three normal strains.** $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)] $$ $$ \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)] $$ **Step 2 — Add them.** $$ e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z $$ $$ e = \frac{1}{E}\big[(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) - \nu(2\sigma_x + 2\sigma_y + 2\sigma_z)\big] $$ **Step 3 — Factor.** $$ e = \frac{(1 - 2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) $$ **Step 4 — Introduce mean stress.** Let $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z}{3}$. Then: $$ e = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{E}\,\sigma_m $$ This confirms that the volumetric strain depends *only* on the mean (hydrostatic) stress, not on the deviatoric part. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Plane Strain Out-of-Plane Stress $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ **Step 1 — Plane strain assumption: $\varepsilon_z = 0$.** $$ 0 = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\big] $$ **Step 2 — Solve for $\sigma_z$.** $$ \sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) $$ **Step 3 — Substitute back into the expressions for $\varepsilon_x$ and $\varepsilon_y$.** For $\varepsilon_x$: $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y))\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu^2\sigma_x - \nu^2\sigma_y\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\big[\sigma_x(1 - \nu^2) - \nu\sigma_y(1 + \nu)\big] $$ **Step 4 — Factor out $(1+\nu)$.** $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\big] $$ Similarly, by symmetry: $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x\big] $$ This is the standard plane-strain form. $\blacksquare$ --- ### 3.5 Derivation of the Special Case: Uniaxial Tension (Sanity Check) Set $\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$ and apply $\sigma_x = \sigma$: $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{\sigma}{E}, \qquad \varepsilon_y = \varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu\sigma}{E} $$ - Axial strain matches the elementary definition of E. - Lateral strain confirms $\nu = -\dfrac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{axial}}$. - Volumetric strain: $e = \dfrac{\sigma(1-2\nu)}{E}$. For most metals, $0 < \nu < 0.5$, so $e > 0$ under tension (the body swells slightly in volume). --- ### 3.6 Summary Matrix — Where Each Equation is Used | Condition | Key Equation Simplification | Use | |---|---|---| | Uniaxial stress ($\sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$) | $\varepsilon_x = \sigma/E$ | Tensile test | | Biaxial stress (plane stress) | $\varepsilon_z = -\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)/E$ | Thin pressure vessel wall | | Hydrostatic (equal triaxial) | $\varepsilon = (1-2\nu)\sigma/E$ | Underwater structures | | Plane strain | $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ | Long dams, thick cylinders | | Pure shear | $\gamma = 2(1+\nu)\tau/E$ | Shafts in torsion | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block — Elastic Constants Inter-Relationship Map ```mermaid graph LR E["YOUNGS MODULUS E"]:::indep NU["POISSONS RATIO NU"]:::indep G["SHEAR MODULUS G"]:::dep K["BULK MODULUS K"]:::dep L["LAMEDA MU"]:::dep E -- "G = E / 2(1+NU)" --> G NU -- "G = E / 2(1+NU)" --> G E -- "K = E / 3(1-2NU)" --> K NU -- "K = E / 3(1-2NU)" --> K E -- "L = NU E / (1+NU)(1-2NU)" --> L NU -- "L = NU E / (1+NU)(1-2NU)" --> L G -- "MU = G" --> L K -- "L = K - 2G / 3" --> L classDef indep fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef dep fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** *E* and *ν* are the **independent** (input) constants. *G*, *K*, and *λ* (Lamé's first constant) are **derived** from them. This is the canonical KTU 2024 framework. --- ### 4.2 Mermaid Block — Functional Flow: From Stress Input to Strain Output ```mermaid flowchart TD A[APPLIED 3D STRESS STATE
sx sy sz txy tyz tzx]:::input B{ISOTROPIC LINEAR
ELASTIC MATERIAL}:::process C[NORMAL STRAINS
ex ey ez]:::out1 D[SHEAR STRAINS
gxy gyz gzx]:::out2 E[VOLUMETRIC STRAIN
e = ex + ey + ez]:::out3 A --> B B -- "E and NU parameters" --> C B -- "G = E / 2(1+NU)" --> D C --> E C:::out1 D:::out2 E:::out3 classDef input fill:#f4b183,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef process fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef out1 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef out2 fill:#bdd7ee,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef out3 fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** A general 3D stress state enters the constitutive model. Normal strains emerge using *E* and *ν*; shear strains emerge using *G*; volumetric strain is then assembled from the three normal strains. --- ### 4.3 Mermaid Block — Plane Stress vs. Plane Strain Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S[START: Identify Geometry and Loading]:::start Q1{Thin plate?
sigma z = 0?}:::decision Q2{Long body?
epsilon z = 0?}:::decision PS[PLANE STRESS
sz = 0
ez = -nu sx+sy / E]:::case1 PE[PLANE STRAIN
ez = 0
sz = nu sx+sy]:::case2 S --> Q1 Q1 -- YES --> PS Q1 -- NO --> Q2 Q2 -- YES --> PE Q2 -- NO --> F[3D GENERALIZED
HOOKES LAW]:::case3 classDef start fill:#7030a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff classDef decision fill:#ffe699,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef case1 fill:#b4c7e7,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef case2 fill:#c5e0b4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 classDef case3 fill:#f4b183,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the diagram:** This is the **KTU favourite** decision tree. Identify whether the geometry enforces *σ_z = 0* (plane stress — thin plates) or *ε_z = 0* (plane strain — long dams, thick cylinders). If neither, use the full 3D generalized Hooke's law. --- ### 4.4 Schematic Description of Stress Element (Text-Based) For visualisation when a Mermaid block is insufficient, consider a cube of side *a*: ``` +Y | | τxy |-------→ | ↘ | σy ↓ σx → X | ↓ Z (out of page) ``` - $\sigma_x$ acts on the face whose normal is the +X direction. - $\sigma_y$ acts on the face whose normal is the +Y direction. - $\tau_{xy}$ acts on the same faces, parallel to the opposite axis. - All six faces of the cube carry one normal and two shear components; the cube remains in static equilibrium. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** *Define a linear elastic isotropic material. Why does it require only two independent elastic constants to fully describe its behaviour?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **linear elastic isotropic material** is one that obeys Hooke's Law (stress is linearly proportional to strain) and exhibits identical mechanical properties in all directions at any point. **Why only two constants (2 marks for explanation):** In the most general 3D anisotropic case, Hooke's law involves 21 independent constants. Isotropy enforces equivalence of all directions, which mathematically reduces the number of independent constants to **two**. Common choices are (E, ν) or (K, G). The other moduli such as shear modulus G and bulk modulus K are derivable from E and ν via $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ and $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** *Write the expressions for the three normal strains and the volumetric strain for a 3D element under stresses $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ in terms of E and ν.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** The three normal strains are: $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)] $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)] $$ $$ \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)] $$ The volumetric (dilatation) strain is: $$ e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) $$ --- ### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]* **(a)** *For a 3D linear elastic isotropic material, derive the relation $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ between the shear modulus G, Young's modulus E, and Poisson's ratio ν.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A steel cube of side 50 mm is subjected to a uniform hydrostatic pressure of 150 MPa. Given $E = 200$ GPa and $\nu = 0.30$, determine (i) the volumetric strain, (ii) the change in volume, and (iii) the bulk modulus K.* **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation of $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$ (7 marks):** - 1 mark: State the goal and identify the stress state (pure shear with $\sigma_1 = \tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$, $\sigma_3 = 0$). - 2 marks: Compute principal strains using generalized Hooke's law: $\varepsilon_1 = \tau(1+\nu)/E$ and $\varepsilon_2 = -\tau(1+\nu)/E$. - 2 marks: Relate engineering shear strain to principal strains: $\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = 2\tau(1+\nu)/E$. - 1 mark: Apply $G = \tau/\gamma_{xy}$ to obtain $G = E/[2(1+\nu)]$. - 1 mark: Final boxed answer with correct unit context (GPa). **Part (b) — Hydrostatic Pressure on a Steel Cube (7 marks):** **Given:** $a = 50$ mm, $p = 150$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.30$. **(i) Volumetric strain (2 marks):** For hydrostatic stress: $\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = -p$ (compressive). $$ e = \frac{(1-2\nu)}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z) = \frac{(1-2\nu)(-3p)}{E} $$ $$ e = \frac{(1 - 0.60)(-3 \times 150)}{200 \times 10^3} = \frac{(0.40)(-450)}{200000} $$ $$ e = \frac{-180}{200000} = -9.0 \times 10^{-4} $$ The negative sign indicates volume *decrease* under pressure. **|e| = 9.0 × 10⁻⁴** (compressive). **(ii) Change in volume (2 marks):** Original volume $V_0 = a^3 = (0.050)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^{-4}$ m³. $$ \Delta V = e \cdot V_0 = (-9.0 \times 10^{-4})(1.25 \times 10^{-4}) $$ $$ \Delta V = -1.125 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^3 $$ The volume *decreases* by $1.125 \times 10^{-7}$ m³. **(iii) Bulk modulus (1 mark):** $$ K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{200}{3(1 - 0.60)} = \frac{200}{1.2} = 166.67 \text{ GPa} $$ **Verification using $K = -p/e$ (2 marks):** $$ K = \frac{-p}{e} = \frac{-150 \times 10^6}{-9.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 1.667 \times 10^{11} \text{ Pa} = 166.67 \text{ GPa} \;\checkmark $$ --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]* **(a)** *Derive the expressions for the normal strains $\varepsilon_x$ and $\varepsilon_y$ in a state of **plane strain** in terms of E and ν.* **(7 marks)** **(b)** *A long retaining wall is subjected to in-plane stresses $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa and $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa. Assuming plane strain conditions, and given $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.25$, find the strains $\varepsilon_x$, $\varepsilon_y$, and the out-of-plane stress $\sigma_z$.* **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Plane Strain Derivation (7 marks):** - 1 mark: State the plane strain assumption $\varepsilon_z = 0$. - 2 marks: Substitute into the generalized Hooke's law for $\varepsilon_z$ and solve: $0 = (1/E)[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)] \;\Rightarrow\; \sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$. - 2 marks: Substitute $\sigma_z$ back into the expressions for $\varepsilon_x$ and $\varepsilon_y$, expand, and factor. - 1 mark: Final boxed answers: $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\big]$ and $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x\big]$. - 1 mark: Mention engineering significance (long dams, tunnels, thick cylinders). **Part (b) — Numerical Application (7 marks):** **Given:** $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa, $\nu = 0.25$. **Step 1 — Out-of-plane stress (2 marks):** $$ \sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) = 0.25(80 + 40) = 0.25(120) = 30 \text{ MPa} $$ **Step 2 — Compute $\varepsilon_x$ (2 marks):** $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1.25}{210 \times 10^3}\big[0.75(80) - 0.25(40)\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = \frac{1.25}{210000}\big[60 - 10\big] = \frac{1.25 \times 50}{210000} = \frac{62.5}{210000} $$ $$ \varepsilon_x = 2.976 \times 10^{-4} \quad (\text{tensile}) $$ **Step 3 — Compute $\varepsilon_y$ (2 marks):** $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\big[(1-\nu)\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{1.25}{210000}\big[0.75(40) - 0.25(80)\big] $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = \frac{1.25}{210000}\big[30 - 20\big] = \frac{1.25 \times 10}{210000} = \frac{12.5}{210000} $$ $$ \varepsilon_y = 5.952 \times 10^{-5} \quad (\text{tensile}) $$ **Step 4 — Verify $\varepsilon_z = 0$ (1 mark):** Substitute $\sigma_z = 30$ MPa into the full 3D expression: $$ \varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)] = \frac{1}{210000}[30 - 0.25(120)] = \frac{1}{210000}[30 - 30] = 0 \;\checkmark $$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Sign error in shear strain equation:** Many students write $\gamma = \tau \cdot 2(1+\nu)/E$ but forget that engineering shear strain is twice the tensorial shear strain, i.e. $\gamma_{xy} = 2\varepsilon_{xy}$. Either form is correct, but do not mix factors of 2 inconsistently. > 2. **Confusing plane stress and plane strain:** Plane stress $\Rightarrow \sigma_z = 0$ (thin plates). Plane strain $\Rightarrow \varepsilon_z = 0$ (long bodies). A student who swaps these loses 3–4 marks immediately. > 3. **Forgetting to substitute $\sigma_z$ back** when deriving plane-strain expressions. Always write the final result in terms of only $\sigma_x$ and $\sigma_y$. > 4. **Unit inconsistency:** E is typically in GPa, stress in MPa. Convert before substituting: 1 GPa = 1000 MPa. Mixing units is the single most common reason for numerical errors. > 5. **Volumetric strain under hydrostatic compression is negative** (volume decreases). The sign of $e$ must reflect the loading direction. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Generalized Hooke's Law** in 3D: three normal strain equations + three shear strain equations. - ✅ **Two independent constants** define an isotropic linear elastic solid: (E, ν) is the standard pair. - ✅ **Derived relations (must memorize):** - $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ - $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ - $E = \dfrac{9KG}{3K+G}$ - $\nu = \dfrac{3K - 2G}{2(3K + G)}$ - ✅ **Volumetric strain** depends *only* on the *mean* (hydrostatic) stress, not on the deviatoric stress. - ✅ **Plane stress** = thin plate = $\sigma_z = 0$, $\varepsilon_z \neq 0$. - ✅ **Plane strain** = long body = $\varepsilon_z = 0$, $\sigma_z \neq 0$ (and equals $\nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$). - ✅ **Typical values:** Steel — $E \approx 200$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.30$; Aluminium — $E \approx 70$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.33$; Rubber — $E \approx 0.01$–$0.1$ GPa, $\nu \approx 0.45$–$0.49$. - ✅ **Theoretical bounds on ν:** $−1 < \nu < 0.5$ for stable isotropic materials. Most engineering materials have $0 < \nu < 0.5$. - ✅ **Sanity check:** For uniaxial tension, $\varepsilon_x = \sigma/E$ (must recover the 1D form). - ✅ **Common exam trick:** Verify that the bulk modulus relation $K = E/[3(1-2\nu)]$ is dimensionally consistent and that ν approaches 0.5 → K → ∞ (incompressible limit).

topics.title: Hooke's Law for Plane Stress and Plane Strain Conditions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hooke's Law for Plane Stress and Plane Strain Conditions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Generalized Hooke's Law (The Parent Equation)

In its most general three-dimensional form, **Hooke's Law** states that for a linearly elastic, homogeneous, and isotropic material, each normal strain component is a linear function of all three normal stress components, while each shear strain is linearly related to its corresponding shear stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalized Hooke's Law (3D):**
>
> $$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$
> $$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$
> $$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$
> $$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}$$

Where:
- **E** = Young's Modulus of Elasticity (units: **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- **ν (nu)** = Poisson's Ratio (dimensionless, typically **0 < ν < 0.5**)
- **G** = Modulus of Rigidity, related by **G = E / [2(1+ν)]**

## 1.2 The "Why" Behind Plane Stress and Plane Strain

In real engineering problems, we rarely need all six stress/strain components. Most practical structures can be idealized into one of two special 2D states:

> [!NOTE]
> **Plane Stress (Thin Structures):**
> A state of stress where the stress components acting on one face of an element vanish. Typically occurs in **thin plates loaded in their plane** (e.g., a thin metal diaphragm, a flat gear, a thin-walled pressure vessel far from edges).
>
> **Plane Strain (Thick/Long Structures):**
> A state of strain where the strain components in one direction vanish. Typically occurs in **long bodies with uniform cross-section** loaded perpendicular to their length (e.g., a long retaining wall, a gravity dam, a tunnel, a pipe under internal pressure with end caps rigidly constrained).

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogies

**Analogy 1 — Plane Stress = A Postcard on a Table:**
Imagine pushing on a thin postcard lying flat on a table. You apply forces in the plane of the card. The stresses on its top and bottom faces are essentially **zero** because the card is so thin that no internal force can be sustained in the thickness direction. The card, however, will still stretch sideways (Poisson effect — the thin dimension contracts freely). This is *plane stress*.

**Analogy 2 — Plane Strain = A Slice of Toast in a Long Loaf:**
Take a very long, thick loaf of bread. Cut one slice from the middle. Every slice experiences identical conditions because the ends are far away. The slice cannot stretch in the longitudinal direction (the bread in front and behind hold it rigid), so **ε_longitudinal = 0**. However, a longitudinal stress *does* develop internally because of Poisson's effect — this is *plane strain*.

## 1.4 Formal KTU 2024 Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane Stress Condition:**
> A state of stress in which the normal stress and both shear stresses acting perpendicular to a given plane are zero.
> Mathematically: **σz = 0, τyz = 0, τzx = 0**
> The remaining in-plane components σx, σy, τxy are non-zero.
> **Important:** εz is *not* zero — it is given by the Poisson contraction: **εz = -(ν/E)(σx + σy)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plane Strain Condition:**
> A state of strain in which the normal strain and both shear strains in a given direction are zero.
> Mathematically: **εz = 0, γyz = 0, γzx = 0**
> The in-plane strains εx, εy, γxy are non-zero.
> **Important:** σz is *not* zero — it is given by **σz = ν(σx + σy)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Assumptions

Both reduced forms of Hooke's Law rely on these four assumptions (which form the foundation of **Linear Elasticity Theory**):

- **Linearity:** Stress is proportional to strain (valid below the proportional limit).
- **Homogeneity:** Material properties are the same at every point.
- **Isotropy:** Material properties are the same in every direction at a given point.
- **No Initial Stresses:** The body is initially stress-free.

## 2.2 Plane Stress — Derivation Logic in Steps

Starting from the general 3D Hooke's law, we apply the plane stress assumptions to eliminate the z-components:

1. **Step 1:** Set σz = 0, τyz = 0, τzx = 0 in the general equations.
2. **Step 2:** Observe that the shear strain equations γyz = τyz/G and γzx = τzx/G become zero trivially.
3. **Step 3:** Simplify εx and εy by substituting σz = 0.
4. **Step 4:** Compute εz — although σz = 0, εz is not zero because of **Poisson's effect** from σx and σy.
5. **Step 5:** The remaining shear equation γxy = τxy/G is preserved.

## 2.3 Plane Strain — Derivation Logic in Steps

The plane strain derivation is *subtler* because we cannot simply set stresses to zero; we must use the strain conditions to *solve for* the stress:

1. **Step 1:** Set εz = 0, γyz = 0, γzx = 0.
2. **Step 2:** From εz = 0 in the general Hooke's law: solve algebraically for σz in terms of σx and σy.
3. **Step 3:** Substitute this σz back into the εx and εy equations to eliminate σz.
4. **Step 4:** Simplify the resulting expressions using algebraic identities.
5. **Step 5:** Alternatively, use **Lamé's constants (λ and G)** to express the stress-strain relations compactly.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are board-exam favorites. Memorize the boxed values of ν, E, G inter-relations.**

### Master Formula Table

| **Condition** | **Equation** | **Physical Meaning** |
|---|---|---|
| **Plane Stress** | $\varepsilon_x = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y)$ | x-strain under biaxial in-plane stress |
| **Plane Stress** | $\varepsilon_y = \dfrac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x)$ | y-strain under biaxxial in-plane stress |
| **Plane Stress** | $\varepsilon_z = -\dfrac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ | Out-of-plane Poisson contraction (non-zero!) |
| **Plane Stress** | $\gamma_{xy} = \dfrac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$ | In-plane shear strain |
| **Plane Strain** | $\sigma_x = \dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y\right]$ | x-stress from in-plane strains |
| **Plane Strain** | $\sigma_y = \dfrac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[\nu\varepsilon_x + (1-\nu)\varepsilon_y\right]$ | y-stress from in-plane strains |
| **Plane Strain** | $\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)$ | Out-of-plane stress (non-zero!) |
| **Plane Strain** | $\tau_{xy} = G\gamma_{xy}$ | In-plane shear stress |
| **Universal** | $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear-Young relation |
| **Universal** | $\lambda = \dfrac{\nu E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} = \dfrac{2G\nu}{1-2\nu}$ | First Lamé constant |

### Reduced Stiffness (Q-Matrix) for Plane Stress

In **matrix form** for plane stress, the stress-strain relation is $\{sigma\} = [Q]\{\varepsilon\}$:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{E}{1-\nu^2} \begin{bmatrix} 1 & \nu & 0 \\ \nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & \dfrac{1-\nu}{2} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

### Reduced Stiffness (D-Matrix) for Plane Strain

For plane strain using **Lamé constants**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} \lambda + 2G & \lambda & 0 \\ \lambda & \lambda + 2G & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & G \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Where These Equations Save the Day

- **Aerospace:** Thin wing skins in aircraft are modeled as plane stress elements to compute thermal and aerodynamic loads.
- **Civil:** Long dams, tunnels, retaining walls, and pipelines are analyzed using plane strain to predict settlement and failure.
- **Pressure Vessel Design:** The thin-wall approximation assumes plane stress to compute hoop and longitudinal stresses.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Embankments and slopes are modeled under plane strain conditions.
- **Composite Laminates (Aerospace/Automotive):** Each ply is treated as a plane stress element; stiffnesses are then stacked using Classical Lamination Theory.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Plane Stress Reduced Hooke's Law

**Starting Point (Generalized 3D Hooke's Law):**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]
$$

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}
$$

**Apply Plane Stress Assumption:** σz = 0, τyz = 0, τzx = 0.

**Step 1 — Simplify εx:**
Substitute σz = 0 into the εx equation.

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + 0)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y)}
$$

**Step 2 — Simplify εy:**
By symmetry of substitution (swap x and y in Step 1).

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + 0)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu \sigma_x)}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute εz (the non-trivial result):**
Substitute σz = 0 but recognize that εz depends on σx and σy through Poisson's effect.

$$
\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[0 - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)}
$$

This is the **Poisson contraction** in the thickness direction of a thin plate under in-plane loading. The negative sign indicates contraction when σx + σy > 0 (tension).

**Step 4 — Shear strain equations:**
Since τyz = 0 and τzx = 0, we get γyz = 0 and γzx = 0 trivially. The only remaining shear strain is:

$$
\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Plane Strain Reduced Hooke's Law

**Starting Point:** Same generalized 3D Hooke's law.

**Apply Plane Strain Assumption:** εz = 0, γyz = 0, γzx = 0.

**Step 1 — Solve for σz from εz = 0:**

$$
0 = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)
$$

This is the **fundamental result** — plane strain does not mean σz = 0; instead, σz is internally induced by the constraint on εz.

**Step 2 — Substitute σz into the εx equation:**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y - \nu \cdot \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu \sigma_y - \nu^2 \sigma_x - \nu^2 \sigma_y\right]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x(1 - \nu^2) - \nu \sigma_y(1 + \nu)\right]
$$

**Step 3 — Factor (1+ν) from both terms:**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}\left[\sigma_x(1-\nu) - \nu \sigma_y\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Invert the relation to express σx in terms of εx, εy:**

By symmetry of the system (treat εx and εy symmetrically), the inverse relationship is obtained by inverting the 2×2 matrix relating (εx, εy) to (σx, σy). The result is:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y\right]
$$

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[\nu\varepsilon_x + (1-\nu)\varepsilon_y\right]
$$

**Step 5 — Derive the shear stress:**

$$
\tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}
$$

**Final Plane Strain Hooke's Law Set:**

$$
\boxed{\sigma_x = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}\left[\nu\varepsilon_x + (1-\nu)\varepsilon_y\right]}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}}
$$

## 3.3 Alternative Derivation Using Lamé Constants (λ and G)

For elegance in tensor analysis and FEM, we use **Lamé constants**:

$$
\sigma_x = \lambda \cdot e + 2G\varepsilon_x, \quad \sigma_y = \lambda \cdot e + 2G\varepsilon_y, \quad \sigma_z = \lambda \cdot e + 2G\varepsilon_z
$$

Where **e = εx + εy + εz** is the **volumetric (dilatation) strain**.

**For Plane Strain (εz = 0):**

$$
e = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + 0 = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y
$$

Therefore:

$$
\sigma_x = \lambda(\varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y) + 2G\varepsilon_x = (\lambda + 2G)\varepsilon_x + \lambda \varepsilon_y
$$

$$
\sigma_y = (\lambda + 2G)\varepsilon_y + \lambda \varepsilon_x
$$

Substituting **λ = νE/[(1+ν)(1-2ν)]** and **λ + 2G = E(1-ν)/[(1+ν)(1-2ν)]** recovers the same result as Steps 1–5 above.

**Engineering Significance of Lamé Constants:**
- **λ** controls volumetric response.
- **G** controls shape (deviatoric) response.
- The decomposition σ_ij = λ·e·δ_ij + 2G·ε_ij is the basis of all linear elastic FEM codes.

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Plane Stress vs Plane Strain

| **Aspect** | **Plane Stress** | **Plane Strain** |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Application** | Thin plates, diaphragms | Long dams, tunnels, thick cylinders |
| **Zero Stress Components** | σz = τyz = τzx = 0 | None (only strains are zero) |
| **Zero Strain Components** | None (εz ≠ 0 due to Poisson) | εz = γyz = γzx = 0 |
| **Out-of-plane variable** | εz is computed (non-zero) | σz is computed (non-zero) |
| **Equation Form** | Strains are functions of stresses | Stresses are functions of strains |
| **Stiffness Factor** | E/(1−ν²) | E/[(1+ν)(1−2ν)] |
| **Shear relation** | γxy = τxy/G (same) | τxy = G·γxy (same) |
| **Symmetry** | About thickness direction | About longitudinal axis |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flowchart — Decision Tree for Applying Hooke's Law

The following Mermaid diagram summarizes the conceptual logic for choosing the correct form of Hooke's law based on the geometry and loading of the structural element:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Structural Element with Linear Elastic Material"] --> B{"Geometry and Loading Analysis"}
    B -->|Thin element in-plane loaded| C["Plane Stress Condition"]
    B -->|Long thick element with constrained ends| D["Plane Strain Condition"]
    C --> C1["Sigma z equals zero"]
    C --> C2["Tau yz equals zero"]
    C --> C3["Tau zx equals zero"]
    C1 --> E["Epsilon z is nonzero from Poisson contraction"]
    C2 --> F["Gamma yz equals zero"]
    C3 --> G["Gamma zx equals zero"]
    E --> H["Reduced Hookes Law Plane Stress"]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> D1["Epsilon z equals zero"]
    D --> D2["Gamma yz equals zero"]
    D --> D3["Gamma zx equals zero"]
    D1 --> I["Sigma z is nonzero equals nu times sigma x plus sigma y"]
    D2 --> J["Tau yz equals zero"]
    D3 --> K["Tau zx equals zero"]
    I --> L["Reduced Hookes Law Plane Strain"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    H --> M["Solve for in plane strains given stresses"]
    L --> N["Solve for in plane stresses given strains"]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Data Flow in Solving Plane Problems

The following block diagram illustrates the data flow when solving plane stress or plane strain problems using the matrix formulation:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["Input Stresses: Sigma x, Sigma y, Tau xy"] --> B1["Plane Stress Stiffness Matrix Q"]
    A2["Input Strains: Epsilon x, Epsilon y, Gamma xy"] --> B2["Plane Strain Stiffness Matrix D"]
    B1 --> C1["Multiplication Q times epsilon"]
    B2 --> C2["Multiplication D times epsilon"]
    C1 --> D1["Output Strains Epsilon x, Epsilon y, Gamma xy and Epsilon z"]
    C2 --> D2["Output Stresses Sigma x, Sigma y, Tau xy and Sigma z"]
    D1 --> E1["Verify compatibility and equilibrium"]
    D2 --> E2["Verify compatibility and equilibrium"]
```

## 4.3 Physical Schematic — Stress Element Comparison

The two stress states can be visualized as follows:

```
       PLANE STRESS ELEMENT                  PLANE STRAIN ELEMENT
       (Thin Plate)                          (Long Body Cross-Section)

          ↑ σy                                   ↑ σy
          |                                      |
    σx ←——●———→ σx                         σx ←——●———→ σx
          |                                      |
          ↓ σy                                   ↓ σy

   Top/Bottom faces: ZERO stress            Top/Bottom faces: NON-ZERO σz
   (because the plate is thin)              (because εz is constrained)
```

In **plane stress**, imagine looking at the *side* of a thin plate. The top and bottom surfaces are *free*, so no stress can act on them.

In **plane strain**, imagine looking at a *cross-section cut* through a very long dam. The material in front of and behind the section prevents any longitudinal deformation, generating an internal σz.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]**
**Q: Distinguish between plane stress and plane strain conditions with one suitable engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Plane Stress** | **Plane Strain** |
|---|---|
| Stresses on one plane are zero: σz = τyz = τzx = 0 | Strains in one direction are zero: εz = γyz = γzx = 0 |
| εz is generally non-zero (Poisson contraction) | σz is generally non-zero (induced by constraint) |
| **Example:** Thin metallic diaphragm under in-plane pressure | **Example:** A long gravity dam under hydrostatic pressure |
| Typical geometry: thickness much smaller than in-plane dimensions | Typical geometry: length much greater than cross-section dimensions |

### Question A2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
**Q: For a plane stress problem, the stresses are σx = 120 MPa, σy = 60 MPa, and τxy = 0. Find the strain εz. Take E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Using the plane stress relation:

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{0.3}{200 \times 10^3 \, \text{MPa}}(120 + 60) \, \text{MPa}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{0.3}{200 \times 10^3}(180) = -\frac{54}{200 \times 10^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_z = -2.7 \times 10^{-4} \text{ (dimensionless)}}
$$

The negative sign indicates **contraction** in the z-direction due to Poisson's effect.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "zero stress" with "zero strain":** Students frequently write σz = 0 for plane strain. WRONG. In plane strain, σz ≠ 0; it equals ν(σx + σy).
> 2. **Forgetting εz ≠ 0 in plane stress:** When asked for strains in plane stress, students often omit εz. KTU examiners specifically test this — losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up G with E:** Always substitute G = E/[2(1+ν)] before computing shear strain. Direct use of E gives wrong answers.
> 4. **Sign errors in Poisson term:** The minus sign in εx = (1/E)(σx − νσy) is critical. Wrong sign → wrong answer.
> 5. **Units inconsistency:** E must be in the same units as σ (both MPa or both GPa). KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unit mismatch.

---

### Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Regular Exam, Module 1]**

**(a)** Starting from the generalized 3D Hooke's law, derive the reduced stress-strain relations for a **plane stress** condition. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thin steel plate of dimensions 200 mm × 150 mm × 4 mm is subjected to plane stress with σx = 100 MPa, σy = 50 MPa, and τxy = 40 MPa. Given E = 210 GPa and ν = 0.3, determine the strains εx, εy, εz, and γxy. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State the Generalized Hooke's Law (2 Marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)]
$$
$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)]
$$
$$
\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]
$$
$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{yz} = \frac{\tau_{yz}}{G}, \quad \gamma_{zx} = \frac{\tau_{zx}}{G}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Plane Stress Assumption (1 Mark):**

For plane stress: σz = 0, τyz = 0, τzx = 0.

**Step 3 — Substitute and Simplify (3 Marks):**

Substituting σz = 0 into εx and εy:

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y), \quad \varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x)
$$

For εz (although σz = 0, Poisson effect contributes):

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)
$$

For shear strains, since τyz = τzx = 0:

$$
\gamma_{yz} = 0, \quad \gamma_{zx} = 0, \quad \gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}
$$

**Step 4 — Final Result in Matrix Form (1 Mark):**

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \varepsilon_x \\ \varepsilon_y \\ \gamma_{xy} \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{E}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -\nu & 0 \\ -\nu & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 2(1+\nu) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x \\ \sigma_y \\ \tau_{xy} \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- σx = 100 MPa, σy = 50 MPa, τxy = 40 MPa
- E = 210 GPa = 210 × 10³ MPa, ν = 0.3
- G = E/[2(1+ν)] = 210000/[2(1.3)] = 80769.23 MPa

**Step 1 — Compute εx (2 Marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y) = \frac{1}{210000}(100 - 0.3 \times 50)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{100 - 15}{210000} = \frac{85}{210000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_x = 4.048 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute εy (2 Marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}(\sigma_y - \nu\sigma_x) = \frac{1}{210000}(50 - 0.3 \times 100)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_y = \frac{50 - 30}{210000} = \frac{20}{210000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_y = 9.524 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute εz (1 Mark):**

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) = -\frac{0.3}{210000}(100 + 50)
$$

$$
\varepsilon_z = -\frac{0.3 \times 150}{210000} = -\frac{45}{210000}
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_z = -2.143 \times 10^{-4}}
$$

The negative sign confirms **contraction** in the z-direction (Poisson effect).

**Step 4 — Compute γxy (2 Marks):**

$$
\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{40}{80769.23}
$$

$$
\boxed{\gamma_{xy} = 4.953 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians}}
$$

---

### Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Exam, Module 1]**

**(a)** Starting from the generalized 3D Hooke's law, derive the reduced stress-strain relations for a **plane strain** condition. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A long concrete retaining wall is analyzed under plane strain conditions. The measured strains are εx = 150 × 10⁻⁶ and εy = 80 × 10⁻⁶, with γxy = 50 × 10⁻⁶ radians. Take E = 30 GPa and ν = 0.2. Determine σx, σy, σz, and τxy. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — State Assumptions and Starting Equation (1 Mark):**

Plane strain: εz = 0, γyz = 0, γzx = 0.

Generalized Hooke's law: same as in Question A.

**Step 2 — Derive σz from εz = 0 (2 Marks):**

$$
0 = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)] \implies \sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute σz into εx (2 Marks):**

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y - \nu^2(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}[(1-\nu^2)\sigma_x - \nu(1+\nu)\sigma_y]
$$

$$
\varepsilon_x = \frac{(1+\nu)}{E}[(1-\nu)\sigma_x - \nu\sigma_y]
$$

**Step 4 — Invert to get σx and σy in terms of strains (2 Marks):**

By symmetry and matrix inversion:

$$
\sigma_x = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}[(1-\nu)\varepsilon_x + \nu\varepsilon_y]
$$

$$
\sigma_y = \frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)}[\nu\varepsilon_x + (1-\nu)\varepsilon_y]
$$

$$
\tau_{xy} = G\gamma_{xy}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- εx = 150 × 10⁻⁶, εy = 80 × 10⁻⁶, γxy = 50 × 10⁻⁶
- E = 30 GPa = 30 × 10³ MPa, ν = 0.2
- G = E/[2(1+ν)] = 30000/[2(1.2)] = 12500 MPa = 12.5 GPa

**Step 1 — Compute the stiffness factor (1 Mark):**

$$
\frac{E}{(1+\nu)(1-2\nu)} = \frac{30000}{(1.2)(1-0.4)} = \frac{30000}{(1.2)(0.6)} = \frac{30000}{0.72}
$$

$$
= 41666.67 \, \text{MPa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute σx (2 Marks):**

$$
\sigma_x = 41666.67 \times [(1-0.2)(150 \times 10^{-6}) + 0.2 \times (80 \times 10^{-6})]
$$

$$
\sigma_x = 41666.67 \times [0.8 \times 150 \times 10^{-6} + 0.2 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}]
$$

$$
\sigma_x = 41666.67 \times [120 \times 10^{-6} + 16 \times 10^{-6}]
$$

$$
\sigma_x = 41666.67 \times 136 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_x = 5.667 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute σy (2 Marks):**

$$
\sigma_y = 41666.67 \times [0.2 \times 150 \times 10^{-6} + 0.8 \times 80 \times 10^{-6}]
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 41666.67 \times [30 \times 10^{-6} + 64 \times 10^{-6}]
$$

$$
\sigma_y = 41666.67 \times 94 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_y = 3.917 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute σz (1 Mark):**

$$
\sigma_z = \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) = 0.2 \times (5.667 + 3.917)
$$

$$
\sigma_z = 0.2 \times 9.584
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_z = 1.917 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute τxy (1 Mark):**

$$
\tau_{xy} = G\gamma_{xy} = 12500 \times 50 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{xy} = 0.625 \, \text{MPa}}
$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute Revision:**

- ✅ **Hooke's Law (3D)** has six independent equations — three normal, three shear.
- ✅ **Plane Stress** = σz = τyz = τzx = 0; occurs in **thin** structures loaded in-plane.
- ✅ **Plane Strain** = εz = γyz = γzx = 0; occurs in **long** structures with constrained ends.
- ✅ **Crucial Pitfall:** In plane stress, εz ≠ 0; in plane strain, σz ≠ 0. Both are computed from the *other* two.
- ✅ **Plane Stress εz formula:** εz = −(ν/E)(σx + σy).
- ✅ **Plane Strain σz formula:** σz = ν(σx + σy).
- ✅ **Shear relation is universal:** γxy = τxy/G in *both* cases.
- ✅ **G-E-ν relation:** G = E/[2(1+ν)] — must be computed before solving shear problems.
- ✅ **Lamé constants:** λ = νE/[(1+ν)(1-2ν)]; useful in compact tensor notation.
- ✅ **Plane Stress Stiffness Matrix Q:** Leading factor E/(1−ν²); off-diagonal νE/(1−ν²); shear term E/[2(1+ν)].
- ✅ **Plane Strain Stiffness Matrix D:** Leading factor E/[(1+ν)(1-2ν)]; shear term G.
- ✅ **Inverse form for plane strain:** Stress is expressed in terms of strains, not vice versa.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Tension positive, compression negative (KTU standard).
- ✅ **Units:** Always keep E and σ in the same units (MPa with MPa, GPa with GPa).
- ✅ **Engineering Examples:** Thin pressure vessel wall → plane stress; long gravity dam → plane strain.
- ✅ **Symmetry of derivation:** In plane stress, εx and εy are obtained by simply exchanging x and y.
- ✅ **Why "plane"?** Because the deformation/stress is confined to the x-y plane; the z-direction is either stress-free or strain-free.
- ✅ **Real-world link:** These equations are the *backbone* of FEM software (ANSYS, Abaqus) for 2D element formulations (CPS4, CPE4 elements).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relations Between Elastic Constants E, ν and K
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Relations Between Elastic Constants E, ν and K

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Three Elastic Constants — Formal Definitions

In the linear theory of elasticity (Hooke's Law regime), an isotropic homogeneous material exhibits three fundamental independent elastic constants. For the scope of this topic, we focus on:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Young's Modulus (E)**
> Defined as the ratio of longitudinal (axial) stress to longitudinal strain within the elastic limit.
> **Units:** N/m² (Pa) | **Symbol:** E
> It measures the **stiffness** of a solid — the resistance of a material to axial elongation or compression.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Poisson's Ratio (ν)**
> Defined as the negative of the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain, when a specimen is subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load.
> **Units:** Dimensionless (pure number) | **Symbol:** ν (nu)
> It captures the **lateral contraction/expansion** behaviour of a material under axial loading.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bulk Modulus (K)**
> Defined as the ratio of uniform hydrostatic (volumetric) pressure to the corresponding volumetric strain.
> **Units:** N/m² (Pa) | **Symbol:** K
> It measures the material's resistance to **uniform compression from all sides** — a measure of incompressibility.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Stress Ball" Concept

Imagine a soft rubber stress ball (a cube of sponge-rubber). The three constants describe its response to three distinct experiments:

- **Young's Modulus (E)** → Stretch the ball in **one direction** (pull both ends like a rubber band). How hard do you have to pull to make it 1% longer? That resistance per unit strain is **E**. The ball also gets **thinner** in the middle — that thinning behaviour is governed by **ν**.

- **Poisson's Ratio (ν)** → Squeeze the ball along one axis. It expands sideways. The ratio of how much it expands sideways to how much it compresses axially is **ν**. A rubber ball has ν ≈ **0.49** (very high), while concrete has ν ≈ **0.1** to **0.2**.

- **Bulk Modulus (K)** → Now drop the ball deep into a swimming pool. Water squeezes it **equally from ALL sides** (hydrostatic pressure). The ratio of pressure to fractional volume change is **K**. A steel ball has very high K; a foam ball has very low K.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The three constants E, G, K are not independent. For a **homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic** material, **only two** are independent. Knowing any two allows computation of the third and the shear modulus G.

## 1.3 Standard Range of Poisson's Ratio

| Material | Approximate ν | Behaviour |
|----------|---------------|-----------|
| Concrete | 0.10 – 0.20 | Lateral contraction is small |
| Steel | 0.27 – 0.30 | Typical engineering metal |
| Copper | 0.31 – 0.34 | Ductile metal |
| Rubber | 0.45 – 0.49 | Nearly incompressible |
| Cork | ≈ 0.00 | Almost no lateral coupling |
| Theoretical limit | −1.0 < ν < 0.5 | Bounds from elasticity stability |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Multiaxial Hooke's Law

For a 3D stress state acting on an isotropic linear elastic material, the strain in any principal direction is:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_y - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_z)\right]$$

$$\varepsilon_z = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_z - \nu(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> The bracketed term is the **stress deviatoric contribution** — only the stress component in the direction of interest causes axial strain, while the lateral stresses cause opposite-sign lateral strains scaled by ν.

## 2.2 Logic Steps for Deriving the K-E-ν Relation

1. Apply a **uniform hydrostatic pressure** p on all six faces of a cubic element. This is the only loading that changes the **volume** uniformly without producing shear.
2. Set σx = σy = σz = −p (compressive) or +p (tensile, depending on sign convention). For pure volume change, **all three normal stresses are equal**.
3. Compute the **volumetric strain** by adding the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z$$

4. Apply the definition of **Bulk Modulus** as the ratio of pressure to volumetric strain:

$$K = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v}$$

5. Substitute, simplify, and arrive at the master relation.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Relation | Standard Use in KTU Problems |
|---------|----------|------------------------------|
| $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ | K expressed via E and ν | Direct 3-mark definition; 14-mark derivation |
| $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ | E expressed via K and ν | Solving for E when K and ν are given |
| $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ | ν expressed via E and K | Solving for ν when E and K are given |
| $\nu = \dfrac{E - 2G}{2G}$ | ν expressed via E and G | Combined with shear modulus questions |
| $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1 + \nu)}$ | Shear modulus relation | Cross-verification of all constants |
| $\lambda = \dfrac{E\nu}{(1 + \nu)(1 - 2\nu)}$ | Lamé's first constant | Advanced problems, plate/shell analysis |
| Volumetric strain: $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{3p(1 - 2\nu)}{E}$ | For hydrostatic pressure p | Step in derivation of K |
| Strain in one direction: $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$ | Hydrostatic component | Isolating pressure response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip (KTU 2024 Scheme):** A common question pattern gives two of (E, ν, K, G) and asks for the other two. Memorise the **triangle of relations** so that any one formula can be derived in the exam by manipulating the others.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Material Selection for Pressure Vessels:** K dictates how much a material's volume shrinks under internal fluid pressure — critical for deep-sea submersibles (hulls) and gas cylinders.
- **Civil Engineering — Concrete Dams:** The low ν of concrete (≈ 0.15) reduces lateral expansion, which is why dams resist hydrostatic loads from reservoirs efficiently.
- **Rubber Bearings in Bridges:** High ν rubber (≈ 0.49) makes the bearing nearly incompressible, allowing it to absorb vertical load while transmitting minimal horizontal thrust.
- **Geophysics:** K is a key parameter in modelling the Earth's mantle response to tectonic loading.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Relation: $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$

### Step 1 — Set Up the Hydrostatic Loading

Consider a small cubic element of side length **a** in a homogeneous isotropic material. Subject it to equal triaxial normal stresses on all faces:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = p \quad (\text{hydrostatic pressure})$$

The shear stresses are zero. This is the canonical case for **volumetric deformation**.

### Step 2 — Write Strains in Each Principal Direction

Using the generalized Hooke's Law for isotropic materials (as given in Section 2.1):

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right]$$

Substituting σx = σy = σz = p:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[p - \nu(p + p)\right] = \frac{1}{E}\left[p - 2\nu p\right] = \frac{p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

By the symmetry of the loading (all three normal stresses are equal), the same result holds for the other two directions:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{p}{E}(1 - 2\nu) \qquad \varepsilon_z = \frac{p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 3 — Sum the Strains to Get Volumetric Strain

The volumetric strain εv is the fractional change in volume. For small deformations, this equals the sum of the three normal strains:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{3p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)$$

### Step 4 — Apply the Definition of Bulk Modulus

By definition:

$$K = \frac{\text{Hydrostatic Pressure}}{\text{Volumetric Strain}} = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v}$$

Substituting the expression for εv:

$$K = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p}{E}(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

The pressure **p** cancels from the numerator and denominator:

$$K = \frac{p \cdot E}{3p(1 - 2\nu)} = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

This is the **principal relation** linking Bulk Modulus, Young's Modulus, and Poisson's ratio. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of the Term (1 − 2ν):** As ν → 0.5, the term (1 − 2ν) → 0, which forces K → ∞. This is the algebraic statement that an **incompressible material** cannot be hydrostatically compressed at all — consistent with rubber (ν ≈ 0.49, K is huge) and water (ν ≈ 0.5, K is finite but large for water).

## 3.2 Inverse Relations

### 3.2.1 Expressing E in terms of K and ν

Starting from:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

Cross-multiply to isolate E:

$$3K(1 - 2\nu) = E$$

$$\boxed{E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)}$$

### 3.2.2 Expressing ν in terms of E and K

Start again from $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ and solve for (1 − 2ν):

$$1 - 2\nu = \frac{E}{3K}$$

$$2\nu = 1 - \frac{E}{3K} = \frac{3K - E}{3K}$$

$$\boxed{\nu = \frac{3K - E}{6K}}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Solving for Any Constant

| Given Constants | Required Constant | Working Formula |
|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| E, ν | K | $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ |
| E, K | ν | $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$ |
| ν, K | E | $E = 3K(1 - 2\nu)$ |
| E, G | ν | $\nu = \dfrac{E - 2G}{2G}$ |
| E, G | K | $K = \dfrac{EG}{3(3G - E)}$ |
| G, ν | E | $E = 2G(1 + \nu)$ |
| G, ν | K | $K = \dfrac{2G(1 + \nu)}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The last two rows (involving G) are derived by combining $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ with the master K-E-ν relation. KTU questions occasionally test these extended forms.

## 3.4 Verification of Limiting Behaviour

**Case A: ν = 0.30 (typical steel)**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \frac{E}{3(0.4)} = \frac{E}{1.2} \approx 0.833\,E$$

So Bulk Modulus is roughly **83%** of Young's Modulus for steel — a useful rule-of-thumb.

**Case B: ν = 0.50 (theoretical incompressible limit)**

Denominator $3(1 - 2(0.5)) = 3(0) = 0$ → K → ∞. Confirms that the algebra is consistent: an incompressible material has infinite resistance to volume change.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — The Elastic Constant Network

The following block diagram illustrates the interrelations among the four primary elastic constants (E, ν, G, K) and the experimental setup that defines each. It serves as a high-yield revision map for the KTU 2024 examination.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["STRESS STATE INPUT"] --> B{"Type of Loading"}
    B -- "Uniaxial Tension" --> C["Young's Modulus E"]
    B -- "Lateral Coupling" --> D["Poisson's Ratio NU"]
    B -- "Pure Shear" --> E["Shear Modulus G"]
    B -- "Hydrostatic Pressure" --> F["Bulk Modulus K"]

    C -- "Stress / Strain Ratio" --> G["E defines axial stiffness"]
    D -- "Lateral Strain / Axial Strain" --> H["NU defines lateral coupling"]
    E -- "Shear Stress / Shear Strain" --> I["G defines shape distortion"]
    F -- "Pressure / Volumetric Strain" --> J["K defines volume stiffness"]

    C -- "Paired with NU" --> K["MASTER RELATION K = E OVER 3 OPEN 1 MINUS 2 NU CLOSE"]
    C -- "Paired with NU" --> L["G = E OVER 2 OPEN 1 PLUS NU CLOSE"]
    F -- "Paired with NU" --> M["E = 3K OPEN 1 MINUS 2 NU CLOSE"]
    E -- "Paired with NU" --> N["NU = E MINUS 2G OVER 2G"]

    K --> O["OUTPUT: 4 constants with only 2 independent"]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O

    O --> P["Isotropic Linear Elastic Material"]
    P --> Q["Civil: Concrete, Steel Selection"]
    P --> R["Mechanical: Pressure Vessel Design"]
    P --> S["Aerospace: Composite Layup"]
    P --> T["Geotech: Soil and Rock Mechanics"]
```

## 4.2 Schematic of the Hydrostatic Loading Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1["Pressure p on Left Face"] --> ELEM["Cubical Element of Side a"]
    R1["Pressure p on Right Face"] --> ELEM
    T1["Pressure p on Top Face"] --> ELEM
    B1["Pressure p on Bottom Face"] --> ELEM
    F1["Pressure p on Front Face"] --> ELEM
    BK1["Pressure p on Back Face"] --> ELEM

    ELEM -- "Equal triaxial stress" --> V1["Original Volume V = a cubed"]
    ELEM -- "Equal triaxial stress" --> V2["Deformed Volume V minus delta V"]
    V1 -- "Ratio" --> STRAIN["Volumetric Strain e_v = delta V over V"]
    V2 -- "Ratio" --> STRAIN
    STRAIN -- "Pressure p divided by e_v" --> OUT["Bulk Modulus K"]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Triangle — Two Constants Fix the Other Two

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP["ISOTROPIC LINEAR ELASTIC MATERIAL"] --> E["E TOP"]
    TOP --> NU["NU LEFT"]
    TOP --> G["G RIGHT"]
    TOP --> K["K BOTTOM"]

    E -- "K = E / 3(1 - 2NU)" --> K
    NU -- "K = E / 3(1 - 2NU)" --> K
    E -- "G = E / 2(1 + NU)" --> G
    NU -- "G = E / 2(1 + NU)" --> G
    E -- "NU = (E - 2G) / 2G" --> G
    G -- "E = 2G(1 + NU)" --> E
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each arrow represents an equation. If you can locate any two of the four elastic constants (E, ν, G, K) on the diagram, the formula on the connecting path allows you to determine the other two — which is exactly the skill KTU 2024 ESE questions test.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Q1]**
**Define Poisson's ratio. State its theoretical limits and the value for a perfectly incompressible material.**

**Model Answer:**

Poisson's ratio (ν) is defined as the **negative of the ratio of lateral strain to longitudinal strain** when a material is subjected to a uniaxial tensile or compressive load within the elastic limit:

$$\nu = -\frac{\varepsilon_{lateral}}{\varepsilon_{longitudinal}} = -\frac{\varepsilon_y}{\varepsilon_x}$$

**Theoretical Limits:** Based on thermodynamic and stability considerations of isotropic linear elasticity, the allowable range is:

$$-1.0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$$

**Perfectly Incompressible Material:** For a perfectly incompressible material, the volumetric strain εv = 0 for any hydrostatic pressure, which from $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ requires the denominator to vanish. Hence:

$$\nu = 0.5 \quad \text{(for perfectly incompressible material)}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for limits, 1 for incompressible value)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Q2]**
**State the relationship between the three elastic constants E, K and ν. Mention the physical significance of bulk modulus.**

**Model Answer:**

The relationship between the three elastic constants is:

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1 - 2\nu)}$$

where E = Young's modulus, K = Bulk modulus, and ν = Poisson's ratio.

**Physical Significance of Bulk Modulus:** Bulk Modulus quantifies a material's resistance to **uniform compression from all directions**. It is the ratio of hydrostatic pressure to the resulting volumetric strain. A high K means the material is very difficult to compress in volume (e.g., steel); a low K means the material compresses easily (e.g., foam). The reciprocal of K is called the **Compressibility (β)**.

*(3 marks: 2 for formula, 1 for physical significance)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Standard Derivation + Numerical
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the relation $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$ for a homogeneous isotropic material subjected to hydrostatic pressure. State the assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metal specimen has Young's modulus E = 210 GPa and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.30. Calculate the values of **(i)** Bulk modulus K, **(ii)** Shear modulus G, and **(iii)** Rigidity modulus when the same material is subjected to a hydrostatic pressure of 150 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Assumptions:**
1. The material is **homogeneous** (same properties at every point).
2. The material is **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
3. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linear elastic behaviour).
4. The deformations are **small** (infinitesimal strain theory valid).

**Step 1:** Consider a cubic element of side **a** subjected to equal triaxial normal stresses:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_y = \sigma_z = p$$

**Step 2:** Using the 3D Hooke's law, the strain along x-axis:

$$\varepsilon_x = \frac{1}{E}\left[\sigma_x - \nu(\sigma_y + \sigma_z)\right] = \frac{1}{E}[p - \nu(2p)] = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

By symmetry:

$$\varepsilon_y = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}, \qquad \varepsilon_z = \frac{p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 3:** Volumetric strain:

$$\varepsilon_v = \varepsilon_x + \varepsilon_y + \varepsilon_z = \frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$$

**Step 4:** Bulk Modulus:

$$K = \frac{p}{\varepsilon_v} = \frac{p}{\dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}} = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$$

Hence the relation is established. *(7 marks: 1 for assumptions, 2 for setup, 2 for strain computation, 2 for final step)*

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** E = 210 GPa = 210 × 10⁹ Pa, ν = 0.30, p = 150 MPa = 150 × 10⁶ Pa.

**(i) Bulk Modulus K:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{3(0.4)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{1.2}$$

$$K = 175 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} = 175 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Shear Modulus G:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2(1 + 0.30)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2(1.30)} = \frac{210 \times 10^9}{2.6}$$

$$G = 80.77 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa} \approx 80.77 \text{ GPa}$$

**(iii) Volumetric Strain under Hydrostatic Pressure:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E} = \frac{3 \times 150 \times 10^6 \times (1 - 0.6)}{210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{3 \times 150 \times 10^6 \times 0.4}{210 \times 10^9} = \frac{180 \times 10^6}{210 \times 10^9}$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 8.571 \times 10^{-4} = 0.000857 \text{ (dimensionless)}$$

Alternatively, εv = p/K = (150 × 10⁶) / (175 × 10⁹) = 8.571 × 10⁻⁴ ✓

*(7 marks: 2 for K, 2 for G, 3 for volumetric strain including the verification check)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** For a steel specimen, the following properties are given: Young's modulus E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.30. Determine: **(i)** Bulk modulus K, **(ii)** Shear modulus G, and **(iii)** Modulus of rigidity of the material. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bar of length 200 mm, width 40 mm, and thickness 20 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 80 kN. If E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² and ν = 0.32, find the change in **(i)** Length, **(ii)** Width, **(iii)** Thickness, and **(iv)** Volume of the bar. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Elastic Constants of Steel (7 marks):**

**Given:** E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa, ν = 0.30.

**(i) Bulk Modulus K:**

$$K = \frac{E}{3(1-2\nu)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{3(1 - 0.6)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{1.2} = 1.667 \times 10^5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Shear Modulus G:**

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2(1.3)} = \frac{2 \times 10^5}{2.6} = 7.692 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(iii) Modulus of Rigidity:** The Modulus of Rigidity is identical to the Shear Modulus G:

$$G = 7.692 \times 10^4 \text{ N/mm}^2 \approx 76.92 \text{ kN/mm}^2$$

---

**(b) Bar Under Axial Tension (7 marks):**

**Given:** L = 200 mm, b = 40 mm, t = 20 mm, P = 80 kN = 80,000 N, E = 2 × 10⁵ N/mm², ν = 0.32.

**Cross-sectional Area A = b × t = 40 × 20 = 800 mm²**

**Axial Stress σ = P/A = 80,000 / 800 = 100 N/mm²**

**(i) Change in Length (ΔL):**

$$\Delta L = \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{80{,}000 \times 200}{800 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^7}{1.6 \times 10^8} = 0.1 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) Change in Width (Δb):**

Lateral strain in width direction:

$$\varepsilon_{lateral} = -\nu \cdot \varepsilon_{axial} = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma}{E}$$

$$\Delta b = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma}{E} \cdot b = -0.32 \times \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5} \times 40$$

$$\Delta b = -0.32 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 40 = -6.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.0064 \text{ mm}$$

(Magnitude of contraction = 0.0064 mm)

**(iii) Change in Thickness (Δt):**

$$\Delta t = -\nu \cdot \frac{\sigma}{E} \cdot t = -0.32 \times \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5} \times 20$$

$$\Delta t = -0.32 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 20 = -3.2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ mm} = -0.0032 \text{ mm}$$

(Magnitude of contraction = 0.0032 mm)

**(iv) Change in Volume (ΔV):**

For uniaxial stress, volumetric strain:

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{\Delta V}{V} = \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - 2\nu) = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5}(1 - 0.64)$$

$$\varepsilon_v = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 0.36 = 1.8 \times 10^{-4}$$

Original volume V = L × b × t = 200 × 40 × 20 = 160,000 mm³

$$\Delta V = \varepsilon_v \cdot V = 1.8 \times 10^{-4} \times 160{,}000 = 28.8 \text{ mm}^3$$

*(7 marks: 2 for axial strain, 2 for lateral strains, 3 for volume change with explicit formula)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors:** Lateral strains under tensile loading are **negative** (contraction). Many students drop the negative sign and lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing mm² with N/m² is a guaranteed zero for the numerical part. Convert all values to **consistent units** (N and mm, or N and m) **before** substituting into formulas.
> 3. **Forgetting (1 − 2ν) Factor:** In the volumetric strain for uniaxial stress, the factor is $(1-2\nu)$, **not** $(1-\nu)$ or $(1-2\nu)E$. This is a frequent algebraic slip.
> 4. **Not Cancelling 'p' in Derivation:** Step 4 of the derivation has the pressure p in both numerator and denominator. Students sometimes move p to a wrong side, producing $K = 3p(1-2\nu)/E$, which is dimensionally wrong. **Always check units** — K has units of stress (Pa), not dimensionless.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- The **three master constants** for isotropic elastic materials are E (stiffness), ν (lateral coupling), and K (incompressibility). The fourth constant G (shear rigidity) is derivable from any two of the first three.

- **Core formula (must memorise):** $K = \dfrac{E}{3(1-2\nu)}$

- **Inverse relations (must memorise):** $E = 3K(1-2\nu)$ and $\nu = \dfrac{3K - E}{6K}$

- **Companion formula:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ — used in cross-verification and in combined E-G-K problems.

- **Range of ν:** $-1.0 \leq \nu \leq 0.5$. Most engineering materials have ν in the range **0.20 to 0.35**.

- **Limiting cases:**
  - $\nu = 0.5$ → Material is **incompressible** → K → ∞
  - $\nu = 0$ → No lateral strain under axial load (e.g., cork)
  - $\nu < 0$ → Auxetic material (rare; expands laterally when stretched)

- **Hydrostatic Loading is the key to K:** Whenever you see **equal stresses in all three directions** or a problem mentioning **pressure on all faces**, you should immediately think of the volumetric strain $\varepsilon_v = \dfrac{3p(1-2\nu)}{E}$ and the K relation.

- **Volumetric strain for any triaxial stress state:**

$$\varepsilon_v = \frac{1-2\nu}{E}(\sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z)$$

- **Modulus of Rigidity ≡ Shear Modulus ≡ G:** These three terms are synonymous. Don't be confused if a question uses an alternative name.

- **Practical numerical ratio:** For steel (ν = 0.30), **K ≈ 0.833 E** and **G ≈ 0.385 E**. These shortcuts help in sanity-checking answers during the exam.

- **Derivation Approach for 7-mark sub-part:** Always start with the assumptions (1 mark), set up the hydrostatic stress state (2 marks), compute strains (2 marks), and conclude with the K definition (2 marks). This structure guarantees full marks under the KTU 2024 marking scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Calculation of Stress, Strain and Change in Length in Axially Loaded Members with Single and Composite Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Calculation of Stress, Strain and Change in Length in Axially Loaded Members with Single and Composite Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

When an axial load is applied along the centroidal longitudinal axis of a structural member, the member experiences **internal forces** that resist the applied load. The intensity of this internal resistance, measured per unit cross-sectional area, is defined as **Normal Stress** ($\sigma$). The corresponding deformation per unit original length is termed **Normal Strain** ($\epsilon$).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):**
> This topic is the *foundation* of the entire Mechanics of Solids course. Mastery of axial deformation formulas ($\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}$) is mandatory, as the same principle is extended to thermal stresses, indeterminate problems, and combined loading in later modules.

### 1.2 Fundamental Definitions

**Normal Stress ($\sigma$):**
The internal restoring force per unit area that develops on a cross-section perpendicular to the line of action of the applied axial load.

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Axial load (tension or compression), in **Newtons (N)**
- $A$ = Cross-sectional area, in **mm²** or **m²**
- $\sigma$ = Normal stress, in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **N/m² (Pa)**

**Normal Strain ($\epsilon$):**
The ratio of change in length ($\delta L$) to the original length ($L$).

$$\epsilon = \frac{\delta L}{L}$$

Where:
- $\delta L$ = Change in length (elongation if positive, shortening if negative)
- $L$ = Original length of the member
