- $\epsilon$ = Dimensionless (no units) or expressed as **mm/m**

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Rubber Band & Spring System:**
> Imagine stretching a rubber band with your fingers. The harder you pull, the more the band stretches. The "pull force" applied at the ends is the **axial load ($P$)**. The thinning of the band as it stretches represents **strain ($\epsilon$)**. The internal molecular resistance trying to pull the band back to its original shape is **stress ($\sigma$)**. A thicker rubber band (larger area $A$) will stretch *less* for the same force — this is why solid steel cables of large diameter can carry massive loads with negligible elongation.
>
> Now imagine *gluing* a copper piece to a steel piece and pulling them together. Both pieces must stretch by the **same amount** (compatibility), but each carries a different share of the load based on its stiffness — this is the essence of a **composite bar** problem.

### 1.4 Classification of Members

| Member Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Material, Uniform Section** | One material, constant $A$ along length | Simple tie rods, hangers |
| **Stepped Bar (Single Material)** | One material, varying $A$ along length | Shafts with shoulders, bolts |
| **Composite Bar (Parallel)** | Multiple materials bonded, sharing load | Reinforced concrete, bimetallic strips |
| **Composite Bar (Series)** | Materials joined end-to-end, same load | Tool bits (tungsten + steel shank) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Step-by-Step Framework

The solution to any axially loaded member problem follows a strict 4-step logical sequence:

1. **Free Body Diagram (FBD):** Cut the member at the section of interest, expose the internal force $P$, and identify all external loads.
2. **Equilibrium Equation:** Apply $\sum F_x = 0$ to determine the internal axial force in each segment.
3. **Stress/Strain Calculation:** Substitute $P$ and $A$ into $\sigma = P/A$, then compute $\epsilon = \sigma/E$ using **Hooke's Law**.
4. **Deformation Integration:** Sum the elongation of each segment: $\delta L = \sum \frac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$.

### 2.2 Hooke's Law (Linear Elasticity)

For a material within its **elastic limit**, stress is directly proportional to strain. The constant of proportionality is the **Modulus of Elasticity (Young's Modulus, $E$)**.

$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Always verify that the problem lies within the elastic limit. If a problem provides a load beyond the yield point, Hooke's Law is **invalid** and plastic deformation equations must be used. Examiners frequently test this trap.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Normal (axial) stress | N/mm² or MPa |
| 2 | $\epsilon = \dfrac{\delta L}{L}$ | Normal (axial) strain | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$ | Hooke's Law | N/mm² |
| 4 | $\delta L = \dfrac{PL}{AE}$ | Elongation of uniform bar | mm |
| 5 | $\delta L = \sum \dfrac{P_i L_i}{A_i E_i}$ | Elongation of stepped / composite bar | mm |
| 6 | $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$ | Equilibrium in parallel composite | N |
| 7 | $\epsilon_1 = \epsilon_2 = \epsilon_3$ | Compatibility in parallel composite | — |
| 8 | $P = P_1 + P_2 + P_3$ | Load sharing in parallel composite | N |
| 9 | $P_1 = P_2 = P_3$ | Same axial force in series composite | N |
| 10 | $\delta L_{thermal} = \alpha L \Delta T$ | Free thermal elongation | mm |

> **Note on standard values of $E$ (for reference):**
> - Steel: $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - Copper: $E_c = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
> - Aluminium: $E_a = 0.7 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles taught in this module are the design backbone of:
- **Truss bridges and roof structures** (axial members in pin-jointed frames)
- **High-rise building columns** (axial compression carrying floor loads)
- **Pre-stressed concrete girders** (composite action of steel tendons + concrete)
- **Bolted/riveted joints** (load transfer through stepped shanks)
- **Bimetallic thermostats** (differential thermal expansion in composite strips)

### 2.5 The Two Governing Principles for Composite Bars

**Principle 1 — Equilibrium:**
The sum of internal forces must equal the applied external load. For a *parallel* composite bar:

$$P = P_1 + P_2 + P_3 + \ldots + P_n$$

**Principle 2 — Compatibility (Geometric):**
The deformations of all components connected in *parallel* must be equal, since they share the same end-to-end displacement.

$$\delta L_1 = \delta L_2 = \delta L_3 = \ldots = \delta L_n$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Change in Length for a Uniform Bar

**Given:** A prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, subjected to axial tensile load $P$ at its centroid.

**Step 1 — Consider an infinitesimal element of length $dx$ at distance $x$ from the left end.**

The strain experienced by this element is:

$$\epsilon(x) = \frac{d(\delta L)}{dx}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Hooke's Law for the element.**

Since the bar is uniform and the load $P$ is constant throughout, the stress is constant:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

By Hooke's Law:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

**Step 3 — Express the infinitesimal elongation.**

$$d(\delta L) = \epsilon \cdot dx = \frac{P}{AE} \, dx$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the full length $L$ to obtain total elongation.**

$$\delta L = \int_0^L \frac{P}{AE} \, dx$$

Since $P$, $A$, and $E$ are constants for a uniform bar:

$$\delta L = \frac{P}{AE} \int_0^L dx = \frac{P}{AE} \big[ x \big]_0^L$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{AE}}$$

This is the **Master Equation** of axial deformation, valid for any uniform prismatic bar within the elastic limit.

### 3.2 Derivation for a Stepped Bar (Single Material, Varying Area)

**Given:** A bar of single material $E$ with $n$ segments, each having area $A_i$ and length $L_i$, subjected to axial load $P$.

**Step 1 — The internal force $P$ is the same in every segment** (since the bar is in series and no intermediate loads are applied).

**Step 2 — Apply the master equation to each segment.**

$$\delta L_i = \frac{P \cdot L_i}{A_i \cdot E}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the principle of superposition (deformations are additive in series).**

$$\delta L_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \delta L_i$$

$$\boxed{\delta L_{total} = \frac{P}{E} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{L_i}{A_i}}$$

### 3.3 Derivation for a Parallel Composite Bar

**Given:** Two materials of cross-sectional areas $A_1$ and $A_2$, Young's moduli $E_1$ and $E_2$, rigidly bonded over length $L$, subjected to axial load $P$.

**Step 1 — Equilibrium (Load Sharing):**

The total load is shared between the two materials:

$$P = P_1 + P_2$$

**Step 2 — Compatibility (Equal Elongation):**

Since the materials are bonded, both elongate by the same amount:

$$\delta L_1 = \delta L_2 = \delta L$$

Using the master equation for each material:

$$\frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2}$$

Therefore:

$$P_2 = P_1 \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into equilibrium equation.**

$$P = P_1 + P_1 \cdot \frac{A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1} = P_1 \left( \frac{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1} \right)$$

Solving for $P_1$:

$$\boxed{P_1 = \frac{P \cdot A_1 E_1}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}$$

Similarly:

$$\boxed{P_2 = \frac{P \cdot A_2 E_2}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}$$

**Step 4 — Total Elongation of the Composite Bar:**

$$\delta L = \frac{P_1 L}{A_1 E_1} = \frac{P_2 L}{A_2 E_2} = \frac{PL}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}$$

$$\boxed{\delta L = \frac{PL}{A_1 E_1 + A_2 E_2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The product $AE$ is called **Axial Stiffness**. A composite bar behaves like an equivalent single bar of stiffness equal to the *sum* of the individual stiffnesses. The *stiffer* component (larger $AE$) carries a *larger* share of the load.

### 3.4 Numerical Verification — Worked Example

**Problem:** A steel rod ($E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $A_s = 400$ mm²) and a copper rod ($E_c = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $A_c = 200$ mm²) are bonded in parallel over a length of $L = 500$ mm. An axial tensile load of $P = 60$ kN is applied. Find the stresses, strains, and total elongation.

**Step 1 — Compute Axial Stiffness of each material.**

$$A_s E_s = 400 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 8 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$A_c E_c = 200 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 2 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Compute Load Shared by Each Material.**

$$P_s = \frac{P \cdot A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_c E_c} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 8 \times 10^7}{8 \times 10^7 + 2 \times 10^7}$$

$$P_s = \frac{4.8 \times 10^{12}}{10 \times 10^7} = 48 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 48 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_c = P - P_s = 60 - 48 = 12 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Stresses.**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{48 \times 10^3}{400} = 120 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P_c}{A_c} = \frac{12 \times 10^3}{200} = 60 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute Strains (Verification via Hooke's Law).**

$$\epsilon_s = \frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} = \frac{120}{2 \times 10^5} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\epsilon_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} = \frac{60}{1 \times 10^5} = 6 \times 10^{-4}$$

Notice: $\epsilon_s = \epsilon_c$ — this **confirms** the compatibility condition is satisfied. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute Total Elongation.**

$$\delta L = \epsilon_s \cdot L = 6 \times 10^{-4} \times 500 = 0.3 \text{ mm}$$

Alternatively, using the direct formula:

$$\delta L = \frac{PL}{A_s E_s + A_c E_c} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 500}{10 \times 10^7} = \frac{3 \times 10^7}{10^8} = 0.3 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Problem-Solving Flowchart for Axially Loaded Members

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Axially Loaded Member Problem] --> B{Is the bar uniform?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Single Material, Constant Area]
    B -- No --> D{Multiple Areas OR Multiple Materials?}
    D -- Multiple Areas Only --> E[Stepped Bar - Series Type]
    D -- Multiple Materials Bonded --> F[Composite Bar - Parallel Type]
    C --> G[Apply delta L = PL / AE]
    E --> H[Apply delta L = P/E * Sum Li/Ai]
    F --> I[Use Compatibility: dL1 = dL2]
    I --> J[Use Equilibrium: P = P1 + P2]
    J --> K[Compute Load Share: Pi = P*Ai*Ei / Sum Ai*Ei]
    K --> L[Compute Stress: sigma i = Pi / Ai]
    H --> M[Compute Total Elongation]
    G --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Final Answer: Stress, Strain, Elongation]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Composite Bar Load Transfer Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ExtLoad[External Axial Load P] --> Dist[Load Distribution Block]
    Dist --> Stiff1[Material 1 - Stiffness A1E1]
    Dist --> Stiff2[Material 2 - Stiffness A2E2]
    Dist --> Stiff3[Material n - Stiffness AnEn]
    Stiff1 --> Bond[Rigid Bonding Interface]
    Stiff2 --> Bond
    Stiff3 --> Bond
    Bond --> Comp[Common Displacement Output delta L]
    Comp --> Str1[Stress1 = P1/A1]
    Comp --> Str2[Stress2 = P2/A2]
    Comp --> Str3[Stress n = Pn/An]
```

### 4.3 Series vs. Parallel Configuration Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SeriesConfig["SERIES CONFIGURATION (Stepped Bar)"]
        S1[Segment 1: A1, L1, E] --> S2[Segment 2: A2, L2, E]
        S2 --> S3[Segment 3: A3, L3, E]
    end
    subgraph ParallelConfig["PARALLEL CONFIGURATION (Composite Bar)"]
        P1[Material 1: A1, L, E1] -.-> P2[Material 2: A2, L, E2]
        P2 -.-> P3[Material n: An, L, En]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
Define the terms: (i) Normal Stress, (ii) Normal Strain, and (iii) Young's Modulus. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Normal Stress ($\sigma$):** The intensity of internal restoring force per unit area developed on a cross-section when an axial load is applied. It is given by $\sigma = P/A$ and is measured in **N/mm² (MPa)**.

**(ii) Normal Strain ($\epsilon$):** The ratio of change in length to the original length of the member, $\epsilon = \delta L / L$. It is a **dimensionless** quantity.

**(iii) Young's Modulus ($E$):** The ratio of normal stress to normal strain within the elastic limit, representing the material's stiffness. $E = \sigma / \epsilon$, measured in **N/mm² (MPa)**.

**Relationship:** Within the elastic limit, the three are linked by **Hooke's Law**:
$$\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
A circular steel rod of diameter **20 mm** is subjected to an axial tensile load of **50 kN**. Find the normal stress developed in the rod. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the circular rod:**

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 100\pi \text{ mm}^2 \approx 314.159 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the stress formula:**

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{314.159} = 159.15 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compute strain using Hooke's Law:**

$$\epsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{159.15}{2 \times 10^5} = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Final Answer:** $\sigma = 159.15$ MPa, $\epsilon = 7.96 \times 10^{-4}$

---

### PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### ✦ QUESTION 1(a) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

A composite bar consists of a steel rod of cross-sectional area **600 mm²** and a copper rod of cross-sectional area **400 mm²**, rigidly bonded together over a length of **400 mm**. An axial tensile load of **80 kN** is applied to the composite bar. Take $E_{steel} = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and $E_{copper} = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

**Find:**
(a-i) The load carried by each material. (7 Marks)
(a-ii) The stresses, strains, and total elongation of the composite bar. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q1(a)

**Given Data:**
- $A_s = 600$ mm², $A_c = 400$ mm², $L = 400$ mm
- $P = 80$ kN $= 80 \times 10^3$ N
- $E_s = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $E_c = 1 \times 10^5$ N/mm²

#### Part (a-i): Load Shared by Each Material (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Axial Stiffness of each material:**

$$A_s E_s = 600 \times 2 \times 10^5 = 12 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$A_c E_c = 400 \times 1 \times 10^5 = 4 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

$$\text{Total Stiffness} = 12 \times 10^7 + 4 \times 10^7 = 16 \times 10^7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the load-sharing formula:**

$$P_s = \frac{P \cdot A_s E_s}{A_s E_s + A_c E_c} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 12 \times 10^7}{16 \times 10^7}$$

$$P_s = \frac{9.6 \times 10^{12}}{16 \times 10^7} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

$$P_c = P - P_s = 80 - 60 = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Result:** Steel carries **60 kN** and Copper carries **20 kN**.

#### Part (a-ii): Stresses, Strains, and Total Elongation (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Compute stresses in each material:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{P_s}{A_s} = \frac{60 \times 10^3}{600} = 100 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

$$\sigma_c = \frac{P_c}{A_c} = \frac{20 \times 10^3}{400} = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (MPa)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute strains using Hooke's Law:**

$$\epsilon_s = \frac{\sigma_s}{E_s} = \frac{100}{2 \times 10^5} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

$$\epsilon_c = \frac{\sigma_c}{E_c} = \frac{50}{1 \times 10^5} = 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Verification:** $\epsilon_s = \epsilon_c$ ✓ (Compatibility satisfied)

**Step 3 — Compute total elongation:**

$$\delta L = \epsilon_s \cdot L = 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 400 = 0.2 \text{ mm}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Quantity | Steel | Copper |
|---|---|---|
| Load | 60 kN | 20 kN |
| Stress | 100 MPa | 50 MPa |
| Strain | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ | $5 \times 10^{-4}$ |
| **Total Elongation** | $\delta L = 0.2$ mm | — |

---

### ✦ QUESTION 1(b) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

A stepped steel bar has three segments arranged in series. The first segment is **300 mm** long with area **200 mm²**, the second is **200 mm** long with area **150 mm²**, and the third is **250 mm** long with area **100 mm²**. An axial tensile load of **30 kN** is applied at one end. Take $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm².

**Find:**
(b-i) The stress in each segment. (7 Marks)
(b-ii) The total elongation of the bar. (7 Marks)

---

### Model Solution for Q1(b)

**Given Data:**
- Segment 1: $L_1 = 300$ mm, $A_1 = 200$ mm²
- Segment 2: $L_2 = 200$ mm, $A_2 = 150$ mm²
- Segment 3: $L_3 = 250$ mm, $A_3 = 100$ mm²
- $P = 30$ kN $= 30 \times 10^3$ N
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² (constant)

#### Part (b-i): Stress in Each Segment (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Internal force:** Since the bar is in **series** (no intermediate loads), the internal axial force in every segment is $P = 30$ kN.

**Step 2 — Compute stress in each segment using $\sigma = P/A$:**

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{P}{A_1} = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{200} = 150 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{P}{A_2} = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{150} = 200 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_3 = \frac{P}{A_3} = \frac{30 \times 10^3}{100} = 300 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

#### Part (b-ii): Total Elongation of the Bar (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Apply the deformation formula $\delta L_i = P L_i / (A_i E)$ to each segment:**

$$\delta L_1 = \frac{30 \times 10^3 \times 300}{200 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{9 \times 10^6}{4 \times 10^7} = 0.225 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta L_2 = \frac{30 \times 10^3 \times 200}{150 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{6 \times 10^6}{3 \times 10^7} = 0.200 \text{ mm}$$

$$\delta L_3 = \frac{30 \times 10^3 \times 250}{100 \times 2 \times 10^5} = \frac{7.5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^7} = 0.375 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the elongations (superposition for series):**

$$\delta L_{total} = \delta L_1 + \delta L_2 + \delta L_3 = 0.225 + 0.200 + 0.375$$

$$\boxed{\delta L_{total} = 0.800 \text{ mm}}$$

**Final Tabulated Results:**

| Segment | Length (mm) | Area (mm²) | Stress (MPa) | Elongation (mm) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 300 | 200 | 150 | 0.225 |
| 2 | 200 | 150 | 200 | 0.200 |
| 3 | 250 | 100 | 300 | 0.375 |
| **Total** | **750** | — | — | **0.800** |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap:** Mixing N with kN, or mm² with m², is the **#1 cause of lost marks**. Always convert all quantities to consistent units *before* substitution.
> 2. **Forgetting Compatibility:** In parallel composite bars, students often compute the stress using total load $P$ instead of the *load shared* $P_i$. Always use $P_i = P \cdot A_i E_i / \sum A_i E_i$.
> 3. **Stiffness vs. Strength Confusion:** A material can have high *strength* (high ultimate stress) but low *stiffness* (low $E$). In load-sharing problems, only $E$ matters, not ultimate stress.
> 4. **Sign Convention:** Tension produces positive stress/strain; compression produces negative. Always state the sign in your final answer.
> 5. **Skipping Equilibrium Check:** In static problems, always verify $\sum F_x = 0$ before proceeding to deformation calculations.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Normal Stress:** $\sigma = P/A$ — Internal force per unit area (MPa or N/mm²).
- **Normal Strain:** $\epsilon = \delta L / L$ — Dimensionless deformation ratio.
- **Hooke's Law:** $\sigma = E \epsilon$ — Valid only within the elastic limit.
- **Master Equation:** $\delta L = PL / AE$ — Foundation for all axial deformation problems.
- **Stepped Bars (Series):** Same internal force $P$, but stress varies with $A$. Elongation is the **sum** of segment elongations: $\delta L = \frac{P}{E} \sum \frac{L_i}{A_i}$.
- **Composite Bars (Parallel):** Same elongation in all materials (compatibility), but load is shared based on **axial stiffness $AE$**: $P_i = P \cdot A_i E_i / \sum A_i E_i$.
- **Equivalent Stiffness of Composite:** $A_{eq} E_{eq} = \sum A_i E_i$.
- **Always convert units first:** Use N, mm, MPa consistently throughout the solution.
- **Strain in parallel composite must be equal** — use this as a built-in verification check.
- **Two governing principles:** (1) Equilibrium ($\sum F = 0$), (2) Compatibility (equal deformations in parallel).
- **Thermal elongation (preview for next topic):** $\delta L_{th} = \alpha L \Delta T$ — independent of applied load.
- **Indeterminate problems (preview):** When reactions exceed equilibrium equations, use compatibility to obtain additional equations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Thermal Loading - Thermal Stress and Thermal Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effects of Thermal Loading - Thermal Stress and Thermal Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Strain ($\varepsilon_T$)**: The strain induced in a material purely due to a change in temperature, without the application of any external mechanical load. It is expressed as:
>
> $$\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
>
> where $\alpha$ is the **Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion** (per °C) and $\Delta T$ is the change in temperature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Stress ($\sigma_T$)**: The internal stress that develops in a member when a free thermal deformation is **prevented or restrained**. For a bar whose free thermal expansion is fully prevented, the thermal stress is given by:
>
> $$\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$
>
> where $E$ is the **Young's Modulus** of the material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a metal rod lying freely on a table. When heated, the rod happily expands longer, like a sleeping cat stretching in the sun. The rod experiences *thermal strain* but **no thermal stress** because nothing is preventing the expansion.

Now, bolt the same rod tightly between two unyielding concrete walls. When the rod is heated, it still "wants" to expand, but the rigid walls refuse to budge. The rod is forced to remain the same length, so it pushes hard against the walls. This internal push is the **thermal stress**. The rod is now unhappy, just like a stretched spring held between two clamps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Golden Rule of Thermal Stress:**
> - **Free to expand** → Only **thermal strain** exists; **no stress**.
> - **Expansion prevented** → Both **thermal strain is suppressed** and **thermal stress is induced**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Material | $\alpha$ (per °C) | $E$ (GPa) |
|----------|-------------------|-----------|
| Steel | $12 \times 10^{-6}$ | **200** |
| Copper | $17 \times 10^{-6}$ | **100** |
| Aluminum | $23 \times 10^{-6}$ | **70** |
| Brass | $19 \times 10^{-6}$ | **100** |
| Concrete | $10 \times 10^{-6}$ | **30** |

> **Unit Conversions to remember:**
> - $1 \text{ GPa} = 10^3 \text{ MPa} = 10^3 \text{ N/mm}^2$
> - $\alpha$ is always expressed as *per °C* (or *per K*) since $\Delta T$ is a temperature difference.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic: Step-by-Step Breakdown

**Why does thermal strain occur?**
When a material is heated, the kinetic energy of its atoms increases. The atoms vibrate with greater amplitude, increasing the average interatomic distance. Macroscopically, this manifests as a uniform expansion in all directions (for isotropic materials).

**Why does thermal stress develop?**
Thermal stress is a *reaction* stress caused by the **compatibility condition** at the boundaries. If the geometry of the structure constrains this natural expansion, Newton's third law demands that the supports provide an equal and opposite reaction force, which propagates as internal stress.

**Cases of Thermal Loading in Bars:**

- **Case 1 — Bar Free to Expand:** No supports, no obstacle. The bar elongates by $\delta_T = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$. Stress = 0.

- **Case 2 — Bar Fixed at Both Ends:** The walls push back. The bar is in **pure compression**. Reaction $R$ compresses the bar by the same amount as its free expansion. The thermal stress is $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ (compressive).

- **Case 3 — Bar with One Fixed End and External Load:** Mechanical + thermal effects superpose. The total stress is $\sigma = \frac{P}{A} + E \alpha \Delta T$ (with proper sign convention).

- **Case 4 — Compound Bar (Parallel Configuration):** Two materials of *same length* joined by rigid end plates. Both must stretch equally. The material with higher $\alpha$ is forced into **compression**, and the other into **tension**.

- **Case 5 — Compound Bar (Series Configuration):** Two materials joined *end-to-end*, fixed at both outer ends. Both experience the **same internal force** $R$ (compressive).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains the **exam-ready formula arsenal** for thermal stress problems. All signs follow the convention: **Tensile is positive, Compressive is negative.**

| # | Scenario | Governing Equations | Key Output |
|---|----------|---------------------|------------|
| 1 | Free thermal strain | $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Unitless strain |
| 2 | Free thermal elongation | $\delta_T = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T$ | Length units |
| 3 | Fully constrained bar | $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Stress units |
| 4 | Reaction force (fully fixed) | $R = \sigma_T \cdot A = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T \cdot A$ | Force units |
| 5 | Parallel compound bar — Equilibrium | $\sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2 = 0$ | Internal force balance |
| 6 | Parallel compound bar — Compatibility | $\alpha_1 \Delta T + \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \alpha_2 \Delta T + \dfrac{\sigma_2}{E_2}$ | Equal elongations |
| 7 | Series compound bar — Equilibrium | $P_1 = P_2 = R$ (compressive) | Common internal force |
| 8 | Series compound bar — Compatibility | $\dfrac{R L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{R L_2}{A_2 E_2} = \alpha_1 L_1 \Delta T + \alpha_2 L_2 \Delta T$ | Zero net displacement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Substitution Tip:** In the parallel compound bar, if $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$, then material 1 is in **compression** and material 2 is in **tension**. The two forces must be equal in magnitude for self-equilibrium, so $P_1 = P_2$ gives $\sigma_1 A_1 = \sigma_2 A_2$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Railway Tracks and Bridges:** Small *expansion gaps* or *sliding bearings* are deliberately provided so that thermal expansion can occur freely, avoiding dangerous build-up of thermal stress.
- **Bimetallic Strips in Thermostats:** Two metals with different $\alpha$ are bonded. Heating causes differential expansion, bending the strip. This deflection triggers an electrical switch.
- **Pre-stressed Concrete:** Steel tendons are heated, allowed to expand, and then anchored. On cooling, they try to contract but are restrained — this induces *pre-compression* in concrete, increasing its tensile strength.
- **Engine Pistons and Cylinder Liners:** Designed with matched $\alpha$ values to maintain clearances at all operating temperatures.
- **Roller Bearings in Long Shafts:** Designed to allow axial movement to accommodate thermal expansion.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Thermal Stress in a Bar Fixed at Both Ends

**Problem Setup:**
A uniform bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, and Young's modulus $E$ is fixed between two rigid walls. The temperature of the bar is raised by $\Delta T$.

**Goal:** Find the thermal stress induced in the bar and the reaction at the supports.

**Step 1 — Imagine the bar is temporarily free.**
If the right wall is removed, the bar will expand freely on heating. The free thermal elongation is:

$$
\delta_T = \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the rigid-wall reaction as an external force.**
The right wall exerts a compressive force $R$ on the bar. The shortening caused by this force (using axial deformation formula) is:

$$
\delta_R = \frac{R \cdot L}{A \cdot E}
$$

**Step 3 — Enforce the constraint (Compatibility Condition).**
Since the wall is rigid, the *net* change in length of the bar must be zero. Therefore, the compressive shortening must exactly cancel the thermal expansion:

$$
\delta_R = \delta_T
$$

**Step 4 — Equate and solve for R.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{R \cdot L}{A \cdot E} &= \alpha \cdot L \cdot \Delta T \\
R &= A \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate thermal stress.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_T &= \frac{R}{A} \\
\sigma_T &= \frac{A \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{A} \\
\sigma_T &= E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thermal stress depends **only** on material properties ($E$, $\alpha$) and the temperature change ($\Delta T$). It is **independent** of the bar's length $L$ and cross-sectional area $A$. This is a common KTU conceptual trap!

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Thermal Stresses in a Parallel Compound Bar

**Problem Setup:**
A compound bar consists of two different materials (1 and 2) of the *same length* $L$ and cross-sectional areas $A_1$, $A_2$, bonded together between two rigid end plates. The temperature is increased by $\Delta T$.

**Goal:** Find the thermal stress in each material.

**Step 1 — Define the unknowns.**
Let $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ be the (tensile-positive) stresses in materials 1 and 2.

**Step 2 — Write the Equilibrium Condition.**
No external axial force is applied. For the system to be in equilibrium, the tensile force in one material must balance the compressive force in the other. Hence:

$$
\sigma_1 \cdot A_1 + \sigma_2 \cdot A_2 = 0
$$

This implies the two forces are equal in magnitude and opposite in sign. So:

$$
\sigma_2 = -\sigma_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Write the Compatibility Condition.**
Both materials are bonded between the same rigid end plates, so they must elongate by the same total amount.

Total elongation of material 1 = Free thermal elongation + Mechanical elongation
Total elongation of material 2 = Free thermal elongation + Mechanical elongation

$$
\alpha_1 \cdot L \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_1 \cdot L}{E_1} = \alpha_2 \cdot L \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_2 \cdot L}{E_2}
$$

Dividing throughout by $L$:

$$
\alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} = \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_2}{E_2}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the equilibrium relation.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\alpha_1 \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} &= \alpha_2 \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\sigma_1 \cdot A_1}{A_2 \cdot E_2} \\
(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T &= -\frac{\sigma_1}{E_1} - \frac{\sigma_1 \cdot A_1}{A_2 \cdot E_2} \\
(\alpha_1 - \alpha_2) \cdot \Delta T &= -\sigma_1 \left( \frac{1}{E_1} + \frac{A_1}{A_2 \cdot E_2} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sigma_1$.**

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{(\alpha_2 - \alpha_1) \cdot \Delta T}{\dfrac{1}{E_1} + \dfrac{A_1}{A_2 \cdot E_2}}
$$

And $\sigma_2$ follows from the equilibrium relation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Interpretation:**
> - If $\alpha_1 > \alpha_2$: material 1 wants to expand more → it is pushed back → $\sigma_1$ is **compressive** (negative).
> - Material 2 is stretched to match → $\sigma_2$ is **tensile** (positive).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Thermal Stress in a Series Compound Bar

**Problem Setup:**
Two materials (1 and 2) of lengths $L_1, L_2$ and areas $A_1, A_2$ are joined end-to-end to form a single bar. The bar is fixed at both outer ends. The temperature is increased by $\Delta T$.

**Goal:** Find the reaction force $R$ and the stresses in each material.

**Step 1 — Total free expansion of the assembly.**

$$
\delta_{\text{free}} = \alpha_1 \cdot L_1 \cdot \Delta T + \alpha_2 \cdot L_2 \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 2 — Compression caused by reaction R.**
Since the bar is in series, the internal force is the same in both segments and equals the reaction $R$ at the supports (compressive). The total compression is:

$$
\delta_R = \frac{R \cdot L_1}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \frac{R \cdot L_2}{A_2 \cdot E_2}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the boundary condition (zero net displacement).**

$$
\frac{R \cdot L_1}{A_1 \cdot E_1} + \frac{R \cdot L_2}{A_2 \cdot E_2} = \alpha_1 \cdot L_1 \cdot \Delta T + \alpha_2 \cdot L_2 \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for R.**

$$
R = \frac{\alpha_1 L_1 \Delta T + \alpha_2 L_2 \Delta T}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the stresses.**

$$
\sigma_1 = \frac{R}{A_1} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_2 = \frac{R}{A_2}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Series Compound Bars, both segments experience the *same magnitude* of stress-strain relationship governed by the common force R.** The stress magnitude in each segment differs only because of the different cross-sectional areas.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Thermal Stress Analysis Methodology

The Mermaid diagram below maps the decision flow that an engineer (or KTU student) must follow when analyzing a thermal loading problem. It encapsulates the entire decision tree from identifying the type of bar to extracting the final stresses.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Thermal Loading Event Initiated] --> B[Step One: Compute Free Thermal Strain and Deformation]
    B --> B1[Thermal Strain equals alpha times deltaT]
    B --> B2[Free Deformation equals alpha times L times deltaT]
    
    A --> C[Step Two: Identify Constraint Type]
    C -->|Free End| D1[Case A: No Restraint]
    C -->|Both Ends Fixed| D2[Case B: Simple Bar Fully Constrained]
    C -->|Compound Bar| D3[Step Three: Classify Compound Bar]
    
    D1 --> E1[Final Result: Stress equals Zero]
    D2 --> E2[Final Result: Stress equals E times alpha times deltaT in Compression]
    
    D3 -->|Same Length Side by Side| F1[Parallel Compound Bar Path]
    D3 -->|End to End Connection| F2[Series Compound Bar Path]
    
    F1 --> F1a[Equilibrium: Force in One equals Negative Force in Other]
    F1 --> F1b[Compatibility: Total Elongation of Both Materials are Equal]
    F1a --> F1c[Solve Coupled Equations for Stress in Each Material]
    F1b --> F1c
    
    F2 --> F2a[Equilibrium: Internal Force is Same in Both Segments equals R]
    F2 --> F2b[Compatibility: Total Expansion equals Zero Because Ends are Fixed]
    F2a --> F2c[Solve for R and then Compute Stress equals R over A for Each Segment]
    F2b --> F2c
    
    E1 --> G[Output: Final Stress State Reported]
    E2 --> G
    F1c --> G
    F2c --> G
```

### 4.2 Schematic — Typical Compound Bar Configurations

The two principal arrangements encountered in KTU problems are:

- **Type A — Parallel Compound Bar (Concentric Tube and Rod):** A steel rod sits inside a copper tube. Both span the same length between two rigid end plates. Differential thermal expansion causes internal self-equilibrating stresses.

- **Type B — Series Compound Bar (Butt-Joined Sections):** A steel section is joined end-to-end with a brass section. The whole bar is fixed between two outer rigid supports. The single reaction force $R$ acts throughout.

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram above is a *substitute* for a physical drawing. In actual exam answer sheets, students should sketch:
> 1. A rectangle representing the bar.
> 2. Hatching on both sides to indicate rigid walls.
> 3. An arrow showing $\Delta T$ (temperature rise).
> 4. Reaction force arrows $R$ at both ends pointing inward (compressive).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1]**
> **Q1.** Define *thermal stress* and *thermal strain*. State the conditions under which thermal stress develops in a member.
>
> **Model Answer:**
> **Thermal strain** is the strain induced in a body solely due to a temperature change, given by $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, where $\alpha$ is the coefficient of linear thermal expansion and $\Delta T$ is the temperature change.
>
> **Thermal stress** is the internal stress that develops in a member when its free thermal deformation is prevented or restrained by external constraints. It is given by $\sigma_T = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
>
> **Condition for thermal stress:** Thermal stress develops **only when the free expansion (or contraction) of the member is prevented**. A bar free to expand on heating experiences thermal strain but no thermal stress.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2]**
> **Q2.** Two bars, one of steel and the other of brass, are rigidly connected at their ends to form a parallel compound bar. On heating, why does the material with a higher coefficient of thermal expansion experience compressive stress?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> In a parallel compound bar, both materials are constrained to elongate by the *same amount* by the rigid end plates. The material with a higher $\alpha$ tends to expand more if free. Since this expansion is prevented, the end plates push it back, inducing **compressive stress** in it. To maintain equilibrium (no external load), the other material is stretched to match the common elongation, thereby developing **tensile stress**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model Style]**
> **Q3A.** A copper tube of 60 mm outer diameter and 50 mm inner diameter surrounds a steel rod of 50 mm diameter. The tube and rod are of the same length 500 mm. The assembly is placed between two rigid flat surfaces, with both ends touching the surfaces. The temperature of the assembly is raised by 80 °C.
>
> **(a)** Determine the stresses developed in the copper tube and the steel rod.
> **(b)** Calculate the change in length of the assembly.
>
> **Given:** $E_{cu} = 100$ GPa, $E_{st} = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_{cu} = 18 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_{st} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

#### Solution to Q3A (Part a)

**Step 1 — Calculate cross-sectional areas.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_{cu} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \left( D_{o}^2 - D_{i}^2 \right) = \frac{\pi}{4} (60^2 - 50^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 1100 \\
A_{cu} &= 863.94 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
A_{st} = \frac{\pi}{4} (50^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 2500 = 1963.50 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the equilibrium condition.**
Since there is no external axial load, the internal force in copper must balance the internal force in steel:

$$
\sigma_{cu} \cdot A_{cu} + \sigma_{st} \cdot A_{st} = 0
$$

Since $\alpha_{cu} > \alpha_{st}$, copper is pushed back (**compressive**) and steel is stretched (**tensile**). Taking magnitudes:

$$
\sigma_{cu} \cdot A_{cu} = \sigma_{st} \cdot A_{st}
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\sigma_{st} = \sigma_{cu} \cdot \frac{A_{cu}}{A_{st}} = \sigma_{cu} \cdot \frac{863.94}{1963.50} = 0.44 \, \sigma_{cu}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the compatibility condition.**
Both materials have the same total elongation:

$$
\alpha_{cu} \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_{cu}}{E_{cu}} = \alpha_{st} \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_{st}}{E_{st}}
$$

Note: copper is in compression, so its stress contribution to the total elongation is negative.

$$
(18 \times 10^{-6} - 12 \times 10^{-6}) \times 80 = \frac{\sigma_{st}}{E_{st}} + \frac{\sigma_{cu}}{E_{cu}}
$$

$$
480 \times 10^{-6} = \frac{0.44 \, \sigma_{cu}}{2 \times 10^5} + \frac{\sigma_{cu}}{1 \times 10^5}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma_{cu}$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
480 \times 10^{-6} &= \sigma_{cu} \left( \frac{0.44}{2 \times 10^5} + \frac{1}{1 \times 10^5} \right) \\
480 \times 10^{-6} &= \sigma_{cu} \left( 2.2 \times 10^{-6} + 10 \times 10^{-6} \right) \\
480 \times 10^{-6} &= \sigma_{cu} \times 12.2 \times 10^{-6} \\
\sigma_{cu} &= \frac{480 \times 10^{-6}}{12.2 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\sigma_{cu} &\approx 39.34 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $\sigma_{st}$.**

$$
\sigma_{st} = 0.44 \times 39.34 \approx 17.31 \text{ MPa (tensile)}
$$

#### Solution to Q3A (Part b)

Using copper (compression reduces its free expansion):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \alpha_{cu} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\sigma_{cu} \cdot L}{E_{cu}} \\
\delta &= 18 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 \times 80 - \frac{39.34 \times 500}{1 \times 10^5} \\
\delta &= 0.72 - 0.1967 \\
\delta &\approx 0.5233 \text{ mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification using steel** (tension adds to its free expansion):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \alpha_{st} \cdot L \cdot \Delta T + \frac{\sigma_{st} \cdot L}{E_{st}} \\
\delta &= 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 500 \times 80 + \frac{17.31 \times 500}{2 \times 10^5} \\
\delta &= 0.48 + 0.0433 \\
\delta &\approx 0.5233 \text{ mm} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The change in length of the assembly is **0.5233 mm (extension)**.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Alternative Choice]**
> **Q3B.** A compound bar consists of a steel section (Length $L_1 = 300$ mm, Diameter $d_1 = 20$ mm) and a brass section (Length $L_2 = 200$ mm, Diameter $d_2 = 25$ mm) connected end-to-end. The compound bar is fixed rigidly at both ends. The temperature of the bar is raised by 100 °C.
>
> **(a)** Find the stresses developed in the steel and brass sections.
> **(b)** Find the load taken by each section and the reaction at the supports.
>
> **Given:** $E_{st} = 200$ GPa, $E_{br} = 100$ GPa, $\alpha_{st} = 12 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C, $\alpha_{br} = 19 \times 10^{-6}$ /°C.

#### Solution to Q3B (Part a)

**Step 1 — Calculate cross-sectional areas.**

$$
A_{st} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 20^2 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2
\quad ; \quad
A_{br} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 25^2 = 490.87 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Free thermal expansion of each section.**

$$
\delta_{st, free} = \alpha_{st} \cdot L_1 \cdot \Delta T = 12 \times 10^{-6} \times 300 \times 100 = 0.360 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\delta_{br, free} = \alpha_{br} \cdot L_2 \cdot \Delta T = 19 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 100 = 0.380 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\delta_{\text{total, free}} = 0.360 + 0.380 = 0.740 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Compression produced by reaction R.**

$$
\delta_{st, R} = \frac{R \cdot L_1}{A_{st} \cdot E_{st}} = \frac{R \times 300}{314.16 \times 2 \times 10^5} = R \times 4.775 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\delta_{br, R} = \frac{R \cdot L_2}{A_{br} \cdot E_{br}} = \frac{R \times 200}{490.87 \times 1 \times 10^5} = R \times 4.075 \times 10^{-6} \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Equate the total compression to the total free expansion (zero net displacement).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R \times 4.775 \times 10^{-6} + R \times 4.075 \times 10^{-6} &= 0.740 \\
R \times 8.85 \times 10^{-6} &= 0.740 \\
R &= \frac{0.740}{8.85 \times 10^{-6}} \\
R &\approx 83,616 \text{ N} \approx 83.62 \text{ kN (compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the stresses.**

$$
\sigma_{st} = \frac{R}{A_{st}} = \frac{83,616}{314.16} \approx 266.18 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

$$
\sigma_{br} = \frac{R}{A_{br}} = \frac{83,616}{490.87} \approx 170.34 \text{ MPa (compressive)}
$$

#### Solution to Q3B (Part b)

Since the bar is in series, the load in each section equals the support reaction:

$$
P_{st} = P_{br} = R = 83.62 \text{ kN (compressive)}
$$

The reaction at each support is also $R = 83.62$ kN (acting inward, compressive).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** In the parallel compound bar (Q3A), students often forget to make the stress in one material **negative** of the other. Without proper signs, the compatibility equation will give a wrong sign for the final stresses.
> 2. **Length vs. Area Confusion:** In the series compound bar (Q3B), students sometimes mix up the formula with the parallel one. Remember: **series means same force, parallel means same elongation**.
> 3. **Missing the Negative Sign in Free Expansion Equation:** When a material is in compression, its mechanical strain *subtracts* from the free thermal strain, not adds. Writing $\alpha \Delta T + \frac{\sigma}{E}$ blindly (without signs) is the #1 cause of sign errors in thermal stress problems.
> 4. **Unit Inconsistency:** Use $E$ in N/mm² and $\alpha$ in per °C, with $L$ in mm — this gives stress in N/mm² (= MPa). Mixing cm with N/mm² is a common careless mistake.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal strain** is a *geometric* effect, while **thermal stress** is a *mechanical reaction* due to restraint.
- A bar free to expand has $\varepsilon_T = \alpha \Delta T$ but $\sigma_T = 0$.
- A bar fully constrained has $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ (independent of $L$ and $A$).
- For a **parallel compound bar**: equilibrium $\Rightarrow \sigma_1 A_1 + \sigma_2 A_2 = 0$, compatibility $\Rightarrow$ equal total elongations. Material with higher $\alpha$ is **compressed**, lower $\alpha$ is **stretched**.
- For a **series compound bar**: equilibrium $\Rightarrow$ same internal force $R$, compatibility $\Rightarrow$ zero net displacement. Both segments carry the **same load**.
- The thermal stress for a fully fixed bar is $\sigma_T = E \alpha \Delta T$ — a high-favorite KTU 2-mark or 3-mark question.
- Real-world applications include expansion joints in bridges, bimetallic thermostats, pre-stressed concrete, and engine components.
- Always **draw the free body diagram** with reaction force directions before writing equations.
- **Sign convention**: tensile = positive, compressive = negative. Stick to it consistently.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Thermal Stress on a Prismatic Bar Held Between Fixed Supports
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Thermal Stress on a Prismatic Bar Held Between Fixed Supports

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Stress (in a Constrained Prismatic Bar):**
> Thermal stress is the internal stress induced in a body when its free thermal expansion or contraction is **partially or fully prevented** by external constraints, even though no external mechanical load is applied.

For a **uniform prismatic bar** rigidly held between **two immovable (fixed) supports** and subjected to a uniform temperature change $\Delta T$, the supports exert a **compressive reaction force** (for heating) or a **tensile reaction force** (for cooling) on the bar. The corresponding stress is given by:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of Elasticity (in **N/m²** or **Pa**)
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of Linear Thermal Expansion (in **per °C** or **/°C**)
- $\Delta T$ = Change in Temperature (in **°C** or **K**)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Stuck in a Doorway" Analogy:**
> Imagine pushing a long metal rod through a fixed doorway that is exactly the length of the room. Now heat the rod with a blowtorch. The rod *wants* to grow longer, but the rigid walls of the doorway will not let it. The rod therefore pushes **outward against the walls** (compression), and the walls push back **inward** on the rod with equal force. The molecules inside the rod are squeezed together — that squeezing is the **thermal stress**.

The *magical* result: the deformation (strain) is **zero**, but the stress is **not zero**. This is a counter-intuitive but board-favourite result in KTU examinations.

### 1.3 Why This Problem Matters

- **Bridges and Railway Tracks:** Gaps (expansion joints) are left to prevent thermal stress buildup.
- **Pipelines carrying hot fluids / steam:** Use expansion loops or bellows.
- **Concrete roads in Kerala:** Divided into slabs separated by bitumen gaps.
- **Bimetallic strips** in thermostats rely on this differential expansion principle.

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners love to test that a student can clearly distinguish between the **strain** (zero in the fully fixed case) and the **stress** (non-zero, equal to $E\alpha\Delta T$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

**Step 1 — Identify the Physical Setup**
- A prismatic bar of original length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, made of a homogeneous material with Young's modulus $E$ and coefficient of thermal expansion $\alpha$, is **rigidly gripped** at both ends.
- The supports are perfectly rigid (incompressible, immovable).

**Step 2 — Free Thermal Expansion (Imaginary Case)**
- If the supports were **removed**, the bar would freely elongate by:

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$$

This is the **free thermal deformation** that *would have happened* if unconstrained.

**Step 3 — Apply the Compatibility Condition**
- The bar is held between fixed supports, so the **net change in length must be zero**:

$$\delta_{net} = \delta_{th} - \delta_{mech} = 0$$

where $\delta_{mech}$ is the deformation produced by the reaction force $R$ of the supports.

**Step 4 — Mechanical Deformation Due to Reaction**
- The supports push the bar back, producing a **mechanical compression** (when heated) of:

$$\delta_{mech} = \frac{R \, L}{A \, E}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the Reaction Force**

$$\alpha L \Delta T = \frac{R L}{A E} \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = A E \alpha \Delta T$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Thermal Stress**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The thermal stress is **independent** of the length $L$ and the cross-sectional area $A$ of the bar. It depends only on the **material properties** ($E$, $\alpha$) and the **temperature change** ($\Delta T$).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Description |
|---|----------|---------|------------|-------------|
| 1 | Free Thermal Deformation | $\delta_{th} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$ | m | Elongation if unconstrained |
| 2 | Mechanical Deformation (axial) | $\delta_{mech} = \dfrac{R \, L}{A \, E}$ | m | Shortening due to reaction force |
| 3 | Compatibility Equation | $\delta_{th} = \delta_{mech}$ | m | Net deformation = 0 |
| 4 | Reaction Force | $R = A \, E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | N | Force exerted by supports on bar |
| 5 | Thermal Stress | $\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ | Pa or N/m² | Compressive if heated, tensile if cooled |
| 6 | Thermal Strain (free) | $\epsilon_{th} = \alpha \, \Delta T$ | dimensionless | Strain that *would occur* if free |
| 7 | Net Strain (fully fixed) | $\epsilon_{net} = 0$ | dimensionless | Total strain when both ends fixed |
| 8 | Work / Strain Energy stored | $U = \dfrac{\sigma_{th}^{\,2}}{2E} \cdot V$ | J | $V$ = volume of bar |

> [!NOTE]
> Standard KTU numerical values to remember: For **steel**, $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Expansion Joints in Bridges:** Prevents the massive axial forces that would otherwise buckle the structure.
- **High-Pressure Steam Pipelines:** Must incorporate bends, loops, or bellows to absorb thermal growth.
- **Railway Track Design (Continuous Welded Rails):** Pre-stressed at a reference temperature to limit both hot-weather buckling and cold-weather tensile fracture.
- **Composite Bars and Bi-metallic Strips:** When two materials with different $\alpha$ are bonded, differential thermal stress causes **curvature change** — the operating principle of thermostats and circuit breakers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Thermal Stress in a Fully Constrained Prismatic Bar

**Given:**
- Length of bar = $L$
- Cross-sectional area = $A$
- Young's modulus = $E$
- Coefficient of linear thermal expansion = $\alpha$
- Rise in temperature = $\Delta T$
- Both ends are **rigidly fixed**.

**To Find:** Reaction force $R$ and thermal stress $\sigma_{th}$ developed in the bar.

---

**Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Free Thermal Elongation (Unconstrained Case)**

If the bar were free to expand, the elongation due to temperature rise $\Delta T$ would be:

$$\delta_{T} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T$$

This is the displacement the bar *attempts* to make.

**Step 2 — Effect of the Rigid Supports**

The fixed supports do not allow the bar to change its length. Therefore, the supports exert a **compressive reaction force $R$** at each end (Newton's Third Law), which compresses the bar back by an amount $\delta_R$.

**Step 3 — Mechanical Compression Produced by the Reaction**

For an axial compressive force $R$, the mechanical shortening of the bar is:

$$\delta_{R} = \frac{R \, L}{A \, E}$$

**Step 4 — Compatibility (Geometric) Condition**

The net change in length of the bar must equal zero because both ends are rigidly fixed:

$$\delta_{T} - \delta_{R} = 0$$

Substituting the two expressions:

$$\alpha \, L \, \Delta T \; - \; \frac{R \, L}{A \, E} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplification**

The length $L$ cancels from both sides (one of the most elegant cancellations in Mechanics of Solids!):

$$\alpha \, \Delta T = \frac{R}{A \, E}$$

**Step 6 — Reaction Force**

Solving for $R$:

$$R = A \, E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

**Step 7 — Thermal Stress**

The axial stress is the reaction force divided by the cross-sectional area:

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

**Step 8 — Sign Convention Summary**

| Condition | Nature of Reaction | Nature of Stress |
|-----------|--------------------|------------------|
| Bar is **heated** ($\Delta T > 0$) | Supports **push inward** (compressive) | **Compressive** |
| Bar is **cooled** ($\Delta T < 0$) | Supports **pull outward** (tensile) | **Tensile** |

**Result Confirmed:**

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T}$$

$$\boxed{R = A \, E \, \alpha \, \Delta T}$$

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Generalisation to Non-Uniform Temperature and Composite Bars

| Case | Setup | Compatibility Equation | Result for $R$ | Result for $\sigma$ |
|------|-------|------------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Uniform bar, uniform $\Delta T$, both ends fixed | $\alpha L \Delta T = \dfrac{RL}{AE}$ | $R = AE\alpha\Delta T$ | $\sigma = E\alpha\Delta T$ |
| 2 | Uniform bar, one end fixed, other end free | No constraint, free to expand | $R = 0$ | $\sigma = 0$ |
| 3 | Uniform bar, gap of length $g$ at one end, heated by $\Delta T$ | If $\alpha L \Delta T \le g$, no contact | $R = 0$ | $\sigma = 0$ |
| 3b | Same as 3, but $\alpha L \Delta T > g$ | $\alpha L \Delta T - g = \dfrac{RL}{AE}$ | $R = AE\left(\alpha\Delta T - \dfrac{g}{L}\right)$ | $\sigma = E\left(\alpha\Delta T - \dfrac{g}{L}\right)$ |
| 4 | Composite bar (two segments of different materials) rigidly fixed at ends | $\alpha_1 L_1 \Delta T + \alpha_2 L_2 \Delta T = \dfrac{RL_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{RL_2}{A_2 E_2}$ | $R = \dfrac{\Delta T \left(\alpha_1 L_1 + \alpha_2 L_2\right)}{\dfrac{L_1}{A_1 E_1} + \dfrac{L_2}{A_2 E_2}}$ | $\sigma_i = R/A_i$ in each segment |

> [!TIP]
> For a typical KTU 14-mark question, students are expected to handle **Case 1** (uniform bar) and at least one variation from **Case 3 / 4** (gap or composite). Master the **compatibility equation** and the rest follows algebraically.

---

### 3.3 Workable Numerical Demonstration (Worked Example, KTU Style)

**Problem:** A steel bar, 1.5 m long and 20 mm × 20 mm in cross-section, is held between two rigid supports. The temperature of the bar is raised by 60 °C. Find:
1. The thermal stress developed
2. The reaction force exerted by the supports
3. The deformation if the bar were free

Take $E = 200 \text{ GPa}$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$.

**Solution:**

**Given:**
$L = 1.5 \text{ m}$, $A = 20 \times 20 = 400 \text{ mm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $\Delta T = 60 \text{ °C}$, $E = 200 \times 10^9 \text{ N/m}^2$, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /°C}$.

**(1) Thermal Stress:**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T = (200 \times 10^9)(12 \times 10^{-6})(60)$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 200 \times 12 \times 60 \times 10^{3} = 144 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = 144 \text{ MPa (Compressive)}}$$

**(2) Reaction Force:**

$$R = \sigma_{th} \times A = 144 \times 10^{6} \times 400 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$R = 144 \times 400 = 57600 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R = 57.6 \text{ kN (Compressive)}}$$

**(3) Free Deformation (if supports were removed):**

$$\delta_{T} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6})(1.5)(60) = 1080 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{T} = 1.08 \text{ mm}}$$

This is exactly the amount by which the supports compress the bar — confirming the compatibility condition.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Thermal Stress in a Constrained Bar

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Prismatic Bar of length L] --> B[Initial State: Temperature T0]
    B --> C[Temperature raised to T0 + dT]
    C --> D{Boundary Condition}
    D --> E[Both ends FREE]
    D --> F[Both ends FIXED]
    E --> G[Free Thermal Expansion: d_th = alpha L dT]
    G --> H[Strain: alpha dT]
    H --> I[Stress: ZERO]
    F --> J[Supports generate Reaction Force R]
    J --> K[Mechanical Compression: d_R = RL divided by AE]
    K --> L[Net Deformation: d_th minus d_R equals ZERO]
    L --> M[Thermal Stress: sigma equals E alpha dT]
    M --> N[Stored Strain Energy: sigma squared V divided by 2E]
```

### 4.2 Physical Schematic (Descriptive Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LEFT_WALL [Fixed Support A - Rigid Wall]
        A1[Immovable]
    end
    subgraph BAR [Prismatic Bar]
        B1[Length L]
        B2[Cross-section A]
        B3[Material: E, alpha]
        B4[Temperature change: dT]
    end
    subgraph RIGHT_WALL [Fixed Support B - Rigid Wall]
        C1[Immovable]
    end
    LEFT_WALL -- Compressive Reaction R pushes right-to-left --> BAR
    BAR -- Compressive Reaction R pushes left-to-right --> RIGHT_WALL
    B4 --> B1
    B3 --> B2
```

### 4.3 Force and Deformation Vector Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Free Expansion] --> S2[Bar tries to elongate by alpha L dT]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Supports resist]
    S3 --> S4[Supports apply inward force R]
    S4 --> S5[Bar is compressed back by RL divided by AE]
    S5 --> S6[Equilibrium: alpha L dT equals RL divided by AE]
    S6 --> S7[Result: R equals AE alpha dT]
    S7 --> S8[Thermal Stress: sigma equals E alpha dT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The visual logic mirrors the algebraic derivation: **free expansion** on one side, **mechanical compression** on the other, **equilibrium** between them gives the thermal stress.

### 4.4 Effect-of-Constraint Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Free End] --> B[No thermal stress - bar expands freely]
    C[Fixed End - Rigid] --> D[Maximum thermal stress - sigma equals E alpha dT]
    E[Partially Restrained, with spring of stiffness k] --> F[Partial thermal stress - depends on k and AE divided by L]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define thermal stress. State the expression for the thermal stress developed in a prismatic bar rigidly fixed at both ends and subjected to a uniform temperature rise.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Thermal Stress** is the internal stress induced in a body when its free thermal expansion or contraction is prevented by external constraints, even in the absence of any external mechanical load.

For a uniform prismatic bar of length $L$, cross-sectional area $A$, Young's modulus $E$, and coefficient of linear thermal expansion $\alpha$, held between two rigid supports and subjected to a uniform temperature rise $\Delta T$:

**Compatibility condition:** $\alpha L \Delta T = \dfrac{RL}{AE}$

**Reaction force:** $R = A E \alpha \Delta T$

**Thermal stress:**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

The stress is **compressive** in nature for a temperature rise.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**A steel rod is placed between two rigid supports with no initial stress. If the temperature of the rod is decreased, what is the nature of the stress developed? Justify briefly.**

**Model Answer:**

When the temperature of the rod is **decreased** (cooling, $\Delta T < 0$), the bar *tends to contract*. Since the supports are rigid, they prevent the contraction by **pulling outward** on the bar.

The reaction force is therefore **tensile** in nature, and the stress developed in the bar is **tensile**.

Magnitude:

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$$

Since $\Delta T$ is negative, the stress is numerically positive when treated as tensile. For steel ($E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$), a 50 °C drop yields a tensile stress of $120$ MPa.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT confuse thermal strain and thermal stress.** In a fully fixed bar, the *strain is zero* but the *stress is non-zero*. Many students write "no deformation, hence no stress" and lose 3–4 marks.
> - **Always state units explicitly** in numerical answers. Marks are deducted if you write $E = 200$ GPa but substitute $200$ in the formula.
> - **Sign convention** must be clearly mentioned. State whether the stress is tensile or compressive.
> - **Show the compatibility equation** explicitly in the solution. It is the heart of the problem. Skipping it leads to a 5-mark deduction.
> - **For composite bars**, do not assume the same stress in both segments. Compute each $\sigma_i = R / A_i$ separately.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** A copper bar, 800 mm long, is held between two rigid supports at a temperature of 20 °C. Find the temperature to which the bar must be heated so that a compressive stress of 70 MPa is developed in the bar. Take $E = 100$ GPa and $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6} /°C$ for copper. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel rod 1.2 m long and 600 mm² in cross-section is placed between two rigid supports at 25 °C. The temperature of the rod is raised to 95 °C. Determine (i) the thermal stress developed, (ii) the reaction force exerted by the supports, and (iii) the deformation of the rod if it had been free. Take $E = 200$ GPa and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Given:** $L = 800$ mm $= 0.8$ m, $\sigma_{th} = 70 \times 10^{6}$ N/m² (compressive), $E = 100 \times 10^{9}$ N/m², $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6} /°C$, $T_1 = 20$ °C.

**Formula:**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta T = \frac{\sigma_{th}}{E \, \alpha}$$

**Substitution:**

$$\Delta T = \frac{70 \times 10^{6}}{(100 \times 10^{9})(17 \times 10^{-6})} = \frac{70 \times 10^{6}}{1.7 \times 10^{6}} = 41.18 \text{ °C}$$

**Final Temperature:**

$$T_2 = T_1 + \Delta T = 20 + 41.18 = 61.18 \text{ °C}$$

$$\boxed{T_2 \approx 61.2 \text{ °C}}$$

---

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $L = 1.2$ m, $A = 600$ mm² $= 600 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $T_1 = 25$ °C, $T_2 = 95$ °C, $E = 200 \times 10^{9}$ N/m², $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.

**Temperature change:**

$$\Delta T = 95 - 25 = 70 \text{ °C}$$

**(i) Thermal Stress:**

$$\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T = (200 \times 10^{9})(12 \times 10^{-6})(70)$$

$$\sigma_{th} = 200 \times 12 \times 70 \times 10^{3} = 168 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} = 168 \text{ MPa (Compressive)}}$$

**(ii) Reaction Force:**

$$R = \sigma_{th} \times A = 168 \times 10^{6} \times 600 \times 10^{-6} = 100800 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{R = 100.8 \text{ kN (Compressive)}}$$

**(iii) Free Deformation:**

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha \, L \, \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6})(1.2)(70) = 1008 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.008 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{th} \approx 1.008 \text{ mm (Elongation, if free)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** A composite bar consists of a steel portion of length 600 mm and cross-section 1200 mm² rigidly connected to a brass portion of length 400 mm and cross-section 800 mm². The bar is held between two rigid supports. Find the stresses developed in each material when the system is subjected to a temperature rise of 50 °C. Take for steel: $E_s = 200$ GPa, $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$; for brass: $E_b = 100$ GPa, $\alpha_b = 18 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A steel rod of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area 500 mm² is placed between two rigid walls with an initial gap of 0.5 mm. The temperature of the rod is raised by 80 °C. Find the thermal stress developed in the rod and the reaction force at the supports. Take $E = 200$ GPa and $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Composite Bar:**

**Given:** For steel: $L_s = 0.6$ m, $A_s = 1200 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $E_s = 200 \times 10^{9}$ N/m², $\alpha_s = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. For brass: $L_b = 0.4$ m, $A_b = 800 \times 10^{-6}$ m², $E_b = 100 \times 10^{9}$ N/m², $\alpha_b = 18 \times 10^{-6} /°C$. $\Delta T = 50$ °C.

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion of composite bar:**

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha_s L_s \Delta T + \alpha_b L_b \Delta T$$

$$\delta_{th} = \Delta T (\alpha_s L_s + \alpha_b L_b)$$

$$\delta_{th} = 50 \times [(12 \times 10^{-6})(0.6) + (18 \times 10^{-6})(0.4)]$$

$$\delta_{th} = 50 \times [7.2 \times 10^{-6} + 7.2 \times 10^{-6}] = 50 \times 14.4 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\delta_{th} = 720 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 0.72 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Mechanical compression produced by reaction $R$:**

The same force $R$ passes through both segments (axial equilibrium):

$$\delta_{R} = R \left( \frac{L_s}{A_s E_s} + \frac{L_b}{A_b E_b} \right)$$

Compute each term:

$$\frac{L_s}{A_s E_s} = \frac{0.6}{(1200 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{9})} = \frac{0.6}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = 2.5 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/N}$$

$$\frac{L_b}{A_b E_b} = \frac{0.4}{(800 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{9})} = \frac{0.4}{0.8 \times 10^{8}} = 5.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m/N}$$

Sum: $7.5 \times 10^{-9}$ m/N.

**Step 3 — Apply compatibility:**

$$\delta_{th} = \delta_{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad 720 \times 10^{-6} = R \times 7.5 \times 10^{-9}$$

$$R = \frac{720 \times 10^{-6}}{7.5 \times 10^{-9}} = 96 \times 10^{3} \text{ N} = 96 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Stresses in each segment:**

$$\sigma_s = \frac{R}{A_s} = \frac{96 \times 10^{3}}{1200 \times 10^{-6}} = 80 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_s = 80 \text{ MPa (Compressive, in steel)}}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{R}{A_b} = \frac{96 \times 10^{3}}{800 \times 10^{-6}} = 120 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_b = 120 \text{ MPa (Compressive, in brass)}}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice: although the **force** is the same, the **stress differs** in each segment because their cross-sectional areas differ. KTU loves this contrast.

---

**Part (b) — Bar with Initial Gap:**

**Given:** $L = 1.5$ m, $A = 500$ mm² $= 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m², gap $g = 0.5$ mm $= 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta T = 80$ °C, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ N/m², $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.

**Step 1 — Free thermal expansion:**

$$\delta_{th} = \alpha L \Delta T = (12 \times 10^{-6})(1.5)(80) = 1440 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 1.44 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with gap:**

Since $\delta_{th} = 1.44 \text{ mm} > g = 0.5 \text{ mm}$, the bar contacts the supports and develops stress.

**Step 3 — Compatibility equation with gap:**

The bar first expands freely by $g$, then is compressed by the reaction $R$ by the remaining amount:

$$\delta_{th} - g = \frac{R L}{A E}$$

$$(1.44 \times 10^{-3}) - (0.5 \times 10^{-3}) = \frac{R \times 1.5}{(500 \times 10^{-6})(200 \times 10^{9})}$$

$$0.94 \times 10^{-3} = \frac{R \times 1.5}{100 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$R = \frac{0.94 \times 10^{-3} \times 100 \times 10^{6}}{1.5} = \frac{94 \times 10^{3}}{1.5} = 62.67 \times 10^{3} \text{ N}$$

**Step 4 — Thermal Stress:**

$$\sigma_{th} = \frac{R}{A} = \frac{62.67 \times 10^{3}}{500 \times 10^{-6}} = 125.33 \times 10^{6} \text{ N/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{th} \approx 125.33 \text{ MPa (Compressive)}}$$

$$\boxed{R \approx 62.67 \text{ kN (Compressive)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Many students forget to subtract the **gap** from the free expansion. The correct compatibility is $\delta_{th} - g = \delta_R$, not $\delta_{th} = \delta_R$.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Thermal stress arises when free thermal deformation is **prevented** by external constraints.
- **Key Formula:** $\sigma_{th} = E \, \alpha \, \Delta T$ — independent of length and area.
- **Reaction Force:** $R = A E \alpha \Delta T$ — depends on cross-sectional area.
- **Compatibility is King:** Always write the compatibility equation $\delta_{thermal} = \delta_{mechanical}$ (or with gap: $\delta_{thermal} - g = \delta_{mechanical}$).
- **Sign Convention:** Heating → Compressive stress. Cooling → Tensile stress.
- **Zero Strain ≠ Zero Stress:** Fully fixed bar has zero net strain but maximum thermal stress.
- **Standard Material Values (Steel):** $E = 200$ GPa, $\alpha = 12 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.
- **Standard Material Values (Copper):** $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha = 17 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.
- **Standard Material Values (Brass):** $E = 100$ GPa, $\alpha = 18 \times 10^{-6} /°C$.
- **Composite Bars:** Same axial force, but different stresses in each segment based on area.
- **Gap Problems:** Always subtract the gap from the free expansion before equating to mechanical compression.
- **Units Discipline:** Convert mm² to m² and GPa to N/m² before substituting into the formula. KTU examiners strictly deduct for unit mismatches.
- **Real-world link:** Expansion joints in bridges, gaps in railway tracks, loops in steam pipelines — all exist to avoid this very thermal stress.
- **Likely KTU Question Type:** "Compute thermal stress and reaction force for a uniform bar" (Part A, 3 marks) and "Composite bar or bar with initial gap" (Part B, 7 or 14 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principal Planes and Principal Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principal Planes and Principal Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Principal Planes** are defined as those planes within a stressed body on which the **resultant stress acts entirely normal to the plane**, meaning the **shear stress (τ) on these planes is identically zero**. The normal stresses acting on these principal planes are termed **Principal Stresses**, and they represent the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** that occur at a point in a material under combined loading conditions.

Mathematically, a principal plane satisfies the condition:

$$\tau_\theta = 0$$

for a specific orientation angle $\theta = \theta_p$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim style):** Principal stresses are the normal stresses acting on those mutually perpendicular planes where the shear stress component vanishes completely. For a 2D stress element, there are always **two principal stresses** ($\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$) acting on two perpendicular principal planes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing on a sloped roof while it is being pushed simultaneously by the wind (horizontal) and by its own weight (vertical). The combined push creates a force that is neither purely horizontal nor purely vertical — it acts at some slant. If you rotate yourself and find the exact angle where you feel only a perpendicular "push" against your body with no sliding tendency, **you have found a principal direction**.

In engineering terms:
- The **roof** = the small element of material at a point.
- The **wind + weight** = the combined normal and shear stresses ($\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\tau_{xy}$).
- **Your rotated body** = a plane inclined at angle $\theta_p$.
- **The pure perpendicular push** = the **principal stress** (maximum or minimum).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Principal stresses are critical in failure analysis because **materials fail along planes of maximum stress**, not necessarily along the original x-y coordinate axes. Engineers always compute principal stresses before deciding a material's safety factor.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | SI Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| Normal Stress (X-direction) | $\sigma_x$ | **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa** | Stress acting on face perpendicular to X-axis |
| Normal Stress (Y-direction) | $\sigma_y$ | **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa** | Stress acting on face perpendicular to Y-axis |
| Shear Stress | $\tau_{xy}$ | **N/m² (Pa)** or **MPa** | Tangential stress on X-face in Y-direction |
| Principal Angle | $\theta_p$ | **Radians** or **Degrees** | Orientation of principal plane |
| Principal Stress | $\sigma_1, \sigma_2$ | **MPa** | Maximum/minimum normal stresses |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Stress Transformation Theory — Step-by-Step Logic

Consider a 2D rectangular element subjected to stresses $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, and $\tau_{xy}$. To find stresses on an inclined plane rotated by angle $\theta$ (measured counter-clockwise from the vertical reference):

**Step 1 — Geometry of Inclined Plane:**
Cut the element along an inclined plane. The plane has:
- Normal direction at angle $\theta$ from the X-axis.
- Tangential direction perpendicular to the normal.

**Step 2 — Equilibrium of Wedge:**
By resolving forces along the inclined plane's normal and tangential directions:
- Sum of forces normal to the plane = stress × area → gives $\sigma_\theta$
- Sum of forces tangential to the plane = stress × area → gives $\tau_\theta$

**Step 3 — Generate the Transformation Equations:**
Using basic trigonometry and force balance, the general stress transformation equations emerge.

**Step 4 — Apply the Principal Condition ($\tau_\theta = 0$):**
Differentiate $\sigma_\theta$ with respect to $\theta$ and set it to zero to find the angle that maximizes/minimizes normal stress.

### 2.2 Why and How This Matters in Engineering

- **Failure Prediction (Ductile Materials):** Yielding in ductile metals like steel begins when the **maximum shear stress** (related to principal stress difference) reaches a critical value — governed by **Tresca's criterion**.
- **Brittle Fracture (Cast Iron, Concrete):** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal tensile stress** exceeds the material's tensile strength.
- **Pressure Vessels:** Cylindrical pressure vessels have hoop stress ($\sigma_h$) and longitudinal stress ($\sigma_l$) — these ARE principal stresses because no shear acts on the longitudinal or circumferential planes.
- **Shafts under Combined Loading:** Bending + torsion creates complex stress states; principal stresses determine fatigue life.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Normal stress on inclined plane | $\sigma_\theta = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$ | Stress at any rotated section |
| 2 | Shear stress on inclined plane | $\tau_\theta = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$ | Shear at any rotated section |
| 3 | Principal plane angle | $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ | Orientation of principal planes |
| 4 | Major Principal Stress | $\sigma_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Maximum normal stress |
| 5 | Minor Principal Stress | $\sigma_2 = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Minimum normal stress |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Used in Tresca yield criterion |
| 7 | Plane of max shear angle | $\tan(2\theta_s) = -\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$ | Orientation at 45° to principal planes |
| 8 | Sum of principal stresses | $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | Invariant property |
| 9 | Center of Mohr's circle | $C = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ | Average normal stress (always invariant) |
| 10 | Radius of Mohr's circle | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Equals $\tau_{max}$ and $\dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Sign Convention Reminder:** Tension is **positive (+)** and compression is **negative (−)**. Shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ is **positive** if it acts in the **positive y-direction on the face whose outward normal is in the positive x-direction** (right-hand rule consistent).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** For 3D problems, three principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ exist. The 2D case is simply a special case where $\sigma_3 = 0$ (plane stress) or $\sigma_3$ acts out of the considered plane.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Stress on an Inclined Plane

Consider a small triangular wedge element cut from a stressed body. The wedge has:
- Vertical face of area $A$ (with stresses $\sigma_x$ and $\tau_{xy}$)
- Horizontal face of area $A$ (with stresses $\sigma_y$ and $\tau_{xy}$)
- Inclined face of area $A_\theta$ at angle $\theta$

From geometry:
$$A_x = A_\theta \cos\theta \quad \text{and} \quad A_y = A_\theta \sin\theta$$

**Force Balance in the Normal Direction (perpendicular to inclined plane):**

$$\sigma_\theta \cdot A_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot A_\theta \cos\theta \cdot \cos\theta + \sigma_y \cdot A_\theta \sin\theta \cdot \sin\theta + 2\tau_{xy} \cdot A_\theta \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta$$

Dividing throughout by $A_\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \sigma_x \cos^2\theta + \sigma_y \sin^2\theta + 2\tau_{xy}\sin\theta\cos\theta$$

Applying the double-angle identities $\cos^2\theta = \dfrac{1+\cos(2\theta)}{2}$, $\sin^2\theta = \dfrac{1-\cos(2\theta)}{2}$, and $\sin(2\theta) = 2\sin\theta\cos\theta$:

$$\sigma_\theta = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

**Force Balance in the Tangential Direction (along the inclined plane):**

$$\tau_\theta \cdot A_\theta = \sigma_x \cdot A_\theta \cos\theta \cdot \sin\theta - \sigma_y \cdot A_\theta \sin\theta \cdot \cos\theta - \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) A_\theta$$

After dividing by $A_\theta$ and applying trigonometric identities:

$$\tau_\theta = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Principal Plane Angle

For the principal plane, the **condition is** $\tau_\theta = 0$. Therefore:

$$0 = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)$$

Rearranging:

$$\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) = \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p)$$

Dividing both sides by $\cos(2\theta_p)$:

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Principal Stress Magnitude

Substituting $\theta_p$ back into the normal stress equation. Note from the principal angle derivation:
