$$\sin(2\theta_p) = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

$$\cos(2\theta_p) = \frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Substituting into the equation for $\sigma_\theta$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot\frac{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)/2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}} + \tau_{xy}\cdot\frac{\tau_{xy}}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

Combining the last two terms over a common denominator:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}{\sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}}$$

This simplifies to the final elegant result:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Maximum Shear Stress

Maximum shear occurs when $\dfrac{d\tau_\theta}{d\theta} = 0$, which gives the plane of max shear angle:

$$\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}}$$

Substituting this angle into the shear stress equation:

$$\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

And the normal stress on this plane equals the **center of Mohr's circle**:

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$$

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** At a point in a stressed material, $\sigma_x = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 40$ MPa, and $\tau_{xy} = 30$ MPa (positive). Find the principal stresses, principal plane angles, and maximum shear stress.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the center (average normal stress):**

$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 40}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the radius:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(20)^2 + (30)^2} = \sqrt{400 + 900} = \sqrt{1300} \approx 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 36.06 = 96.06 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 60 - 36.06 = 23.94 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Principal plane angles:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(30)}{80 - 40} = \frac{60}{40} = 1.5$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(1.5) \approx 56.31°$$

$$\theta_{p1} \approx 28.15° \quad \text{(for } \sigma_1\text{)}, \quad \theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90° \approx 118.15° \quad \text{(for } \sigma_2\text{)}$$

**Step 5 — Maximum shear stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = 36.06 \text{ MPa}$$

**Verification using invariant:**

$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = 96.06 + 23.94 = 120 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y = 80 + 40 = 120 \quad \checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stress Analysis Methodology

The following flowchart illustrates the complete analytical pipeline a KTU student must follow when solving principal stress problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["GIVEN: Sigma_x Sigma_y Tau_xy"] --> node2["COMPUTE AVERAGE NORMAL STRESS"]
    node2 --> node3["C EQUALS sigma_x plus sigma_y divided by 2"]
    node3 --> node4["COMPUTE RADIUS OF MOHR CIRCLE"]
    node4 --> node5["R EQUALS sqrt of bracket sigma_x minus sigma_y divided by 2 squared plus Tau_xy squared"]
    node5 --> node6["CALCULATE PRINCIPAL STRESSES"]
    node6 --> node7["Sigma 1 EQUALS C plus R"]
    node6 --> node8["Sigma 2 EQUALS C minus R"]
    node5 --> node9["CALCULATE MAX SHEAR STRESS"]
    node9 --> node10["Tau max EQUALS R"]
    node1 --> node11["CALCULATE PRINCIPAL PLANE ANGLE"]
    node11 --> node12["tan 2 Theta EQUALS 2 Tau_xy divided by sigma_x minus sigma_y"]
    node7 --> node13["FINAL ANSWER ASSEMBLY"]
    node8 --> node13
    node10 --> node13
    node12 --> node13
    node13 --> node14["OUTPUT: Sigma_1 Sigma_2 Theta_p Tau_max"]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Physical Interpretation of Principal Planes

The block diagram below maps the geometric and physical interactions between stress components on an inclined element.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT["ORIGINAL STRESS STATE"]
        a1["Sigma_x Face"]
        a2["Sigma_y Face"]
        a3["Tau_xy Shear"]
    end
    subgraph TRANSFORM["STRESS TRANSFORMATION"]
        b1["Rotate element by angle Theta"]
        b2["Apply force equilibrium equations"]
        b3["Derive Sigma_Theta and Tau_Theta"]
    end
    subgraph PRINCIPAL["PRINCIPAL STATE CONDITION"]
        c1["Set Tau_Theta EQUALS 0"]
        c2["Solve d Sigma by d Theta EQUALS 0"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["PRINCIPAL STRESS RESULT"]
        d1["Sigma_1 MAXIMUM"]
        d2["Sigma_2 MINIMUM"]
        d3["Tau MAX at 45 degrees to principal"]
    end
    a1 --> b1
    a2 --> b1
    a3 --> b1
    b1 --> b2
    b2 --> b3
    b3 --> c1
    b3 --> c2
    c1 --> d1
    c1 --> d2
    c2 --> d3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Tip for Students:** In Mermaid `flowchart` syntax, every node ID (like `node1`, `b1`, `c2`) starts with a letter and contains only alphanumeric characters. Labels inside quotes use plain uppercase text — no asterisks, no formatting tags — to ensure clean rendering across all Mermaid-compatible viewers.

### 4.3 Conceptual Sketch of Inclined Element (Text Description)

For a 2D stress element with the X-axis horizontal and Y-axis vertical:
- **Original Element:** Square with $\sigma_x$ arrows pointing outward (tension), $\sigma_y$ arrows pointing outward, and $\tau_{xy}$ arrows showing complementary shear on all four faces.
- **Rotated Element:** Cut by a plane at angle $\theta$ from vertical; the new normal stress $\sigma_\theta$ acts perpendicular to this plane; shear $\tau_\theta$ acts along it.
- **Principal Orientation:** When $\theta = \theta_p$, only $\sigma_1$ (or $\sigma_2$) acts on the plane — no shear component exists.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the terms **principal plane** and **principal stress**. How are they related to each other?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
A **principal plane** is a plane on which the shear stress is zero. The **principal stress** is the normal stress acting on this principal plane. The relationship is that principal stresses are the normal stresses acting on principal planes. At any point in a 2D stress field, there are **two principal planes** perpendicular to each other, carrying the **major principal stress $\sigma_1$** (maximum) and the **minor principal stress $\sigma_2$** (minimum). **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]** What is meant by the **invariance** of principal stresses? State the invariant relationship for a 2D stress state.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Invariance means that the **sum of normal stresses** on any two perpendicular planes through a point remains constant, regardless of the orientation of the coordinate axes. For a 2D stress state, the invariant relationship is:

$$\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$$

This value is independent of the angle of rotation, making it a fundamental property used to verify principal stress calculations. **(3 marks)**

### 5.2 Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> At a point in a structural member, the stresses on a horizontal and vertical plane are measured as: $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa.
>
> **(a)** Determine the principal stresses and the orientation of the principal planes. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Find the maximum shear stress and the normal stress on the plane of maximum shear. Also, determine the orientation of this plane. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given: $\sigma_x = 100$ MPa, $\sigma_y = -50$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 40$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Average normal stress (Center of Mohr's Circle):**

$$C = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{100 + (-50)}{2} = \frac{50}{2} = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Radius of Mohr's Circle:**

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{100 - (-50)}{2}\right)^2 + (40)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(75)^2 + (40)^2} = \sqrt{5625 + 1600} = \sqrt{7225} = 85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = C + R = 25 + 85 = 110 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = C - R = 25 - 85 = -60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Principal Plane Angle:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2(40)}{100 - (-50)} = \frac{80}{150} = 0.5333$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.5333) = 28.07°$$

$$\theta_{p1} = 14.04° \quad \text{(plane for } \sigma_1\text{)}, \quad \theta_{p2} = 104.04° \quad \text{(plane for } \sigma_2\text{)}$$

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1 — Maximum Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{max} = R = 85 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Normal stress on the plane of maximum shear:**

$$\sigma_{on\_max\_shear} = C = 25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Orientation of the plane of maximum shear:**

$$\tan(2\theta_s) = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2\tau_{xy}} = -\frac{100 - (-50)}{2(40)} = -\frac{150}{80} = -1.875$$

$$2\theta_s = \tan^{-1}(-1.875) = -61.93°$$

$$\theta_s = -30.96° \approx -30.96° \text{ (or equivalently } 59.04°\text{)}$$

**Verification:** $\theta_s = \theta_{p1} - 45° = 14.04° - 45° = -30.96°$ ✓

### 5.3 Part B — Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model Question]**

> A point in a steel shaft is subjected to combined bending and torsion. The bending stress is $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa (tensile) and the torsional shear stress is $\tau_{xy} = 50$ MPa. There is no stress in the Y-direction.
>
> **(a)** Compute the magnitude and direction of the principal stresses. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Using **Mohr's circle**, graphically (analytically) determine the normal and shear stresses on a plane inclined at $\theta = 30°$ to the X-axis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

Given: $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 0$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 50$ MPa.

**Step 1 — Center and Radius:**

$$C = \frac{120 + 0}{2} = 60 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (50)^2} = \sqrt{(60)^2 + (50)^2} = \sqrt{3600 + 2500} = \sqrt{6100} \approx 78.10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Principal Stresses:**

$$\sigma_1 = 60 + 78.10 = 138.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 60 - 78.10 = -18.10 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Principal Angle:**

$$\tan(2\theta_p) = \frac{2(50)}{120 - 0} = \frac{100}{120} = 0.8333$$

$$2\theta_p = \tan^{-1}(0.8333) = 39.81°$$

$$\theta_p = 19.90°$$

**Part (b) Solution (Using Stress Transformation Equations):**

**Step 1 — Apply $\theta = 30°$ (so $2\theta = 60°$):**

$$\cos(2\theta) = \cos(60°) = 0.5$$

$$\sin(2\theta) = \sin(60°) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866$$

**Step 2 — Normal Stress on Inclined Plane:**

$$\sigma_{30°} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)$$

$$\sigma_{30°} = 60 + 60(0.5) + 50(0.866) = 60 + 30 + 43.30 = 133.30 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Shear Stress on Inclined Plane:**

$$\tau_{30°} = -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)$$

$$\tau_{30°} = -60(0.866) + 50(0.5) = -51.96 + 25 = -26.96 \text{ MPa}$$

The **negative sign** indicates the shear acts in the opposite direction to the assumed positive orientation.

**Verification (Mohr's Circle Check):** On Mohr's circle, the angle $2\theta = 60°$ measured from the X-face point should land at the coordinates $(\sigma, \tau) = (133.30, -26.96)$. The distance from center: $\sqrt{(133.30-60)^2 + (-26.96)^2} = \sqrt{73.30^2 + 26.96^2} = \sqrt{5372.89 + 726.84} = \sqrt{6099.73} \approx 78.10 = R$ ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Shear Direction:** Forgetting that $\tau_{30°}$ came out **negative**, indicating direction reversal — losing 1 mark.
> 2. **Wrong Angle Convention:** Mixing up $\theta$ vs $2\theta$ in the tangent formula — losing up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the Two Solutions:** Always giving only one principal angle $\theta_p$ instead of $\theta_{p1}$ and $\theta_{p2} = \theta_{p1} + 90°$ — losing 2 marks.
> 4. **Unit Omission:** Writing "110" instead of "110 MPa" in the final answer — losing 0.5 marks for dimensional inconsistency.
> 5. **Invariance Check Skipped:** Not verifying $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ — examiners award **bonus marks** for this verification step.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Board Exams:**

- ✅ **Principal Plane:** Plane where shear stress $\tau_\theta = 0$; only normal stress exists.
- ✅ **Two Principal Stresses:** $\sigma_1$ (algebraically maximum) and $\sigma_2$ (algebraically minimum), acting on mutually perpendicular planes.
- ✅ **Angle Formula:** $\tan(2\theta_p) = \dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}$ — gives **two values** differing by $90°$.
- ✅ **Principal Stress Formula:** $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$.
- ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2}$.
- ✅ **Max Shear Plane Angle:** Always **45° away** from the principal planes.
- ✅ **Normal stress on max-shear plane:** Equals the **average normal stress** $C = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}$ (always the **center** of Mohr's circle).
- ✅ **Invariance Property:** $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ — use this to **verify** your final numerical answer.
- ✅ **Sign Convention:** Tension **+**, Compression **−**, Shear positive when it tends to rotate element **clockwise** (right-hand rule).
- ✅ **3D Extension:** For triaxial stress, three principal stresses $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$ exist, and $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$.
- ✅ **Quick Sanity Check:** If $\tau_{xy} = 0$, then the existing planes are themselves principal planes and $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \sigma_x, \sigma_y$.
- ✅ **Special Case:** If $\sigma_x = \sigma_y$, then $\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \sigma_x \pm \tau_{xy}$, with $\theta_p = 45°$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mohr's Circle for 2D Case
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mohr's Circle for 2D Case

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Mohr's Circle (2D Case)**: A graphical two-dimensional representation of the **stress transformation equations**, originally developed by the German civil engineer **Christian Otto Mohr in 1882**. It provides a geometric visualization of the state of stress at a point under plane stress (or plane strain) conditions, allowing quick determination of normal stresses, shear stresses, principal stresses, and maximum shear stress on any arbitrarily oriented plane passing through that point.

The circle is plotted in a **σ–τ coordinate space** (abscissa = normal stress, ordinate = shear stress), where every point on the circumference represents the normal and shear stress components acting on a particular inclined plane whose orientation is encoded in the angular position on the circle.

### 1.2 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The center of the Mohr's Circle always lies on the **σ-axis** (since the sum of normal stresses on perpendicular planes is invariant and produces zero resultant shear in the principal frame).
- The **radius** of the circle is denoted as **R**, measured in units of stress: **N/m² (Pa)**, with common engineering multiples being **MPa = 10⁶ N/m²** and **GPa = 10⁹ N/m²**.
- All angles on Mohr's circle are measured as **2θ**, meaning the circle rotates at **twice the physical angle** of the actual element.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Spinning Compass"**: Imagine a square rubber stamp pressed against a wall. No matter how you tilt the stamp, it still represents the same point in the material, but the stresses you "see" change. Mohr's Circle is like a **compass that always knows where North is** (the principal direction) — as you rotate the stamp, your reading traces a perfect circle, and the diameter of that circle tells you the maximum and minimum values of stress. The center never moves because the *average* normal stress is invariant, but the diameter reveals how "stretched" the stress state really is.

A second analogy is the **stretched egg**: a single point in a stressed material behaves like a point on the surface of a stretched egg — the principal stresses are the longest and shortest diameters of the egg's cross-section, and Mohr's circle is the *drawing* of that cross-section in stress-space.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention Followed (KTU Standard)**: Tensile normal stresses are **positive**; compressive normal stresses are **negative**. Shear stress is taken as **positive** when it tends to rotate the element **clockwise** and **negative** when it tends to rotate it **counter-clockwise**. Some textbooks adopt the opposite shear convention; the KTU board follows the convention where τ plotted is the shear *on the plane whose outward normal is the x-axis*. In the standard 2D case, point X (representing the x-face) is plotted at coordinates (σₓ, **−τₓᵧ**) so that the circle traces clockwise as θ increases counter-clockwise on the physical element.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations

The state of stress at a point in a 2D continuum is fully described by **three independent stress components**: σₓ, σᵧ, and τₓᵧ. When we consider a different orientation of the element, rotated by an angle θ counter-clockwise from the original x-axis, the stresses on the new x'-face and y'-face are given by the **stress transformation equations**.

#### 2.1.1 The Transformation Equations (Origin of Mohr's Circle)

These are derived by applying static equilibrium to a wedge-shaped element cut from the original element at angle θ:

**Step 1 — Identify the wedge geometry**: An infinitesimally small triangular wedge is cut such that one face has area dA (the inclined face at angle θ) and the other two faces have areas dA·cos(θ) and dA·sin(θ) (perpendicular to x and y).

**Step 2 — Apply force equilibrium in x-direction**: The sum of forces in the x-direction on the wedge must equal zero (neglecting body forces at the point level).

**Step 3 — Apply force equilibrium in y-direction**: The sum of forces in the y-direction must also vanish.

**Step 4 — Solve the two simultaneous linear equations**: This yields the well-known transformation equations.

**Step 5 — Observe the geometric pattern**: The resulting equations reveal a sinusoidal dependence on 2θ, which is the geometric signature of a **circle** when plotted in (σ, τ) space.

#### 2.1.2 The Mohr's Circle Construction Logic

- **Why the circle?** Because eliminating the parameter θ from the two transformation equations gives a single equation of the form (σ − σ_avg)² + τ² = R², which is unmistakably the equation of a circle.
- **Why does the center lie on the σ-axis?** Because the sum σₓ' + σᵧ' = σₓ + σᵧ for all orientations (the first stress invariant), and the average of the two transformed stresses is always the constant (σₓ + σᵧ)/2, with zero shear stress — placing the center exactly on the horizontal axis.
- **Why is the angle doubled?** Because both σₓ' and τₓ'ᵧ' are functions of 2θ, the angular coordinate on the circle must represent 2θ, not θ.

#### 2.1.3 Key Geometric Properties of the Circle

- The **two points where the circle intersects the σ-axis** correspond to the **principal planes** (planes of zero shear), and the abscissas of these points are the **principal stresses σ₁ and σ₂**.
- The **two points on the circle farthest from the σ-axis** (top and bottom) correspond to the **planes of maximum shear stress**.
- **Diametrically opposite points** on the circle represent **complementary planes** (perpendicular to each other in the physical element).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAM GOLD**: The table below contains every formula you need to solve 2D Mohr's Circle problems. Memorize the form, not just the symbols.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Remarks / Units |
|---|----------|---------|-----------------|
| 1 | Average Normal Stress (Center of Circle) | σ_avg = (σₓ + σᵧ) / 2 | Also called C; located on σ-axis. Units: **MPa** |
| 2 | Radius of Mohr's Circle | R = √[ ((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ² ] | Always positive. Units: **MPa** |
| 3 | Principal Stress — Major (σ₁) | σ₁ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 + √[ ((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ² ] | Maximum normal stress. Units: **MPa** |
| 4 | Principal Stress — Minor (σ₂) | σ₂ = (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 − √[ ((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ² ] | Minimum normal stress. Units: **MPa** |
| 5 | Maximum Shear Stress | τ_max = √[ ((σₓ − σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ² ] | Equals the radius R. Units: **MPa** |
| 6 | Principal Angle | tan(2θₚ) = 2τₓᵧ / (σₓ − σᵧ) | Gives 2θₚ on Mohr's circle. Units: **radians** |
| 7 | Maximum Shear Angle | θₛ = θₚ ± 45° | Always 45° from principal plane. |
| 8 | Transformed Normal Stress | σₓ' = (σₓ+σᵧ)/2 + (σₓ−σᵧ)/2·cos(2θ) + τₓᵧ·sin(2θ) | Standard sign convention. |
| 9 | Transformed Shear Stress | τₓ'ᵧ' = −(σₓ−σᵧ)/2·sin(2θ) + τₓᵧ·cos(2θ) | Sign indicates rotation sense. |
| 10 | Invariant — Sum | σₓ + σᵧ = σₓ' + σᵧ' | I₁ — First stress invariant. |
| 11 | Invariant — Product | σₓ·σᵧ − τₓᵧ² = σₓ'·σᵧ' − τₓ'ᵧ'² | I₂ — Second stress invariant. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL — Absolute Value Notation**: In the table, the term √[ ((σₓ−σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ² ] involves the **squared difference** of stresses, NOT a literal absolute value. Do not write the vertical pipe symbol \vert inside markdown tables; use parentheses with the explicit square and a square root, as shown.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mohr's Circle is **indispensable** in several branches of engineering:
- **Civil Engineering**: Design of dams, retaining walls, and foundations where principal stress directions dictate crack propagation and reinforcement placement.
- **Mechanical Engineering**: Shaft design under combined bending and torsion, where the maximum shear stress on the 45° plane controls the failure mode (ductile yielding in shafts).
- **Geotechnical Engineering**: Slope stability analysis, soil failure envelopes (Mohr-Coulomb theory directly extends Mohr's circle concept).
- **Pressure Vessel Design**: Determining the orientation of maximum stress in cylindrical and spherical pressure vessels.
- **Aerospace Engineering**: Stress analysis in thin-walled structures and composite laminates.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Stress Transformation Equations

Consider an infinitesimal element of unit thickness with stresses σₓ, σᵧ, τₓᵧ acting on its faces. Now imagine cutting a wedge from this element such that the inclined face makes an angle θ with the original x-axis. The normal to this inclined face points outward in the direction making angle θ from the x-axis. Let σₓ' be the normal stress and τₓ'ᵧ' be the shear stress on this inclined face.

**Geometry of the Wedge:**
- Inclined face: area = dA (per unit thickness)
- Face perpendicular to x-axis: area = dA·cos(θ)
- Face perpendicular to y-axis: area = dA·sin(θ)

**Equilibrium in the x-direction** (sum of forces = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x'} \cdot dA \cdot \cos(\theta) - \sigma_x \cdot dA \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \cos(\theta) - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \cos(\theta) \\
- \sigma_y \cdot dA \cdot \sin(\theta) \cdot \sin(\theta) - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \cos(\theta) \cdot \sin(\theta) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by dA and simplifying using cos²(θ) = (1+cos(2θ))/2 and sin²(θ) = (1−cos(2θ))/2 and 2·sin(θ)·cos(θ) = sin(2θ):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x'} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Equilibrium in the y-direction** (sum of forces = 0):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{x'y'} \cdot dA - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \cos^2(\theta) + \sigma_x \cdot dA \cdot \cos(\theta)\sin(\theta) - \tau_{xy} \cdot dA \cdot \sin^2(\theta) - \sigma_y \cdot dA \cdot \sin(\theta)\cos(\theta) &= 0
\end{aligned}
$$

After applying the same trigonometric identities and simplifying:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{x'y'} &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

These are the **two stress transformation equations** that govern Mohr's circle.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Circle Equation

We now eliminate the angle 2θ from the two transformation equations to reveal the circle.

**Rearrange** σₓ' to isolate the cosine and sine terms:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x'} - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} &= \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Square** both equations to use the identity sin²(2θ) + cos²(2θ) = 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sigma_{x'} - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 &= \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 \cos^2(2\theta) + 2\cdot\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}^2\sin^2(2\theta) \\
(\tau_{x'y'})^2 &= \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 \sin^2(2\theta) - 2\cdot\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cdot\tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta)\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}^2\cos^2(2\theta)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Add** the two squared equations — the cross-product terms cancel, and cos²(2θ) + sin²(2θ) = 1:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sigma_{x'} - \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + (\tau_{x'y'})^2 &= \left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

This is unmistakably the **equation of a circle** in the (σ, τ) plane:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\sigma - \sigma_{\text{avg}}\right)^2 + \tau^2 &= R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

where the **center** is at C = (σ_avg, 0) and the **radius** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of Principal Stresses

The principal stresses occur where the circle intersects the σ-axis, i.e., where τₓ'ᵧ' = 0. Setting the shear transformation equation to zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
-\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta_p) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta_p) &= 0 \\
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the **principal angle** θₚ. To find the principal stresses, substitute this back into the normal stress equation. A faster way is to recognize that the principal stresses are simply **center ± radius**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1, \sigma_2 &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation of Maximum Shear Stress

The maximum shear stress occurs at the **topmost or bottommost point** of the Mohr's circle, where the τ-coordinate equals the radius. Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

The angle at which this occurs satisfies cos(2θₛ) = 0, i.e., 2θₛ = 90° or θₛ = 45°, **measured from the principal direction**. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s &= \theta_p \pm 45°
\end{aligned}
$$

On these planes of maximum shear, the **normal stress is the average stress** (σₓ + σᵧ)/2 — a frequently tested point in KTU examinations.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Step-by-Step Construction Procedure (Block Flowchart)

The following Mermaid flowchart captures the exact algorithmic procedure for constructing Mohr's circle from given stress components — the sequence examiners expect you to follow in graphical solution attempts.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Read given stresses sigmaX, sigmaY, tauXY from problem] --> B[Step 2: Set up Cartesian axes with sigma horizontal and tau vertical]
    B --> C[Step 3: Establish a sign scale e.g. 1 cm = 10 MPa]
    C --> D[Step 4: Plot Point X at coordinates sigmaX and minus tauXY]
    D --> E[Step 5: Plot Point Y at coordinates sigmaY and plus tauXY]
    E --> F[Step 6: Join X and Y with a straight line this is a diameter of Mohr circle]
    F --> G[Step 7: Locate the center C at midpoint of XY on the sigma axis]
    G --> H[Step 8: Draw circle with center C and radius CX equal to CY]
    H --> I[Step 9: Identify the two intersections of circle with sigma axis as sigma1 and sigma2]
    I --> J[Step 10: The topmost and bottommost points give tau max and corresponding normal stress]
    J --> K[Step 11: Measure angle 2 theta from X to a chosen point to obtain physical angle theta]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Mohr's Circle

This block diagram maps the **inputs, processing functions, and outputs** of Mohr's circle as an engineering computational tool. It is useful for problem-solving strategy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INPUT[Input Block: sigmaX, sigmaY, tauXY in MPa] --> PROC1[Process A: Compute center C equals sigmaX plus sigmaY divided by 2]
    INPUT --> PROC2[Process B: Compute radius R from half difference squared plus tauXY squared then square root]
    PROC1 --> GEOM[Geometric Block: Draw sigma tau axes and plot circle]
    PROC2 --> GEOM
    GEOM --> OUT1[Output 1: Principal stresses sigma1 and sigma2 from circle sigma axis intercepts]
    GEOM --> OUT2[Output 2: Maximum shear stress tau max equals R from circle vertical extrema]
    GEOM --> OUT3[Output 3: Principal angle thetaP from arc measurement divided by 2]
    GEOM --> OUT4[Output 4: Transformed stresses on any inclined plane at angle theta]
```

### 4.3 Reference Schematic — A Skeleton Mohr's Circle

The following is a textual schematic showing the relative position of all the key points on Mohr's circle when σₓ is greater than σᵧ and τₓᵧ is positive.

```
            τ (shear stress)
                  ↑
                  |
        τ_max ●───┼───┐                    ← Topmost point: max shear
                  |   |\
                  |   | \
                  |   |  \
       Point Y ●──┼───┼───● Point X        ← Y at (σᵧ, +τₓᵧ), X at (σₓ, −τₓᵧ)
                  |   |  /                  ← Line XY is a diameter
                  |   | /
                  |   |/
       -τ_max ●───┼───┘                    ← Bottommost point: max negative shear
                  |
        ─────────●────────────────→ σ       ← Center C on σ-axis
                  C   σ₂   σ_avg    σ₁
                  
   Center C at (σ_avg, 0); σ₁ = C + R, σ₂ = C − R
   All angular measurements along the circle are 2θ, NOT θ.
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Visual Cue**: On Mohr's circle, the diameter XY always lies **across** the circle. Point X and point Y are on **opposite ends** of the diameter (this is because they represent perpendicular planes in the physical element). The angle from point X to any other point P on the circle, measured **at the center C**, equals **2θ**, where θ is the physical counter-clockwise rotation from the original x-face to the new x'-face.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1. Define Mohr's circle. What does each point on the circle represent?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Mohr's circle is a graphical representation of the stress transformation equations, plotted in a σ–τ coordinate system, used to determine stresses on inclined planes at a point. **(1 mark)**
The **center** of the circle lies on the σ-axis at the average normal stress (σₓ + σᵧ)/2, and the **radius** equals the maximum shear stress. **(1 mark)**
**Each point on the circumference** of Mohr's circle represents the normal stress (abscissa) and shear stress (ordinate) acting on a specific inclined plane in the physical element, with the angular position (measured from a reference point on the circle) equal to **2θ**, where θ is the physical rotation angle. **(1 mark)**

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, Module 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2. State the significance of the principal stresses and the maximum shear stress obtained from Mohr's circle.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
**Principal stresses (σ₁, σ₂)** are the **maximum and minimum normal stresses** that occur on two mutually perpendicular planes (called **principal planes**) on which the **shear stress is zero**. They are the abscissas of the points where Mohr's circle intersects the σ-axis. **(1.5 marks)**
**Maximum shear stress (τ_max)** is the largest shear stress magnitude at the point, equal to the **radius of Mohr's circle**, and occurs on planes oriented at **45° to the principal planes**. On these planes, the normal stress equals the average stress (σₓ + σᵧ)/2. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** At a point in a structural member, the stresses on a rectangular element are: σₓ = **80 MPa (tensile)**, σᵧ = **40 MPa (compressive)**, and τₓᵧ = **30 MPa**. Determine the **principal stresses** and the **orientation of the principal planes** at this point. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** For the same stress state, determine the **maximum shear stress** and the **normal stress on the plane of maximum shear**. Also find the **orientation of the plane of maximum shear**. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Principal Stresses and Principal Plane Orientation:**

**Step 1**: Write down given data.
σₓ = +80 MPa, σᵧ = −40 MPa, τₓᵧ = +30 MPa.

**Step 2**: Compute the average normal stress (center of Mohr's circle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + (-40)}{2} = \frac{40}{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Compute the radius of Mohr's circle (which equals τ_max in magnitude).

$$
\begin{aligned}
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - (-40)}{2}\right)^2 + (30)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{(60)^2 + (30)^2} \\
  &= \sqrt{3600 + 900} = \sqrt{4500} = 30\sqrt{5} \text{ MPa} \\
  &\approx 67.08 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Compute principal stresses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= \sigma_{\text{avg}} + R = 20 + 67.08 = 87.08 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(tensile)} \\
\sigma_2 &= \sigma_{\text{avg}} - R = 20 - 67.08 = -47.08 \text{ MPa} \quad \text{(compressive)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5**: Compute principal angle.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tan(2\theta_p) &= \frac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y} = \frac{2 \times 30}{80 - (-40)} = \frac{60}{120} = 0.5 \\
2\theta_p &= \tan^{-1}(0.5) = 26.57° \\
\theta_p &= 13.28° \quad \text{(counter-clockwise from x-axis)}
\end{aligned}
$$

There is a second principal plane at θ_p + 90° = 103.28°.

**Part (b) — Maximum Shear Stress and Plane Orientation:**

**Step 1**: Maximum shear stress is the radius.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= R = 67.08 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Normal stress on the plane of maximum shear is the average normal stress (center of the circle).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{on max shear plane}} &= \sigma_{\text{avg}} = 20 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Orientation of the plane of maximum shear.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\theta_s &= \theta_p \pm 45° = 13.28° + 45° = 58.28° \quad \text{(or } -31.72°\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Verification using the transformation equations at θₛ = 58.28°:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x'} &= \frac{80-40}{2} + \frac{80-(-40)}{2}\cos(2 \times 58.28°) + 30\sin(2 \times 58.28°) \\
            &= 20 + 60\cos(116.56°) + 30\sin(116.56°) \\
            &= 20 + 60(-0.4472) + 30(0.8944) \\
            &= 20 - 26.83 + 26.83 = 20 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{x'y'} &= -\frac{80-(-40)}{2}\sin(116.56°) + 30\cos(116.56°) \\
            &= -60(0.8944) + 30(-0.4472) \\
            &= -53.66 - 13.42 = -67.08 \text{ MPa} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Results Summary**:
- σ₁ = **87.08 MPa**, σ₂ = **−47.08 MPa**
- θ_p = **13.28° CCW** from x-axis
- τ_max = **67.08 MPa** on planes at **58.28°** and **−31.72°** from x-axis
- Normal stress on max shear planes = **20 MPa** (the average stress)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2, 14 Marks]**
> **(a)** The state of plane stress at a point is given by σₓ = **60 MPa**, σᵧ = **20 MPa**, τₓᵧ = **25 MPa**. **(i)** Construct Mohr's circle graphically (describe coordinates of center, radius, key points). **(ii)** Determine the **stresses on a plane whose normal makes 30° with the x-axis**. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A point in a machine component is subjected to biaxial tension with σₓ = **120 MPa**, σᵧ = **40 MPa** and a shear stress τₓᵧ = **50 MPa**. Determine the **principal stresses**, the **maximum shear stress**, and verify your results by computing the **second stress invariant** I₂. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Construction Parameters of Mohr's Circle:**

Center C = (σ_avg, 0) = ((60+20)/2, 0) = (40, 0) MPa.

Radius R = √[((60−20)/2)² + (25)²] = √[(20)² + (25)²] = √(400 + 625) = √1025 ≈ 32.02 MPa.

Point X: (σₓ, −τₓᵧ) = (60, −25) MPa.
Point Y: (σᵧ, +τₓᵧ) = (20, +25) MPa.
Check: midpoint of X and Y = ((60+20)/2, (−25+25)/2) = (40, 0) ✓ — matches the center C.
Distance CX = √[(60−40)² + (−25−0)²] = √(400 + 625) = √1025 ≈ 32.02 ✓ — matches R.

**Part (a)(ii) — Stresses on Plane at θ = 30°:**

Using the transformation equations directly:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{x'} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} + \frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\cos(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\sin(2\theta) \\
            &= \frac{60+20}{2} + \frac{60-20}{2}\cos(60°) + 25\sin(60°) \\
            &= 40 + 20(0.5) + 25(0.8660) \\
            &= 40 + 10 + 21.65 = 71.65 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{x'y'} &= -\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\sin(2\theta) + \tau_{xy}\cos(2\theta) \\
            &= -20\sin(60°) + 25\cos(60°) \\
            &= -20(0.8660) + 25(0.5) \\
            &= -17.32 + 12.50 = -4.82 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result**: σₓ' = **71.65 MPa** (tensile), τₓ'ᵧ' = **−4.82 MPa** (sense of rotation: counter-clockwise on the x'-face).

**Verification using Mohr's circle geometry**: On the circle, starting from point X (60, −25), rotate by 2θ = 60° counter-clockwise about center C(40, 0). Using the parametric form σ = C + R·cos(φ), τ = R·sin(φ) where φ is the angle from the positive σ-direction measured at C, the angle for point X is φ_X = atan2(−25, 60−40) = atan2(−25, 20) = −51.34°. Adding 2θ = 60° gives φ_new = 8.66°. Then σ = 40 + 32.02·cos(8.66°) = 40 + 32.02(0.9886) = 71.65 MPa ✓, and τ = 32.02·sin(8.66°) = 32.02(0.1506) = 4.82 MPa. The sign is positive here because Mohr's circle plotting convention is mirrored from physical, so we report τₓ'ᵧ' = −4.82 MPa per the standard sign convention.

**Part (b) — Principal Stresses, Max Shear, and Invariant Verification:**

Given: σₓ = 120 MPa, σᵧ = 40 MPa, τₓᵧ = 50 MPa.

**Step 1**: Center and radius.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{avg}} &= \frac{120 + 40}{2} = 80 \text{ MPa} \\
R &= \sqrt{\left(\frac{120 - 40}{2}\right)^2 + (50)^2} = \sqrt{(40)^2 + 2500} = \sqrt{1600 + 2500} = \sqrt{4100} \approx 64.03 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2**: Principal stresses.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_1 &= 80 + 64.03 = 144.03 \text{ MPa} \\
\sigma_2 &= 80 - 64.03 = 15.97 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3**: Maximum shear stress.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{\max} &= R = 64.03 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4**: Verification using the second stress invariant.

The second stress invariant in 2D is I₂ = σₓ·σᵧ − τₓᵧ². It must equal σ₁·σ₂ (this is an identity derivable from the product of the principal stress formula).

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_2^{\text{original}} &= \sigma_x \cdot \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2 = (120)(40) - (50)^2 = 4800 - 2500 = 2300 \\
I_2^{\text{principal}} &= \sigma_1 \cdot \sigma_2 = (144.03)(15.97) = 2299.96 \approx 2300 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The invariant is preserved (rounding accounts for the 0.04 difference). **This confirms the calculation is correct.**

**Final Results Summary**:
- σ₁ = **144.03 MPa**, σ₂ = **15.97 MPa**
- τ_max = **64.03 MPa**
- I₂ = **2300 MPa²** (verified by both methods)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Shear Stress Plotting (loses 2-3 marks)**: Forgetting to plot Point X at (σₓ, **−τₓᵧ**) and Point Y at (σᵧ, **+τₓᵧ**) is the **single most common mistake**. The Mohr's circle sign convention is mirrored relative to the physical element to make the rotation direction consistent. If you plot X at (σₓ, +τₓᵧ), your angle 2θ will be **reversed**, leading to a wrong principal angle.
> 2. **Angle Doubling Confusion (loses 1-2 marks)**: Many students report the **angle measured on the circle** as θ instead of dividing by 2. Always remember: **angle on circle = 2θ_physical**.
> 3. **Forgetting the "+" before the square root in σ₁ (loses 1 mark)**: σ₁ is the **larger** principal stress; using the minus sign gives σ₂. Examiners explicitly check this ordering.
> 4. **Confusing τ_max formula with σ₁ formula (loses 2 marks)**: τ_max is the **radius of the circle alone**, NOT center + radius. Writing τ_max = (σₓ+σᵧ)/2 + R loses full marks for that part.
> 5. **Not specifying the orientation of both principal planes (loses 1 mark)**: There are **two** principal planes, separated by 90°. Always give both: θ_p and θ_p + 90°.
> 6. **Not stating units (loses 0.5 mark per quantity)**: Every numerical answer must carry the **MPa** (or kN/m²) unit. Even symbolic final answers lose partial credit if units are omitted.
> 7. **Not verifying with the invariant (loses 0.5-1 mark)**: In the rare question that allows it, the second stress invariant I₂ = σₓ·σᵧ − τₓᵧ² = σ₁·σ₂ is a **free check** — use it.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision Bullet List — Mohr's Circle (2D):**

- **Definition**: A circle in the σ–τ plane representing all possible (σ, τ) pairs on planes through a point.
- **Center on σ-axis**: Always at C = ((σₓ+σᵧ)/2, 0), reflecting the first stress invariant.
- **Radius formula**: R = √[((σₓ−σᵧ)/2)² + τₓᵧ²] — this is the **maximum shear stress** and is always **non-negative**.
- **Principal stresses**: σ₁,₂ = (σₓ+σᵧ)/2 ± R, located where circle crosses the σ-axis. **Both must be computed** and ordered (σ₁ > σ₂).
- **Principal angle**: tan(2θₚ) = 2τₓᵧ / (σₓ − σᵧ). **Second solution at θₚ + 90°** is the second principal plane.
- **Max shear planes**: Always at **θₚ ± 45°** from the principal direction.
- **Normal stress on max shear plane**: Always equals (σₓ+σᵧ)/2, the **center coordinate**.
- **Angle doubling**: Angles on Mohr's circle are **2θ** where θ is the physical rotation.
- **Point X plotting convention**: X = (σₓ, **−τₓᵧ**); Point Y = (σᵧ, **+τₓᵧ**). The midpoint of XY is the center C.
- **Complementary planes**: Diametrically opposite points on the circle represent planes **perpendicular** in the physical element.
- **Invariants (must be constant)**: I₁ = σₓ+σᵧ and I₂ = σₓ·σᵧ − τₓᵧ². These are excellent **verification tools**.
- **Sign convention**: Tension positive, compression negative; shear positive when tending to rotate element **clockwise** (depending on textbook).
- **Engineering use**: Shafts under combined loading, pressure vessels, dams, slope stability, fracture analysis.
- **Unit check**: Every stress answer must carry **MPa** (or consistent equivalent) unit.
- **Drawing scale**: When solving graphically, state the chosen scale clearly (e.g., 1 cm = 10 MPa) to gain full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Circumferential and Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Vessel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Circumferential and Longitudinal Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Vessel

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **thin cylindrical vessel** is a closed, hollow cylindrical structure whose wall thickness ($t$) is small compared to its mean radius ($r$) or internal diameter ($d$). The KTU 2024 scheme formally recognizes a vessel as *thin* when:

$$\frac{t}{d} \le \frac{1}{20} \quad \text{(some texts use } \frac{1}{10} \text{ as the upper bound)}$$

When a thin cylinder is subjected to an **internal fluid pressure** ($p$), two principal tensile stresses develop on the wall of the cylinder:

1. **Circumferential Stress (Hoop Stress), $\sigma_h$** — acts along the circumference of the cylinder (tangential to the cross-section).
2. **Longitudinal Stress (Axial Stress), $\sigma_l$** — acts along the axis (length) of the cylinder.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** *Hoop stress is the stress induced in a pressure vessel wall in the circumferential direction due to internal pressure, while longitudinal stress is induced along the length of the vessel due to the same internal pressure acting on the projected end area.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a balloon (a very thin spherical/cylindrical pressure vessel).

- **Circumferential Stress Analogy:** Take a slice of the balloon like a ring. When you blow air inside, the ring wants to expand outward, and the material along the ring is pulled apart. This *pulling apart along the ring* is **hoop stress**.
- **Longitudinal Stress Analogy:** Now, if you imagine the balloon as a row of stacked rings, the internal pressure pushes the top and bottom ends apart. The material along the length resists this pull — that is **longitudinal stress**.
- **Numerical Insight:** Hoop stress is always **twice** the longitudinal stress in a thin cylinder. This means the cylinder is most likely to split open *lengthwise* (along a generator), not across its ends.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is hoop stress double the longitudinal stress?**
> Because the internal pressure tries to burst the cylinder in *two* perpendicular directions simultaneously, but the cross-sectional area resisting the circumferential pull is *half* of the area resisting the longitudinal pull. This will be proven mathematically in Section 3.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Assumptions (For KTU Board Answers)

- **Internal Pressure ($p$):** Measured in **N/mm² (MPa)** or **bar** (1 bar ≈ 0.1 N/mm²).
- **Diameter ($d$):** Mean diameter (or internal diameter, as the wall is thin).
- **Wall thickness ($t$):** In mm.
- **Stress ($\sigma$):** In **N/mm² (MPa)**.
- **Assumptions (Must be written in every KTU answer):**
  1. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
  2. The thickness $t$ is uniform throughout.
  3. The plane stress condition is assumed (neglecting radial stress $\sigma_r \approx 0$).
  4. End caps contribute only to longitudinal stress, not hoop stress.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Conceptual Breakdown

To understand how these stresses arise, we slice the cylinder in two perpendicular ways:

### A. Logic Behind Circumferential (Hoop) Stress
- **Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder with a transverse plane (perpendicular to its axis), exposing a ring of diameter $d$ and thickness $t$.
- **Step 2:** The internal pressure $p$ pushes outward on the *projected area* of this ring.
- **Step 3:** The projected area resisting this force is the rectangle $d \times L$ (diameter × length).
- **Step 4:** The ring resists bursting via **two** cross-sectional walls, each of area $t \times L$.
- **Step 5:** Equating the bursting force to the resisting force gives the hoop stress.

### B. Logic Behind Longitudinal (Axial) Stress
- **Step 1:** Imagine cutting the cylinder with a longitudinal plane (along its axis), exposing the cylindrical cross-section.
- **Step 2:** The internal pressure acts on the end cap (circular area $\frac{\pi}{4}d^2$).
- **Step 3:** This end force tries to pull the cylinder apart lengthwise.
- **Step 4:** The resisting area is the cylindrical wall cross-section: $\pi d t$.
- **Step 5:** Equating the end force to the resisting force gives the longitudinal stress.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the ratio is 2:1?**
> Hoop stress resisting area = $2 \times (t \times L)$ — *two walls*.
> Longitudinal stress resisting area = $\pi \times d \times t$ — *one cylindrical wall perimeter*.
> Since the bursting force on a transverse slice is $p \times d \times L$ and on an end cap is $p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$, the ratio simplifies exactly to 2:1.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{2 t}$ | Circumferential tensile stress | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p \cdot d}{4 t}$ | Axial tensile stress | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Ratio | $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ | Hoop is always double longitudinal | Dimensionless |
| Bursting Force (Circumferential) | $F_h = p \cdot d \cdot L$ | Force trying to split cylinder lengthwise | N |
| End Force (Longitudinal) | $F_l = p \cdot \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Force pushing end cap off | N |
| Thin Wall Criterion | $t \le \dfrac{d}{20}$ | Validity of thin-wall formulas | mm |
| Maximum Shear Stress (2D) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{4} = \dfrac{p d}{8 t}$ | Mohr's circle radius | N/mm² (MPa) |
| Change in Diameter | $\Delta d = \dfrac{p d^2}{2 t E} \left( 1 - \dfrac{\mu}{2} \right)$ | Using Hooke's law in 2D | mm |
| Change in Length | $\Delta L = \dfrac{p d L}{4 t E}$ | Axial deformation | mm |
| Change in Volume | $\Delta V = \dfrac{p d L}{4 t E} (5 - 4\mu) \cdot A$ | Volumetric strain formulation | mm³ |
| Spherical Vessel Hoop Stress | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | Stress is uniform in all directions | N/mm² (MPa) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Whenever asked for "stresses in a thin cylindrical shell," you MUST state both $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$ and clearly show that $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$. This is a 2-mark short-answer favorite.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Cookers, LPG Cylinders, Boilers:** The cylindrical body is designed with the hoop stress as the governing factor (since it's twice the longitudinal stress).
- **Pipelines and Penstocks:** Hydroelectric and oil pipelines use hoop stress for wall thickness design.
- **Aircraft Fuselages and Rocket Casings:** Operating at high internal pressures, the longitudinal seams must withstand half the hoop stress.
- **Steam and Gas Storage Tanks:** Use these formulas daily for code-compliant design (ASME, IS 2825).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Hoop (Circumferential) Stress $\sigma_h$

**Setup:** Consider a thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter $d$, thickness $t$, and length $L$, subjected to internal pressure $p$.

**Step 1:** Imagine a section of the cylinder cut by a **transverse plane** (i.e., a plane perpendicular to the axis $x$). The cut exposes a circular ring of diameter $d$ and thickness $t$.

**Step 2:** The internal pressure $p$ acts radially outward on the inner surface of this ring. The total bursting force (tending to split the cylinder into two halves) is the pressure multiplied by the *projected area* on the cutting plane.

$$F_{bursting} = p \times A_{projected} = p \times (d \times L)$$

*Explanation:* The projected area is a rectangle of length $L$ and width $d$ (the diameter), because we are looking at the force along the axial direction pushing the two halves apart.

**Step 3:** This bursting force is resisted by the **tensile stress $\sigma_h$** acting uniformly along the two cut edges of the wall.

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma_h \times (2 \times t \times L)$$

*Explanation:* The wall has *two* cut edges (one on each half of the cylinder), each of cross-sectional area $t \times L$. Therefore, total resisting area is $2tL$.

**Step 4:** Apply **equilibrium** (the cylinder is in static equilibrium, so bursting force = resisting force).

$$p \times d \times L = \sigma_h \times 2 \times t \times L$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $L$ from both sides (a key simplifying step for thin shells).

$$p \times d = 2 \sigma_h \times t$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $\sigma_h$.

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation uses the projected area method, which is the KTU board's preferred approach because it directly uses force balance without needing integration.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Longitudinal (Axial) Stress $\sigma_l$

**Step 1:** Consider the same cylinder. Now, imagine a **longitudinal section** (a plane passing through the axis, splitting the cylinder into two halves lengthwise).

**Step 2:** The internal pressure $p$ acts on the **end cap** of the cylinder, pushing it off along the axial direction. The area of the end cap is a circle of diameter $d$:

$$A_{end} = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 3:** The total **axial (end) force** due to internal pressure is:

$$F_{axial} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 4:** This force tends to separate the two ends of the cylinder. The resisting area is the **entire cylindrical wall cross-section** (the area you see when looking at a longitudinal cut).

$$A_{resisting} = \pi d t$$

*Explanation:* This is the perimeter of the circle ($\pi d$) multiplied by the wall thickness ($t$).

**Step 5:** The longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ acts uniformly over this resisting area. The resisting force is:

$$F_{resisting} = \sigma_l \times \pi d t$$

**Step 6:** Apply equilibrium:

$$p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_l \times \pi d t$$

**Step 7:** Cancel $\pi d$ from both sides:

$$p \times \frac{d}{4} = \sigma_l \times t$$

**Step 8:** Solve for $\sigma_l$.

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 t}$$

**Step 9:** Compare the two stresses. Dividing $\sigma_h$ by $\sigma_l$:

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p d / (2t)}{p d / (4t)} = \frac{4t}{2t} = 2$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l}$$

---

## 3.3 Bonus: Stress in a Thin Spherical Vessel

A thin spherical vessel of diameter $d$ and thickness $t$ subjected to internal pressure $p$ develops a **uniform tensile stress** in all directions on its surface (no distinction between hoop and longitudinal because of symmetry).

**Step 1:** Cut the sphere into two halves. The internal pressure acts on the projected circular area $\frac{\pi d^2}{4}$.

$$F_{bursting} = p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$$

**Step 2:** The resisting area is the **circular wall cross-section** at the cut, which is a ring of circumference $\pi d$ and thickness $t$:

$$A_{resisting} = \pi d t$$

**Step 3:** Equilibrium:

$$p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_{sp} \times \pi d t$$

**Step 4:** Solve for the spherical stress:

$$\sigma_{sp} = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Comparison:** A thin spherical vessel experiences **half the stress** of a thin cylindrical vessel for the same diameter, pressure, and thickness. This is why spherical pressure tanks (e.g., LPG bullet tanks with hemispherical ends) are more efficient than purely cylindrical ones.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of **300 mm** and a wall thickness of **6 mm**. It carries an internal fluid pressure of **2 N/mm²**. Find:
(i) Hoop stress
(ii) Longitudinal stress
(iii) Maximum shear stress on the surface

**Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 300$ mm, $t = 6$ mm, $p = 2$ N/mm²

**(i) Hoop Stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p \cdot d}{2 t} = \frac{2 \times 300}{2 \times 6} = \frac{600}{12} = 50 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Longitudinal Stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p \cdot d}{4 t} = \frac{2 \times 300}{4 \times 6} = \frac{600}{24} = 25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Verification:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l \Rightarrow 50 = 2 \times 25$ ✓

**(iii) Maximum Shear Stress (2D Mohr's Circle):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_h - \sigma_l}{2} = \frac{50 - 25}{2} = 12.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free Body Diagram of a Thin Cylindrical Vessel (Hoop Stress)

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the transverse cut used to derive hoop stress. The internal pressure pushes the two halves apart, and the wall material resists along two edges.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Internal Pressure p"] --> B["Projected Area = d x L"]
    B --> C["Bursting Force = p d L"]
    C --> D["Resisting Force = sigma_h x 2tL"]
    D --> E["Two Cut Edges of the Wall"]
    E --> F["Equilibrium: p d L = 2 sigma_h t L"]
    F --> G["Final Formula: sigma_h = p d / 2t"]
    H["Longitudinal Section"] --> I["End Cap Area = pi d2 / 4"]
    I --> J["Axial Force = p pi d2 / 4"]
    J --> K["Resisting Area = pi d t"]
    K --> L["Equilibrium: p pi d2 / 4 = sigma_l pi d t"]
    L --> M["Final Formula: sigma_l = p d / 4t"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Stress Interaction in a Thin Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT["Input Parameters"]
        P["Internal Pressure p"]
        D["Diameter d"]
        T["Wall Thickness t"]
        L["Length L"]
    end
    subgraph GEOMETRY["Geometric Projections"]
        A1["Transverse Projection: d x L"]
        A2["End Cap Projection: pi d2 / 4"]
    end
    subgraph STRESSES["Principal Stresses"]
        S1["Hoop Stress sigma_h = p d / 2t"]
        S2["Longitudinal Stress sigma_l = p d / 4t"]
        S3["Max Shear tau_max = p d / 8t"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT["Design Check"]
        O1["sigma_h = 2 sigma_l"]
        O2["Check against allowable stress"]
    end
    P --> A1
    P --> A2
    D --> A1
    D --> A2
    L --> A1
    T --> S1
    T --> S2
    A1 --> S1
    A2 --> S2
    S1 --> S3
    S2 --> S3
    S1 --> O1
    S2 --> O1
    S1 --> O2
    S2 --> O2
```

## 4.3 Mohr's Circle Representation (2D Stress State)

The plane of the cylinder wall has two principal stresses: $\sigma_h$ (along the circumference) and $\sigma_l$ (along the length). The third principal stress in the radial direction is taken as zero for thin shells.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X["sigma_l along axis"] --> Y["Mohr Circle Center = sigma_l + sigma_h / 2"]
    X1["sigma_h along circumference"] --> Y
    Y --> Z["Radius = sigma_h minus sigma_l / 2"]
    Z --> W["tau_max on 45 degree plane"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Mermaid diagrams:** The `flowchart LR` syntax forces left-to-right rendering. Node labels are kept as plain uppercase alphanumeric text (e.g., "sigma_h = p d / 2t") to avoid any markdown parsing issues inside the diagram labels.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Define hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical shell. Show that hoop stress is twice the longitudinal stress.**

**Model Answer:**

Hoop stress $\sigma_h$ is the circumferential tensile stress developed in the wall of a thin cylindrical vessel due to internal pressure. Longitudinal stress $\sigma_l$ is the axial tensile stress developed along the length of the same vessel.

The two stresses are given by:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} \quad \text{and} \quad \sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$

Taking the ratio:

$$\frac{\sigma_h}{\sigma_l} = \frac{p d / 2t}{p d / 4t} = 2$$

Therefore, $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$, proving the statement. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**State the assumptions made in the analysis of stresses in a thin cylindrical shell.**

**Model Answer:**

The key assumptions are: **(3 marks)**

1. The material of the shell is **homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's Law**.
2. The wall thickness $t$ is **uniform** throughout the vessel.
3. The ratio $t/d \le 1/20$, justifying the **thin-wall approximation**.
4. The radial stress $\sigma_r$ is **negligible** compared to $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$ (plane stress condition).
5. The end caps are **flat and contribute only to longitudinal stress**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** Derive expressions for the circumferential and longitudinal stresses in a thin cylindrical shell subjected to an internal fluid pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin cylindrical shell of internal diameter **400 mm** and wall thickness **8 mm** is filled with a fluid at a pressure of **3 N/mm²**. Calculate:
   (i) The hoop and longitudinal stresses.
   (ii) The change in diameter, assuming $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and Poisson's ratio $\mu = 0.3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**

*Hoop Stress:* Consider a transverse section. The bursting force is $p \times d \times L$. The resisting force is $\sigma_h \times 2 t L$. Equating:

$$p d L = 2 \sigma_h t L \Rightarrow \sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t}$$

*Longitudinal Stress:* Consider a longitudinal section. The end force is $p \times \frac{\pi d^2}{4}$. The resisting area is $\pi d t$. Equating:

$$p \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \sigma_l \pi d t \Rightarrow \sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $d = 400$ mm, $t = 8$ mm, $p = 3$ N/mm², $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $\mu = 0.3$

**(i) Stresses:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} = \frac{3 \times 400}{2 \times 8} = \frac{1200}{16} = 75 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t} = \frac{3 \times 400}{4 \times 8} = \frac{1200}{32} = 37.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**(ii) Change in Diameter:**

The diametral strain is:

$$\varepsilon_d = \frac{\sigma_h}{E} - \mu \frac{\sigma_l}{E} = \frac{1}{E} \left( \sigma_h - \mu \sigma_l \right)$$

$$\varepsilon_d = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^5} \left( 75 - 0.3 \times 37.5 \right) = \frac{1}{2 \times 10^5} \left( 75 - 11.25 \right) = \frac{63.75}{2 \times 10^5}$$

$$\varepsilon_d = 3.1875 \times 10^{-4}$$

Change in diameter:

$$\Delta d = \varepsilon_d \times d = 3.1875 \times 10^{-4} \times 400 = 0.1275 \text{ mm}$$

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel of internal diameter **500 mm** and wall thickness **10 mm** is subjected to an internal pressure of **2.5 N/mm²**. Find the hoop and longitudinal stresses. Also, find the change in length if $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and the original length is **2 m**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the stresses in a thin cylindrical and a thin spherical vessel of the same diameter, thickness, and internal pressure. Why is a spherical vessel more efficient? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $d = 500$ mm, $t = 10$ mm, $p = 2.5$ N/mm², $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $L = 2000$ mm

**Hoop Stress:**

$$\sigma_h = \frac{p d}{2 t} = \frac{2.5 \times 500}{2 \times 10} = 62.5 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Longitudinal Stress:**

$$\sigma_l = \frac{p d}{4 t} = \frac{2.5 \times 500}{4 \times 10} = 31.25 \text{ N/mm}^2$$

**Change in Length:**

$$\Delta L = \frac{\sigma_l \cdot L}{E} = \frac{31.25 \times 2000}{2 \times 10^5} = \frac{62500}{200000} = 0.3125 \text{ mm}$$

**(b) Comparison:**

| **Parameter** | **Thin Cylinder** | **Thin Sphere** |
|---|---|---|
| Hoop Stress | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ | $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ |
| Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ | (Equal in all directions) |
| Maximum Stress | $p d / 2t$ | $p d / 4t$ |

A spherical vessel carries **half the maximum stress** of a cylindrical vessel of the same dimensions. Hence, for the same allowable stress, a spherical vessel can be made with **half the wall thickness**, making it more material-efficient and economical. Additionally, spherical shapes have no preferred weak axis (no hoop vs. longitudinal distinction), distributing stress uniformly. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 2:1 ratio statement** — Always explicitly write $\sigma_h = 2\sigma_l$ after computing both. Lose 1 mark if omitted.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency** — If $d$ is in mm and $p$ in N/mm², then $t$ must also be in mm. Do NOT mix cm and mm.
> 3. **Missing Assumptions** — KTU evaluators deduct 1 mark if the thin-wall assumptions (Section 1.3) are not stated in derivation questions.
> 4. **Confusing Diametral Strain with Hoop Strain** — The change in *diameter* uses BOTH $\sigma_h$ and $\sigma_l$ (with Poisson effect), while the hoop strain uses only $\sigma_h$ and the axial Poisson contraction.
> 5. **Sign Convention** — Hoop and longitudinal stresses are **tensile** (positive) for internal pressure. Radial stress is compressive but **neglected** in thin-shell analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ A **thin cylindrical shell** has $t/d \le 1/20$; radial stress is neglected.
- ✅ **Hoop stress:** $\sigma_h = \dfrac{p d}{2 t}$ — the dominant stress, governs wall thickness design.
- ✅ **Longitudinal stress:** $\sigma_l = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ — half of hoop stress.
- ✅ **Crucial Relation:** $\sigma_h = 2 \sigma_l$ — *must* be stated in every answer.
- ✅ A thin **spherical** vessel has uniform stress: $\sigma_{sp} = \dfrac{p d}{4 t}$ — half the maximum stress of a cylinder.
- ✅ **Maximum shear stress** on the wall surface: $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{p d}{8 t}$.
- ✅ **Change in diameter:** $\Delta d = \dfrac{p d^2}{2 t E} \left( 1 - \dfrac{\mu}{2} \right)$.
- ✅ **Change in length:** $\Delta L = \dfrac{p d L}{4 t E}$.
- ✅ **Failure mode:** A thin cylinder under internal pressure splits **lengthwise** (along a generator), not across its ends, because hoop stress is double.
- ✅ **Assumptions checklist:** Homogeneous, isotropic, Hookean material; uniform thickness; thin wall; plane stress; flat end caps.
- ✅ **Engineering applications:** Boilers, LPG cylinders, penstocks, pipelines, aircraft fuselages, rocket casings.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stresses in a Thin Spherical Vessel
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stresses in a Thin Spherical Vessel**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Thin Spherical Vessel:** A closed hollow spherical container whose wall thickness **t** is very small compared to its mean radius **R** (i.e., **t ≤ R/20** or **t/R ≤ 1/20**), and which is subjected to an internal fluid pressure **p**, is termed a **Thin Spherical Vessel** or **Thin Spherical Shell**.

Because the wall is thin, the stress distribution is assumed to be **uniform across the thickness**, and the radial stress is considered negligible compared to the tangential (membrane) stresses.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why "Spherical"?** A sphere is the most efficient pressure vessel geometry — for a given volume and wall thickness, it requires the least material to withstand a given internal pressure. This is why storage tanks for gases, LPG bullets, and aerospace fuel tanks are often spherical.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Pressurized Balloon"

Imagine blowing air into a **rubber balloon**. As the pressure inside increases, the rubber stretches uniformly in **all directions** — there is no "long" or "short" axis. The surface is curved everywhere identically. 

This is precisely why a spherical pressure vessel develops **two equal and uniform membrane stresses** (unlike a cylinder, which has a "weak" and "strong" direction):

- The rubber balloon membrane stretches **equally along every great circle** on its surface.
- No matter which "diameter" line you trace from one side to the other through the center, the tensile force per unit length is the same.

> [!TIP]  
> **Think of a soccer ball vs. a tin can:** A soccer ball (sphere) feels uniform "tension" in every direction. A tin can (cylinder) feels greater tension around its belly (hoop) than along its length. The sphere wins on efficiency.

## 1.3 Key Terminology

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Internal Pressure | **p** | Gauge pressure of fluid inside (N/m² or Pa) |
| Mean Radius | **R** | Radius measured to the **mid-thickness** of the shell |
| Wall Thickness | **t** | Thickness of the spherical shell (must satisfy t/R ≤ 1/20) |
| Hoop Stress | **σₕ** | Stress acting along any **great circle** (equator-like) |
| Meridional / Longitudinal Stress | **σₘ** | Stress acting along any **meridian** (pole-to-pole line) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation

When a thin spherical vessel is subjected to internal pressure **p**, the resulting membrane stresses are derived from **static equilibrium** of imaginary cut-sections. Due to the geometry of a sphere:

- **Every great circle on the sphere is a line of symmetry.**
- **All meridional lines converge at the poles.**
- The stresses in any two perpendicular directions on the surface are **equal in magnitude.**

This leads to the elegant conclusion:

$$\sigma_h = \sigma_m = \sigma$$

That is, **a thin spherical vessel is an isostatic (equi-stressed) surface** — a property unique to spheres in pressure vessel design.

## 2.2 Why Two Stresses Are Equal in a Sphere

In a cylinder, the geometry is *anisotropic*: the axial direction is straight (one radius of curvature = ∞), but the circumferential direction is curved (radius = R). Hence the two stresses differ.

In a sphere, **both principal radii of curvature equal R** in every direction. The membrane equilibrium equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\sigma_1}{R_1} + \frac{\sigma_2}{R_2} = \frac{p}{t}$$

With $R_1 = R_2 = R$, this simplifies to $\sigma_1 = \sigma_2$.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — Memorize These for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t}$ | Hoop/Meridional stress in thin spherical shell | N/m² (Pa) |
| 2 | $t = \dfrac{pR}{2\sigma_{allow}}$ | Required thickness for given allowable stress | m |
| 3 | $p = \dfrac{2t\sigma}{R}$ | Bursting / Safe pressure | Pa or N/m² |
| 4 | $R = \dfrac{2t\sigma}{p}$ | Required radius for given stress | m |
| 5 | $V_{sphere} = \dfrac{4}{3}\pi R^3$ | Internal volume of sphere | m³ |
| 6 | $A_{surface} = 4\pi R^2$ | Internal surface area of sphere | m² |
| 7 | Efficiency $\eta = \dfrac{\sigma_{cyl}}{\sigma_{sphere}} = 2$ | Sphere uses half the stress of cylinder for same p,R,t | — |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Remember the Ratio:** For the **same p, R, t**:  
> - Cylindrical hoop stress = $\dfrac{pR}{t}$  
> - Spherical membrane stress = $\dfrac{pR}{2t}$  
> - So a sphere carries **half** the maximum stress of a cylinder. **That's why spheres are preferred.**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **LPG Storage Bullets** — spherical tanks at refineries.
- **Submersible Pressure Hulls** — diving spheres (bathyscaphes).
- **Hydropneumatic Accumulators** — nitrogen pre-charge vessels.
- **Aerospace Propellant Tanks** — rocket fuel tanks (often spherical for cryogenic fuels).
- **Boilers and Reactor Vessels** — end closures of cylindrical boilers use **spherical dished ends** (Segel or Torispherical) precisely because spherical geometry minimizes stress.

## 2.5 Design Checks (KTU Board Favourite)

1. **Strength Check:** Maximum induced stress $\sigma_{induced} \leq \sigma_{allowable}$
2. **Thickness Check:** Required thickness $t_{required} \leq t_{provided}$
3. **Efficiency Comparison:** Always state why sphere > cylinder for the same duty.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Membrane (Hoop/Meridional) Stress in a Thin Spherical Vessel

### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions

Consider a thin spherical shell of:
- Internal radius **R** (to the mid-surface)
- Wall thickness **t**
- Internal gauge pressure **p**

Assume the sphere is cut into **two equal halves** by a diametrical plane. We analyze the equilibrium of one hemisphere.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Step-by-step logic:** We replace the removed half with the internal pressure acting on the projected circular area (πR²). The wall resists this force via tensile stress σ acting on the cut-section area (π × 2Rt).

### 3.1.2 The Equilibrium Equation

The internal pressure pushes the upper hemisphere **upward** with a force equal to pressure × projected area:

$$F_{pressure} = p \times \pi R^2$$

The resisting tensile force in the shell acts along the cut (the great-circle ring). The area of this ring on the cut-section is:

$$A_{ring} = 2\pi R \times t$$

This is because the cut is a **circle of radius R**, and the wall has thickness **t** in the radial direction, giving a circumference $2\pi R$ times thickness **t**.

The resisting tensile force is:

$$F_{tensile} = \sigma \times A_{ring} = \sigma \times (2\pi R t)$$

### 3.1.3 Applying Static Equilibrium ($F_{pressure} = F_{tensile}$)

For equilibrium of the hemisphere:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma \times (2\pi R t) &= p \times \pi R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing both sides by $\pi R$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma \times (2t) &= p \times R
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for $\sigma$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{pR}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Result:** $\boxed{\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t}}$  
> This single formula governs **both** the hoop stress **and** the meridional stress in a thin spherical shell.

### 3.1.4 Why This Works for Both Directions

If we had chosen the cutting plane to pass through the **poles** (meridian cut), the projected area would again be $\pi R^2$ and the ring area would again be $2\pi R t$ — the **same equation results**, confirming that $\sigma_h = \sigma_m$.

### 3.1.5 Numerical Verification (Sanity Check)

Suppose **p** = 2 MPa, **R** = 1 m, **t** = 10 mm = 0.01 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{(2 \times 10^6)(1)}{2(0.01)} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{0.02}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= 100 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Compare with cylinder** (same p, R, t):  
$\sigma_{hoop, cyl} = \dfrac{pR}{t} = 200$ MPa → sphere is **2× more efficient**. ✓

### 3.2 Derivative Relation — Maximum Shear Stress

In a thin spherical shell, the **two principal stresses are equal** ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_2 = \sigma$) and the third (radial) is negligible. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma - 0}{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{pR}{4t}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]  
> **Strain expression (KTU often asks):** Using Hooke's law with $\nu$ as Poisson's ratio:  
> $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu)$  
> (because both in-plane strains are equal and radial strain is small)

### 3.3 Change in Diameter (Volume-Strain Approach)

The increase in diameter under internal pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta D &= \frac{pR^2}{2tE}(2 - \nu)
\end{aligned}
$$

This formula is occasionally asked in KTU derivations and is derived by combining the biaxial strain in two equal directions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Stress Analysis of a Thin Spherical Vessel

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Internal Pressure p] --> B[Spherical Shell Geometry R, t]
    B --> C{Cut the Sphere}
    C --> D1[Horizontal Cut: Great Circle]
    C --> D2[Vertical Cut: Meridian]
    D1 --> E1[Projected Area = pi R squared]
    D2 --> E2[Projected Area = pi R squared]
    E1 --> F1[Tensile Ring Area = 2 pi R t]
    E2 --> F2[Tensile Ring Area = 2 pi R t]
    F1 --> G1[Force Balance: sigma times 2 pi R t = p pi R squared]
    F2 --> G2[Force Balance: sigma times 2 pi R t = p pi R squared]
    G1 --> H[Hoop Stress sigma h = pR / 2t]
    G2 --> I[Meridional Stress sigma m = pR / 2t]
    H --> J[Equivalence: sigma h = sigma m]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Output: Uniform Membrane Stress sigma = pR / 2t]
    K --> L[Design Verification]
    L --> M1[Strength Check: sigma less than sigma allow]
    L --> M2[Thickness Check: t req less than t prov]
    L --> M3[Efficiency: Sphere is 2x more efficient than cylinder]
```

## 4.2 Free-Body Diagram Description (Verbal Schematic)

- **Sphere of radius R** with wall thickness **t**.
- **Cut horizontally** through the equator → two hemispheres.
- **Upper hemisphere** experiences:
  - Upward force from internal pressure on projected circular area **πR²**.
  - Downward membrane tensile force distributed uniformly around the cut-ring of area **2πRt**.
- **Setting these equal** yields σ = pR/(2t).

> [!NOTE]  
> Since Mermaid cannot natively render 3D spheres with shaded pressure arrows, the flow diagram above conveys the **logical architecture** of the derivation — the actual physical picture is described textually above for the student's exam sketch.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1  
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**  
*Define a thin spherical shell. State the expression for the hoop stress in a thin spherical shell and explain why it is equal to the meridional stress.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A thin spherical shell is a closed hollow sphere whose wall thickness **t** is small compared to its mean radius **R** such that **t/R ≤ 1/20** (1 mark).

The hoop stress is given by:

$$\sigma = \frac{pR}{2t} \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

It equals the meridional stress because a sphere has **equal principal radii of curvature** in every direction (R₁ = R₂ = R), making the membrane stress uniform along every great circle and every meridian (1 mark).

---

### Question A2  
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`**  
*A thin spherical pressure vessel of diameter 1.5 m and wall thickness 12 mm is filled with a gas at a pressure of 1.8 MPa. Find the hoop stress induced in the material.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Given: D = 1.5 m → R = 0.75 m; t = 12 mm = 0.012 m; p = 1.8 MPa = 1.8 × 10⁶ Pa.

Using the formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{(1.8 \times 10^6)(0.75)}{2 \times 0.012}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{1.35 \times 10^6}{0.024} = 56.25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Hoop Stress = 56.25 MPa** (3 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question B1 (Choice A)  
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper 2024]`**  

**(a)** Derive the expression for the hoop stress in a thin spherical shell subjected to an internal pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin spherical shell of 1.2 m diameter is required to contain a gas at a pressure of 2.5 MPa.
