If the maximum allowable tensile stress in the shell material is 100 MPa, find: (i) the minimum required thickness of the shell, and (ii) the percentage increase in diameter under pressure if E = 200 GPa and ν = 0.3. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B1(a)

**Step 1:** Consider a thin spherical shell of internal radius **R** and thickness **t** subjected to internal pressure **p** (1 mark).

**Step 2:** Imagine the sphere cut into **two hemispheres** by a diametrical plane. Consider equilibrium of the upper hemisphere (1 mark).

**Step 3:** The vertical force due to internal pressure acting on the projected area πR² is:

$$F_p = p \times \pi R^2 \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 4:** The resisting tensile force in the shell acts across the cut-section. The cut-section is a ring of circumference 2πR and thickness t, so its area is:

$$A = 2\pi R t \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 5:** The tensile force in the shell is:

$$F_t = \sigma \times 2\pi R t \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

**Step 6:** Equating for equilibrium:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma \times 2\pi R t &= p \times \pi R^2
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{pR}{2t}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **hoop stress = meridional stress** in a thin spherical shell (2 marks).

---

#### Model Solution to B1(b)

**Given:** D = 1.2 m → R = 0.6 m; p = 2.5 MPa; σ_allow = 100 MPa; E = 200 GPa; ν = 0.3.

**(i) Minimum thickness:**

Using $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{pR}{2\sigma_{allow}} = \frac{(2.5 \times 10^6)(0.6)}{2 \times (100 \times 10^6)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t &= \frac{1.5 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^8} = 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**t_min = 7.5 mm** (3 marks).

**(ii) Percentage increase in diameter:**

The hoop strain in the shell (since both principal stresses are equal):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu) = \frac{100 \times 10^6}{200 \times 10^9}(1 - 0.3)
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= (5 \times 10^{-4})(0.7) = 3.5 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Increase in diameter:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta D &= \varepsilon \times D = (3.5 \times 10^{-4})(1.2) = 4.2 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Percentage increase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \delta D &= \frac{4.2 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2} \times 100 = 0.035\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Percentage increase = 0.035 %** (4 marks).

---

### Question B2 (Choice B)  
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023 / Supplementary Exam 2024]`**  

**(a)** With a neat sketch, derive the expression for the hoop stress and longitudinal stress in a thin cylindrical shell. Compare the stresses in a spherical and cylindrical shell of the same diameter, thickness, and internal pressure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thin spherical pressure vessel of diameter 2 m and wall thickness 15 mm is filled with a fluid under a pressure of 3 MPa. Determine: (i) the hoop stress, (ii) the meridional stress, (iii) the maximum shear stress in the shell material, and (iv) the safe internal pressure if the allowable stress is 75 MPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B2(a)

> This sub-question is a *comparison* question (cylindrical vs. spherical). The expected answer outline is:

**Cylindrical shell formulas (brief recall, 3 marks):**
- Hoop stress: $\sigma_h = \dfrac{pR}{t}$
- Longitudinal stress: $\sigma_L = \dfrac{pR}{2t}$

**Spherical shell formula (2 marks):**
- $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t}$ (uniform in all directions).

**Comparison (2 marks):**
- For same p, R, t: $\sigma_{cyl,hoop} = 2 \times \sigma_{sphere}$
- A spherical shell carries **half** the maximum stress of a cylindrical shell.
- Hence a sphere is **twice as efficient** in material usage and is preferred wherever feasible (e.g., LPG bullets, end closures of boilers).

---

#### Model Solution to B2(b)

**Given:** D = 2 m → R = 1 m; t = 15 mm = 0.015 m; p = 3 MPa = 3 × 10⁶ Pa; σ_allow = 75 MPa.

**(i) Hoop stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= \frac{pR}{2t} = \frac{(3 \times 10^6)(1)}{2(0.015)} = \frac{3 \times 10^6}{0.03}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_h &= 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(ii) Meridional stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_m = \sigma_h = 100 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(1 mark)**

**(iii) Maximum shear stress:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{pR}{4t} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

**(iv) Safe internal pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{safe} &= \frac{2t\sigma_{allow}}{R} = \frac{2(0.015)(75 \times 10^6)}{1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
p_{safe} &= 2.25 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.25 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(2 marks)**

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Mixing up sphere vs. cylinder formulas:** Students often write $\sigma = pR/t$ for a sphere — **wrong**. The sphere is $pR/(2t)$. Memorize: *sphere divides by 2; cylinder does not (for hoop).*  
> 2. **Forgetting to halve R for the projected area:** Use the *radius* R (not diameter) when computing $\pi R^2$.  
> 3. **Using inner or outer radius blindly:** KTU expects *mean radius* (or just the given R, assuming it's the mean).  
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Convert mm → m before plugging into formulas. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark per wrong unit.  
> 5. **Skipping the "Why equal" justification:** When asked why $\sigma_h = \sigma_m$, students often skip the explanation. Always mention **equal principal radii of curvature**.  
> 6. **In (b)(i) above, writing only hoop stress:** KTU expects both hoop **and** meridional explicitly stated.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 2:**

- **Definition:** Thin spherical vessel = t/R ≤ 1/20, internal pressure only, uniform membrane stress.
- **Master Formula:** $\sigma = \dfrac{pR}{2t}$ — applies to **both** hoop and meridional directions.
- **Equality of Stresses:** Arises because **R₁ = R₂ = R** (sphere has identical curvature everywhere).
- **Sphere vs. Cylinder Efficiency:** $\sigma_{sphere} = \dfrac{1}{2} \sigma_{hoop,cylinder}$ → sphere uses **half** the material stress.
- **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{pR}{4t}$ (radial stress ≈ 0).
- **Hoop Strain:** $\varepsilon = \dfrac{\sigma}{E}(1 - \nu)$ where $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio.
- **Diameter Change:** $\delta D = \dfrac{pR^2}{2tE}(2 - \nu)$.
- **Design Inequalities:** Always verify $\sigma_{induced} \leq \sigma_{allow}$ and $t_{required} \leq t_{provided}$.
- **Real-world Examples:** LPG bullets, aerospace fuel tanks, boiler dished ends, diving spheres, hydropneumatic accumulators.
- **Common Units in KTU Problems:** p in MPa, R and t in mm (convert to m before calculation), σ in MPa.
- **Typical KTU Question Patterns:**
  1. Pure derivation (7 marks) — draw FBD, write force balance, derive σ = pR/(2t).
  2. Numerical on thickness / stress / pressure (7 marks).
  3. Comparison: sphere vs. cylinder (carry 7 marks when asked).
  4. Combined: derivation + numerical in same 14-mark question.
- **Examiner's Favourite Trap:** Forgetting that radial stress = 0 in thin-wall assumption; students sometimes wrongly include it in the principal stress set.

> **End of Module 2 — Stresses in a Thin Spherical Vessel — PCMRT303 Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elastic Strain Energy for Axial Loading, Transverse Shear, Bending and Torsional Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Elastic Strain Energy for Axial Loading, Transverse Shear, Bending and Torsional Loads # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Strain Energy (U)** is defined as the internal work done by the internal resisting forces of an elastic body when it is deformed under the action of external loads. Mathematically, it is the energy **stored** within a body due to its elastic deformation, which is fully recoverable upon unloading. When a body is loaded within its **proportional limit** and **elastic limit**, the work done by the external forces equals the strain energy stored. $$U = W = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$$ where $P$ is the gradually applied load and $\delta$ is the corresponding deformation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The four canonical cases of strain energy are: > 1. **Axial Loading** (tension/compression) > 2. **Transverse Shear Loading** (direct shear in beams) > 3. **Bending Loads** (pure bending / flexure) > 4. **Torsional Loads** (twisting of shafts) The **Strain Energy Density ($u$)** is the strain energy per unit volume of the material: $$u = \frac{U}{V} = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \frac{1}{2}E\varepsilon^{2}$$ Two critical energy-based material properties are: - **Modulus of Resilience ($U_{r}$):** The maximum strain energy stored per unit volume within the proportional limit, equal to the area under the elastic portion of the stress-strain diagram. It represents the material's ability to **absorb energy elastically**. $$U_{r} = \frac{\sigma_{y}^{2}}{2E}$$ - **Modulus of Toughness ($U_{t}$):** The total strain energy per unit volume a material can absorb before **fracture** (area under the entire stress-strain curve up to failure). ## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world analogy — The Spring:** Imagine stretching a rubber band or compressing a spring. The effort you put in to deform it does not disappear; it is *stored* inside the deformed shape as potential energy. Release the rubber band and that energy launches it back. That stored energy is **strain energy**. A **stiffer** spring (higher $E$ or $G$) stores *less* energy for the same deformation because it resists more strongly and deforms less. A **softer** spring stores more. Similarly, when a structural member (beam, column, shaft) is loaded: - A **long axial bar** under tension stretches slightly → energy stored along its length. - A **beam bending** stores energy in the bent fibers (top compressed, bottom stretched). - A **transverse shear** stores energy due to sliding of horizontal layers. - A **shaft under torsion** stores energy in the twisted helical fibers. > [!IMPORTANT] > The expression $\frac{1}{2}$ (not 1) appears in every strain energy equation because the load is applied **gradually** from zero to its final value — only the **average** load ($\frac{1}{2}P$) does work through the full deformation. --- ## Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Symbol | Quantity | Typical Units (SI) | |---|---|---| | $E$ | Young's Modulus | $\text{GPa} = 10^{9}\,\text{N/m}^{2}$ | | $G$ | Shear Modulus (Modulus of Rigidity) | $\text{GPa}$ | | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Relationship between $E$, $G$, and Poisson's ratio $\nu$ | — | | For steel: $E \approx 200\,\text{GPa}$, $G \approx 80\,\text{GPa}$, $\nu \approx 0.3$ | — | — | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Four Loading Cases The strain energy derivation follows a **universal 3-step pattern** for every loading type: 1. **Identify the internal stress** ($\sigma$ or $\tau$) at any point in terms of the applied load and geometry. 2. **Compute the strain energy density** $u = \dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}$ (for normal stress) or $u = \dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}$ (for shear stress). 3. **Integrate** $u$ over the **volume** of the member to obtain total strain energy. $$U = \int_{V} \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}\,dV \quad \text{(for normal stresses)}$$ $$U = \int_{V} \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G}\,dV \quad \text{(for shear stresses)}$$ ### 2.1.1 Case 1 — Axial Loading (Tension/Compression) - **Internal stress:** $\sigma = P/A$ (uniform over cross-section for centric load). - **Deformation:** $\delta = PL/(AE)$. - **Total strain energy:** $$U_{\text{axial}} = \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE} = \frac{1}{2}\,P\,\delta$$ - **For a non-uniform bar** (stepped or tapered): $$U_{\text{axial}} = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{P^{2}(x)}{2A(x)E}\,dx$$ ### 2.1.2 Case 2 — Transverse Shear Loading For a beam carrying a transverse shear force $V$, the shear stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is: $$\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$$ where: - $I$ = second moment of area about the neutral axis, - $b$ = width of the section at the level where $\tau$ is computed, - $Q$ = first moment of area of the portion beyond the cut about the neutral axis. The **shear strain energy** is: $$U_{\text{shear}} = \int_{0}^{L}\int_{A} \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G}\,dA\,dx = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{V^{2}}{2G}\left[\frac{1}{I^{2}}\int_{A}\frac{Q^{2}}{b^{2}}\,dA\right]dx$$ This integral over the cross-section is consolidated using a **shear coefficient $k$** (also called $A_s$ or shape factor) so that: $$\boxed{U_{\text{shear}} = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{V^{2}}{kGA}\,dx}$$ | Cross-section Shape | Shear Coefficient $k$ | |---|---| | Rectangular | $6/5 = 1.20$ | | Solid circular | $10/9 \approx 1.11$ | | Thin-walled hollow tube | $\approx 2.0$ | | I-section (web-dominated) | $\approx 1.0$ (use web area) | > [!NOTE] > For **slender beams** ($L/h > 20$), bending strain energy **dominates** shear strain energy, and shear contribution is often neglected. For **short deep beams**, shear strain energy can be significant. ### 2.1.3 Case 3 — Bending Loads (Flexure) - **Bending stress:** $\sigma = \dfrac{My}{I}$, where $M$ = bending moment at the section. - **Bending strain energy:** $$U_{\text{bending}} = \int_{V} \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}\,dV = \int_{0}^{L} \int_{A} \frac{M^{2}y^{2}}{2EI^{2}}\,dA\,dx$$ Since $\int_{A} y^{2}\,dA = I$: $$\boxed{U_{\text{bending}} = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}}{EI}\,dx}$$ - For a **constant moment** $M$ over length $L$: $U = M^{2}L/(2EI)$. - For a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)** $w$ on a simply supported beam of span $L$: $$U = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{(wx(L-x)/2)^{2}}{2EI}\,dx = \frac{w^{2}L^{5}}{240\,EI}$$ - For a **point load $P$ at midspan** on a simply supported beam of span $L$: $$U = \frac{P^{2}L^{3}}{96\,EI}$$ ### 2.1.4 Case 4 — Torsional Loads (Twisting of Circular Shafts) - **Torsional shear stress:** $\tau = \dfrac{Tr}{J}$, where $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ (solid shaft) or $J = \dfrac{\pi (d_{o}^{4} - d_{i}^{4})}{32}$ (hollow shaft). - **Twist angle:** $\phi = TL/(GJ)$. - **Total strain energy:** $$\boxed{U_{\text{torsion}} = \frac{T^{2}L}{2GJ} = \frac{1}{2}\,T\,\phi}$$ - For a **non-uniform** shaft: $$U_{\text{torsion}} = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{T^{2}(x)}{2G\,J(x)}\,dx$$ --- ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **single most important revision resource** for this topic. Memorize it. Every KTU exam question on strain energy is solved using one of these formulas. | # | Loading Type | Strain Energy Equation | Stress Form Used | Special Cases | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Axial** (uniform) | $U = \dfrac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$ | $\sigma = P/A$ | Variable area: $\int P^{2}/(2AE)\,dx$ | | 2 | **Transverse Shear** | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{L}\dfrac{V^{2}}{kGA}\,dx$ | $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ | Rectangular: $k=6/5$; Circular: $k=10/9$ | | 3 | **Bending** | $U = \dfrac{1}{2}\displaystyle\int_{0}^{L}\dfrac{M^{2}}{EI}\,dx$ | $\sigma = My/I$ | UDL: $w^{2}L^{5}/(240EI)$; Pt. load mid: $P^{2}L^{3}/(96EI)$ | | 4 | **Torsion** (circular) | $U = \dfrac{T^{2}L}{2GJ}$ | $\tau = Tr/J$ | Variable: $\int T^{2}/(2GJ)\,dx$ | | 5 | **General** | $U = \displaystyle\int_{V}\dfrac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}\,dV$ | — | For any normal-stress distribution | | 6 | **General** | $U = \displaystyle\int_{V}\dfrac{\tau^{2}}{2G}\,dV$ | — | For any shear-stress distribution | | 7 | **Strain Energy Density** | $u = \sigma^{2}/(2E) = \tau^{2}/(2G)$ | — | Units: $\text{N·m/m}^{3} = \text{J/m}^{3}$ | | 8 | **Modulus of Resilience** | $U_{r} = \sigma_{y}^{2}/(2E)$ | — | Area under elastic portion of $\sigma$-$\varepsilon$ curve | | 9 | **Combined Loading** | $U_{\text{total}} = U_{\text{axial}} + U_{\text{shear}} + U_{\text{bend}} + U_{\text{tors}}$ | — | Superposition holds within elastic limit | --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility | Engineering Field | Application of Strain Energy Concept | |---|---| | **Structural Steel Design** | Sizing beams to limit deflection → uses $U_{\text{bending}}$ to find deflection via **Castigliano's Theorem** or **virtual work**. | | **Machine Design (Shafts)** | Design of drive shafts/axles in automobiles uses $U_{\text{torsion}}$ to predict **angle of twist** under service torque. | | **Spring Design** | Helical springs store strain energy in **torsion** of the wire — same formula $T^{2}L/(2GJ)$. | | **Impact Loading** | Sudden loads store strain energy dynamically — capacity of beams to absorb energy without permanent set → uses **modulus of resilience**. | | **Pressure Vessels** | Spherical/cylindrical vessels under internal pressure use combined axial + hoop stress → total $U = \int \sigma^{2}/(2E)\,dV$. | | **Aerospace & Auto** | Crash absorption zones designed to maximize strain energy (toughness) before failure. | | **Civil — Seismic** | Buildings designed for earthquake loading rely on strain energy dissipation in ductile members. | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Strain Energy Due to Axial Loading ### Setup Consider a prismatic bar of **length $L$**, **uniform cross-sectional area $A$**, made of a homogeneous material with **Young's modulus $E$**. A gradually applied axial tensile force $P$ stretches the bar by an elongation $\delta$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the internal normal stress.** The internal resisting force at any cross-section is $P$. The uniform normal stress is: $$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$ **Step 2 — Use Hooke's Law to find the strain.** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$ **Step 3 — Find the total elongation.** $$\delta = \varepsilon \cdot L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$ This is the same as $\delta = \dfrac{P L}{A E}$ (a direct stiffness relation). **Step 4 — Compute the work done by the external (gradually applied) force.** Since the load is applied *gradually* from 0 to $P$, the force at any intermediate stage is $P(x) = kx$ (linear). The work done is the area under the load–deflection curve (a triangle): $$U = W = \int_{0}^{\delta} P(x)\,dx = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$$ **Step 5 — Substitute $\delta$:** $$U_{\text{axial}} = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \frac{PL}{AE} = \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE}$$ **Alternative form (using strain energy density):** $$u = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E} = \frac{P^{2}}{2A^{2}E}$$ $$U = u \cdot V = \frac{P^{2}}{2A^{2}E} \cdot (A L) = \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE} \checkmark$$ > [!NOTE] > **Physical insight:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is non-negotiable. It arises from the fact that the load builds up gradually — the resistance at any instant is only the instantaneous load, not the final load $P$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain Energy Due to Bending (Flexure) ### Setup Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ subjected to a bending moment $M(x)$ that varies along the span. Cross-section has moment of inertia $I$ about the neutral axis, and the material has Young's modulus $E$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Bending stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis.** $$\sigma_{x} = \frac{M(x)\,y}{I}$$ **Step 2 — Strain energy density at the fiber located at $y$:** $$u = \frac{\sigma_{x}^{2}}{2E} = \frac{M^{2}(x)\,y^{2}}{2EI^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Strain energy in the infinitesimal element $dx$ and area $dA$:** $$dU = u\,dA\,dx = \frac{M^{2}(x)\,y^{2}}{2EI^{2}}\,dA\,dx$$ **Step 4 — Integrate over the cross-section $A$ (eliminates $y$):** $$dU = \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI^{2}} \left(\int_{A} y^{2}\,dA\right) dx$$ By definition $\int_{A} y^{2}\,dA = I$ (second moment of area). Therefore: $$dU = \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\,dx$$ **Step 5 — Integrate along the length of the beam:** $$\boxed{U_{\text{bending}} = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}(x)}{EI}\,dx}$$ ### Special Case — Point Load $P$ at Center of Simply Supported Beam The bending moment at distance $x$ from the left support is $M(x) = \dfrac{Px}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le L/2$. By symmetry, total strain energy is **twice** the integral from 0 to $L/2$: $$U = 2 \int_{0}^{L/2} \frac{1}{EI}\left(\frac{Px}{2}\right)^{2} dx$$ $$U = 2 \int_{0}^{L/2} \frac{P^{2}x^{2}}{4EI}\,dx = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI}\int_{0}^{L/2} x^{2}\,dx$$ $$U = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI}\left[\frac{x^{3}}{3}\right]_{0}^{L/2} = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI} \cdot \frac{L^{3}}{24}$$ $$\boxed{U = \frac{P^{2}L^{3}}{96\,EI}}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strain Energy Due to Transverse Shear ### Setup Consider a beam carrying a transverse shear force $V(x)$. The shear stress distribution across the cross-section is given by the Jourawski formula: $$\tau = \frac{V(x)\,Q(y)}{I\,b(y)}$$ where $Q(y)$ is the first moment of area of the portion of the cross-section lying *beyond* the cut at $y$, and $b(y)$ is the width at that level. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Shear strain energy density:** $$u = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} = \frac{V^{2}(x)\,Q^{2}(y)}{2G\,I^{2}\,b^{2}(y)}$$ **Step 2 — Strain energy in infinitesimal element $dA\,dx$:** $$dU = \frac{V^{2}(x)\,Q^{2}(y)}{2G\,I^{2}\,b^{2}(y)}\,dA\,dx$$ **Step 3 — Integrate over the cross-section area $A$:** $$dU = \frac{V^{2}(x)}{2G\,I^{2}}\left[\int_{A} \frac{Q^{2}(y)}{b^{2}(y)}\,dA\right]dx$$ Define the integral bracket as $\dfrac{I^{2}}{A_s}$ where $A_s = kA$ is the **effective shear area** and $k$ is the **shear coefficient** (shape factor). The result is a compact form: $$dU = \frac{V^{2}(x)}{2G\,(kA)}\,dx$$ **Step 4 — Integrate over the length of the beam:** $$\boxed{U_{\text{shear}} = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{V^{2}(x)}{kGA}\,dx}$$ ### Special Case — Constant Shear Force $V$ over Length $L$ (Cantilever with End Load, or SSB with End Couple) For a cantilever of length $L$ with end load $P$, $V = P$ (constant): $$U_{\text{shear}} = \frac{P^{2}L}{2\,k\,G\,A}$$ For a **rectangular cross-section** ($k = 6/5$, $A = bh$): $$U_{\text{shear}} = \frac{5\,P^{2}L}{12\,G\,b\,h}$$ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Strain Energy Due to Torsion (Circular Shaft) ### Setup Consider a solid circular shaft of length $L$, diameter $d$, polar moment of inertia $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$, subjected to a gradually applied torque $T$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Torsional shear stress at radius $r$:** $$\tau = \frac{T\,r}{J}$$ **Step 2 — Shear strain energy density:** $$u = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} = \frac{T^{2}\,r^{2}}{2G\,J^{2}}$$ **Step 3 — Strain energy in volume element $dV = 2\pi r\,dr\,dx$:** $$dU = u\,dV = \frac{T^{2}\,r^{2}}{2G\,J^{2}} \cdot 2\pi r\,dr\,dx$$ $$dU = \frac{T^{2}\,\pi}{G\,J^{2}}\,r^{3}\,dr\,dx$$ **Step 4 — Integrate over the radius from 0 to $R = d/2$:** $$\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\,dr = \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{1}{4}\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4} = \frac{d^{4}}{64}$$ $$dU = \frac{T^{2}\,\pi}{G\,J^{2}} \cdot \frac{d^{4}}{64}\,dx = \frac{T^{2}}{2GJ}\,dx$$ because $J = \pi d^{4}/32$, so $J^{2} = \pi^{2}d^{4}/1024$, and: $$\frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{64\,G\,J^{2}} = \frac{\pi\,d^{4}}{64\,G \cdot \pi^{2}d^{4}/1024} = \frac{1024}{64\,\pi\,G} = \frac{16}{\pi\,G} = \frac{1}{2G} \cdot \frac{32}{\pi J}$$ After simplification (algebra is valid for $J = \pi d^{4}/32$): $$dU = \frac{T^{2}}{2GJ}\,dx$$ **Step 5 — Integrate over length $L$:** $$\boxed{U_{\text{torsion}} = \frac{T^{2}L}{2GJ} = \frac{1}{2}T\phi \quad \text{where} \quad \phi = \frac{TL}{GJ}}$$ --- ## 3.5 Analytical Summary Matrix | Loading | Stress in Member | Strain Energy (Differential) | Strain Energy (Total) | Deflection / Angle | |---|---|---|---|---| | Axial | $\sigma = P/A$ | $dU = P^{2}/(2AE)\,dx$ | $U = P^{2}L/(2AE)$ | $\delta = PL/(AE)$ | | Bending | $\sigma = My/I$ | $dU = M^{2}/(2EI)\,dx$ | $U = \int M^{2}/(2EI)\,dx$ | Slope & deflection via Castigliano | | Transverse Shear | $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ | $dU = V^{2}/(2kGA)\,dx$ | $U = \int V^{2}/(2kGA)\,dx$ | Shear deflection $\delta_s$ | | Torsion | $\tau = Tr/J$ | $dU = T^{2}/(2GJ)\,dx$ | $U = T^{2}L/(2GJ)$ | $\phi = TL/(GJ)$ | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Strain Energy in an Elastic Body The following Mermaid flow diagram illustrates the **logical pipeline** that links external loading to strain energy storage, for all four loading types. This is the architecture you should internalize — every problem flows through the same set of conceptual nodes. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[External Load Applied
P, V, M, or T] --> B[Identify Internal Action
Normal force, Shear force, Moment, Torque] B --> C[Determine Stress Distribution
sigma, tau] C --> D[Apply Hooke's Law
strain = stress/modulus] D --> E[Compute Strain Energy Density
u = sigma^2 / 2E or tau^2 / 2G] E --> F[Integrate over Volume dV
dU = u dV] F --> G[Total Strain Energy U] G --> H{Axial} G --> I{Transverse Shear} G --> J{Bending} G --> K{Torsion} H --> H1[U = P^2 L / 2AE] I --> I1[U = integral V^2 / 2kGA dx] J --> J1[U = integral M^2 / 2EI dx] K --> K1[U = T^2 L / 2GJ] H1 --> L[Resilience and Toughness
Material Property] I1 --> L J1 --> L K1 --> L L --> M[Engineering Application
Deflection, Castigliano, Impact] ``` **Reading the diagram:** - The path from external load to total strain energy $U$ is **universal** (Steps 1–6). - Only the *specific integration* changes (the four branching arms at the bottom). - All four energies are **additive** within the elastic limit (superposition). - The output feeds directly into deflection calculation (Castigliano's theorem) and material selection (resilience/toughness). ## 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Formula to Use? ```mermaid flowchart TD S[Start: Identify the type of internal loading in the member] --> S1{Is the load along the member axis?} S1 -->|Yes| SA[Use Axial Strain Energy formula] SA --> SA1[U = P^2 L / 2AE] S1 -->|No| S2{Is the load a twisting moment about the member axis?} S2 -->|Yes| ST[Use Torsional Strain Energy formula] ST --> ST1[U = T^2 L / 2GJ] S2 -->|No| S3{Is the load transverse to the axis producing a bending moment?} S3 -->|Yes| SB[Check if member is slender] SB -->|Slender L/h greater than 20| SB1[Bending dominates U = integral M^2 / 2EI dx] SB -->|Short deep beam| SB2[Add Shear contribution U = integral V^2 / 2kGA dx] ``` ## 4.3 Free-Body & Stress Distribution Reminder (Text-Based) Although physical drawings of stress blocks cannot be rendered here, the following text sketch summarizes the **stress state** for each loading case — useful when you draw free-hand in the exam. ``` +----------------------------+ +----------------------------+ | AXIAL (Bar) | | TORSION (Circular Shaft) | | | | | | ──────► P ──────► | | T (couple) | | ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓ | | ▲ | | σ = P/A (uniform) | | │ | | (no variation in A) | | │ τ = Tr/J | +----------------------------+ | │ (linear in r) | | ▼ | +----------------------------+ | ┌──────────┐ | | BENDING (Beam) | | │ τ max │ | | | | │ at r=R │ | | ▲ y | | └──────────┘ | | │ | +----------------------------+ | ├──── Neutral Axis ───── | | │ σ_top = -Mc/I (comp.) | +----------------------------+ | │ σ_bot = +Mc/I (tens.) | | TRANSVERSE SHEAR (Beam) | | │ linear in y | | | | ▼ | | V ───────► ║ | +----------------------------+ | ║ τ = VQ/Ib | | ║ parabolic in y| | max τ at NA, zero at | | top and bottom | +----------------------------+ ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Two compulsory 3-mark questions in Part A. Answers should be 5–8 lines with a derivation or formula at the end. --- ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define strain energy and modulus of resilience. A steel rod of length 1.5 m and cross-sectional area 600 mm² is subjected to an axial pull of 50 kN. Find the strain energy stored. Take $E = 200$ GPa.** **Model Answer:** **Strain Energy:** It is the energy stored in a body within its elastic limit due to deformation, equal to the work done by the external load. **Modulus of Resilience:** It is the strain energy stored per unit volume of a material up to its elastic limit. Numerically, it equals the area under the stress–strain diagram up to the proportional limit. $U_r = \sigma_y^2 / (2E)$. **Calculation:** $$U = \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE} = \frac{(50 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 1.5}{2 \times 600 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{9}}$$ $$U = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{9} \times 1.5}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{3.75 \times 10^{9}}{2.4 \times 10^{8}} = 15.625\,\text{J}$$ $$\boxed{U = 15.625\,\text{J}}$$ --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Explain why a factor of $\frac{1}{2}$ appears in the strain energy equation $U = \frac{1}{2}P\delta$. Mention the strain energy density expression for pure shear.** **Model Answer:** The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ appears because the load $P$ is applied **gradually** from 0 to its final value, in equilibrium with the internal resistance at every instant. The work done is the area under the load–deflection curve, which (within the elastic limit) is a triangle with base $\delta$ and height $P$, hence $W = \frac{1}{2}P\delta = U$. This is the **gradual/static loading** assumption used in all strain energy derivations; it does **not** apply to sudden or impact loads. **Strain energy density for pure shear:** $$u_{\text{shear}} = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} = \frac{1}{2}\tau\gamma$$ where $G$ is the shear modulus and $\gamma$ is the engineering shear strain. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) > **KTU Pattern:** Part B has a 14-mark question with **internal choice** (Module-level). You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part is typically 7 marks. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Strain Energy in Bending + Axial Combined** **(a) Derive the expression for strain energy due to bending of a beam. Also derive the strain energy for a simply supported beam of span $L$ carrying a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over the entire span. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** Consider a beam segment $dx$ at a section where bending moment is $M(x)$. Bending stress at distance $y$ from the neutral axis: $$\sigma = \frac{M(x)\,y}{I}$$ Strain energy in volume element $dA\,dx$: $$dU = \frac{\sigma^{2}}{2E}\,dA\,dx = \frac{M^{2}(x)\,y^{2}}{2EI^{2}}\,dA\,dx$$ Integrating over the cross-section (using $\int_A y^2\,dA = I$): $$dU = \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\,dx$$ Integrating over the length: $$U_{\text{bending}} = \frac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}(x)}{EI}\,dx \quad \text{...(i)}$$ **Application: SSB with UDL $w$ over span $L$:** Bending moment at distance $x$ from the left support: $$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^{2}}{2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$ Squaring: $$M^{2}(x) = \frac{w^{2}x^{2}(L-x)^{2}}{4}$$ Substituting into (i): $$U = \frac{1}{2EI}\int_{0}^{L} \frac{w^{2}x^{2}(L-x)^{2}}{4}\,dx$$ $$U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI}\int_{0}^{L}\left(L^{2}x^{2} - 2Lx^{3} + x^{4}\right)dx$$ $$U = \frac{w^{2}}{8EI}\left[L^{2}\cdot\frac{L^{3}}{3} - 2L\cdot\frac{L^{4}}{4} + \frac{L^{5}}{5}\right]$$ $$U = \frac{w^{2}L^{5}}{8EI}\left[\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{5}\right]$$ LCM of (3, 2, 5) = 30: $$\left[\frac{10}{30} - \frac{15}{30} + \frac{6}{30}\right] = \frac{1}{30}$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{U_{\text{UDL}} = \frac{w^{2}L^{5}}{240\,EI}}$$ --- **(b) A circular bar of 25 mm diameter and 1.2 m long is subjected to an axial tensile load of 40 kN. Simultaneously the same bar is used as a cantilever to carry a transverse load of 1.5 kN at the free end. Find the total strain energy stored in the bar. Take $E = 200$ GPa. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $d = 25\,\text{mm} = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $L = 1.2\,\text{m}$, $P_{\text{axial}} = 40\,\text{kN}$, $P_{\text{trans}} = 1.5\,\text{kN}$, $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$. **Cross-sectional properties:** $$A = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^{2}}{4} = 4.909 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^{2}$$ $$I = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.025)^{4}}{64} = 1.9175 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^{4}$$ **Component 1: Axial strain energy.** $$U_1 = \frac{P^{2}L}{2AE} = \frac{(40 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times 1.2}{2 \times 4.909 \times 10^{-4} \times 200 \times 10^{9}}$$ $$U_1 = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{9} \times 1.2}{1.9636 \times 10^{8}} = \frac{1.92 \times 10^{9}}{1.9636 \times 10^{8}} = 9.78\,\text{J}$$ **Component 2: Bending strain energy (cantilever, end load $P$):** For a cantilever, $M(x) = P(L - x)$. Strain energy: $$U_2 = \int_{0}^{L} \frac{M^{2}(x)}{2EI}\,dx = \frac{1}{2EI}\int_{0}^{L} P^{2}(L-x)^{2}\,dx$$ Let $u = L - x$, $du = -dx$. When $x = 0$, $u = L$; when $x = L$, $u = 0$: $$U_2 = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI}\int_{L}^{0} u^{2}(-du) = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI}\int_{0}^{L} u^{2}\,du = \frac{P^{2}}{2EI}\cdot\frac{L^{3}}{3} = \frac{P^{2}L^{3}}{6EI}$$ $$U_2 = \frac{(1.5 \times 10^{3})^{2} \times (1.2)^{3}}{6 \times 200 \times 10^{9} \times 1.9175 \times 10^{-8}}$$ $$U_2 = \frac{2.25 \times 10^{6} \times 1.728}{2.301 \times 10^{4}} = \frac{3.888 \times 10^{6}}{2.301 \times 10^{4}} = 168.97\,\text{J}$$ > **Neglecting shear energy** (slender bar, $L/d = 48 \gg 20$). **Total strain energy:** $$U_{\text{total}} = U_1 + U_2 = 9.78 + 168.97 = 178.75\,\text{J}$$ $$\boxed{U_{\text{total}} \approx 178.75\,\text{J}}$$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Strain Energy in Torsion + Shear** **(a) Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a circular shaft subjected to a torque $T$. Hence, find the strain energy per unit volume at the outer surface of a solid shaft of diameter $D$. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Derivation:** A solid circular shaft of diameter $D$ (radius $R = D/2$) and length $L$ is subjected to a torque $T$. The polar moment of inertia is $J = \pi D^4 / 32$. Shear stress at radius $r$: $$\tau = \frac{Tr}{J}$$ Strain energy density: $$u = \frac{\tau^{2}}{2G} = \frac{T^{2}r^{2}}{2GJ^{2}}$$ For a thin ring of area $dA = 2\pi r\,dr$ and length $L$: $$dU = u \cdot 2\pi r L\,dr = \frac{T^{2}r^{2}}{2GJ^{2}} \cdot 2\pi r L\,dr = \frac{\pi T^{2}L}{G J^{2}}\,r^{3}\,dr$$ Integrating from $0$ to $R$: $$U = \frac{\pi T^{2}L}{GJ^{2}} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$ Substituting $R = D/2$ and $J = \pi D^4/32$: $$R^{4} = \frac{D^{4}}{16}, \quad J^{2} = \frac{\pi^{2}D^{8}}{1024}$$ $$U = \frac{\pi T^{2}L}{G} \cdot \frac{D^{4}/16}{4 \cdot \pi^{2}D^{8}/1024} = \frac{\pi T^{2}L}{G} \cdot \frac{1024}{64\pi^{2}D^{4}} = \frac{T^{2}L}{2GJ}$$ $$\boxed{U = \frac{T^{2}L}{2GJ}}$$ **Strain energy density at the outer surface ($r = R = D/2$):** At the outer fiber, $\tau_{\max} = TR/J = T(D/2)/J$. Since $J = \pi D^{4}/32$: $$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}$$ Therefore: $$u_{\text{outer}} = \frac{\tau_{\max}^{2}}{2G} = \frac{1}{2G}\left(\frac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}\right)^{2} = \frac{128\,T^{2}}{G\,\pi^{2}D^{6}}$$ $$\boxed{u_{\text{outer}} = \frac{128\,T^{2}}{\pi^{2} G D^{6}}\,\text{J/m}^{3}}$$ --- **(b) A solid steel shaft of 60 mm diameter and 2 m length is subjected to a torque of 6 kN·m. Find (i) the strain energy stored in the shaft, and (ii) the angle of twist. Take $G = 80$ GPa. (7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given:** $D = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.06\,\text{m}$, $L = 2\,\text{m}$, $T = 6\,\text{kN·m} = 6000\,\text{N·m}$, $G = 80 \times 10^{9}\,\text{Pa}$. **Polar moment of inertia:** $$J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^{4}$$ **Part (i): Strain energy stored.** $$U = \frac{T^{2}L}{2GJ} = \frac{(6000)^{2} \times 2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$U = \frac{3.6 \times 10^{7} \times 2}{2.0352 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{7.2 \times 10^{7}}{2.0352 \times 10^{5}}$$ $$\boxed{U = 353.78\,\text{J} \approx 353.8\,\text{J}}$$ **Part (ii): Angle of twist.** $$\phi = \frac{TL}{GJ} = \frac{6000 \times 2}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\phi = \frac{12000}{1.0176 \times 10^{5}} = 0.1179\,\text{rad}$$ Converting to degrees: $$\phi = 0.1179 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 6.756^{\circ}$$ $$\boxed{\phi \approx 0.118\,\text{rad} \approx 6.76^{\circ}}$$ --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students lose marks in strain energy problems:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor $\frac{1}{2}$:** The single most common error. The expression is $\frac{1}{2}P\delta$, *not* $P\delta$. This applies to **all four** loading types. > > 2. **Using $E$ instead of $G$ for shear/torsion:** The strain energy density for **shear** stress is $\tau^2/(2G)$, **not** $\tau^2/(2E)$. Mixing $E$ and $G$ costs full marks. > > 3. **Neglecting the shear coefficient $k$ in transverse shear:** When asked to include shear strain energy, students often write $V^2/(2GA)$ instead of $V^2/(2kGA)$. For a rectangle, $k = 6/5$. > > 4. **Not using $J$ correctly for torsion:** The polar moment of inertia is $J = \pi D^4/32$ for solid and $J = \pi (D_o^4 - D_i^4)/32$ for hollow shafts. Using $I$ (second moment about a diameter) instead of $J$ is a common error. > > 5. **Forgetting the units conversion:** Powers of 10 mistakes — e.g., writing $E = 200$ without $\times 10^9$ when using SI units, or missing the factor of $10^{-6}$ for mm². KTU examiners specifically watch for this. > > 6. **In integration problems (bending), missing the $\frac{1}{2}$ inside the integral** as well — the formula is $U = \frac{1}{2}\int M^2/(EI)\,dx$, with the $\frac{1}{2}$ present *before* the integration. > > 7. **For slender beams, neglecting shear contribution** when the problem says "find total strain energy" — you must include both bending and shear. The phrasing is critical. > > 8. **No free-body / moment diagram in the solution:** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a proper bending moment expression derivation. Always write $M(x) = \ldots$ explicitly. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Summary — Read this the night before the exam.** - ✅ **Strain energy** = internal work stored due to elastic deformation = $\frac{1}{2} \times \text{load} \times \text{deformation}$. - ✅ The factor $\frac{1}{2}$ is **mandatory** and arises from **gradual** (static) loading. - ✅ **Four canonical formulas** to memorize: - Axial: $U = P^{2}L/(2AE)$ - Bending: $U = \frac{1}{2}\int M^{2}/(EI)\,dx$ - Shear: $U = \frac{1}{2}\int V^{2}/(kGA)\,dx$ - Torsion: $U = T^{2}L/(2GJ)$ - ✅ **Modulus of resilience** $U_r = \sigma_y^{2}/(2E)$ — energy stored per unit volume up to elastic limit. - ✅ **Modulus of toughness** — total area under $\sigma$–$\varepsilon$ curve (up to fracture). - ✅ **Strain energy density** $u = \sigma^{2}/(2E)$ for normal stress; $u = \tau^{2}/(2G)$ for shear stress. - ✅ **Shear coefficient $k$** values to remember: Rectangle → $6/5$; Solid circle → $10/9$; Thin tube → $\approx 2$. - ✅ **Polar moment of inertia:** $J = \pi D^{4}/32$ (solid), $J = \pi (D_o^{4} - D_i^{4})/32$ (hollow). - ✅ **Second moment of area:** $I = \pi D^{4}/64$ (solid circular). - ✅ **Slenderness criterion:** If $L/h > 20$, shear strain energy is **negligible**; if $L/h < 5$, both bending and shear must be included. - ✅ **Cantilever formulas worth memorizing:** - Bending strain energy for end load: $U = P^{2}L^{3}/(6EI)$. - Bending strain energy for UDL: $U = w^{2}L^{5}/(40EI)$. - ✅ **Simply supported beam formulas:** - Center point load: $U = P^{2}L^{3}/(96EI)$. - UDL: $U = w^{2}L^{5}/(240EI)$. - ✅ **Twist angle** $\phi = TL/(GJ)$ and strain energy $U = \frac{1}{2}T\phi$ — analogous to $\delta$ and $U = \frac{1}{2}P\delta$. - ✅ **Units check:** $P^{2}L/(AE)$ should yield $\text{N}^{2}\cdot\text{m}/(\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{Pa}) = \text{N}^{2}\cdot\text{m}/(\text{N}/\text{m}^{2}\cdot\text{m}^{2}) = \text{N}\cdot\text{m} = \text{J}$ ✓ - ✅ **Castigliano's theorem** (next-module hint): $\delta = \partial U/\partial P$ — strain energy is the foundation for deflection analysis. - ✅ **For impact loads**, the dynamic strain energy is $\frac{1}{2}P_{\text{dyn}}\delta$ where $P_{\text{dyn}} = P[1 + \sqrt{1 + 2h/\delta_{\text{st}}}]$ — a follow-up concept in Module 3. - ✅ **Always show integration steps** — KTU examiners give **process marks** even if the final answer slips by a small numerical error. ---

topics.title: Expressions for Strain Energy in Terms of Load, Geometry and Material Properties of the Body for Axial, Shearing, Bending and Torsional Loads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Expressions for Strain Energy in Terms of Load, Geometry and Material Properties of the Body for Axial, Shearing, Bending and Torsional Loads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Strain Energy (U)** is defined as the internal work done by the internal resisting forces of a body when it undergoes elastic deformation under the action of external loads. Mathematically, it is the work stored in a body as a consequence of its elastic deformation, which is fully recoverable upon unloading.

For an element subjected to a gradually applied load, the strain energy is given by:

$$U = \int_0^\delta P \, d\delta = \frac{1}{2} P \delta$$

where **P** is the gradually applied load and **δ** is the deformation produced in the direction of the load. The factor of **½** arises because the load is applied *gradually* from zero to its final value, meaning the average force is **P/2**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** The strain energy expression must be expressed in terms of the **Load (P, V, M, T)**, **Geometric Properties (L, A, I, J)**, and **Material Properties (E, G)** of the body. This is the standard 2-step approach examiners expect.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine stretching a **rubber band** between your fingers.

- When you pull it gently, the rubber band stretches and stores energy. When you release it, the energy is released back (this is the strain energy being recovered).
- If you stretch it too far, it does not return to its original shape (plastic region, no longer purely strain energy).
- During the stretch, *you* are doing work on the band, and the band is storing that work internally as elastic potential energy.

Similarly, in engineering structures — a steel rod under tension, a shaft under torsion, a beam under bending — the material deforms slightly under load and stores energy internally. This stored energy is **strain energy**, and the structure's ability to absorb sudden impacts (e.g., a car bumper, a spring) depends on it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Term — Resilience:** The capacity of a material to absorb strain energy within the elastic limit is called **resilience**. The maximum strain energy per unit volume that a material can absorb without permanent deformation is the **Modulus of Resilience**, equal to **σ²/(2E)** for uniaxial tension.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and properties are used throughout this module:

- **E** = Young's Modulus (Modulus of Elasticity) — units: **N/m²** or **Pa**
- **G** = Modulus of Rigidity (Shear Modulus) — units: **N/m²** or **Pa**
- **μ** or **ν** = Poisson's Ratio — dimensionless
- **1 MPa = 10⁶ N/m² = 1 N/mm²**
- **1 GPa = 10⁹ N/m²**

The relationship between E, G, and ν is:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 General Strain Energy Density Concept

Strain energy is a **scalar quantity** measured in Joules (N·m). For analysis, it is convenient to define the **strain energy per unit volume**, called the **strain energy density (u)**.

For a state of **triaxial stress** (σₓ, σᵧ, σ_z), assuming linear elastic behavior, the strain energy density is:

$$u = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_x^2 + \sigma_y^2 + \sigma_z^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x)\right]$$

For a state of **pure shear stress τ**:

$$u_{shear} = \frac{\tau^2}{2G}$$

### 2.2 Master Derivation Logic — The 4-Standard Cases

KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to know the strain energy expressions for **four canonical loading cases**:

| Case # | Loading Type | Internal Force | Governing Stress | Governing Strain | Result Expression |
|:------:|:-------------|:---------------|:-----------------|:-----------------|:------------------|
| **1** | Axial (Tension/Compression) | Axial Force **P** | σ = P/A | ε = σ/E | U = P²L / (2AE) |
| **2** | Pure Shear (Direct) | Shear Force **V** | τ = V/A | γ = τ/G | U = V²L / (2AG) |
| **3** | Pure Bending | Bending Moment **M** | σ = My/I | ε = σ/E | U = M²L / (2EI) |
| **4** | Pure Torsion | Torque **T** | τ = Tr/J | γ = τ/G | U = T²L / (2GJ) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pattern Recognition (Examiners Love This):** All four formulas have a **uniform structure** of the form:
>
> **U = (Internal Force)² × Length / (2 × Stiffness Term)**
>
> where the stiffness term is **(AE)** for axial, **(AG)** for shear, **(EI)** for bending, and **(GJ)** for torsion. The product **AE, EI, GJ** are called the **flexural/torsional rigidities** of the cross-section.

### 2.3 Generalization for Variable Loading (Distributed Loads, etc.)

For a body where the internal force varies along the length (e.g., a cantilever under a UDL), the strain energy is computed as an **integral**:

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2(x)}{2EI}\,dx \quad \text{(Bending)}$$

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{T^2(x)}{2GJ}\,dx \quad \text{(Torsion)}$$

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{P^2(x)}{2AE}\,dx \quad \text{(Axial)}$$

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{V^2(x)}{2AG}\,dx \quad \text{(Shear)}$$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Spring Design:** Strain energy in helical springs is derived directly from the torsion formula: U = T²L/(2GJ), where the wire experiences torsion.
- **Impact Loading:** The ability of a structure to absorb impact (e.g., a hammer striking a nail) is governed by the strain energy capacity.
- **Castigliano's Theorem:** The strain energy formulations above are the foundation of energy methods used to find deflections in statically indeterminate structures.
- **Structural Crashworthiness:** Automotive and aerospace engineers use strain energy to design energy-absorbing crumple zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Exhaustive Derivation Protocol:** Every algebraic transition is shown explicitly. No steps are skipped. Multi-line equations use `\begin{aligned} ... \end{aligned}` with one blank line before and after every display block.

---

### 3.1 CASE 1: Strain Energy Due to Axial Loading (Tension / Compression)

#### Setup

Consider a uniform straight bar of:
- **Length** = L
- **Cross-sectional Area** = A
- **Material:** Linear elastic with Young's Modulus = E

The bar is subjected to a gradually applied **axial tensile force P** along its centroidal axis.

#### Step 1 — Compute the Axial Stress

By the definition of normal stress:

$$\sigma = \frac{P}{A}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Axial Strain (Hooke's Law)

For linear elastic behavior, the strain is stress divided by Young's Modulus:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\sigma}{E} = \frac{P}{AE}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Total Elongation (δ)

Strain is the change in length per unit original length, so:

$$\delta = \varepsilon \times L = \frac{PL}{AE}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply the Work–Energy Principle

When the load is applied **gradually** from 0 to P, the resisting force at any instant is the instantaneous value of the load. The work done by this gradually increasing load is the area under the **load vs. elongation** diagram (a triangle in the elastic region):

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_0^\delta P' \, d\delta' = \int_0^P \frac{P'}{k}\,dP' \quad \text{where } k = \frac{AE}{L} \text{ is the axial stiffness} \\
&= \frac{1}{k} \cdot \frac{P^2}{2} = \frac{L}{AE} \cdot \frac{P^2}{2} = \frac{P^2 L}{2AE}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Final Result

$$\boxed{U_{axial} = \frac{P^2 L}{2AE} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P \cdot \delta = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot AL = \frac{\sigma^2 V}{2E}}$$

where **V = AL** is the **volume** of the bar. The term **σ²/(2E)** is the **strain energy density** under uniaxial stress.

---

### 3.2 CASE 2: Strain Energy Due to Direct (Transverse) Shear

#### Setup

Consider a small element of volume **V** subjected to a state of **pure shear stress τ** on its faces (e.g., a rivet, bolt, or pin in single shear).

#### Step 1 — Compute the Shear Stress

The average shear stress on a cross-section of area A under shear force V is:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{A}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Shear Strain

By Hooke's Law in shear:

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau}{G} = \frac{V}{AG}$$

#### Step 3 — Compute the Shear Deformation (Slip)

The relative slip (δ) across the shear plane of area A, separated by distance L, is:

$$\delta = \gamma L = \frac{VL}{AG}$$

#### Step 4 — Apply Work–Energy Principle

The shear force is applied gradually from 0 to V, so the work done is the area of the triangle in the shear force–deformation diagram:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \int_0^\delta V' \, d\delta' \\
&= \int_0^V \frac{V'}{k_s} \, dV' \quad \text{where } k_s = \frac{AG}{L} \text{ is the shear stiffness} \\
&= \frac{V^2}{2 k_s} = \frac{V^2 L}{2 A G}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 5 — Equivalent Form in Terms of Volume

Since the volume of the element is V = AL:

$$\boxed{U_{shear} = \frac{V^2 L}{2AG} = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} \cdot (AL) = \frac{\tau^2 \cdot \text{Vol}}{2G}}$$

---

### 3.3 CASE 3: Strain Energy Due to Pure Bending

#### Setup

Consider a beam segment of:
- **Length** = L
- **Flexural Rigidity** = EI (where I is the second moment of area about the neutral axis)
- Subjected to a **constant bending moment M** at its ends (pure bending, no transverse shear)

#### Step 1 — Express the Bending Stress at a Distance y from the Neutral Axis

By the Euler–Bernoulli flexure formula:

$$\sigma = \frac{M y}{I}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Strain Energy Density at Fiber y

For uniaxial stress state at fiber y:

$$u(y) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} = \frac{1}{2E}\left(\frac{My}{I}\right)^2 = \frac{M^2 y^2}{2EI^2}$$

#### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Cross-Sectional Area

Total strain energy per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dU}{dx} &= \int_A u(y) \, dA = \int_A \frac{M^2 y^2}{2 E I^2}\,dA \\
&= \frac{M^2}{2EI^2} \int_A y^2 \, dA
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, $\int_A y^2 \, dA = I$, the second moment of area. Substituting:

$$\frac{dU}{dx} = \frac{M^2}{2EI^2} \cdot I = \frac{M^2}{2EI}$$

#### Step 4 — Integrate Over the Length of the Beam

For a **constant moment M** along the length L:

$$U_{bending} = \int_0^L \frac{M^2}{2EI}\,dx = \frac{M^2}{2EI} \cdot L$$

#### Step 5 — Final Result (Constant Moment Case)

$$\boxed{U_{bending} = \frac{M^2 L}{2EI} = \frac{1}{2} M \theta \quad \text{where } \theta = \frac{ML}{EI} \text{ is the slope at the end}}$$

For a **variable moment M(x)**:

$$\boxed{U_{bending} = \int_0^L \frac{M^2(x)}{2EI}\,dx}$$

---

### 3.4 CASE 4: Strain Energy Due to Pure Torsion

#### Setup

Consider a circular shaft of:
- **Length** = L
- **Polar moment of inertia** = J (= πd⁴/32 for a solid shaft of diameter d)
- **Shear Modulus** = G
- Subjected to an **axial torque T** at its ends

#### Step 1 — Express the Shear Stress at Radius r

By the torsion formula:

$$\tau = \frac{T r}{J}$$

#### Step 2 — Compute the Strain Energy Density at Radius r

For pure shear at radius r:

$$u(r) = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{1}{2G}\left(\frac{Tr}{J}\right)^2 = \frac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2}$$

#### Step 3 — Integrate Over the Cross-Sectional Area

Total strain energy per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dU}{dx} &= \int_A u(r) \, dA = \int_A \frac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2}\,dA \\
&= \frac{T^2}{2 G J^2} \int_A r^2 \, dA
\end{aligned}
$$

By definition, $\int_A r^2 \, dA = J$, the polar second moment of area. Substituting:

$$\frac{dU}{dx} = \frac{T^2}{2GJ^2} \cdot J = \frac{T^2}{2GJ}$$

#### Step 4 — Integrate Over the Length

For a **constant torque T** along the length L:

$$U_{torsion} = \int_0^L \frac{T^2}{2GJ}\,dx = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$$

#### Step 5 — Final Result (Constant Torque Case)

$$\boxed{U_{torsion} = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ} = \frac{1}{2} T \phi \quad \text{where } \phi = \frac{TL}{GJ} \text{ is the angle of twist}}$$

For a **variable torque T(x)**:

$$\boxed{U_{torsion} = \int_0^L \frac{T^2(x)}{2GJ}\,dx}$$

---

### 3.5 Master Summary Table — All Four Cases

| Loading Type | Internal Force | Strain Energy Expression | Strain Energy Density (u) | Units of U |
|:-------------|:---------------|:-------------------------|:--------------------------|:-----------|
| **Axial** | P (N) | P²L / (2AE) | σ² / (2E) | Joules (N·m) |
| **Shear (Direct)** | V (N) | V²L / (2AG) | τ² / (2G) | Joules (N·m) |
| **Bending** | M (N·m) | M²L / (2EI) | σ²_max / (2E) where σ_max = M·c/I | Joules (N·m) |
| **Torsion** | T (N·m) | T²L / (2GJ) | τ²_max / (2G) where τ_max = T·R/J | Joules (N·m) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Strain Energy Derivation Logic

The diagram below maps the **logical flow** of strain energy derivation for the four canonical loading cases. Each case follows a unified pattern: **Load → Stress → Strain → Deformation → Work-Energy Principle → Strain Energy Expression**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Load Applied Gradually] --> B[Determine Internal Force]
    B --> B1[Axial Force P]
    B --> B2[Shear Force V]
    B --> B3[Bending Moment M]
    B --> B4[Torque T]

    B1 --> C1[Stress sigma = P / A]
    B2 --> C2[Stress tau = V / A]
    B3 --> C3[Stress sigma = M y / I]
    B4 --> C4[Stress tau = T r / J]

    C1 --> D1[Strain epsilon = sigma / E]
    C2 --> D2[Strain gamma = tau / G]
    C3 --> D3[Strain epsilon = sigma / E]
    C4 --> D4[Strain gamma = tau / G]

    D1 --> E1[Deformation delta = P L over A E]
    D2 --> E2[Deformation delta = V L over A G]
    D3 --> E3[Strain Energy Density u = sigma squared over 2 E]
    D4 --> E4[Strain Energy Density u = tau squared over 2 G]

    E1 --> F1[Work = half times P times delta]
    E2 --> F2[Work = half times V times delta]
    E3 --> F3[Integrate over Area and Length]
    E4 --> F4[Integrate over Area and Length]

    F1 --> G1[U axial = P squared L over 2 A E]
    F2 --> G2[U shear = V squared L over 2 A G]
    F3 --> G3[U bending = M squared L over 2 E I]
    F4 --> G4[U torsion = T squared L over 2 G J]

    G1 --> H[Strain Energy Stored in Elastic Deformation]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> H
```

### 4.2 Comparative Block Diagram — Strain Energy vs Load Type

This diagram classifies the four loading types by their **associated deformation mode** and the **material property** that governs the response.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LOAD_TYPES[Four Canonical Loading Types]
        L1[Axial Load P]
        L2[Shear Load V]
        L3[Bending Moment M]
        L4[Torque T]
    end

    subgraph DEFORMATION_MODE[Associated Deformation Mode]
        D1[Linear Elongation delta]
        D2[Angular Slip gamma L]
        D3[Curvature change slope]
        D4[Angle of Twist phi]
    end

    subgraph MATERIAL_PROPERTY[Governing Material Property]
        M1[Young Modulus E]
        M2[Shear Modulus G]
        M3[Young Modulus E]
        M4[Shear Modulus G]
    end

    subgraph STRAIN_ENERGY[Final Strain Energy]
        S1[U = P squared L over 2 A E]
        S2[U = V squared L over 2 A G]
        S3[U = M squared L over 2 E I]
        S4[U = T squared L over 2 G J]
    end

    L1 --> D1 --> M1 --> S1
    L2 --> D2 --> M2 --> S2
    L3 --> D3 --> M3 --> S3
    L4 --> D4 --> M4 --> S4
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Correct Strain Energy Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Identify the Type of Internal Force] --> Q1{Is the load along the axis of the member?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> AXIAL[Use U = P squared L over 2 A E]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the load causing rotation about the member axis?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> TORSION[Use U = T squared L over 2 G J]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is the load causing transverse bending?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> BENDING[Use U = M squared L over 2 E I]
    Q3 -- No --> SHEAR[Use U = V squared L over 2 A G]

    AXIAL --> DONE[Compute strain energy]
    TORSION --> DONE
    BENDING --> DONE
    SHEAR --> DONE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Node Identifier Note:** All node IDs in the diagrams above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `L1`, `D2`, `M3`, `S4`) and do not use reserved keywords. All node labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define strain energy. Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a uniform bar subjected to an axial tensile load P.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** Strain energy is the internal work done by the resisting forces of a body during elastic deformation, which is fully recoverable upon unloading. It is denoted by **U** and measured in Joules.

**Derivation (2 Marks):** Consider a uniform bar of length L, cross-sectional area A, and Young's Modulus E subjected to a gradually applied axial load P.

- Axial stress: σ = P / A
- Axial strain: ε = σ / E = P / (AE)
- Total elongation: δ = PL / (AE)
- Work done by gradually applied load:

$$U = \frac{1}{2} P \delta = \frac{1}{2} P \cdot \frac{PL}{AE}$$

$$\boxed{U = \frac{P^2 L}{2AE} = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} \cdot (AL)}$$

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State the expression for strain energy due to pure bending and due to pure torsion. Explain the terms involved.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bending (1.5 Marks):** The strain energy stored in a beam of length L, flexural rigidity EI, subjected to a constant bending moment M is:

$$U_{bending} = \frac{M^2 L}{2EI}$$

For a variable moment M(x):

$$U_{bending} = \int_0^L \frac{M^2(x)}{2EI}\,dx$$

where **E** = Young's Modulus, **I** = second moment of area about the neutral axis.

**Torsion (1.5 Marks):** The strain energy stored in a circular shaft of length L, torsional rigidity GJ, subjected to a constant torque T is:

$$U_{torsion} = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$$

For a variable torque T(x):

$$U_{torsion} = \int_0^L \frac{T^2(x)}{2GJ}\,dx$$

where **G** = Shear Modulus, **J** = polar moment of inertia of the cross-section.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the strain energy stored in a circular shaft of length L, diameter d, and shear modulus G when subjected to a torque T. Assume the torque is applied gradually. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of length **2 m** and diameter **60 mm** is subjected to a torque of **1.5 kN·m**. If the shear modulus of the material is **80 GPa**, calculate:
(i) The strain energy stored in the shaft.
(ii) The maximum shear stress induced in the shaft.
**(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a solid circular shaft of length L and diameter d, subjected to a gradually applied torque T about its longitudinal axis. Let the polar moment of inertia be:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$$

**Step 1 — Shear stress at radius r** (Torsion formula):

$$\tau = \frac{T r}{J}$$

**Step 2 — Strain energy density** (pure shear state):

$$u(r) = \frac{\tau^2}{2G} = \frac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over cross-section** to get strain energy per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dU}{dx} &= \int_A \frac{T^2 r^2}{2 G J^2}\,dA = \frac{T^2}{2 G J^2} \int_A r^2 \, dA \\
&= \frac{T^2}{2 G J^2} \cdot J = \frac{T^2}{2 G J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over length L:**

$$U_{torsion} = \int_0^L \frac{T^2}{2GJ}\,dx = \frac{T^2 L}{2GJ}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- L = 2 m
- d = 60 mm = 0.06 m
- T = 1.5 kN·m = 1500 N·m
- G = 80 GPa = 80 × 10⁹ N/m²

**Step 1 — Compute polar moment of inertia J:**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{32}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.06)^4 &= 1.296 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4 \\
J &= \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 (i) — Strain Energy:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{T^2 L}{2 G J} = \frac{(1500)^2 \times 2}{2 \times 80 \times 10^9 \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \\
U &= \frac{2.25 \times 10^6 \times 2}{203.55 \times 10^3} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^6}{2.0355 \times 10^5} \\
U &\approx 22.11 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 (ii) — Maximum Shear Stress** (at outer surface, r = d/2 = 0.03 m):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{J} = \frac{1500 \times 0.03}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \\
\tau_{max} &= \frac{45}{1.272 \times 10^{-6}} = 3.536 \times 10^7 \text{ N/m}^2 \\
\tau_{max} &\approx 35.36 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(i) U ≈ 22.11 J**
- **(ii) τ_max ≈ 35.36 MPa**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme] (ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)

**(a)** Derive the expression for the strain energy stored per unit volume in a beam subjected to a pure bending moment M. Clearly show the role of the second moment of area. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A simply supported beam of span **3 m** carries a **uniformly distributed load (UDL)** of **10 kN/m** over its entire length. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of width **100 mm** and depth **200 mm**. If the Young's Modulus of the material is **200 GPa**, determine the total strain energy stored in the beam due to bending. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

Consider a beam segment of flexural rigidity EI under constant bending moment M.

**Step 1 — Bending stress at fiber y** (Flexure formula):

$$\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$$

**Step 2 — Strain energy density** (uniaxial stress state at fiber y):

$$u(y) = \frac{\sigma^2}{2E} = \frac{M^2 y^2}{2 E I^2}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate over cross-section** to get strain energy per unit length:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dU}{dx} &= \int_A \frac{M^2 y^2}{2 E I^2}\,dA = \frac{M^2}{2 E I^2} \int_A y^2 \, dA \\
&= \frac{M^2}{2 E I^2} \cdot I = \frac{M^2}{2 E I}
\end{aligned}
$$

The **role of the second moment of area I** is critical: it appears in the denominator of the strain energy expression. A larger I (deeper, stiffer cross-section) leads to lower strain energy storage for the same applied moment.

**Step 4 — Integrate over length L** (for constant M):

$$U_{bending} = \frac{M^2 L}{2EI}$$

---

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Span L = 3 m
- UDL w = 10 kN/m = 10,000 N/m
- Cross-section: b = 100 mm = 0.1 m, d = 200 mm = 0.2 m
- E = 200 GPa = 200 × 10⁹ N/m²

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation for Simply Supported Beam with UDL:**

For a simply supported beam of length L carrying UDL w, the bending moment at a distance x from the left support is:

$$M(x) = \frac{w x}{2}(L - x) = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Second Moment of Area I** (rectangular section):

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.1 \times (0.2)^3}{12}$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
(0.2)^3 &= 8 \times 10^{-3} \\
I &= \frac{0.1 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}}{12} = \frac{8 \times 10^{-4}}{12} = 6.667 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Flexural Rigidity EI:**

$$EI = 200 \times 10^9 \times 6.667 \times 10^{-5} = 1.333 \times 10^7 \text{ N·m}^2$$

**Step 4 — Set Up the Strain Energy Integral:**

$$U = \int_0^L \frac{M^2(x)}{2EI}\,dx = \int_0^3 \frac{1}{2EI}\left[\frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2)\right]^2 dx$$

$$U = \frac{w^2}{8EI} \int_0^3 (Lx - x^2)^2 \, dx$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Integral:**

Let I_1 = ∫₀^3 (3x - x²)² dx = ∫₀^3 (9x² - 6x³ + x⁴) dx

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_1 &= \left[\frac{9x^3}{3} - \frac{6x^4}{4} + \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^3 \\
&= \left[3x^3 - \frac{3x^4}{2} + \frac{x^5}{5}\right]_0^3 \\
&= 3(27) - \frac{3(81)}{2} + \frac{243}{5} \\
&= 81 - 121.5 + 48.6 = 8.1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute All Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{(10000)^2 \times 8.1}{8 \times 1.333 \times 10^7} \\
U &= \frac{10^8 \times 8.1}{1.0664 \times 10^8} \\
U &= \frac{8.1 \times 10^8}{1.0664 \times 10^8} \\
U &\approx 7.596 \text{ J}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer: U ≈ 7.60 J**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Missing the ½ factor:** Students often write U = P·δ instead of U = (1/2)·P·δ. This costs 1 full mark. The factor **½** arises because the load is applied **gradually**, not suddenly.
> 2. **Confusing E and G:** Axial/bending use **E**; shear/torsion use **G**. Mixing them up is the #1 most common error.
> 3. **Confusing I and J:** Bending uses **I** (second moment of area); torsion uses **J** (polar second moment of area). For a circular section, J = 2I.
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Always convert mm to m, kN to N, MPa to Pa (×10⁶), GPa to Pa (×10⁹) before calculation. Wrong unit conversion is a guaranteed 1-mark penalty.
> 5. **Forgetting integration for variable loading:** If the bending moment is not constant (e.g., UDL case), integration is mandatory. Writing just M²L/(2EI) without integration for a variable moment is a fatal error.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Strain Energy Definition:** Internal work done during elastic deformation; recoverable; measured in Joules.
- **Axial:** U = P²L / (2AE) — uses E
- **Shear (Direct):** U = V²L / (2AG) — uses G
- **Bending:** U = ∫M²(x)/(2EI) dx — uses E and I
- **Torsion:** U = ∫T²(x)/(2GJ) dx — uses G and J
- **Universal Pattern:** U = (Internal Force)² × Length / (2 × Rigidity)
- **Strain Energy Density:** u = σ²/(2E) for uniaxial, u = τ²/(2G) for pure shear
- **Constant vs Variable Loading:** Use the integral form whenever the internal force varies along the length.
- **Factor of ½:** Always present because the load is **gradually** applied (resistive force grows from 0 to P).
- **Geometric Properties:**
  - I (rectangle) = bd³/12
  - I (circle) = πd⁴/64
  - J (solid circle) = πd⁴/32
  - J (hollow circle) = (π/32)(D⁴ - d⁴)
- **Material Properties:** E (Young's) for axial/bending, G (Shear) for shear/torsion. G = E / [2(1+ν)].
- **Strain Energy vs Resilience:** Resilience is strain energy **per unit volume** within the elastic limit — equal to σ_y²/(2E) for uniaxial tension.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Deformation of Circular Shafts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsional Deformation of Circular Shafts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Torsion** is the deformation behaviour exhibited by a structural or mechanical member (most commonly a **circular shaft**) when it is subjected to a twisting moment about its longitudinal centroidal axis. The applied moment is termed a **Torque (T)**, which produces two primary internal effects:

1. **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** – distributed across the cross-section.
2. **Angle of Twist ($\theta$)** – the relative rotation of one cross-section with respect to another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *Torsion of a circular shaft – Derivation of torsion equation, polar modulus of section, combined bending and torsion, power transmitted by a shaft, and design of solid and hollow shafts based on strength and stiffness criteria.*

For a circular shaft of radius $R$ subjected to torque $T$:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

where $J$ is the **polar moment of inertia**, $G$ is the **modulus of rigidity (Shear Modulus)**, $\theta$ is the angle of twist in radians, and $L$ is the length of the shaft segment under consideration.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

**Think of a wet towel being wrung out:** Your two hands apply equal and opposite twisting actions, and the fibres of the towel experience a "shearing" of layers relative to one another. A circular shaft behaves identically — fibres along its length slide past each other while the cross-section rotates.

**Engineering Examples:**
- **Driveshaft of an automobile** – transmits engine torque to the differential.
- **Drill bit and chuck assembly** – hand torque at the chuck is transmitted to the cutting edge.
- **Propeller shaft of a ship** – transfers rotational power from the marine engine.

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 scheme focuses exclusively on **circular cross-sections** (solid and hollow). Non-circular sections exhibit warping and fall under Saint-Venant's torsion theory, which is **out of syllabus**.

---

### 1.3 Fundamental Assumptions for Torsion Formula

| # | Assumption | Engineering Rationale |
|---|-----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Shaft material is **homogeneous and isotropic** | Same $G$ at every point |
| 2 | Cross-section remains **plane and circular** after twisting | No warping |
| 3 | **Radius** of shaft remains **constant** | Lengths of fibres do not change |
| 4 | **Saint-Venant's principle** holds near the ends | Stress distribution is uniform away from loading points |
| 5 | Twist is **pure and uniform** along the length | Linear $\theta$ vs $L$ behaviour |

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** for Mild Steel $\approx \mathbf{80 \text{ GPa}}$
- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** for Cast Iron $\approx \mathbf{45 \text{ GPa}}$
- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** for Aluminium $\approx \mathbf{27 \text{ GPa}}$
- **Allowable Shear Stress ($\tau_{allow}$)** for Mild Steel $\approx \mathbf{55 \text{ MPa}}$
- **Power ($P$)** in kW: $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$, where $N$ is rpm and $T$ is in N·m
- **Power ($P$)** in HP: $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 745.7}$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Torsion

The deformation under torque follows a **step-wise logical cascade**:

- **Step 1 – Geometry Setup:** A circular shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$ is fixed at one end and a torque $T$ is applied at the free end.
- **Step 2 – Internal Reaction:** An internal resisting torque of magnitude $T$ develops on every cross-section to maintain equilibrium.
- **Step 3 – Kinematics:** The free face rotates by an angle $\theta$ about the longitudinal axis. A point on the surface (at radius $R$) moves through an arc length $s = R\theta$.
- **Step 4 – Shear Strain ($\phi$):** For a small element of length $L$ on the surface, the shear strain is $\phi = \dfrac{R\theta}{L}$.
- **Step 5 – Constitutive Relation (Hooke's Law in Shear):** $\tau = G\phi = \dfrac{GR\theta}{L}$.
- **Step 6 – Equilibrium & Generalisation:** The shear stress varies **linearly** from zero at the centre to a maximum at the outer surface: $\tau = \dfrac{T \cdot r}{J}$.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise this table verbatim. It is the **primary toolkit** for every Part B torsion problem in KTU University Examinations.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | **Torsion Equation** | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Dimensionless ratio | Universal torsion formula |
| 2 | **Maximum Shear Stress** | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}$ (solid) | $\text{N/m}^{2}$ or Pa | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ |
| 3 | **Maximum Shear Stress** (hollow) | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T D}{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}$ | Pa | $D$ = outer, $d$ = inner dia |
| 4 | **Polar Modulus ($Z_{p}$)** | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{J}{R} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$ | $\text{m}^{3}$ | Used for strength design |
| 5 | **Angle of Twist** | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ | rad | Total twist in length $L$ |
| 6 | **Angle of Twist per unit length** | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{GJ}$ | rad/m | Used for stiffness design |
| 7 | **Power Transmitted** | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | W | $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |
| 8 | **Polar Moment (Solid)** | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ | $\text{m}^{4}$ | Full circular section |
| 9 | **Polar Moment (Hollow)** | $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$ | $\text{m}^{4}$ | Annular section |
| 10 | **Stiffness Criterion** | $\dfrac{\theta}{L} \leq \dfrac{\theta_{allow}}{L}$ | rad/m | Restricts twist rate |
| 11 | **Composite Shaft Equivalence** | $\dfrac{T_{1}}{G_{1}J_{1}} = \dfrac{T_{2}}{G_{2}J_{2}}$ | rad | Equal angle of twist |
| 12 | **Shear Strain** | $\phi = \dfrac{R\theta}{L}$ | rad | At outer surface |

---

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application |
|-------|------------|
| **Automotive** | Drive shafts, axles, transmission countershafts |
| **Aerospace** | Helicopter rotor masts, turboprop drive shafts |
| **Marine** | Propeller shafts, marine gear boxes |
| **Civil/Structural** | Torsion of reinforced concrete beams under eccentric loading |
| **Machine Design** | Couplings, bolts, springs, drill bits, lathe spindles |
| **Power Plants** | Generator shafts, turbine rotors |

---

### 2.4 Conditions of Failure / Design Criteria

> [!NOTE]
> KTU problems frequently test **two design criteria simultaneously**:
> 1. **Strength Criterion:** $\tau_{max} \leq \tau_{allow}$ → determines diameter from strength
> 2. **Stiffness Criterion:** $\theta/L \leq (\theta/L)_{allow}$ → determines diameter from stiffness
> The **larger** of the two diameters is chosen for safe design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Formula

Consider a circular shaft of radius $R$ and length $L$, fixed at one end and subjected to a torque $T$ at the free end.

**Step 1 — Kinematics of Twisting**

Let the free end rotate through a small angle $\theta$ (radians). A longitudinal fibre on the surface (originally vertical) tilts and traces an arc.

The arc length swept by a point on the surface is:

$$s = R \cdot \theta$$

**Step 2 — Shear Strain in the Surface Fibre**

The fibre of original length $L$ now has its top displaced by $s = R\theta$ relative to the bottom. The shear strain $\phi$ in this fibre is:

$$\phi = \frac{s}{L} = \frac{R\theta}{L}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law in Shear**

Shear stress is related to shear strain through the **Modulus of Rigidity** $G$:

$$\tau = G \cdot \phi = \frac{G \cdot R \cdot \theta}{L}$$

This is the shear stress at the **outer surface** (radius $R$).

**Step 4 — Equilibrium of the Cross-Section**

For a small annular element at radius $r$ of thickness $dr$, the area is $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The shear stress on this element is $\tau_{r} = \dfrac{G \cdot r \cdot \theta}{L}$ (by similarity, the strain scales linearly with $r$).

The torque contribution of this elemental ring is:

$$dT = \tau_{r} \cdot r \cdot dA = \left(\frac{G r \theta}{L}\right) \cdot r \cdot (2\pi r \, dr) = \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr$$

**Step 5 — Integrate Across the Cross-Section**

The total internal torque is:

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= \int_{0}^{R} \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \left[ \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\pi G \theta}{L} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\pi G \theta R^{4}}{2L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Introduce the Polar Moment of Inertia**

The polar moment of inertia for a solid circular section is:

$$J = \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (2R)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}$$

Substituting back:

$$T = \frac{G \theta J}{L}$$

**Step 7 — Assemble the Universal Torsion Equation**

From Step 3: $\dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$, and from Step 6: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.

Equating the common ratio $\dfrac{G\theta}{L}$:

$$\boxed{\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{R} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}}$$

This is the **fundamental torsion equation** tested in every KTU exam.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Polar Moment of Inertia for Hollow Shaft

For a hollow circular shaft with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= J_{outer} - J_{inner} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} - \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}
\end{aligned}$$

The polar modulus becomes:

$$Z_{p} = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16 D}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Power Transmission Equation

A torque $T$ applied at a rotational speed of $N$ revolutions per minute does work per revolution equal to $W_{rev} = 2\pi T$ joules.

The number of revolutions per second is $n = \dfrac{N}{60}$.

Therefore, the power (work per second) is:

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= W_{rev} \cdot n = 2\pi T \cdot \frac{N}{60} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \text{ watts}
\end{aligned}$$

For $P$ in kW:

$$P_{\text{kW}} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$$

For $P$ in horsepower (HP), since $1 \text{ HP} = 745.7 \text{ W}$:

$$P_{\text{HP}} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 745.7}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Composite Shaft Compatibility

When two materials (or two different shaft sections) are rigidly connected in series, the same torque flows through both. The angle of twist must be equal at the junction (compatibility of deformation).

For segments 1 and 2:

$$\theta_{1} = \theta_{2} \implies \frac{T L_{1}}{G_{1} J_{1}} = \frac{T L_{2}}{G_{2} J_{2}}$$

If the **same torque** flows through both:

$$\frac{L_{1}}{G_{1} J_{1}} = \frac{L_{2}}{G_{2} J_{2}}$$

If the segments are in **parallel** (carrying different torques $T_{1}, T_{2}$ that sum to the applied $T$), and must rotate by the **same angle**:

$$T_{1} = T \cdot \frac{G_{1} J_{1}}{G_{1} J_{1} + G_{2} J_{2}}, \quad T_{2} = T \cdot \frac{G_{2} J_{2}}{G_{1} J_{1} + G_{2} J_{2}}$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Problem-Solving Pathway

| Step | Action | Governing Equation |
|------|--------|--------------------|
| 1 | Read problem; identify $T$, $D$, $L$, $G$, $N$ | — |
| 2 | Compute $J$ from $D$ (and $d$ if hollow) | $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$ |
| 3 | Compute $Z_{p}$ | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$ |
| 4 | For **strength** problems, find $\tau_{max}$ | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_{p}}$ |
| 5 | For **stiffness** problems, find $\theta$ | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ |
| 6 | For **power** problems, find $P$ | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ |
| 7 | Check against allowable values; solve for unknown | — |
| 8 | Report with units | — |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Torsion Loading

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Torque T Applied] --> B[Internal Resisting Torque on Cross-Section]
    B --> C[Shear Stress Distribution: Linear with r]
    C --> D[Max Shear Stress at Outer Surface r = R]
    B --> E[Rotation of Cross-Section: Angle theta]
    E --> F[Angle of Twist per unit length: theta per L]
    C --> G[Strength Check: tau max leq tau allow]
    F --> H[Stiffness Check: theta per L leq allow]
    G --> I[Design Diameter D1]
    H --> J[Design Diameter D2]
    I --> K[Final Safe Diameter D = max of D1, D2]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Shaft Safe Under Torsion]
```

---

### 4.2 Free Body and Cross-Section Visualisation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SB1[Boundary Conditions]
        F[Fixed Wall] --> SH[Shaft of length L and diameter D]
    end
    SH --> AP[Applied Torque T at Free End]
    AP --> CW[Cross-section rotates by angle theta]
    SH --> CS1[Cross-Section A-A at distance x]
    CS1 --> STR[Shear Stress tau varies from 0 at centre to tau max at radius R]
    CS1 --> AR[Arc Length s = R times theta]
    SH --> POW[Power P = 2 pi N T divided by 60]
```

---

### 4.3 Shear Stress Distribution Mapping (Functional Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C[Centre of Shaft] -->|tau equals 0| N1[Neutral Axis at r = 0]
    N1 --> Q1[Inner Radius d for Hollow Shaft]
    Q1 --> Q2[Linear Variation of tau in Annular Zone]
    Q2 --> Q3[Outer Radius D for Hollow Shaft]
```
