```mermaid
    Q3 --> Q4[Solid Shaft: tau max at r = R]
    Q4 --> M[tau max equals 16 T divided by pi D cubed for solid]
    Q4 --> N[tau max equals 16 T D divided by pi D to the 4 minus d to the 4 for hollow]
```

---

### 4.4 Series vs Parallel Composite Shaft Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Composite Shaft System] --> B[Series Configuration]
    A --> C[Parallel Configuration]
    B --> B1[Segment 1: Material 1, Length L1]
    B1 --> B2[Joint with Compatibility: theta1 = theta2]
    B2 --> B3[Segment 2: Material 2, Length L2]
    B3 --> B4[Total Angle: theta total = theta1 + theta2]
    C --> C1[Shaft 1: Material 1, Stiffness G1J1]
    C1 --> C2[Common Junction: theta common]
    C2 --> C3[Shaft 2: Material 2, Stiffness G2J2]
    C3 --> C4[Total Torque: T total = T1 + T2]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual reading guide:** In the **series** diagram, both segments share the **same torque** but twists add up. In the **parallel** diagram, both segments share the **same angle of twist** but torques distribute by stiffness ratio.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State the torsion equation and explain the physical significance of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

The torsion equation for a circular shaft is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{R} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

- $T$ — Applied torque (N·m) producing internal twist.
- $J$ — Polar moment of inertia ($\text{m}^{4}$); geometric resistance of the cross-section to twisting.
- $\tau$ — Shear stress (Pa) at radius $r$.
- $R$ — Outer radius (m) of the shaft cross-section.
- $G$ — Modulus of rigidity (Pa); material property resisting shear deformation.
- $\theta$ — Angle of twist (rad) over length $L$.
- $L$ — Length (m) of the shaft over which twist is measured.

> The equation indicates that shear stress is **linearly proportional** to the radius, and the angle of twist is **linearly proportional** to the applied torque and length.

---

#### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define polar modulus. How is it useful in the design of shafts?**

**Model Answer:**

**Polar Modulus ($Z_{p}$)** is defined as the ratio of the polar moment of inertia $J$ to the radius of the shaft $R$:

$$Z_{p} = \frac{J}{R}$$

For a solid circular shaft:

$$Z_{p} = \frac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$$

**Utility in Design:**
The polar modulus allows direct calculation of the maximum shear stress:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_{p}}$$

This is the **strength criterion** used to determine the minimum required shaft diameter for a given torque and allowable shear stress. It is the torsional analogue of the section modulus used in bending.

---

### 5.2 Part B — University Exam Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

A solid circular shaft of diameter $80 \text{ mm}$ transmits $90 \text{ kW}$ at $300 \text{ rpm}$. The allowable shear stress is $60 \text{ MPa}$ and the maximum permissible twist per metre length is $1^{\circ}$. Take $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$.

**(a)** Determine the maximum torque transmitted. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Check whether the shaft is safe under strength and stiffness criteria. If not, find a suitable diameter. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Maximum Torque Transmitted**

Given: $P = 90 \text{ kW} = 90{,}000 \text{ W}$, $N = 300 \text{ rpm}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P &= \frac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000} \quad \text{(since P is in kW)} \\[6pt]
90 &= \frac{2\pi \times 300 \times T}{60{,}000} \\[6pt]
T &= \frac{90 \times 60{,}000}{2\pi \times 300} \\[6pt]
T &= \frac{5{,}400{,}000}{1884.96} \\[6pt]
T &= 2864.79 \text{ N·m}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{T = 2.865 \text{ kN·m}}$$

---

**Part (b) — Strength and Stiffness Check**

Given: $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.08 \text{ m}$, $\tau_{allow} = 60 \text{ MPa}$, $(\theta/L)_{allow} = 1^{\circ}/\text{m} = \dfrac{\pi}{180} \text{ rad/m} = 0.01745 \text{ rad/m}$, $G = 80 \text{ GPa} = 80 \times 10^{9} \text{ Pa}$.

**Strength Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{max} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi D^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{16 \times 2864.79}{\pi \times (0.08)^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{45{,}836.6}{1.6085 \times 10^{-3}} \\[6pt]
&= 28.50 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 28.50 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

Since $28.50 \text{ MPa} < 60 \text{ MPa}$, **shaft is safe in strength**.

**Stiffness Check:**

$$\begin{aligned}
J &= \frac{\pi D^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (0.08)^{4}}{32} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 4.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{\theta}{L} &= \frac{T}{G J} = \frac{2864.79}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 4.021 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2864.79}{321{,}680} \\[6pt]
&= 8.905 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to degrees: $8.905 \times 10^{-3} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 0.5102^{\circ}/\text{m}$

Since $0.5102^{\circ}/\text{m} < 1^{\circ}/\text{m}$, **shaft is safe in stiffness**.

**Conclusion:** The shaft is safe in **both strength and stiffness** for the given loading. No redesign needed.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

A composite shaft consists of a steel section of diameter $60 \text{ mm}$ and length $1.2 \text{ m}$ rigidly joined to a brass section of diameter $40 \text{ mm}$ and length $0.8 \text{ m}$. A torque of $1.5 \text{ kN·m}$ is applied at the free brass end. The fixed end is at the steel section. Take $G_{steel} = 80 \text{ GPa}$ and $G_{brass} = 40 \text{ GPa}$.

**(a)** Determine the maximum shear stress in each section and state which is critical. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the total angle of twist of the free end. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Shear Stress in Each Section**

Given: $T = 1.5 \text{ kN·m} = 1500 \text{ N·m}$, $D_{steel} = 0.06 \text{ m}$, $D_{brass} = 0.04 \text{ m}$.

Since the same torque flows through both sections (series configuration), $T_{steel} = T_{brass} = 1500 \text{ N·m}$.

**Steel Section:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{steel} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi D_{steel}^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 1500}{\pi \times (0.06)^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{24{,}000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
&= 35.37 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 35.37 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Brass Section:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau_{brass} &= \frac{16 T}{\pi D_{brass}^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 1500}{\pi \times (0.04)^{3}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{24{,}000}{2.011 \times 10^{-4}} \\[6pt]
&= 119.36 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} = 119.36 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}$$

**Critical Section:** **Brass section** with $\tau_{max} = 119.36 \text{ MPa}$.

---

**Part (b) — Total Angle of Twist**

**Polar Moments of Inertia:**

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{steel} &= \frac{\pi (0.06)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 1.296 \times 10^{-5}}{32} = 1.272 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4} \\[6pt]
J_{brass} &= \frac{\pi (0.04)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56 \times 10^{-6}}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^{4}
\end{aligned}$$

**Angle of Twist in Steel:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{steel} &= \frac{T L_{steel}}{G_{steel} J_{steel}} = \frac{1500 \times 1.2}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 1.272 \times 10^{-6}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1800}{101{,}788} = 0.01768 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Angle of Twist in Brass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{brass} &= \frac{T L_{brass}}{G_{brass} J_{brass}} = \frac{1500 \times 0.8}{40 \times 10^{9} \times 2.513 \times 10^{-7}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{1200}{10{,}053} = 0.11937 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

**Total Angle of Twist:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta_{total} &= \theta_{steel} + \theta_{brass} \\[6pt]
&= 0.01768 + 0.11937 \\[6pt]
&= 0.13705 \text{ rad}
\end{aligned}$$

Converting to degrees: $0.13705 \times \dfrac{180}{\pi} = 7.85^{\circ}$

$$\boxed{\theta_{total} = 0.1371 \text{ rad} = 7.85^{\circ}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing $D$ in mm with $G$ in Pa causes a $10^{3}$ error. Always convert $D$ to **metres** before substituting.
> 2. **Power Unit Confusion:** KTU uses both kW and HP. If $P$ is in kW, use $P = 2\pi NT / 60{,}000$. If in W, use $P = 2\pi NT / 60$.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\pi$ in $J$:** Writing $J = D^{4}/32$ instead of $J = \pi D^{4}/32$ is a **1-mark deduction** that propagates to all subsequent values.
> 4. **Degree vs Radian:** The torsion equation requires $\theta$ in **radians**. Always convert $(\theta/L)_{allow}$ from degrees to radians.
> 5. **Composite Shaft Direction:** Students often forget that for **series** shafts, the **same torque** flows; for **parallel** shafts, the **same angle** rotates. This is a **2-mark** conceptual error.
> 6. **Strength vs Stiffness Diameter:** The final design diameter is the **larger** of the two; choosing the smaller is a critical safety flaw worth **2-3 marks**.
> 7. **Sign of Torque:** Always show sign conventions in the FBD (e.g., using right-hand rule). KTU examiners award **1 mark** for a clear, labelled diagram.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorise for KTU 2024 Exam:**

- **Torsion Equation (Heart of the Topic):** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$
- **Polar Moment for Solid:** $J = \dfrac{\pi D^{4}}{32}$
- **Polar Moment for Hollow:** $J = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$
- **Polar Modulus (Solid):** $Z_{p} = \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}$
- **Polar Modulus (Hollow):** $Z_{p} = \dfrac{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}{16 D}$
- **Maximum Shear Stress (Solid):** $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi D^{3}}$
- **Angle of Twist:** $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ (in **radians**)
- **Power (Watts):** $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$
- **Power (kW):** $P_{\text{kW}} = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60{,}000}$
- **Stiffness Criterion:** $\dfrac{\theta}{L} \leq \left(\dfrac{\theta}{L}\right)_{allow}$
- **Series Composite:** Same $T$, angles add
- **Parallel Composite:** Same $\theta$, torques distribute by $G_{i}J_{i}$ ratio
- **Material Property:** $G = \dfrac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$ — not in syllabus but useful for verification
- **Typical $G$ Values:** Steel $\approx 80$ GPa, Brass $\approx 40$ GPa, Cast Iron $\approx 45$ GPa, Aluminium $\approx 27$ GPa
- **Always:** Convert $D$ to metres, $\theta$ to radians, and use SI units throughout
- **Hollow vs Solid:** Hollow shafts are more efficient — for equal weight, they transmit **more** torque and resist twist **more** effectively
- **Saint-Venant's Principle:** Stress distribution is uniform only **far from the points of application of torque**; near the ends, results must be applied cautiously
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Assumptions for Shafts Subjected to Torsion Within Elastic Deformation Range
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Assumptions for Shafts Subjected to Torsion Within Elastic Deformation Range

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Theory of Torsion (Coulomb's Classical Theory)**, when a circular shaft is subjected to a twisting moment (torque) and the deformation remains within the **elastic limit** of the material, the analysis is performed under a specific set of idealised conditions. These conditions are termed the **Assumptions for Shafts Subjected to Torsion Within Elastic Deformation Range**.

These assumptions collectively form the foundation for deriving the **Torsion Equation**:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> These assumptions are *not* optional. If any one of them is violated (for example, if the material yields, or if the cross-section is non-circular, or if warping is restrained), the simple torsion equation above becomes **invalid** and advanced theories (plastic torsion, non-circular cross-section torsion, restrained warping theory) must be applied instead.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Wet Towel Twist

Imagine you are standing in a bathroom holding a **wet towel** with both hands, palms facing each other, and you twist your hands in opposite directions to wring water out of it.

- The towel represents the **shaft**.
- Your hands twisting in opposite directions represent the **applied torque** $T$.
- The fibres of the towel (running longitudinally) that were originally straight now form a **helix**, and the cross-section that was a flat circle remains a flat circle — only rotated. This is exactly the behaviour described by the assumption **"plane sections remain plane"**.
- If you twist **gently** (elastic range), the towel returns to its original shape when you release. If you twist **violently** (plastic range), the towel stays deformed.
- The *wettest* part (water being squeezed) is the *outer surface* of the towel, and the *driest* part is the *centre* — analogous to the shear stress being **maximum at the outer surface** and **zero at the centre axis**.

### 1.3 The Complete List of Assumptions (KTU Board-Standard)

> [!NOTE]
> The following ten assumptions collectively define the **elastic torsion of a circular shaft**. Examiners frequently test these point-by-point.

1. **Material Homogeneity and Isotropy** — The shaft material is *homogeneous* (same properties at every point) and *isotropic* (same properties in all directions at any point).
2. **Hookean (Perfectly Elastic) Behaviour** — The material obeys **Hooke's Law in shear**: $\tau = G\gamma$, where $G$ is the *Shear Modulus* / *Modulus of Rigidity* (typically **80 GPa for steel**, **27 GPa for aluminium**, **44 GPa for copper**).
3. **Uniform Circular Cross-Section** — The shaft has a *constant* circular cross-section (either solid or hollow) along its entire length.
4. **Shaft is Initially Straight** — The longitudinal axis is a *perfectly straight line* in the unstressed (unloaded) state.
5. **Plane Sections Remain Plane and Perpendicular to the Axis** — Cross-sections that are plane and normal to the axis *before* twisting remain plane and normal to the axis *after* twisting. This is also called the **"no warping" assumption** in its strict form.
6. **Radii Remain Straight, Radial, and Unchanged in Length** — Every line drawn from the axis to the surface (a radius) remains a straight line, remains radial, and its length does not change. Only its angular position rotates.
7. **Stress is Pure Shear** — The only stress induced in the shaft is *shear stress* $\tau$. No normal (tensile or compressive) stress is developed on the cross-section. This is the **state of pure shear**.
8. **Stress Varies Linearly with Radius** — Shear stress $\tau$ varies *linearly* from **zero at the centre** to **maximum at the outer surface**. That is, $\tau \propto r$.
9. **Deformation is Uniform** — Either the applied torque is *constant* along the length, or it varies *gradually* (no abrupt changes), so that Saint-Venant's principle is satisfied and end effects are negligible.
10. **Twist Angle is Small (Geometric Linearity)** — The angle of twist $\theta$ is *small* in radians. For typical engineering shafts, $\theta$ in degrees is well under **5°**, so that $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ (radians).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Each Assumption Matters — The "Why and How"

| # | Assumption | Engineering Reason ("Why") | Mathematical Consequence ("How") |
|---|------------|----------------------------|----------------------------------|
| 1 | Homogeneous & Isotropic | Real engineering metals (steel, brass) are approximately so in the elastic range. | Material constants $E$, $G$, $\nu$ are **scalars**, not tensors. |
| 2 | Hooke's Law in shear | The elastic range is defined by this linear relationship. | Permits $\tau = G\gamma$ to be used directly. |
| 3 | Uniform circular cross-section | Simplifies $J = \int r^{2} dA$ into a closed form $\frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$. | Polar moment of inertia $J$ is a *constant* along the length. |
| 4 | Initially straight shaft | Ensures no *initial curvature stresses* exist. | Shaft reference geometry is trivial. |
| 5 | Plane sections remain plane | Adopted from bending theory (Bernoulli-Euler). | Distortion / warping function $w(x,y,z) = 0$. |
| 6 | Radii remain straight & radial | The cross-section **rotates as a rigid disc** about the axis. | Cross-section rotates by angle $\theta$ about the axis like a rigid body. |
| 7 | Pure shear stress state | The applied torque is a *pure couple*; equilibrium requires only shear. | Principal stresses appear at $\pm 45^{\circ}$ to the axis. |
| 8 | Linear stress distribution | A direct consequence of the rigid rotation of the cross-section. | $\tau = \frac{T r}{J}$ (linear in $r$). |
| 9 | Uniform / gradual torque | Validates the use of Saint-Venant's principle at the ends. | Torsion equation valid at all points except very near the ends. |
| 10 | Small twist angle | Permits linearisation of trigonometric functions. | $\tan\theta \approx \theta \approx \sin\theta$ (radians). |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Connection with Other Modules:**
> Assumption 7 (pure shear) leads to the result that principal stresses act at $45^{\circ}$ to the shaft axis, with magnitudes $\sigma_{1,2} = +\tau$ and $\sigma_{3} = -\tau$. This is the basis for **combined loading and failure theories** (Maximum Shear Stress, Distortion Energy) studied in higher semesters.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units |
|---|----------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Torsion Equation (Master Equation)** | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | $T$ = torque (N·m), $J$ = polar moment of inertia (m⁴ or mm⁴), $\tau$ = shear stress (Pa or N/m² or MPa), $r$ = radius (m or mm), $G$ = shear modulus (Pa or N/m² or MPa), $\theta$ = angle of twist (radians), $L$ = length (m or mm) |
| 2 | Polar moment of inertia — **Solid shaft** | $J_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$ | $d$ = diameter |
| 3 | Polar moment of inertia — **Hollow shaft** | $J_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}{32}$ | $D$ = outer diameter, $d$ = inner diameter |
| 4 | Maximum shear stress (solid) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$ | Occurs at the outer surface ($r = d/2$) |
| 5 | Maximum shear stress (hollow) | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T D}{\pi \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}$ | Occurs at the outer surface ($r = D/2$) |
| 6 | Angle of twist (radians) | $\theta = \dfrac{T L}{G J}$ | Pure torsion, constant $T$ |
| 7 | Angle of twist (degrees) | $\theta^{\circ} = \dfrac{T L}{G J} \times \dfrac{180}{\pi}$ | Conversion to degrees |
| 8 | Shear strain at radius $r$ | $\gamma = \dfrac{\tau}{G} = \dfrac{r \theta}{L}$ | Hooke's law in shear |
| 9 | Strain energy stored in torsion | $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J} = \dfrac{1}{2} T \theta$ | $U$ in Joules (N·m) |
| 10 | Power transmitted by shaft | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | $P$ in W, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unit Consistency Rule (KTU Valuation Key):**
> Always work in a *single consistent unit system*. Common KTU-acceptable combinations:
> - **SI:** $T$ in N·m, $d, D, L$ in **m**, $G$ in **Pa (= N/m²)** → stress in **Pa**, $J$ in **m⁴**.
> - **Mixed (Engineering):** $T$ in N·m or N·mm, $d, D, L$ in **mm**, $G$ in **N/mm² (= MPa)** → stress in **MPa**, $J$ in **mm⁴**.
> Mixing units within a single problem is the **single most common mark-losing mistake**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Drive Shafts:** The drive shaft of a car transmitting power from the gearbox to the differential is designed using these exact assumptions. Knowing the engine torque and the permissible shear stress of steel, the required diameter is computed from $\tau_{\max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$.
- **Turbo-Generator Shafts:** In power plants, turbine shafts running at 3000 rpm are designed for elastic torsion to ensure that transient overloads do not cause permanent twist.
- **Drill Bits and Boring Bars:** Long slender shafts in machine tools experience torsional vibration; the elastic-torsion theory is the first step in the vibration analysis.
- **Cardiovascular Stents and Orthopaedic Screws:** Even biomedical implants use miniature versions of the torsion equation (modified for biocompatible alloys like Ti-6Al-4V).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Torsion Equation from the Assumptions

The following derivation shows *how* the assumptions stated in §1.3 are used mathematically to arrive at the **Master Torsion Equation**.

#### Step 1 — Consider an elemental length of the shaft

Take a small element of the shaft of length $\mathrm{d}x$ (or $\mathrm{d}L$) cut between two cross-sections. Let the cross-section at the right end rotate through an additional angle $\mathrm{d}\theta$ relative to the left end.

#### Step 2 — Geometry of deformation of a fibre

Consider a fibre on the outer surface of the shaft, originally aligned with the axis (length = $\mathrm{d}x$). After twisting:

- The bottom of the fibre stays on the original axis.
- The top of the fibre moves tangentially by an arc length $r \cdot \mathrm{d}\theta$, where $r$ is the radius at which the fibre lies.
- The fibre, originally vertical, now tilts and has a new length $\sqrt{(\mathrm{d}x)^{2} + (r\,\mathrm{d}\theta)^{2}}$.

#### Step 3 — Shear strain from the geometry

The shear strain $\gamma$ is the angle (in radians) by which the originally vertical fibre has tilted. For small deformations:

$$\gamma = \lim_{\mathrm{d}\theta \to 0} \frac{r\,\mathrm{d}\theta}{\mathrm{d}x} = \frac{r\,\mathrm{d}\theta}{\mathrm{d}x}$$

If the twist is uniform, $\dfrac{\mathrm{d}\theta}{\mathrm{d}x} = \dfrac{\theta}{L}$, and therefore:

$$\gamma = \frac{r\theta}{L}$$

This is the **geometric relation** — derived directly from the assumption that the cross-section rotates as a rigid disc (Assumption 6).

#### Step 4 — Hooke's Law in shear (Assumption 2)

Within the elastic range, the material obeys:

$$\tau = G\gamma$$

Substituting the value of $\gamma$ from Step 3:

$$\tau = G \cdot \frac{r\theta}{L} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

This is the **second equality** of the torsion equation, obtained purely from geometry + Hooke's law.

#### Step 5 — Static equilibrium of the cross-section

The applied torque $T$ is resisted by the distributed shear stresses $\tau$ acting on the cross-sectional area $A$. Each elemental ring of area $\mathrm{d}A$ at radius $r$ contributes a resisting moment $\tau \cdot \mathrm{d}A \cdot r$. For equilibrium:

$$T = \int_A \tau \cdot r \, \mathrm{d}A$$

#### Step 6 — Substitute the linear stress law

From Step 4, $\tau = \dfrac{G\theta}{L} \cdot r$, so the integrand becomes:

$$\tau \cdot r \, \mathrm{d}A = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}A$$

Therefore:

$$T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \int_A r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}A$$

#### Step 7 — Definition of the polar moment of inertia

By definition:

$$J = \int_A r^{2} \, \mathrm{d}A$$

Substituting:

$$T = \frac{G\theta}{L} \cdot J \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

#### Step 8 — Combine the kinematic and static results

From Step 4: $\dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.

From Step 7: $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.

Both equal $\dfrac{G\theta}{L}$, hence:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **Master Torsion Equation** is the algebraic distillation of all ten assumptions. It is the single most-asked formula in KTU Mechanics of Solids.

### 3.2 Closed-Form Evaluation of $J$ for a Solid Circular Shaft

$$J_{\text{solid}} = \int_A r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}A = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{R} r^{2} \cdot r\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\phi = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\phi$$

Evaluate the inner integral:

$$\int_{0}^{R} r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r = \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{R^{4}}{4}$$

Evaluate the outer integral:

$$\int_{0}^{2\pi} \frac{R^{4}}{4}\,\mathrm{d}\phi = \frac{R^{4}}{4} \cdot 2\pi = \frac{\pi R^{4}}{2}$$

Convert radius to diameter using $R = d/2$:

$$J_{\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi}{2} \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{4} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$

### 3.3 Closed-Form Evaluation of $J$ for a Hollow Circular Shaft

For a hollow shaft with outer radius $R$ and inner radius $R_i$:

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \int_{0}^{2\pi}\!\!\int_{R_i}^{R} r^{3}\,\mathrm{d}r\,\mathrm{d}\phi = 2\pi \left[\frac{r^{4}}{4}\right]_{R_i}^{R} = \frac{\pi}{2}\left(R^{4} - R_i^{4}\right)$$

Substituting $R = D/2$ and $R_i = d/2$:

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi}{32}\left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Demonstration (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A solid circular shaft of diameter **$60$ mm** and length **$2$ m** is subjected to a torque of **$5$ kN·m**. Take $G = 80$ GPa. Find (i) the maximum shear stress, (ii) the angle of twist in degrees, and (iii) the shear strain at the outer surface.

**Solution:**

**Step (i) — Compute the polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (60)^{4}}{32}\;\text{mm}^{4} = \frac{\pi \times 12{,}960{,}000}{32} = 1.2723 \times 10^{6}\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum shear stress (at $r = d/2 = 30$ mm):**

From $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$:

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 \times (5 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N·mm})}{\pi \times (60)^{3}\;\text{mm}^{3}} = \frac{8 \times 10^{7}}{\pi \times 216{,}000} = \frac{8 \times 10^{7}}{6.7858 \times 10^{5}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} \approx 117.9\;\text{N/mm}^{2} = 117.9\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step (iii) — Angle of twist (in degrees):**

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{6}) \times (2 \times 10^{3})}{(80 \times 10^{3}) \times (1.2723 \times 10^{6})}\;\text{radians}$$

$$\theta = \frac{1 \times 10^{10}}{1.0178 \times 10^{11}} = 0.0983\;\text{rad}$$

Convert to degrees:

$$\theta^{\circ} = 0.0983 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 5.63^{\circ}$$

**Step (iv) — Shear strain at the outer surface:**

$$\gamma = \frac{\tau_{\max}}{G} = \frac{117.9}{80{,}000} = 1.474 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{rad} \approx 0.0844^{\circ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The twist of $5.63^{\circ}$ is well within the small-angle assumption ($\theta_{\text{rad}} = 0.0983 \ll 1$), so all the linearised equations are valid.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Elastic Torsion Assumptions

The following **Mermaid block diagram** maps the logical hierarchy of the ten assumptions and how they feed into the three pillars of the master equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Elastic Torsion of Circular Shaft] --> B[Material Assumptions]
    A --> C[Geometric Assumptions]
    A --> D[Loading Assumptions]
    A --> E[Master Torsion Equation]

    B --> B1[Homogeneous and Isotropic Material]
    B --> B2[Hookean Behaviour tau = G gamma]
    B --> B3[Pure Shear Stress State]

    C --> C1[Straight Uniform Circular Cross Section]
    C --> C2[Plane Sections Remain Plane]
    C --> C3[Radii Remain Straight Radial Unchanged]
    C --> C4[Linear Stress Variation with r]
    C --> C5[Small Twist Angle radian approx]

    D --> D1[Uniform or Gradually Varying Torque]
    D --> D2[Saint Venant End Effects Negligible]

    B2 --> F[Kinematic Relation gamma = r theta over L]
    C3 --> F
    C5 --> F

    B1 --> G[Static Equilibrium T equals integral of tau r dA]
    B3 --> G

    F --> E
    G --> E
    C1 --> H[Polar Moment J closed form]
    H --> E

    E --> E1[T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L]
    E --> E2[tau max equals 16 T over pi d cubed]
    E --> E3[theta equals T L over G J]
```

### 4.2 Shear Stress Distribution Across a Circular Cross-Section

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Centre Axis r equals 0] -->|tau equals 0| B[Linear Increase]
    B -->|tau proportional to r| C[Outer Surface r equals R]
    C -->|tau max equals T R over J| D[Maximum Shear Stress]
    D -->|divided by 2 at d equals 2 R| E[For solid shaft: tau max equals 16 T over pi d cubed]
```

### 4.3 Deformation Schematic (Before and After Twist)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Before Twist: Vertical Reference Line on Surface] -->|Applied Torque T| P2[After Twist: Reference Line Rotates by theta]
    P2 --> P3[Shear Strain gamma equals r theta over L]
    P3 --> P4[Hooke's Law: tau equals G gamma]
    P4 --> P5[Stress at Radius r: tau equals T r over J]
```

### 4.4 Logical Flow From Assumption to Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Ten Assumptions of Elastic Torsion]) --> Geo[Geometric Kinematics]
    Start --> Hooke[Hooke's Law in Shear]
    Start --> Equil[Static Equilibrium of Cross Section]

    Geo --> GeoOut[gamma equals r theta over L]
    Hooke --> HookeOut[tau equals G gamma]
    Equil --> EquilOut[T equals integral tau r dA]

    GeoOut --> Combine[Combine to yield Master Equation]
    HookeOut --> Combine
    EquilOut --> Combine

    Combine --> Final([T over J equals tau over r equals G theta over L])

    Final --> Use1[Design of Shafts]
    Final --> Use2[Deflection Analysis]
    Final --> Use3[Combined Loading Studies]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023, Model Paper]**

**State the assumptions made in the theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft within the elastic range.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard, 3 marks):**

The theory of pure torsion of a circular shaft, within the elastic deformation range, is based on the following assumptions:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and perfectly elastic**, obeying Hooke's law in shear ($\tau = G\gamma$).
2. The shaft is **initially straight** and has a **uniform circular cross-section** (solid or hollow) throughout its length.
3. **Plane sections, perpendicular to the axis, remain plane and perpendicular** to the axis after twisting. The cross-section rotates as a **rigid disc** about the longitudinal axis.
4. **Radii of the cross-section remain straight, radial, and unchanged in length** during twisting (no warping of the cross-section).
5. The applied torque is **uniform or varies gradually** along the length, so that Saint-Venant's principle applies and end effects are neglected.
6. The **angle of twist is small**, permitting the use of small-angle approximations ($\sin\theta \approx \theta \approx \tan\theta$, in radians).
7. The **only stress** induced in the shaft is **shear stress**, which varies **linearly** from zero at the centre to a maximum at the outer surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution (KTU 2024):** Any **seven** of the above points = 3 marks. Listing fewer than seven will lead to a partial score of 0–2.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**Explain the term "polar moment of inertia" of a circular cross-section. State its value for (i) a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, and (ii) a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$.**

**Model Answer (Board-Standard, 3 marks):**

The **polar moment of inertia** $J$ of a cross-section, about its longitudinal centroidal axis, is defined as the **second moment of area** of the cross-section about that axis. Mathematically:

$$J = \int_A r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}A$$

where $r$ is the perpendicular distance of the elemental area $\mathrm{d}A$ from the axis of the shaft.

**(i) For a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$:**

$$J_{\text{solid}} = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$

**(ii) For a hollow circular shaft of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:**

$$J_{\text{hollow}} = \frac{\pi \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}{32}$$

The unit of $J$ is $\text{m}^{4}$ (SI) or $\text{mm}^{4}$ (mm-based). It represents the **resistance of the cross-section to twisting** — analogous to the moment of inertia $I$ in bending.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**A solid circular shaft of diameter $80$ mm transmits $120$ kW of power at $180$ rpm. The shaft is made of steel with $G = 80$ GPa and the maximum permissible shear stress is $60$ MPa. Determine:**

**(a)** The maximum torque transmitted by the shaft, and check whether the given diameter is safe. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The angle of twist of the shaft in degrees per metre length, and the total twist in degrees if the shaft length is $3$ m. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the angular speed:**

$$N = 180\;\text{rpm} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 180}{60} = 6\pi\;\text{rad/s} = 18.8496\;\text{rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the torque from the power equation $P = T\omega$:**

$$T = \frac{P}{\omega} = \frac{120 \times 10^{3}}{18.8496} = 6366.2\;\text{N·m} = 6.366 \times 10^{6}\;\text{N·mm}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the maximum shear stress in the given shaft of $d = 80$ mm:**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 6.366 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \times (80)^{3}}\;\text{MPa}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{1.0186 \times 10^{8}}{\pi \times 512{,}000} = \frac{1.0186 \times 10^{8}}{1.6085 \times 10^{6}} \approx 63.32\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the permissible stress:**

$$\tau_{\max} = 63.32\;\text{MPa} > \tau_{\text{permissible}} = 60\;\text{MPa}$$

**Conclusion:** The given diameter of **$80$ mm is NOT safe**, since the induced stress exceeds the permissible stress by about $5.5\%$. The shaft must be redesigned with a larger diameter.

> **Marks split:** Power-torque relation = 1, Torque calculation = 2, Stress formula and substitution = 2, Comparison and conclusion = 2.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Compute the polar moment of inertia for $d = 80$ mm:**

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (80)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 40{,}960{,}000}{32} = 4.0212 \times 10^{6}\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the angle of twist per metre length:**

For $L = 1$ m $= 1000$ mm:

$$\theta_{\text{per m}} = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{6.366 \times 10^{6} \times 1000}{80{,}000 \times 4.0212 \times 10^{6}}\;\text{rad}$$

$$\theta_{\text{per m}} = \frac{6.366 \times 10^{9}}{3.2169 \times 10^{11}} = 0.01979\;\text{rad/m}$$

Convert to degrees per metre:

$$\theta_{\text{per m}}^{\circ} = 0.01979 \times \frac{180}{\pi} \approx 1.134^{\circ}\;\text{per metre}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the total angle of twist for $L = 3$ m:**

$$\theta_{\text{total}} = 0.01979 \times 3 = 0.05937\;\text{rad} = 3.40^{\circ}$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft twists at approximately **$1.13^{\circ}$ per metre**, giving a total twist of about **$3.40^{\circ}$** over a $3$ m length — well within the small-angle assumption.

> **Marks split:** Polar moment = 1, Formula and substitution = 2, Conversion to degrees/m = 2, Total twist = 2.

---

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, derive the **torsion equation** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$, clearly stating the assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hollow circular shaft has an **outer diameter of $100$ mm** and an **inner diameter of $60$ mm**. If the shaft is required to transmit a torque of **$25$ kN·m**, determine: **(i)** the maximum shear stress induced, and **(ii)** the angle of twist in degrees over a length of **$4$ m**, given $G = 80$ GPa. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)**

**Assumptions (write 4–5 key ones for marks):**

1. Material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and elastic** ($\tau = G\gamma$).
2. Shaft is **straight** with a **uniform circular cross-section**.
3. **Plane sections remain plane** and perpendicular to the axis (rigid disc rotation).
4. **Radii remain straight, radial, and unchanged** in length.
5. The **angle of twist is small** (radian measure).
6. The applied torque is **uniform or varies gradually** along the length.

**Derivation Outline:**

1. Consider an elemental length $\mathrm{d}L$ of the shaft. The right cross-section rotates through an additional angle $\mathrm{d}\theta$ relative to the left.
2. A surface fibre of length $\mathrm{d}L$ at radius $r$ tilts by shear strain $\gamma = \dfrac{r\,\mathrm{d}\theta}{\mathrm{d}L}$. For uniform twist, $\gamma = \dfrac{r\theta}{L}$.
3. From Hooke's law: $\tau = G\gamma = \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L} \;\Rightarrow\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.
4. From static equilibrium of the cross-section: $T = \int_A \tau\, r\,\mathrm{d}A$.
5. Substituting $\tau = \dfrac{Gr\theta}{L}\,r$ in the integral:

$$T = \frac{G\theta}{L}\int_A r^{2}\,\mathrm{d}A = \frac{G\theta}{L}\cdot J$$

6. Therefore $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.
7. Combining the two results:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}\;}$$

> **Marks split:** Assumptions = 2 marks, Geometry of deformation = 1.5 marks, Hooke's law substitution = 1 mark, Equilibrium + integration = 1.5 marks, Final boxed equation = 1 mark.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)**

**Given:** $D = 100$ mm, $d = 60$ mm, $T = 25$ kN·m $= 25 \times 10^{6}$ N·mm, $L = 4$ m $= 4000$ mm, $G = 80{,}000$ MPa (N/mm²).

**Step 1 — Compute the polar moment of inertia:**

$$J = \frac{\pi \left(D^{4} - d^{4}\right)}{32} = \frac{\pi \left[(100)^{4} - (60)^{4}\right]}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \left[1 \times 10^{8} - 1.296 \times 10^{7}\right]}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 8.704 \times 10^{7}}{32}$$

$$J = 8.5430 \times 10^{6}\;\text{mm}^{4}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum shear stress (at outer surface $r = D/2 = 50$ mm):**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{T r}{J} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6} \times 50}{8.5430 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{9}}{8.5430 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\tau_{\max} \approx 146.3\;\text{N/mm}^{2} = 146.3\;\text{MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Angle of twist (radians):**

$$\theta = \frac{T L}{G J} = \frac{25 \times 10^{6} \times 4000}{80{,}000 \times 8.5430 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$\theta = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{11}}{6.8344 \times 10^{11}} = 0.1463\;\text{rad}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to degrees:**

$$\theta^{\circ} = 0.1463 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.1463 \times 57.2958 \approx 8.38^{\circ}$$

**Conclusion:** Maximum shear stress $\tau_{\max} \approx 146.3$ MPa, and total angle of twist $\theta \approx 8.38^{\circ}$ over $4$ m.

> **Marks split:** Polar moment of inertia (hollow) = 2 marks, Shear stress computation = 2 marks, Angle of twist formula and substitution = 2 marks, Unit conversion and final answer = 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Catastrophe:** Writing $T = 25$ kN·m, $d, D$ in mm, and $G$ in N/m² *simultaneously* without conversion. This single error makes the entire numerical answer wrong and costs **4–6 marks**.
> 2. **Using Solid-Shaft Formula for Hollow Shaft:** Substituting $J = \pi d^{4}/32$ when the problem says hollow. Marks deducted: **2**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $\frac{180}{\pi}$ Conversion:** Reporting $\theta$ in radians when the question asks for degrees. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 4. **Writing Angle of Twist as $\theta$ when asked "per metre length":** If the question says "twist per metre", you must compute $\theta/L$, not $\theta$ for the total length. Marks deducted: **1**.
> 5. **Omitting the "small angle" check in design problems:** A shaft twisting by more than ~$5^{\circ}$ violates Assumption 10. Mention this explicitly for full marks.
> 6. **Confusing $\tau_{\text{avg}}$ and $\tau_{\max}$:** For a solid circular shaft, $\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{2}{3}\tau_{\text{max}}$. Students often report the average as the maximum.
> 7. **Wrong application of $P = T\omega$ vs $P = 2\pi N T / 60$:** Use $N$ in **rpm** for the latter, and $\omega$ in **rad/s** for the former. Mixing them is a guaranteed $1$-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] The torsion equation $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ is **valid only** when **all ten elastic-torsion assumptions** hold simultaneously.
- [x] **Polar moment of inertia** $J$ is the **resistance of the cross-section to twisting**; larger $J$ ⇒ lower stress and lower twist for the same torque.
- [x] **Solid shaft:** $J = \dfrac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$, $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16T}{\pi d^{3}}$.
- [x] **Hollow shaft:** $J = \dfrac{\pi(D^{4} - d^{4})}{32}$, $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T D}{\pi (D^{4} - d^{4})}$.
- [x] Shear stress is **zero at the axis** and **maximum at the outer surface** (linear variation with $r$).
- [x] The **angle of twist $\theta$ is in radians** when using the master equation; multiply by $180/\pi$ for degrees.
- [x] For a shaft of length $L$ with **uniform torque**, $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$.
- [x] The **strain energy** stored is $U = \dfrac{T^{2} L}{2 G J} = \dfrac{1}{2} T \theta$.
- [x] **Power transmission:** $P = T\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m, $P$ in W).
- [x] **Pure shear** state implies principal stresses at $\pm 45^{\circ}$ with magnitudes $\sigma_{1,2} = +\tau$ and $\sigma_{3} = -\tau$ — critical for combined-loading analysis.
- [x] **Unit discipline** is non-negotiable: keep **all** quantities in one consistent system throughout a single numerical problem.
- [x] The **small-angle assumption** requires $\theta < 0.1$ rad (≈ $5.7^{\circ}$). If $\theta$ exceeds this, the linearised torsion equation begins to lose accuracy.
- [x] The torsion equation is **dimensionally consistent** on both sides: $[T/J] = \dfrac{\text{N·m}}{\text{m}^{4}} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^{3}}$, and $[\tau/r] = \dfrac{\text{N/m}^{2}}{\text{m}} = \dfrac{\text{N}}{\text{m}^{3}}$. A quick dimensional check before writing the final answer is a hallmark of a top KTU scorer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Torsion Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Torsion Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Torsion** is the twisting of a structural member (typically a circular shaft) about its longitudinal axis when subjected to equal and opposite couples (torques) acting in planes perpendicular to the axis of the member. The **Torsion Formula** establishes the fundamental elastic relationship governing the shear stress distribution and angle of twist in a circular shaft under pure torsion.

The standard **Torsion Equation** is expressed as:

$$
\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta}{L}
$$

where every term carries a precise physical meaning in the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 3**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Wet Towel Twist**
> Imagine wringing out a wet towel. As your hands twist in opposite directions, the towel fibers at the outer surface stretch and shear the most, while fibers at the central core barely deform. The towel's cross-sections (originally circular) remain circular but rotate relative to each other, and the towel as a whole twists through a measurable angle. A solid circular shaft behaves identically — **surface fibers experience the maximum shear strain and shear stress**, and the central axis experiences **zero shear stress**. This radial variation is the very heart of the torsion formula.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** — measured in **N/m²** or **Pa**, denoting material resistance to shear deformation.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** — measured in **m⁴** or **mm⁴**, representing the cross-section's resistance to twisting.
- **Polar Modulus (Zₚ)** — measured in **m³** or **mm³**, used for direct shear-stress calculation.
- **Standard values** for common shaft materials (mild steel: G ≈ 80 GPa; cast iron: G ≈ 45 GPa; bronze: G ≈ 40 GPa) are highlighted in **bold** in the engineering data tables.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic — Assumptions Underlying the Derivation

The torsion formula is valid **only** when the following assumptions (highlighted in KTU board evaluations as **essential preliminaries**) are satisfied:

- The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit.
- The shaft has a **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
- The shaft is **straight** and loaded only by **pure torques** applied at its ends (Saint-Venant's principle holds far from load application points).
- Cross-sections **remain plane, circular, and perpendicular to the axis** after twisting (no warping).
- The **distance between any two cross-sections remains unchanged** (no axial/normal strain).
- The **radii of cross-sections remain straight and unchanged in length** (only rotation occurs).
- The **angle of twist θ** is small and uniform along the length.

## 2.2 Why Each Assumption Matters

- *Homogeneity & Isotropy* → Guarantees G is uniform in every direction.
- *Circular Cross-Section* → Symmetry ensures no warping; torsional rigidity is purely a function of J.
- *Plane Sections Remain Plane* → Validates the linear shear-strain distribution $\gamma \propto r$.
- *No Axial Strain* → Means **no longitudinal normal stress** is generated by pure torsion.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most-referenced artifact** in KTU University Exam answers for Module 3. Memorise every row.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Standard Units |
|---|---------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ | Master torsion equation | Universal |
| 2 | $\gamma = \dfrac{r\,\theta}{L}$ | Shear strain at radius $r$ | radians |
| 3 | $\tau = \dfrac{G\,\theta\,r}{L}$ | Shear stress at radius $r$ | Pa or N/m² |
| 4 | $T = \dfrac{G\,J\,\theta}{L}$ | Torque–twist relationship | N·m |
| 5 | $J_{\text{solid}} = \dfrac{\pi\,D^{4}}{32} = \dfrac{\pi\,R^{4}}{2}$ | Polar moment of inertia — solid shaft | m⁴ |
| 6 | $J_{\text{hollow}} = \dfrac{\pi}{32}\,(D^{4}-d^{4})$ | Polar moment of inertia — hollow shaft | m⁴ |
| 7 | $Z_{p} = \dfrac{J}{R}$ | Polar section modulus | m³ |
| 8 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T}{Z_{p}} = \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,D^{3}}$ | Maximum shear stress at outer surface | Pa |
| 9 | $\theta = \dfrac{T\,L}{G\,J}$ | Angle of twist over length $L$ | radians |
| 10 | $\phi = \dfrac{\theta}{L} = \dfrac{T}{G\,J}$ | Angle of twist per unit length | rad/m |

**Notation Table** (also frequently asked as a 3-mark direct question):

| Symbol | Quantity | Symbol | Quantity |
|--------|----------|--------|----------|
| $T$ | Applied torque | $J$ | Polar moment of inertia |
| $\tau$ | Shear stress at radius $r$ | $r$ | Radial distance from centre |
| $G$ | Modulus of rigidity | $\theta$ | Angle of twist |
| $L$ | Length of shaft | $R$ | Outer radius |
| $D$ | Outer diameter | $d$ | Inner diameter (hollow) |
| $\gamma$ | Shear strain | $Z_{p}$ | Polar section modulus |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The torsion formula is the **backbone design equation** for every rotating shaft in industry: turbine rotors, automotive drive shafts, drill collars, propeller shafts in ships, helicopter rotor masts, and even the humble screwdriver bit. KTU examiners frequently test practical context by asking "Why does a hollow shaft transmit more power per kg than a solid one?" — the answer lies directly in the $J$ formula, where material near the neutral axis contributes nothing to torsional resistance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Torsion Formula

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is written out in full. **No step is skipped.** This is the model derivation style expected in KTU 2024 ESE 14-mark answers.

### Step 1 — Conceptual Setup

Consider a **solid circular shaft** of length $L$ and radius $R$, fixed at one end (say, end A) and subjected to a torque $T$ at the free end (end B). The shaft is made of a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic material with **modulus of rigidity $G$**.

Let $\theta$ be the total angle of twist of the free end B relative to the fixed end A, measured in **radians**.

### Step 2 — Geometry of Deformation (Shear Strain Analysis)

After twisting, every cross-section rotates about the longitudinal axis by an amount that varies linearly along the length. A point on the surface of the shaft at radius $R$, initially at position P, moves to position P′. The arc length traced by this surface point on the surface of the shaft is:

$$
\text{Arc length } PP' \;=\; R \cdot \theta
$$

This arc length, when normalised by the shaft length $L$, gives the **shear strain $\gamma$** experienced by the surface fibre (because the originally longitudinal line on the surface has tilted by this small angle):

$$
\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{R\,\theta}{L}
$$

This step uses the geometric fact that for small angles, $\tan\gamma \approx \sin\gamma \approx \gamma$, and the perpendicular distance between the cross-sections remains $L$ (no axial strain assumption).

### Step 3 — Stress–Strain Relationship (Hooke's Law in Shear)

Since the material obeys Hooke's law in shear, the **shear stress $\tau$** at any point is directly proportional to the **shear strain $\gamma$** at that point:

$$
\tau \;=\; G \cdot \gamma
$$

Substituting the expression for $\gamma$ from Step 2:

$$
\tau \;=\; G \cdot \dfrac{R\,\theta}{L}
$$

### Step 4 — Shear Stress Distribution at Radius $r$

The derivation in Steps 2–3 was for a point on the outer surface at radius $R$. For a generic fibre at an intermediate radius $r$ (where $0 \le r \le R$), the same geometric reasoning applies with $R$ replaced by $r$:

$$
\tau_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta\,r}{L}
$$

This establishes the **linear variation of shear stress from zero at the centre to maximum at the surface** — a critical result for KTU numerical problems.

### Step 5 — Equilibrium of the Cross-Section (Torque–Stress Relationship)

To relate the applied torque $T$ to the internal stress distribution, consider an infinitesimal annular ring of radius $r$, thickness $dr$, and area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$. The shear force acting on this ring (which contributes to resisting the applied torque) is:

$$
dF \;=\; \tau_{r} \cdot dA \;=\; \left( \dfrac{G\,\theta\,r}{L} \right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr
$$

The moment of this elemental shear force about the central axis (i.e., its contribution to resisting $T$) is:

$$
dT \;=\; r \cdot dF \;=\; \left( \dfrac{G\,\theta\,r}{L} \right) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \cdot r
$$

$$
dT \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,G\,\theta}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr
$$

### Step 6 — Integration Across the Cross-Section

The total resisting torque is obtained by integrating $dT$ from $r = 0$ to $r = R$:

$$
T \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} \dfrac{2\pi\,G\,\theta}{L} \cdot r^{3} \, dr
$$

Pulling constants out of the integral:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,G\,\theta}{L} \cdot \int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr
$$

Evaluating the integral:

$$
\int_{0}^{R} r^{3} \, dr \;=\; \left[ \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} \;=\; \dfrac{R^{4}}{4}
$$

Therefore:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,G\,\theta}{L} \cdot \dfrac{R^{4}}{4} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,G\,\theta\,R^{4}}{2L}
$$

### Step 7 — Introducing the Polar Moment of Inertia $J$

By definition, the **polar moment of inertia** for a solid circular cross-section of radius $R$ (or diameter $D = 2R$) is:

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,R^{4}}{2} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D^{4}}{32}
$$

Substituting this into the torque expression from Step 6:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta\,J}{L}
$$

### Step 8 — Final Assembly of the Master Torsion Equation

From Step 7 we have:

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta\,J}{L}
$$

From Step 4 we have:

$$
\tau_{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta\,r}{L}
$$

Dividing the Step 4 equation by $r$ and equating with the Step 7 expression divided by $J$:

$$
\dfrac{\tau_{r}}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{T}{J}
$$

Hence, in the **canonical KTU form**:

$$
\boxed{\,\dfrac{T}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{\tau}{r} \;=\; \dfrac{G\,\theta}{L}\,}
$$

This is the **Torsion Formula**, also known as the **elastic torsion equation** or **Bredt-Batho equation** for circular shafts.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Boundary and Limiting Conditions

| Condition | Resulting Value | Engineering Significance |
|-----------|----------------|--------------------------|
| At the axis, $r = 0$ | $\tau = 0$ | No shear stress at the neutral axis |
| At the surface, $r = R$ | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{T\,R}{J}$ | Design-governing maximum stress |
| For a hollow shaft, $r = d/2$ | $\tau_{\min}$ at inner surface | Stress exists on **both** inner and outer surfaces |
| At fixed end (encastre) | $\theta = 0$ | Boundary condition for integration |
| At free loaded end | $\theta = \dfrac{TL}{GJ}$ | Maximum twist in a uniform shaft |

## 3.3 Auxiliary Result — Polar Section Modulus

Dividing both sides of $\tau = Tr/J$ by $T$ and using $Z_p = J/R$:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T}{Z_{p}}
$$

For a solid circular shaft:

$$
Z_{p} \;=\; \dfrac{J}{R} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi D^{3}}{16}
$$

This is the form most often quoted in KTU design-problem answers (e.g., "Find the diameter of a shaft transmitting 50 kW at 300 rpm").

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of the Derivation

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **logical architecture** of the torsion-formula derivation — from physical setup, through geometric and constitutive laws, to the integrated master equation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Setup: Solid Circular Shaft Fixed at A, Torque T Applied at B] --> B[Define Geometric Parameters: Length L, Radius R, Twist Angle Theta]
    B --> C[Geometry of Deformation: Surface Point P Moves to P-Prime]
    C --> D[Compute Shear Strain Gamma equals R times Theta divided by L]
    D --> E[Apply Hookes Law in Shear: Tau equals G times Gamma]
    E --> F[Generalise to Radius r: Tau sub r equals G Theta r divided by L]
    F --> G[Define Infinitesimal Area Element dA equals 2 pi r dr]
    G --> H[Compute Elemental Torque dT equals r times Tau times dA]
    H --> I[Integrate dT from r equals 0 to r equals R]
    I --> J[Introduce Polar Moment of Inertia J equals pi R to power 4 divided by 2]
    J --> K[Derive T equals G Theta J divided by L]
    K --> L[Combine with Tau equals G Theta r divided by L]
    L --> M[Final Master Equation: T over J equals Tau over r equals G Theta over L]
    M --> N[Define Polar Section Modulus Zp equals J over R]
    N --> O[Design Equation: Tau max equals T over Zp equals 16 T over pi D cubed]
```

## 4.2 Logical Architecture — Module-3 Stress Propagation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Applied Torque T] --> B1[Resisting Torque via Shear Stress Tau]
    B1 --> C1[Linear Stress Distribution: Tau proportional to r]
    C1 --> D1[Zero at Centre]
    C1 --> E1[Maximum Tau max at Outer Surface r equals R]
    E1 --> F1[Governs Shaft Design: Diameter Selection]
    D1 --> G1[Core Material Under-Stressed]
    G1 --> H1[Engineering Insight: Hollow Shafts More Efficient]
    F1 --> I1[KTU Design Problem: Find D for Given T and Allowable Tau]
```

## 4.3 Why a Block Diagram Instead of a Physical Sketch?

> [!NOTE]
> For this derivation, the logical sequence is more important than a literal 3-D sketch. The block-level architecture above maps the **causal dependency** between each step — which is exactly what KTU examiners look for when awarding full 14-mark credit. A physical sketch of a twisted shaft is often shown alongside, but the **flow of logic** is what earns the marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the assumptions made in the derivation of the torsion formula.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The torsion formula is derived under the following key assumptions:

1. The shaft material is **homogeneous**, **isotropic**, and obeys **Hooke's Law** within the elastic limit.
2. The shaft is of **uniform circular cross-section** throughout its length.
3. Cross-sections **remain plane, circular, and perpendicular to the axis** after twisting.
4. The **distance between cross-sections remains unchanged** (no axial strain).
5. The **radii of cross-sections remain straight and unchanged in length**; only rotation occurs.
6. The shaft is loaded by **pure torques only**, with **Saint-Venant's principle** applicable away from load points.

*(All six points for full 3 marks.)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define polar moment of inertia. Write its expression for a solid and hollow circular shaft.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The **polar moment of inertia $J$** of a cross-section about its longitudinal centroidal axis is defined as the **sum (integral) of the squares of the elemental areas** multiplied by their perpendicular distance from the axis. It represents the cross-section's **resistance to twisting**.

Mathematically:

$$
J \;=\; \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA
$$

For a **solid circular shaft** of diameter $D$ (or radius $R$):

$$
J_{\text{solid}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D^{4}}{32} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,R^{4}}{2}
$$

For a **hollow circular shaft** with outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$:

$$
J_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32}\,\left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right)
$$

Units: **m⁴** or **mm⁴**.

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Always state the assumptions** before starting the derivation. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if assumptions are omitted.
> 2. The shear-strain expression $\gamma = R\theta / L$ is derived from **geometry** — explicitly show the arc-length argument. Skipping this costs a mark.
> 3. When integrating $dT$, **show the integral limits** (0 to R) and the **evaluation step**. Never write the final integrated form without justification.
> 4. In numerical problems, **keep units consistent**: convert mm to m, or N·mm to N·m, before substituting. Mixed-unit answers are penalised.
> 5. **Polar section modulus** is sometimes asked in lieu of $J$ — know both forms.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**(a)** *Derive the torsion formula* $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ for a solid circular shaft, stating all assumptions clearly. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A solid circular shaft of diameter **80 mm** transmits a torque of **6 kN·m**. If the modulus of rigidity of the shaft material is **80 GPa**, determine: **(i)** the maximum shear stress induced in the shaft, and **(ii)** the angle of twist per metre length of the shaft. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**
Refer to the **complete step-by-step derivation in Section 3.1** of these notes. The expected answer structure for full credit is:

1. **Assumptions** (1 Mark) — List all six assumptions.
2. **Shear-strain derivation from geometry** (1 Mark) — Show $\gamma = R\theta / L$.
3. **Hooke's Law application** (1 Mark) — $\tau = G\gamma$ leading to $\tau = G\theta R / L$.
4. **Generalisation to radius $r$** (1 Mark) — $\tau_r = G\theta r / L$.
5. **Elemental torque expression** (1 Mark) — $dT = r \cdot \tau_r \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$.
6. **Integration and introduction of $J$** (1.5 Marks) — $T = G\theta J / L$ and final assembly.
7. **Final boxed equation** (0.5 Marks) — $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- Diameter $D = 80$ mm $= 0.080$ m, so radius $R = 0.040$ m.
- Torque $T = 6$ kN·m $= 6000$ N·m.
- Modulus of rigidity $G = 80$ GPa $= 80 \times 10^{9}$ N/m².

*Step 1 — Polar moment of inertia:*

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\,D^{4}}{32} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times (0.080)^{4}}{32}
$$

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \times 4.096 \times 10^{-5}}{32} \;=\; \dfrac{1.2868 \times 10^{-4}}{32}
$$

$$
J \;=\; 4.021 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

*Step 2 — Maximum shear stress (at $r = R = 0.040$ m):*

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot R}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{6000 \times 0.040}{4.021 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{240}{4.021 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 5.968 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\boxed{\tau_{\max} \;\approx\; 59.68 \text{ MPa}}
$$

*Step 3 — Angle of twist per metre length:*

Using $\theta / L = T / (G J)$:

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{6000}{80 \times 10^{9} \times 4.021 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{L} \;=\; \dfrac{6000}{3.2168 \times 10^{5}} \;=\; 0.01865 \text{ rad/m}
$$

Converting to degrees (optional):

$$
\dfrac{\theta}{L} \;\approx\; 1.069^{\circ} \text{ per metre}
$$

$$
\boxed{\dfrac{\theta}{L} \;\approx\; 0.01865 \text{ rad/m}}
$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]`

**(a)** *Derive the expression for the polar moment of inertia for a hollow circular shaft*, and hence obtain the maximum shear stress formula in terms of outer diameter $D$ and inner diameter $d$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hollow shaft of outer diameter **100 mm** and inner diameter **60 mm** is required to transmit **120 kW at 200 rpm**. If the allowable shear stress is **60 MPa**, determine whether the shaft is safe. Take the modulus of rigidity as **80 GPa**. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation of $J$ for a hollow circular shaft (7 Marks):**

Consider a hollow circular cross-section with outer radius $R_o = D/2$ and inner radius $R_i = d/2$.

By definition:

$$
J \;=\; \int_{A} r^{2} \, dA
$$

Using an annular elemental area $dA = 2\pi r \, dr$, the integration proceeds from $R_i$ to $R_o$:

$$
J \;=\; \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^{2} \cdot 2\pi r \, dr \;=\; 2\pi \int_{R_i}^{R_o} r^{3} \, dr
$$

$$
J \;=\; 2\pi \left[ \dfrac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{R_i}^{R_o} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{2} \left( R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} \right)
$$

Substituting $R_o = D/2$ and $R_i = d/2$:

$$
R_o^{4} - R_i^{4} \;=\; \left( \dfrac{D}{2} \right)^{4} - \left( \dfrac{d}{2} \right)^{4} \;=\; \dfrac{D^{4} - d^{4}}{16}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{J_{\text{hollow}} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32} \left( D^{4} - d^{4} \right)}
$$

For maximum shear stress, set $r = R_o = D/2$ in $\tau = T r / J$:

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot (D/2)}{\dfrac{\pi}{32}(D^{4}-d^{4})} \;=\; \dfrac{16\,T}{\pi\,D^{3}\,\left( 1 - k^{4} \right)}
$$

where $k = d/D$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

*Given:*
- Outer diameter $D = 100$ mm $= 0.100$ m.
- Inner diameter $d = 60$ mm $= 0.060$ m.
- Power $P = 120$ kW; speed $N = 200$ rpm.
- Allowable shear stress $\tau_{\text{allow}} = 60$ MPa $= 60 \times 10^{6}$ N/m².
- Modulus of rigidity $G = 80$ GPa.

*Step 1 — Compute the applied torque from power-speed relation:*

$$
P \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60} \;\Rightarrow\; T \;=\; \dfrac{60\,P}{2\pi N}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{60 \times 120\,000}{2\pi \times 200} \;=\; \dfrac{7.2 \times 10^{6}}{1256.64}
$$

$$
T \;=\; 5729.58 \text{ N·m} \;\approx\; 5.73 \text{ kN·m}
$$

*Step 2 — Compute polar moment of inertia:*

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32}\,(D^{4} - d^{4}) \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32}\,\left( 0.100^{4} - 0.060^{4} \right)
$$

$$
D^{4} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}, \quad d^{4} = 1.296 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32} \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4} - 0.01296 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
J \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{32} \times 8.704 \times 10^{-5} \;=\; 8.542 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^{4}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute maximum shear stress:*

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{T \cdot R_o}{J} \;=\; \dfrac{5729.58 \times 0.050}{8.542 \times 10^{-6}}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; \dfrac{286.479}{8.542 \times 10^{-6}} \;=\; 3.354 \times 10^{7} \text{ N/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\tau_{\max} \;\approx\; 33.54 \text{ MPa}
$$

*Step 4 — Compare with allowable stress:*

$$
\tau_{\max} \;=\; 33.54 \text{ MPa} \;<\; \tau_{\text{allow}} \;=\; 60 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Conclusion:** Since the induced maximum shear stress (**33.54 MPa**) is well below the allowable shear stress (**60 MPa**), **the shaft is SAFE**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Derivation of Torsion Formula**

- ⭐ **Master equation:** $\dfrac{T}{J} = \dfrac{\tau}{r} = \dfrac{G\theta}{L}$ — memorise cold; it is the most frequently quoted equation in Module 3.
- ⭐ **Six assumptions** must be stated before any derivation — examiners routinely allocate 1–2 marks here.
- ⭐ **Geometric origin of shear strain:** $\gamma = R\theta / L$ comes from the arc-length relation $s = R\theta$, **not** from Hooke's law.
- ⭐ **Shear stress varies linearly with radius:** $\tau \propto r$ — zero at the centre, maximum at the surface.
- ⭐ **Polar moment of inertia (solid):** $J = \pi D^{4} / 32 = \pi R^{4} / 2$.
- ⭐ **Polar moment of inertia (hollow):** $J = \pi (D^{4} - d^{4}) / 32$.
- ⭐ **Polar section modulus:** $Z_p = J / R = \pi D^{3} / 16$ (solid shaft).
- ⭐ **Maximum shear stress shortcut:** $\tau_{\max} = T / Z_p = 16T / (\pi D^{3})$ for solid shafts.
- ⭐ **Angle of twist:** $\theta = TL / (GJ)$ — total twist; per-unit-length twist is $\theta/L = T/(GJ)$.
- ⭐ **Units discipline:** $T$ in N·m, $J$ in m⁴, $G$ in Pa, $L$ in m, $D/d$ in m. Convert mm → m before substituting.
- ⭐ **Power–torque relation:** $P = 2\pi N T / 60$, where $P$ in watts, $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m.
- ⭐ **Hollow vs solid insight:** Hollow shafts deliver higher $J$ per unit weight — the **core contributes nothing** to torsional resistance.
- ⭐ **Hooke's Law in shear** is the constitutive bridge between geometry ($\gamma$) and stress ($\tau$).
- ⭐ **Equilibrium integration** $T = \int r \cdot \tau_r \cdot dA$ is the link between micro-stress and macro-torque.
- ⭐ **Final assembly** combines three equalities: the equilibrium ratio $T/J$, the stress ratio $\tau/r$, and the twist ratio $G\theta/L$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torsional Rigidity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Torsional Rigidity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Torsional Rigidity** is defined as the product of the **Modulus of Rigidity (G)** and the **Polar Moment of Inertia (J)** of a shaft's cross-section about its longitudinal axis. It quantifies the inherent ability of a shaft to resist angular deformation (twisting) when subjected to a torque.

$$T_{rigidity} = G \cdot J$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology:** The product $G \cdot J$ is referred to as the **Absolute Torsional Rigidity** of the shaft, while the ratio $\frac{G \cdot J}{L}$ is the **Torsional Stiffness** (or relative torsional rigidity), where $L$ is the length over which the twist occurs.

A shaft is said to possess **high torsional rigidity** when only a small angle of twist is produced for a given applied torque. A shaft is said to be **flexible in torsion** when the angle of twist is large for a given torque.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine holding a thick steel rod and a thin rubber rod of equal length between your two hands. Now, twist both ends in opposite directions using the *same amount of effort*.

- The **steel rod** barely rotates — it feels "stiff" against twisting.
- The **rubber rod** rotates easily — it feels "loose" or "flexible."

The reason is that steel has a much higher **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$)** AND a larger cross-section (larger $J$). The combined product $G \cdot J$ — the **Torsional Rigidity** — is what physically determines how much the shaft resists being twisted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Insight:**
> - **Automotive drive shafts** require high $GJ$ to transmit engine power without excessive twisting (which would cause lag between accelerator input and wheel response).
> - **Precision instrument shafts** (e.g., in a torsion balance or clock mechanism) need carefully tuned $GJ$ values.
> - **Rubber bushings** are intentionally designed with low $GJ$ to act as flexible couplings.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Parameters

- **Modulus of Rigidity ($G$):** Measured in $\text{N/m}^2$ or $\text{Pa}$. Typical value for **mild steel** is $\approx 79.3 \text{ GPa}$, for **aluminum** $\approx 27 \text{ GPa}$.
- **Polar Moment of Inertia ($J$):** Measured in $\text{m}^4$ or $\text{mm}^4$. Depends purely on the cross-sectional geometry.
- **Units of Torsional Rigidity ($GJ$):** $\text{N·m}^2$ in SI units, or $\text{N·mm}^2$ in millimeter-based problems.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** Always state the units in their final form when presenting $GJ$ values. Examiners frequently deduct marks for missing units in the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Torsion Equation — Foundation for Torsional Rigidity

The classical torsion equation for a circular shaft under pure torsion is:

$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G \cdot \theta}{L}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Applied torque (N·m)
- $J$ = Polar moment of inertia of the cross-section (m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $\tau$ = Shear stress at radius $r$ (Pa or N/mm²)
- $r$ = Radial distance from the center of the shaft (m or mm)
- $G$ = Modulus of rigidity (Pa or N/mm²)
- $\theta$ = Angle of twist (radians)
- $L$ = Length of the shaft (m or mm)

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic Leading to Torsional Rigidity

1. **Rearrange the torsion equation** to isolate the applied torque in terms of the angle of twist:
$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

2. **Observe the structure:** The applied torque $T$ is directly proportional to the angle of twist $\theta$, with a proportionality constant equal to $\frac{G \cdot J}{L}$.

3. **Define this constant** as the **Torsional Stiffness** (or angular stiffness):
$$k_t = \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$

4. **Identify the numerator** $G \cdot J$ as the **Torsional Rigidity** — the *material-and-geometry-dependent* intrinsic resistance to twisting, *independent of length*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $GJ$ | Absolute Torsional Rigidity | N·m² |
| 2 | $\frac{GJ}{L}$ | Torsional Stiffness (Relative Rigidity) | N·m/rad |
| 3 | $J = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | Polar MI for *solid* circular shaft (diameter $D$) | m⁴ |
| 4 | $J = \frac{\pi}{32}(D_o^4 - D_i^4)$ | Polar MI for *hollow* circular shaft ($D_o$, $D_i$) | m⁴ |
| 5 | $\theta = \frac{TL}{GJ}$ | Angle of twist in radians | rad |
| 6 | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3}$ | Max shear stress (solid shaft) | Pa or N/mm² |
| 7 | $\tau_{max} = \frac{16T D_o}{\pi(D_o^4 - D_i^4)}$ | Max shear stress (hollow shaft) | Pa or N/mm² |
| 8 | $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ | Torsion equation | — |
| 9 | $P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power transmitted by shaft ($N$ in rpm) | W or kW |
| 10 | $T_{eq} \approx 0.6 \cdot \sigma_y \cdot Z_p$ | Equivalent torque from bending (combined loading) | N·m |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Note:** When the problem uses **mm**, the corresponding units are $T$ in N·mm, $G$ in N/mm², $J$ in mm⁴, and $\theta$ remains in radians. **Never mix** mm and m in the same equation.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Torsional rigidity is a *design-defining parameter* in:

- **Transmission shafts** of automobiles, ships, and turbines — to minimize power-transmission lag.
- **Helicopter rotor shafts** — where torsional flexibility affects flight dynamics.
- **Torsion springs** in watches, vehicle suspensions, and electrical relays — where a *specific* angular deflection is required.
- **Drive shafts of machine tools** — to maintain positional accuracy under load.

> [!TIP]
> A **hollow shaft** is preferred over a **solid shaft** of the same material and length for the same $GJ$ because it saves material and weight while keeping the same torsional rigidity. (This is a guaranteed KTU 14-mark derivation question!)

## 2.5 Physical Meaning Summary

- **Large $GJ$** → Stiff shaft → Less twist for given torque → Used where angular deflection must be minimal.
- **Small $GJ$** → Flexible shaft → More twist for given torque → Used in torsion springs and flexible couplings.
- **$GJ$ depends on:** Material choice ($G$) and cross-section geometry ($J$), **not** on length.
- **$\frac{GJ}{L}$ depends on:** Material, geometry, **and** length.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Torsional Rigidity from the Torsion Equation

### Starting Point
The classical torsion equation is:
$$\frac{T}{J} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$$

### Step 1: Cross-multiply the equation
$$T \cdot L = G \cdot J \cdot \theta$$

### Step 2: Solve for $T$
$$T = \frac{G \cdot J \cdot \theta}{L}$$

### Step 3: Identify the proportional relationship
The equation $T = k_t \cdot \theta$ has the form of a linear spring equation, where the equivalent torsional spring constant is:
$$k_t = \frac{G \cdot J}{L}$$

### Step 4: Isolate the length-independent term
Multiply both sides by $\frac{L}{\theta}$:
$$G \cdot J = \frac{T \cdot L}{\theta}$$

### Step 5: Define torsional rigidity
$$\boxed{GJ = \frac{TL}{\theta}}$$

This is the **absolute torsional rigidity** — a measure of how much torque-meter-length is needed to produce a unit radian of twist.

## 3.2 Derivation of $J$ for Solid Circular Shaft

Consider a solid shaft of diameter $D$ and radius $R = D/2$. The polar moment of inertia is the second moment of area about the polar (longitudinal) axis.

$$J = \int_A r^2 \, dA$$

For a circle, use the differential ring element of radius $r$ and thickness $dr$:

$$dA = 2\pi r \, dr$$

### Step-by-step integration:
$$J = \int_0^R r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr$$

$$J = 2\pi \int_0^R r^3 \, dr$$

$$J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_0^R$$

$$J = 2\pi \cdot \frac{R^4}{4} = \frac{\pi R^4}{2}$$

### Substitute $R = D/2$:
$$J = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^4 = \frac{\pi}{2} \cdot \frac{D^4}{16} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J_{solid} = \frac{\pi D^4}{32}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of $J$ for Hollow Circular Shaft

For a hollow shaft with outer diameter $D_o$ and inner diameter $D_i$:

$$J = \int_{D_i/2}^{D_o/2} r^2 \cdot (2\pi r) \, dr$$

$$J = 2\pi \int_{D_i/2}^{D_o/2} r^3 \, dr$$

$$J = 2\pi \left[ \frac{r^4}{4} \right]_{D_i/2}^{D_o/2}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi}{2} \left[ \left(\frac{D_o}{2}\right)^4 - \left(\frac{D_i}{2}\right)^4 \right]$$

$$\boxed{J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{32} \left( D_o^4 - D_i^4 \right)}$$

## 3.4 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Solid vs Hollow Shaft

Define the ratio $k = \frac{D_i}{D_o}$ (a value between 0 and 1, where 0 = solid, 1 = thin shell).

| Property | Solid Shaft | Hollow Shaft | Ratio (Hollow/Solid) |
|----------|-------------|--------------|----------------------|
| Polar MI $J$ | $\frac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | $\frac{\pi D_o^4}{32}(1 - k^4)$ | $1 - k^4$ |
| Cross-section Area $A$ | $\frac{\pi D^2}{4}$ | $\frac{\pi D_o^2}{4}(1 - k^2)$ | $1 - k^2$ |
| Weight (for same $L$ & material) | $W_s$ | $W_h = W_s(1-k^2)$ | $1 - k^2$ |
| Torsional Rigidity $GJ$ | $G \cdot \frac{\pi D^4}{32}$ | $G \cdot \frac{\pi D_o^4}{32}(1 - k^4)$ | $1 - k^4$ |
| Max Shear Stress (same $T$) | $\frac{16T}{\pi D^3}$ | $\frac{16T}{\pi D_o^3 (1 - k^4)}$ | $\frac{1}{1 - k^4}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Insight:** For the **same outer diameter and same material**, the hollow shaft is *lighter* (factor $1-k^2$) but has *lower* torsional rigidity (factor $1-k^4$). However, for the **same weight and same material**, the hollow shaft has **higher** torsional rigidity than a solid shaft. This is the classic 14-mark KTU problem.

## 3.5 Proof: Hollow Shaft Has Higher Rigidity for Same Weight

Let the solid shaft have diameter $D_s$ and the hollow shaft have outer $D_o$ and inner $D_i = kD_o$.

### Equate weights (cross-sectional areas):
$$\frac{\pi D_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi D_o^2}{4} (1 - k^2)$$

$$D_s^2 = D_o^2 (1 - k^2) \implies D_s = D_o \sqrt{1 - k^2}$$

### Equate torsional rigidities (ratio form):
$$\frac{GJ_{hollow}}{GJ_{solid}} = \frac{1 - k^4}{1} \cdot \frac{1}{(D_s/D_o)^4}$$

Wait — using the same $D_o$ for both, the ratio is simply $1 - k^4$, which is *less than 1*. To compare on the basis of equal weight, we use the same $D_o$ but compare with a solid shaft of diameter $D_s$ (the equivalent weight).

### Ratio of rigidities for equal weight:
$$\frac{J_{hollow}}{J_{solid,eq}} = \frac{(1 - k^4) D_o^4}{D_s^4} = \frac{(1 - k^4) D_o^4}{(1 - k^2)^2 D_o^4}$$

$$= \frac{1 - k^4}{(1 - k^2)^2}$$

Using the identity $1 - k^4 = (1 - k^2)(1 + k^2)$:

$$= \frac{(1 - k^2)(1 + k^2)}{(1 - k^2)^2} = \frac{1 + k^2}{1 - k^2}$$

$$\boxed{\frac{J_{hollow}}{J_{solid,eq}} = \frac{1 + k^2}{1 - k^2} > 1 \text{ for all } 0 < k < 1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since $\frac{1 + k^2}{1 - k^2} > 1$ for all $0 < k < 1$, the **hollow shaft has strictly greater torsional rigidity** than a solid shaft of the **same weight and material**. This is the proof that engineers prefer hollow shafts for high-performance applications (e.g., aircraft, racing cars).

## 3.6 Worked Numerical Demonstration

**Given:** Two shafts, both of length $L = 2 \text{ m}$, material $G = 80 \text{ GPa}$.
- **Solid shaft A:** $D = 50 \text{ mm}$
- **Hollow shaft B:** $D_o = 50 \text{ mm}$, $D_i = 30 \text{ mm}$

**Find:** Compare torsional rigidities.

### Solid shaft:
$$J_A = \frac{\pi (50)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot 6.25 \times 10^6}{32}$$

$$J_A = 6.136 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$(GJ)_A = 80 \times 10^3 \cdot 6.136 \times 10^5 = 4.909 \times 10^{10} \text{ N·mm}^2$$

### Hollow shaft:
$$J_B = \frac{\pi}{32}(50^4 - 30^4) = \frac{\pi}{32}(6.25 \times 10^6 - 8.1 \times 10^5)$$

$$J_B = \frac{\pi}{32}(5.44 \times 10^6) = 5.341 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$(GJ)_B = 80 \times 10^3 \cdot 5.341 \times 10^5 = 4.273 \times 10^{10} \text{ N·mm}^2$$

### Comparison ratio (same outer diameter):
$$\frac{GJ_B}{GJ_A} = \frac{5.341}{6.136} = 0.870 \text{ (or } 87.0\% \text{)}$$

So with the **same outer diameter**, the hollow shaft has **lower** torsional rigidity but is **lighter**.

### Now compare on the basis of equal weight:
The hollow shaft has weight ratio $1 - k^2 = 1 - (0.6)^2 = 0.64$. So a *solid equivalent* of equal weight would have:
$$D_s = D_o \sqrt{0.64} = 50 \cdot 0.8 = 40 \text{ mm}$$

$$J_{solid,eq} = \frac{\pi (40)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \cdot 2.56 \times 10^6}{32} = 2.513 \times 10^5 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$\frac{J_{hollow}}{J_{solid,eq}} = \frac{5.341 \times 10^5}{2.513 \times 10^5} = 2.125$$

$$\boxed{\frac{J_B}{J_{A,eq-weight}} = \frac{1 + k^2}{1 - k^2} = \frac{1.36}{0.64} = 2.125}$$

The hollow shaft is **2.125 times** more torsionally rigid than a solid shaft of equal weight. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
