Compare the angles of twist per metre length. --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A** #### Part (a) — Solid Shaft Analysis [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Torque Calculation** Given: **P = 150 kW = 150,000 W**, **N = 240 rpm**, **d = 75 mm**. $$T = \frac{60 \, P}{2 \pi N} = \frac{60 \times 150000}{2 \pi \times 240}$$ $$T = \frac{9 \times 10^6}{480 \pi} = \frac{9 \times 10^6}{1507.96}$$ $$\boxed{T = 5968.31 \, \text{N·m} = 5.968 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}}$$ **Step 2 — Maximum Shear Stress** $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 5.968 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (75)^3}$$ $$(75)^3 = 421875 \, \text{mm}^3$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 421875} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^6}{1.3254 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 72.04 \, \text{MPa}}$$ **Step 3 — Angle of Twist per Metre Length** Polar moment of inertia: $$J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times (75)^4}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 3.164 \times 10^7}{32}$$ $$J = 3.105 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^4 = 3.105 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}^4$$ Angle of twist per unit length: $$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J} = \frac{5968.31}{80 \times 10^9 \times 3.105 \times 10^{-6}}$$ $$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{5968.31}{2.484 \times 10^5} = 0.02403 \, \text{rad/m}$$ Converting to degrees: $$\boxed{\frac{\theta}{L} = 0.02403 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.377^\circ / \text{m}}$$ --- #### Part (b) — Hollow Shaft Comparison [7 Marks] **Given:** k = d/D = 0.6 (ratio of inner to outer diameter) **Condition:** Same weight (same cross-sectional area), same material, same maximum shear stress. **Step 1 — Relate Solid Shaft Diameter to Hollow Shaft Outer Diameter** For equal area (same weight): $$\frac{\pi d_s^2}{4} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$$ $$d_s^2 = D^2 - d^2 = D^2(1 - k^2) = D^2(1 - 0.36) = 0.64 D^2$$ $$d_s = 0.8 D \quad \Longrightarrow \quad D = \frac{d_s}{0.8} = \frac{75}{0.8} = 93.75 \, \text{mm}$$ Inner diameter: $$d = 0.6 \times D = 0.6 \times 93.75 = 56.25 \, \text{mm}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Maximum Shear Stress in Hollow Shaft** $$\tau_{max, hollow} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 \left[1 - k^4\right]}$$ $$1 - (0.6)^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$ $$\tau_{max, hollow} = \frac{16 \times 5.968 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (93.75)^3 \times 0.8704}$$ $$(93.75)^3 = 8.239 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm}^3$$ $$\tau_{max, hollow} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 8.239 \times 10^5 \times 0.8704} = \frac{95.49 \times 10^6}{2.253 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max, hollow} = 42.38 \, \text{MPa}}$$ Since τ_max for the hollow shaft (42.38 MPa) is **less than** τ_max for the solid shaft (72.04 MPa), the hollow shaft is **over-designed** for equal weight. To match stress exactly, the hollow shaft could be made smaller. **Step 3 — Percentage Weight Saving for Equal Strength** For **equal maximum shear stress** under the same torque, the solid and hollow shaft outer dimensions must satisfy: $$d_s^3 = D^3 \left[1 - k^4\right]$$ $$75^3 = D^3 \times 0.8704$$ $$D^3 = \frac{421875}{0.8704} = 484678$$ $$D = 78.66 \, \text{mm}$$ Inner diameter: **d = 0.6 × 78.66 = 47.20 mm** Weight ratio (areas): $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{d_s^2} = \frac{(78.66)^2 - (47.20)^2}{(75)^2}$$ $$= \frac{6187.4 - 2227.8}{5625} = \frac{3959.6}{5625} = 0.704$$ $$\boxed{\text{Percentage weight saving} = (1 - 0.704) \times 100\% = 29.6\%}$$ **Step 4 — Comparison of Angles of Twist** Polar moment of inertia of hollow shaft: $$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(78.66)^4 - (47.20)^4]}{32}$$ $$(78.66)^4 = 3.823 \times 10^7, \quad (47.20)^4 = 4.964 \times 10^6$$ $$J_{hollow} = \frac{\pi \times (3.327 \times 10^7)}{32} = 3.266 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^4$$ Angle of twist per metre: $$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{T}{G J_{hollow}} = \frac{5.968 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^3 \times 3.266 \times 10^6} \, \text{rad/mm}$$ Converting units carefully (T in N·mm, G in N/mm², J in mm⁴): $$G = 80 \times 10^3 \, \text{N/mm}^2$$ $$\frac{\theta}{L} = \frac{5.968 \times 10^6}{80 \times 10^3 \times 3.266 \times 10^6} = \frac{5.968 \times 10^6}{2.613 \times 10^{11}}$$ $$\frac{\theta}{L} = 2.284 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{rad/mm} = 2.284 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{rad/m}$$ In degrees per metre: $$\frac{\theta}{L} = 0.02284 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 1.308^\circ / \text{m}$$ **Comparison:** Hollow shaft twist (1.308°/m) is **slightly less** than solid shaft twist (1.377°/m), making the hollow shaft **stiffer in torsion** as well as lighter. ✅ --- ### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, S3 B.Tech]** **(a) [7 Marks]** A hollow shaft of outer diameter **100 mm** and inner diameter **60 mm** transmits **200 kW** at **180 rpm**. Determine: 1. The torque transmitted. 2. The maximum shear stress in the shaft. 3. The polar moment of inertia and polar section modulus. **(b) [7 Marks]** A solid shaft is to be designed to transmit the same torque at the same maximum shear stress. Find: 1. The required diameter of the solid shaft. 2. The percentage reduction in weight if the hollow shaft is replaced by this solid shaft of equal strength. --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B** #### Part (a) — Hollow Shaft Analysis [7 Marks] **Given:** D = 100 mm, d = 60 mm, P = 200 kW, N = 180 rpm. **Step 1 — Torque** $$T = \frac{60 \times 200 \times 10^3}{2 \pi \times 180} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{360 \pi} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{1130.97}$$ $$\boxed{T = 10610.3 \, \text{N·m} = 10.610 \times 10^6 \, \text{N·mm}}$$ **Step 2 — Polar Moment of Inertia** $$J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32} = \frac{\pi [(100)^4 - (60)^4]}{32}$$ $$(100)^4 = 10^8, \quad (60)^4 = 12.96 \times 10^6$$ $$J = \frac{\pi \times (87.04 \times 10^6)}{32} = \frac{2.734 \times 10^8}{32}$$ $$\boxed{J = 8.544 \times 10^6 \, \text{mm}^4}$$ **Step 3 — Polar Section Modulus** $$Z_p = \frac{J}{R} = \frac{J}{D/2} = \frac{2J}{D} = \frac{2 \times 8.544 \times 10^6}{100}$$ $$\boxed{Z_p = 1.709 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm}^3}$$ **Step 4 — Maximum Shear Stress** Method 1 (direct formula): $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi D^3 \left[1 - (d/D)^4\right]}$$ $$d/D = 0.6, \quad (0.6)^4 = 0.1296$$ $$1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 10.610 \times 10^6}{\pi \times (100)^3 \times 0.8704}$$ $$\tau_{max} = \frac{169.76 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 10^6 \times 0.8704} = \frac{169.76 \times 10^6}{2.734 \times 10^6}$$ $$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 62.09 \, \text{MPa}}$$ Method 2 (verification using section modulus): $$\tau_{max} = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{10.610 \times 10^6}{1.709 \times 10^5} = 62.08 \, \text{MPa} \;\; ✓$$ --- #### Part (b) — Equivalent Solid Shaft [7 Marks] **Step 1 — Required Solid Shaft Diameter for Equal τ_max** Setting the solid shaft formula equal to τ_max = 62.09 MPa: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d_s^3} = 62.09 \, \text{MPa}$$ $$d_s^3 = \frac{16 T}{\pi \tau_{max}} = \frac{16 \times 10.610 \times 10^6}{\pi \times 62.09}$$ $$d_s^3 = \frac{169.76 \times 10^6}{195.05} = 8.704 \times 10^5 \, \text{mm}^3$$ $$d_s = (8.704 \times 10^5)^{1/3}$$ $$\boxed{d_s \approx 95.39 \, \text{mm}}$$ **Step 2 — Weight Comparison** Area of hollow shaft: $$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(100^2 - 60^2) = \frac{\pi}{4}(10000 - 3600)$$ $$A_{hollow} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 6400 = 5026.55 \, \text{mm}^2$$ Area of equivalent solid shaft: $$A_{solid} = \frac{\pi}{4}(d_s)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (95.39)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 9099.3 = 7146.6 \, \text{mm}^2$$ Weight ratio (same length, same material): $$\frac{W_{hollow}}{W_{solid}} = \frac{A_{hollow}}{A_{solid}} = \frac{5026.55}{7146.6} = 0.7033$$ $$\boxed{\text{Weight saving of hollow shaft} = (1 - 0.7033) \times 100\% = 29.67\%}$$ **Conclusion:** The hollow shaft weighs **~29.7% less** than the solid shaft while transmitting the same torque at the same maximum shear stress — confirming the **strength-to-weight superiority** of hollow shafts. ✅ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch:** Forgetting to convert **kW to W** before computing torque (factor of 1000 error). Always write "**P = 150 × 10³ W**" explicitly. > 2. **Misuse of Inner vs Outer Diameter:** Using **d (inner)** as the outer radius in the hollow shaft formula is a classic blunder. The outer radius is always **D/2**. > 3. **Incorrect Polar Moment Formula:** Students sometimes write **J = π(d⁴ - D⁴)/32** (negative). Always ensure **D > d** in the subtraction. > 4. **Angle of Twist Units:** Reporting angle of twist in **radians** without converting when the question asks for **degrees**. Always check the units requested. > 5. **Forgetting Shear Modulus:** When the question asks for angle of twist but does not provide G, students often skip it. G is essential for **θ** but **not for τ_max** — know the difference. > 6. **Comparison Basis Error:** When comparing solid vs hollow, clearly state the basis — **equal torque**, **equal weight**, or **equal strength**. Marks are given for explicitly stating the comparison criterion. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Save this checklist for last-night revision before KTU ESE.** - ✅ **Torsion Equation:** $\frac{T}{J} = \frac{\tau}{r} = \frac{G\theta}{L}$ - ✅ **Polar Moment of Inertia — Solid:** $J = \frac{\pi d^4}{32}$ - ✅ **Polar Moment of Inertia — Hollow:** $J = \frac{\pi (D^4 - d^4)}{32}$ - ✅ **Max Shear Stress — Solid:** $\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3}$ - ✅ **Max Shear Stress — Hollow:** $\tau_{max} = \frac{16T}{\pi D^3 \left[1 - (d/D)^4\right]}$ - ✅ **Power–Torque–Speed:** $T = \frac{60 P}{2\pi N}$ (P in W, N in rpm → T in N·m) - ✅ **Quick kW Formula:** $T = \frac{9549 \times P_{kW}}{N}$ N·m - ✅ **Angle of Twist — Solid:** $\theta = \frac{32 TL}{\pi G d^4}$ radians - ✅ **Angle of Twist — Hollow:** $\theta = \frac{32 TL}{\pi G (D^4 - d^4)}$ radians - ✅ **Equal-Strength Relation:** $d_s^3 = D^3 \left[1 - (d/D)^4\right]$ - ✅ **Weight Ratio (Hollow to Solid):** $\frac{W_h}{W_s} = \frac{D^2 - d^2}{d_s^2}$ - ✅ **Optimal K Ratio:** K = D/d ≈ 1.5 to 2.0 for efficient hollow shaft design - ✅ **Shear stress is zero at center, maximum at outer surface** — always linear with r. - ✅ **Hollow shafts are stronger per unit weight** than solid shafts. - ✅ **Always state units** in every line of the numerical solution. - ✅ **Conversion check:** 1 rad = 180/π degrees; 1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ Pa. - ✅ **G values (commonly used in KTU):** Steel = 80 GPa; Cast Iron = 45 GPa; Bronze = 40 GPa.

topics.title: Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever and Simply Supported Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Shear Force and Bending Moment Diagrams for Cantilever and Simply Supported Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear Force (SF / V):**
The algebraic sum of the vertical forces (transverse loads and reactions) acting on either side of a section of a beam is called the **Shear Force** at that section. It represents the tendency of one part of the beam to slide vertically relative to the adjacent part along the cross-section.

**Bending Moment (BM / M):**
The algebraic sum of the moments of all the forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of a section of a beam, taken about the centroidal axis of that section, is called the **Bending Moment** at that section. It represents the tendency of the beam to bend (curve) at that location.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Questions on SFD and BMD are extremely high-weightage in Part B (14 marks). Students MUST master both *qualitative sketching* (qualitative/conventional method) and *quantitative point-by-point calculations*. Cantilever and Simply Supported beams are the **two foundational cases** upon which all advanced beam problems are built.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Diving Board

Imagine standing at the free end of a **diving board** (cantilever beam fixed at one end). The board bends downward under your weight:

- The **internal resistance** that tries to "slice" the board vertically into two parts sliding past each other = **Shear Force**.
- The **internal resistance** that tries to "fold" or "bend" the board into a curve = **Bending Moment**.

If you walk to the fixed end, the shear force stays the same (your weight), but the bending moment grows larger and larger because the lever arm increases. This is why the board feels "stiffer" or more prone to snap near the wall.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **Civil:** Design of building floor beams, bridge girders, balcony cantilevers.
> - **Mechanical:** Design of machine tool beds, crane jibs, truck chassis.
> - **Aerospace:** Aircraft wing spars behave like cantilever beams.
> - The SFD tells engineers where to **place stirrups** (shear reinforcement) in RCC beams.
> - The BMD tells engineers where to **place main reinforcement bars** (bending reinforcement) — at top for negative moments and bottom for positive moments.

### 1.3 Standard Sign Conventions (KTU Convention)

The KTU board strictly follows these conventions:

| Quantity | Positive Sign Condition | Negative Sign Condition |
|---|---|---|
| **Shear Force (V)** | Left portion pushed **UP** and right portion pushed **DOWN** | Left portion pushed **DOWN** and right portion pushed **UP** |
| **Bending Moment (M)** | Beam sags (concave **UP**, "smiling") — tension at bottom | Beam hogs (concave **DOWN**, "frowning") — tension at top |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic:** *"Positive BM = Smile, Negative BM = Frown"*. Positive SF creates a clockwise couple on the element; Negative BM creates a sagging shape (like a hammock with weight).

### 1.4 Sign Convention Diagram (Reference)

```
LEFT SIDE OF SECTION          RIGHT SIDE OF SECTION
                                                   
    ↑ +V (Up)                      
    │                              
  ══╪══════[SECTION]═══════►        
    │                              
    │                               +V (Down) — for right side
```

```
POSITIVE BM (Sagging)    │    NEGATIVE BM (Hogging)
   ╭─────╮               │         ╭─────╮
   │  ⌣  │ ←Sags down    │         │  ⌢  │ ←Hogs up
   ╰─────╯               │         ╰─────╯
Tension at BOTTOM        │     Tension at TOP
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Fundamental Differential Relationships

Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ loaded with a distributed load $w(x)$ (intensity, force per unit length):

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the negative sign in the first equation?** This is a convention from mechanics: $w(x)$ is considered positive when acting **downward** (gravity-like), while $V$ is positive when it tries to rotate the element **clockwise**. The downward load reduces $V$ as $x$ increases, hence the negative sign.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Cheat Sheet

| # | Loading Type on Span | Shear Force Diagram (SFD) Shape | Bending Moment Diagram (BMD) Shape | Key Value(s) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Point Load $P$ at free end of cantilever** | Rectangle (constant) | Triangle (linear) | $V = -P$ (constant), $M_{max} = -PL$ at fixed end |
| 2 | **Point Load $P$ at mid-span of SS beam** | Two rectangles (step function) | Two triangles | $V = \pm P/2$, $M_{max} = PL/4$ at center |
| 3 | **UDL $w$ over full span of cantilever** | Triangle (linear from 0 to $-wL$) | Parabola (cubic-like) | $V_{max} = -wL$, $M_{max} = -wL^2/2$ at fixed end |
| 4 | **UDL $w$ over full span of SS beam** | Linear from $+wL/2$ to $-wL/2$ | Parabola (peak at center) | $V = 0$ at center, $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ at center |
| 5 | **Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from left support of SS beam (span $L$)** | Step function | Two straight lines meeting at load point | $V_{left} = Pb/L$, $V_{right} = -Pa/L$, $M_{load\,pt} = Pab/L$ |
| 6 | **UVL (triangle, zero at left, $w_0$ at right) over full SS beam** | Parabolic SFD | Cubic BMD | $R_A = w_0 L/6$, $R_B = w_0 L/3$, $M_{max} = w_0 L^2 / 9\sqrt{3}$ at $x = L/\sqrt{3}$ |
| 7 | **Couple $M_0$ at free end of cantilever** | Zero (no SF) | Rectangle (constant) | $V = 0$, $M = M_0$ throughout |
| 8 | **Couple $M_0$ at mid-span of SS beam** | Zero (no SF) | Step in BMD | $V = 0$, jump of $M_0$ at center |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Convention Used in KTU:** Forces in **kN** (or N), lengths in **m** (or mm), distributed loads in **kN/m** (or N/mm), moments in **kN·m** (or N·mm). Mixing units is a **2-mark deduction** as per KTU valuation norms.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Procedure to Draw SFD & BMD (KTU Board Method)

1. **Step 1 — Reactions:** Calculate all support reactions using equilibrium equations $\sum F_y = 0$ and $\sum M = 0$.
2. **Step 2 — Sections:** Divide the beam into segments based on the type of loading (a new section starts wherever load type changes or a point load is applied).
3. **Step 3 — Shear Force Equation:** For each section, write $V(x)$ as a function of $x$ using the forces on the **left side** (or right side) of the cut.
4. **Step 4 — Bending Moment Equation:** For each section, write $M(x)$ using moments of forces on the left (or right) side about the cut section.
5. **Step 5 — Plot:** Sketch $V(x)$ vs $x$ (SFD) and $M(x)$ vs $x$ (BMD) on the same axis line, marking all critical values.
6. **Step 6 — Locate Maxima:** Set $dM/dx = 0$ (i.e., $V = 0$) to find points of **maximum/minimum bending moment**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Point of Zero Shear = Point of Maximum BM:** This is the most important design rule — the engineer places the **maximum depth section** of the beam at this location.
- **Maximum BM Location for UDL:** For a simply supported beam with UDL, this is at the **midspan** (because SF is zero there).
- **For Cantilever:** The maximum BM occurs at the **fixed end** because the lever arm is maximum.
- **Shear Force Diagram significance:** Shows where the beam needs **shear reinforcement** (closer spacing of stirrups where $V$ is large).
- **Bending Moment Diagram significance:** Shows the **flexural reinforcement** requirements — tension side determines the placement of main steel bars.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Relations

Consider an elemental portion of a beam of length $dx$, subjected to a distributed load $w(x)$ per unit length (acting downward).

The forces acting on this element are:
- Shear force $V$ on the left face (acting downward as per equilibrium convention)
- Shear force $V + dV$ on the right face (acting upward)
- Distributed load $w \cdot dx$ acting downward

**Force equilibrium in vertical direction ($\sum F_y = 0$):**

$$V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0$$

Simplifying:

$$-dV - w \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

**Moment equilibrium about the right face ($\sum M = 0$):**

Taking moments about the right edge of the element (counter-clockwise positive):

$$-M + (M + dM) - V \cdot dx + w \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$

Ignoring the second-order differential $w \cdot dx^2/2$ (infinitesimal of higher order):

$$dM - V \cdot dx = 0$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

**Combining the two results:**

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These three relations are the **backbone of KTU board problems**. Memorize them — they appear in nearly every Part B question either directly or as intermediate steps.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Standard Cases with Worked Derivations

#### **Case A: Cantilever Beam with Point Load at Free End**

**Setup:** A cantilever of length $L$ is fixed at the left end A and carries a point load $P$ at the free end B (downward).

**Reactions at Fixed End A:**

$$R_A = P \quad (\uparrow) \qquad M_A = PL \quad (\text{counter-clockwise, to balance})$$

**Shear Force at a section at distance $x$ from A:**

Considering the left portion (only $R_A$ acts):

$$V(x) = R_A = P \quad \text{(constant throughout)}$$

But by sign convention, $V$ on the **left side of section** acts **downward** on the element when $R_A$ is upward, so:

$$V(x) = -P \quad (\text{negative throughout})$$

**Bending Moment at distance $x$ from A:**

$$M(x) = -R_A \cdot x = -P \cdot x$$

At free end ($x = L$): $M = 0$
At fixed end ($x = 0$): $M = -PL$

$$M_{max} = -PL \quad (\text{magnitude } PL, \text{ hogging})$$

#### **Case B: Simply Supported Beam with Central Point Load**

**Setup:** SS beam of span $L$, point load $P$ at midspan.

**Reactions (by symmetry):**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2} \quad (\uparrow)$$

**For section $0 \leq x < L/2$ (left half):**

$$V(x) = R_A = +\frac{P}{2}$$

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**For section $L/2 < x \leq L$ (right half):**

Considering forces to the right of section: $R_B = P/2 \uparrow$, so on right side the upward force gives:

$$V(x) = -\frac{P}{2}$$

$$M(x) = R_B \cdot (L - x) = \frac{P(L - x)}{2}$$

**At midspan ($x = L/2$):**

$$M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{PL}{4} \quad (\text{sagging, positive})$$

#### **Case C: Simply Supported Beam with UDL Over Entire Span**

**Setup:** SS beam of span $L$, UDL of intensity $w$ per unit length over entire span.

**Reactions (by symmetry):**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**For a section at distance $x$ from A:**

The left portion carries: $R_A$ upward and $w \cdot x$ downward (from UDL over length $x$).

$$V(x) = R_A - w \cdot x = \frac{wL}{2} - wx = w\left(\frac{L}{2} - x\right)$$

**Bending Moment at distance $x$:**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2} \cdot x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

$$M(x) = \frac{w}{2}(Lx - x^2) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

**Location of Maximum BM** (set $dM/dx = 0$):

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = \frac{w}{2}(L - 2x) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{L}{2}$$

**Maximum BM at midspan:**

$$M_{max} = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2}\left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{w}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

**Shear Force values:**
- At $x = 0$: $V = +wL/2$
- At $x = L/2$: $V = 0$ (zero shear = max BM location)
- At $x = L$: $V = -wL/2$

#### **Case D: Cantilever Beam with UDL Over Entire Span**

**Setup:** Cantilever of length $L$, fixed at A, UDL $w$ over entire span.

**Reactions at A:**

$$R_A = wL \quad (\uparrow) \qquad M_A = \frac{wL^2}{2} \quad (\text{counter-clockwise})$$

**Shear Force at distance $x$ from A:**

$$V(x) = R_A - wx = wL - wx = w(L - x)$$

At $x = 0$ (fixed end): $V = +wL$
At $x = L$ (free end): $V = 0$

**Bending Moment at distance $x$:**

$$M(x) = -R_A \cdot x + wx \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -wLx + \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

At $x = 0$: $M = -wL^2/2$ (maximum, hogging)
At $x = L$: $M = -wL^2 + wL^2/2 = -wL^2/2$

Wait, let me recompute carefully — using **left side analysis with sign convention**:

$$M(x) = -wL \cdot x + w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = -wLx + \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$
At $x = L$: $M = -wL^2 + \frac{wL^2}{2} = -\frac{wL^2}{2}$

So the **maximum hogging moment** is at the **fixed end**:

$$|M|_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{2}$$

#### **Case E: Simply Supported Beam with UVL (Triangular Load)**

**Setup:** SS beam, span $L$, UVL with intensity $0$ at A and $w_0$ at B (linearly increasing).

**Total Load:** $W = \frac{1}{2} w_0 L$, acting at centroid $\frac{2L}{3}$ from A.

**Reactions:**

$$\sum M_B = 0: \quad R_A \cdot L = W \cdot \frac{L}{3} = \frac{w_0 L}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{3}$$

$$R_A = \frac{w_0 L}{6}$$

$$\sum F_y = 0: \quad R_B = W - R_A = \frac{w_0 L}{2} - \frac{w_0 L}{6} = \frac{w_0 L}{3}$$

**Load intensity at distance $x$ from A:** $w(x) = \frac{w_0 x}{L}$

**Shear Force at distance $x$:**

$$V(x) = R_A - \frac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot w(x) = \frac{w_0 L}{6} - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L}$$

**Location of $V = 0$:**

$$\frac{w_0 L}{6} - \frac{w_0 x^2}{2L} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x^2 = \frac{L^2}{3} \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}}$$

**Maximum Bending Moment:**

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - \frac{1}{2} \cdot x \cdot w(x) \cdot \frac{x}{3} = \frac{w_0 L x}{6} - \frac{w_0 x^3}{6L}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L}{6} \cdot \frac{L}{\sqrt{3}} - \frac{w_0}{6L} \cdot \frac{L^3}{3\sqrt{3}} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6\sqrt{3}} - \frac{w_0 L^2}{18\sqrt{3}}$$

$$M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6\sqrt{3}}\left(1 - \frac{1}{3}\right) = \frac{w_0 L^2}{6\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{2}{3} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} = \frac{w_0 L^2 \sqrt{3}}{27}$$

$$\boxed{M_{max} = \frac{w_0 L^2}{9\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.0642 \, w_0 L^2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The location $x = L/\sqrt{3} \approx 0.577 L$ is the **point of maximum bending moment** — NOT at midspan (unlike UDL). This is a classic KTU trap question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow — SFD/BMD Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Beam with Loads Given] --> B[Step 1: Identify Supports and Loads]
    B --> C[Step 2: Calculate Support Reactions]
    C --> D{Reactions Verified?}
    D -->|No| E[Recheck Equilibrium: sum Fy and sum M]
    E --> C
    D -->|Yes| F[Step 3: Divide Beam into Segments]
    F --> G[Step 4: Write Vx Equation for Each Segment]
    G --> H[Step 5: Write Mx Equation for Each Segment]
    H --> I[Step 6: Find Critical Points - V=0, x=0, x=L, load points]
    I --> J[Step 7: Compute V and M at all Critical Points]
    J --> K[Step 8: Plot SFD - Shear Force vs x]
    K --> L[Step 9: Plot BMD - Bending Moment vs x]
    L --> M[Step 10: Identify Maximum Vmax and Mmax]
    M --> N[END: Mark values on diagrams]

    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style N fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style C fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style G fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style H fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Differential Relationship Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L["Distributed Load w(x)"] -->|integrate once| V["Shear Force V(x)"]
    V -->|integrate once| M["Bending Moment M(x)"]
    L -->|integrate twice| M2["Bending Moment M(x)"]

    V -->|dV/dx equals -w| L
    M -->|dM/dx equals V| V
    M2 -->|d2M/dx2 equals -w| L

    style L fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style V fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#00008B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style M2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Standard Result Pattern Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CANTILEVER [CANTILEVER BEAM CASES]
        C1[Point Load at Free End] --> C1R[SF: Rectangle, BM: Triangle]
        C2[UDL Over Entire Span] --> C2R[SF: Triangle, BM: Parabola]
        C3[UVL Over Entire Span] --> C3R[SF: Parabola, BM: Cubic]
    end

    subgraph SIMPLY [SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAM CASES]
        S1[Central Point Load] --> S1R[SF: Two Rectangles, BM: Two Triangles]
        S2[UDL Over Full Span] --> S2R[SF: Linear, BM: Parabola Max at Center]
        S3[Point Load at Distance a] --> S3R[SF: Step Function, BM: Triangle Peak at Load Point]
        S4[UVL Triangular] --> S4R[SF: Parabola, BM: Cubic Max at L by sqrt 3]
    end

    subgraph KEYRULE [KEY DESIGN RULE]
        K1[Where V crosses Zero] --> K2[That location has MAXIMUM BM]
    end

    CANTILEVER --> KEYRULE
    SIMPLY --> KEYRULE

    style CANTILEVER fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style SIMPLY fill:#E0FFFF,stroke:#008B8B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style KEYRULE fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#FF4500,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    style K2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 ASCII Schematic — Typical Beam Loading Patterns

```
CANTILEVER BEAMS:

  Case 1: Point Load           Case 2: UDL
  ┌──┐                              ┌──┐
  │██│═══════════════►              │██│═══════════════►
  │██│  L                            │██│↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓│
  │██│                              │██│ w per unit    │
  └──┘                              └──┘
   A   ↓P (free end)                 A    B (free end)


SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAMS:

  Case 1: Point Load           Case 2: UDL
  △                              △
  │██│═══════════════►           │██│═══════════════►
  │██│  ↓P                       │██│↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓↓│
  │██│  at center                │██│   w per unit   │
  △                              △
  A    B                          A    B


LOAD CONVENTION:
  ↑ = Upward Reaction or Force
  ↓ = Downward Applied Load
  △ = Pin/Roller Support (Simply Supported)
  ██ = Fixed/Wall (Cantilever support)
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Shear Force and Bending Moment with neat sketches. State the sign convention used.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Shear Force (SF):** The algebraic sum of all vertical forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of a section of a beam is called the shear force at that section. It is denoted by $V$ or $SF$.

**Bending Moment (BM):** The algebraic sum of the moments of all forces (loads and reactions) acting on either side of a section, taken about the centroid of the section, is called the bending moment. It is denoted by $M$ or $BM$.

**Sign Convention:**
- **Positive SF:** Causes clockwise rotation of the element (left side up, right side down).
- **Negative SF:** Causes anticlockwise rotation of the element.
- **Positive BM:** Sagging (concave up, "smile"), tension at bottom fibers.
- **Negative BM:** Hogging (concave down, "frown"), tension at top fibers.

*(Diagrams: 1 mark; Definitions: 1 mark; Sign convention: 1 mark)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the relation between load intensity, shear force, and bending moment.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The three fundamental differential relations are:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) \qquad \frac{dM}{dx} = V(x) \qquad \frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

**Proof:** Consider an elemental beam of length $dx$ with distributed load $w(x)$.

Vertical equilibrium: $V - (V + dV) - w \cdot dx = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$.

Moment equilibrium: $dM = V \cdot dx \Rightarrow \dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$.

Combining: $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$. **QED.**

*(Three relations: 2 marks; Proof: 1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice Format

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over the entire span and a point load of 20 kN at a distance of 2 m from the left support. Draw the SFD and BMD, and find the position and magnitude of maximum bending moment.**

**(a)** Calculate the support reactions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the SFD and BMD and locate the maximum bending moment. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Support Reactions:**

Let $R_A$ and $R_B$ be the reactions at the left and right supports.

UDL total = $10 \times 6 = 60$ kN acting at midspan (3 m from A).

Taking moments about A ($\sum M_A = 0$):

$$R_B \times 6 = (10 \times 6) \times 3 + 20 \times 2$$

$$R_B \times 6 = 180 + 40 = 220$$

$$R_B = 36.67 \text{ kN}$$

Vertical equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):

$$R_A + R_B = 60 + 20 = 80 \text{ kN}$$

$$R_A = 80 - 36.67 = 43.33 \text{ kN}$$

**(b) SFD and BMD Construction:**

**Section 1: $0 \leq x < 2$ m (between A and point load):**

$$V(x) = 43.33 - 10x$$

At $x = 0$: $V = +43.33$ kN
At $x = 2^-$: $V = 43.33 - 20 = +23.33$ kN

$$M(x) = 43.33x - \frac{10x^2}{2} = 43.33x - 5x^2$$

At $x = 0$: $M = 0$
At $x = 2$: $M = 86.66 - 20 = 66.67$ kN·m

**Section 2: $2 \leq x \leq 6$ m (between point load and B):**

$$V(x) = 43.33 - 20 - 10x = 23.33 - 10x$$

At $x = 2^+$: $V = 23.33 - 20 = +3.33$ kN (drop of 20 kN due to point load)
At $x = 6$: $V = 23.33 - 60 = -36.67$ kN ✓

**Location of zero shear (for max BM):**

$$23.33 - 10x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x = 2.333 \text{ m}$$

$$M_{max} = 43.33(2.333) - 20(0.333) - 5(2.333)^2$$

$$M_{max} = 101.10 - 6.67 - 27.21 = 67.22 \text{ kN·m}$$

**At $x = 6$:** $M = 0$ ✓

**Summary of Values:**

| Position $x$ (m) | $V$ (kN) | $M$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (A) | $+43.33$ | $0$ |
| 2 (load point) | jumps: $+23.33 \to +3.33$ | $66.67$ |
| 2.333 | $0$ | $\mathbf{67.22}$ (max) |
| 6 (B) | $-36.67$ | $0$ |

$$\boxed{M_{max} = 67.22 \text{ kN·m at } x = 2.333 \text{ m from A}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A cantilever beam of length 4 m is fixed at the left end A and carries a uniformly distributed load of 6 kN/m over a length of 3 m from the fixed end, and a point load of 12 kN at the free end. Draw the SFD and BMD and find the maximum bending moment.**

**(a)** Determine the support reactions at the fixed end. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Draw the SFD and BMD, and calculate the maximum bending moment. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reactions at Fixed End A:**

Total UDL = $6 \times 3 = 18$ kN acting at 1.5 m from A (centroid).

Vertical equilibrium:

$$R_A = 18 + 12 = 30 \text{ kN} \quad (\uparrow)$$

Moment equilibrium about A (fixed-end moment):

$$M_A = (18 \times 1.5) + (12 \times 4) = 27 + 48 = 75 \text{ kN·m (counter-clockwise)}$$

**(b) SFD and BMD:**

**Section 1: $0 \leq x \leq 3$ m (within UDL zone), measured from A:**

Considering forces on the **right side** of section (easier for cantilever):

$$V(x) = -[(12) + (6)(3 - x)] = -[12 + 18 - 6x] = -[30 - 6x] = -30 + 6x$$

At $x = 0$: $V = -30$ kN
At $x = 3^-$: $V = -30 + 18 = -12$ kN

$$M(x) = -[12(4 - x) + 6(3 - x) \cdot \frac{(3 - x)}{2}]$$

$$M(x) = -12(4 - x) - 3(3 - x)^2$$

At $x = 0$: $M = -12(4) - 3(9) = -48 - 27 = -75$ kN·m ✓
At $x = 3^-$: $M = -12(1) - 3(0) = -12$ kN·m

**Section 2: $3 \leq x \leq 4$ m (only point load zone):**

$$V(x) = -12 \text{ kN (constant)}$$

$$M(x) = -12(4 - x)$$

At $x = 3$: $M = -12$ kN·m ✓ (continuity)
At $x = 4$: $M = 0$ ✓

**Maximum Bending Moment:**

The BM is **most negative** at the **fixed end** ($x = 0$):

$$|M|_{max} = 75 \text{ kN·m (hogging)}$$

| Position $x$ (m) | $V$ (kN) | $M$ (kN·m) |
|---|---|---|
| 0 (A - fixed) | $-30$ | $\mathbf{-75}$ (max) |
| 3 (end of UDL) | $-12$ | $-12$ |
| 4 (B - free) | $-12$ | $0$ |

$$\boxed{V_{max} = 30 \text{ kN at fixed end}, \quad |M|_{max} = 75 \text{ kN·m at fixed end}}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks (Each = 2 Mark Penalty):**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (Most Frequent!):** Forgetting to mark SF as **negative** in cantilever beams when load is downward. Always write $V = -P$ (or $-wL$), not $+P$.
>
> 2. **Wrong Reaction Direction:** Assuming both reactions are upward without checking. If the answer comes out negative, it means the assumed direction was wrong.
>
> 3. **Missing the "Point of Zero Shear" Step:** In UDL problems, students calculate $M$ at midspan directly as $wL^2/8$ without first proving $V = 0$ there. KTU examiners demand **derivation**, not just formula substitution.
>
> 4. **Confusing Cantilever and Simply Supported:** In cantilever, $M_{max}$ is at the **fixed end**; in simply supported, $M_{max}$ is usually at **midspan** or at the **point load**.
>
> 5. **Forgetting Units:** Writing "M = 67" without "kN·m" → **0.5 mark deducted** per occurrence.
>
> 6. **Not Labeling Diagrams:** SFD and BMD must be labeled clearly with **ordinate values**, **signs (+ or −)**, and **shaded regions** (for BMD in RCC design).
>
> 7. **Mistake in UVL Problems:** Using $wL/2$ as total load for **triangular UVL** (it is $\frac{1}{2}w_0 L$, not $w_0 L$). Centroid is at $\frac{2L}{3}$ from the zero-intensity end.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition Triad:** SF = sum of transverse forces; BM = sum of moments; both are at a cross-section.
- **Key Differential Relations:** $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w$, $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V$, $\dfrac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w$ — memorize these.
- **Sign Convention:** Positive SF = clockwise couple on element; Positive BM = sagging (smile).
- **Standard Formulae to Memorize:**
  - SS beam with central point load: $M_{max} = PL/4$
  - SS beam with UDL: $M_{max} = wL^2/8$ at center
  - Cantilever with end point load: $M_{max} = PL$ at fixed end
  - Cantilever with UDL: $M_{max} = wL^2/2$ at fixed end
- **Design Rule:** Always locate **zero shear** point to find **maximum bending moment** — this is KTU's most-tested concept.
- **Diagram Convention:** SFD is plotted **above the beam line** (for positive values), BMD is plotted on the **tension side** (below for sagging, above for hogging).
- **Symmetry Trick:** For symmetric loading on SS beams, $R_A = R_B$ and max BM is at midspan — save calculation time.
- **Quick Check:** Sum of areas under SFD = change in BM between two sections. This is the **"no calculation" verification** method.
- **Important:** Always **state the units** explicitly in your final answer — a KTU board pet peeve.
- **For Combined Loads:** Superposition principle applies — draw separate SFD/BMD for each load type, then add ordinates algebraically.
- **Bending Moment Sign Trick:** If the **lower fibers are in tension** → positive BM (sagging). For RCC beams, **main reinforcement is provided on the tension side**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Differential Equations Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Differential Equations Between Load, Shear Force and Bending Moment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Strength of Materials** (Mechanics of Solids) framework, the relationship between distributed load intensity $w(x)$, Shear Force $V(x)$, and Bending Moment $M(x)$ along the length of a beam is governed by a set of three first-order and second-order **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)**. These equations form the analytical backbone for constructing Shear Force Diagrams (SFD) and Bending Moment Diagrams (BMD) — two graphical tools that are *mandatory* in every KTU board examination question involving beam analysis.

The three governing relationships are formally stated as:

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

where:
- $w(x)$ = Intensity of distributed load (force per unit length, **N/m**)
- $V(x)$ = Shear force at a section (force, **N**)
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment at a section (force × length, **N·m**)
- $x$ = Longitudinal coordinate along the beam axis (**m**)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** These differential equations are listed as a *core learning outcome* under Module 3 of PCMRT303. A direct 3-mark question on "deriving the relation dV/dx = -w" is a recurring pattern in university exams. Students who skip the sign convention almost always lose full marks.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **The River Analogy:** Imagine a river flowing along the length of a beam. The **distributed load** is like rain falling onto the river — it changes how much water (shear force) is in the channel at any point. The **shear force** is the cumulative flow of water at any cross-section. The **bending moment** is the cumulative "twisting effort" or torque that the water exerts on the banks (the beam's fibres) at that cross-section. The faster the rain falls (steeper load), the faster the river flow changes.

In simpler terms:
- **Load** is the *cause* (an external input).
- **Shear Force** is the *rate of change* of bending moment.
- **Bending Moment** is the *cumulative integral* of shear force.

These equations are essentially the **calculus of beams** — they tell us that SFDs and BMDs are nothing but graphs of derivatives and integrals of the load function $w(x)$.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Sign Convention

For KTU evaluation purposes, the **standard sign convention** (adopted universally in Indian engineering curricula) is:

| Quantity | Positive Sign Meaning |
|----------|----------------------|
| Distributed load $w(x)$ | **Downward** (acting in the direction of gravity) |
| Shear force $V$ | The **left portion** of the beam tends to slide **upward** relative to the right |
| Bending moment $M$ | The beam **sags** (concave upwards), causing tension in the **bottom fibres** |

> [!WARNING]
> The negative sign in $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ is a *direct consequence* of the convention that downward loads are positive. If your textbook uses the opposite convention, the sign flips. KTU strictly follows the *downward-positive load* convention.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Why These Equations Exist

The derivation rests on a single principle: **Static Equilibrium**. For an infinitesimal element of a beam of length $dx$, the net force and net moment must be zero (since the element is in equilibrium).

The logical chain is:

1. **Free Body of an Element:** Cut an infinitesimal slice of length $dx$ from the beam.
2. **Force Balance (Vertical):** Sum of vertical forces = 0 → yields the relationship between load intensity and shear force gradient.
3. **Moment Balance:** Sum of moments about a point on the element = 0 → yields the relationship between bending moment gradient and shear force.
4. **Combining Both:** Differentiating the moment equation and substituting the force equation gives the second-order relation.

## 2.2 The Three Governing Equations Explained

### Equation 1: Load–Shear Relationship
$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

- **The "Why":** The rate of change of shear force at any point equals the negative of the load intensity at that point.
- **The "How":** If you load a beam uniformly ($w$ = constant), the shear force changes **linearly** with slope $-w$.
- **Physical meaning:** A positive (downward) load *reduces* the shear force as you move along $x$ in the positive direction.

### Equation 2: Shear–Moment Relationship
$$\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$$

- **The "Why":** The rate of change of bending moment at any section equals the shear force at that section.
- **The "How":** Where $V = 0$, the bending moment is at an **extremum** (maximum or minimum) — this is a *critical point* for design.
- **Physical meaning:** Shear force is the "slope" of the bending moment diagram.

### Equation 3: Load–Moment Relationship
$$\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$$

- **The "Why":** The second derivative of bending moment equals the negative of the load.
- **The "How":** If load is uniform ($w$ = constant), the bending moment curve is a **parabola**. If load is zero (no distributed load), the bending moment is **linear**.
- **Physical meaning:** Curvature of the BMD is dictated by the load type.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the *high-yield* formula bank. Memorize these — they appear in **every** KTU university exam paper for this module.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Application |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ | Load-shear relation | Slope of SFD = negative of load |
| 2 | $\dfrac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ | Shear-moment relation | Slope of BMD = shear force |
| 3 | $\dfrac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$ | Load-moment relation | Curvature of BMD = negative of load |
| 4 | $\Delta V = -\displaystyle\int_{x_1}^{x_2} w(x)\,dx$ | Change in shear | Jump in SFD = area under load (with sign) |
| 5 | $\Delta M = \displaystyle\int_{x_1}^{x_2} V(x)\,dx$ | Change in moment | Jump in BMD = area under SFD |
| 6 | $V = 0 \Rightarrow \dfrac{dM}{dx} = 0$ | Point of contraflexure / max moment | Locate maxima for design |
| 7 | Uniform load: $V(x) = -wx + C_1$ | Linear SFD | For UDL segments |
| 8 | Uniform load: $M(x) = -\dfrac{wx^2}{2} + C_1 x + C_2$ | Parabolic BMD | For UDL segments |
| 9 | Point load $P$ at $x = a$ | $V$ jumps by $-P$ | Discontinuity in SFD |
| 10 | Couple $M_0$ at $x = a$ | $M$ jumps by $M_0$ | Discontinuity in BMD |

**Standard Units for KTU calculations:**
- Length: **metre (m)** or **millimetre (mm)**
- Force: **Newton (N)** or **kiloNewton (kN)**
- Moment: **N·m** or **kN·m**
- Load intensity: **N/m** or **kN/m**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not merely academic — they are the foundation of:

- **Structural design of buildings:** Beams, lintels, floor slabs
- **Bridge engineering:** Girders, deck slabs, piers
- **Machine design:** Shafts, crane arms, robot linkages
- **Aerospace:** Wing spars, fuselage frames
- **Automotive chassis:** Crash analysis uses these exact relations

A civil engineer who misinterprets a Bending Moment Diagram risks **catastrophic structural failure** — this is why KTU dedicates an entire module to the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$

### Step 1: Consider an Infinitesimal Element
Take a small element of length $dx$ cut from the beam at distance $x$ from the origin. The forces acting on this element are:
- Shear force $V$ on the left face (acting upward, positive)
- Shear force $V + dV$ on the right face (acting downward, positive)
- Distributed load $w(x)$ acting downward over the element

### Step 2: Apply Vertical Force Equilibrium
Sum of vertical forces = 0 (since the element is in static equilibrium):

$$V - (V + dV) - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$$

### Step 3: Simplify the Equation
Expand and cancel the $V$ terms:

$$V - V - dV - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dV - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$$

### Step 4: Rearrange and Divide by $dx$
Move $dV$ to the right-hand side and divide both sides by $dx$:

$$-w(x) \cdot dx = dV$$

$$\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)}$$

> **Logic Check:** If $w(x) > 0$ (downward load), then $\frac{dV}{dx} < 0$, meaning shear force *decreases* as $x$ increases. This matches the physical intuition that the load "consumes" the shear force.

## 3.2 Derivation of $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$

### Step 1: Set Up Moment Equilibrium
On the same element of length $dx$, the bending moments acting are:
- $M$ on the left face (counter-clockwise, positive)
- $M + dM$ on the right face (clockwise, positive)
- Shear $V$ on the right face at a distance $dx$ from the left

### Step 2: Sum Moments About the Left Edge
Taking moments about the left edge of the element (counter-clockwise positive):

$$M - (M + dM) + V \cdot dx - w(x) \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2} = 0$$

### Step 3: Neglect the Higher-Order Term
The term $w(x) \cdot dx \cdot \frac{dx}{2}$ is of order $dx^2$ (second-order infinitesimal), which becomes negligible as $dx \to 0$. We drop it:

$$M - M - dM + V \cdot dx = 0$$

$$-dM + V \cdot dx = 0$$

### Step 4: Rearrange and Divide
$$V \cdot dx = dM$$

$$\boxed{\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)}$$

> **Logic Check:** A positive shear force produces a positive slope in the BMD. This is consistent with the geometric interpretation: the SFD is the "slope meter" for the BMD.

## 3.3 Derivation of $\frac{d^2M}{dx^2} = -w(x)$

### Step 1: Differentiate the Second Equation
Differentiate $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ with respect to $x$:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\frac{dM}{dx}\right) = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

$$\frac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = \frac{dV}{dx}$$

### Step 2: Substitute the First Equation
From the first derivation, we have $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$. Substituting:

$$\boxed{\frac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)}$$

## 3.4 Verification with a Simple Case Study

**Case:** A simply-supported beam of length $L$ with a uniformly distributed load $w$ (downward, constant).

**Solution using the differential equations:**

Step 1: Integrate $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ to get:

$$V(x) = -wx + C_1$$

Step 2: Integrate $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x) = -wx + C_1$:

$$M(x) = -\frac{wx^2}{2} + C_1 x + C_2$$

Step 3: Apply boundary conditions:
- At $x = 0$, $M(0) = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$
- At $x = L$, $M(L) = 0$ → $-\frac{wL^2}{2} + C_1 L = 0$ → $C_1 = \frac{wL}{2}$

Step 4: Final expressions:

$$V(x) = \frac{wL}{2} - wx$$

$$M(x) = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

Step 5: Verify maximum moment occurs at $x = L/2$:

$$M_{\max} = \frac{w(L/2)}{2}\left(L - \frac{L}{2}\right) = \frac{wL^2}{8}$$

This is the **standard KTU textbook result** for a UDL on a simply supported beam — confirming that our differential equations are correct.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Free-Body Diagram of an Infinitesimal Beam Element

The following Mermaid block-diagram represents the analytical interactions of the variables in the differential equations. It is rendered as a functional architecture flow because the original physical FBD requires precise hand-drawn vector arrows, which mermaid cannot perfectly replicate.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["BEAM ELEMENT OF LENGTH dx"] --> B["FORCE BALANCE dV by dx equals NEGATIVE w OF x"]
    A --> C["MOMENT BALANCE dM by dx equals V OF x"]
    B --> D["EQUATION 1 Slope of SFD is NEGATIVE LOAD"]
    C --> E["EQUATION 2 Slope of BMD is SHEAR FORCE"]
    B --> F["DIFFERENTIATE EQ2"]
    F --> G["SUBSTITUTE EQ1 INTO DIFFERENTIATED EQ2"]
    G --> H["EQUATION 3 Curvature of BMD is NEGATIVE LOAD"]
    D --> I["PRACTICAL USE Construct SFD"]
    E --> J["PRACTICAL USE Construct BMD"]
    H --> K["PRACTICAL USE Locate MAX MOMENT and INFLECTION POINTS"]
    I --> L["FINAL OUTPUT DESIGN OF BEAM CROSS SECTION"]
    J --> L
    K --> L
```

## 4.2 Sign Convention Schematic (Functional Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POSITIVE["POSITIVE SIGN CONVENTION PER KTU"]
        P1["LOAD w Downward is POSITIVE"]
        P2["SHEAR V Left side goes UP is POSITIVE"]
        P3["MOMENT M Beam SAGS bottom fibres in TENSION is POSITIVE"]
    end
    subgraph APPLICATION["HOW IT AFFECTS THE EQUATIONS"]
        A1["dV by dx is NEGATIVE w means shear DROPS along beam"]
        A2["dM by dx is V means BMD slope EQUALS shear"]
        A3["d2M by dx2 is NEGATIVE w means BMD CURVES DOWNWARD under UDL"]
    end
    POSITIVE --> APPLICATION
```

## 4.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Load Type vs. SFD/BMD Shape

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q["WHAT IS THE LOAD TYPE?"] --> R1["POINT LOAD P"]
    Q --> R2["UNIFORMLY DISTRIBUTED LOAD w"]
    Q --> R3["UNIFORMLY VARYING LOAD triangular"]
    Q --> R4["NO LOAD between supports"]
    R1 --> S1["SFD is STEP FUNCTION jump of P"]
    R1 --> T1["BMD is LINEAR with kink at load point"]
    R2 --> S2["SFD is LINEAR sloped line"]
    R2 --> T2["BMD is PARABOLIC second degree curve"]
    R3 --> S3["SFD is PARABOLIC"]
    R3 --> T3["BMD is CUBIC third degree curve"]
    R4 --> S4["SFD is HORIZONTAL CONSTANT"]
    R4 --> T4["BMD is LINEAR sloped"]
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: From Differential Equations to Beam Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["INPUTS Beam geometry Support conditions Load type w OF x"]
    START --> APPLY["APPLY EQUILIBRIUM Sum of forces and moments equals zero"]
    APPLY --> SOLVE["SOLVE THE THREE ODEs Integrate with boundary conditions"]
    SOLVE --> VFN["OBTAIN V OF x Shear force function"]
    SOLVE --> MFN["OBTAIN M OF x Moment function"]
    VFN --> PLOT1["PLOT SFD Shear Force Diagram"]
    MFN --> PLOT2["PLOT BMD Bending Moment Diagram"]
    PLOT1 --> CRIT["LOCATE CRITICAL SECTIONS V equals zero Max moment locations"]
    PLOT2 --> CRIT
    CRIT --> DESIGN["DESIGN BEAM Check bending stress and shear stress at critical sections"]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Derive the relation $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$ for a beam element.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Consider an elementary portion of the beam of length $dx$. The forces acting on it are: shear force $V$ on the left face (upward), shear force $V + dV$ on the right face (downward), and downward distributed load $w(x) \cdot dx$ over the length.
- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply vertical equilibrium: $V - (V + dV) - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$, which simplifies to $-dV - w(x) \cdot dx = 0$.
- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Rearranging: $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$. This is the required relation, indicating that the rate of change of shear force equals the negative of load intensity.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: State and explain the physical significance of the relation $\frac{dM}{dx} = V$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (1 mark):** The rate of change of bending moment with respect to $x$ is equal to the shear force at that section, i.e., $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$.
- **Physical significance — slope interpretation (1 mark):** This equation implies that the slope of the Bending Moment Diagram at any point equals the shear force at that point. Therefore, where $V = 0$, the bending moment attains a local maximum or minimum.
- **Design implication (1 mark):** The location of maximum bending moment (critical section for design) is found by locating the point where the shear force is zero — a direct and essential application in beam design.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` (14 Marks)

**Q: A simply supported beam of length 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over its entire span. Using the differential relations between load, shear force, and bending moment, derive the equations of shear force and bending moment. Also, draw the SFD and BMD, and locate the point of maximum bending moment.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Shear Force and Bending Moment Equations (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks):** For a simply supported beam of length $L = 6$ m with UDL $w = 10$ kN/m, the total load is $W = wL = 60$ kN. By symmetry, each support carries:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{10 \times 6}{2} = 30 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks):** Apply $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ and integrate:

$$V(x) = -wx + C_1 = -10x + C_1$$

Apply boundary condition at $x = 0$: $V(0) = R_A = 30$ kN → $C_1 = 30$.

$$\boxed{V(x) = 30 - 10x \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Apply $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$ and integrate:

$$M(x) = \int (30 - 10x)\,dx = 30x - 5x^2 + C_2$$

Apply boundary condition at $x = 0$: $M(0) = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$.

$$\boxed{M(x) = 30x - 5x^2 \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Verification (1 mark):** At $x = 6$ m: $M(6) = 30(6) - 5(36) = 180 - 180 = 0$ ✓ (correct for simply supported beam).

#### Part (b) — SFD, BMD, and Maximum Moment (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): SFD values:**
- At $x = 0$: $V = 30$ kN
- At $x = 3$ m: $V = 30 - 30 = 0$ kN
- At $x = 6$ m: $V = 30 - 60 = -30$ kN
- SFD is a straight line sloping from +30 kN to -30 kN.

**Step 2 (2 marks): BMD values:**
- At $x = 0$: $M = 0$ kN·m
- At $x = 3$ m: $M = 30(3) - 5(9) = 90 - 45 = 45$ kN·m
- At $x = 6$ m: $M = 0$ kN·m
- BMD is a parabola opening downward.

**Step 3 (1 mark): Location of maximum moment:** Set $V = 0$:

$$30 - 10x = 0 \Rightarrow x = 3 \text{ m (midspan)}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks): Maximum moment value:**

$$M_{\max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{10 \times 36}{8} = 45 \text{ kN·m}$$

**SFD & BMD Schematic:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SFD["SHEAR FORCE DIAGRAM kN"]
        SF1["x equals 0 V equals PLUS 30 kN"]
        SF2["x equals 3 m V equals 0 kN"]
        SF3["x equals 6 m V equals MINUS 30 kN"]
    end
    subgraph BMD["BENDING MOMENT DIAGRAM kN m"]
        BM1["x equals 0 M equals 0"]
        BM2["x equals 3 m M equals PLUS 45 kN m MAXIMUM"]
        BM3["x equals 6 m M equals 0"]
    end
    SFD --> BMD
```

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q: A cantilever beam of length 4 m is subjected to a uniformly varying load (UVL) of intensity 0 at the free end and 8 kN/m at the fixed end. Using the differential equations, derive the expressions for shear force and bending moment, and calculate the values at every 1 m interval.**

#### Part (a) — Load Function and Shear Force Derivation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (2 marks): Express the load as a function of $x$.** Let $x$ be measured from the **free end**. At $x = 0$ (free end), $w = 0$. At $x = 4$ m (fixed end), $w = 8$ kN/m. So the load function is:

$$w(x) = 2x \text{ kN/m}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Integrate the first ODE.** From $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x) = -2x$:

$$V(x) = -x^2 + C_1$$

**Step 3 (2 marks): Apply boundary condition.** At the free end ($x = 0$), the shear force equals zero (no load to the right of free end):

$$V(0) = 0 \Rightarrow C_1 = 0$$

$$\boxed{V(x) = -x^2 \text{ kN}}$$

**Verification (1 mark):** At $x = 4$ m: $V(4) = -16$ kN. Total load = $\frac{1}{2} \times 4 \times 8 = 16$ kN, which equals $|V|$ ✓.

#### Part (b) — Bending Moment Derivation and Tabulation (7 marks)

**Step 1 (3 marks): Integrate the second ODE.** From $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x) = -x^2$:

$$M(x) = -\frac{x^3}{3} + C_2$$

Apply boundary condition: At the free end ($x = 0$), $M(0) = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$.

$$\boxed{M(x) = -\frac{x^3}{3} \text{ kN·m}}$$

**Step 2 (2 marks): Tabulate values at every 1 m interval.**

| $x$ (m) | $V(x) = -x^{2}$ (kN) | $M(x) = -\dfrac{x^{3}}{3}$ (kN·m) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 1 | -1 | -0.333 |
| 2 | -4 | -2.667 |
| 3 | -9 | -9.000 |
| 4 | -16 | -21.333 |

**Step 3 (2 marks): Key observations.**
- The SFD is a **parabola** opening downward.
- The BMD is a **cubic curve** (third-degree).
- Maximum moment at the fixed end: $M_{\max} = -21.33$ kN·m (negative indicates hogging — characteristic of cantilever).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (2–3 marks lost frequently):** Students often forget that downward load is **positive** in KTU convention. Writing $\frac{dV}{dx} = +w$ instead of $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ cascades into *every* subsequent calculation, losing 4–5 marks easily.
>
> 2. **Skipping Boundary Conditions (1–2 marks):** Integration constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ MUST be evaluated using the correct boundary conditions. Examiners will not give step marks if you leave them as symbols.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Verify (1 mark):** Always verify your final $V$ and $M$ values at the supports. For a simply supported beam, $M = 0$ at both ends. For a cantilever, $M = 0$ at the free end and $V = 0$ at the free end.
>
> 4. **Units Inconsistency (1 mark):** Mix of kN and N, or m and mm, leads to numerical errors. Stick to one consistent unit system throughout the solution.
>
> 5. **Missing the SFD/BMD Diagram (2–3 marks):** KTU questions explicitly ask to "draw the SFD and BMD." A correct numerical answer without a diagram loses significant marks. Always include a clear, labeled sketch.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- ✅ The three governing differential equations are: $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w(x)$, $\frac{dM}{dx} = V(x)$, and $\frac{d^{2}M}{dx^{2}} = -w(x)$.
- ✅ All three are derived from **static equilibrium** of an infinitesimal beam element of length $dx$.
- ✅ The negative sign in $\frac{dV}{dx} = -w$ comes from the **downward-positive load convention** (KTU standard).
- ✅ **Slope of SFD** = negative of load intensity.
- ✅ **Slope of BMD** = shear force at that section.
- ✅ **Curvature of BMD** = negative of load intensity.
- ✅ The point where $V = 0$ corresponds to **maximum/minimum bending moment** — the critical design section.
- ✅ For **uniform load $w$**: SFD is linear, BMD is parabolic.
- ✅ For **point load $P$**: SFD has a sudden jump of magnitude $P$, BMD has a kink (slope discontinuity).
- ✅ For **point couple $M_0$**: SFD is unaffected, BMD has a sudden jump of magnitude $M_0$.
- ✅ For **no load region**: SFD is constant, BMD is linear.
- ✅ For **UVL/triangular load**: SFD is parabolic, BMD is cubic.
- ✅ Always include a **free-body diagram (FBD)**, an **SFD**, and a **BMD** in long-answer questions.
- ✅ Verify final answers using the support conditions and equilibrium checks.
- ✅ Standard results to memorize: $M_{\max} = \frac{wL^{2}}{8}$ (UDL on SSB), $M_{\max} = \frac{PL}{4}$ (central point load on SSB), $M_{\max} = \frac{wL^{2}}{2}$ (UDL on cantilever).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Normal and Shear Stress in Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Normal and Shear Stress in Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Normal Stress in Beams (Bending Stress)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Normal stress in beams**, also called **flexural stress** or **bending stress**, is the internal longitudinal stress distribution developed on a transverse cross-section of a beam when it is subjected to a **bending moment (M)**. The stress varies **linearly** across the depth of the section, being **maximum at the extreme fibres** (top and bottom) and **zero at the neutral axis**.

The governing relationship is known as the **Flexure Formula** or **Euler-Bernoulli Bending Equation**:

$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

Where:
- $\sigma$ = Bending (normal) stress at a fibre located distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis (N.A.), in **N/m² (Pa)**
- $M$ = Bending moment at the section, in **N·m**
- $y$ = Perpendicular distance from the Neutral Axis to the fibre, in **m**
- $I$ = Second Moment of Area (Area Moment of Inertia) about the Neutral Axis, in **m⁴**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCMRT303, Module 3):** The flexure formula is derived under two **fundamental assumptions** — (1) the beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (Hooke's Law applicable) and (2) **plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis after bending** (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis). Transverse shear deformation is neglected.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine holding a soft sponge rubber slab horizontally with both hands and pressing your **right hand downward** while **lifting your left hand upward** (a couple). The top surface stretches (gets longer) and the bottom surface compresses (gets shorter). Somewhere in the middle there is a layer that doesn't change length at all — this is the **Neutral Axis**.

- The fibres **above** the neutral axis are in **tension** (+) — they are being stretched.
- The fibres **below** the neutral axis are in **compression** (−) — they are being squeezed.
- The stretching/squeezing is **proportional to the distance** from the neutral axis (linear variation).

This linearly varying stress is the **Normal Stress in Beams**. The **extreme fibres** (top and bottom) experience the **maximum stress**, which is why a beam under bending usually fails there first.

---

## 1.2 Shear Stress in Bears (Transverse Shear Stress)

### Formal KTU Definition
**Shear stress in beams** is the internal tangential stress acting **along the longitudinal plane** of the beam due to the action of a **transverse shear force (V)**. Unlike normal stress, the shear stress is **NOT uniformly distributed** across the cross-section. Its intensity at any fibre is given by the **Shear Stress Formula (Zhuravskii / Jourawski Equation)**:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $\tau$ = Transverse shear stress at a horizontal plane, in **N/m² (Pa)**
- $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section, in **N**
- $Q$ = First Moment of Area of the portion of cross-section **beyond the plane** (or between the plane and the extreme fibre) about the Neutral Axis, in **m³**
- $I$ = Second Moment of the entire cross-section about the Neutral Axis, in **m⁴**
- $b$ = Width of the cross-section at the level where shear stress is being calculated, in **m**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Q = First Moment of Area beyond the plane"?** Because shear stress at a level depends on the area of cross-section lying between that level and the free (outer) surface. This area is often denoted as $A'$, and $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$, where $\bar{y}'$ is the centroidal distance of $A'$ from the Neutral Axis.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Take a stack of paper and try to slide the top sheets relative to the bottom sheets. The internal friction that resists this sliding is exactly what **shear stress** represents in a beam. When a beam carries a transverse load, every horizontal layer tries to slide over the adjacent one. The bond between layers resists this sliding, and that resistance is the **horizontal (longitudinal) shear stress**. The **complementary vertical shear stress** of equal magnitude acts on the vertical cross-section — this is what we calculate using the shear formula.

For a **rectangular beam**, the shear stress distribution is **parabolic**:
- **Zero** at the top and bottom extreme fibres
- **Maximum** at the Neutral Axis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Board Exams:** Bending (normal) stress is computed using the **flexure formula** $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$, while shear stress uses the **Zhuravskii formula** $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$. Confusing these two is one of the most common mistakes in KTU valuations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Assumptions of the Euler-Bernoulli Bending Theory

For the normal stress formula to be valid, the following **KTU-required assumptions** must hold:

1. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
2. The material is **homogeneous** (same properties at all points) and **isotropic** (same properties in all directions).
3. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (linearly elastic).
4. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (or bending with negligible shear).
5. **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (no warping).
6. The radius of curvature is **large** compared to beam dimensions (small deflection).
7. Self-weight is **neglected** unless explicitly included.

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — The "Why" Behind the Flexure Formula

The derivation is a classic KTU Module-3 proof. It follows this logic flow:

- **Step 1:** Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the beam, originally bounded by two plane cross-sections.
- **Step 2:** Due to pure bending moment $M$, the element bends into an arc of radius $\rho$ (radius of curvature).
- **Step 3:** A fibre at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis has its length change. The **longitudinal strain** at that fibre is:
$$\epsilon = \frac{\text{change in length}}{\text{original length}} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

- **Step 4:** Applying **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$:
$$\sigma = \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$$

- **Step 5:** For **equilibrium**, the moment of these stresses about the Neutral Axis must equal the applied bending moment $M$:
$$M = \int \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int \frac{E \cdot y^2}{\rho} \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \int y^2 \, dA$$

- **Step 6:** Define the **Second Moment of Area** $I = \int y^2 \, dA$. Thus:
$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

- **Step 7:** Substituting back, the **Flexure Formula** is obtained:
$$\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

## 2.3 Bending Stress — Maximum Value & Section Modulus

The **maximum bending stress** occurs at the **extreme fibre** (farthest from N.A.), at $y = y_{max}$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M \cdot y_{max}}{I} = \frac{M}{Z}$$

Where $Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$ is the **Section Modulus**, measured in **m³**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** A higher Section Modulus $Z$ implies a stronger beam for the same bending moment. This is why **I-sections** (with most material in the flanges, far from the N.A.) are highly efficient as bending members.

## 2.4 Sign Convention (KTU Standard)

| Region of Cross-Section | Bending Direction | Sign of $\sigma$ | Physical State |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Above N.A. ($y > 0$) | **Sagging** (+) M | **+** Tensile | Stretching |
| Below N.A. ($y < 0$) | **Sagging** (+) M | **−** Compressive | Shortening |
| Above N.A. ($y > 0$) | **Hogging** (−) M | **−** Compressive | Shortening |
| Below N.A. ($y < 0$) | **Hogging** (−) M | **+** Tensile | Stretching |

> [!WARNING]
> Always treat $y$ with the sign of the side of the N.A. in KTU problems. KTU examiners often award **½ mark** for incorrectly signed stresses.

## 2.5 Derivation Logic — Shear Stress in Beams (Zhuravskii's Method)

Consider a beam segment of length $dx$ carrying shear force $V$ and bending moment $M$ on the left face, and $M + dM$ on the right face. The vertical forces on the cross-section differ by $dV = V - (V + dV)$... wait, by equilibrium of the segment the **horizontal force imbalance** on the top portion must be balanced by **shear on the horizontal plane**.

- **Step 1:** Consider a fibre at distance $y_1$ from the N.A. Take the **portion of the cross-section ABOVE** this fibre (call it area $A'$).
- **Step 2:** The bending stresses on this portion create a net horizontal force $F_1$ on the left face and $F_1 + dF_1$ on the right face.
- **Step 3:** The **difference** $dF_1$ must be balanced by the **shear force** on the **horizontal plane** of area $b \cdot dx$:
$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = dF_1$$

- **Step 4:** Computing $F_1$ from bending stresses:
$$F_1 = \int_{y_1}^{y_{max}} \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA = \frac{M}{I} \int_{y_1}^{y_{max}} y \, dA = \frac{M \cdot Q}{I}$$

Where $Q = \int y \, dA$ = first moment of area $A'$ about N.A.

- **Step 5:** Therefore, $dF_1 = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I} = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot Q}{I}$ (since $V = \frac{dM}{dx}$).

- **Step 6:** Equating: $\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I} \, dx$, giving the **Shear Formula**:
$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

## 2.6 Shear Stress Distribution in Common Sections

| Cross-Section | $\tau_{max}$ (location) | $\tau_{avg} = V/A$ | Shape of Distribution |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| **Rectangular** ($b \times d$) | **1.5 V/A** at Neutral Axis | $V/A$ | **Parabolic** |
| **Solid Circular** (diameter $D$) | $\frac{4V}{3A}$ at N.A. | $V/A$ | Parabolic |
| **Hollow Circular** (D, d) | $\frac{4V}{3A}\left[\frac{D^2 + Dd + d^2}{D^2 + d^2}\right]$ at N.A. | $V/A$ | Parabolic |
| **Triangular** (apex up) | $\frac{3V}{A}$ at $h/2$ from apex | $V/A$ | Parabolic (skewed) |
| **I-Section** | $\frac{V}{b_{web} \cdot d}$ in web, large at junction | $V/A$ | Almost uniform in web |
| **Thin-walled Tube** (uniform thickness) | $\frac{2V}{A}$ at N.A. | $V/A$ | Parabolic (approx.) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** For a **rectangular beam**, $\tau_{max} = 1.5 \times \tau_{avg}$. For a **circular section**, $\tau_{max} = \frac{4}{3} \times \tau_{avg}$. These numerical multipliers are **directly asked** in KTU exams.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units (SI) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| 1 | $\sigma = \dfrac{M \cdot y}{I}$ | Bending stress at distance $y$ from N.A. | Pa |
| 2 | $\sigma_{max} = \dfrac{M}{Z}$ | Maximum bending stress (extreme fibre) | Pa |
| 3 | $\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{\rho}$ | Master bending relation | — |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$ | Shear stress at a horizontal plane | Pa |
| 5 | $\tau_{max} = \dfrac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot b_{min}}$ | Maximum shear stress (usually at N.A.) | Pa |
| 6 | $Z = \dfrac{I}{y_{max}}$ | Section Modulus | m³ |
| 7 | $M = \dfrac{E \cdot I}{\rho}$ | Moment–Curvature relationship | N·m |
| 8 | $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$ | First moment of the outer area | m³ |
| 9 | $I = \int y^2 \, dA$ | Second Moment of Area definition | m⁴ |
| 10 | $I = \dfrac{bh^3}{12}$ (rectangle) | For rectangular section about centroidal axis | m⁴ |
| 11 | $I = \dfrac{\pi D^4}{64}$ (circle) | For solid circular section | m⁴ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of These Concepts |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Civil Engineering** | Design of RCC beams in buildings, bridges, flyovers. Bending + shear govern reinforcement placement. |
| **Mechanical Engineering** | Design of machine shafts, crane jibs, gear teeth, leaf springs. |
| **Aerospace** | Wing spar design where bending + shear act together. |
| **Shipbuilding** | Hull girder analysis. |
| **Structural Steel** | I-beams, channels, angles — selected for high $Z$ and efficient $I/b$ ratio. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Flexure Formula $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$

**Given:** A beam segment of length $L$, subjected to a pure bending moment $M$. Material is linearly elastic, homogeneous, isotropic.

**Goal:** Derive the expression for normal stress $\sigma$ at a distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis.

---

### Step 1: Geometry of Deformation

Consider two adjacent cross-sections $A$ and $B$, separated by an original length $dx$. Under pure bending, the beam curves into a circular arc of radius $\rho$.

- The Neutral Axis (N.A.) has length equal to $dx$ (it doesn't change).
- A fibre at distance $y$ from the N.A. has **new length**:
$$L' = (\rho + y) \cdot d\theta$$

where $d\theta = \frac{dx}{\rho}$ is the angle subtended at the centre of curvature.

### Step 2: Compute the Strain in the Fibre

The **original length** of the fibre is $dx$. The **change in length** is:

$$\Delta L = L' - dx = (\rho + y) \cdot d\theta - \rho \cdot d\theta = y \cdot d\theta$$

The **longitudinal strain** $\epsilon$ is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{dx} = \frac{y \cdot d\theta}{\rho \cdot d\theta} = \frac{y}{\rho}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Since $d\theta$ cancels, the strain depends **only** on the radius of curvature $\rho$ and the fibre distance $y$. The strain is **linearly proportional** to $y$.

### Step 3: Apply Hooke's Law

For a linearly elastic material, $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$:

$$\sigma = E \cdot \frac{y}{\rho}$$

### Step 4: Equilibrium — Sum of Forces = Zero

The normal stresses on the cross-section form an axial force system. For **pure bending** (no axial force), the resultant axial force must be zero:

$$\int_A \sigma \, dA = 0 \Rightarrow \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} \, dA = 0 \Rightarrow \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y \, dA = 0$$

Since $E/\rho \neq 0$, we conclude:

$$\int_A y \, dA = 0$$

This integral is the **first moment of area** about the N.A. — its vanishing defines the position of the N.A. as the **centroidal axis**.

### Step 5: Equilibrium — Moment of Stress = Applied Moment

Taking moments of the stress distribution about the N.A.:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} \cdot y \, dA = \frac{E}{\rho} \int_A y^2 \, dA$$

By definition, $I = \int_A y^2 \, dA$, so:

$$M = \frac{E \cdot I}{\rho} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$$

### Step 6: Combine the Results

Substituting $\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E \cdot I}$ into $\sigma = \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho}$:

$$\sigma = \frac{E \cdot y}{\rho} = E \cdot y \cdot \frac{M}{E \cdot I} = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}}$$

This is the **Flexure Formula** — the foundation of beam design.

### Step 7: Master Relation

Combining the strain and stress results, the unified flexure relation is:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Hint:** Memorize this master equation. It directly gives any of the three unknowns ($\sigma$, $y$, $\rho$) when the other two are known.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Shear Stress Formula $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$

**Given:** A beam of arbitrary cross-section, carrying a transverse shear force $V$ and bending moment $M(x)$ varying along its length.

**Goal:** Derive the longitudinal (and complementary transverse) shear stress at a fibre located at distance $y_1$ from the N.A.

---

### Step 1: Consider an Element of Length $dx$

The element is bounded by two cross-sections. The left face has bending moment $M$ and the right face has $M + dM$ (since bending moment varies along the beam, $dM/dx = V$).

### Step 2: Isolate the Portion Above the Plane $y = y_1$

A horizontal plane at height $y_1$ from the N.A. divides the cross-section. The **upper portion** (call its area $A'$) extends from $y_1$ to $y_{max}$. The centroid of $A'$ is at distance $\bar{y}'$ from the N.A.

The **first moment of area** of this upper portion about the N.A. is:

$$Q = \int_{A'} y \, dA = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$$

### Step 3: Horizontal Force on the Left Face of the Portion

The normal stress at distance $y$ on the left face is $\sigma_1 = \frac{M \cdot y}{I}$.

The **resultant horizontal force** on the left face (compressive or tensile) is:

$$F_1 = \int_{A'} \sigma_1 \, dA = \int_{A'} \frac{M \cdot y}{I} \, dA = \frac{M}{I} \int_{A'} y \, dA = \frac{M \cdot Q}{I}$$

### Step 4: Horizontal Force on the Right Face of the Portion

The bending moment on the right face is $M + dM$. The horizontal force is:

$$F_2 = \frac{(M + dM) \cdot Q}{I} = \frac{M \cdot Q}{I} + \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I} = F_1 + \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

### Step 5: Net Unbalanced Horizontal Force

The difference between the right and left forces is:

$$dF = F_2 - F_1 = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

### Step 6: Equilibrium on the Horizontal Plane

This unbalanced force $dF$ must be balanced by the **shear force** acting on the horizontal plane of area $b \cdot dx$ (where $b$ is the width of the section at level $y_1$):

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = dF = \frac{dM \cdot Q}{I}$$

### Step 7: Apply $V = dM/dx$

Since shear force equals the rate of change of bending moment, $dM = V \cdot dx$:

$$\tau \cdot b \cdot dx = \frac{V \cdot dx \cdot Q}{I}$$

Dividing both sides by $b \cdot dx$:

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}}$$

This is the **Zhuravskii / Jourawski Shear Formula** — universally required for KTU board exam problems on shear stress.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Example — Worked Derivation: Shear Stress in a Rectangular Beam

**Problem:** A rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$ carries a shear force $V$. Derive the shear stress distribution and find the maximum value.

**Step A — Geometric Quantities:**

For the full section:
$$I = \frac{b \cdot d^3}{12}$$

**Step B — First Moment of Area Q at Level y₁:**

The area above the plane $y = y_1$ is a rectangle of width $b$ and height $(d/2 - y_1)$. Its centroid is at distance:
$$\bar{y}' = \frac{y_1 + d/2}{2} = \frac{y_1}{2} + \frac{d}{4}$$

So:
$$Q = b \left(\frac{d}{2} - y_1\right) \cdot \left(\frac{y_1}{2} + \frac{d}{4}\right) = \frac{b}{2}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right)$$

**Step C — Shear Stress at Level y₁:**

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{V \cdot \frac{b}{2}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right)}{\frac{b d^3}{12} \cdot b} = \frac{6V}{b d^3}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{6V}{b d^3} \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right)$$

**Step D — Maximum Shear Stress (at Neutral Axis, $y_1 = 0$):**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{6V}{b d^3} \cdot \frac{d^2}{4} = \frac{6V}{4 b d} = \frac{3V}{2bd} = \frac{3V}{2A}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5 \times \frac{V}{A}}$$

**Step E — Average Shear Stress:**

$$\tau_{avg} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{V}{bd}$$

**Step F — Ratio:**

$$\frac{\tau_{max}}{\tau_{avg}} = 1.5$$

This confirms the **parabolic distribution** with maximum at the N.A. equal to 1.5 times the average.

> [!NOTE]
