> **KTU Insight:** For a rectangular beam, the shear stress varies as $\tau \propto \left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right)$, which is a **parabola** symmetric about the N.A. — this is a frequently asked KTU question.

---

## 3.4 Master Reference Table for $Q$ in Common Sections

| Cross-Section | $Q$ at Neutral Axis | $\bar{y}'$ of $A'$ | $b$ (at N.A.) |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Rectangle** ($b \times d$) | $\dfrac{b d^2}{8}$ | $d/4$ | $b$ |
| **Solid Circle** ($D$) | $\dfrac{D^3}{12}$ | $\dfrac{4D}{3\pi}$ | $D$ |
| **Hollow Circle** ($D, d$) | $\dfrac{D^3 - d^3}{12}$ | varies | $D - d$ (if thin) |
| **I-Section** (flanges + web) | $Q_{flange} + Q_{web}$ | computed in parts | $b_w$ (web) |
| **T-Section** | $Q_{flange} + Q_{web}$ | computed in parts | varies |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Step-by-Step Rule for I-Section / Built-up Sections:** Compute $Q$ as the **sum of first moments of each individual rectangle** about the N.A. Use $Q = A_1 \bar{y}_1 + A_2 \bar{y}_2 + \ldots$ where the area is taken **above (or below)** the plane of interest.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bending Stress Distribution Diagram (Linear Variation)

This Mermaid diagram visualises how the **normal (bending) stress** varies linearly across the depth of a beam under **sagging moment**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Beam Cross-Section: Rectangle b × d"] --> B["Sagging Moment M applied at the section"]
    B --> C["Top Fibre: y = +d/2, σ = +σmax (Tension)"]
    B --> D["Neutral Axis: y = 0, σ = 0"]
    B --> E["Bottom Fibre: y = -d/2, σ = -σmax (Compression)"]
    C --> F["Linear Distribution: σ = My/I"]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G["Max Bending Stress: σmax = M / Z"]
    G --> H["Where Z = I / ymax = bd² / 6"]
```

## 4.2 Shear Stress Distribution Diagram (Parabolic Variation)

This Mermaid diagram shows the **transverse shear stress** distribution across a rectangular section.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Shear Force V applied to a Rectangle b × d"] --> B["Stress is Zero at Top Fibre: y = +d/2"]
    A --> C["Stress Increases Parabolically Towards N.A."]
    A --> D["τmax = 1.5 V / A at the Neutral Axis y = 0"]
    A --> E["Stress Decreases Parabolically Below N.A."]
    A --> F["Stress is Zero at Bottom Fibre: y = -d/2"]
    B --> G["Distribution: τ = (6V / bd³) × (d²/4 - y²)"]
    C --> G
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["Average Shear: τavg = V / A = V / bd"]
    G --> I["Ratio: τmax / τavg = 1.5"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture: Shear Stress Calculation Flow

This Mermaid block depicts the **step-by-step functional flow** for solving any KTU shear stress problem using the Zhuravskii formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Problem: Calculate Shear Stress at level y₁"]) --> S1["Step 1: Identify Shear Force V at the section"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Calculate I of FULL section about N.A."]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify area A' OUTSIDE the level y₁"]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Locate centroid of A' → distance ȳ' from N.A."]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Compute Q = A' × ȳ'"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Measure width b at level y₁"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Substitute in τ = VQ / I b"]
    S7 --> S8["Step 8: For τmax, evaluate at N.A. where b is minimum"]
    S8 --> End(["Result: Shear Stress Distribution"])
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Bending Stress Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["Problem: Calculate Bending Stress"]) --> S1["Step 1: Compute Bending Moment M at the section"]
    S1 --> S2["Step 2: Identify N.A. and Extreme Fibre distance ymax"]
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Calculate I about N.A."]
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Section Modulus Z = I / ymax"]
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: σmax = M / Z (at extreme fibre)"]
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: For intermediate y, use σ = M y / I"]
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Apply sign: + tensile, - compressive"]
    S7 --> End(["Result: Linear Stress Profile"])
```

## 4.5 Comparative Visual: Normal vs Shear Stress Behaviour

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Normal Stress σ"] --> A1["Linear across depth"]
    A --> A2["Max at extreme fibre y = ±d/2"]
    A --> A3["Zero at N.A."]
    A --> A4["Source: Bending Moment M"]
    A --> A5["Formula: σ = My / I"]
    B["Shear Stress τ"] --> B1["Parabolic across depth (rectangle)"]
    B --> B2["Max at N.A. for most sections"]
    B --> B3["Zero at extreme fibres"]
    B --> B4["Source: Shear Force V"]
    B --> B5["Formula: τ = VQ / Ib"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Diagram Question Tip:** Examiners often ask: *"Sketch the bending and shear stress distribution for a rectangular beam."* Use the two facts — bending is **linear** with maxima at the extremes; shear is **parabolic** with maximum at the N.A. Always label **units** and **signs**.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: State the assumptions used in the derivation of the flexure formula.**

**Model Answer:**

The flexure formula $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$ is derived under the following assumptions:

1. The beam material is **homogeneous** and **isotropic** (uniform elastic properties throughout).
2. The material obeys **Hooke's Law** (stress is directly proportional to strain).
3. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
4. **Plane sections before bending remain plane and perpendicular to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
5. The beam is subjected to **pure bending** (effect of transverse shear is neglected).
6. The **radius of curvature is large** compared to the cross-sectional dimensions (small deflection).
7. The beam is **free from any initial stresses** before loading.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Write the shear stress formula in beams. What is the significance of $Q$ in the formula?**

**Model Answer:**

The **shear stress formula** (Zhuravskii's equation) is:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Where:
- $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section
- $Q$ = First moment of the area of the cross-section **beyond the level** (between the level of interest and the extreme free surface) about the Neutral Axis
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the **entire** cross-section about the Neutral Axis
- $b$ = Width of the section at the level where shear stress is calculated

**Significance of $Q$:**
- $Q$ is a **geometric property** of the cross-section.
- It quantifies the **contribution of the outer area** to the shear stress at a given level.
- At the Neutral Axis, $Q = Q_{max}$, which (combined with minimum $b$) gives the **maximum shear stress** in the section.
- It is calculated as $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}'$, where $A'$ is the area beyond the level and $\bar{y}'$ is its centroidal distance from the N.A.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (KTU ESE Module Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**A simply supported beam of span 6 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 20 kN/m over its entire length. The beam has a rectangular cross-section 200 mm wide and 400 mm deep. Find:**
**(a)** The maximum bending stress in the beam.
**(b)** The maximum shear stress in the beam and the shear stress at a level 100 mm above the neutral axis.

---

#### Solution

**Step 1 — Reactions and Maximum Internal Actions:**

For a simply supported beam with UDL:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{20 \times 6}{2} = 60 \text{ kN}$$

Maximum bending moment at mid-span:
$$M_{max} = \frac{wL^2}{8} = \frac{20 \times 6^2}{8} = 90 \text{ kN·m} = 90 \times 10^3 \text{ N·m}$$

Maximum shear force at supports:
$$V_{max} = 60 \text{ kN} = 60 \times 10^3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Section Properties (Rectangle 200 mm × 400 mm):**

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.4)^3}{12} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.064}{12} = 1.0667 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

$$Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}} = \frac{I}{d/2} = \frac{b d^2}{6} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.4)^2}{6} = 5.333 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

$$A = b \times d = 0.2 \times 0.4 = 0.08 \text{ m}^2$$

#### (a) Maximum Bending Stress

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max}}{Z} = \frac{90 \times 10^3}{5.333 \times 10^{-3}} = 16.875 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_{max} = 16.875 \text{ MPa}}$$

This occurs at the top and bottom extreme fibres at mid-span.

#### (b) Maximum Shear Stress and Shear Stress at 100 mm Above N.A.

**Maximum shear stress (at N.A.):**
$$\tau_{max} = 1.5 \times \frac{V}{A} = 1.5 \times \frac{60 \times 10^3}{0.08} = 1.125 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 1.125 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Shear stress at 100 mm above N.A. (i.e., at $y_1 = 0.1$ m):**

Area above the level $y_1 = 0.1$ m is a rectangle of width $b = 0.2$ m and height $(d/2 - y_1) = (0.2 - 0.1) = 0.1$ m.

Centroid of this area from N.A.:
$$\bar{y}' = y_1 + \frac{(d/2 - y_1)}{2} = 0.1 + 0.05 = 0.15 \text{ m}$$

$$Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}' = (0.2 \times 0.1) \times 0.15 = 3 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Width at the level:
$$b = 0.2 \text{ m}$$

Shear stress:
$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b} = \frac{60 \times 10^3 \times 3 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0667 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.2} = \frac{180}{2.1334 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\tau = 0.84375 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 0.844 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{y=100\text{mm}} = 0.844 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Verification by parabolic formula:**
$$\tau = \frac{6V}{b d^3}\left(\frac{d^2}{4} - y_1^2\right) = \frac{6 \times 60 \times 10^3}{0.2 \times (0.4)^3}\left(\frac{(0.4)^2}{4} - (0.1)^2\right) = 0.844 \text{ MPa} \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 — Alternative Choice]**

**An I-section beam has the following dimensions: Flange width = 200 mm, Flange thickness = 20 mm, Web thickness = 20 mm, Overall depth = 300 mm. If the maximum shear force on the beam is 80 kN, find:**
**(a)** The moment of inertia of the section about the neutral axis.
**(b)** The maximum shear stress in the beam.

---

#### Solution

**Section Layout:**
- Overall depth $D = 300$ mm = 0.3 m
- Flange width $b_f = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Flange thickness $t_f = 20$ mm = 0.02 m
- Web thickness $t_w = 20$ mm = 0.02 m
- Web height $h_w = D - 2 t_f = 300 - 40 = 260$ mm = 0.26 m

**Note:** Section is symmetric about horizontal N.A. (passing through mid-depth).

#### (a) Moment of Inertia

Using the **subtraction method**: treat the I-section as a large rectangle $(b_f \times D)$ minus the two side cut-outs.

**Method 1: Direct Calculation (Sum of parts)**

Top flange + Bottom flange (each about N.A.):
$$I_{flange} = \frac{b_f \cdot t_f^3}{12} + b_f \cdot t_f \cdot \left(\frac{D - t_f}{2}\right)^2$$

$$I_{flange} = \frac{0.2 \times (0.02)^3}{12} + 0.2 \times 0.02 \times (0.14)^2$$

$$I_{flange} = 1.333 \times 10^{-7} + 7.84 \times 10^{-4} = 7.841 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

For both flanges: $2 \times 7.841 \times 10^{-4} = 1.568 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$

Web (about its own centroidal axis = N.A. of the whole section):
$$I_{web} = \frac{t_w \cdot h_w^3}{12} = \frac{0.02 \times (0.26)^3}{12} = \frac{0.02 \times 0.017576}{12} = 2.929 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^4$$

Total:
$$I = 1.568 \times 10^{-3} + 2.929 \times 10^{-5} = 1.597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4$$

$$\boxed{I = 1.597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^4}$$

#### (b) Maximum Shear Stress

**Maximum shear stress occurs in the web at the neutral axis**, where $Q$ is maximum and $b$ is minimum (= web thickness $t_w$).

**Compute $Q$ at the N.A. = first moment of area of the section above the N.A. (flange + half-web).**

- Top flange: $A_f = b_f \times t_f = 0.2 \times 0.02 = 4 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
  - Distance from N.A. to flange centroid: $\bar{y}_f = \frac{D - t_f}{2} = \frac{0.3 - 0.02}{2} = 0.14$ m

- Half web (above N.A.): $A_{hw} = t_w \times \frac{h_w}{2} = 0.02 \times 0.13 = 2.6 \times 10^{-3}$ m²
  - Distance from N.A. to half-web centroid: $\bar{y}_{hw} = \frac{h_w/2}{2} = \frac{0.13}{2} = 0.065$ m

$$Q_{max} = A_f \cdot \bar{y}_f + A_{hw} \cdot \bar{y}_{hw}$$

$$Q_{max} = (4 \times 10^{-3})(0.14) + (2.6 \times 10^{-3})(0.065)$$

$$Q_{max} = 5.6 \times 10^{-4} + 1.69 \times 10^{-4} = 7.29 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3$$

**Shear stress in web at N.A.:**
$$\tau_{max} = \frac{V \cdot Q_{max}}{I \cdot t_w} = \frac{80 \times 10^3 \times 7.29 \times 10^{-4}}{1.597 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.02}$$

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{58.32}{3.194 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.826 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} = 1.826 \text{ MPa}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the parallel axis term** for the flange in computing $I$ — the flange centroid is **not** at the N.A. Students forget the $A \cdot d^2$ term and lose up to **3 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing $Q$ for shear stress** with $Q$ for whole section — KTU students often use full web area instead of half-web.
> 3. **Using flange width** as $b$ for the N.A. calculation in an I-section — the correct $b$ at N.A. is the **web thickness**, not the flange width.
> 4. **Sign errors** in $y$ when using the flexure formula — always check whether the fibre is in tension or compression and apply sign accordingly.
> 5. **Unit errors** — KTU strictly requires SI units: $I$ in m⁴, $Q$ in m³, $V$ in N, $b$ in m, stress in Pa (or N/mm² = MPa).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### Quick-Reference Checklist (Last-Minute Revision)

- [x] **Flexure Formula:** $\sigma = \frac{My}{I}$ — derived from Hooke's Law + plane-section assumption
- [x] **Maximum Bending Stress:** $\sigma_{max} = \frac{M}{Z}$, where $Z = \frac{I}{y_{max}}$
- [x] **Section Modulus** is a measure of bending strength — higher $Z$ means stronger section
- [x] **Shear Formula:** $\tau = \frac{VQ}{Ib}$ (Zhuravskii / Jourawski equation)
- [x] **$Q$ = first moment of area** beyond the level about the N.A.; maximum at the N.A.
- [x] **Rectangular beam:** $\tau_{max} = 1.5 \times \frac{V}{A}$ at N.A. (parabolic distribution)
- [x] **Solid circular beam:** $\tau_{max} = \frac{4V}{3A}$ at N.A.
- [x] **I-section:** Max $\tau$ occurs in the web at N.A.; use web thickness as $b$
- [x] **Master Relation:** $\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{\rho}$
- [x] **Sign of $\sigma$:** +ve = tensile, −ve = compressive. Sagging beam → top fibre tensile, bottom fibre compressive
- [x] **Plane sections remain plane and perpendicular to N.A.** — Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis
- [x] **Homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic** — three pillars of bending theory
- [x] **SI Units only** in KTU solutions: $I$ in m⁴, $Q$ in m³, $\sigma,\tau$ in Pa (N/m²) or MPa
- [x] **Complementary shear:** Horizontal longitudinal shear = vertical transverse shear (equal magnitude)
- [x] **$Q_{max}$ at N.A., $b_{min}$ at N.A. (for I-section):** together they give $\tau_{max}$

### Common KTU Numerical Traps

| Trap | Correct Approach |
| :--- | :--- |
| Using $\frac{M}{A}$ instead of $\frac{M}{Z}$ | $Z$ depends on section shape; $A$ is wrong for bending |
| Forgetting units (mixing mm and m) | Convert all dimensions to **m** consistently |
| Computing $Q$ wrongly for built-up sections | Sum the first moment of each rectangle separately |
| Treating $b$ as full width in I-sections | At the web, $b$ = **web thickness** $t_w$ |
| Ignoring sign of $y$ | Always carry the sign (above N.A. = +ve, below = −ve) |
| Confusing $I_{xx}$ and $I_{yy}$ | Use $I$ about the axis **perpendicular to the load** (usually $I_{xx}$ for vertical load) |

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy:** In ESE 14-mark questions, **always sketch the stress distribution** as a final step. Examiners often award **½ to 1 mark** for a correctly labelled diagram showing the linear (bending) and parabolic (shear) stress profiles. This small effort can push a 12-mark answer to 14 marks!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Derivation of Shear Stress Distribution for a Rectangular Section
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Derivation of Shear Stress Distribution for a Rectangular Section

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCMRT303 – Mechanics of Solids)**, the **Shear Stress Distribution across a Rectangular Cross-Section** refers to the variation of transverse (vertical) shear stress $\tau$ acting on horizontal fibers (laminae) within a beam of rectangular cross-section when subjected to a transverse shear force $V$.

The derivation is fundamentally based on the **complementary shear theorem** (proved in Module 3) and the **general shear stress formula** (often called Zhuravskii's formula or the **Flexure Shear Formula**):

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

where:
- $V$ = Transverse shear force at the section
- $Q$ = First moment of area of the portion of the cross-section lying beyond the fiber under consideration, taken about the Neutral Axis (NA)
- $I$ = Second moment of area of the entire cross-section about the NA
- $b$ = Width of the cross-section at the fiber where shear stress is computed

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** The quantity $Q$ is *always* measured about the Neutral Axis (NA), and the area considered for $Q$ must lie *on one side only* of the fiber being analyzed. Most students lose marks by taking $Q$ about a wrong axis or by considering the wrong portion of the cross-section.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Real-World Intuition

Imagine a thick **deck of playing cards** glued along the edges (representing the beam). If you push the top of the deck sideways while the bottom is held fixed, each card slides slightly relative to its neighbor. The *stickiness between the cards* represents **shear stress**. In a beam:

- The **top and bottom fibers** carry the *most* bending (axial) stress but **zero transverse shear stress** because they are free edges.
- The **middle fiber (Neutral Axis)** carries the **maximum shear stress** because the layer above and below it has the largest accumulated "pulling-back" tendency.
- Hence, shear stress varies from **zero at the top/bottom** to a **maximum at the neutral axis**, producing a **parabolic** profile across the depth — not a uniform distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is this important in Kerala engineering practice?**
> KTU examiners frequently test this topic because **timber beams in traditional Kerala roofing**, **reinforced concrete lintels**, and **T-beam/B-beam flanges in civil structures** rely on accurate shear stress predictions to prevent *horizontal shear cracking* (a common failure mode seen in short, deep beams).

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit (SI) | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shear Force | $V$ | **N (Newton)** | Net transverse force on the section |
| Bending Stress / Moment of Inertia | $I$ | **m⁴** | Resistance to bending of full section |
| Width of section | $b$ | **m** | Width at the fiber being checked |
| Depth of section | $d$ | **m** | Total depth of rectangular beam |
| Shear Stress | $\tau$ | **Pa (N/m²)** or **N/mm²** | The output quantity |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Stepwise Logic

The derivation of shear stress distribution in a rectangular section rests on four pillars which the KTU examiner expects students to explicitly state:

**Pillar 1 — Equilibrium of Horizontal Forces on a Cut Element**
A small elemental block of the beam, of length $dx$, when subjected to bending moments $M$ on the left face and $M + dM$ on the right face, develops unequal bending stresses on opposite faces. The net horizontal force imbalance is **resisted by the horizontal shear force** on the bottom face of the element.

**Pillar 2 — Complementary Shear Theorem**
Horizontal shear on the bottom face implies an *equal vertical shear* on the vertical face of the element (this is why a transverse force creates both vertical and horizontal shear in beams).

**Pillar 3 — First Moment of Area ($Q$)**
The horizontal force on the bottom face equals the integral of bending stress over the area above the cut. This integral simplifies to $\sigma \cdot dA \rightarrow V \cdot Q / I$, where $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$.

**Pillar 4 — Parabolic Variation**
Substituting the specific geometry of a rectangle into $Q$ (a function of $y$) produces a quadratic expression in $y$ — geometrically, this is a **parabola**.

### 2.2 Why Maximum Shear Stress Occurs at the Neutral Axis (The 'Why')

- The fibers at the extreme top and bottom have **no area beyond them** on one side, so $Q = 0 \Rightarrow \tau = 0$.
- The fiber at the neutral axis has **half the section's area** beyond it, so $Q$ is maximum $\Rightarrow \tau$ is maximum.
- The variation of $Q$ with $y$ is **quadratic**, while $b$ is constant for a rectangle → so $\tau$ is purely quadratic in $y$.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula needed to solve any KTU question on this topic. Memorize this sheet thoroughly — it is the single most-tested derivation in Module 4.

| # | Formula | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = \dfrac{V Q}{I b}$ | General flexure shear formula (Zhuravskii) | Foundation for all shear stress problems |
| 2 | $I = \dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}$ | Moment of inertia of rectangle about NA | Substituting geometry |
| 3 | $A' = b \left(\dfrac{d}{2} - y\right)$ | Area of rectangle below the fiber at distance $y$ from NA | Used to find $Q$ |
| 4 | $\bar{y} = \dfrac{1}{2}\left(y + \dfrac{d}{2}\right) = \dfrac{2y + d}{4}$ | Centroidal distance of $A'$ from NA | Used to find $Q$ |
| 5 | $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y} = \dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$ | First moment of area (rectangle) | Plug into formula 1 |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{6V}{b d^{3}}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$ | **Final shear stress equation for rectangle** | Final derived result |
| 7 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2 b d} = \dfrac{3}{2}\cdot\dfrac{V}{A} = 1.5\,\tau_{\text{avg}}$ | Maximum shear stress (at $y=0$) | KTU favourite result |
| 8 | $\tau_{\text{avg}} = \dfrac{V}{A} = \dfrac{V}{bd}$ | Average shear stress | Used to compare with $\tau_{\max}$ |
| 9 | $\tau = 0$ | At extreme fibers ($y = \pm d/2$) | Boundary condition |

**Boundary Conditions Checklist (for full marks):**
- At $y = 0$ (Neutral Axis): $\tau = \tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd}$
- At $y = \pm \dfrac{d}{2}$ (Top/Bottom): $\tau = 0$
- Distribution shape: **Parabolic (Quadratic in $y$)**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Timber Beam Design (Kerala)** | Ensures horizontal shear failure does not occur in traditional wooden joists used in sloped-roof houses. |
| **Steel I-Section Webs** | Although the question is for a rectangle, the *same formula* is applied to the web of I-sections, making this derivation the **building block for all built-up sections**. |
| **Reinforced Concrete** | Critical for designing **stirrups** and checking **diagonal shear cracks** in deep beams and short lintels. |
| **Machine Tool Frames** | Rectangular guideways in lathe beds are designed considering shear stress concentration at neutral axis. |

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Expectation:** For full marks on a 14-mark derivation question, the KTU Board strictly requires (a) a clear free-body diagram explanation, (b) every algebraic step shown, (c) the final expression boxed, and (d) the values of $\tau_{\max}$ and boundary conditions explicitly stated. Skipping the derivation of $Q$ costs at least 4 marks.

### 3.1 Setup and Geometry

Consider a simply-supported rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$, subjected to a transverse shear force $V$ at the section under consideration.

**Coordinate System:**
- Origin: Centroid of the cross-section (i.e., the Neutral Axis).
- $y$-axis: Measured from the NA (positive downward in our convention).
- Total depth varies from $y = -d/2$ (top) to $y = +d/2$ (bottom).

**Key Geometric Quantities of the Rectangle:**

$$I = \frac{b d^{3}}{12} \quad \text{(about the Neutral Axis)}$$

$$A = b \cdot d \quad \text{(Total cross-sectional area)}$$

### 3.2 Identifying the Fiber and Computing $Q$

We wish to determine the shear stress at a horizontal fiber located at a vertical distance $y$ below the Neutral Axis (where $-d/2 \le y \le +d/2$).

**Step 1 — Area Beyond the Fiber:**
Consider the portion of the cross-section lying **below** the fiber (from $y$ to $+d/2$). The width is uniform $b$, so:

$$A' = b \cdot \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)$$

**Step 2 — Centroidal Distance of $A'$ from NA:**
The area $A'$ is a rectangle extending from $y$ to $d/2$. Its centroid is located at the mid-point of this range:

$$\bar{y} = \frac{y + \dfrac{d}{2}}{2} = \frac{2y + d}{4}$$

**Step 3 — First Moment of Area $Q$:**

$$Q = A' \cdot \bar{y}$$

$$Q = b \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right) \cdot \frac{2y + d}{4}$$

**Step 4 — Algebraic Simplification of $Q$:**
Notice that $\left(\dfrac{d}{2} - y\right)$ and $\left(\dfrac{2y + d}{4}\right)$ together form a difference-of-squares pattern:

$$Q = \frac{b}{4} \left(\frac{d}{2} - y\right)(d + 2y)$$

$$Q = \frac{b}{4} \left[\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)(d) + \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)(2y) - y(d) - y(2y)\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{b}{4} \left[\frac{d^{2}}{2} + d y - d y - 2y^{2}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{b}{4} \left[\frac{d^{2}}{2} - 2y^{2}\right]$$

$$Q = \frac{b}{2} \left[\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right]$$

### 3.3 Substituting into the General Shear Formula

The general shear stress equation is:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot Q}{I \cdot b}$$

Substituting $Q$ and $I$:

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot \dfrac{b}{2}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)}{\left(\dfrac{b d^{3}}{12}\right) \cdot b}$$

**Step 5 — Cancellation:**

$$\tau = \frac{V \cdot b \left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)}{2} \cdot \frac{12}{b^{2} d^{3}}$$

$$\tau = \frac{12 V}{2 b d^{3}} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{6 V}{b d^{3}} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)\;}$$

This is the **final equation for shear stress distribution** at any fiber located at distance $y$ from the Neutral Axis of a rectangular beam.

### 3.4 Maximum and Minimum Shear Stress Analysis

**Maximum Shear Stress (at $y = 0$):**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - 0\right) = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}} \cdot \frac{d^{2}}{4} = \frac{3V}{2 b d}$$

Since $A = b d$:

$$\boxed{\;\tau_{\max} = \frac{3V}{2A} = 1.5\,\tau_{\text{avg}}\;}$$

**Minimum Shear Stress (at extreme fibers $y = \pm d/2$):**

$$\tau_{\min} = \frac{6V}{b d^{3}} \left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - \frac{d^{2}}{4}\right) = 0$$

### 3.5 Alternative (Clean) Form of the Shear Stress Equation

Dividing both sides of the final equation by $\tau_{\max}$:

$$\frac{\tau}{\tau_{\max}} = \frac{\dfrac{6V}{bd^{3}}\left(\dfrac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)}{\dfrac{3V}{2bd}} = \frac{4}{d^{2}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right)$$

$$\frac{\tau}{\tau_{\max}} = 1 - \frac{4y^{2}}{d^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\tau = \tau_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{4y^{2}}{d^{2}}\right)\;}$$

This compact form is **highly preferred by KTU examiners** for the final answer because it directly shows the parabolic nature and matches the boundary conditions at $y = \pm d/2$.

### 3.6 Verification of Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Value of $y$ | $\tau$ from equation | Expected | ✓ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| At Neutral Axis | $y = 0$ | $\tau_{\max}(1 - 0) = \tau_{\max}$ | $\dfrac{3V}{2bd}$ | ✓ |
| At Top Fiber | $y = -d/2$ | $\tau_{\max}\left(1 - 4 \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}/4}{d^{2}}\right) = 0$ | $0$ | ✓ |
| At Bottom Fiber | $y = +d/2$ | $\tau_{\max}\left(1 - 4 \cdot \dfrac{d^{2}/4}{d^{2}}\right) = 0$ | $0$ | ✓ |

### 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Shape of the Curve

Since $\tau$ is a **quadratic function of $y$** (with a negative coefficient for $y^{2}$), the curve is a **downward-opening parabola** symmetric about the Neutral Axis. Numerical tabulation confirms:

| $y / (d/2)$ | $\tau / \tau_{\max}$ | Position |
|---|---|---|
| $-1.0$ | $0$ | Top extreme fiber |
| $-0.5$ | $0.75$ | Upper quarter-depth |
| $0.0$ | $1.00$ | Neutral Axis (max) |
| $+0.5$ | $0.75$ | Lower quarter-depth |
| $+1.0$ | $0$ | Bottom extreme fiber |

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C["Step 3: Identify horizontal fiber at distance y from NA"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Compute area A dash below the fiber"]
    D --> D1["A dash equals b times d half minus y"]
    C --> E["Step 5: Compute centroidal distance y bar of A dash from NA"]
    E --> E1["y bar equals 2y plus d divided by 4"]
    D1 --> F["Step 6: First moment of area Q equals A dash times y bar"]
    E1 --> F
    F --> F1["Q equals b by 2 times d squared by 4 minus y squared"]
    F1 --> G["Step 7: Substitute Q and I into general shear formula"]
    G --> G1["tau equals VQ divided by I times b"]
    G1 --> H["Step 8: Simplify to obtain final equation"]
    H --> H1["tau equals 6V by bd cubed times d squared by 4 minus y squared"]
    H1 --> I["Step 9: Apply boundary condition y equals 0"]
    I --> I1["tau max equals 3V divided by 2bd equals 1.5 times tau average"]
    H1 --> J["Step 10: Apply boundary condition y equals plus or minus d by 2"]
    J --> J1["tau min equals 0 at extreme fibers"]
    I1 --> K["Step 11: Final compact form tau equals tau max times 1 minus 4y squared by d squared"]
    J1 --> K
    K --> L["Parabolic distribution across depth with max at NA"]
```

> Since a true 3D beam schematic cannot be rendered in Mermaid, the following **conceptual schematic** describes the FBD that the KTU examiner expects you to draw on paper:

```
        ↑ σ_max (tension)
   ┌─────────────────────────┐
   │  ─────────────────────  │ ← y = -d/2 : τ = 0
   │  ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒  │
   │  ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒  │
   │═════════════════════════│ ← y = -d/4 : τ = 0.75 τ_max
   │█████████████████████████│
   │═════════════════════════│ ← y = 0 (NA) : τ = τ_max = 1.5 V/A
   │█████████████████████████│
   │═════════════════════════│ ← y = +d/4 : τ = 0.75 τ_max
   │  ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒  │
   │  ▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒▒  │
   └─────────────────────────┘
        ↓ σ_max (compression)
   ←————————————— b —————————————→
```

- Solid horizontal lines (`═══`) represent fibers carrying **high shear stress** near the NA.
- Dotted sections (`▒`) represent fibers with **diminishing shear stress** approaching the top/bottom.
- The width $b$ is uniform along the depth (rectangle-specific feature that makes $\tau$ purely parabolic).
