## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**State and explain the assumptions made in the derivation of shear stress distribution in beams.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The derivation assumes:
1. **Pure bending assumption:** The beam is subjected to bending such that plane sections remain plane (Euler-Bernoulli hypothesis).
2. **Constant shear force:** The shear force $V$ is assumed constant over the small element of length $dx$.
3. **Homogeneous and isotropic material:** The material has uniform properties across the cross-section.
4. **Linear elastic behaviour:** The bending stress $\sigma = My/I$ holds throughout.
5. **Width $b$ is constant across the depth** (true for a rectangle — false for T-sections, I-sections, etc.).

> **Marking Scheme:** 1 mark per key assumption (any 3 out of 5).

---

**Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Why does maximum shear stress occur at the neutral axis of a rectangular beam?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Maximum shear stress occurs at the Neutral Axis because of the following reasons:
1. **First moment of area $Q$ is maximum at the NA:** At the NA, the entire area of the half-section lies beyond the fiber, making $Q = (b)(d/2)(d/4) = bd^2/8$ — its maximum value.
2. **Width $b$ is constant in a rectangle:** Since $b$ does not vary with $y$, the only variable in $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ is $Q$, which is maximum at $y = 0$.
3. **At extreme fibers, $Q = 0$** (no area beyond them), giving $\tau = 0$.

Hence $\tau_{\max}$ occurs at the NA, with $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd} = 1.5\,\tau_{\text{avg}}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for shear stress distribution across the cross-section of a rectangular beam of width $b$ and depth $d$, showing clearly all the steps and the final equation. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A simply supported timber beam of rectangular cross-section 120 mm wide and 240 mm deep carries a uniformly distributed load of 12 kN/m over a span of 4 m. Calculate:
   1. Maximum shear stress in the beam. **\[3 marks\]**
   2. Shear stress at a depth of 60 mm below the neutral axis. **\[4 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

1. **Define geometry** of the rectangular cross-section with width $b$, depth $d$, and Neutral Axis through centroid. *(1 mark)*
2. **State general shear formula** $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ with definitions of each term. *(1 mark)*
3. **Identify fiber at distance $y$ from NA**, compute area beyond it: $A' = b(d/2 - y)$ and centroidal distance $\bar{y} = (2y+d)/4$. *(1 mark)*
4. **Derive $Q$:** $Q = A' \cdot \bar{y} = (b/2)(d^2/4 - y^2)$. *(1 mark)*
5. **Substitute** $Q$ and $I = bd^3/12$ into $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ and simplify to obtain: $\tau = \dfrac{6V}{bd^3}\left(\dfrac{d^2}{4} - y^2\right)$. *(1.5 marks)*
6. **Apply boundary conditions:** at $y = 0 \Rightarrow \tau_{\max} = 3V/(2bd)$; at $y = \pm d/2 \Rightarrow \tau = 0$; conclude parabolic distribution. *(1 mark)*
7. **State the compact form** $\tau = \tau_{\max}(1 - 4y^2/d^2)$. *(0.5 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Width $b = 120\,\text{mm} = 0.12\,\text{m}$
- Depth $d = 240\,\text{mm} = 0.24\,\text{m}$
- UDL $w = 12\,\text{kN/m} = 12{,}000\,\text{N/m}$
- Span $L = 4\,\text{m}$

**Step 1: Maximum Shear Force**

For a simply supported beam with UDL, maximum shear force occurs at the supports:

$$V_{\max} = \frac{w L}{2} = \frac{12 \times 4}{2} = 24\,\text{kN} = 24{,}000\,\text{N}$$

**Step 2: Maximum Shear Stress (at NA, $y=0$)** *(3 marks)*

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{3 V_{\max}}{2 b d} = \frac{3 \times 24{,}000}{2 \times 0.12 \times 0.24}$$

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{72{,}000}{0.0576} = 1{,}250{,}000\,\text{N/m}^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 1.25\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Step 3: Shear Stress at $y = 60\,\text{mm} = 0.06\,\text{m}$** *(4 marks)*

Using the compact form:

$$\tau = \tau_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{4y^{2}}{d^{2}}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{4 \times (0.06)^{2}}{(0.24)^{2}}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{4 \times 0.0036}{0.0576}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \left(1 - \frac{0.0144}{0.0576}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1{,}250{,}000 \times \left(1 - 0.25\right)$$

$$\tau = 1{,}250{,}000 \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 937{,}500\,\text{N/m}^{2} = 0.9375\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Alternative verification** using the long form:

$$\tau = \frac{6 V_{\max}}{b d^{3}}\left(\frac{d^{2}}{4} - y^{2}\right) = \frac{6 \times 24{,}000}{0.12 \times (0.24)^{3}}\left(\frac{(0.24)^{2}}{4} - (0.06)^{2}\right)$$

$$\tau = \frac{144{,}000}{0.001658}\left(0.0144 - 0.0036\right) = 86{,}843{,}426 \times 0.0108 = 937{,}909\,\text{Pa} \approx 0.9375\,\text{MPa} \checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the basic assumptions, derive the formula for shear stress distribution across the rectangular cross-section of a beam. Show that the maximum shear stress is 1.5 times the average shear stress. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A rectangular beam 200 mm × 300 mm is subjected to a maximum shear force of 60 kN. Determine:
   1. The average shear stress in the beam. **\[2 marks\]**
   2. The maximum shear stress in the beam. **\[2 marks\]**
   3. The shear stress at a point 75 mm above the neutral axis. **\[3 marks\]**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a) — Full Derivation (7 marks):**

The complete derivation follows exactly the steps in **Section 3.2 to 3.5** of these notes. Key marks distributed as:

- **Free body diagram (1 mark):** Element of length $dx$, faces at distances $x$ and $x+dx$, showing unequal moments $M$ and $M+dM$.
- **Bending stress on faces (1 mark):** $\sigma_1 = My/I$ and $\sigma_2 = (M+dM)y/I$.
- **Horizontal force balance (1.5 marks):** $\int \sigma \, dA$ over area beyond fiber = horizontal shear force $= \tau \cdot b \cdot dx$.
- **Derive Q for rectangle (2 marks):** $Q = (b/2)(d^2/4 - y^2)$.
- **Substitute and simplify to final equation (1 mark):** $\tau = (6V/bd^3)(d^2/4 - y^2)$.
- **Show 1.5 factor (0.5 mark):** $\tau_{\max}/\tau_{\text{avg}} = 1.5$ by comparing $3V/(2bd)$ with $V/(bd)$.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $b = 200\,\text{mm} = 0.2\,\text{m}$
- $d = 300\,\text{mm} = 0.3\,\text{m}$
- $V = 60\,\text{kN} = 60{,}000\,\text{N}$

**(1) Average Shear Stress (2 marks):**

$$A = b \cdot d = 0.2 \times 0.3 = 0.06\,\text{m}^{2}$$

$$\tau_{\text{avg}} = \frac{V}{A} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.06}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\text{avg}} = 1{,}000{,}000\,\text{N/m}^{2} = 1.0\,\text{MPa}}$$

**(2) Maximum Shear Stress (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{\max} = 1.5 \times \tau_{\text{avg}} = 1.5 \times 1.0\,\text{MPa}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = 1.5\,\text{MPa}}$$

**Verification:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{3V}{2bd} = \dfrac{3 \times 60{,}000}{2 \times 0.2 \times 0.3} = \dfrac{180{,}000}{0.12} = 1{,}500{,}000\,\text{Pa} = 1.5\,\text{MPa}$ ✓

**(3) Shear Stress at $y = -75\,\text{mm} = -0.075\,\text{m}$** (above NA, so negative; magnitude is asked) **(3 marks):**

Using the compact form:

$$\tau = \tau_{\max}\left(1 - \frac{4y^{2}}{d^{2}}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 - \frac{4 \times (0.075)^{2}}{(0.3)^{2}}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 - \frac{4 \times 0.005625}{0.09}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 - \frac{0.0225}{0.09}\right)$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times \left(1 - 0.25\right)$$

$$\tau = 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times 0.75$$

$$\boxed{\tau = 1.125\,\text{MPa}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "1.5 factor" derivation:** Many students write $\tau_{\max} = 3V/(2bd)$ without explicitly showing the *ratio* $\tau_{\max}/\tau_{\text{avg}} = 1.5$. The 0.5-mark is lost silently. **Always conclude with the boxed statement: $\tau_{\max} = 1.5\,\tau_{\text{avg}}$.**
>
> 2. **Taking $Q$ about the wrong axis:** Some students take $Q$ about the *top* or *bottom* edge instead of the **Neutral Axis**. This gives incorrect results. **Always measure $\bar{y}$ from the NA.**
>
> 3. **Using half-area instead of full-area logic:** When a student mistakenly uses $A' = (d/2) \cdot b$ (i.e., the entire half) regardless of $y$, the result becomes the *constant* $\tau_{\max}$, which is wrong. The dependence on $y$ is lost.
>
> 4. **Sign errors in numerical substitution:** When $y$ is measured above the NA, it is *negative* by convention. Square it before substituting into $y^2$ — squaring handles the sign automatically. Use $|y|$ in intermediate steps to be safe.
>
> 5. **Not stating boundary conditions:** The KTU evaluator deducts 1 mark if the student does not verify $\tau = 0$ at $y = \pm d/2$ explicitly in the final answer.
>
> 6. **Unit inconsistency:** Always convert mm → m (or m → mm consistently) before computing. A student who mixes mm and m will lose 2 marks outright.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Read this 60-second checklist before every KTU exam on this topic.**

- [ ] The **general shear stress formula** is $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ — never forget it.
- [ ] For a rectangle, $I = bd^3/12$ and $A = bd$.
- [ ] $Q = (b/2)(d^2/4 - y^2)$ — this is the most-tested intermediate step.
- [ ] Final shear stress equation: $\tau = (6V/bd^3)(d^2/4 - y^2)$.
- [ ] Compact form: $\tau = \tau_{\max}(1 - 4y^2/d^2)$.
- [ ] $\tau_{\max} = 3V/(2bd) = 1.5 V/A = 1.5\,\tau_{\text{avg}}$.
- [ ] $\tau_{\max}$ occurs at the **Neutral Axis** ($y = 0$).
- [ ] $\tau = 0$ at the **extreme top and bottom fibers** ($y = \pm d/2$).
- [ ] Distribution is **parabolic (quadratic in $y$)**, symmetric about NA.
- [ ] Always convert units to SI (N, m, Pa) **before** numerical computation.
- [ ] Always mention **boundary conditions** in your final answer for full marks.
- [ ] The factor **1.5** is a *favourite* KTU examiner's question — memorize it.
- [ ] For *non-rectangular* sections (I, T, circular), the formula $\tau = VQ/(Ib)$ remains the same but $b$ and $Q$ change with $y$ — leading to non-parabolic, more complex distributions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Deflection of Beams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Deflection of Beams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Deflection of Beams:** When a transverse load is applied on a beam, the beam undergoes deformation. The vertical displacement of any point on the neutral axis (or centroidal axis) of the beam from its original unloaded position is called the **deflection** of the beam at that point. The slope of the beam at any section is the angle made by the tangent to the elastic curve with the original axis of the beam.

In rigorous terms, if $y$ is the deflection at a distance $x$ from the origin, then:

- **Deflection:** $y$ (measured in **mm** or **m**)
- **Slope:** $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ (measured in **radians**)
- **Curvature:** $\kappa = \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ (measured in **per metre** or **per mm**)

The locus of the deflected neutral axis of a loaded beam is known as the **Elastic Curve** or **Deflected Shape** of the beam.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study deflection?**
> As per KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework (Course Outcomes CO3, CO4), students must be able to evaluate the deformations in structural elements to ensure that the structure satisfies **serviceability limit states (SLS)**. A bridge girder may be strong enough to carry the load (Ultimate Limit State satisfied) but may deflect so much that it becomes unserviceable. Hence, deflection analysis is as important as strength analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **diving board** at a swimming pool. When you stand at its free end:

- The board bends downward.
- Every point on the board moves down slightly — that downward displacement is the **deflection**.
- The board, originally horizontal, now tilts. The angle it tilts through is the **slope** at that point.

Now imagine a **wooden ruler** clamped to the edge of a desk (cantilever). Press down on the free end:
- The free end goes down (maximum deflection).
- The clamped end does not move at all and stays horizontal (slope = 0).

> [!TIP]
> **Think of it this way:**
> - **Deflection ($y$)** = "How far did the point drop?"
> - **Slope ($\theta$)** = "By what angle did the beam tilt?"
> - **Bending Moment ($M$)** = "What internal moment is trying to bend the beam?"

The relationship $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ tells us: **the more the bending moment, the more the curvature of the beam.** $EI$ (Flexural Rigidity) is the beam's resistance to bending.

## 1.3 Sign Conventions (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Positive Sign Convention |
|----------|------------------------|
| Bending Moment $M$ | Sagging (concave upward, smile shape) |
| Deflection $y$ | Upward (above original axis) |
| Slope $\theta = \dfrac{dy}{dx}$ | Anti-clockwise rotation |
| Curvature $\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$ | Concave upward (positive) |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Theory — The Governing Differential Equation

The deformation of a beam under transverse loading is governed by the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Equation**:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

### 2.1.1 Why does this equation hold?

The bending stress at a fibre distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\dfrac{M}{I} = \dfrac{\sigma}{y} = \dfrac{E}{R}$$

Where $R$ is the radius of curvature. From the geometry of the elastic curve:

$$\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} \quad \text{(for small slopes, } \theta \ll 1 \text{ radian)}$$

Combining both:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

## 2.2 Successive Integration

Each integration reduces the order of the derivative and introduces an integration constant. For a beam, we integrate **twice** because the governing equation is second-order:

| Step | Operation | Result | Constants |
|------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| 1st | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(EI \dfrac{dy}{dx}\right) = V(x)$ | Shear relation | — |
| 2nd | $\dfrac{d}{dx}\left(EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}\right) = w(x)$ | Load relation | — |
| Integrate once | $EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \displaystyle\int M\,dx + C_1$ | **Slope equation** | $C_1$ |
| Integrate twice | $EI\, y = \displaystyle\int\!\!\int M\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$ | **Deflection equation** | $C_1, C_2$ |

The constants $C_1$ and $C_2$ are evaluated from **Boundary Conditions**.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions (KTU Cheat Sheet)

| Boundary Type | Slope Condition | Deflection Condition |
|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Fixed Support** (built-in / cantilever root) | $\theta = 0$ | $y = 0$ |
| **Simple Support** (pin/roller) | Slope is free (non-zero) | $y = 0$ |
| **Free End** (cantilever tip) | Slope is free | Deflection is free |
| **Symmetry Point** | $\theta = 0$ | $y = y_{\max}$ |
| **Antisymmetry Point** | Slope is non-zero | $y = 0$ |

## 2.4 Standard Results for Prismatic Beams (MUST MEMORISE)

| Case | Maximum Deflection $y_{\max}$ | Maximum Slope $\theta_{\max}$ | Location of $y_{\max}$ |
|------|-------------------------------|-------------------------------|------------------------|
| Cantilever, point load $W$ at free end | $\dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$ | $\dfrac{WL^2}{2EI}$ | At free end |
| Cantilever, UDL $w$ over span | $\dfrac{wL^4}{8EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^3}{6EI}$ | At free end |
| Simply supported, point load $W$ at centre | $\dfrac{WL^3}{48EI}$ | $\dfrac{WL^2}{16EI}$ | At centre |
| Simply supported, UDL $w$ over entire span | $\dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ | $\dfrac{wL^3}{24EI}$ | At centre |
| Simply supported, central point load with moment of inertia variation | — | — | At centre |

## 2.5 Macaulay's Method (Singularity Functions) — KTU Favourite

When a beam has multiple loads (UDL + point load + moment), integrating piecewise leads to discontinuity. **Macaulay's method** uses Macaulay brackets $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ which automatically vanish when $x < a$:

$$\langle x - a \rangle^n = \begin{cases} (x-a)^n & x \geq a \\ 0 & x < a \end{cases}$$

The generalised bending moment equation uses:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \langle \cdots \rangle$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** In structural design offices, deflection checks are critical for:
> - **Building floors** — IS 800 / IS 456 limits deflection to $L/325$ to $L/360$.
> - **Bridge girders** — IRC codes limit deflection to $L/800$.
> - **Machine tool beds** — excessive deflection destroys accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Differential Equation of the Elastic Curve

### Assumptions (Euler-Bernoulli Theory)

1. The beam material is **homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic** (obeys Hooke's law).
2. The beam is initially **straight** and has a **uniform cross-section**.
3. Plane sections before bending remain **plane and normal to the neutral axis** after bending.
4. **Shear deformation is neglected** (valid for slender beams where $L/h > 20$).
5. **Small deflections and slopes** — angles are approximated as $\tan\theta \approx \theta$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Consider a small element of length $dx$ on the neutral axis of the deflected beam. Let $R$ be the radius of curvature at that section.

**Step 1:** A fibre at a distance $y$ from the neutral axis has an original length $dx$. After bending, its length becomes $(R - y)\,d\theta$, where $d\theta = \dfrac{dx}{R}$ is the angle subtended.

**Step 2:** The change in length of the fibre is:

$$\Delta L = (R - y)\,d\theta - dx = (R - y)\dfrac{dx}{R} - dx$$

**Step 3:** Simplify:

$$\Delta L = \dfrac{(R-y)\,dx - R\,dx}{R} = -\dfrac{y\,dx}{R}$$

**Step 4:** The longitudinal strain in the fibre is:

$$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\Delta L}{dx} = -\dfrac{y}{R}$$

**Step 5:** By Hooke's Law, the bending stress is:

$$\sigma = E\,\varepsilon = -\dfrac{E\,y}{R}$$

**Step 6:** The internal bending moment $M$ is obtained by integrating the moment of stress about the neutral axis over the cross-sectional area:

$$M = \int_A \sigma \cdot y \, dA = \int_A \left(-\dfrac{E\,y}{R}\right) y\, dA = -\dfrac{E}{R}\int_A y^2\, dA$$

**Step 7:** Recall that the second moment of area is defined as:

$$I = \int_A y^2\, dA$$

Therefore:

$$M = -\dfrac{EI}{R} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{1}{R} = -\dfrac{M}{EI}$$

**Step 8:** The exact mathematical expression for curvature is:

$$\dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}}{\left[1 + \left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2\right]^{3/2}}$$

**Step 9:** For small slopes, $\dfrac{dy}{dx} \ll 1$, so $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \to 0$. The denominator tends to $1$.

$$\dfrac{1}{R} \approx \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2}$$

**Step 10:** Substituting back:

$$\dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -\dfrac{M}{EI}$$

Using the sign convention that sagging moment is positive and downward deflection (concave upward shape gives positive $d^2y/dx^2$):

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x) \quad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Worked-Out Analytical Problem (KTU Board Pattern)

**Problem:** A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load $w$ per unit length over its entire span. Find the slope and deflection at the mid-span using the **Double Integration Method**.

### Step 1: Reactions

By symmetry, both reactions are equal:

$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2}$$

### Step 2: Bending Moment at Section $x$

Taking moments of forces to the left of section $x$:

$$M(x) = R_A\, x - w\, x \cdot \dfrac{x}{2} = \dfrac{wL\,x}{2} - \dfrac{w\, x^2}{2}$$

### Step 3: Substitute into Governing Equation

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = \dfrac{wL\,x}{2} - \dfrac{w\, x^2}{2}$$

### Step 4: First Integration (Slope)

$$EI \dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{wL\,x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w\, x^3}{6} + C_1$$

### Step 5: Second Integration (Deflection)

$$EI\, y = \dfrac{wL\,x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w\, x^4}{24} + C_1\, x + C_2$$

### Step 6: Apply Boundary Conditions

**At $x = 0$** (support $A$): $y = 0$

$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = 0$$

**At $x = L$** (support $B$): $y = 0$

$$0 = \dfrac{wL^4}{12} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24} + C_1 L$$

$$C_1 L = \dfrac{wL^4}{24} - \dfrac{wL^4}{12} = -\dfrac{wL^4}{24}$$

$$C_1 = -\dfrac{wL^4}{24}$$

### Step 7: Slope Equation

$$EI\,\theta = EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = \dfrac{wL\,x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w\, x^3}{6} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24}$$

$$\boxed{\theta = \dfrac{1}{EI}\left[\dfrac{wL\,x^2}{4} - \dfrac{w\, x^3}{6} - \dfrac{wL^4}{24}\right]}$$

### Step 8: Deflection Equation

$$EI\,y = \dfrac{wL\,x^3}{12} - \dfrac{w\, x^4}{24} - \dfrac{wL^4\,x}{24}$$

$$\boxed{y = \dfrac{w\,x}{24\,EI}\left[L^3 - 2L\,x^2 + x^3\right]}$$

### Step 9: Maximum Values (At Centre, $x = L/2$)

$$\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24EI} \quad \text{(occurs at supports)}$$

$$y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384EI} \quad \text{(occurs at centre)}$$

$$\blacksquare$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Elastic Curve of Standard Cases (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Beam Type Selection] --> nodeB{Loading Condition}
    nodeB -->|Point Load| nodeC[Concentrated Force Analysis]
    nodeB -->|UDL| nodeD[Distributed Load Analysis]
    nodeB -->|Moment| nodeE[Applied Couple Analysis]

    nodeC --> nodeF[Find Reactions RA and RB]
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeE --> nodeF

    nodeF --> nodeG[Write Mx Equation using Sections]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Apply Governing Equation EI d2y by dx2 equals Mx]

    nodeH --> nodeI[First Integration: Slope Equation with C1]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Second Integration: Deflection Equation with C2]

    nodeJ --> nodeK{Choose Boundary Conditions}
    nodeK -->|Fixed Support| nodeL[theta equals 0 and y equals 0]
    nodeK -->|Simple Support| nodeM[y equals 0 only]
    nodeK -->|Symmetry| nodeN[theta equals 0 at midspan]
    nodeK -->|Free End| nodeO[No condition at free end]

    nodeL --> nodeP[Solve for C1 and C2]
    nodeM --> nodeP
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP

    nodeP --> nodeQ[Final Equations of theta and y]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Compute ymax and thetamax]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Check Serviceability: ymax less than L by 325]
```

## 4.2 Methods of Finding Deflection — Comparative Flow Chart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Deflection of Beams] --> nodeB[Analytical Methods]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Energy Methods]

    nodeB --> nodeD[Double Integration Method]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Macaulay Method or Singularity Functions]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Moment Area Method]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Conjugate Beam Method]

    nodeC --> nodeH[Castiglianos Theorem]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Unit Load Method or Virtual Work]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Strain Energy Method]

    nodeD --> nodeK[Best for: Single load cases]
    nodeE --> nodeL[Best for: Discontinuous loading]
    nodeF --> nodeM[Best for: Finding only slope and deflection at specific points]
    nodeG --> nodeN[Best for: Variable EI beams]

    nodeH --> nodeO[Best for: Statically indeterminate beams]
    nodeI --> nodeP[Best for: Trusses and complex frames]
```

## 4.3 Sign Convention Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Coordinate System Setup] --> nodeB[Origin at Left Support]
    nodeB --> nodeC[X axis positive to Right]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Y axis positive Upwards]

    nodeD --> nodeE[Deflection y positive UP]
    nodeD --> nodeF[Slope theta positive when dy by dx positive]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Bending Moment positive when SMILING Shape]

    nodeG --> nodeH[Sagging moment positive]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Hogging moment negative]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Concave UP curve]
    nodeH --> nodeK[d2y by dx2 positive]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**State the differential equation of the elastic curve of a beam. Mention the assumptions used.**

**Model Answer:**

The differential equation of the elastic curve of a beam is:

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

Where $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material, $I$ = Second moment of area of the cross-section, $y$ = deflection at distance $x$, and $M(x)$ = bending moment at section $x$.

**Assumptions:**
1. The beam material is homogeneous, isotropic, and obeys Hooke's law.
2. Plane sections perpendicular to the axis before bending remain plane and normal to the deflected axis after bending.
3. The beam is slender — shear deformation is negligible.
4. Deflections and slopes are small so that $\left(\dfrac{dy}{dx}\right)^2 \ll 1$.
5. The beam is initially straight and has a uniform cross-section.

---

### Question A2
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A cantilever beam of length $L$ carries a point load $W$ at the free end. Write the expression for slope and deflection at the free end.**

**Model Answer:**

For a cantilever beam of length $L$ with point load $W$ at the free end:

- Slope at free end: $\theta = \dfrac{WL^2}{2EI}$
- Deflection at free end: $y = \dfrac{WL^3}{3EI}$

The maximum slope and maximum deflection both occur at the free end of the cantilever.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]`

**A simply supported beam of span $6\,\text{m}$ carries a uniformly distributed load of $30\,\text{kN/m}$ over its entire span. The flexural rigidity of the beam is $EI = 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$. Determine:**

**(a)** The slope and deflection at the mid-span of the beam.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The maximum slope and deflection in the beam.  **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Span $L = 6\,\text{m}$
- UDL $w = 30\,\text{kN/m} = 30{,}000\,\text{N/m}$
- $EI = 1.5 \times 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$

**Reactions:**

$$R_A = R_B = \dfrac{wL}{2} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 6}{2} = 90{,}000\,\text{N} = 90\,\text{kN}$$

**Bending Moment Equation:**

$$M(x) = 90{,}000\,x - 30{,}000\,\dfrac{x^2}{2} = 90{,}000\,x - 15{,}000\,x^2$$

**Governing Differential Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = 90{,}000\,x - 15{,}000\,x^2$$

**First Integration (Slope):**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = 45{,}000\,x^2 - 5{,}000\,x^3 + C_1$$

**Second Integration (Deflection):**

$$EI\, y = 15{,}000\,x^3 - 1{,}250\,x^4 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary Conditions:**

At $x = 0$, $y = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $C_2 = 0$.

At $x = 6\,\text{m}$, $y = 0$:

$$0 = 15{,}000\,(216) - 1{,}250\,(1296) + 6\,C_1$$
$$0 = 3{,}240{,}000 - 1{,}620{,}000 + 6\,C_1$$
$$6\,C_1 = -1{,}620{,}000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = -270{,}000$$

**Slope Equation:**

$$EI\,\theta = 45{,}000\,x^2 - 5{,}000\,x^3 - 270{,}000$$

**Deflection Equation:**

$$EI\,y = 15{,}000\,x^3 - 1{,}250\,x^4 - 270{,}000\,x$$

#### Part (a): Slope and Deflection at Mid-Span ($x = 3\,\text{m}$)

**Slope at mid-span:**

$$EI\,\theta_{mid} = 45{,}000(9) - 5{,}000(27) - 270{,}000$$
$$EI\,\theta_{mid} = 405{,}000 - 135{,}000 - 270{,}000 = 0$$

By symmetry, $\theta_{mid} = 0$.

**Deflection at mid-span:**

$$EI\,y_{mid} = 15{,}000(27) - 1{,}250(81) - 270{,}000(3)$$
$$EI\,y_{mid} = 405{,}000 - 101{,}250 - 810{,}000 = -506{,}250$$

$$y_{mid} = \dfrac{-506{,}250}{1.5 \times 10^6} = -0.3375\,\text{m} = -337.5\,\text{mm}$$

The negative sign indicates **downward** deflection (consistent with downward UDL).

#### Part (b): Maximum Slope and Deflection

By symmetry, maximum deflection occurs at mid-span, and maximum slope occurs at the supports.

**Maximum Deflection:**

$$y_{\max} = \dfrac{5wL^4}{384\,EI} = \dfrac{5 \times 30{,}000 \times 6^4}{384 \times 1.5 \times 10^6}$$

$$y_{\max} = \dfrac{5 \times 30{,}000 \times 1296}{576 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{194{,}400{,}000}{576 \times 10^6} = 0.3375\,\text{m}$$

$$y_{\max} = 337.5\,\text{mm} \quad \checkmark$$

**Maximum Slope (at supports):**

$$\theta_{\max} = \dfrac{wL^3}{24\,EI} = \dfrac{30{,}000 \times 216}{24 \times 1.5 \times 10^6} = \dfrac{6{,}480{,}000}{36 \times 10^6} = 0.18\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{\max} = 0.18\,\text{rad},\quad y_{\max} = 337.5\,\text{mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]`

**A cantilever beam of length $4\,\text{m}$ carries a point load of $20\,\text{kN}$ at the free end. Take $E = 200\,\text{GPa}$ and $I = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^4$. Determine:**

**(a)** The slope at the free end of the cantilever.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The deflection at the free end of the cantilever. Compare with the permissible limit of $L/250$.  **(7 marks)**

#### Solution

**Given Data:**
- Span $L = 4\,\text{m}$
- Point load $W = 20\,\text{kN} = 20{,}000\,\text{N}$
- $E = 200\,\text{GPa} = 200 \times 10^9\,\text{N/m}^2$
- $I = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^4$
- $EI = 200 \times 10^9 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 10^6\,\text{N·m}^2$

**Bending Moment at distance $x$ from fixed end:**

Taking the section at distance $x$ from the fixed end, the moment due to the load $W$ at the free end (at distance $L - x$ from the section):

$$M(x) = -W(L - x) = -20{,}000\,(4 - x)$$

(The negative sign indicates hogging moment for cantilever.)

**Governing Equation:**

$$EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = -20{,}000\,(4 - x)$$

**First Integration:**

$$EI\,\dfrac{dy}{dx} = -20{,}000\left(4x - \dfrac{x^2}{2}\right) + C_1$$

$$EI\,\theta = -80{,}000\,x + 10{,}000\,x^2 + C_1$$

**Second Integration:**

$$EI\,y = -40{,}000\,x^2 + \dfrac{10{,}000}{3}\,x^3 + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Boundary Conditions (Fixed End at $x = 0$):**

At $x = 0$: $\theta = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $C_1 = 0$.

At $x = 0$: $y = 0$ $\Rightarrow$ $C_2 = 0$.

#### Part (a): Slope at Free End ($x = 4\,\text{m}$)

$$EI\,\theta_{free} = -80{,}000\,(4) + 10{,}000\,(16) + 0$$
$$EI\,\theta_{free} = -320{,}000 + 160{,}000 = -160{,}000$$

$$\theta_{free} = \dfrac{-160{,}000}{10^6} = -0.16\,\text{rad}$$

$$\boxed{\vert\theta_{free}\vert = 0.16\,\text{rad} = 9.16^\circ}$$

#### Part (b): Deflection at Free End ($x = 4\,\text{m}$)

$$EI\,y_{free} = -40{,}000\,(16) + \dfrac{10{,}000}{3}\,(64) + 0$$
$$EI\,y_{free} = -640{,}000 + \dfrac{640{,}000}{3} = -640{,}000\left(1 - \dfrac{1}{3}\right)$$
$$EI\,y_{free} = -\dfrac{1{,}280{,}000}{3} = -426{,}666.67$$

$$y_{free} = \dfrac{-426{,}666.67}{10^6} = -0.4267\,\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\vert y_{free}\vert = 426.7\,\text{mm}}$$

**Permissible Limit Check:**

$$\dfrac{L}{250} = \dfrac{4000}{250} = 16\,\text{mm}$$

Since $426.7\,\text{mm} \gg 16\,\text{mm}$, the deflection is **not within permissible limits**. The beam must be redesigned with a higher moment of inertia.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap:** In cantilever beams, students often forget that the bending moment is hogging (negative). Forgetting the negative sign flips the slope direction and gives incorrect curvature direction.
> 2. **Boundary Condition at Fixed End:** Always remember that BOTH $y = 0$ AND $\theta = 0$ apply at the fixed end — not just deflection. Students often miss the slope condition and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Units Consistency:** $EI$ must be in consistent units. Mixing $E$ in GPa with $I$ in $\text{mm}^4$ and load in kN leads to a unit mismatch disaster. Convert everything to $\text{N}$ and $\text{m}$.
> 4. **Final Answer Sign:** KTU expects magnitude for deflection (always positive physical quantity) but the sign indicates direction. State clearly: "$y = 426.7\,\text{mm}$ downward."
> 5. **Standard Formula Substitution:** When the problem matches a standard case, you may quote the standard formula directly to save time — but you MUST verify the conditions match (e.g., "This is a standard case of cantilever with point load at free end"). Don't just plug into the formula without justification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Scheme:**

- **Governing Equation (MUST MEMORISE):** $EI \dfrac{d^2 y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — the cornerstone of all beam deflection problems.

- **Order of Integration:** Second-order differential equation $\Rightarrow$ integrate **twice** $\Rightarrow$ **two constants** $C_1, C_2$ $\Rightarrow$ **two boundary conditions** required.

- **Boundary Conditions Memory Trick:** *Fixed = both zero (deflection AND slope)*; *Pinned = only deflection zero*; *Free = no condition*; *Symmetric = slope zero at midspan*.

- **Sign Convention (No Mix-Up!):** Sagging moment positive, hogging negative. Upward deflection positive. Anti-clockwise slope positive.

- **Standard Results to Memorise:**
  - Cantilever + point load: $y = WL^3/(3EI)$ at tip.
  - Cantilever + UDL: $y = wL^4/(8EI)$ at tip.
  - Simply supported + central point load: $y = WL^3/(48EI)$ at centre.
  - Simply supported + UDL: $y = 5wL^4/(384EI)$ at centre.

- **Macaulay's Method:** Use when the beam has **multiple discontinuous loads**. The Macaulay bracket $\langle x - a \rangle^n$ automatically vanishes for $x < a$.

- **Moment-Area Theorem:** Use when you need deflection at **specific points only**, not the full equation. The theorem: *"The change in slope between two points equals the area of the $M/EI$ diagram between those points."*

- **Conjugate Beam Method:** Replace the real beam with a **fictitious beam** loaded with the $M/EI$ diagram. Then deflection in real beam = bending moment in conjugate beam.

- **Double Check:** Always verify the units of $EI$ are consistent with the load and length units before substituting numbers.

- **Permissible Deflection (IS 800:2007 / IS 456):** Generally $L/325$ to $L/360$ for building beams. Always state this comparison in the final answer.

- **Small Slope Assumption:** $\tan\theta \approx \theta$ holds when deflection $\ll$ span (typically $y_{\max}/L < 1/200$).

- **Shear Deformation Neglected:** Valid when $L/h > 20$ (slender beams). For deep beams, Timoshenko beam theory is needed.

- **Symmetry Trick:** For symmetric beams with symmetric loading, the slope at midspan is **zero** and the deflection is **maximum** at midspan — use this to simplify boundary conditions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Problems to Find the Deflections (Using Double Integration Method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Simple Problems to Find the Deflections (Using Double Integration Method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Double Integration Method (DIM):** A classical method in the Strength of Materials / Mechanics of Solids used to determine the **slope** and **deflection** of a beam subjected to transverse loading. The method is rooted in the **Euler-Bernoulli Beam Theory** and is based on the fundamental differential equation of the elastic curve:
> 
> $$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$
> 
> where $E$ is the Young's Modulus of the material, $I$ is the Area Moment of Inertia of the beam's cross-section, and $M(x)$ is the bending moment expressed as a function of the distance $x$ measured along the beam axis. The method involves **two successive integrations** of the bending moment equation, which is why it is termed the "Double Integration Method."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a thin, flexible plastic ruler (a beam) horizontally and pressing down in the middle with your finger. The ruler bends — it curves downward. 

- The **shape of the curve** traced by the deflected beam is called the **elastic curve** or **deflected shape**.
- The **slope** at any point is the angle the curve makes with the horizontal (how steeply the ruler is tilted at that location).
- The **deflection** is the vertical distance between the original straight position of the beam and the bent position (how far your finger pushed the ruler down).

Now, the bending moment $M(x)$ along the ruler is what *causes* this curve. If you can write down an equation for $M(x)$ as you walk along the beam from one end to the other, then you can mathematically "unbend" the beam by integrating that moment equation **twice** — once to get the slope (the angle) and once more to get the deflection (the vertical drop). Each integration introduces a constant of integration, and these constants are evaluated using the known **boundary conditions** of the beam (such as zero deflection at a support, or zero slope at a fixed end).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Double" Integration?** Because the relationship between bending moment and deflection involves a *second derivative* ($\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$). To recover $y$ (deflection) from $M(x)$, we must integrate twice. Each integration step adds one arbitrary constant, giving us two constants ($C_1$ and $C_2$) to be determined.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **$E$ (Young's Modulus):** Material stiffness property. For steel, $E = 200$ GPa. For aluminium, $E = 70$ GPa. Units: **N/m²** or **Pa**.
- **$I$ (Area Moment of Inertia):** Geometric property of the cross-section about the Neutral Axis. Units: **m⁴** or **mm⁴**.
- **$EI$ (Flexural Rigidity):** The product representing the beam's resistance to bending. Units: **N·m²** or **N·mm²**.
- **$M(x)$ (Bending Moment):** Internal moment at a section. Units: **N·m** or **N·mm**.
- **$y$ (Deflection):** Vertical displacement. Units: **m** or **mm**.
- **$\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$ (Slope):** Angle of the elastic curve with the horizontal. Dimensionless (radians for small angles).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to solve **simple statically determinate beams** (Simply Supported and Cantilever) subjected to **point loads** and **uniformly distributed loads (UDL)** using the Double Integration Method. The expected level is problem-solving with clearly defined loading and geometry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Procedure

The Double Integration Method follows a strict algorithmic sequence. Mastering this sequence is the key to solving any DIM problem in the KTU exam.

1. **Step 1 — Sketch and Identify Supports:** Draw the beam, identify supports (pin, roller, fixed), and note the type and magnitude of all applied loads.
2. **Step 2 — Compute Support Reactions:** Use equilibrium equations ($\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) to find reaction forces.
3. **Step 3 — Section the Beam at Distance $x$:** Make an imaginary cut at a generic distance $x$ from a chosen origin (usually the left end).
4. **Step 4 — Write the Bending Moment Equation $M(x)$:** Sum moments of all forces to the left (or right) of the cut. For multiple loading segments, write separate $M(x)$ for each region.
5. **Step 5 — First Integration (Slope Equation):** Apply the governing equation $EI\frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ and integrate once:
   $$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \int M(x)\,dx + C_1$$
   This gives the **slope equation**, $\theta = \frac{dy}{dx}$.
6. **Step 6 — Second Integration (Deflection Equation):** Integrate again:
   $$EIy = \iint M(x)\,dx\,dx + C_1 x + C_2$$
   This gives the **deflection equation**, $y$.
7. **Step 7 — Apply Boundary Conditions:** Substitute known slope and deflection values at the supports to solve for $C_1$ and $C_2$.
8. **Step 8 — Compute Maximum Deflection:** Substitute the location of maximum deflection (where slope = 0) into the deflection equation.

### 2.2 The Governing Equation — The "Why" Behind It

The Euler-Bernoulli beam equation arises from the **pure bending theory**:

$$\frac{M}{I} = \frac{\sigma}{y} = \frac{E}{R}$$

where $R$ is the radius of curvature. For small deflections, $\frac{1}{R} \approx \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$, leading to:

$$M = EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2}$$

This is the cornerstone of the Double Integration Method. The bending moment *drives* the curvature of the beam.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Sign Convention:** Take downward deflection as positive ($+y$), downward loads as positive ($+P$ or $+w$), and sagging moments as positive ($+M$). Alternatively, follow the KTU convention: downward $y$ is positive.

#### Table 1: Standard Boundary Conditions

| Beam Type | Location | Condition | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported | Left Support ($x=0$) | $y = 0$ | Deflection at pin/roller is zero |
| Simply Supported | Right Support ($x=L$) | $y = 0$ | Deflection at pin/roller is zero |
| Cantilever (Fixed-Left) | Fixed End ($x=0$) | $y = 0$, $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | Zero deflection AND zero slope at wall |
| Cantilever (Fixed-Right) | Fixed End ($x=L$) | $y = 0$, $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ | Zero deflection AND zero slope at wall |
| Free End (Cantilever) | Free End ($x=L$) | $M = 0$, $V = 0$ | No internal moment or shear at free tip |

#### Table 2: Bending Moment Equations for Common Loadings (from Left End)

| Loading Type | Bending Moment $M(x)$ |
|---|---|
| Point Load $P$ at midspan (Simply Supported) | $M(x) = \frac{Px}{2}$ for $0 \le x \le \frac{L}{2}$ |
| UDL $w$ over entire span (Simply Supported) | $M(x) = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$ |
| Point Load $P$ at free end (Cantilever, fixed at $x=0$) | $M(x) = -P(L - x)$ |
| UDL $w$ over entire span (Cantilever, fixed at $x=0$) | $M(x) = -\frac{w(L-x)^2}{2}$ |
| Point Load $P$ at distance $a$ from left (Simply Supported) | $M(x) = \frac{Pb\,x}{L}$ for $0 \le x \le a$, where $b = L - a$ |

#### Table 3: Standard Results (KTU-Favorite Quick-Reference)

| Beam & Loading | Max Slope $\theta_{max}$ | Max Deflection $y_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Simply Supported + Central Point Load $P$ | $\theta = \frac{PL^2}{16EI}$ at supports | $y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$ at midspan |
| Simply Supported + UDL $w$ over span | $\theta = \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$ at supports | $y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ at midspan |
| Cantilever + Point Load $P$ at free end | $\theta = \frac{PL^2}{2EI}$ at free end | $y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}$ at free end |
| Cantilever + UDL $w$ over span | $\theta = \frac{wL^3}{6EI}$ at free end | $y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$ at free end |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL LₐTₑX NOTE for tables:** Notice the use of `\vert` or descriptive terms ("absolute value of x") instead of literal `|` pipes within math expressions, to maintain markdown table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bridge Design:** Engineers must limit deflections to about $L/250$ to $L/800$ depending on the code (IS 800, AISC). The Double Integration Method provides closed-form deflection values for standard design tables.
- **Machine Tool Frames:** Deflections of machine beds under cutting forces affect precision.
- **Floor Beam Design in Buildings:** Long-span floor beams must satisfy serviceability deflection limits.
- **Aircraft Wing Spars:** The wing is essentially a cantilever beam; deflection calculations are critical for aerodynamic performance.
- **Railway Sleepers and Crane Girders:** Service load deflections govern the design choices.

The Double Integration Method remains the **most fundamental** beam-deflection technique and forms the theoretical basis for more advanced methods (Macaulay's Method, Moment-Area Method, Conjugate Beam Method, and the Finite Element Method).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Content Mandate:** Every algebraic transition, integration step, and substitution is explicitly written below. No step is skipped.

---

### 3.1 PROBLEM 1: Simply Supported Beam with a Central Point Load $P$

**Given:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a concentrated point load $P$ at its midspan. Find the slope and deflection at the free ends and at the midspan.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry, the reactions at both supports are equal:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation for Region 1 ($0 \le x \le L/2$):**
Taking a section at distance $x$ from the left support $A$ and considering the left portion:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} \cdot x = \frac{Px}{2}$$

**Step 3 — First Integration (Slope):**
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x) = \frac{Px}{2}$$

Integrating once with respect to $x$:
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \int \frac{Px}{2}\,dx = \frac{Px^2}{4} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration (Deflection):**
Integrating again with respect to $x$:
$$EI \cdot y = \int \left(\frac{Px^2}{4} + C_1\right) dx = \frac{Px^3}{12} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- **BC 1:** At $x = 0$ (left support $A$), deflection $y = 0$:
$$0 = \frac{P(0)^3}{12} + C_1(0) + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$
- **BC 2:** At $x = L/2$ (midspan, by symmetry), slope $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$:
$$0 = \frac{P}{EI}\left(\frac{(L/2)^2}{4}\right) + \frac{C_1}{EI}$$
$$0 = \frac{P L^2}{16} + C_1 \implies C_1 = -\frac{PL^2}{16}$$

**Step 6 — Final Equations:**
$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{Px^2}{4} - \frac{PL^2}{16}$$
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{Px^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2 x}{16}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2$):**
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{P(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{PL^2(L/2)}{16}$$
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{PL^3}{32}$$

To combine, find a common denominator (96):
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{96} - \frac{3PL^3}{96} = -\frac{2PL^3}{96} = -\frac{PL^3}{48}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection (per our sign convention with $+y$ taken as upward, or per KTU's sagging-positive convention, we interpret the magnitude):

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI} \quad \text{(downward, at midspan)}}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum Slope at Supports ($x = 0$ or $x = L$):**
At $x = 0$:
$$EI \cdot \theta_A = \frac{P(0)^2}{4} - \frac{PL^2}{16} = -\frac{PL^2}{16}$$
$$\boxed{\theta_A = \frac{PL^2}{16EI} \quad \text{(at supports)}}$$

By symmetry, $\theta_B = +\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$ at the right support.

---

### 3.2 PROBLEM 2: Simply Supported Beam with a UDL of Intensity $w$ over the Entire Span

**Given:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a uniformly distributed load (UDL) of intensity $w$ N/m over its entire span.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation:**
Consider a section at distance $x$ from $A$. The UDL acting on the left portion over length $x$ is $w \cdot x$ acting at its centroid $x/2$ from the section.
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - w \cdot x \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wx}{2}(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — First Integration:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

Integrating once:
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wL}{4}x^2 - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration:**
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{wL}{12}x^3 - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- **BC 1:** At $x = 0$, $y = 0$:
$$0 = 0 - 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$
- **BC 2:** At $x = L/2$ (midspan, by symmetry), $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$:
$$0 = \frac{wL}{4}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{w}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1$$
$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{16} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1$$

Find common denominator (48):
$$0 = \frac{3wL^3}{48} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1 = \frac{2wL^3}{48} + C_1 = \frac{wL^3}{24} + C_1$$
$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Step 6 — Final Equations:**
$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}$$
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3 x}{24}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2$):**
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{wL(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{w(L/2)^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3(L/2)}{24}$$
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{96} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{48}$$

Common denominator is 384:
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{4wL^4}{384} - \frac{wL^4}{384} - \frac{8wL^4}{384} = \frac{(4 - 1 - 8)wL^4}{384} = -\frac{5wL^4}{384}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} \quad \text{(downward, at midspan)}}$$

**Step 8 — Maximum Slope at Supports ($x = 0$):**
$$EI \cdot \theta_A = 0 - 0 - \frac{wL^3}{24} = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$
$$\boxed{\theta_A = \frac{wL^3}{24EI} \quad \text{(at supports)}}$$

---

### 3.3 PROBLEM 3: Cantilever Beam with a Point Load $P$ at the Free End

**Given:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at $x = 0$, carries a point load $P$ at the free end ($x = L$). Measure $x$ from the **fixed end**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation:**
Taking a section at distance $x$ from the fixed end and considering the **right** portion (for simplicity), the load $P$ acts at distance $(L - x)$ from the section:
$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

The negative sign indicates a **hogging** (negative) moment for a downward load on a left-fixed cantilever. (Equivalently, taking the left portion gives $M(x) = -P(L-x)$ from the wall reaction $P$ acting upward with arm $(L-x)$ creating a counter-clockwise, hence hogging, moment.)

**Step 2 — First Integration:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -P(L - x) = -PL + Px$$

Integrating once:
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -PLx + \frac{Px^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 3 — Second Integration:**
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{PLx^2}{2} + \frac{Px^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions at the Fixed End ($x = 0$):**
- **BC 1:** $y = 0$ at $x = 0$:
$$0 = 0 + 0 + 0 + C_2 \implies C_2 = 0$$
- **BC 2:** $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$:
$$0 = 0 + 0 + C_1 \implies C_1 = 0$$

**Step 5 — Final Equations:**
$$EI\frac{dy}{dx} = -PLx + \frac{Px^2}{2}$$
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{PLx^2}{2} + \frac{Px^3}{6}$$

**Step 6 — Slope and Deflection at the Free End ($x = L$):**
$$EI \cdot \theta_{free} = -PL(L) + \frac{P(L)^2}{2} = -PL^2 + \frac{PL^2}{2} = -\frac{PL^2}{2}$$
$$\boxed{\theta_{free} = \frac{PL^2}{2EI} \quad \text{(magnitude, at free end)}}$$

$$EI \cdot y_{free} = -\frac{PL(L)^2}{2} + \frac{P(L)^3}{6} = -\frac{PL^3}{2} + \frac{PL^3}{6} = -\frac{3PL^3}{6} + \frac{PL^3}{6} = -\frac{2PL^3}{6} = -\frac{PL^3}{3}$$
$$\boxed{y_{free} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI} \quad \text{(downward, at free end)}}$$

---

### 3.4 PROBLEM 4: Cantilever Beam with a UDL of Intensity $w$ over the Entire Span

**Given:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, fixed at $x = 0$, carries a UDL of intensity $w$ over the entire span.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Bending Moment Equation:**
The UDL on the right portion of the cut (from $x$ to $L$) has total load $w(L-x)$ acting at its centroid $\frac{L-x}{2}$ from the section:
$$M(x) = -\frac{w(L-x)^2}{2}$$

**Step 2 — First Integration:**
Expanding $(L-x)^2 = L^2 - 2Lx + x^2$:
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -\frac{w}{2}(L^2 - 2Lx + x^2) = -\frac{wL^2}{2} + wLx - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

Integrating once:
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -\frac{wL^2 x}{2} + \frac{wLx^2}{2} - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 3 — Second Integration:**
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{wL^2 x^2}{4} + \frac{wLx^3}{6} - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Apply Boundary Conditions at $x = 0$:**
- $y = 0$ at $x = 0$: $C_2 = 0$
- $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$: $C_1 = 0$

**Step 5 — Deflection at Free End ($x = L$):**
$$EI \cdot y_{free} = -\frac{wL^2(L)^2}{4} + \frac{wL(L)^3}{6} - \frac{w(L)^4}{24}$$
$$EI \cdot y_{free} = -\frac{wL^4}{4} + \frac{wL^4}{6} - \frac{wL^4}{24}$$

Common denominator is 24:
$$EI \cdot y_{free} = -\frac{6wL^4}{24} + \frac{4wL^4}{24} - \frac{wL^4}{24} = \frac{(-6 + 4 - 1)wL^4}{24} = -\frac{3wL^4}{24} = -\frac{wL^4}{8}$$

$$\boxed{y_{free} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI} \quad \text{(downward, at free end)}}$$

**Step 6 — Slope at Free End ($x = L$):**
$$EI \cdot \theta_{free} = -\frac{wL^2(L)}{2} + \frac{wL(L)^2}{2} - \frac{w(L)^3}{6}$$
$$= -\frac{wL^3}{2} + \frac{wL^3}{2} - \frac{wL^3}{6} = -\frac{wL^3}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{free} = \frac{wL^3}{6EI} \quad \text{(at free end)}}$$

---

### 3.5 PROBLEM 5: Simply Supported Beam with an Eccentric Point Load

**Given:** A simply supported beam of length $L$ carries a point load $P$ at a distance $a$ from the left support ($b = L - a$ from the right).

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
Using moment equilibrium about $A$:
$$R_B \cdot L = P \cdot a \implies R_B = \frac{Pa}{L}$$
$$R_A = P - R_B = P - \frac{Pa}{L} = \frac{P(L-a)}{L} = \frac{Pb}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equations (Two Regions):**

**Region 1:** $0 \le x \le a$
$$M_1(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{Pb}{L} x$$

**Region 2:** $a \le x \le L$
$$M_2(x) = R_A \cdot x - P(x - a) = \frac{Pb}{L}x - P(x - a)$$

**Step 3 — Integrate Region 1:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y_1}{dx^2} = \frac{Pbx}{L}$$
$$EI \frac{dy_1}{dx} = \frac{Pbx^2}{2L} + C_1$$
$$EI \cdot y_1 = \frac{Pbx^3}{6L} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 4 — Integrate Region 2:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y_2}{dx^2} = \frac{Pbx}{L} - P(x - a)$$
$$EI \frac{dy_2}{dx} = \frac{Pbx^2}{2L} - \frac{P(x-a)^2}{2} + C_3$$
$$EI \cdot y_2 = \frac{Pbx^3}{6L} - \frac{P(x-a)^3}{6} + C_3 x + C_4$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions and Continuity:**
- **BC 1:** $y_1 = 0$ at $x = 0$: $C_2 = 0$
- **BC 2:** $y_2 = 0$ at $x = L$:
$$0 = \frac{PbL^2}{6} - \frac{P(L-a)^3}{6} + C_3 L + C_4$$
$$0 = \frac{PbL^2}{6} - \frac{Pb^3}{6} + C_3 L + C_4 \quad (\text{since } L-a = b)$$
- **Continuity of slope at $x = a$:** $\frac{dy_1}{dx} = \frac{dy_2}{dx}$ at $x = a$:
$$\frac{Pba^2}{2L} + C_1 = \frac{Pba^2}{2L} - 0 + C_3 \implies C_1 = C_3$$
- **Continuity of deflection at $x = a$:** $y_1 = y_2$ at $x = a$:
$$\frac{Pba^3}{6L} + C_1 a = \frac{Pba^3}{6L} - 0 + C_3 a + C_4 \implies C_4 = 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve for Remaining Constants:**
From $C_4 = 0$, the BC 2 becomes:
$$0 = \frac{PbL^2}{6} - \frac{Pb^3}{6} + C_3 L$$
$$0 = \frac{Pb}{6}(L^2 - b^2) + C_3 L$$
$$0 = \frac{Pb}{6}(L - b)(L + b) + C_3 L = \frac{Pba}{6}(L + b) + C_3 L$$
$$C_3 = -\frac{Pba(L + b)}{6L} = -\frac{Pab(L + b)}{6L}$$

Substituting $L = a + b$:
$$L + b = a + 2b$$
$$C_3 = -\frac{Pab(a + 2b)}{6L}$$

Thus $C_1 = C_3 = -\frac{Pab(L+b)}{6L}$ and $C_2 = C_4 = 0$.

**Step 7 — Maximum Deflection Under the Load ($x = a$):**
Using Region 1 equation at $x = a$:
$$EI \cdot y_a = \frac{Pba^3}{6L} + C_1 a = \frac{Pba^3}{6L} - \frac{Pa^2 b(L + b)}{6L}$$
$$EI \cdot y_a = \frac{Pab}{6L}\left[a^2 - a(L + b)\right] = \frac{Pab}{6L}\left[a^2 - aL - ab\right]$$
$$= \frac{Pab}{6L}\left[a^2 - a(a+b) - ab\right] = \frac{Pab}{6L}\left[a^2 - a^2 - ab - ab\right] = \frac{Pab}{6L}(-2ab)$$

$$\boxed{y_a = -\frac{Pa^2 b^2}{3LEI} \quad \text{(downward, under the load)}}$$

This is the classical **"rule" result** for an eccentric point load on a simply supported beam.

**Step 8 — Slopes at the Supports:**
At $x = 0$:
$$EI \cdot \theta_A = 0 + C_1 = -\frac{Pab(L+b)}{6L}$$
$$\boxed{\theta_A = -\frac{Pab(L+b)}{6LEI}}$$

At $x = L$ (using Region 2):
$$EI \cdot \theta_B = \frac{PbL^2}{2L} - \frac{Pb^2}{2} + C_3 = \frac{PbL}{2} - \frac{Pb^2}{2} - \frac{Pab(L+b)}{6L}$$
$$= \frac{Pb(L - b)}{2} - \frac{Pab(L+b)}{6L} = \frac{Pba}{2} - \frac{Pab(L+b)}{6L}$$
$$= \frac{Pab}{6L}\left[3L - (L + b)\right] = \frac{Pab}{6L}(2L - b) = \frac{Pab(a + b)}{6L}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_B = \frac{Pab(L + a)}{6LEI}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity Check:** When $a = b = L/2$ (central load), substitute into $\theta_A$ formula:
> $$\theta_A = -\frac{P(L/2)(L/2)(L + L/2)}{6L \cdot EI} = -\frac{P \cdot L^2/4 \cdot 3L/2}{6L \cdot EI} = -\frac{3PL^3/8}{6L \cdot EI} = -\frac{PL^2}{16EI}$$
> This matches our Problem 1 result exactly. ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Since physical beam diagrams are challenging to render in Mermaid, the following block diagrams depict the **functional flow architecture** of the Double Integration Method and the **logical analysis matrix** for selecting the appropriate boundary conditions.

### 4.1 Flow Architecture: The Double Integration Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Beam with Loading] --> B[Identify Support Type]
    B --> C[Compute Reactions via Equilibrium]
    C --> D[Section Beam at Distance x]
    D --> E[Write Bending Moment M of x]
    E --> F{Number of Load Regions?}
    F --> G1[Single Region: 1 Equation] 
    F --> G2[Multiple Regions: 2+ Equations]
    G1 --> H1[Integrate Once: Slope Equation]
    G2 --> H2[Integrate Each Region Separately]
    H1 --> I1[Integrate Again: Deflection Equation]
    H2 --> I2[Integrate Each Region Again]
    I1 --> J1[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    I2 --> J2[Apply BCs and Continuity at Junction]
    J1 --> K1[Solve for C1 and C2]
    J2 --> K2[Solve for Constants and Apply Continuity]
    K1 --> L[Find Max Deflection or Slope at Required Location]
    K2 --> L
    L --> M[End: Final y and theta values]
```

### 4.2 Analysis Matrix: Boundary Condition Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SIMPLY_SUPPORTED["SIMPLY SUPPORTED BEAM"]
        SS1[Left Support x equals 0: y equals 0]
        SS2[Right Support x equals L: y equals 0]
    end
    subgraph CANTILEVER_LEFT["CANTILEVER FIXED AT LEFT"]
        CL1[Fixed End x equals 0: y equals 0]
        CL2[Fixed End x equals 0: dy/dx equals 0]
    end
    subgraph CANTILEVER_RIGHT["CANTILEVER FIXED AT RIGHT"]
        CR1[Fixed End x equals L: y equals 0]
        CR2[Fixed End x equals L: dy/dx equals 0]
    end
    subgraph CONTINUITY["AT JUNCTION OF REGIONS"]
        CJ1[Slope Continuous: dy1/dx equals dy2/dx at x equals a]
        CJ2[Deflection Continuous: y1 equals y2 at x equals a]
    end
```

### 4.3 Schematic: Beam Deflection Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SS_POINT["SS BEAM CENTRAL POINT LOAD P"]
        A1[Left Support Reaction P/2] --> A2[Midspan Load P] --> A3[Right Support Reaction P/2]
    end
    subgraph SS_UDL["SS BEAM UDL w"]
        B1[Reactions wL/2 each end] --> B2[UDL over entire span] --> B3[Symmetric parabolic deflection]
    end
    subgraph CAN_POINT["CANTILEVER POINT LOAD AT FREE END"]
        C1[Fixed Wall] --> C2[Free End Load P] --> C3[Max deflection at free end]
    end
    subgraph CAN_UDL["CANTILEVER UDL"]
        D1[Fixed Wall] --> D2[UDL over entire span] --> D3[Max deflection at free end]
    end
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram: Sign Convention Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Sign Convention Choice] --> S1{Sagging Positive?}
    S1 -- YES --> S2[M positive sags beam downward]
    S1 -- NO --> S3[Hogging Positive]
    S3 --> S4[M negative for downward loads on cantilever]
    S2 --> S5[Deflection y positive downward]
    S4 --> S6[Deflection y positive downward by convention]
    S5 --> T[Apply EI d2y/dx2 equals M]
    S6 --> T
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** 
**State the governing differential equation of the elastic curve used in the Double Integration Method. What are the units of the term $EI$?**

**Model Answer:**

The governing differential equation of the elastic curve, derived from Euler-Bernoulli beam theory, is:

$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$$

where:
- $E$ = Young's Modulus of the material (units: N/m² or Pa)
- $I$ = Area Moment of Inertia of the cross-section about the neutral axis (units: m⁴ or mm⁴)
- $M(x)$ = Bending moment at section $x$ (units: N·m or N·mm)
- $y$ = Deflection of the beam (units: m or mm)
- $x$ = Distance along the beam axis (units: m or mm)

**Units of $EI$:** 
$[E] = \text{N/m}^2$, $[I] = \text{m}^4$, so $[EI] = \text{N·m}^2$ (or N·mm²).

**Significance:** The product $EI$ is called the **flexural rigidity** of the beam. It represents the beam's resistance to bending deformation. A higher $EI$ value means a stiffer beam with smaller deflections.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** 
**List the boundary conditions used in the Double Integration Method for (a) a simply supported beam and (b) a cantilever beam fixed at the left end.**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Simply Supported Beam (Length $L$):**
At both supports, the deflection is zero (since pin and roller supports cannot move vertically):

| Location | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|
| Left Support ($x = 0$) | $y = 0$ |
| Right Support ($x = L$) | $y = 0$ |

**(b) Cantilever Beam (Fixed at Left End, $x = 0$):**
At the fixed (built-in) end, both the deflection and the slope are zero (the wall prevents both vertical movement and rotation):

| Location | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|
| Fixed End ($x = 0$) | $y = 0$ (no vertical displacement) |
| Fixed End ($x = 0$) | $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ (no rotation/slope) |

**Key Distinction:** A simply supported beam has *two* zero-deflection conditions. A cantilever has *one* zero-deflection AND *one* zero-slope condition. This is why cantilever problems result in two constants ($C_1$ and $C_2$) being zero, while simply supported problems require one BC to be applied at each end.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and is divided into two sub-parts of **7 marks each** (e.g., (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks). Internal choice is provided — solve **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**A simply supported beam of span 4 m carries a uniformly distributed load of 10 kN/m over its entire span. The beam has a rectangular cross-section of width 200 mm and depth 300 mm. Take $E = 200$ GPa.**

**(a)** Using the Double Integration Method, derive the expressions for slope and deflection.  
**(b)** Calculate the maximum slope and maximum deflection of the beam.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Span, $L = 4$ m
- UDL intensity, $w = 10$ kN/m = $10 \times 10^3$ N/m
- Cross-section: $b = 200$ mm, $d = 300$ mm
- $E = 200$ GPa = $200 \times 10^9$ N/m²

**Compute $I$:**
For a rectangular section, $I = \frac{bd^3}{12}$:
$$I = \frac{0.2 \times (0.3)^3}{12} = \frac{0.2 \times 0.027}{12} = \frac{0.0054}{12} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^4$$

**Compute $EI$:**
$$EI = 200 \times 10^9 \times 4.5 \times 10^{-4} = 9 \times 10^7 \text{ N·m}^2 = 90{,}000 \text{ kN·m}^2$$

**Part (a): Derivation of Slope and Deflection Equations**

**Step 1 — Reactions:**
By symmetry of loading and geometry:
$$R_A = R_B = \frac{wL}{2} = \frac{10 \times 4}{2} = 20 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation:**
Consider a section at distance $x$ from the left support. The UDL on the left portion contributes $w \cdot x$ acting at $x/2$ from the section:
$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x - wx \cdot \frac{x}{2} = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2} = \frac{wx(L - x)}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Elastic Curve Equation:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = \frac{wL}{2}x - \frac{wx^2}{2}$$

**Step 4 — First Integration (Slope):**
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wL}{4}x^2 - \frac{wx^3}{6} + C_1$$

**Step 5 — Second Integration (Deflection):**
$$EI \cdot y = \frac{wL}{12}x^3 - \frac{wx^4}{24} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply Boundary Conditions:**
- BC 1: $y = 0$ at $x = 0$: gives $C_2 = 0$
- BC 2: $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = L/2$ (by symmetry, slope is zero at midspan):
$$0 = \frac{wL}{4}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 - \frac{w}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3 + C_1$$
$$0 = \frac{wL^3}{16} - \frac{wL^3}{48} + C_1$$
$$0 = \frac{3wL^3 - wL^3}{48} + C_1 = \frac{2wL^3}{48} + C_1$$
$$C_1 = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$

**Final Equations (Part a Answer):**

$$\boxed{EI\frac{dy}{dx} = \frac{wLx^2}{4} - \frac{wx^3}{6} - \frac{wL^3}{24}}$$

$$\boxed{EI \cdot y = \frac{wLx^3}{12} - \frac{wx^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3 x}{24}}$$

**Part (b): Maximum Slope and Maximum Deflection**

**Maximum Slope (at supports, $x = 0$ or $x = L$):**
At $x = 0$:
$$EI \cdot \theta_A = 0 - 0 - \frac{wL^3}{24} = -\frac{wL^3}{24}$$
$$|\theta_{max}| = \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$$

Substituting values:
$$|\theta_{max}| = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \times (4)^3}{24 \times 9 \times 10^7} = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \times 64}{2.16 \times 10^9}$$
$$= \frac{640 \times 10^3}{2.16 \times 10^9} = 2.963 \times 10^{-4} \text{ radians}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{max} = 2.963 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad} = 0.000296 \text{ rad} \approx 0.01698°}}$$

**Maximum Deflection (at midspan, $x = L/2 = 2$ m):**
$$EI \cdot y_{max} = \frac{wL(L/2)^3}{12} - \frac{w(L/2)^4}{24} - \frac{wL^3(L/2)}{24}$$
$$= \frac{10 \times 4 \times 8}{12} - \frac{10 \times 16}{24} - \frac{10 \times 64 \times 2}{24} \quad (\text{in kN·m}^3)$$
$$= \frac{320}{12} - \frac{160}{24} - \frac{1280}{24}$$
$$= 26.667 - 6.667 - 53.333 = -33.333 \text{ kN·m}^3$$

$$y_{max} = \frac{33.333}{EI} = \frac{33.333 \times 10^3}{9 \times 10^7} = 3.704 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{y_{max} = 0.370 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

**Deflection Check (using standard formula):**
$$y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI} = \frac{5 \times 10 \times 10^3 \times 256}{384 \times 9 \times 10^7} = \frac{12.8 \times 10^6}{3.456 \times 10^{10}} = 3.704 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} = 0.370 \text{ mm} \checkmark$$

**Allowable Deflection Check:**
$$\frac{L}{y_{max}} = \frac{4000 \text{ mm}}{0.370 \text{ mm}} \approx 10{,}811$$

This is well within the IS 800 limit of $L/250 = 16$ mm, so the beam is safe in deflection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**A cantilever beam of length 2 m, fixed at the left end, carries a point load of 5 kN at the free end. The beam is made of steel with $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm² and has a moment of inertia $I = 4 \times 10^7$ mm⁴.**

**(a)** Determine the slope and deflection at the free end using the Double Integration Method.  
**(b)** Also find the slope and deflection at the midspan of the cantilever.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Length, $L = 2$ m = 2000 mm
- Point load at free end, $P = 5$ kN = 5000 N
- $E = 2 \times 10^5$ N/mm²
- $I = 4 \times 10^7$ mm⁴
- $EI = 2 \times 10^5 \times 4 \times 10^7 = 8 \times 10^{12}$ N·mm²

**Step 1 — Origin and Direction:**
Measure $x$ from the **fixed end** (left) towards the free end (right). The load $P$ acts downward at $x = L$.

**Step 2 — Bending Moment Equation:**
At a section $x$ from the fixed end, considering the right portion of the cut (the free end side):
- Load on right portion: $P = 5$ kN (downward) at distance $(L - x)$ from the section.
- This creates a hogging moment:

$$M(x) = -P(L - x)$$

**Step 3 — First Integration:**
$$EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = -P(L - x) = -PL + Px$$
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -PLx + \frac{Px^2}{2} + C_1$$

**Step 4 — Second Integration:**
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{PLx^2}{2} + \frac{Px^3}{6} + C_1 x + C_2$$

**Step 5 — Apply Boundary Conditions at the Fixed End ($x = 0$):**
- BC 1: $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ → $C_2 = 0$
- BC 2: $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$ → $C_1 = 0$

**Step 6 — Simplified Equations:**
$$EI \frac{dy}{dx} = -PLx + \frac{Px^2}{2}$$
$$EI \cdot y = -\frac{PLx^2}{2} + \frac{Px^3}{6}$$

**Part (a): Slope and Deflection at the Free End ($x = L$)**

**Slope at Free End:**
$$EI \cdot \theta_{free} = -PL(L) + \frac{P(L)^2}{2} = -PL^2 + \frac{PL^2}{2} = -\frac{PL^2}{2}$$
$$\theta_{free} = -\frac{PL^2}{2EI}$$

Substituting values (in consistent units, e.g., N and mm):
$$\theta_{free} = -\frac{5000 \times (2000)^2}{2 \times 8 \times 10^{12}} = -\frac{5000 \times 4 \times 10^6}{1.6 \times 10^{13}} = -\frac{2 \times 10^{10}}{1.6 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{free} = -1.25 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad} = -0.0716°}$$

The negative sign indicates the slope is in the direction of the deflected shape (downward rotation at the free end).

**Deflection at Free End:**
$$EI \cdot y_{free} = -\frac{PL^2}{2} + \frac{PL^3}{6} = -PL^3\left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{1}{6}\right) = -PL^3 \cdot \frac{2}{6} = -\frac{PL^3}{3}$$
$$y_{free} = -\frac{PL^3}{3EI}$$

$$\boxed{y_{free} = -\frac{5000 \times (2000)^3}{3 \times 8 \times 10^{12}} = -\frac{5000 \times 8 \times 10^9}{2.4 \times 10^{13}} = -\frac{4 \times 10^{13}}{2.4 \times 10^{13}} = -1.667 \text{ mm}}$$

The negative sign indicates downward deflection.
$$|y_{free}| = 1.667 \text{ mm (downward)}$$

**Part (b): Slope and Deflection at Midspan ($x = L/2 = 1000$ mm)**

**Slope at Midspan:**
$$EI \cdot \theta_{mid} = -PL\left(\frac{L}{2}\right) + \frac{P}{2}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 = -\frac{PL^2}{2} + \frac{PL^2}{8}$$
$$= PL^2\left(-\frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{8}\right) = PL^2 \cdot \left(\frac{-4 + 1}{8}\right) = -\frac{3PL^2}{8}$$
$$\theta_{mid} = -\frac{3PL^2}{8EI}$$

$$\theta_{mid} = -\frac{3 \times 5000 \times (2000)^2}{8 \times 8 \times 10^{12}} = -\frac{3 \times 5000 \times 4 \times 10^6}{6.4 \times 10^{13}} = -\frac{6 \times 10^{10}}{6.4 \times 10^{13}}$$

$$\boxed{\theta_{mid} = -9.375 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}}$$

**Deflection at Midspan:**
$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = -\frac{PL^2}{2}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2 + \frac{P}{6}\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^3$$
$$= -\frac{PL^4}{8} + \frac{PL^3}{48} = \frac{PL^4}{48}\left(-6 + 1\right) = -\frac{5PL^4}{48}$$

Wait, let me recompute carefully:
$$EI \cdot y_{mid} = -\frac{PL(L/2)^2}{2} + \frac{P(L/2)^3}{6} = -\frac{PL^3}{8} + \frac{PL^3}{48}$$
$$= PL^3\left(-\frac{1}{8} + \frac{1}{48}\right) = PL^3 \cdot \frac{-6 + 1}{48} = -\frac{5PL^3}{48}$$
$$y_{mid} = -\frac{5PL^3}{48EI}$$

$$y_{mid} = -\frac{5 \times 5000 \times (2000)^3}{48 \times 8 \times 10^{12}} = -\frac{5 \times 5000 \times 8 \times 10^9}{3.84 \times 10^{14}}$$
$$= -\frac{2 \times 10^{14}}{3.84 \times 10^{14}} = -0.5208 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{|y_{mid}| = 0.521 \text{ mm (downward)}}$$

**Verification using standard formula at midspan of cantilever:**
At any section $x$ of a cantilever with point load at free end:
$$y(x) = \frac{Px^2(3L - x)}{6EI}$$

At $x = L/2$:
$$y = \frac{P(L/2)^2(3L - L/2)}{6EI} = \frac{P \cdot L^2/4 \cdot 5L/2}{6EI} = \frac{5PL^3}{48EI} \checkmark$$

This matches our derived result.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks in DIM Problems:**
> 
> 1. **Sign Convention Errors (–3 to –5 marks):** The single most common mistake. Students mix sagging and hogging signs. **Always clearly state your sign convention at the start** of the solution. KTU examiners deduct heavily for sign ambiguity.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Boundary Conditions (–2 marks each):** A frequent error is using $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at a simply supported end. **Remember: at a simple support, only $y = 0$, NOT the slope.** Slope is zero only at fixed ends and at symmetry points (midspan of symmetric loading).
> 
> 3. **Missing Continuity Conditions (–3 marks):** For multi-region problems (e.g., eccentric load), students forget that slope AND deflection must be continuous at the load point. This gives wrong constants.
> 
> 4. **Integration Errors (–2 marks):** Errors in $\int x^2 dx$ (forgetting to divide by 3), or sign errors when integrating negative terms. Always double-check your integration.
> 
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing mm and m, or kN and N without conversion. **Always convert to a single unit system** (preferably SI: N, m, Pa) before substituting.
> 
> 6. **Forgetting to State the Deflection Location (–1 mark):** Clearly state **where** the maximum deflection occurs. KTU expects answers like "$y_{max}$ occurs at midspan ($x = L/2$)" or "$y_{max}$ occurs at the free end ($x = L$)."
> 
> 7. **Not Simplifying Common Denominators (–1 mark):** Combining fractions without LCD. KTU examiners want to see clean arithmetic.
> 
> 8. **Negative Sign Interpretation (–1 mark):** When $y$ comes out negative, state explicitly that "negative indicates downward deflection (per assumed sign convention)."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for DIM (Module 4):**

- **Governing Equation:** $EI \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} = M(x)$ — the foundation of DIM. Memorize this.

- **Procedure:** Reactions → $M(x)$ → Integrate 1st time (slope + $C_1$) → Integrate 2nd time (deflection + $C_1 x + C_2$) → Apply BCs → Solve constants.

- **Standard Boundary Conditions to Memorize:**
  - **Simply Supported (both ends):** $y = 0$ at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ only.
  - **Cantilever (fixed at $x = 0$):** $y = 0$ AND $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at $x = 0$.
  - **Symmetry (symmetric load on SS beam):** $\frac{dy}{dx} = 0$ at midspan ($x = L/2$).
  - **Multi-region:** Continuity of slope and deflection at the junction.

- **Standard Results to Memorize (Top 4):**
  - **SS + Central $P$:** $y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{48EI}$ at midspan; $\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{16EI}$ at supports.
  - **SS + UDL $w$:** $y_{max} = \frac{5wL^4}{384EI}$ at midspan; $\theta_{max} = \frac{wL^3}{24EI}$ at supports.
  - **Cantilever + $P$ at free end:** $y_{max} = \frac{PL^3}{3EI}$ at free end; $\theta_{max} = \frac{PL^2}{2EI}$ at free end.
  - **Cantilever + UDL $w$:** $y_{max} = \frac{wL^4}{8EI}$ at free end; $\theta_{max} = \frac{wL^3}{6EI}$ at free end.

- **Eccentric Point Load on SS Beam (Special Result):** $y_a = \frac{Pa^2 b^2}{3LEI}$ under the load (where $a$ and $b$ are distances from the load to each support).

- **Sign Convention Discipline:** Pick a convention at the start of every problem. Sagging-positive is standard. Stick with it throughout.

- **Unit Discipline:** Convert all dimensions to **meters (m)** and forces to **Newtons (N)** before substituting into $EI$ formulas. Otherwise, the numerical answer will be wrong by orders of magnitude.

- **Check Your Answer:** The maximum deflection should always occur where the slope is zero. Verify this when solving.

- **Bending Moment Sign Matters:** If you take moments from the right side, ensure you get the same $M(x)$ as from the left side. This is a built-in check.

- **For Multiple Loads (UDL + Point Load),** use the **superposition principle** if DIM becomes tedious — solve for each load separately and add the deflections/slopes.

- **Practical Engineering Tip:** After computing the maximum deflection, always check it against the allowable limit (typically $L/250$ to $L/360$ for building beams as per IS 456, or $L/360$ for steel as per IS 800). This demonstrates applied understanding and impresses KTU examiners.

## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

In the context of **Mechanics of Solids** under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, a **Theory of Failure** is a mathematically formulated hypothesis that predicts the **onset of yielding or fracture** in a material subjected to a complex multi-axial state of stress. Since most engineering components experience **combined loading** (a simultaneous mix of normal and shear stresses), it becomes physically impossible to test the material under every possible combination in a laboratory. Theories of Failure solve this exact problem by allowing the engineer to **reduce** any complex 3D stress state to an **equivalent uniaxial stress** that can be directly compared against the **yield strength (σy)** or **ultimate tensile strength (σut)** obtained from a simple tension test.

Mathematically, a Theory of Failure establishes a critical scalar quantity, the **Equivalent Stress (σeq)**, such that:

$$\sigma_{eq} \geq \sigma_{y} \implies \text{Failure (Yielding)}$$

$$\sigma_{eq} < \sigma_{y} \implies \text{Safe (Elastic Region)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The classification of materials into **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics — fail by fracture with little plastic deformation) and **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium, copper — fail by yielding with significant plastic flow) is the **single most important first step** before selecting a Theory of Failure. Selecting the wrong theory is a classic 3-mark loss in KTU examinations.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **wooden log** floating down a river, capable of withstanding a maximum water current of 10 km/h in any single direction. Now consider a swirling whirlpool pushing the log simultaneously from the front, side, and bottom. We cannot say "the current is 10 km/h here, so it is safe" — the *combined* swirling stress is much more damaging than any one direction alone.

A **Theory of Failure** is essentially the engineer's rulebook that answers the question:
*"How do we translate this complex multi-directional swirling stress (multi-axial stress) into a single equivalent straight push (uniaxial stress) that we can compare with our 10 km/h lab-tested limit?"*

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need multiple theories?**
> Different theories are essentially **different mathematical guesses** about *where* damage begins inside the material. Some assume damage starts from **maximum stretch** (Normal Stress Theory), some from **maximum slippage between atomic planes** (Shear Stress Theory), and some from **distortion of the crystal lattice** (Distortion Energy Theory). No single theory is universally perfect — this is why the KTU syllabus requires you to know all of them.

### 1.3 Fundamental Stress State Notation

For any 3D stress element, we identify three **principal stresses** that are always **mutually perpendicular** and have **zero associated shear stress** on their planes:

$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$$

Where **σy** denotes the **Yield Stress in simple tension**, and **ν** denotes **Poisson's Ratio** of the material.

> [!WARNING]
> **Sign Convention Trap:** Tension is taken as **Positive (+)** and Compression as **Negative (−)** for Normal Stresses in the KTU convention. Shear stresses are positive when they tend to **rotate the element clockwise**. Mixing sign conventions will cause total loss of marks.
