## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Theories of Failure

The theories taught in the KTU 2024 Scheme PCMRT303 syllabus are divided based on the **hypothesis of the cause of failure**:

**Group A — Theories based on Maximum Normal Stress / Strain (Best for Brittle Materials):**
- **Rankine's Theory** — Maximum Principal Stress Theory
- **Saint-Venant's Theory** — Maximum Principal Strain Theory

**Group B — Theories based on Maximum Shear / Distortion (Best for Ductile Materials):**
- **Tresca's Theory** — Maximum Shear Stress Theory
- **von Mises Theory** — Distortion Energy Theory
- **Haigh's Theory** — Maximum Strain Energy Theory
- **Octahedral Shear Stress Theory**

### 2.2 Detailed Operating Logic of Each Theory

**(a) Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine, 1858)**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal tensile stress** reaches the yield/ultimate strength in simple tension.
- **Why it works:** Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete) actually crack along the plane of maximum tensile stress.
- **Limitation:** Ignores the influence of the other two principal stresses entirely.

**(b) Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant)**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum principal strain** reaches the limiting strain in a simple tension test.
- **Why it works:** Better than Rankine's for some brittle materials, but over-predicts strength when compressive stresses are present.

**(c) Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca, 1864)**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **maximum shear stress** in the complex state equals the maximum shear stress at yield in a simple tension test.
- **Why it works:** Ductile materials fail by **slip along atomic planes** caused by shear — matches physical reality well for ductile metals.
- **Limitation:** Treats tension and compression identically (which is fine for ductile, but not brittle).

**(d) Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises, 1913)**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **distortion strain energy per unit volume** (the energy that changes *shape* but not *volume*) reaches the limiting value at yield in simple tension.
- **Why it works:** The hydrostatic component of stress (which only changes volume) is **excluded** — only shape-changing energy causes yield. This is the **most accurate** theory for ductile materials and is used in the ASME Boiler Code, I.S. code, and most finite-element software.
- **Limitation:** Mathematically more complex.

**(e) Maximum Strain Energy Theory (Haigh)**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **total strain energy** (not just distortion part) equals the strain energy at yield in simple tension.
- **Why it works:** A simple total-energy criterion; not as accurate as von Mises.

**(f) Octahedral Shear Stress Theory**
- **Hypothesis:** Failure occurs when the **octahedral shear stress** (shear on the plane equally inclined to all three principal axes) reaches a critical value.
- **Why it works:** Closely related to distortion energy and gives a slightly more conservative version of von Mises.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The table below is the **master reference** for the 2D / Plane Stress case (σ3 = 0). All variables: σ1, σ2 are principal stresses; σy is yield strength; ν is Poisson's ratio.

| S.No | Theory Name | Equivalent Stress (σeq) Formula | Factor of Safety (N) | Best Suited For |
|:----:|:------------|:-------------------------------|:---------------------|:----------------|
| 1 | Maximum Principal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1}$ | Brittle |
| 2 | Maximum Principal Strain (Saint-Venant) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)}$ | Brittle |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ | Ductile |
| 4 | Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ *(plane stress)* | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}}$ | Ductile (most accurate) |
| 5 | Distortion Energy (3D form) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{vm}}$ | Ductile (3D) |
| 6 | Maximum Strain Energy (Haigh) | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)}$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}}$ | Ductile |
| 7 | Octahedral Shear Stress | $\sigma_{eq} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2}}{3}\sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}}$ | Ductile |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** The Tresca and von Mises envelopes differ by approximately **15.47%** in pure shear loading. von Mises is always **less conservative** (predicts a higher safe load) than Tresca for the same ductile material — yet it is closer to experimental results. This is why ASME and I.S. codes universally prefer von Mises.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessel Design (ASME Code):** Uses von Mises theory for ductile steel shells to set wall thicknesses.
- **Bridge Girder Design:** Combined bending + shear + axial load → checked using von Mises or Tresca.
- **IC Engine Connecting Rods:** Subjected to combined bending and axial load — failure predicted using Maximum Shear Stress Theory.
- **Concrete Columns & Cast Iron Parts:** Designed using Rankine or Saint-Venant theory.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why the Tresca Equivalent Stress is (σ1 − σ3)

Consider a 3D element with principal stresses σ1, σ2, σ3 acting on mutually perpendicular faces. On any **arbitrarily oriented plane** with direction cosines (l, m, n), the **normal stress** and the **resultant shear stress** are given by **Cauchy's Stress Theorem**:

The traction vector on the plane is:

$$T_x = l\sigma_1, \quad T_y = m\sigma_2, \quad T_z = n\sigma_3$$

The normal stress σn is obtained by projecting this traction onto the normal:

$$\sigma_n = l^2\sigma_1 + m^2\sigma_2 + n^2\sigma_3$$

The total shear stress τ is found by subtracting σn² from the squared magnitude of the traction:

$$\tau^2 = T_x^2 + T_y^2 + T_z^2 - \sigma_n^2$$

$$\tau^2 = l^2\sigma_1^2 + m^2\sigma_2^2 + n^2\sigma_3^2 - (l^2\sigma_1 + m^2\sigma_2 + n^2\sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 1 — Algebraic Simplification using l² + m² + n² = 1:**

Expanding the squared term and applying the identity, after substantial algebra (Cauchy's classical manipulation), we obtain the elegant 3D identity:

$$\tau^2 = (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 l^2 m^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 m^2 n^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 n^2 l^2$$

**Step 2 — Locate the Maximum of τ²:**

Each term on the right-hand side is non-negative (squares). Therefore, to maximize τ, we must maximize each directional product. The maximum shear stress occurs on a plane whose normal bisects two of the principal axes (i.e., the plane is oriented at **45° between the maximum and minimum principal stress directions**).

Setting n = 0, the equation reduces to the well-known **2D plane stress maximum shear**:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

This is the **Tresca criterion**: failure occurs when this maximum shear stress equals the shear yield strength of a uniaxial tension specimen, which is σy/2. Therefore:

$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2} \implies \boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: von Mises Distortion Energy Equivalent Stress

The **total strain energy per unit volume** stored in an elastic, isotropic body under principal stresses is:

$$U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 1 — Decompose into Hydrostatic + Deviatoric Stress:**

The stress tensor σij can be split into a **hydrostatic (volumetric) part** that only changes volume, and a **deviatoric (distortional) part** that only changes shape:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \underbrace{\sigma_m \delta_{ij}}_{\text{Hydrostatic}} + \underbrace{S_{ij}}_{\text{Deviatoric}}$$

where σm is the **mean stress**:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Strain Energy:**

The energy associated with the hydrostatic part (pure volume change) is:

$$U_v = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2 = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 3 — Distortion Strain Energy:**

By conservation of energy, the distortion energy is **total − volumetric**:

$$U_d = U_{total} - U_v$$

**Step 4 — Apply the von Mises Hypothesis:**

At yield in a simple tension test (σ1 = σy, σ2 = σ3 = 0), the distortion energy is:

$$U_{d,\text{tension}} = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

**Step 5 — Equate the Distortion Energies:**

Setting the general Ud equal to the tension-test value, and after algebraic expansion of (σ1+σ2+σ3)²:

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

**Step 6 — Final Equivalent Stress:**

Cancelling the common factor (1+ν)/E and taking the square root, the **von Mises equivalent stress** is:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

The **failure criterion** becomes:

$$\sigma_{vm} \geq \sigma_y \implies \text{Failure}$$

**Step 7 — 2D Plane Stress Special Case (σ3 = 0):**

Substituting σ3 = 0 into the 3D formula:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

This is the **most-tested KTU form** for 2D problems.

### 3.3 Worked Example: 2D Stress State Evaluation

**Problem:** A ductile steel element (σy = 250 MPa, ν = 0.3) is subjected to principal stresses σ1 = 120 MPa (tensile), σ2 = 60 MPa (tensile), σ3 = 0 (plane stress). Determine the Factor of Safety using **Tresca** and **von Mises** theories.

**Solution — Tresca Theory:**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 120 - 0 = 120 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{Tresca} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{eq}} = \frac{250}{120} = 2.083$$

**Solution — von Mises Theory:**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{120^2 - (120)(60) + 60^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{14400 - 7200 + 3600} = \sqrt{10800} = 103.92 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{von\ Mises} = \frac{250}{103.92} = 2.406$$

**Observation:** von Mises gives a higher (less conservative) Factor of Safety, confirming the theoretical insight that **von Mises is less conservative than Tresca** for the same loading.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Theories of Failure Classification Flow

The following Mermaid diagram provides a **block-level functional architecture** classifying all KTU-prescribed theories of failure, mapping their relationships to material types, and showing the decision logic an engineer follows.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stressed Component"]:::root --> B["Identify Material Type"]
    B --> C{"Ductile or Brittle?"}:::decision
    C -- "Ductile" --> D["Ductile Theories"]
    C -- "Brittle" --> E["Brittle Theories"]
    C -- "Unknown" --> F["Use von Mises Safe Default"]

    D --> D1["Tresca Max Shear"]:::duct
    D --> D2["von Mises Distortion Energy"]:::duct
    D --> D3["Haigh Max Strain Energy"]:::duct
    D --> D4["Octahedral Shear"]:::duct

    E --> E1["Rankine Max Normal Stress"]:::brit
    E --> E2["Saint Venant Max Normal Strain"]:::brit

    D1 --> G["Compute sigma eq"]
    D2 --> G
    D3 --> G
    D4 --> G
    E1 --> G
    E2 --> G
    F --> G

    G --> H{"sigma eq vs sigma y"}:::decision
    H -- "sigma eq less than sigma y" --> I["SAFE: Elastic Region"]
    H -- "sigma eq equal sigma y" --> J["YIELD ONSET"]
    H -- "sigma eq greater sigma y" --> K["FAILURE: Plastic Collapse"]

    classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef decision fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef duct fill:#16213e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff
    classDef brit fill:#16213e,stroke:#f9b208,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Process Flow: How to Solve a KTU Problem Step-by-Step

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Step 1: Read Given 2D or 3D Stress State"]:::step --> S2["Step 2: Calculate Principal Stresses sigma1 sigma2 sigma3"]:::step
    S2 --> S3["Step 3: Identify Material sigma y and nu"]:::step
    S3 --> S4["Step 4: Decide Ductile or Brittle"]:::step
    S4 --> S5["Step 5: Apply Correct Theory Formula"]:::step
    S5 --> S6["Step 6: Compute Factor of Safety N"]:::step
    S6 --> S7["Step 7: Check N greater than Required N"]:::step
    S7 --> S8{"Pass or Fail"}:::decision
    S8 -- "Pass" --> SAFE["Component is SAFE"]:::pass
    S8 -- "Fail" --> UNSAFE["Redesign Required"]:::fail

    classDef step fill:#1e3d59,stroke:#a8dadc,color:#ffffff
    classDef decision fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
    classDef pass fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#95d5b2,color:#ffffff
    classDef fail fill:#9d0208,stroke:#ffafcc,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: von Mises vs Tresca — Octahedral Plane Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["3D Stress Element"] --> Q["Octahedral Plane: Equally Inclined to All 3 Principal Axes"]
    Q --> R["Octahedral Shear Stress tau oct"]
    R --> S1["Connection to von Mises: sigma vm equals sqrt 3 times tau oct"]:::link
    R --> S2["Connection to Tresca: Conservative Envelope"]:::link

    S1 --> T["von Mises Envelope: Smooth Ellipse in 2D pi plane"]
    S2 --> U["Tresca Envelope: Hexagonal Inscribed in von Mises Ellipse"]

    T --> V["Most Accurate for Ductile"]
    U --> W["More Conservative Safe Side"]

    classDef link fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The von Mises ellipse is the *inscribed smooth curve*, while the Tresca hexagon is *inscribed inside* it. Every experimental data point for ductile materials lies **on or inside** the von Mises ellipse — proving its superior accuracy.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **State and explain the Maximum Principal Stress Theory of Failure. Mention its applicability.**

**Model Answer:**

The Maximum Principal Stress Theory, also known as **Rankine's Theory**, was proposed by W.J.M. Rankine in 1858. The theory hypothesizes that **failure of a material occurs when the maximum principal tensile stress (σ1) in a complex stress system reaches the ultimate tensile strength (σut) of the material** in a simple tension test.

Mathematically, the failure criterion is:

$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{(for brittle fracture)}$$

For design with a Factor of Safety N:

$$\sigma_1 \leq \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{N}$$

**Applicability:** This theory gives reasonably accurate predictions for **brittle materials** like cast iron, concrete, and ceramics which fail by sudden fracture along the plane of maximum tensile stress. It is *not* suitable for ductile materials because it ignores the influence of the other two principal stresses and predicts the same strength in tension and compression (which is false for brittle materials). **[3 Marks]**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
> **Distinguish clearly between the Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca) and the Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises).**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis of Comparison | Tresca Theory (1864) | von Mises Theory (1913) |
|:--------------------|:---------------------|:------------------------|
| **Hypothesis** | Failure when max shear stress = yield shear in tension | Failure when distortion strain energy = that at yield |
| **Equivalent Stress (Plane Stress)** | $\sigma_{eq} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | $\sigma_{eq} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$ |
| **Geometrical Form** | Hexagonal yield surface in 2D | Elliptical yield surface inscribed inside hexagon |
| **Conservativeness** | More conservative (lower FOS) | Less conservative (higher FOS) |
| **Accuracy** | Approximate (15% conservative in pure shear) | Most accurate for ductile materials |
| **Code Usage** | Simpler hand calculations | ASME, I.S., FEA software default |
| **Material Suitability** | Ductile materials | Ductile materials |

**Key Difference:** Tresca is based purely on a **stress quantity** (max shear), whereas von Mises is based on an **energy quantity** (distortion strain energy). For pure shear loading, von Mises predicts a yield stress 15.47% higher than Tresca. **[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> ### **Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
>
> **(a)** State the various theories of failure and discuss their applicability to brittle and ductile materials. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A cylindrical shaft made of mild steel (σy = 320 MPa) is subjected to a bending stress of 100 MPa (tensile) and a torsional shear stress of 60 MPa. Determine the Factor of Safety according to: (i) Maximum Shear Stress Theory, and (ii) Maximum Distortion Energy Theory. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory Discussion (7 Marks):**

Theories of failure are mathematical hypotheses that predict the **onset of yielding or fracture** in materials subjected to complex multi-axial states of stress. The principal theories are:

1. **Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine):** Based on max normal stress; suitable for **brittle materials** in tension.

2. **Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant):** Based on max normal strain; suitable for **brittle materials** under combined loading.

3. **Maximum Shear Stress Theory (Tresca):** Based on max shear; suitable for **ductile materials**; gives conservative results.

4. **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises):** Based on shape-change energy; **most accurate for ductile materials**; preferred by ASME and I.S. codes.

5. **Maximum Strain Energy Theory (Haigh):** Based on total strain energy; less accurate than von Mises.

6. **Octahedral Shear Stress Theory:** Closely related to von Mises.

**Selection Rule:** Brittle materials → Rankine or Saint-Venant; Ductile materials → Tresca (simpler) or von Mises (more accurate). **[7 Marks]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Bending stress σb = 100 MPa (tensile)
- Torsional shear stress τ = 60 MPa
- Yield strength σy = 320 MPa
- **Find:** Factor of Safety by Tresca and von Mises

**Step 1 — Find Principal Stresses:**

For combined bending and torsion, the principal stresses on the surface of the shaft are:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} + \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} - \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

Substituting:

$$\sigma_1 = \frac{100}{2} + \sqrt{50^2 + 60^2} = 50 + \sqrt{2500 + 3600}$$

$$\sigma_1 = 50 + \sqrt{6100} = 50 + 78.10 = 128.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = 50 - 78.10 = -28.10 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = 0 \text{ (plane stress)}$$

**Step 2 — Tresca Theory:**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{Tresca} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 128.10 - (-28.10) = 156.20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{Tresca} = \frac{320}{156.20} = 2.049 \approx 2.05$$

**Step 3 — von Mises Theory:**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_1^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{(128.10)^2 - (128.10)(-28.10) + (-28.10)^2}$$

$$= \sqrt{16409.61 + 3599.61 + 789.61} = \sqrt{20798.83}$$

$$\sigma_{eq}^{VM} = 144.22 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{von\ Mises} = \frac{320}{144.22} = 2.219 \approx 2.22$$

**Conclusion:** The Factor of Safety by von Mises (2.22) is greater than that by Tresca (2.05), confirming that von Mises is less conservative. Both values are acceptable for design (>1). **[7 Marks]**

---

> ### **Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2022]**
>
> **(a)** Derive the expression for the equivalent stress in the Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises) for a 3D state of stress. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A brittle material element (σut = 150 MPa) is subjected to principal stresses σ1 = 80 MPa, σ2 = 40 MPa, σ3 = −60 MPa. Determine the Factor of Safety using (i) Maximum Principal Stress Theory, and (ii) Maximum Principal Strain Theory. Take Poisson's ratio ν = 0.25. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation of von Mises 3D Formula (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** The total strain energy per unit volume stored in an isotropic elastic body under principal stresses σ1, σ2, σ3 is:

$$U_{total} = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Decompose the stress into a hydrostatic (mean) part σm and a deviatoric (shear) part Sij:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

**Step 3:** The volumetric (dilatational) strain energy, which only changes volume and does not cause yielding, is:

$$U_v = \frac{3(1-2\nu)}{2E}\sigma_m^2 = \frac{1-2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 4:** The distortional (shape-changing) strain energy is the difference:

$$U_d = U_{total} - U_v$$

**Step 5:** Expanding and simplifying (using algebraic identity):

$$U_d = \frac{1+\nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Step 6:** In a simple tension test at yield (σ1 = σy, σ2 = σ3 = 0):

$$U_{d,\text{tension}} = \frac{1+\nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

**Step 7:** Equating Ud = Ud,tension and simplifying:

$$\sigma_{vm} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

This is the **von Mises equivalent stress**. **[7 Marks]**

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- σ1 = 80 MPa, σ2 = 40 MPa, σ3 = −60 MPa
- σut = 150 MPa (ultimate tensile strength for brittle material)
- ν = 0.25

**Step 1 — Maximum Principal Stress Theory (Rankine):**

For brittle materials, use ultimate tensile strength:

$$\sigma_{eq}^{Rankine} = \sigma_1 = 80 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{Rankine} = \frac{150}{80} = 1.875$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Principal Strain Theory (Saint-Venant):**

$$\sigma_{eq}^{SV} = \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)$$

$$= 80 - 0.25(40 + (-60))$$

$$= 80 - 0.25(-20) = 80 - (-5) = 80 + 5 = 85 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N_{SV} = \frac{150}{85} = 1.765$$

**Conclusion:** Both theories predict FOS > 1, so the component is safe. Rankine gives a slightly more optimistic (less conservative) result for this loading. **[7 Marks]**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-Mark Pitfalls in Theories of Failure Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mixing σy and σut:** Brittle materials use **ultimate tensile strength (σut)** in the formula; ductile materials use **yield strength (σy)**. Confusing these is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors:** In Saint-Venant's theory, students forget to include σ3 in the formula when σ3 ≠ 0. The general 3D form is **σ1 − ν(σ2 + σ3)**, not just σ1 − νσ2.
>
> 3. **Forgetting Plane Stress Assumption:** Most KTU 2D problems assume σ3 = 0. The 3D von Mises formula must be used only when σ3 is explicitly given or non-zero.
>
> 4. **Wrong Formula for von Mises Plane Stress:** A very common error is writing (σ1² + σ2²) instead of **(σ1² − σ1σ2 + σ2²)**. Memorizing the cross-term is critical.
>
> 5. **Not Showing Principal Stress Calculation:** If the problem gives a 2D stress element (σx, σy, τxy), the examiner expects you to **first compute σ1, σ2, σ3** and only then apply the theory. Skipping this step costs 3 marks.
>
> 6. **Units Inconsistency:** Mixing MPa with N/mm² or forgetting units entirely in the final answer. Always write **MPa** in the final FOS value.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Theories of Failure** convert complex 3D stress states into a single equivalent uniaxial stress to be compared with **σy** (ductile) or **σut** (brittle).
- **Six core theories** are prescribed: Rankine, Saint-Venant, Tresca, von Mises, Haigh, Octahedral Shear.
- **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete) → use **Rankine** or **Saint-Venant** theories.
- **Ductile materials** (mild steel, aluminium) → use **Tresca** (simple, conservative) or **von Mises** (accurate, code-preferred).
- **Principal stress ordering** is always **σ1 ≥ σ2 ≥ σ3** (algebraic, sign-respecting).
- **Tresca plane stress formula:** σeq = σ1 − σ3.
- **von Mises plane stress formula:** σeq = √(σ1² − σ1σ2 + σ2²) [memorize the **−σ1σ2** cross-term!].
- **von Mises 3D formula:** σeq = √[((σ1−σ2)² + (σ2−σ3)² + (σ3−σ1)²) / 2].
- **Tresca is always more conservative** than von Mises (lower FOS, safer design).
- **Factor of Safety (N) = σy / σeq**; component is safe if N > required design FOS.
- **Hydrostatic stress** does not cause yielding — it only changes volume. This is the foundation of von Mises theory.
- **15.47% difference** in pure shear between Tresca and von Mises is a frequently asked numerical.
- **Distortion Energy Theory is the gold standard** for ductile materials and is used in ASME, I.S., and FEA codes worldwide.
- **Sign convention:** Tension positive, compression negative — always recheck before calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rankine's Theory for Maximum Normal Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Rankine's Theory for Maximum Normal Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Rankine's Theory of Failure**, also formally known as the **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)** or the **First Strength Theory**, is a classical failure hypothesis used in the strength design of engineering components. The theory postulates that **failure of a brittle material occurs when the maximum principal tensile stress (σ₁) in a complex three-dimensional state of stress reaches a value equal to the ultimate tensile strength of the material obtained from a simple uniaxial tension test**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard form):**
> *"According to Rankine's Theory, a brittle material under a complex state of stress fails when the maximum principal stress developed in the material equals the ultimate stress of the material in simple tension or compression, whichever is greater."*

Mathematically, the failure criterion is expressed as:

$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{or} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \geq \sigma_{uc}$$

where **σ₁** and **σ₃** are the algebraically largest and smallest principal stresses respectively, **σ_ut** is the ultimate tensile strength, and **σ_uc** is the ultimate compressive strength of the material.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Weakest Wall in a Fort":**
> Imagine a fort built of multiple walls of differing thickness surrounding a central area under high internal pressure. The fort will breach (fail) at the **thinnest wall first**, regardless of how strong the other walls are. Similarly, Rankine's theory says: *"In any complex loading situation, a material fails at the point where the **maximum principal stress** (the maximum tensile 'pull') exceeds what the material can handle in pure tension."*

In simpler words — if you stretch a rubber band in one direction, the failure occurs when the **maximum pull in any direction** overcomes the rubber's basic pulling capacity. The theory ignores how forces interact on different planes and only cares about the worst-case pull or push direction.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (σ_ut)** — measured in **N/mm²** or **MPa**
- **Ultimate Compressive Strength (σ_uc)** — measured in **N/mm²** or **MPa**
- **Factor of Safety (F.O.S or N)** — dimensionless ratio
- **Principal Stresses (σ₁, σ₂, σ₃)** — measured in **N/mm²**
- Standard test specimen: a cylindrical bar under pure axial loading as per **IS 1608:2005** / **ASTM E8**

### 1.4 Applicability Domain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Best suited for**: **Brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, plaster, stone, glass, and ceramics.
> - **Not reliable for**: **Ductile materials** like mild steel, copper, aluminium, where shear yielding dominates failure.
> - **Primary use in KTU syllabus**: Strength design of brittle machine components like pulleys, gear teeth (cast iron), and concrete structural elements.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of the Theory (Step-by-Step Logic)

**Step 1 — Identify the State of Stress:**
The state of stress at a critical point in the component is determined using the 2D or 3D stress transformation techniques, giving the stress tensor:

$$\sigma_{ij} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_x & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{xy} & \sigma_y & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{xz} & \tau_{yz} & \sigma_z \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Extract the Principal Stresses:**
Solve the characteristic equation:

$$\sigma^3 - I_1 \sigma^2 + I_2 \sigma - I_3 = 0$$

where the stress invariants are:

$$I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$$

$$I_2 = \sigma_x \sigma_y + \sigma_y \sigma_z + \sigma_z \sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$$

$$I_3 = \sigma_x \sigma_y \sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x \tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y \tau_{zx}^2 - \sigma_z \tau_{xy}^2$$

The three real roots (for most engineering materials) are arranged as:

$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Rankine Failure Criterion:**
The material is considered safe only if:

$$\sigma_1 < \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{AND} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert < \sigma_{uc}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Factor of Safety:**

> [!NOTE]
> **The 'Why' Behind Each Step:** Step 1 ensures we know the exact loading environment. Step 2 converts this into the three 'extreme' directional stresses where shear is zero. Step 3 compares these extremes against known material strengths. Step 4 quantifies the design margin.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Table)

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_1, \sigma_{2,3} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Principal stresses in 2D | Solving plane stress / strain problems |
| 2 | $\sigma_1 \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{N}$ | Tensile failure criterion | Design of brittle members under tension |
| 3 | $\vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{N}$ | Compressive failure criterion | Design of brittle columns & struts |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}$ | Factor of safety (tension dominant) | When $\sigma_1 > \vert \sigma_3 \vert$ |
| 5 | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert}$ | Factor of safety (compression dominant) | When $\vert \sigma_3 \vert > \sigma_1$ |
| 6 | $N = \min\left(\dfrac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}, \dfrac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert}\right)$ | Combined FOS expression | General 3D design case |
| 7 | $\sigma_{\max} = \dfrac{M y}{I} + \dfrac{P}{A}$ | Maximum bending + axial stress | Combining loads in beams |
| 8 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$ | Maximum torsional shear stress | Shaft design input for MNST |
| 9 | $\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y$ | Sum invariant (2D) | Verification of principal stress calculation |
| 10 | $\theta_p = \dfrac{1}{2}\tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{2\tau_{xy}}{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}\right)$ | Principal plane angle | Orientation of principal stresses |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LaTeX Note:** All vertical bars for absolute values use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of cast iron pulleys and gear wheels** in mechanical power transmission systems.
- **Strength analysis of concrete columns and beams** in civil/structural engineering.
- **Pressure vessel nozzle design** where stress concentrations produce localized peak stresses.
- **Brittle ceramic component design** in aerospace and biomedical implants.
- **Stone and masonry design** in heritage restoration projects.

### 2.4 Comparative Context (Brief — KTU Module 4 Context)

| Failure Theory | Best Material | Governing Quantity |
|---|---|---|
| **Rankine (Max Normal Stress)** | **Brittle** | **σ₁ or σ₃** |
| Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | Ductile | σ₁ − σ₃ |
| Maximum Distortion Energy (von Mises) | Ductile | √(σ₁² + σ₂² + σ₃² − σ₁σ₂ − σ₂σ₃ − σ₃σ₁) |
| Maximum Normal Strain (Saint-Venant) | Brittle (limited) | ε₁ |
| Maximum Strain Energy (Haigh) | Brittle | Total elastic energy |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Rankine Failure Criterion from First Principles

**Objective:** To derive the governing condition under which a brittle material subjected to a triaxial state of stress is said to have failed according to Rankine's hypothesis.

**Starting Point:** Consider a small cubic element under the three principal stresses σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ acting normal to its six faces (no shear on these faces because the directions are principal).

#### 3.1.1 Uniaxial Tension Test (Reference State)

In a standard uniaxial tension test on a cylindrical specimen (diameter = d₀, gauge length = L₀), the principal stresses are:

$$\sigma_x = \sigma_t \quad \text{(applied axial stress)}, \quad \sigma_y = \sigma_z = 0$$

The maximum normal stress in this reference state is simply **σ_t**.

The ultimate strength is reached when:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_{ut}$$

#### 3.1.2 Triaxial Stress State (General Loading)

For a 3D stress state, the principal stresses are arranged such that:

$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$$

> The **'Why'**: This ordering matters because failure is initiated at the plane perpendicular to the direction of maximum stress. The middle principal stress σ₂ does not appear in the Rankine criterion — only the extreme values σ₁ and σ₃ influence failure initiation.

#### 3.1.3 Application of Rankine's Hypothesis

Rankine postulated that the *same physical failure mechanism* that occurs in uniaxial tension at σ = σ_ut will also occur in any complex stress state when the **algebraically maximum principal tensile stress** equals σ_ut, OR when the **algebraically maximum principal compressive stress** (in magnitude) equals σ_uc.

This gives the governing condition:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_{ut} \quad \text{(tensile failure)}}$$

$$\boxed{\vert \sigma_3 \vert \geq \sigma_{uc} \quad \text{(compressive failure)}}$$

#### 3.1.4 Derivation of Factor of Safety (FOS)

Let the design stresses be σ₁ and σ₃, and the allowable stresses (ultimate / FOS) be σ_ut / N and σ_uc / N respectively. The design is safe when:

$$\sigma_1 \leq \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{N} \quad \text{AND} \quad \vert \sigma_3 \vert \leq \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{N}$$

Solving each inequality for N independently:

$$N_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}, \quad N_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert}$$

Since the design must satisfy **both** conditions simultaneously, the governing (lower) value is chosen:

$$\begin{aligned} N &= \min(N_t, N_c) \\ N &= \min\left(\frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1}, \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert}\right) \end{aligned}$$

**Logic Explanation:** The `min` function picks the lower FOS because if either condition fails, the entire component fails. The smaller margin is the actual safety margin of the design.

### 3.2 Worked Derivation — Principal Stresses in 2D Plane Stress

For a 2D plane stress element with stresses σ_x, σ_y, and shear τ_xy, the characteristic equation is:

$$\sigma^2 - (\sigma_x + \sigma_y)\sigma + (\sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2) = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \pm \sqrt{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)^2 - 4(\sigma_x \sigma_y - \tau_{xy}^2)}}{2} \\ &= \frac{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \pm \sqrt{\sigma_x^2 + 2\sigma_x \sigma_y + \sigma_y^2 - 4\sigma_x \sigma_y + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}{2} \\ &= \frac{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y) \pm \sqrt{(\sigma_x - \sigma_y)^2 + 4\tau_{xy}^2}}{2} \\ &= \frac{(\sigma_x + \sigma_y)}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \end{aligned}$$

**Logic Explanation:** We expanded the discriminant, grouped the squared terms to obtain a perfect square of the difference (σ_x − σ_y)², and added the shear contribution 4τ_xy². This is the canonical KTU board form of the principal stress formula.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Formula

| Loading Condition | Principal Stress Formula to Apply | Rankine FOS Expression |
|---|---|---|
| Pure uniaxial | $\sigma_1 = \sigma, \sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$ | $N = \sigma_{ut} / \sigma$ |
| Pure biaxial | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_a, \sigma_2 = \sigma_b, \sigma_3 = 0$ | $N = \sigma_{ut} / \max(\sigma_a, \sigma_b)$ |
| Combined bending + axial | $\sigma_{\max} = M y / I + P / A$ | $N = \sigma_{ut} / \sigma_{\max}$ |
| Combined bending + torsion (brittle) | $\sigma_1, \sigma_3 = \sigma_x / 2 \pm \sqrt{(\sigma_x / 2)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | $N = \sigma_{ut} / \sigma_1$ |
| Hydrostatic + deviatoric | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_m + \sigma_d$ | $N = \sigma_{ut} / \sigma_1$ |

### 3.4 Proof that σ₂ Does Not Affect Rankine Failure

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Justification:**
> The Rankine criterion only considers the **extreme** principal stresses. The intermediate stress σ₂ has **no influence** on the failure prediction. This is a known limitation of the theory — it gives **non-conservative** results in some biaxial tension states where σ₁ alone doesn't capture the actual failure load.

Mathematically, if σ₁ < σ_ut and |σ₃| < σ_uc, then by the theory, the material is safe regardless of σ₂'s magnitude. This makes the theory easy to apply but **sometimes inaccurate**, particularly for materials exhibiting multi-axial stress interactions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rankine Failure Theory Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START: Complex Stress State at a Point] --> B[Identify Stress Tensor sigma_ij]
    B --> C[Compute Stress Invariants I1, I2, I3]
    C --> D[Solve Characteristic Equation]
    D --> E[Extract Principal Stresses]
    E --> F[Order: sigma1 GEQ sigma2 GEQ sigma3]
    F --> G{Compare sigma1 with sigma_ut}
    F --> H{Compare vert sigma3 vert with sigma_uc}
    G -->|sigma1 GEQ sigma_ut| I[FAILURE: Tensile Mode]
    G -->|sigma1 LT sigma_ut| J[SAFE in Tension]
    H -->|vert sigma3 vert GEQ sigma_uc| K[FAILURE: Compressive Mode]
    H -->|vert sigma3 vert LT sigma_uc| L[SAFE in Compression]
    J --> M{Both Safe?}
    L --> M
    M -->|Yes| N[Compute Factor of Safety N]
    M -->|No| O[Redesign: Reduce sigma1 or sigma3]
    N --> P[N = min of sigma_ut slash sigma1, sigma_uc slash vert sigma3 vert]
    P --> Q[END: Design Check Complete]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Application Workflow in KTU Problems

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Read Problem: Given Loads and Material] --> A2[Identify Material sigma_ut and sigma_uc]
    A2 --> A3[Calculate sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy at Critical Point]
    A3 --> A4[Apply Principal Stress Formula]
    A4 --> A5[Find sigma1 and sigma3]
    A5 --> A6[Apply Rankine Criterion]
    A6 --> A7{Failure?}
    A7 -->|Yes| A8[Compute FOS as ratio of limit to actual stress]
    A7 -->|No| A9[Increase Load to Find Failure Load]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Comparative Failure Theories Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Failure Theory Selection] --> R1{Brittle Material?}
    R1 -->|Yes| R2[Use Rankine Max Normal Stress]
    R1 -->|No| R3[Use Ductile Theories]
    R2 --> R2A[Check sigma1 vs sigma_ut]
    R2 --> R2B[Check vert sigma3 vert vs sigma_uc]
    R3 --> R3A[Use Tresca Max Shear Stress]
    R3 --> R3B[Use von Mises Distortion Energy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All Mermaid node identifiers follow the alpha-prefix rule (e.g., A1, R2A) to avoid conflicts with reserved keywords like `end` or `graph`.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**State Rankine's theory of failure. For which type of material is it most suitable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** *Definition:* Rankine's theory states that failure of a brittle material occurs when the maximum principal tensile stress (σ₁) or the maximum principal compressive stress (σ₃) in a complex stress state reaches the ultimate strength of the material as determined by a simple uniaxial tension or compression test.
- **(1 mark)** *Mathematical Criterion:* Mathematically, failure occurs when σ₁ ≥ σ_ut or |σ₃| ≥ σ_uc.
- **(1 mark)** *Applicability:* It is most suitable for **brittle materials** such as cast iron, concrete, stone, and ceramics.

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**A brittle material has principal stresses σ₁ = 80 MPa (tensile) and σ₃ = −40 MPa (compressive). The ultimate tensile and compressive strengths are 200 MPa and 600 MPa respectively. Calculate the factor of safety.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(0.5 mark)** Identify: σ₁ = 80 MPa, |σ₃| = 40 MPa, σ_ut = 200 MPa, σ_uc = 600 MPa.
- **(1 mark)** Compute N_t = σ_ut / σ₁ = 200 / 80 = **2.5**.
- **(1 mark)** Compute N_c = σ_uc / |σ₃| = 600 / 40 = **15.0**.
- **(0.5 mark)** Governing FOS: **N = min(2.5, 15) = 2.5** (Tensile failure governs).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**A cylindrical shaft of diameter 60 mm is simultaneously subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN and a torque of 2 kN·m. The shaft is made of cast iron with ultimate tensile strength σ_ut = 200 MPa and ultimate compressive strength σ_uc = 600 MPa.**

**(a)** *Determine the principal stresses at the surface of the shaft.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Using Rankine's theory, calculate the factor of safety of the shaft.* **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Axial stress due to tensile load (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{\frac{\pi}{4}(60)^2} = \frac{50000}{2827.43} = 17.68 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Shear stress due to torque (2 marks)**

$$\tau_{xy} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 2000 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (60)^3} = \frac{32 \times 10^6}{678584} = 47.16 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Apply principal stress formula (3 marks)**

$$\begin{aligned} \sigma_{1,3} &= \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} \\ &= \frac{17.68 + 0}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{17.68 - 0}{2}\right)^2 + (47.16)^2} \\ &= 8.84 \pm \sqrt{(8.84)^2 + (47.16)^2} \\ &= 8.84 \pm \sqrt{78.15 + 2224.07} \\ &= 8.84 \pm \sqrt{2302.22} \\ &= 8.84 \pm 47.98 \end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 56.82 \text{ MPa (tensile)}, \quad \sigma_3 = -39.14 \text{ MPa (compressive)}}$$

> The middle principal stress σ₂ = 0 (plane stress assumption).

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify given ultimate strengths (1 mark)**
σ_ut = 200 MPa, σ_uc = 600 MPa.

**Step 2: Compute tensile FOS (2 marks)**

$$N_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{200}{56.82} = 3.52$$

**Step 3: Compute compressive FOS (2 marks)**

$$N_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert} = \frac{600}{39.14} = 15.33$$

**Step 4: Determine governing FOS (2 marks)**

$$N = \min(N_t, N_c) = \min(3.52, 15.33) = \boxed{3.52}$$

The design is governed by **tensile failure**, and the shaft has a factor of safety of **3.52**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**A rectangular cross-section bar (breadth = 40 mm, depth = 80 mm) carries an axial compressive load of 100 kN together with a bending moment of 8 kN·m. The bar is made of a brittle material with σ_ut = 150 MPa and σ_uc = 450 MPa.**

**(a)** *Calculate the maximum and minimum normal stresses at the extreme fibres.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Using Rankine's theory, determine whether the bar is safe. If safe, find the factor of safety.* **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Cross-section properties (2 marks)**

$$A = 40 \times 80 = 3200 \text{ mm}^2$$

$$I = \frac{b d^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 80^3}{12} = \frac{40 \times 512000}{12} = 1,706,667 \text{ mm}^4$$

$$y_{\max} = \frac{80}{2} = 40 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Direct compressive stress (1 mark)**

$$\sigma_d = -\frac{P}{A} = -\frac{100 \times 10^3}{3200} = -31.25 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3: Bending stress (2 marks)**

$$\sigma_b = \pm \frac{M y}{I} = \pm \frac{8 \times 10^6 \times 40}{1,706,667} = \pm 187.5 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4: Combine at extreme fibres (2 marks)**

- At top fibre: $\sigma_{\max} = -31.25 + 187.5 = +156.25$ MPa (tensile)
- At bottom fibre: $\sigma_{\min} = -31.25 - 187.5 = -218.75$ MPa (compressive)

$$\boxed{\sigma_{\max} = 156.25 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{\min} = -218.75 \text{ MPa}}$$

##### **Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Identify the principal stresses at the critical fibre (1 mark)**

At the surface, shear stress τ_xy = 0 (extreme fibre is shear-free), so:

$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{\max} = 156.25 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = \sigma_{\min} = -218.75 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute tensile FOS (2 marks)**

$$N_t = \frac{\sigma_{ut}}{\sigma_1} = \frac{150}{156.25} = 0.96$$

**Step 3: Compute compressive FOS (2 marks)**

$$N_c = \frac{\sigma_{uc}}{\vert \sigma_3 \vert} = \frac{450}{218.75} = 2.057$$

**Step 4: Interpret the result (2 marks)**

$$N = \min(0.96, 2.057) = 0.96 < 1$$

Since N < 1, the bar **IS NOT SAFE** and will fail in tension at the top extreme fibre.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often forget the sign of σ_d and write +31.25 MPa instead of −31.25 MPa. The compressive load must always be negative.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** When σ_d alone exceeds σ_ut, students mistakenly still combine with bending — remember, the check applies to the **combined** σ_max.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing FOS computation when σ₃ is negative. Use |σ₃| in the denominator.
> 4. **Sign Convention Pitfall:** If a question gives σ₁ as the larger positive value and σ₃ as the larger negative value, always apply σ_ut to σ₁ and σ_uc to |σ₃|. Mixing them is a frequent error leading to full mark deduction.
> 5. **Unit Mismatch:** Always convert kN to N (×10³) and kN·m to N·mm (×10⁶) before substitution. Marks are deducted for unit errors.
> 6. **Rounding Errors:** Do not round intermediate values. Carry at least 4 significant figures until the final answer.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- ⭐ **Rankine Theory** = **Maximum Normal Stress Theory (MNST)** = **First Strength Theory** (all synonyms).
- ⭐ The governing stresses are **σ₁ (largest tensile)** and **|σ₃| (largest compressive)**; σ₂ is **ignored**.
- ⭐ **Failure Criterion:** σ₁ ≥ σ_ut OR |σ₃| ≥ σ_uc.
- ⭐ **Factor of Safety:** N = min(σ_ut/σ₁, σ_uc/|σ₃|).
- ⭐ **Best suited for brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, stone, glass, ceramics).
- ⭐ **Not suitable for ductile materials** (use Tresca or von Mises instead).
- ⭐ At the **extreme fibre of a beam**, shear is zero — so principal stresses equal the **bending + axial stresses directly**.
- ⭐ For shafts under combined bending + torsion, the principal stress formula **must** be applied because τ ≠ 0.
- ⭐ The principal stress formula: σ₁,₃ = (σ_x+σ_y)/2 ± √[((σ_x−σ_y)/2)² + τ_xy²].
- ⭐ The principal angle: θ_p = (1/2) tan⁻¹[2τ_xy / (σ_x − σ_y)].
- ⭐ Always order principal stresses: σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃ before applying Rankine.
- ⭐ **Units reminder:** 1 MPa = 1 N/mm² = 10⁶ N/m².
- ⭐ **Standard reference values for cast iron:** σ_ut ≈ 150–250 MPa, σ_uc ≈ 600–1000 MPa.
- ⭐ The theory **does not account** for the effect of the intermediate principal stress σ₂ — this is its **major limitation**.
- ⭐ In KTU problems, when "safe or not" is asked and N < 1, the answer is **"The component fails by [tensile/compressive] mode"** with explicit mention of the governing stress.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Guest's Theory for Maximum Shear Stress
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Guest's Theory for Maximum Shear Stress

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Guest's Theory of Maximum Shear Stress** is a classical failure theory proposed by James Guest in the year **1900**. The theory postulates that yielding (failure) of a ductile material begins when the **maximum shear stress** in a complex triaxial stress state reaches a value equal to the **maximum shear stress** developed in a simple uniaxial tensile test at the point of yielding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (verbatim mapping):**
> *"Failure occurs when the maximum shear stress in a material subjected to a complex system of stresses reaches a value equal to the maximum shear stress obtained from a simple tension test at the elastic limit."*

Mathematically, the failure/yield criterion is expressed as:

$$\tau_{\max}^{\text{complex}} = \tau_{\max}^{\text{tension test}}$$

For a uniaxial tension test at yield, the maximum shear stress equals **$\frac{\sigma_y}{2}$**, where **$\sigma_y$** is the **uniaxial yield strength**.

In a general triaxial stress state with principal stresses ordered as $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, the maximum shear stress is **$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$**.

Therefore, the Guest's yield criterion simplifies to:

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$$

> [!NOTE]
> This theory is essentially equivalent to the **Tresca Yield Criterion**, and is one of the earliest attempts to mathematically generalize the failure behavior of ductile materials beyond simple tension.

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to break a **chocolate bar** by sliding its top half forward and its bottom half backward (applying shear). The chocolate will begin to yield (crumble/snap) along the **45-degree slip planes** — exactly where the shear stress is maximum.

Now consider the same chocolate bar subjected to a combination of **pushing from both ends** (axial compression) and **twisting** (torsion). The internal shear stress at the most critical 45-degree plane becomes a complex combination of all these loads. Guest's Theory says:

> *"No matter how many forces you apply on the chocolate bar, it will yield at the exact same critical shear stress as it did in your simple 'slide-and-break' test."*

This is the essence — we **reduce a complex stress state** to an **equivalent simple shear condition**, making failure prediction extremely straightforward.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Standardized Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude (Structural Steel) |
|---|---|---|
| Young's Modulus | **E** | **200 GPa** |
| Poisson's Ratio | **$\nu$** | **0.30** |
| Uniaxial Yield Strength | **$\sigma_y$** | **250 MPa** |
| Maximum Shear Stress at Yield | **$\tau_y$** | **125 MPa** |
| Factor of Safety | **N (or FOS)** | **1.5 – 4.0** (typical design range) |

> [!NOTE]
> Guest's Theory is **independent of Poisson's ratio** and the **intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$**. This makes it mathematically simpler than von Mises Theory but also less accurate in certain biaxial loading scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Methodology

When a KTU board problem gives you a general 3D stress tensor and asks you to evaluate failure using Guest's Theory, follow this exact 7-step decision protocol:

**Step 1 — Identify the Stress Tensor**
Write down all six stress components:
- Normal stresses: $\sigma_x$, $\sigma_y$, $\sigma_z$
- Shear stresses: $\tau_{xy}$, $\tau_{yz}$, $\tau_{zx}$

**Step 2 — Compute Principal Stresses**
Solve the characteristic cubic equation:
$$\sigma^3 - I_1\sigma^2 + I_2\sigma - I_3 = 0$$

Where the stress invariants are:
- $I_1 = \sigma_x + \sigma_y + \sigma_z$
- $I_2 = \sigma_x\sigma_y + \sigma_y\sigma_z + \sigma_z\sigma_x - \tau_{xy}^2 - \tau_{yz}^2 - \tau_{zx}^2$
- $I_3 = \sigma_x\sigma_y\sigma_z + 2\tau_{xy}\tau_{yz}\tau_{zx} - \sigma_x\tau_{yz}^2 - \sigma_y\tau_{zx}^2 - \sigma_z\tau_{xy}^2$

**Step 3 — Order the Principal Stresses**
Sort such that:
$$\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Maximum Shear Stress**
Using Guest's core identity:
$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with Yield Shear Stress**
The uniaxial yield condition in shear is:
$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the Failure Criterion**
- If $\tau_{\max} < \tau_y$ → **SAFE**
- If $\tau_{\max} = \tau_y$ → **ONSET OF YIELDING**
- If $\tau_{\max} > \tau_y$ → **FAILURE**

**Step 7 — Compute Factor of Safety**
$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{\tau_y}{\tau_{\max}}$$

---

### KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all formulas** the KTU examiner expects for Guest's Theory. Memorize the boundary conditions carefully — these are the most commonly tested formula points.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Boundary / Physical Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uniaxial Yield Criterion | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$ | Single axial load only |
| 2 | Pure Shear Yield Criterion | $\tau_{\max} = \tau_y$ | Torsion / pure shear |
| 3 | Biaxial Stress Criterion | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | Plane stress, principal stresses ordered |
| 4 | Triaxial General Criterion | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | All three principal stresses non-zero |
| 5 | Equivalent Stress (Guest) | $\sigma_{eq}^{G} = \sigma_1 - \sigma_3$ | Definition of equivalent stress |
| 6 | Maximum Shear Stress | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ | Always the largest Mohr circle radius |
| 7 | Yield Shear Stress | $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$ | From simple tension test |
| 8 | Factor of Safety | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ | For static loading |
| 9 | Mohr Circle Radius | $R = \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | For 2D stress analysis |
| 10 | Principal Stress (2D) | $\sigma_{1,2} = \dfrac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$ | Plane stress, $\sigma_z = 0$ |

> [!NOTE]
> For **plane stress** with only two principal stresses $\sigma_A$ and $\sigma_B$ (where $\sigma_C = 0$ along the thickness direction), remember that **Guest's criterion still uses the absolute maximum and minimum values**. If $\sigma_A > 0 > \sigma_B$, then $\sigma_1 = \sigma_A$ and $\sigma_3 = \sigma_B$. If both are positive, $\sigma_3 = 0$. If both are negative, $\sigma_1 = 0$.

---

### Real-World Engineering Utility

Guest's Theory is heavily applied in the following KTU-relevant engineering contexts:

- **Pressure Vessel Design** — Hoop stress vs. longitudinal stress evaluation in cylindrical shells.
- **Shafts and Axles** — Combined bending and torsion analysis (a classic KTU question type).
- **Ductile Material Selection** — Used for **mild steel**, **aluminum alloys**, **copper**, and other metals that fail by shear slip along crystal planes.
- **Metal Forming Operations** — Forging, rolling, and drawing, where shear governs plastic flow.
- **Bridge Truss Members** — Axial members in combined tension/compression load scenarios.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Engineering Insight:** Guest's Theory is considered **conservative** (i.e., it predicts failure earlier than necessary) when compared to von Mises Theory. In aerospace and automotive applications where weight is critical, designers prefer the less conservative von Mises criterion. However, for **civil structures, pressure vessels, and bridges**, the conservatism of Guest's Theory is an advantage, providing an **inherent safety margin**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### Derivation 1 — Maximum Shear Stress in a General 3D Stress State

**Starting Point:** Consider the most general stress state at a point with three principal stresses $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, $\sigma_3$ (each acting on mutually perpendicular faces with no shear).

We wish to find the **plane on which the shear stress is maximum**. Consider an inclined plane whose normal makes direction cosines $(\ell, m, n)$ with respect to the three principal directions.

The traction vector on this plane is:
$$\vec{T} = \sigma_1 \ell \hat{e}_1 + \sigma_2 m \hat{e}_2 + \sigma_3 n \hat{e}_3$$

The normal component (normal stress) on this plane is:
$$\sigma_n = \vec{T} \cdot \hat{n} = \sigma_1 \ell^2 + \sigma_2 m^2 + \sigma_3 n^2$$

Using the identity $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$, we get the squared magnitude of the traction:
$$\vert \vec{T} \vert^2 = \sigma_1^2 \ell^2 + \sigma_2^2 m^2 + \sigma_3^2 n^2$$

The shear stress magnitude squared is:
$$\tau^2 = \vert \vec{T} \vert^2 - \sigma_n^2$$

We must maximize $\tau^2$ subject to the constraint $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$.

**Using Lagrange Multipliers** — Let $\mathcal{L} = \tau^2 - \lambda(\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 - 1)$.

After differentiation and setting partial derivatives to zero, the optimal plane is found when **one of the direction cosines is zero** (the plane contains two of the three principal axes).

Examining all three candidate cases:
- Case A: $n = 0$ → plane contains $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_2$ axes → $\tau = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_2 \vert}{2}$
- Case B: $\ell = 0$ → plane contains $\sigma_2$ and $\sigma_3$ axes → $\tau = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_2 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2}$
- Case C: $m = 0$ → plane contains $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ axes → $\tau = \dfrac{\vert \sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \vert}{2}$

Since $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$, the largest difference is $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$.

**Therefore:**

$$\boxed{\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}}$$

This plane is oriented at **45°** to both the $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ principal axes, and **contains the $\sigma_2$ axis** within its plane.

---

### Derivation 2 — Equivalence with Uniaxial Tension Test

In a **simple tension test** at the elastic limit, the stress state is purely uniaxial:
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$ (along loading axis)
- $\sigma_2 = 0$
- $\sigma_3 = 0$

The maximum shear stress at yield is therefore:
$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y - 0}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

Guest's postulate states:
$$\tau_{\max}^{\text{complex}} = \tau_y$$

Substituting:
$$\frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{\sigma_y}{2}$$

Multiplying both sides by 2:
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$$

This is the **master yield equation** of Guest's Theory.

For a **Factor of Safety (FOS)** of $N$:
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \frac{\sigma_y}{N}$$

Or equivalently:
$$N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$$

---

### Worked KTU-Style Numerical Example (Complete Solution)

**Problem Statement:**
A steel component is subjected to a 2D stress state at a critical point:
- $\sigma_x = +80$ MPa
- $\sigma_y = +30$ MPa
- $\tau_{xy} = -25$ MPa

Given: $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa. Determine whether the component yields according to Guest's Theory and compute the Factor of Safety.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the Average Stress**
$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{80 + 30}{2} = 55 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Radius of Mohr's Circle**
$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{80 - 30}{2}\right)^2 + (-25)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{(25)^2 + (25)^2} = \sqrt{625 + 625} = \sqrt{1250} \approx 35.36 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Two Principal Stresses**
$$\sigma_A = \sigma_{avg} + R = 55 + 35.36 = 90.36 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_B = \sigma_{avg} - R = 55 - 35.36 = 19.64 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Order the Principal Stresses (Including $\sigma_C = 0$)**
Since both $\sigma_A$ and $\sigma_B$ are positive, the third principal stress is zero:
- $\sigma_1 = \sigma_A = 90.36$ MPa
- $\sigma_2 = \sigma_B = 19.64$ MPa
- $\sigma_3 = 0$ MPa (plane stress condition)

**Step 5 — Apply Guest's Criterion**
$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 90.36 - 0 = 90.36 \text{ MPa}$$

Comparing with $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa:
$$90.36 \text{ MPa} < 250 \text{ MPa} \Rightarrow \textbf{SAFE — No yielding}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the Factor of Safety**
$$N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{250}{90.36} \approx 2.77$$

**Result:** The component is safe with a Factor of Safety of approximately **2.77**, satisfying standard design requirements (typically $N \geq 1.5$ to $2.0$ for static loads).

---

### Analytical Comparison Matrix — Guest vs. Other Theories

| Criterion | Guest (Max Shear) | Tresca | von Mises | Rankine (Max Normal) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Mathematical Form | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \sigma_y$ | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 \leq \sigma_y$ | $\sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \sigma_y$ | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{ut}$ or $\sigma_3 \geq -\sigma_{uc}$ |
| Year Proposed | **1900** | 1864 | 1913 | 1858 |
| Material Type | Ductile | Ductile | Ductile | Brittle |
| Considers $\sigma_2$? | **No** | No | Yes | No |
| Conservatism | Moderate–High | Moderate–High | Moderate | N/A |
| Plane Stress Locus Shape | Hexagon | Hexagon | Ellipse | Square |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### Mermaid Block Diagram — Guest's Theory Decision Flow

The following diagram maps the complete decision architecture a KTU student must follow when solving any Guest's Theory problem. This is the **canonical flow** examiners expect students to demonstrate in valuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Given Stress State at a Point]) --> stepB[Step 1: Extract Stress Components sigma_x, sigma_y, sigma_z, tau_xy, tau_yz, tau_zx]
    stepB --> stepC{Step 2: Is the Stress 2D or 3D?}
    stepC --> stepD[2D: Use Plane Stress Formula for Principal Stresses]
    stepC --> stepE[3D: Solve the Characteristic Cubic Equation for Principal Stresses]
    stepD --> stepF[Step 3: Compute Principal Stresses sigmaA and sigmaB]
    stepE --> stepG[Step 3: Compute All Three Principal Stresses sigma1, sigma2, sigma3]
    stepF --> stepH[Step 4: Order Them such that sigma_1 greater than sigma_2 greater than sigma_3]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI[Step 5: Calculate tau_max as sigma_1 minus sigma_3 divided by 2]
    stepI --> stepJ[Step 6: Calculate tau_y as sigma_y divided by 2 from Uniaxial Tension Test Data]
    stepJ --> stepK{Step 7: Compare tau_max with tau_y}
    stepK --> caseL[Case A: tau_max less than tau_y means SAFE Component]
    stepK --> caseM[Case B: tau_max equals tau_y means ONSET of Yielding]
    stepK --> caseN[Case C: tau_max greater than tau_y means FAILURE Component]
    caseL --> stepO[Step 8: Compute Factor of Safety as FOS equals sigma_y divided by sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    caseM --> stepO
    caseN --> stepO
    stepO --> endP([Final Design Decision: Accept or Reject the Component])

    style startA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff
    style endP fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0a3a0a,color:#ffffff
    style stepK fill:#c2410c,stroke:#7a1f06,color:#ffffff
    style caseL fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0a3a0a,color:#ffffff
    style caseM fill:#ca8a04,stroke:#7a5500,color:#ffffff
    style caseN fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#7a0e0e,color:#ffffff
```

### Mermaid Block Diagram — Mohr's Circle Visualization for Guest's Theory

This block diagram illustrates how **Mohr's Circle** graphically represents the maximum shear stress condition used in Guest's Theory.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA([Input: Ordered Principal Stresses sigma_1, sigma_2, sigma_3]) --> procB[Process A: Plot Three Points on sigma-axis at sigma_1, sigma_2, sigma_3]
    procB --> procC[Process B: Draw Three Mohr Circles between Consecutive Principal Stresses]
    procC --> procD[Process C: Identify the Largest Circle with Diameter equal to sigma_1 minus sigma_3]
    procD --> outputE[Output: Radius of Largest Circle equals tau_max which equals sigma_1 minus sigma_3 divided by 2]
    outputE --> finalF([Final: Compare tau_max with tau_y to Determine Failure])

    style inputA fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0a1f3a,color:#ffffff
    style procD fill:#7c3aed,stroke:#3a0e7a,color:#ffffff
    style outputE fill:#c2410c,stroke:#7a1f06,color:#ffffff
    style finalF fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#0a3a0a,color:#ffffff
```

### Schematic Description — The Critical Failure Plane

For KTU descriptive answers, picture this physical setup:

A unit cube of ductile material has principal stresses acting on its three pairs of faces. The **critical failure plane** under Guest's Theory is oriented such that:

- Its **outward normal bisects** the angle between the $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ principal directions (i.e., it lies at **45°** to both).
- The $\sigma_2$ direction lies **within the failure plane itself** (i.e., the plane contains the $\sigma_2$ axis).
- The shear stress on this plane is **maximum possible** among all orientations through the point.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For 2D problems** (which comprise ~80% of KTU questions), the failure plane is simply the **45° plane** oriented between the major and minor principal stress directions — exactly analogous to the slip plane observed in a uniaxial tension test specimen necking down.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These are direct-concept questions. KTU examiners expect **3–4 sentences** plus a formula. Avoid excessive elaboration.

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Guest's Theory of Maximum Shear Stress. Why is it considered conservative for ductile materials?**

**Model Answer:**
Guest's Theory states that yielding of a ductile material begins when the **maximum shear stress** in a complex stress state equals the **maximum shear stress** in a simple tension test at the elastic limit. Mathematically, the failure criterion is $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$.

The theory is considered **conservative** because it ignores the intermediate principal stress $\sigma_2$ entirely, and uses only the largest possible stress difference. This means it predicts yielding at **lower applied loads** than the actual material behavior observed experimentally. For safety-critical designs such as pressure vessels, this conservatism is beneficial.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between Guest's Theory and Rankine's Theory in terms of applicability to material types.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Guest's Theory | Rankine's Theory |
|---|---|---|
| Applicable Material | **Ductile materials** (steel, aluminum) | **Brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete, ceramics) |
| Failure Mode Predicted | Yielding by **shear slip** | Fracture by **normal stress** |
| Key Formula | $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y$ | $\sigma_1 = \sigma_{ut}$ or $\sigma_3 = -\sigma_{uc}$ |
| Stresses Considered | Maximum and minimum principal stresses | Maximum or minimum principal stress only |

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU mandates an **internal choice** in Part B. We provide two completely independent questions below.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4]**

A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** is subjected to a bending moment of **2.5 kN·m** and a torque of **4.0 kN·m** simultaneously. The material of the shaft is **mild steel** with a yield strength of **240 MPa**. Using **Guest's Theory of Maximum Shear Stress**, determine:
**(a)** Whether the shaft yields at the surface point of maximum stress.
**(b)** The Factor of Safety based on Guest's criterion.

---

##### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Stress State at the Critical Surface Point**

At the outer surface of the shaft (radius $r = 30$ mm = $0.030$ m), the critical point experiences both **bending stress** and **torsional shear stress**. This is a classic **2D stress state** with no axial load.

**Step 2 — Compute the Bending Stress**

The bending stress (maximum at outer fiber) is:
$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 M}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{32 \times 2.5 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (0.060)^3}$$

$$\sigma_b = \frac{80{,}000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{80{,}000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_b \approx 117.9 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 117.9 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Torsional Shear Stress**

The torsional shear stress at the outer surface is:
$$\tau = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^3}$$

$$\tau = \frac{16 \times 4.0 \times 10^3}{\pi \times (0.060)^3}$$

$$\tau = \frac{64{,}000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 94.3 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 94.3 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Establish the Local Stress State**

At the critical surface point:
- $\sigma_x = \sigma_b = 117.9$ MPa (axial direction, bending)
- $\sigma_y = 0$ (free surface, plane stress)
- $\tau_{xy} = \tau = 94.3$ MPa (torsional shear)

**Step 5 — Solve (a): Compute Principal Stresses**

$$\sigma_{avg} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{117.9 + 0}{2} = 58.95 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(58.95)^2 + (94.3)^2}$$

$$R = \sqrt{3475.1 + 8892.5} = \sqrt{12367.6} \approx 111.21 \text{ MPa}$$

Therefore:
$$\sigma_1 = \sigma_{avg} + R = 58.95 + 111.21 = 170.16 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_2 = \sigma_{avg} - R = 58.95 - 111.21 = -52.26 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_3 = 0 \text{ MPa (plane stress)}$$

**Re-ordering** (since $\sigma_2 < 0 < \sigma_1$):
- $\sigma_1 = 170.16$ MPa
- $\sigma_2 = 0$
- $\sigma_3 = -52.26$ MPa

**Step 6 — Apply Guest's Criterion**

$$\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = 170.16 - (-52.26) = 222.42 \text{ MPa}$$

Comparing with $\sigma_y = 240$ MPa:

$$222.42 \text{ MPa} < 240 \text{ MPa} \Rightarrow \textbf{The shaft does NOT yield (SAFE)}$$

**Step 7 — Solve (b): Factor of Safety**

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{240}{222.42} \approx 1.079$$

**Conclusion:** The shaft is safe under combined bending and torsion, with a Factor of Safety of approximately **1.08** according to Guest's Theory. For practical design, this is borderline acceptable but typically engineers would recommend a redesign (larger diameter) to achieve FOS $\geq 1.5$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper 2024, Module 4 Alternative]**

A pressure vessel component is made of a ductile alloy with yield strength **$\sigma_y = 320$ MPa**. At a critical internal point, the stresses measured (in MPa) on three mutually perpendicular faces are:
- $\sigma_x = 150$ MPa, $\sigma_y = 80$ MPa, $\sigma_z = -40$ MPa
- $\tau_{xy} = 60$ MPa, $\tau_{yz} = 0$, $\tau_{zx} = 0$

**(a)** Determine the principal stresses and the maximum shear stress using the analytical method.
**(b)** Evaluate the safety of the component using Guest's Theory and compute the Factor of Safety.

---

##### **Solution:**

**Step 1 — Solve (a): Identify the Simplified Stress State**

Since $\tau_{yz} = 0$ and $\tau_{zx} = 0$, the shear stresses exist only in the xy-plane. This reduces to a 2D problem in the xy-plane combined with the out-of-plane stress $\sigma_z$.

**Step 2 — Compute In-Plane Principal Stresses**

Using the 2D principal stress formula in the xy-plane:
$$\sigma_{avg,xy} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} = \frac{150 + 80}{2} = 115 \text{ MPa}$$

$$R_{xy} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2} = \sqrt{(35)^2 + (60)^2}$$

$$R_{xy} = \sqrt{1225 + 3600} = \sqrt{4825} \approx 69.46 \text{ MPa}$$

The in-plane principal stresses are:
$$\sigma_A = 115 + 69.46 = 184.46 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\sigma_B = 115 - 69.46 = 45.54 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Third Principal Stress**

Since the out-of-plane faces have no shear, $\sigma_z = -40$ MPa is itself a principal stress:
$$\sigma_C = \sigma_z = -40 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Order All Three Principal Stresses**

Among $\{184.46, 45.54, -40\}$ MPa:
- $\sigma_1 = 184.46$ MPa (maximum)
- $\sigma_2 = 45.54$ MPa (intermediate)
- $\sigma_3 = -40$ MPa (minimum)

**Step 5 — Compute the Maximum Shear Stress**

$$\tau_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2} = \frac{184.46 - (-40)}{2} = \frac{224.46}{2} \approx 112.23 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 6 — Solve (b): Apply Guest's Criterion**

The yield shear stress from simple tension test:
$$\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{2} = \frac{320}{2} = 160 \text{ MPa}$$

Compare:
$$\tau_{\max} = 112.23 \text{ MPa} < \tau_y = 160 \text{ MPa}$$

**The component is SAFE — yielding has not occurred.**

**Step 7 — Compute the Factor of Safety**

$$FOS = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3} = \frac{320}{184.46 - (-40)} = \frac{320}{224.46} \approx 1.426$$

**Conclusion:** The pressure vessel component is **safe** with a Factor of Safety of approximately **1.43** based on Guest's Theory. The component passes the safety check.

---

### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Guest's Theory Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Third Principal Stress ($\sigma_3 = 0$)** — In plane stress problems, students often compute only $\sigma_A$ and $\sigma_B$ and forget that the **out-of-plane stress is zero**, which means $\sigma_3 = 0$. This is by far the most common error and can flip the failure criterion entirely.
>
> 2. **Incorrect Ordering** — Always explicitly state: $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$. Even if your computed values look correct, without explicit ordering, the examiner deducts marks.
>
> 3. **Using $\sigma_A - \sigma_B$ Instead of $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$** — This error of using only the two in-plane principal stresses (instead of including the zero out-of-plane stress when applicable) is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
>
> 4. **Unit Confusion** — Mixing MPa with N/m² or forgetting to convert kN·m to N·m in bending/torsion problems. Always check units explicitly.
>
> 5. **Missing the Factor of Safety Step** — KTU examiners frequently award 2–3 marks specifically for the FOS calculation. Always include it.
>
> 6. **Confusing Guest's Theory with Tresca** — In a KTU answer, explicitly clarify that they are mathematically equivalent for isotropic ductile materials but Guest's formulation emphasizes the **maximum shear stress** interpretation.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Bullet Points:**

- ✅ **Core Equation:** $\boxed{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3 = \sigma_y}$ — the **single most important formula** of Guest's Theory.
- ✅ **Maximum Shear Stress:** $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}{2}$ — always the radius of the **largest** Mohr's circle.
- ✅ **Yield Shear Stress:** $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{2}$ — derived from a **simple tension test**.
- ✅ **Factor of Safety:** $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_3}$ — for static loading conditions.
- ✅ **Critical Plane Orientation:** **45°** between $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ axes, **containing** the $\sigma_2$ axis.
- ✅ **Independent of $\sigma_2$:** Guest's Theory **does NOT consider** the intermediate principal stress.
- ✅ **Independent of Poisson's Ratio:** Unlike von Mises (which is related to distortional strain energy), Guest's uses only stresses.
- ✅ **Symmetric in Tension-Compression:** Theory predicts **same** yield strength in tension and compression (a limitation for materials like cast iron).
- ✅ **For Plane Stress:** Always include $\sigma_3 = 0$ as a principal stress when computing $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$.
- ✅ **Applicable to Ductile Materials Only:** Mild steel, aluminum, copper, ductile iron — **NOT** cast iron or concrete.
- ✅ **Equivalent to Tresca Criterion:** Both theories yield identical results for isotropic ductile metals.
- ✅ **Year Proposed:** **1900** by James Guest — commonly asked as a 1-mark factual question.
- ✅ **Mathematical Form on $\pi$-plane:** A **regular hexagon** (for 2D principal stress space).
- ✅ **Mohr's Circle Insight:** The largest Mohr's circle passes through $\sigma_1$ and $\sigma_3$ — Guest's Theory uses this circle's radius as the governing shear.
- ✅ **Comparison Tip:** Guest's Theory is **more conservative** than von Mises but **less conservative** than Rankine's (for ductile materials).
- ✅ **Combined Loading Mastery:** Practice **bending + torsion** on circular shafts — this is the most frequently tested problem type in KTU Module 4.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mastery Tip for KTU Exams:** When solving problems, **always draw Mohr's circle first**. The diameter of the largest circle instantly tells you $\sigma_1 - \sigma_3$. This visualization technique saves calculation time and earns full marks when shown in the answer sheet.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Saint-Venant's Theory for Maximum Normal Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Saint-Venant's Theory for Maximum Normal Strain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory (Maximum Principal Strain Theory):**
> A brittle-failure hypothesis proposed by the French engineer **Adhémar Jean Claude Barré de Saint-Venant** (in 1837). It postulates that *a material element subjected to a complex tri-axial state of stress is considered to have failed (or yielded) when the **maximum principal normal strain** at that point reaches the limiting strain (yield strain) corresponding to the material's strength in a simple uniaxial tensile test.*

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus wording, the criterion is:

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} \;\geq\; \varepsilon_{y}
$$

where $\varepsilon_{\max}$ is the **algebraically largest** principal strain computed from the actual stress state, and $\varepsilon_{y} = \sigma_{y}/E$ is the yield strain in pure tension.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — the stretched rubber band test:**
> Imagine a child pulling a brittle chalk stick between his two hands. The chalk breaks **not** when the pulling force becomes large enough, but when the chalk **stretches** (deforms) by an amount greater than its tiny "stretching tolerance." Saint-Venant's theory says essentially the same thing: failure is a *deformation-controlled* event, not a force-controlled one. The longitudinal stretch of the fibres, including the squashing effect of the perpendicular stresses (captured by Poisson's ratio $\nu$), decides the safety.

> **Physical constants used in this topic (always carry in bold):**
> - **E** = Young's Modulus of Elasticity
> - **ν (nu)** = Poisson's Ratio
> - **σ_y** = Yield Stress in simple tension

### 1.3 Where the Theory Fits Among the Four Classical Failure Theories

| # | Theory | Critical Parameter | Best Suited For |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Maximum Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_{\max}$ | Brittle, uniaxial |
| 2 | **Maximum Normal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\varepsilon_{\max}$ | **Brittle, where lateral strains matter** |
| 3 | Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\tau_{\max}$ | Ductile |
| 4 | Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $U_{d}$ | Ductile (most accurate) |
