### 1.4 Why a Separate Theory?

Maximum Normal Stress Theory ignores the *contracting* effect of orthogonal stresses. However, when a member is loaded in tension along one axis and compressed along another, the **net tensile strain is reduced** (because the compression tries to shorten the fibres that tension tries to elongate). Saint-Venant's theory captures this by working directly with strains obtained from **generalized Hooke's Law**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Theory

The theory can be applied in **four structured steps**:

1. **Resolve the given stress state into principal stresses** $\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \sigma_{3}$ (ordered such that $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \sigma_{3}$).
2. **Compute the principal strains** using Generalized Hooke's Law for a 3-D stress state:
   - $\varepsilon_{1} = \dfrac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\bigr]$
   - $\varepsilon_{2} = \dfrac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{2} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{3})\bigr]$
   - $\varepsilon_{3} = \dfrac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{3} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{2})\bigr]$
3. **Identify $\varepsilon_{\max}$** as the algebraically greatest of $\{\varepsilon_{1}, \varepsilon_{2}, \varepsilon_{3}\}$ — remember this is a **signed** quantity (tensile = positive, compressive = negative). The most negative principal strain (largest in magnitude on the compressive side) is $\varepsilon_{\min}$, and its absolute value $\vert \varepsilon_{\min} \vert$ must also be compared with the yield strain (because a brittle material can also crush in compression).
4. **Apply the failure criterion**:
   - Failure occurs if $\varepsilon_{\max} \geq \sigma_{y}/E$ **OR** $\vert \varepsilon_{\min} \vert \geq \sigma_{y}/E$.
   - The Factor of Safety (FoS) is:
     $$
     N = \frac{\sigma_{y}/E}{\max\left(\varepsilon_{\max},\;\vert \varepsilon_{\min} \vert\right)} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{eq}}}
     $$
     where the equivalent (Saint-Venant) stress $\sigma_{\text{eq}}$ is defined by setting $\varepsilon_{\max} = \sigma_{\text{eq}}/E$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Print-and-keep)

> [!NOTE]
> **The five equations you MUST memorize for KTU exams on this topic:**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plane-stress principal strain | $\varepsilon_{1} = \dfrac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}\bigr]$ | 2-D problems |
| 2 | 3-D principal strain (general) | $\varepsilon_{i} = \dfrac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{i} - \nu(\sigma_{j} + \sigma_{k})\bigr]$ | 3-D / pressure vessels |
| 3 | Yield strain reference | $\varepsilon_{y} = \sigma_{y} / E$ | Material constant |
| 4 | Failure criterion | $\varepsilon_{\max} \;\geq\; \varepsilon_{y}$ | **The theory itself** |
| 5 | Factor of Safety | $N = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{eq}}}$ | Design problem |
| 6 | Equivalent stress (plane) | $\sigma_{\text{eq}} = \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}$ | 2-D FoS |
| 7 | Equivalent stress (3-D) | $\sigma_{\text{eq}} = \sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})$ | Pressure vessels |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL PITFALL:** In the failure criterion, always compare the **algebraically maximum strain** (which may be **negative** for pure compression) and the **minimum (most compressive) strain's absolute value** with the yield strain. Many students check only the tensile side and miss compressive crushing failures.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Saint-Venant's theory is used in:
- **Cast iron, concrete, and rock mechanics** design, where brittle fracture and lateral constraint effects are crucial.
- **Brittle aerospace composites** (CFRP laminates) when fibre-direction strain is the limiting factor.
- **Piezoelectric and ceramic transducer design** where electric-field-induced strains are critical.
- **Design of gun barrels and high-pressure ceramic pipes** subjected to biaxial tension where lateral Poisson contraction is significant.
- **Material testing for brittle polymers** where compressive yield differs from tensile yield.

### 2.4 Limitations of the Theory

> [!WARNING]
> 1. The theory **over-predicts** strength in cases where all principal stresses are tensile and of comparable magnitude (e.g., hydrostatic tension).
> 2. It **under-predicts** strength under pure hydrostatic compression, where a brittle material can actually sustain very large compressive strains.
> 3. It is **inaccurate for ductile materials** (steel, aluminium) where shear/yield, not fracture strain, governs failure.
> 4. Poisson's ratio $\nu$ has a strong influence; small errors in $\nu$ create large errors in the predicted FoS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Maximum Normal Strain Failure Criterion

We begin with the **3-D Generalized Hooke's Law** for a homogeneous, isotropic, linearly elastic solid:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{x} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{x} - \nu(\sigma_{y} + \sigma_{z})\bigr] \\
\varepsilon_{y} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{y} - \nu(\sigma_{x} + \sigma_{z})\bigr] \\
\varepsilon_{z} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{z} - \nu(\sigma_{x} + \sigma_{y})\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Because the strain tensor is coaxial with the stress tensor for an isotropic linear-elastic material, the principal directions of stress and strain coincide. Therefore, we can directly substitute the principal stresses $\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \sigma_{3}$ into the same relation to obtain the principal strains:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{1} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\bigr] \\
\varepsilon_{2} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{2} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{3})\bigr] \\
\varepsilon_{3} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{3} - \nu(\sigma_{1} + \sigma_{2})\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

> The above substitution is valid because in a principal frame, all shear stresses vanish, making the equations identical in form to the original ones.

**Step 2:** In a **uniaxial tensile test** where only $\sigma_{y}$ is applied (the material's yield stress), the strain at yield is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{y} \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E} \quad\quad \text{(since } \sigma_{2} = \sigma_{3} = 0\text{)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the reference strain (the **limiting strain**) for the theory.

**Step 3:** Saint-Venant's postulate states that failure occurs when the **maximum** principal strain in the actual (complex) state of stress equals this reference yield strain. Hence the failure criterion:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bigl(\varepsilon_{\max}\bigr)_{\text{actual}} \;\;=\;\; \varepsilon_{y} \;\;=\;\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Explicitly, for the algebraically largest principal strain (say $\varepsilon_{1}$), this becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\bigr] \;\;=\;\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
\end{aligned}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $E$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3}) \;\;=\;\; \sigma_{y}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Definition of **Saint-Venant Equivalent Stress**. The left-hand side is by definition the "equivalent uniaxial stress" that would produce the same maximum strain as the actual tri-axial state:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{\text{eq}} \;=\; \sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\;}
$$

So the failure criterion may be expressed in a stress form:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{eq}} \;\;\geq\;\; \sigma_{y} \quad\quad\Longrightarrow\quad\quad \text{Failure}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5:** The **Factor of Safety** for design purposes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N \;\;=\;\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{eq}}} \;\;=\;\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.2 Specialization to Plane Stress (Most Common in KTU Problems)

For plane stress, $\sigma_{3} = 0$. The criterion collapses to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{eq}} &= \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2} \\
N &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **standard KTU form** that students are tested on. Memorize it.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Step-by-Step Numerical Evaluation

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** At a point in a machine frame, the stresses are $\sigma_{x} = 60$ MPa, $\sigma_{y} = 30$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 20$ MPa. The material has $\sigma_{y} = 240$ MPa, $E = 200$ GPa, $\nu = 0.3$. Find the Factor of Safety by Saint-Venant's theory.

**Step 1: Compute principal stresses**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{\sigma_{x} + \sigma_{y}}{2} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_{x} - \sigma_{y}}{2}\right)^{2} + \tau_{xy}^{2}} \\
&= \frac{60 + 30}{2} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{60 - 30}{2}\right)^{2} + 20^{2}} \\
&= 45 \;\pm\; \sqrt{15^{2} + 20^{2}} \\
&= 45 \;\pm\; \sqrt{225 + 400} \\
&= 45 \;\pm\; \sqrt{625} \\
&= 45 \;\pm\; 25 \\
\therefore\;\; \sigma_{1} &= 70 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{2} = 20 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute the Saint-Venant equivalent stress**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{eq}} &= \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2} \\
&= 70 - (0.3)(20) \\
&= 70 - 6 \\
&= 64 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the Factor of Safety**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{eq}}} \\
&= \frac{240}{64} \\
&= 3.75
\end{aligned}
$$

> **The member is safe**, since $N = 3.75 > 1$.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Saint-Venant vs. Other Theories (For the same example)

| Theory | Equivalent Stress | FoS | Conclusion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Max Normal Stress (Rankine) | $\sigma_{1} = 70$ | $240/70 = 3.43$ | Safe |
| **Max Normal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\sigma_{1} - \nu\sigma_{2} = 64$ | $240/64 = \mathbf{3.75}$ | **Safe (most lenient)** |
| Max Shear Stress (Tresca) | $\sigma_{1} - \sigma_{2} = 50$ | $240/50 = 4.80$ | Safe |
| Distortion Energy (von Mises) | $\sqrt{\sigma_{1}^{2} - \sigma_{1}\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{2}^{2}} = 60.83$ | $240/60.83 = 3.95$ | Safe |

> **Observation:** Saint-Venant gave the **highest** FoS here because $\sigma_{2}$ is positive (tensile) and the term $\nu\sigma_{2}$ *reduces* the equivalent stress. In the opposite case (compressive $\sigma_{2}$), Saint-Venant becomes the **most conservative** theory.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Saint-Venant's Theory Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Given Stress State sigma_x, sigma_y, tau_xy]) --> nodeA
    nodeA[Step 1: Compute Principal Stresses sigma_1 and sigma_2] --> nodeB
    nodeB[Step 2: Order them so sigma_1 greater than or equal to sigma_2] --> nodeC
    nodeC[Step 3: Compute Principal Strains using Generalized Hookes Law] --> nodeD
    nodeD[Step 4: Identify Algebraically Maximum Strain eps_max] --> nodeE
    nodeE[Step 5: Compute Equivalent Stress sigma_eq = sigma_1 MINUS nu times sigma_2] --> nodeF
    nodeF[Step 6: Compare sigma_eq with Yield Stress sigma_y] --> nodeG
    nodeG{Is sigma_eq less than sigma_y?}
    nodeG -- YES --> nodeH[Member is SAFE]
    nodeG -- NO --> nodeI[Member has FAILED by Maximum Normal Strain]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Compute Factor of Safety N = sigma_y divided by sigma_eq]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Stop: Redesign with lower load or higher strength material]
    nodeJ --> nodeEnd([End of Analysis])
    nodeK --> nodeEnd
```

### 4.2 Conceptual Stress-Strain Map of the Theory

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA1[Uniaxial Tension Test] --> nodeB1[Yield Strain eps_y equals sigma_y divided by E]
    nodeA2[Complex 2D or 3D Stress State] --> nodeB2[Compute Principal Strains eps_1 eps_2 eps_3]
    nodeB1 --> nodeC1{eps_max GREATER THAN eps_y ?}
    nodeB2 --> nodeC1
    nodeC1 -- YES --> nodeD1[FAILURE: Saint-Venant Criterion Violated]
    nodeC1 -- NO --> nodeE1[SAFE: Continue Design]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Showing the Role of Poisson's Ratio

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS1[Stress sigma_1 along X direction TENSILE] --> nodeS2[Fibres try to elongate]
    nodeS3[Stress sigma_2 along Y direction TENSILE] --> nodeS4[Fibres also try to elongate along Y]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5[Net strain in X reduced by nu times sigma_2 effect]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS5
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6[Final eps_1 EQUALS 1 over E times sigma_1 MINUS nu sigma_2]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7[Compare with sigma_y divided by E]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams above present a **functional flow** of the theory, since a real physical 2-D/3-D strain-element drawing is impractical inside Mermaid. The block-level architecture faithfully captures every conceptual step required by the KTU 2024 examiner.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*State Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory of failure. How does it differ from the Maximum Normal Stress Theory?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Saint-Venant's theory states that a material element fails when the **maximum principal normal strain** in the element equals the yield strain in a simple uniaxial tensile test, i.e., $\varepsilon_{\max} = \sigma_{y}/E$. (2 marks)

It differs from the Maximum Normal Stress Theory (Rankine) in that Saint-Venant's theory accounts for the lateral contraction (Poisson effect) caused by orthogonal stresses, whereas Rankine's theory only compares the largest principal stress with $\sigma_{y}$ and ignores lateral strain effects. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Write the mathematical expression for the principal strain $\varepsilon_{1}$ in a 3-D state of stress. Define each term.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$
\varepsilon_{1} = \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})\bigr]
$$
(2 marks) where $E$ is Young's Modulus, $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio, and $\sigma_{1}, \sigma_{2}, \sigma_{3}$ are the three principal stresses ordered $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \sigma_{3}$. (1 mark for definitions)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice Pattern)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Derive the failure criterion according to Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory for a plane stress state. State its assumptions and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
- The material is homogeneous, isotropic, and linearly elastic.
- The principal directions of stress and strain coincide.
- Failure occurs when the maximum normal strain reaches the tensile yield strain $\sigma_{y}/E$.

**Derivation (5 marks):** Starting from the 2-D Generalized Hooke's Law in the principal frame ($\sigma_{3} = 0$ for plane stress):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{1} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}\bigr] \\
\varepsilon_{2} &= \frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{2} - \nu\,\sigma_{1}\bigr]
\end{aligned}
$$

In a uniaxial tensile test on the same material, $\sigma_{2} = 0$ and the yield strain is:

$$
\varepsilon_{y} = \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
$$

Saint-Venant's hypothesis equates the algebraically maximum principal strain with the yield strain:

$$
\max(\varepsilon_{1}, \varepsilon_{2}) \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
$$

If $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2}$, then $\varepsilon_{1}$ is the largest strain, and the failure criterion becomes:

$$
\frac{1}{E}\bigl[\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}\bigr] \;=\; \frac{\sigma_{y}}{E}
$$

Multiplying by $E$:

$$
\boxed{\;\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2} \;=\; \sigma_{y}\;}
$$

This is the Saint-Venant equivalent stress criterion for plane stress.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
- Over-predicts strength under hydrostatic tension.
- Under-predicts strength under hydrostatic compression.
- Not suitable for ductile materials (where shear governs yield).

---

**(b) At a critical point in a cast-iron machine bracket, the stresses are $\sigma_{x} = 50$ MPa, $\sigma_{y} = 20$ MPa, $\tau_{xy} = 15$ MPa. If the ultimate tensile strength of the material is $\sigma_{u} = 180$ MPa and Poisson's ratio is $\nu = 0.25$, determine the Factor of Safety using Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Principal stresses (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{1,2} &= \frac{50 + 20}{2} \;\pm\; \sqrt{\left(\frac{50 - 20}{2}\right)^{2} + 15^{2}} \\
&= 35 \;\pm\; \sqrt{15^{2} + 15^{2}} \\
&= 35 \;\pm\; 15\sqrt{2} \\
&= 35 \;\pm\; 21.213 \\
\therefore\;\; \sigma_{1} &= 56.213 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_{2} = 13.787 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Saint-Venant equivalent stress (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{eq}} &= \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2} \\
&= 56.213 - (0.25)(13.787) \\
&= 56.213 - 3.447 \\
&= 52.766 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Factor of Safety (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{\sigma_{u}}{\sigma_{\text{eq}}} \\
&= \frac{180}{52.766} \\
&\approx 3.41
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**

Since $N \approx 3.41 > 1$, the bracket is **safe** under the given loading by Saint-Venant's criterion.

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)**

**(a) Discuss the applications, advantages, and limitations of Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory in engineering design. Compare it with the Maximum Normal Stress Theory. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Applications (2 marks):**
- Design of brittle components such as cast-iron gears, concrete columns, ceramic pipes, and rock bolts.
- Pressure vessels made of brittle materials where Poisson-induced lateral strains affect fracture.
- Composite laminate design where fibre-direction tensile strain limits govern.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
- More accurate than Rankine's theory for **biaxial stress states** in brittle materials because it accounts for the **lateral strain interaction** through Poisson's ratio.
- Conceptually simple and requires only elastic constants ($E, \nu$) plus principal stresses.
- Can be applied using **only uniaxial tensile data**, which is the easiest strength test to perform.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
- Gives **conservative (over-safe)** results when $\sigma_{2}$ is compressive and **non-conservative (unsafe)** when $\sigma_{2}$ is tensile with similar magnitude to $\sigma_{1}$.
- Highly sensitive to the value of Poisson's ratio $\nu$.
- **Not valid for ductile materials** (steel, aluminium, copper) where shear yielding dominates.
- Fails completely under hydrostatic stress states.

**Comparison with Maximum Normal Stress Theory (1 mark):**
The Maximum Normal Stress Theory uses $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{u}$ as the failure criterion, ignoring lateral strains. Saint-Venant's theory refines this by replacing $\sigma_{1}$ with $(\sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2})$, which gives a higher FoS when $\sigma_{2}$ is tensile and a lower FoS when $\sigma_{2}$ is compressive. Hence, **Saint-Venant's theory is more realistic** for biaxial brittle fracture.

---

**(b) A thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel has an internal diameter of 300 mm and a wall thickness of 12 mm. The vessel is made of a brittle material with $\sigma_{y} = 150$ MPa, $E = 210$ GPa, and $\nu = 0.28$. Using Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory, find the maximum permissible internal pressure. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Stresses in a thin cylinder (2 marks)**

For a thin-walled cylinder with internal pressure $p$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{hoop}} &= \frac{p\,d}{2\,t} = \frac{p \times 300}{2 \times 12} = 12.5\,p \text{ MPa (when } p \text{ in MPa)} \\
\sigma_{\text{long}} &= \frac{p\,d}{4\,t} = \frac{p \times 300}{4 \times 12} = 6.25\,p \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

So the principal stresses are $\sigma_{1} = 12.5\,p$ and $\sigma_{2} = 6.25\,p$ (radial stress $\sigma_{3} \approx 0$ for thin wall).

**Step 2: Saint-Venant equivalent stress (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_{\text{eq}} &= \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2} \\
&= 12.5\,p - (0.28)(6.25\,p) \\
&= 12.5\,p - 1.75\,p \\
&= 10.75\,p
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the failure criterion (2 marks)**

For safety at the verge of failure: $\sigma_{\text{eq}} = \sigma_{y}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
10.75\,p &= 150 \\
p &= \frac{150}{10.75} \\
p &= 13.953 \text{ MPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion (1 mark)**

The maximum permissible internal pressure is $p_{\max} \approx 13.95$ MPa. Beyond this, the maximum principal tensile strain in the cylinder wall will exceed the yield strain of the material.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign of Poisson term:** Many students write $\sigma_{1} + \nu\sigma_{2}$ instead of $\sigma_{1} - \nu\sigma_{2}$, especially when $\sigma_{2}$ is tensile. **This inflates the equivalent stress and gives a wrong (conservative) FoS.** The correct form is always $\sigma_{1} - \nu\sigma_{2}$.
> 2. **Ordering of principal stresses:** Failing to reorder such that $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2}$ leads to picking the wrong maximum strain. The criterion uses the **largest** $\sigma$ in the formula, not the smaller one.
> 3. **Ignoring compressive crushing:** Students often check only $\varepsilon_{\max}$ (tensile) and forget to verify $\vert \varepsilon_{\min} \vert \leq \sigma_{y}/E$ for brittle compression failure. **Always check both.**
> 4. **Units mismatch:** Mixing MPa with GPa in the same expression. Convert $E$ to MPa consistently ($E = 200$ GPa $= 200{,}000$ MPa) or carry $\nu$ as dimensionless and let the units cancel.
> 5. **Pressure vessel mistake:** Confusing hoop stress $= pd/2t$ with longitudinal stress $= pd/4t$. Hoop is **always** the larger one (doubled) for an internally pressurised thin cylinder.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density KTU Revision Notes — Saint-Venant's Maximum Normal Strain Theory**

- ☐ **The theory's name and originator** — Adhémar Barré de Saint-Venant (1837); also called *Maximum Principal Strain Theory*.
- ☐ **Core postulate** — Failure when $\varepsilon_{\max} = \sigma_{y}/E$, accounting for Poisson's lateral contraction.
- ☐ **2-D formula (MUST memorize):** $\sigma_{\text{eq}} = \sigma_{1} - \nu\,\sigma_{2}$, FoS $= \sigma_{y}/\sigma_{\text{eq}}$.
- ☐ **3-D formula (MUST memorize):** $\sigma_{\text{eq}} = \sigma_{1} - \nu(\sigma_{2} + \sigma_{3})$.
- ☐ **Ordering rule:** Always reorder such that $\sigma_{1} \geq \sigma_{2} \geq \sigma_{3}$ *before* computing.
- ☐ **Biaxial check rule:** Check *both* $\varepsilon_{\max}$ (tensile) and $\vert \varepsilon_{\min} \vert$ (compressive) against $\sigma_{y}/E$.
- ☐ **Best suited for:** Brittle materials (cast iron, concrete, ceramics, composites) under biaxial stress.
- ☐ **Not suited for:** Ductile materials, pure hydrostatic stress states.
- ☐ **Sign effect of $\nu\sigma_{2}$:**
  - If $\sigma_{2}$ is **tensile** (positive), $\sigma_{\text{eq}} < \sigma_{1}$, so FoS is **higher** than Rankine's.
  - If $\sigma_{2}$ is **compressive** (negative), $\sigma_{\text{eq}} > \sigma_{1}$, so FoS is **lower** than Rankine's.
- ☐ **Sensitivity:** The theory is very sensitive to the accuracy of $\nu$; an error of 0.05 in $\nu$ can change the FoS by 10–20%.
- ☐ **Pressure vessel shortcut:** Hoop stress $= pd/2t$ (larger); longitudinal $= pd/4t$ (smaller); $\sigma_{3} \approx 0$ for thin wall.
- ☐ **Exam strategy:** Always write the formula first, then substitute, then compare with $\sigma_{y}$. Show every algebraic step. Use $\nu$ from the problem statement — never assume $\nu = 0.3$ unless told.
- ☐ **Examiner loves:** A clean derivation of the criterion from Hooke's Law (3 marks), an ordered principal-stress calculation (2 marks), and a clear final FoS statement (2 marks).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hencky-von Mises Theory for Maximum Distortion Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hencky-von Mises Theory for Maximum Distortion Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Hencky–von Mises Theory**, also called the **Maximum Distortion Energy Theory (DET)** or **Shear Strain Energy Theory**, is an elastic failure criterion used to predict the yielding of ductile materials subjected to a complex three-dimensional state of stress.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stated Principle:** A ductile material subjected to any combination of loads is said to fail (yield) when the **distortion strain energy per unit volume** at any point in the body equals the distortion strain energy per unit volume at the yield point in a simple uniaxial tension test of the same material.

**Symbolic Statement:**

$$U_d \leq \left(U_d\right)_{yield}$$

where $U_d$ is the distortion (shape-changing) component of total strain energy density.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you take a **balloon filled with water** and squeeze it from all sides. Two things happen simultaneously:

1. **Volume Change (Dilatation):** The water inside gets compressed — the balloon shrinks in overall size. This part of the strain energy is stored in changing *volume*. It does **not** cause yielding.
2. **Shape Change (Distortion):** The balloon also changes its *shape* — a sphere becomes an ellipsoid. This shape change stores energy that actually *deforms the material skeleton* and is responsible for yielding.

**Real-world insight:** Engineers have proven experimentally that ductile metals do **not** yield when only volume is changed (e.g., under pure hydrostatic pressure — a submarine can withstand enormous ocean pressure!). Failure occurs only when the *shape* tries to distort beyond a critical limit.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Maximum" in the name?** Because the theory uses the *maximum* value of distortion energy the material can withstand before plastic flow begins — this maximum is calibrated from a simple tension test.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities and Constants

- **$\sigma_y$** — **Yield stress in simple tension** (the calibration constant).
- **$E$** — Young's modulus.
- **$\nu$** — Poisson's ratio (always between 0 and 0.5).
- **$U_d$** — Distortion strain energy per unit volume (units: **N·m/m³ = J/m³**).
- **$\sigma_{VM}$** — **von Mises equivalent stress** (a scalar representation of multi-axial stress state).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Strain Energy Decomposition

For a linear elastic, isotropic material under principal stresses $\sigma_1$, $\sigma_2$, $\sigma_3$, the **total strain energy density** is decomposed as:

$$U_{total} = U_{volumetric} + U_{distortion}$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- **Step 1 — Total Strain Energy Density (U):** Sum of all energy stored due to normal and shear strain components.
- **Step 2 — Hydrostatic Decomposition:** Split stresses into a *uniform* (mean) stress and *deviatoric* (difference) stresses. The uniform part changes volume; the deviatoric part changes shape.
- **Step 3 — Identification of Failure Driver:** Only the **deviatoric (distortion)** part can cause yielding in ductile materials.
- **Step 4 — Calibration:** Set the distortion energy equal to that of a simple tension specimen at yield.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mean (Hydrostatic) Stress | $\sigma_m = \dfrac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$ | Volume-change driver |
| 2 | Deviatoric Stresses | $\sigma_1' = \sigma_1 - \sigma_m$, etc. | Shape-change driver |
| 3 | Volumetric Strain Energy | $U_v = \dfrac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}\left(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3\right)^2$ | Energy for volume change |
| 4 | Total Strain Energy Density | $U_t = \dfrac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$ | Full elastic energy |
| 5 | Distortion Strain Energy | $U_d = \dfrac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$ | **Failure-driving energy** |
| 6 | von Mises Equivalent Stress | $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\dfrac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$ | Reduced uniaxial stress |
| 7 | DET Failure Criterion | $\sigma_{VM} \leq \sigma_y$ | **Design inequality** |
| 8 | DET Alternate Form | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_y^2$ | Squared form |
| 9 | 2D Plane Stress Form | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 \leq \sigma_y^2$ | Thin plates, shafts |
| 10 | Pure Shear Form | $\sqrt{3}\,\tau \leq \sigma_y$ (or $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$) | Torsion members |

---

### 2.3 Engineering Utility

- **Pressure Vessel Design:** ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code mandates von Mises stress for ductile material design.
- **Automotive Crashworthiness:** Predicts plastic deformation in crumple zones.
- **Metal Forming (Forging, Rolling, Extrusion):** Predicts when plastic flow initiates.
- **Finite Element Analysis (FEA):** Every commercial FE solver outputs a von Mises contour map.
- **Shaft Design under Combined Bending + Torsion:** Most common KTU exam application.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Note:** The theory was independently proposed by **Richard von Mises (1913)** as a mathematical yield criterion and later given a rigorous energy interpretation by **Heinrich Hencky (1924)** — hence the joint name.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Distortion Energy Formula

#### Step 1: Start with Total Strain Energy Density

For a linear elastic isotropic material under principal stresses, the total strain energy per unit volume is:

$$U_t = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

This comes from substituting Hooke's law $\epsilon_i = \frac{1}{E}[\sigma_i - \nu(\sigma_j + \sigma_k)]$ into $U_t = \frac{1}{2}\sum \sigma_i \epsilon_i$.

---

#### Step 2: Identify the Volumetric Component

The volumetric strain energy depends only on the **mean stress** $\sigma_m$ (the average of the three principal stresses) acting equally in all three directions:

$$\sigma_m = \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3}{3}$$

For a state of pure hydrostatic stress $(\sigma_m, \sigma_m, \sigma_m)$, the strain energy density becomes:

$$U_v = \frac{3(1 - 2\nu)}{2E}\,\sigma_m^2$$

Substituting the value of $\sigma_m$ and simplifying:

$$U_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}\,(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

Expanding the square:

$$U_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}\,\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

---

#### Step 3: Subtract to Get Distortion Energy

By definition:

$$U_d = U_t - U_v$$

Computing the difference:

$$U_d = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right] - \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 + 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

Let $S = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2$ and $P = \sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1$. Then:

$$U_d = \frac{S}{2E} - \frac{\nu P}{E} - \frac{(1 - 2\nu)S}{6E} - \frac{(1 - 2\nu)P}{3E}$$

Combining the $S$ terms:

$$\frac{S}{2E} - \frac{(1 - 2\nu)S}{6E} = \frac{3S - (1 - 2\nu)S}{6E} = \frac{(2 + 2\nu)S}{6E} = \frac{(1 + \nu)S}{3E}$$

Combining the $P$ terms:

$$-\frac{\nu P}{E} - \frac{(1 - 2\nu)P}{3E} = -\frac{3\nu P + (1 - 2\nu)P}{3E} = -\frac{(1 + \nu)P}{3E}$$

Therefore:

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\,(S - P)$$

Now, recall the algebraic identity:

$$S - P = \frac{1}{2}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

Verification: $(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2(\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2) - 2(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) = 2(S - P)$. ✓

Substituting back:

$$\boxed{U_d = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]}$$

---

#### Step 4: Calibrate with Simple Tension at Yield

For a uniaxial tension test at yield: $\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$.

$$\left(U_d\right)_{yield} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_y - 0)^2 + (0 - 0)^2 + (0 - \sigma_y)^2\right]$$

$$\left(U_d\right)_{yield} = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[2\sigma_y^2\right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

---

#### Step 5: Equate and Obtain the Failure Criterion

The Hencky–von Mises theory states $U_d = (U_d)_{yield}$ at failure:

$$\frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

Cancel $\frac{1 + \nu}{E}$ from both sides and simplify:

$$\frac{1}{6}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right] = \frac{\sigma_y^2}{3}$$

Multiply both sides by 6:

$$(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2 = 2\sigma_y^2$$

Divide by 2:

$$\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2} = \sigma_y^2$$

Take the square root:

$$\boxed{\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \sigma_y}$$

---

### 3.2 Verification for Pure Shear

Under pure shear stress $\tau$, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = 0$, $\sigma_3 = -\tau$.

Substitute into the formula:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\tau - 0)^2 + (0 + \tau)^2 + (-\tau - \tau)^2}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{\tau^2 + \tau^2 + 4\tau^2}{2}} = \sqrt{3\tau^2} = \sqrt{3}\,\tau$$

Therefore the criterion becomes:

$$\sqrt{3}\,\tau \leq \sigma_y \quad \Rightarrow \quad \tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_y$$

This matches the experimentally observed **yield in shear = 0.577 × yield in tension** for ductile metals — a major validation of the theory.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — Distortion Energy Failure Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Principal Stresses sigma1, sigma2, sigma3] --> B[Compute Mean Stress sigma_m]
    B --> C[Compute Deviatoric Stresses sigma1', sigma2', sigma3']
    A --> D[Compute Total Strain Energy U_t]
    B --> E[Compute Volumetric Strain Energy U_v]
    C --> F[Compute Distortion Strain Energy U_d]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G{U_d vs U_d_yield}
    G -- Less than yield --> H[SAFE: Elastic Behavior]
    G -- Equal to yield --> I[FAILURE ONSET: Plastic Yield Begins]
    G -- Greater than yield --> J[UNSAFE: Plastic Collapse]
    K[Reference: U_d_yield from uniaxial test] --> G
    L[Compute von Mises Stress sigma_VM] --> M{sigma_VM vs sigma_y}
    M -- Less than or equal to sigma_y --> N[DESIGN IS SAFE]
    M -- Greater than sigma_y --> O[REDESIGN REQUIRED]
    F --> L
```

---

### 4.2 Graphical Representation — Yield Surface in 2D Principal Stress Space (Plane Stress)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Yield Surface: von Mises Ellipse] --> B[sigma1^2 + sigma2^2 - sigma1*sigma2 = sigma_y^2]
    A --> C[Centered at origin]
    A --> D[Touches sigma1 axis at plus sigma_y]
    A --> E[Touches sigma1 axis at minus sigma_y]
    A --> F[Pure shear point at sigma1 = plus sigma_y/sqrt3]
```

For 2D plane stress, the von Mises yield locus is an **ellipse** in the $\sigma_1$–$\sigma_2$ plane (no $\sigma_3$), given by:

$$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 = \sigma_y^2$$

- **Intercept on $\sigma_1$ axis:** $(\sigma_y, 0)$ ✓
- **Intercept on $\sigma_2$ axis:** $(0, \sigma_y)$ ✓
- **Pure shear point:** $\left(+\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}, -\dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$ — confirms $\tau_y = \sigma_y/\sqrt{3}$ ✓

---

### 4.3 Component Hierarchy of Strain Energy Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Strain Energy Density U_t] --> B[Volumetric U_v]
    A --> C[Distortion U_d]
    B --> B1[Depends on mean stress sigma_m]
    B --> B2[Pure hydrostatic compression does NOT cause yield]
    C --> C1[Depends on deviatoric stresses]
    C --> C2[DRIVES YIELD in ductile materials]
    C --> C3[Calibrated against simple tension yield]
    C1 --> D[Von Mises Criterion: U_d equals U_d_yield]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**Q1. State the Hencky–von Mises (Maximum Distortion Energy) theory of failure.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hencky–von Mises theory states that yielding of a ductile material begins when the **distortion strain energy per unit volume** at a point equals the distortion strain energy per unit volume at the yield point in a simple uniaxial tension test. Mathematically, the criterion is:

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}} \leq \sigma_y$$

The volumetric strain energy is *excluded* because hydrostatic pressure alone does not cause yielding in ductile metals. This theory gives the most accurate predictions for ductile materials and matches the experimental observation that yield in pure shear is $0.577\,\sigma_y$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. Why is the volumetric strain energy ignored in the distortion energy theory?**

**Model Answer:**

Experimental evidence shows that ductile materials can withstand very large hydrostatic (uniform) pressures without yielding — for example, deep-sea submarine hulls endure enormous ocean pressure without plastic deformation. This is because volume change alone does **not** distort the atomic lattice in a way that causes slip along slip planes. Only the **deviatoric (shape-changing)** component of strain energy, called the distortion energy, drives plastic flow. Hence, the volumetric energy is excluded from the failure criterion, and only $U_d$ is compared with its yield value. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, Module 4 Choice Pattern]**

---

#### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

A solid circular shaft of diameter **60 mm** is subjected to a **bending moment of 4 kN·m** and a **torque of 3 kN·m** simultaneously. The shaft is made of steel with yield stress $\sigma_y = 320$ MPa and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.3$.

**(a)** Derive the expression for distortion energy failure criterion in terms of principal stresses. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the von Mises criterion, determine the factor of safety for the shaft. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3** of these notes for the full derivation. Summary for answer script:

**Step 1:** Total strain energy density for triaxial stress:
$$U_t = \frac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$$

**Step 2:** Mean stress $\sigma_m = (\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)/3$. Volumetric energy:
$$U_v = \frac{1 - 2\nu}{6E}(\sigma_1 + \sigma_2 + \sigma_3)^2$$

**Step 3:** Subtract $U_v$ from $U_t$ and simplify using identity $S - P = \frac{1}{2}\sum(\sigma_i - \sigma_j)^2$:

$$U_d = \frac{1 + \nu}{6E}\left[(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2\right]$$

**Step 4:** In simple tension at yield ($\sigma_1 = \sigma_y$, $\sigma_2 = \sigma_3 = 0$):
$$(U_d)_{yield} = \frac{1 + \nu}{3E}\sigma_y^2$$

**Step 5:** Equating and simplifying yields the final criterion:

$$\boxed{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_y^2}$$

**(7 marks distributed: 2 for strain energy decomposition, 2 for algebraic simplification, 2 for calibration step, 1 for final boxed criterion)**

---

**Part (b) — Factor of Safety (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Section properties (1 mark):**
Shaft diameter $d = 60$ mm $= 0.06$ m, radius $r = 0.03$ m.

$$\sigma_b = \frac{32M}{\pi d^3} = \frac{32 \times 4000}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} = \frac{128000}{\pi \times 2.16 \times 10^{-4}} = \frac{128000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$\sigma_b = 188.62 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 188.62 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\tau = \frac{16T}{\pi d^3} = \frac{16 \times 3000}{\pi \times (0.06)^3} = \frac{48000}{6.786 \times 10^{-4}} = 70.74 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 — Principal stresses at the surface (2 marks):**

For a 2D state $(\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \tau_{xy}) = (\sigma_b, 0, \tau)$:

$$\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_b}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_b}{2}\right)^2 + \tau^2}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 94.31 \pm \sqrt{(94.31)^2 + (70.74)^2} = 94.31 \pm \sqrt{8894.4 + 5004.1} = 94.31 \pm \sqrt{13898.5}$$

$$\sigma_{1,2} = 94.31 \pm 117.89$$

$$\sigma_1 = 212.20 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 0, \quad \sigma_3 = -23.58 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 3 — von Mises equivalent stress (2 marks):**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{(212.20)^2 + (23.58)^2 + (-235.78)^2}{2}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{45028.8 + 556.0 + 55592.3}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{101177.1}{2}} = \sqrt{50588.6}$$

$$\sigma_{VM} = 224.92 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 4 — Factor of Safety (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{320}{224.92} = 1.423}$$

Since $N > 1$, the shaft is **safe** under the applied loading.

---

#### ⭐ Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Question)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]**

A steel component is subjected to biaxial stresses $\sigma_x = 120$ MPa (tensile), $\sigma_y = -60$ MPa (compressive), and $\sigma_z = 40$ MPa (tensile). The yield stress in simple tension is $\sigma_y = 250$ MPa.

**(a)** State and explain the Maximum Distortion Energy theory. Why is it preferred over the Maximum Normal Stress theory for ductile materials? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the von Mises criterion to determine whether the component will yield under the given stress state. **(7 marks)**

---

##### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Theory Statement and Justification (7 marks):**

**Statement (3 marks):** As in Part A Q1 — the theory equates the distortion strain energy per unit volume under a complex stress state with that in a simple tension test at yield. Final inequality:

$$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_y^2$$

**Why preferred over Maximum Normal Stress theory (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Maximum Normal Stress (MNS) | Maximum Distortion Energy (DET) |
|---|---|---|
| Failure assumption | Yielding controlled by **maximum principal stress alone** | Yielding controlled by **distortion energy (shape change)** |
| Accuracy for ductile materials | Poor — ignores interaction between stresses | Excellent — matches experiments within 5–10% |
| Pure shear prediction | Predicts $\tau_y = \sigma_y$ (wrong) | Predicts $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ ✓ |
| Hydrostatic pressure | Fails (predicts yielding under high pressure) | Correct (no yielding under pure hydrostatic load) |
| KTU Recommendation | Suitable for **brittle** materials | **Standard for ductile materials** |

---

**Part (b) — Application of von Mises Criterion (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Since the shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ is not given and is assumed zero, the given normal stresses **are themselves the principal stresses** (provided shear components are zero). With $\sigma_x, \sigma_y, \sigma_z$ along mutually perpendicular axes and no shear:

$$\sigma_1 = 120 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_2 = 40 \text{ MPa}, \quad \sigma_3 = -60 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2 (3 marks):** Compute the left-hand side:

$$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 = 120^2 + 40^2 + (-60)^2 = 14400 + 1600 + 3600 = 19600 \text{ MPa}^2$$

$$\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1 = (120)(40) + (40)(-60) + (-60)(120) = 4800 - 2400 - 7200 = -4800 \text{ MPa}^2$$

$$\text{LHS} = 19600 - (-4800) = 24400 \text{ MPa}^2$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the right-hand side:

$$\sigma_y^2 = (250)^2 = 62500 \text{ MPa}^2$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Compare:

$$24400 \leq 62500 \quad \checkmark$$

Since the condition is satisfied with margin, the component will **NOT yield**.

**Factor of safety:**

$$\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{24400} = 156.20 \text{ MPa}$$

$$N = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sigma_{VM}} = \frac{250}{156.20} = 1.60$$

The component is safe with a factor of safety of **1.60**.

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2 inside the square root** — students often write $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2}$ without dividing by 2. This gives an answer that is exactly $\sqrt{2}$ times too high and an FoS that is $\sqrt{2}$ times too low. **(Lose 2 marks)**
> 2. **Sign errors in principal stress calculation** — when using $\sigma_{1,2} = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\cdots}$, students often confuse which is $\sigma_1$ (largest positive) and which is $\sigma_3$ (most negative). Always sort them as $\sigma_1 \geq \sigma_2 \geq \sigma_3$. **(Lose 1 mark)**
> 3. **Forgetting to sort principal stresses** — the von Mises formula requires them sorted. Plugging unsorted values gives wrong $\sigma_{VM}$.
> 4. **Not stating "elastic" or "ductile" assumption explicitly** — always write "Assuming the material is ductile and follows Hooke's law up to yield…"
> 5. **In derivation questions, students skip the volumetric energy term entirely** — this loses 2 full marks. Always derive both $U_t$ and $U_v$ separately.
> 6. **Confusing $\sigma_y$ (yield stress) with $\sigma_1$ (principal stress)** — use different notation. KTU examiners deduct marks for symbol confusion.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **Hencky–von Mises Theory** is based on **distortion strain energy** only — **volumetric energy is excluded**.
- ✅ Formula in principal stresses: $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\frac{(\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^2}{2}}$
- ✅ Failure criterion: **$\sigma_{VM} \leq \sigma_y$** for safety; equality indicates yielding onset.
- ✅ For pure shear: $\tau_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}} \approx 0.577\,\sigma_y$ — this is the **theoretical verification**.
- ✅ **Most accurate** failure theory for **ductile** materials (steel, aluminum, copper).
- ✅ **NOT recommended for brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete) — use Rankine or modified Mohr theory instead.
- ✅ **Same as Tresca for pure shear?** No — Tresca predicts $\tau_y = 0.5\,\sigma_y$, von Mises predicts $\tau_y \approx 0.577\,\sigma_y$. Von Mises is **less conservative** but more accurate.
- ✅ **2D plane stress simplification:** $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 = \sigma_y^2$ (used in shaft design with $\sigma_x = \sigma_b$, $\sigma_y = 0$).
- ✅ **Combined bending + torsion formula (KTU favourite):** $\sigma_{VM} = \sqrt{\sigma_b^2 + 3\tau^2}$
- ✅ Derivation order: $U_t \rightarrow U_v \rightarrow U_d = U_t - U_v \rightarrow$ calibrate with uniaxial yield $\rightarrow$ final inequality.
- ✅ **Identity used:** $(\sigma_1-\sigma_2)^2 + (\sigma_2-\sigma_3)^2 + (\sigma_3-\sigma_1)^2 = 2(\sigma_1^2+\sigma_2^2+\sigma_3^2 - \sigma_1\sigma_2 - \sigma_2\sigma_3 - \sigma_3\sigma_1)$. Memorize this!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Haigh's Theory for Maximum Strain Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCMRT303 - MECHANICS OF SOLIDS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Haigh's Theory for Maximum Strain Energy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) **Haigh's Theory of Maximum Strain Energy** is a failure hypothesis for engineering materials under complex states of stress. It states that: > *"Failure of a material subjected to a combined state of stress occurs when the total strain energy absorbed per unit volume in the stressed condition equals the strain energy absorbed per unit volume at the elastic limit in a simple uniaxial tensile test."* The theory was proposed by **B. P. Haigh** in **1918–1925** as a logical and thermodynamically consistent refinement of Saint-Venant's Maximum Principal Strain Theory. The principal contribution of Haigh was the recognition that **hydrostatic (volumetric) stress components** — although they store elastic energy — do not, by themselves, cause yielding or fracture in ductile materials. Therefore, the total strain energy must be considered holistically, but interpreted against the energy storage capacity of a material under pure tension. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Haigh's theory belongs to the *Energy-based Failure Theories* family and is treated as an evolution over Saint-Venant's strain-based theory. The total strain energy in a triaxial stress state involves **all three principal stresses** coupled through Poisson's ratio (ν). > [!NOTE] > **Historical Context:** Haigh's theory preceded and conceptually motivated the modern **Distortion Energy Theory (von Mises – Hencky, 1924)**, which mathematically isolates the distortional (shape-changing) part of the strain energy. Haigh's original theory considers *total* strain energy, which is why it slightly over-predicts failure load for states involving large hydrostatic stresses. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Water-Balloon Under Squeeze Imagine a soft, filled water balloon pressed from multiple directions in a steel box: - **Volume energy (Dilatational):** The water gets compressed but stays liquid — nothing "breaks." This is the energy stored due to uniform squeezing (hydrostatic stress). - **Shape energy (Distortional):** The balloon *bulges* sideways into whatever free space remains. That shape change — the *shear* — is what would tear the material. Haigh's Theory says: the balloon fails when the **total stored energy (compression + shear)** equals the energy the balloon's skin can absorb in a simple pull-test before tearing. In contrast, von Mises' theory (its successor) only counts the *bulging* (distortional) energy. This intuitive distinction is crucial: **Haigh uses total energy**, while **von Mises uses only shape-changing energy** — making von Mises slightly more accurate for ductile metals under high hydrostatic pressure. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Used in KTU Problems) | Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value (Steel) | |:---:|:---|:---:| | **E** | Young's Modulus of Elasticity | **2 × 10⁵ N/mm² = 200 GPa** | | **ν** | Poisson's Ratio | **0.30** (steel) | | **σ_yt** | Yield Stress in Simple Tension | **250 N/mm²** (mild steel) | | **σ_uc** | Ultimate Compressive Stress | Problem-specific | | **σ₁, σ₂, σ₃** | Principal Stresses (σ₁ ≥ σ₂ ≥ σ₃) | — | | **U** | Strain Energy per unit volume | **N·m/m³ = N/m² = Pa** | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — The Three Constituent Stresses Under a triaxial principal stress state **(σ₁, σ₂, σ₃)**, the strain energy density (energy stored per unit volume) in a linearly elastic material is obtained by superposition of three uniaxial stress cases. Each principal stress produces: 1. A direct longitudinal strain $\varepsilon_i = \dfrac{\sigma_i}{E}$ 2. Two lateral strains of magnitude $\dfrac{\nu \sigma_i}{E}$ in the orthogonal directions (due to Poisson's effect) The **total strain energy per unit volume** is the work done by all three normal stresses acting through their respective displacements. After applying the standard energy formula $U = \frac{1}{2} \sigma \varepsilon$ for each principal direction and summing: $$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1 \right) \right]$$ ### 2.2 The Failure Criterion — Energy Capacity Limit In a **simple tensile test** (only σ₁ = σ_yt, σ₂ = σ₃ = 0), the maximum elastic strain energy density is: $$U_{\max} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{2E}$$ According to Haigh's hypothesis, the material remains safe as long as the stored strain energy does **not exceed** this critical value: $$U \leq U_{\max}$$ Substituting and cancelling the common factor $1/(2E)$ from both sides: $$\boxed{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1 \right) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why the term $-2\nu(\sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1)$?** Because when two normal stresses act simultaneously, the lateral strain produced by one is constrained by the other. The lateral strain energy is therefore *subtracted* from the longitudinal strain energy. This is the mathematical signature of Poisson coupling, and is the reason Haigh's formula is **always smaller** than the naive sum of squares $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2$. ### 2.3 Special Case — Biaxial Stress State (σ₃ = 0) This is the **most-asked KTU case** (thin pressure vessels, shafts under combined loading). Setting σ₃ = 0: $$\boxed{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Examination Tip:** When both σ₁ and σ₂ are **tensile (positive)**, the cross term $2\nu\sigma_1\sigma_2$ *reduces* the left-hand side, making the material appear *safer* (more energy is needed to fail). When one is tensile and the other compressive (signs differ), the cross term becomes *negative*, and the left-hand side grows — failure is reached more easily. This is physically reasonable: tension-compression creates high shear through the Mohr's circle. ### 2.4 Special Case — Uniaxial Tension (σ₂ = σ₃ = 0) $$\sigma_1^2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{yt}$$ This is the **calibration condition** — the theory correctly predicts the simple tensile yield strength. This self-consistency is a vital check in any KTU proof. ### 2.5 Geometric Interpretation — The Haigh's Ellipse (2D, σ₃ = 0) In the σ₁–σ₂ plane, the failure curve is an **ellipse centered at the origin** with its axes rotated by 45°. The equation can be rewritten in rotated coordinates as: $$\left( \frac{\sigma_1 + \sigma_2}{1+\nu} \right)^2 + \left( \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{1-\nu} \right)^2 \cdot (1-\nu^2) = \sigma_{yt}^2$$ - **Axis along σ₁ = σ₂ (hydrostatic direction):** Semi-axis = $(1+\nu)\sigma_{yt}$ — very large; hydrostatic stress is "forgiven." - **Axis along σ₁ = −σ₂ (pure shear direction):** Semi-axis = $\sqrt{(1-\nu)/(1+\nu)} \cdot \sigma_{yt}$ — smaller; shear is dangerous. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | When to Use | |:---:|:---|:---|:---| | 1 | $U = \dfrac{1}{2E}\left[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)\right]$ | Total strain energy density (3D) | Triaxial stress state | | 2 | $U_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{2E}$ | Critical strain energy at elastic limit | Calibration condition | | 3 | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$ | **Haigh's failure criterion (3D)** | General 3D problem | | 4 | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu\sigma_1\sigma_2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$ | **Haigh's failure criterion (2D)** | Biaxial (σ₃ = 0) | | 5 | $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{yt}$ | Special case (uniaxial) | Verification check | | 6 | $\tau_{\max} = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{1+\nu}$ | Pure shear failure (from 2D) | Torsion problems | | 7 | $FOS = \dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_{1,actual}}$ (for uniaxial); $FOS = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{LHS}}$ (for combined) | Factor of Safety | Design problems | | 8 | $\nu = 0.30$ (steel), $\nu = 0.33$ (cast iron), $\nu = 0.20$ (concrete) | Standard Poisson's ratios | All KTU problems | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Note on $\tau_{\max}$:** For pure shear, σ₁ = τ, σ₂ = −τ. Substituting into Formula 4: $\tau^2 + \tau^2 - 2\nu(-\tau^2) = 2\tau^2(1+\nu) = \sigma_{yt}^2$ → $\tau_{\max} = \sigma_{yt} / \sqrt{2(1+\nu)}$. For ν = 0.30, this gives $\tau_{\max} \approx 0.620\,\sigma_{yt}$, which is higher than Tresca's $0.50\,\sigma_{yt}$, confirming Haigh's theory is more lenient under pure shear. ### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility | Field | Application | |:---|:---| | **Pressure Vessel Design** | Combined hoop + longitudinal stress (2D Haigh) | | **Shaft Design** | Combined bending + torsion (using von Mises, the modern successor) | | **Dam/Concrete Structures** | Triaxial confinement analysis | | **Aerospace Components** | Multiaxial fatigue analysis | | **Automotive Crash Analysis** | High hydrostatic stress states | | **Geotechnical Engineering** | Soil & rock under triaxial loading | > [!TIP] > **Practical Recommendation:** In current industrial practice, **Haigh's original theory is largely superseded by von Mises' Distortion Energy Theory** (a refinement), because experimental data for ductile metals aligns more closely with the *distortional* part alone. However, for **brittle materials** and certain composite applications, **Haigh's total energy approach** still has merit. KTU examiners expect students to know the historical lineage: Saint-Venant → Haigh → Hencky/von Mises. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Total Strain Energy Density $U$ in a Triaxial Principal Stress State **Goal:** Starting from first principles, derive the strain energy per unit volume for a material element under principal stresses σ₁, σ₂, σ₃. #### Step 1: Define the strain in each principal direction For a linearly elastic, homogeneous, isotropic material, applying Hooke's Law in three orthogonal directions. Consider an elemental cube with sides dx, dy, dz. The principal stresses σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ act normally on the three faces. By the principle of superposition: - The strain along the σ₁ direction: $\varepsilon_1 = \dfrac{\sigma_1}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_2}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_3}{E}$ - The strain along the σ₂ direction: $\varepsilon_2 = -\dfrac{\nu \sigma_1}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_2}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_3}{E}$ - The strain along the σ₃ direction: $\varepsilon_3 = -\dfrac{\nu \sigma_1}{E} - \dfrac{\nu \sigma_2}{E} + \dfrac{\sigma_3}{E}$ > **Logic:** Each direct stress causes a direct strain (σᵢ/E). Each orthogonal stress causes a *lateral* Poisson strain $(-\nu\sigma_j/E)$ in the iᵗʰ direction. #### Step 2: Apply the strain energy formula for a uniaxial case and sum The strain energy per unit volume for a single normal stress σ is $U = \frac{1}{2}\sigma\varepsilon$. For three principal stresses acting on an elemental cube, the total strain energy is the sum of the work done by each stress through its corresponding displacement. The total strain energy density is: $$U = \frac{1}{2} \left( \sigma_1 \varepsilon_1 + \sigma_2 \varepsilon_2 + \sigma_3 \varepsilon_3 \right)$$ #### Step 3: Substitute the strain expressions $$U = \frac{1}{2} \left[ \sigma_1 \left( \frac{\sigma_1}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_2}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_3}{E} \right) + \sigma_2 \left( -\frac{\nu \sigma_1}{E} + \frac{\sigma_2}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_3}{E} \right) + \sigma_3 \left( -\frac{\nu \sigma_1}{E} - \frac{\nu \sigma_2}{E} + \frac{\sigma_3}{E} \right) \right]$$ #### Step 4: Expand and collect like terms $$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 - \nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 - \nu \sigma_1 \sigma_3 - \nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2^2 - \nu \sigma_2 \sigma_3 - \nu \sigma_1 \sigma_3 - \nu \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3^2 \right]$$ Grouping the squared terms and the cross terms: $$U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 - 2\nu \sigma_2 \sigma_3 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_3 \right]$$ #### Step 5: Final compact form $$\boxed{U = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1 \right) \right]}$$ This is the **fundamental Haigh strain energy density** expression. Every KTU problem in this module starts here. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of the Failure Criterion $U \leq U_{\max}$ **Step 1:** In a simple uniaxial tensile test at the elastic limit, σ₁ = σ_yt and σ₂ = σ₃ = 0. Substituting into the energy expression: $$U_{\max} = \frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_{yt}^2 + 0 + 0 - 2\nu(0) \right] = \frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{2E}$$ **Step 2:** Equating the general $U$ to the critical $U_{\max}$ for the onset of failure: $$\frac{1}{2E} \left[ \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1 \right) \right] = \frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{2E}$$ **Step 3:** Multiply both sides by $2E$ and rearrange into the safety condition: $$\boxed{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + \sigma_2 \sigma_3 + \sigma_3 \sigma_1 \right) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Direction of inequality:** The "less than or equal to" sign is critical. The left side represents the actual strain energy density of the stressed state, while the right side is the maximum the material can absorb elastically. The material is safe when the actual is *less* than the maximum. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Biaxial Special Case (σ₃ = 0) **Step 1:** In many engineering components (thin-walled pressure vessels, shafts in plane stress), the third principal stress is negligible. Substitute σ₃ = 0 into the criterion: $$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + 0 - 2\nu \left( \sigma_1 \sigma_2 + 0 + 0 \right) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$$ **Step 2:** Simplify: $$\boxed{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2}$$ This is the **most-frequently tested form** in KTU Module 4 questions. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Pressure Vessel (Biaxial Case) > **Problem (KTU-style):** A thin cylindrical pressure vessel has a hoop stress σ_h = 80 N/mm² (tensile) and a longitudinal stress σ_L = 40 N/mm² (tensile). The material is mild steel with σ_yt = 250 N/mm² and ν = 0.30. Check safety using Haigh's theory and find the Factor of Safety. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Identify the principal stresses. Since the vessel surface is in plane stress with no shear: $$\sigma_1 = 80 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = 40 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$ **Step 2:** Compute the left-hand side (LHS) of the Haigh's criterion: $$LHS = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$ $$LHS = (80)^2 + (40)^2 - 2(0.30)(80)(40)$$ $$LHS = 6400 + 1600 - 1920$$ $$LHS = 6080 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 3:** Compute the right-hand side (RHS): $$RHS = \sigma_{yt}^2 = (250)^2 = 62500 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 4:** Compare: $$6080 < 62500 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{SAFE} \quad \checkmark$$ **Step 5:** Factor of Safety: $$FOS = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2}} = \sqrt{\frac{62500}{6080}} = \sqrt{10.28} \approx 3.21$$ > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Convention:** Always report the FOS to 2 decimal places and explicitly state whether the design is safe (FOS > 1) or unsafe (FOS < 1). --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Haigh's Theory vs. Other Theories (Same Vessel) For the same pressure vessel, here is how the **factor of safety** compares across the four classical theories (computed below for completeness): | Theory | Formula for FOS | Numerical FOS | |:---|:---|:---:| | **Maximum Principal Stress (Rankine)** | $\sigma_{yt}/\sigma_1$ | $250/80 = 3.125$ | | **Maximum Principal Strain (Saint-Venant)** | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \nu \sigma_2}$ | $250 / (80 - 12) = 3.68$ | | **Maximum Shear Stress (Tresca)** | $\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}$ | $250 / 40 = 6.25$ | | **Maximum Strain Energy (Haigh)** | $\sqrt{\dfrac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2}}$ | $\sqrt{62500/6080} = 3.21$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** Tresca's theory is the *most conservative* (lowest FOS in this case is *not* Tresca — note the formula is different). For **tensile-tensile** states, Haigh's FOS lies between Rankine and Saint-Venant. The true experimental FOS for mild steel under this loading is ≈ 3.0, validating Haigh's predictive accuracy for ductile materials in mild biaxial tension. --- ### 3.6 Verification of the Uniaxial Calibration Condition Set σ₂ = σ₃ = 0 in the 3D criterion: $$\sigma_1^2 + 0 + 0 - 2\nu(0 + 0 + 0) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{yt}$$ This reproduces the simple tensile yield criterion, confirming the theory is **self-consistent**. KTU examiners often ask: *"Show that Haigh's theory reduces to the uniaxial result."* This is the proof. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Conceptual Flow — The Haigh's Theory Logic Chain The following block diagram maps the **analytical workflow** a student must follow when applying Haigh's theory to a KTU problem. From the raw stress state to the final safety verdict, every intermediate step is critical. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Given Stresses] --> B{Identify Stress State} B --> C[3D State
sigma1, sigma2, sigma3 all non-zero] B --> D[2D Biaxial State
sigma3 = 0] B --> E[Uniaxial State
sigma2 = sigma3 = 0] C --> F[Compute Total Strain Energy
U = 1/2E times bracket] D --> F2[Compute Biaxial Energy
sigma1 squared + sigma2 squared - 2 nu sigma1 sigma2] E --> F3[Direct Check
sigma1 versus sigma_yt] F --> G[Compare U with U_max
U_max = sigma_yt squared over 2E] F2 --> H[Compare LHS with sigma_yt squared] F3 --> I[sigma1 less than equal sigma_yt] G --> J{Is LHS less than equal RHS} H --> J I --> J J --> K[YES: SAFE Design
Compute FOS] J --> L[NO: UNSAFE Design
Failure Predicted] K --> M[Report Factor of Safety
FOS equals sigma_yt over sigma equivalent] L --> N[Suggest Design Modification
Reduce load or change geometry] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style M fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style L fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style N fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style J fill:#f9a825,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** Every KTU problem on Haigh's theory funnels into the **comparison step** (yellow diamond). Whether the path enters through 3D, 2D, or 1D, the final decision criterion is the same: does the LHS exceed the RHS? Students should internalize this binary decision structure. --- ### 4.2 Architecture of the Strain Energy Components — Conceptual Block The total strain energy can be conceptually decomposed into the **dilatational (volumetric)** part and the **distortional (shape-changing)** part. The figure below shows how **Haigh's theory** relates to the more refined **von Mises theory** through this decomposition. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Total Strain Energy U
Haigh's full expression] --> B[Dilatational Energy U_v
Volume change only] A --> C[Distortional Energy U_d
Shape change only] B --> D[U_v = 3 sigma_h squared over 2E
where sigma_h is hydrostatic] C --> E[U_d = 1 plus nu over 3E times
sum of squared deviatoric stresses] D --> F[Haigh counts this
Conservative inclusion] E --> G[Von Mises counts ONLY this
More accurate for ductile metals] F --> H[Failure if U greater than U_max
Haigh's Criterion] G --> I[Failure if U_d greater than U_d_max
Von Mises Criterion] H --> J[Superior for brittle materials
Under triaxial confinement] I --> K[Superior for ductile materials
Standard industrial use] style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style G fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the Diagram:** The key takeaway is that **Haigh's theory is the parent** of the modern distortion energy theory. By accepting the entire energy (including the harmless hydrostatic part), Haigh's theory is **slightly more conservative** for ductile materials under uniform pressure. For **brittle materials** (cast iron, concrete), however, the dilatational energy *does* contribute to crack propagation, making Haigh's approach more appropriate. --- ### 4.3 Failure Envelope Geometry in the Sigma1-Sigma2 Plane The following schematic depicts the **Haigh's ellipse** in the σ₁–σ₂ plane (with σ₃ = 0), compared with the Tresca hexagon. This is the most common KTU diagram request. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Failure Envelope Comparison
Sigma1 on X-axis, Sigma2 on Y-axis] --> B[Haigh Ellipse
Smooth closed curve] A --> C[Tresca Hexagon
Six straight edges] B --> D[Passes through
sigma_yt, 0] B --> E[Passes through
0, sigma_yt] B --> F[Passes through
-sigma_yt, 0] B --> G[Passes through
0, -sigma_yt] B --> H[Inside the ellipse: SAFE region] B --> I[Outside the ellipse: FAILURE region] C --> J[Six vertices at
plus/minus sigma_yt, 0 and 0, plus/minus sigma_yt
and plus/minus sigma_yt/2, plus/minus sigma_yt/2] C --> K[Inscribed inside Haigh ellipse
in quadrants 1 and 3] C --> L[Outside Haigh ellipse
in quadrants 2 and 4] H --> M[Combined with Tresca, von Mises
For full failure envelope picture] I --> M style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style H fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff style I fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Key Insight:** In quadrants 1 and 3 (tension-tension or compression-compression), Haigh's ellipse **encloses** Tresca's hexagon — meaning Haigh predicts a *larger* safe region and is *less conservative* than Tresca. In quadrants 2 and 4 (mixed tension-compression), the relationship reverses. This explains why the two theories give **different** FOS values for different stress combinations. --- ### 4.4 KTU Module 4 — Failure Theories Hierarchy (Position of Haigh) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Classical Failure Theories
Module 4] --> B[Stress-Based Theories] A --> C[Strain-Based Theories] A --> D[Energy-Based Theories] B --> B1[Max Principal Stress
Rankine 1858] B --> B2[Max Shear Stress
Tresca 1864] C --> C1[Max Principal Strain
Saint-Venant 1837] D --> D1[Max Total Strain Energy
Haigh 1918-1925] D --> D2[Max Distortion Energy
Von Mises and Hencky 1924] B1 --> E[KTU Most Tested] B2 --> E C1 --> E D1 --> E D2 --> E style A fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#000,color:#fff style B fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style C fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D fill:#5d4037,stroke:#000,color:#fff style D1 fill:#c62828,stroke:#000,color:#fff style E fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` > **Reading the Hierarchy:** Haigh's theory (highlighted in red) is the **bridge** between strain-based and modern energy-based failure theories. KTU frequently asks comparative questions: "How does Haigh's theory differ from Saint-Venant's?" or "Why was Haigh's theory refined into von Mises'?" ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State Haigh's theory of maximum strain energy. Write the failure criterion in terms of principal stresses.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Haigh's Theory of Maximum Strain Energy (1.5 marks):** > Haigh's theory states that failure of a material under a combined state of stress occurs when the total strain energy per unit volume in the stressed state equals the strain energy per unit volume at the elastic limit in a simple uniaxial tensile test. > > **Failure Criterion (1.5 marks):** > $$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$$ > > where σ₁, σ₂, σ₃ are the principal stresses, ν is Poisson's ratio, and σ_yt is the yield stress in simple tension. The material is safe when the LHS is less than or equal to the RHS. --- #### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Distinguish between Saint-Venant's maximum principal strain theory and Haigh's maximum strain energy theory.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Saint-Venant's Theory | Haigh's Theory | |:---|:---|:---| | **Basis** | Failure when max principal strain reaches yield strain in uniaxial test | Failure when total strain energy reaches elastic limit energy in uniaxial test | | **Criterion** | $\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \leq \sigma_{yt}$ | $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$ | | **Inclusion of Lateral Stresses** | Only through linear coupling | Through squared and cross-product terms | | **Physical Basis** | Strain (geometric) | Energy (thermodynamic) | | **Accuracy for Hydrostatic Stress** | Over-predicts failure under equal triaxial tension | Still includes dilatational energy (slightly conservative) | > **Key Distinction (1 mark):** Haigh's theory is a **thermodynamically consistent refinement** of Saint-Venant's theory. Both share the same calibration point (uniaxial tension), but Haigh's accounts for the *energy* absorbed in lateral directions, which Saint-Venant's neglects in its energy interpretation. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern (ESE):** Each Module question in Part B carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. The full 14 marks require a complete derivation + numerical computation + verdict. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted]` **A machine component is subjected to a biaxial stress state with σ₁ = 120 N/mm² and σ₂ = −60 N/mm² (tensile and compressive). The material is mild steel with yield stress σ_yt = 320 N/mm² and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.30. Determine the Factor of Safety using:** **(a)** **Haigh's maximum strain energy theory, and** **(b)** **Compare your result with Tresca's maximum shear stress theory. Comment on which theory is more appropriate for this loading.** **Complete Model Solution:** ##### Part (a) — Haigh's Theory (7 marks) **Given:** σ₁ = 120 N/mm², σ₂ = −60 N/mm², σ₃ = 0 (plane stress), σ_yt = 320 N/mm², ν = 0.30. **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the Haigh's criterion for 2D biaxial state: $$\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Compute the LHS: $$LHS = (120)^2 + (-60)^2 - 2(0.30)(120)(-60)$$ $$LHS = 14400 + 3600 - (-4320)$$ $$LHS = 14400 + 3600 + 4320 = 22320 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the RHS: $$RHS = (320)^2 = 102400 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Since $LHS = 22320 < RHS = 102400$, the component is **SAFE**. **Step 5 (2 marks):** Compute the Factor of Safety: $$FOS_{Haigh} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu\sigma_1\sigma_2}}$$ $$FOS_{Haigh} = \sqrt{\frac{102400}{22320}} = \sqrt{4.588} \approx 2.142$$ ##### Part (b) — Tresca Comparison (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write Tresca's criterion: $$FOS_{Tresca} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}}{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum shear stress: $$\tau_{max} = \frac{\sigma_1 - \sigma_2}{2} = \frac{120 - (-60)}{2} = 90 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute the FOS: $$FOS_{Tresca} = \frac{320}{120 - (-60)} = \frac{320}{180} \approx 1.778$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparison table: | Theory | FOS | |:---|:---:| | Haigh (Total Strain Energy) | **2.14** | | Tresca (Max Shear Stress) | **1.78** | **Step 5 (3 marks):** **Comment on Appropriateness:** > For this **tension-compression** stress state (quadrant 2 in the σ₁–σ₂ plane), the two theories diverge significantly. Tresca's theory gives a lower FOS (1.78) and is more conservative. Haigh's theory gives a higher FOS (2.14), which is consistent with the **smaller cross-term contribution** when stresses have opposite signs. > > **For ductile mild steel** under combined tension-compression, **Tresca is more appropriate** because it correctly penalizes the high shear produced by Mohr's circle (which extends from −60 to 120). The actual experimental yield for steel under this loading typically falls closer to Tresca's prediction. > > **Haigh's theory is suitable for brittle materials** or when the energy absorption capacity governs failure (e.g., high-cycle fatigue). For static ductile failure in tension-compression, **Tresca is recommended** in design practice. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign Convention Trap (–2 marks):** Forgetting that σ₂ = −60 (compressive) and using +60 in the cross term. This gives a wrong LHS of 18,000 instead of 22,320, leading to a *higher* FOS of 2.38 — a serious sign error. > 2. **Forgetting to square FOS formula (–1 mark):** The factor of safety in Haigh's theory involves a **square root**, not a direct ratio. Many students write FOS = σ_yt / (something), which is the Tresca form. Always remember: FOS_Haigh = √(σ_yt² / LHS). > 3. **Wrong Formula Selection (–2 marks):** Using 3D formula when σ₃ = 0 is given, or vice-versa. Always identify the stress state first. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]` **A solid circular shaft of diameter 60 mm is subjected to an axial tensile load of 50 kN combined with a torque of 2 kN·m. The shaft material has yield stress σ_yt = 280 N/mm² and Poisson's ratio ν = 0.30.** **(a)** **Determine the principal stresses and check the safety of the shaft using Haigh's maximum strain energy theory.** **(b)** **What is the maximum permissible torque if a Factor of Safety of 2.5 is required using the same theory?** **Complete Model Solution:** ##### Part (a) — Principal Stresses and Safety Check (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute cross-sectional area and section modulus: $$A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (60)^2}{4} = 2827.4 \text{ mm}^2$$ $$Z_p = \frac{\pi d^3}{16} = \frac{\pi (60)^3}{16} = 42411.5 \text{ mm}^3$$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute axial (tensile) stress: $$\sigma_x = \frac{P}{A} = \frac{50 \times 10^3}{2827.4} = 17.68 \text{ N/mm}^2 \text{ (tensile)}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute shear stress due to torque: $$\tau = \frac{T}{Z_p} = \frac{2 \times 10^6}{42411.5} = 47.16 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Compute principal stresses using Mohr's circle: For 2D stress with σ_y = 0, σ_x = 17.68, τ_xy = 47.16: $$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{\sigma_x + \sigma_y}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{\sigma_x - \sigma_y}{2}\right)^2 + \tau_{xy}^2}$$ $$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = \frac{17.68}{2} \pm \sqrt{\left(\frac{17.68}{2}\right)^2 + (47.16)^2}$$ $$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = 8.84 \pm \sqrt{78.15 + 2224.07} = 8.84 \pm \sqrt{2302.22}$$ $$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = 8.84 \pm 47.98$$ $$\boxed{\sigma_1 = 56.82 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_2 = -39.14 \text{ N/mm}^2, \quad \sigma_3 = 0}$$ **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Apply Haigh's criterion: $$LHS = \sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu \sigma_1 \sigma_2$$ $$LHS = (56.82)^2 + (-39.14)^2 - 2(0.30)(56.82)(-39.14)$$ $$LHS = 3228.51 + 1531.94 + 1334.74 = 6095.19 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Compare with RHS: $$RHS = \sigma_{yt}^2 = (280)^2 = 78400 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ $$LHS = 6095 < 78400 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{SAFE} \quad \checkmark$$ ##### Part (b) — Maximum Permissible Torque (7 marks) **Step 1 (1 mark):** Set up the required FOS condition: $$FOS = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{LHS}} = 2.5$$ $$\Rightarrow \frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{LHS} = 6.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad LHS_{max} = \frac{\sigma_{yt}^2}{6.25} = \frac{78400}{6.25} = 12544 \text{ (N/mm}^2)^2$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Express LHS in terms of unknown torque T. With σ_x fixed at 17.68 and τ = T/Z_p as variable: $$\sigma_1, \sigma_2 = 8.84 \pm \sqrt{8.84^2 + \tau^2}$$ Let $R = \sqrt{8.84^2 + \tau^2}$.
