Then σ₁ = 8.84 + R, σ₂ = 8.84 − R. **Step 3 (2 marks):** Compute the LHS symbolically: $$LHS = (8.84 + R)^2 + (8.84 - R)^2 - 2(0.30)(8.84 + R)(8.84 - R)$$ $$LHS = 2(8.84)^2 + 2R^2 - 2(0.30)\left[(8.84)^2 - R^2\right]$$ $$LHS = 2(78.15) + 2R^2 - 0.60(78.15) + 0.60 R^2$$ $$LHS = 156.30 + 2R^2 - 46.89 + 0.60 R^2$$ $$LHS = 109.41 + 2.60 R^2$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** Set equal to LHS_max and solve for R²: $$109.41 + 2.60 R^2 = 12544$$ $$2.60 R^2 = 12434.59$$ $$R^2 = 4782.53 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R = 69.16 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute τ from $R^2 = 8.84^2 + \tau^2$: $$\tau^2 = R^2 - 8.84^2 = 4782.53 - 78.15 = 4704.38$$ $$\tau = 68.59 \text{ N/mm}^2$$ **Step 6 (1 mark):** Compute the maximum torque: $$T_{max} = \tau \cdot Z_p = 68.59 \times 42411.5 = 2.909 \times 10^6 \text{ N·mm}$$ $$\boxed{T_{max} \approx 2.91 \text{ kN·m}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Forgetting to use Z_p for torsion (–1 mark):** The polar section modulus is $Z_p = \pi d^3/16$, not $\pi d^3/32$ (which is for bending). Mixing these up gives a torque that is off by a factor of 2. > 2. **Sign error in σ₂ (–1 mark):** σ₂ comes out negative (compressive); the student must keep the sign in subsequent LHS calculations. > 3. **Wrong formula for FOS (–2 marks):** Remember FOS = √(σ_yt² / LHS), **not** σ_yt / (something). Many students confuse this with Tresca. > 4. **Algebraic slip in symbolic LHS expansion (–1 mark):** The expansion $2.60 R^2$ is the most error-prone step. Verify by plugging R = 0 (pure axial): LHS should equal $2(17.68)^2 = 625.4$ — check if your formula gives that. With our formula: 109.41 + 0 = 109.41. **Wait, this is wrong.** Let me correct: at R = 0, σ₁ = σ₂ = 8.84, so LHS = $(8.84)^2 + (8.84)^2 - 2(0.30)(8.84)(8.84) = 78.15(1 + 1 - 0.6) = 78.15 \times 1.4 = 109.41$. Yes, 109.41 is correct! Good. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist for Haigh's Theory:** - [x] **Definition:** Failure occurs when *total* strain energy density equals the elastic-limit strain energy density of a uniaxial tensile specimen. - [x] **Key Formula (3D):** $U = \frac{1}{2E}[\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1)]$ - [x] **Failure Criterion (3D):** $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 + \sigma_3^2 - 2\nu(\sigma_1\sigma_2 + \sigma_2\sigma_3 + \sigma_3\sigma_1) \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$ - [x] **Biaxial (σ₃ = 0):** $\sigma_1^2 + \sigma_2^2 - 2\nu\sigma_1\sigma_2 \leq \sigma_{yt}^2$ — **the most-tested form** - [x] **Uniaxial Check:** Reduces to $\sigma_1 \leq \sigma_{yt}$ — verify in every solution. - [x] **Pure Shear Limit:** $\tau_{max} = \sigma_{yt}/\sqrt{2(1+\nu)}$ — for ν = 0.3, ≈ 0.620 σ_yt. - [x] **FOS Formula:** $FOS = \sqrt{\sigma_{yt}^2 / LHS}$ — note the **square root** (most common slip-up). - [x] **Sign of Cross-Term:** Same-sign σ₁, σ₂ reduce LHS (safer); opposite-sign increase LHS (riskier). - [x] **Standard Values:** ν = 0.30 (steel), σ_yt = 250 N/mm² (mild steel, general problem). - [x] **Famous Quote for Viva:** *"Haigh's theory is the parent of von Mises' distortion energy theory — both born from the recognition that hydrostatic stress alone does not cause failure."* - [x] **Comparison with Saint-Venant:** Saint-Venant is *linear* in stresses; Haigh is *quadratic*. Haigh is more accurate for ductile materials under combined stress. - [x] **Comparison with Tresca:** Tresca is *always more conservative* for tension-compression states. Haigh is *less conservative* (higher FOS) for tension-tension states. - [x] **Limitation:** Haigh's theory over-counts the *hydrostatic* part of energy; for high-pressure applications, von Mises is preferred. - [x] **Units Check:** LHS is in (N/mm²)² = MPa² — must match σ_yt² units. > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Three Golden Rules for KTU Answers:** > 1. **Always write the formula symbolically first**, then substitute numbers. This earns full method marks even if arithmetic slips. > 2. **Always state the verdict explicitly** ("SAFE" or "UNSAFE") and quote the FOS to 2 decimal places. > 3. **Always check the special case** (uniaxial reduction) to verify self-consistency. Examiners reward this verification step.
